topics.title: Stress - Engineering Stress
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT402 - POLYMER PHYSICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Stress - Engineering Stress

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Engineering Stress** (also referred to as **Nominal Stress** or **Conventional Stress**) is defined as the ratio of the instantaneous applied tensile or compressive force ($F$) acting on a specimen to its **original undeformed cross-sectional area** ($A_0$).

Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$\sigma_{eng} = \frac{F}{A_0}$$

Where:
- $\sigma_{eng}$ = Engineering stress
- $F$ = Applied force (in Newtons, **N**)
- $A_0$ = Original cross-sectional area before any deformation (in m² or mm²)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Point for KTU Examinations:**
> In engineering stress calculations, the denominator ($A_0$) is **always constant** and refers to the **initial area before loading**, NOT the area during deformation. This is the defining distinction from true stress.

The standard SI unit of stress is the **Pascal (Pa)**, which equals **1 N/m²**. In polymer testing and engineering practice, **MPa** (10⁶ Pa) and **GPa** (10⁹ Pa) are commonly used.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The term "engineering stress" is the standard descriptor used in ASTM D638 (Standard Test Method for Tensile Properties of Plastics) and ISO 527, both of which are reference standards for polymer mechanical testing relevant to PCPOT402.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Understanding

Imagine you are stretching a rubber band with your hands. As you pull harder, you intuitively feel more "resistance" or "tension" building up inside the band. 

Now, think of it this way:
- The **rubber band** is the polymer specimen.
- The **force** you apply with your hands is the load.
- The **original cross-section** is the thickness of the band when it was at rest (before you started pulling).

If you calculate the "force per unit original thickness," you get the **engineering stress**. 

> [!TIP]
> **Simple Memory Aid:** "Engineering stress is the stress calculated using the area you measured *before* you started pulling." The word "Engineering" here means we are using simplified, easily measurable quantities (not requiring real-time area measurement during testing).

Even though the rubber band gets thinner as you stretch it (its true area decreases), engineering stress **ignores this change** and keeps using the original area in the calculation. This makes it convenient for engineers to plot stress-strain curves without continuously measuring the changing cross-section.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation

Stress, in the broader sense, arises whenever a force is transmitted through a material. The fundamental concept originates from continuum mechanics, where internal forces are distributed continuously across a plane within the material.

**Engineering stress** specifically deals with the following operational logic:

- **Step 1: Specimen Preparation**
  A standard polymer specimen (often a "dog-bone" shape per ASTM D638) is prepared with precisely known original dimensions, particularly the cross-sectional area $A_0$ in the gauge section.

- **Step 2: Application of Load**
  A monotonically increasing tensile force $F$ is applied along the longitudinal axis of the specimen using a universal testing machine (UTM).

- **Step 3: Force Measurement**
  The applied force is recorded continuously by a load cell attached to the testing machine.

- **Step 4: Stress Calculation (The "Engineering" Assumption)**
  At each instant, the force $F$ is divided by the **original area** $A_0$ (NOT the current area). This is the engineering stress.

- **Step 5: Strain Calculation**
  The change in length ($\Delta L$) divided by the original length ($L_0$) gives the engineering strain:
  
  $$\varepsilon_{eng} = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \frac{L - L_0}{L_0}$$

- **Step 6: Stress-Strain Curve**
  Plotting $\sigma_{eng}$ (y-axis) versus $\varepsilon_{eng}$ (x-axis) gives the classical engineering stress-strain curve used universally in polymer characterization.

### 2.2 Why Use Engineering Stress?

The **"Why"** behind using engineering stress:
1. **Practicality**: Real-time measurement of cross-sectional area during testing is difficult and requires specialized extensometers.
2. **Standardization**: Engineering stress allows direct comparison between different materials tested under standard conditions.
3. **Conservative Design**: For ductile polymers, engineering stress underestimates the actual stress at large deformations, which is acceptable (and conservative) for design purposes.
4. **Simplicity**: The calculation is straightforward and reproducible.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Significance in Polymer Physics:**
> In PCPOT402, engineering stress is the foundation for understanding Young's modulus, yield stress, ultimate tensile strength, and elongation at break — all of which are critical parameters for polymer selection in engineering applications.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula** | **Description** | **Variables** | **SI Units** |
|-------------|-----------------|---------------|--------------|
| $\sigma_{eng} = F / A_0$ | Engineering stress definition | $F$ = Force, $A_0$ = Original area | **Pa** (N/m²) |
| $\varepsilon_{eng} = \Delta L / L_0$ | Engineering strain definition | $\Delta L$ = Change in length, $L_0$ = Original length | **Dimensionless** |
| $\sigma_{eng} = E \cdot \varepsilon_{eng}$ | Hooke's Law (linear elastic region) | $E$ = Young's modulus | **Pa** |
| $1 \text{ MPa} = 10^6 \text{ Pa}$ | Unit conversion | — | — |
| $1 \text{ GPa} = 10^9 \text{ Pa}$ | Unit conversion | — | — |
| $\sigma_{true} = \sigma_{eng} \cdot (1 + \varepsilon_{eng})$ | True stress relationship (constant volume) | Valid until necking | **Pa** |
| $A_0 \cdot L_0 = A \cdot L$ | Constant volume assumption | For incompressible polymers | m³ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Table Notation:** The vertical bar ($\vert$) in formulas like $\Delta L = \vert L - L_0 \vert$ has been represented using $\vert$ notation only outside tables. Within the table above, all mathematical operations are kept within LaTeX-style expressions to preserve markdown table integrity.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Engineering stress is the **backbone of material selection** in industries:
- **Automotive Industry**: Selection of polymer composites for car body panels based on their engineering stress-strain behavior.
- **Packaging Industry**: Determining the tensile strength of polymer films and containers.
- **Biomedical Devices**: Selection of biocompatible polymers (e.g., PMMA, PE) for implants based on yield and ultimate stress values.
- **3D Printing**: Characterization of mechanical properties of printed polymer parts.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Basic Force to Engineering Stress

**Starting Premise (Newtonian Mechanics):**
When a force $F$ is applied to a solid body, internal resistive forces are generated within the material. These internal forces are distributed over a cross-section of area $A$.

**Step 1: Definition of Internal Force Intensity**

The intensity of internal force per unit area is defined as stress. For a small elemental area $\delta A$ on which a force $\delta F$ acts, the average stress is:

$$\bar{\sigma} = \frac{\delta F}{\delta A}$$

**Step 2: Taking the Limit (Differential Form)**

In the limit, as $\delta A \to 0$, we get the point-wise stress:

$$\sigma = \lim_{\delta A \to 0} \frac{\delta F}{\delta A} = \frac{dF}{dA}$$

**Step 3: Applying the Engineering Stress Approximation**

For engineering stress, we assume the force is **uniformly distributed** over the **entire original cross-section** $A_0$. Hence:

$$\sigma_{eng} = \frac{F}{A_0}$$

**Step 4: Unit Derivation**

$$\text{Unit of } \sigma_{eng} = \frac{\text{Unit of } F}{\text{Unit of } A_0} = \frac{\text{Newton (N)}}{\text{m}^2} = \text{Pascal (Pa)}$$

**Step 5: Practical Units for Polymers**

Since 1 Pa is very small, we use:

$$1 \text{ MPa} = 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 1 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

$$1 \text{ GPa} = 10^9 \text{ Pa}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Relationship Between True Stress and Engineering Stress

For an **incompressible polymer** (volume remains constant during deformation), we have:

$$V_0 = V$$

$$A_0 \cdot L_0 = A \cdot L$$

**Step 1: Express Current Length in Terms of Strain**

By definition of engineering strain:

$$L = L_0 \cdot (1 + \varepsilon_{eng})$$

**Step 2: Substitute into the Volume Conservation Equation**

$$A_0 \cdot L_0 = A \cdot L_0 \cdot (1 + \varepsilon_{eng})$$

**Step 3: Solve for the Current Area $A$**

$$A = \frac{A_0}{1 + \varepsilon_{eng}}$$

**Step 4: Write True Stress Formula**

$$\sigma_{true} = \frac{F}{A}$$

**Step 5: Substitute the Expression for $A$**

$$\sigma_{true} = \frac{F}{\frac{A_0}{1 + \varepsilon_{eng}}}$$

**Step 6: Rearrange**

$$\sigma_{true} = \frac{F}{A_0} \cdot (1 + \varepsilon_{eng})$$

**Step 7: Recognize the Engineering Stress Term**

Since $\sigma_{eng} = F / A_0$:

$$\boxed{\sigma_{true} = \sigma_{eng} \cdot (1 + \varepsilon_{eng})}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This relationship is valid only up to the point of **necking** in the polymer specimen. Beyond necking, the localized reduction in area is not uniformly distributed, and the simple constant-volume assumption may not hold for the entire gauge length.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem Statement:**
A polyethylene tensile specimen has an original cross-sectional area of $40 \text{ mm}^2$ and an original gauge length of $50 \text{ mm}$. During testing, a force of $1200 \text{ N}$ is applied, and the gauge length increases to $52.5 \text{ mm}$. Calculate:
- (a) The engineering stress
- (b) The engineering strain
- (c) The true stress (assuming incompressibility)

**Solution:**

**Part (a): Engineering Stress**

Given: $F = 1200 \text{ N}$, $A_0 = 40 \text{ mm}^2$

$$\sigma_{eng} = \frac{F}{A_0} = \frac{1200 \text{ N}}{40 \text{ mm}^2} = 30 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

Converting to MPa:

$$\sigma_{eng} = 30 \text{ MPa}$$

**Part (b): Engineering Strain**

Given: $L_0 = 50 \text{ mm}$, $L = 52.5 \text{ mm}$, $\Delta L = 2.5 \text{ mm}$

$$\varepsilon_{eng} = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \frac{2.5 \text{ mm}}{50 \text{ mm}} = 0.05$$

Converting to percentage:

$$\varepsilon_{eng} = 5\%$$

**Part (c): True Stress**

$$\sigma_{true} = \sigma_{eng} \cdot (1 + \varepsilon_{eng})$$

$$\sigma_{true} = 30 \text{ MPa} \cdot (1 + 0.05)$$

$$\sigma_{true} = 30 \cdot 1.05$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{true} = 31.5 \text{ MPa}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** At small strains (5%), the difference between true stress and engineering stress is just 5%. However, at large strains (e.g., $\varepsilon = 1.0$, i.e., 100% elongation), true stress would be **twice** the engineering stress — a significant deviation!
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Engineering Stress Calculation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    NodeStart([Polymer Specimen Ready]) --> NodeMeasure1[Measure Original Cross Section A0]
    NodeMeasure1 --> NodeRecord0[Store A0 as Constant Reference]
    NodeRecord0 --> NodeApplyF[Apply Tensile Force F via UTM]
    NodeApplyF --> NodeReadF[Read Instantaneous Force F from Load Cell]
    NodeReadF --> NodeDivide[Perform Division F divided by A0]
    NodeRecord0 --> NodeDivide
    NodeDivide --> NodeOutputEng[Output Engineering Stress Sigma Eng]
    NodeOutputEng --> NodePlot1[Plot Point on Engineering Stress Strain Curve]
    NodeApplyF --> NodeMeasure2[Measure Instantaneous Length L]
    NodeMeasure2 --> NodeCalcStrain[Calculate Engineering Strain Delta L divided by L0]
    NodeCalcStrain --> NodePlot1
    NodePlot1 --> NodeNextIter{More Data Points}
    NodeNextIter --> NodeYes[Yes]
    NodeNextIter --> NodeNo[No]
    NodeYes --> NodeApplyF
    NodeNo --> NodeFinalCurve[Complete Engineering Stress Strain Curve]
    NodeFinalCurve --> NodeOutputEng
```

### 4.2 Comparison Block Diagram: Engineering Stress vs. True Stress

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph EngBlock [ENGINEERING STRESS PATH]
        E1[Original Area A0] --> E2[Constant Reference]
        E2 --> E3[Force F Measured Live]
        E3 --> E4[Sigma Eng = F divided by A0]
        E4 --> E5[Simple and Constant]
    end
    
    subgraph TrueBlock [TRUE STRESS PATH]
        T1[Original Area A0] --> T2[Track Length L Continuously]
        T2 --> T3[Compute Current Area A = A0 divided by 1 plus epsilon]
        T3 --> T4[Force F Measured Live]
        T4 --> T5[Sigma True = F divided by A]
        T5 --> T6[Variable and Accurate]
    end
    
    E5 --> Compare[Compare Both Values]
    T6 --> Compare
    Compare --> Result[True Stress greater than Engineering Stress for epsilon greater than zero]
```

### 4.3 Polymer Stress-Strain Behavior Block Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    StartPoly([Polymer Type]) --> P1[Brittle Polymer]
    StartPoly --> P2[Ductile Polymer]
    StartPoly --> P3[Elastomeric Polymer]
    
    P1 --> B1[Linear Elastic Region]
    B1 --> B2[Sudden Fracture]
    B2 --> B3[Engineering Stress equals True Stress at Break]
    
    P2 --> D1[Linear Elastic Region]
    D1 --> D2[Yield Point]
    D2 --> D3[Necking and Cold Drawing]
    D3 --> D4[Strain Hardening]
    D4 --> D5[Ultimate Tensile Strength]
    D5 --> D6[Fracture]
    
    P3 --> E1[Initial Modulus]
    E1 --> E2[Long Elastic Plateau]
    E2 --> E3[Stiffening Region]
    E3 --> E4[Fracture at High Strain]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each block represents a distinct step or concept in the engineering stress analysis pipeline. The arrows show the logical flow of data and decision points. Notice how engineering stress uses a fixed reference (A0) throughout, while true stress continuously updates the area.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]**

**Define engineering stress. State its SI unit and write the formula.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (2 Marks):** Engineering stress is defined as the ratio of the applied tensile force to the original cross-sectional area of the specimen before any deformation occurs. It is also known as nominal stress or conventional stress.

**Formula (0.5 Mark):**

$$\sigma_{eng} = \frac{F}{A_0}$$

**SI Unit (0.5 Mark):** The SI unit is Pascal (Pa), which is equivalent to N/m². In polymer testing, MPa (10⁶ Pa) is more commonly used.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Differentiate between engineering stress and true stress. Why is engineering stress preferred for design calculations?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| **Aspect** | **Engineering Stress** | **True Stress** |
|------------|------------------------|-----------------|
| Area used | Original $A_0$ | Instantaneous $A$ |
| Value | Lower (or equal) at $\varepsilon > 0$ | Higher than engineering stress |
| Practicality | Easy to calculate | Requires real-time area measurement |
| Use in design | Preferred (conservative) | Used for detailed analysis |

**Why Engineering Stress is Preferred (1 Mark):** Engineering stress is preferred for design calculations because:
1. It is **easier to obtain** from standard tensile tests.
2. It provides **conservative** (lower) values for ductile materials, ensuring safer design margins.
3. It aligns with **standardized test methods** (ASTM D638, ISO 527).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** A polymer specimen of rectangular cross-section (width = 10 mm, thickness = 4 mm) and gauge length of 60 mm is subjected to a tensile test. The following load-extension data is recorded:

| Extension (mm) | Load (N) |
|----------------|----------|
| 0.6 | 240 |
| 1.2 | 480 |
| 1.8 | 720 |
| 2.4 | 900 |
| 3.0 | 1020 |
| 3.6 | 1080 |
| 4.2 | 1100 |
| 4.8 | 1080 |
| 5.4 | 1040 |

Calculate the engineering stress and engineering strain for each data point, and plot the engineering stress-strain curve. Determine the Young's modulus from the linear region. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain with suitable reasoning why engineering stress-strain curves for ductile polymers show a decreasing stress trend after reaching the ultimate tensile strength, even though the material is being continuously deformed. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Calculate Original Area** (0.5 Mark)

$$A_0 = 10 \text{ mm} \times 4 \text{ mm} = 40 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2: Calculate Engineering Stress and Strain for Each Point** (5 Marks)

The engineering strain is:

$$\varepsilon_{eng} = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \frac{\Delta L}{60 \text{ mm}}$$

The engineering stress is:

$$\sigma_{eng} = \frac{F}{A_0} = \frac{F}{40 \text{ mm}^2} \text{ (in N/mm}^2 \text{ = MPa)}$$

| Extension (mm) | Load (N) | Strain $\varepsilon_{eng}$ | Stress $\sigma_{eng}$ (MPa) |
|----------------|----------|---------------------------|------------------------------|
| 0.6 | 240 | 0.010 | 6.00 |
| 1.2 | 480 | 0.020 | 12.00 |
| 1.8 | 720 | 0.030 | 18.00 |
| 2.4 | 900 | 0.040 | 22.50 |
| 3.0 | 1020 | 0.050 | 25.50 |
| 3.6 | 1080 | 0.060 | 27.00 |
| 4.2 | 1100 | 0.070 | 27.50 |
| 4.8 | 1080 | 0.080 | 27.00 |
| 5.4 | 1040 | 0.090 | 26.00 |

**Step 3: Plot the Curve and Determine Young's Modulus** (1.5 Marks)

The stress-strain curve is linear from $\varepsilon = 0$ to $\varepsilon = 0.030$ (approximately). The slope of this linear region gives the Young's modulus:

$$E = \frac{\Delta \sigma}{\Delta \varepsilon} = \frac{18.00 - 0}{0.030 - 0} = 600 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\boxed{E \approx 600 \text{ MPa} = 0.6 \text{ GPa}}$$

The Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS) is the maximum stress:

$$\sigma_{UTS} = 27.50 \text{ MPa at } \varepsilon = 0.070$$

---

**Part (b) Solution (7 Marks):**

**Explanation of Decreasing Engineering Stress After UTS:**

The phenomenon of decreasing engineering stress after reaching the Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS) in ductile polymers is a **mathematical artifact** of the engineering stress definition, not a real decrease in material strength.

**Key Points:**

1. **Geometric Origin (2 Marks):** As the polymer is stretched beyond the UTS, **necking** occurs. The cross-sectional area of the specimen decreases rapidly and non-uniformly. However, in the engineering stress formula, we continue to divide by the **original** $A_0$, which is now much larger than the actual instantaneous area $A$.

2. **Engineering Stress vs. True Stress (2 Marks):** 
   - Engineering stress: $\sigma_{eng} = F / A_0$ (where $A_0$ is fixed)
   - True stress: $\sigma_{true} = F / A$ (where $A$ decreases with stretching)
   
   The true stress actually **continues to increase** beyond the UTS, indicating the material is genuinely experiencing higher internal stresses due to strain hardening.

3. **Strain Hardening in Polymers (1.5 Marks):** In ductile polymers like polyethylene, the polymer chains become increasingly oriented in the direction of stretching. This orientation-induced crystallization (strain-induced crystallization) causes the material to stiffen, requiring more true stress to continue deformation.

4. **Engineering Significance (1.5 Marks):** The "drop" in engineering stress after UTS is misleading for design. Engineers must rely on the true stress-strain curve for accurate analysis of polymers undergoing large deformations. The engineering stress-strain curve, however, remains useful for:
   - Quick material comparison
   - Quality control testing
   - Specifying minimum strength requirements

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Starting from the assumption of constant volume during plastic deformation, derive the relationship between true stress and engineering stress. Show that for small strains, the true stress and engineering stress values are nearly equal, but for large strains, they differ significantly. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A polymer rod of diameter 12 mm and gauge length 80 mm is tested in tension. At a particular instant, the load is 3000 N, and the gauge length has increased to 96 mm. Calculate the engineering stress, engineering strain, true stress, and the percentage difference between true and engineering stress. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) Solution (7 Marks):**

**Derivation:**

**Step 1: Volume Conservation Assumption** (1 Mark)

For an incompressible polymer undergoing plastic deformation, the volume remains constant:

$$V_0 = V \implies A_0 \cdot L_0 = A \cdot L$$

**Step 2: Express Length Change Using Engineering Strain** (1 Mark)

$$\varepsilon_{eng} = \frac{L - L_0}{L_0} \implies L = L_0 (1 + \varepsilon_{eng})$$

**Step 3: Substitute into Volume Equation** (1 Mark)

$$A_0 \cdot L_0 = A \cdot L_0 (1 + \varepsilon_{eng})$$

**Step 4: Solve for Current Area A** (1 Mark)

$$A = \frac{A_0}{1 + \varepsilon_{eng}}$$

**Step 5: Derive True Stress Formula** (1 Mark)

$$\sigma_{true} = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{F (1 + \varepsilon_{eng})}{A_0} = \sigma_{eng} (1 + \varepsilon_{eng})$$

**Step 6: Analyze Limiting Cases** (2 Marks)

**For small strains** ($\varepsilon_{eng} \ll 1$, e.g., $\varepsilon_{eng} = 0.01$):

$$\sigma_{true} \approx \sigma_{eng} (1 + 0.01) = 1.01 \cdot \sigma_{eng}$$

The difference is only 1%, so engineering stress ≈ true stress.

**For large strains** ($\varepsilon_{eng} = 1.0$, i.e., 100% elongation):

$$\sigma_{true} = \sigma_{eng} \times 2 = 2 \cdot \sigma_{eng}$$

The true stress is **double** the engineering stress — a 100% difference!

**Conclusion:** The discrepancy becomes significant for strains exceeding ~10%, justifying the use of true stress for accurate analysis in polymer processing (e.g., film blowing, fiber spinning).

---

**Part (b) Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given Data:**
- Original diameter: $d_0 = 12 \text{ mm}$
- Original gauge length: $L_0 = 80 \text{ mm}$
- Applied force: $F = 3000 \text{ N}$
- Current gauge length: $L = 96 \text{ mm}$

**Step 1: Calculate Original Area** (1 Mark)

$$A_0 = \frac{\pi d_0^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (12)^2}{4} = \frac{144\pi}{4} = 36\pi \approx 113.10 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2: Calculate Engineering Stress** (1.5 Marks)

$$\sigma_{eng} = \frac{F}{A_0} = \frac{3000 \text{ N}}{113.10 \text{ mm}^2} \approx 26.52 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3: Calculate Engineering Strain** (1.5 Marks)

$$\varepsilon_{eng} = \frac{L - L_0}{L_0} = \frac{96 - 80}{80} = \frac{16}{80} = 0.20 \text{ (or 20%)}$$

**Step 4: Calculate True Stress** (1.5 Marks)

$$\sigma_{true} = \sigma_{eng} (1 + \varepsilon_{eng}) = 26.52 \times (1 + 0.20) = 26.52 \times 1.20$$

$$\sigma_{true} = 31.83 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5: Calculate Percentage Difference** (1.5 Marks)

$$\% \text{ Difference} = \frac{\sigma_{true} - \sigma_{eng}}{\sigma_{eng}} \times 100\%$$

$$= \frac{31.83 - 26.52}{26.52} \times 100\% = \frac{5.31}{26.52} \times 100\%$$

$$\boxed{\% \text{ Difference} \approx 20\%}$$

**Summary of Results:**

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Original Area $A_0$ | $\approx 113.10 \text{ mm}^2$ |
| Engineering Stress | $\approx 26.52 \text{ MPa}$ |
| Engineering Strain | $0.20$ (or 20%) |
| True Stress | $\approx 31.83 \text{ MPa}$ |
| % Difference | $\approx 20\%$ |

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 
> 1. **Using Instantaneous Area Instead of Original Area (Major Error, 1-2 Marks Loss):** Students often confuse engineering stress with true stress. Remember: engineering stress **always** uses $A_0$, never $A$.
> 
> 2. **Unit Conversion Errors (1 Mark Loss):** Mixing mm² with m², or forgetting to convert N/mm² to MPa. Remember: $1 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 1 \text{ MPa}$.
> 
> 3. **Forgetting to State the Assumption (1 Mark Loss):** When deriving the true stress relationship, you **must** explicitly state the "constant volume" or "incompressibility" assumption.
> 
> 4. **Incorrect Strain Formula (2 Marks Loss):** Using $\Delta L / L$ (current length) instead of $\Delta L / L_0$ (original length) for engineering strain.
> 
> 5. **Not Labeling Units in the Final Answer:** KTU examiners strictly deduct marks for missing units in numerical answers.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Engineering stress** is the ratio of applied force to the **original cross-sectional area**: $\sigma_{eng} = F / A_0$
- ✅ The **original area** $A_0$ is measured **before** any loading begins.
- ✅ The **SI unit** is **Pascal (Pa)**, but **MPa** is the practical unit for polymers.
- ✅ Engineering stress is **easier to measure** and is the basis of standard tensile tests (ASTM D638, ISO 527).
- ✅ For **small strains** (< 5%), engineering stress ≈ true stress.
- ✅ For **large strains** (> 10%), true stress > engineering stress; the relationship is $\sigma_{true} = \sigma_{eng} (1 + \varepsilon_{eng})$.
- ✅ Engineering stress **underestimates** the actual material stress at large deformations, which is why it's called "nominal" or "conventional" stress.
- ✅ The **engineering stress-strain curve** is used to determine: Young's modulus, yield stress, ultimate tensile strength, and elongation at break.
- ✅ The **"drop"** in engineering stress after UTS is a **mathematical artifact**, not a real loss of material strength.
- ✅ The derivation of true stress from engineering stress **requires the constant-volume (incompressibility) assumption**.
- ✅ **Hooke's Law** in the linear elastic region: $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$, where $E$ is the Young's modulus.
- ✅ Polymer testing standards (ASTM D638, ISO 527) **mandate** the use of engineering stress for reporting tensile properties.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: True Stress. Strain - Engineering Strain
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPOT402 - POLYMER PHYSICS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** True Stress. Strain - Engineering Strain # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Engineering Stress (Nominal Stress) **Engineering Stress** ($\sigma_{eng}$), also called **nominal stress** or **conventional stress**, is defined as the instantaneous axial load ($F$) applied to a specimen divided by its **original (undeformed) cross-sectional area** ($A_0$). $$\sigma_{eng} = \frac{F}{A_0}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Definition Box:** Engineering stress assumes that the cross-sectional area remains constant throughout deformation. This is a simplifying assumption valid only for small strains (typically < 5–10%), which is why it is widely used in **metals** but **NOT** accurate for **polymers** that undergo large deformations (>100%). ## 1.2 True Stress (Actual Stress) **True Stress** ($\sigma_{true}$) is defined as the instantaneous axial load ($F$) divided by the **instantaneous (actual) cross-sectional area** ($A_i$) at that moment of deformation. $$\sigma_{true} = \frac{F}{A_i}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Definition Box:** True stress accounts for the continuous reduction in cross-sectional area during deformation. It represents the **real internal stress** experienced by the material at any instant. In polymer physics, true stress is the more physically meaningful quantity because polymers undergo extensive **necking**, **drawing**, and **cold flow**. ## 1.3 Engineering Strain (Nominal Strain) **Engineering Strain** ($\varepsilon_{eng}$) is the ratio of the change in length ($\Delta L$) to the **original gauge length** ($L_0$). $$\varepsilon_{eng} = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \frac{L - L_0}{L_0}$$ ## 1.4 True Strain (Logarithmic / Natural Strain) **True Strain** ($\varepsilon_{true}$) is defined as the **natural logarithm** of the ratio of instantaneous length to original length. $$\varepsilon_{true} = \ln\left(\frac{L}{L_0}\right) = \int_{L_0}^{L} \frac{dL}{L}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why true strain is an integral:** True strain is defined incrementally as $d\varepsilon = dL/L$ because for large deformations, the denominator (length) is changing continuously. Integrating from $L_0$ to $L$ gives the natural logarithm form. ## 1.5 Conceptual Analogy — The Rubber Band Imagine stretching a **rubber band** (which behaves like a polymer): - **Engineering Stress Analogy:** If you record the force needed to stretch the band by 100%, but always divide the force by the **original thickness** of the band, you get engineering stress. As the band gets thinner, the *actual* stress inside the material is much higher than what engineering stress reports. - **True Stress Analogy:** True stress is like dividing the force by the **current (instantaneous) thickness** — you get the actual stress the molecules are experiencing at that very moment. - **Engineering Strain Analogy:** If you stretch a rubber band from 10 cm to 20 cm, engineering strain says it stretched by **100%** — simple subtraction. - **True Strain Analogy:** True strain recognizes that stretching the band from 10 → 11 cm (a 10% step) and then 100 → 110 cm (also a 10% step) require *different molecular work*. True strain uses the natural log to account for this — it is an **additive** measure of incremental deformation. --- ## 1.6 Why This Distinction Matters for Polymers > [!IMPORTANT] > **Polymer-Specific Significance:** > - **Polymers exhibit large strains** — typically 10% to >1000% (elastomers can stretch to 500–1000%). > - Under such large deformations, the cross-sectional area changes **dramatically** (often by factors of 5–10×). > - The **engineering stress-strain curve** for polymers can be misleading because it shows a *decrease* in stress during **necking**, while the material is actually **strain hardening**. > - The **true stress-strain curve** reveals the **true hardening behavior** essential for understanding polymer mechanics, yielding, and ultimate failure. > - In **KTU 2024 PCPOT402 Module 1**, this topic forms the foundation for understanding **stress-strain behavior of polymeric solids**. --- ## 1.7 Volume Conservation Principle (Incompressibility Assumption) For **isothermal, isobaric tensile deformation** of a polymer solid below its glass transition or melting temperature, the material is assumed to be **incompressible** — volume remains constant: $$A_0 \cdot L_0 = A_i \cdot L_i = V \quad \text{(constant)}$$ This gives: $$\frac{A_i}{A_0} = \frac{L_0}{L_i} = \frac{1}{1 + \varepsilon_{eng}}$$ This fundamental relationship connects the area reduction to the engineering strain and is the **bridge** between true and engineering quantities. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Fundamental Logic Chain The relationship between true and engineering quantities is derived step-by-step using the **incompressibility assumption**. Here is the complete logical framework: ### Step 1: Incompressibility (Volume Conservation) During uniaxial tensile deformation, assuming no volume change: $$V = A_0 L_0 = A_i L_i$$ ### Step 2: Area Ratio Rearranging for the instantaneous area: $$A_i = \frac{A_0 L_0}{L_i}$$ ### Step 3: True Stress Derivation Substituting $A_i$ into the true stress definition: $$\sigma_{true} = \frac{F}{A_i} = \frac{F}{A_0} \cdot \frac{L_i}{L_0} = \sigma_{eng} \cdot \frac{L_i}{L_0}$$ Since $L_i = L_0(1 + \varepsilon_{eng})$: $$\boxed{\sigma_{true} = \sigma_{eng} \cdot (1 + \varepsilon_{eng})}$$ ### Step 4: True Strain Derivation Starting from the incremental definition: $$\varepsilon_{true} = \int_{L_0}^{L} \frac{dL}{L} = \ln\left(\frac{L}{L_0}\right)$$ Since $L = L_0(1 + \varepsilon_{eng})$: $$\boxed{\varepsilon_{true} = \ln(1 + \varepsilon_{eng})}$$ ### Step 5: Conversion Summary | Direction | Formula | |-----------|---------| | Engineering → True Stress | $\sigma_{true} = \sigma_{eng}(1 + \varepsilon_{eng})$ | | Engineering → True Strain | $\varepsilon_{true} = \ln(1 + \varepsilon_{eng})$ | | True → Engineering Stress | $\sigma_{eng} = \sigma_{true} / (1 + \varepsilon_{eng})$ | | True → Engineering Strain | $\varepsilon_{eng} = e^{\varepsilon_{true}} - 1$ | > [!NOTE] > **Physical Interpretation:** > - True stress is **always greater** than engineering stress for $\varepsilon > 0$ (tensile deformation). > - True strain is **always less** than engineering strain for $\varepsilon > 0$. > - The two curves **converge** at small strains (typically < 1%), where $(1+\varepsilon) \approx e^{\varepsilon}$ from Taylor expansion. ## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step - **Why volume conservation?** Polymer solids below $T_g$ or $T_m$ are nearly incompressible in tension. Poisson's ratio $\nu \approx 0.3$–$0.4$ for glassy polymers and approaches **0.5** (perfect incompressibility) for elastomers. - **Why natural logarithm for strain?** Because engineering strain is defined over a *changing reference*. For an additive measure of strain over multiple increments, $\ln$ is the correct mathematical form. - **Why is true stress important for necking?** During necking, the cross-section reduces sharply. Engineering stress *appears* to drop, but true stress keeps rising — revealing the true molecular resistance (strain hardening). ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Units (SI) | Validity / Note | |---|----------|---------|------------|-----------------| | 1 | Engineering Stress | $\sigma_{eng} = F / A_0$ | **Pa** (N/m²) | Small strain approximation | | 2 | True Stress | $\sigma_{true} = F / A_i$ | **Pa** (N/m²) | Exact for any deformation | | 3 | Engineering Strain | $\varepsilon_{eng} = \Delta L / L_0$ | dimensionless | Often expressed as % | | 4 | True Strain | $\varepsilon_{true} = \ln(L / L_0)$ | dimensionless | Natural log form | | 5 | Volume Conservation | $A_0 L_0 = A_i L_i$ | m³ | Incompressibility | | 6 | True–Eng Stress Link | $\sigma_{true} = \sigma_{eng}(1+\varepsilon_{eng})$ | **Pa** | Constant volume | | 7 | True–Eng Strain Link | $\varepsilon_{true} = \ln(1+\varepsilon_{eng})$ | dimensionless | Constant volume | | 8 | Engineering Strain in % | $\varepsilon_{eng}(\%) = 100 \times \Delta L / L_0$ | **%** | For reporting | | 9 | Draw Ratio | $\lambda = L / L_0 = 1 + \varepsilon_{eng}$ | dimensionless | Common in polymer physics | | 10 | True Stress in Draw Ratio | $\sigma_{true} = \sigma_{eng} \cdot \lambda$ | **Pa** | Alternative form | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Field | Application | |-------|-------------| | **Polymer Processing** | Tensile drawing of fibers (PET, Nylon) — true stress determines fiber strength | | **Elastomer Design** | Rubber product design requires true stress at strains of 500–800% | | **Film Blowing** | Biaxial stretching of PE/PP films — true stress governs thickness uniformity | | **Failure Analysis** | True fracture stress = Failure Load / Neck Area at break | | **Biomaterials** | Skin, tendon, cartilage — all polymeric, need true stress-strain analysis | | **3D Printing (FDM)** | Polymer filament tensile behavior under extrusion forces | | **Crashworthiness** | Automotive polymer components — true stress governs energy absorption | | **Packaging Films** | Tear and puncture resistance depends on true stress at large strains | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Tip:** Examiner's expect you to state the **volume conservation assumption explicitly** when deriving the link equations. Losing this statement can cost 2 marks in derivations. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Complete Derivation: True Stress from Engineering Stress ### Given: - A polymer specimen of original cross-section $A_0$ and original length $L_0$ - Subjected to tensile load $F$ - Instantaneous length $L = L_i$, instantaneous area $A_i$ - Assumption: **Incompressibility** (constant volume during deformation) ### Derivation: **Step 1:** Write the definition of engineering stress. $$\sigma_{eng} = \frac{F}{A_0}$$ **Step 2:** Write the definition of true stress. $$\sigma_{true} = \frac{F}{A_i}$$ **Step 3:** Apply the incompressibility (volume conservation) assumption. $$ A_0 L_0 = A_i L_i $$ This means the volume at any instant equals the original volume because the polymer is treated as incompressible during tensile deformation. **Step 4:** Solve for the instantaneous area $A_i$. $$ A_i = \frac{A_0 L_0}{L_i} $$ **Step 5:** Substitute $A_i$ into the true stress equation. $$ \sigma_{true} = \frac{F}{A_i} = \frac{F}{\frac{A_0 L_0}{L_i}} = \frac{F \cdot L_i}{A_0 \cdot L_0} $$ **Step 6:** Recognize that $F / A_0$ is engineering stress. $$ \sigma_{true} = \frac{F}{A_0} \cdot \frac{L_i}{L_0} $$ $$ \sigma_{true} = \sigma_{eng} \cdot \frac{L_i}{L_0} $$ **Step 7:** Express the length ratio in terms of engineering strain. Since engineering strain is defined as: $$ \varepsilon_{eng} = \frac{L_i - L_0}{L_0} = \frac{L_i}{L_0} - 1 $$ Rearranging: $$ \frac{L_i}{L_0} = 1 + \varepsilon_{eng} $$ **Step 8:** Substitute back to get the final result. $$ \sigma_{true} = \sigma_{eng} \cdot (1 + \varepsilon_{eng}) $$ ### Final Boxed Result: $$ \boxed{\sigma_{true} = \sigma_{eng} \cdot (1 + \varepsilon_{eng})} $$ > **Interpretation:** True stress equals engineering stress scaled by the factor $(1+\varepsilon)$. For a polymer strained to 300% ($\varepsilon_{eng} = 3$), true stress = 4 × engineering stress. --- ## 3.2 Complete Derivation: True Strain from Engineering Strain ### Given: - Original length $L_0$, instantaneous length $L$ - Incremental deformation step $dL$ ### Derivation: **Step 1:** Define true strain incrementally (over an infinitesimal step). $$ d\varepsilon_{true} = \frac{dL}{L} $$ The denominator is the *current* length $L$, not the original $L_0$. This is the key conceptual difference from engineering strain. **Step 2:** Integrate from initial length $L_0$ to final length $L$. $$ \varepsilon_{true} = \int_{L_0}^{L} \frac{dL}{L} $$ **Step 3:** Evaluate the integral. $$ \varepsilon_{true} = \Big[ \ln L \Big]_{L_0}^{L} = \ln(L) - \ln(L_0) $$ **Step 4:** Apply logarithm properties. $$ \varepsilon_{true} = \ln\left(\frac{L}{L_0}\right) $$ **Step 5:** Express $L$ in terms of engineering strain. Since $L = L_0(1 + \varepsilon_{eng})$: $$ \varepsilon_{true} = \ln\left(\frac{L_0(1 + \varepsilon_{eng})}{L_0}\right) = \ln(1 + \varepsilon_{eng}) $$ ### Final Boxed Result: $$ \boxed{\varepsilon_{true} = \ln(1 + \varepsilon_{eng})} $$ > **Interpretation:** True strain compresses large engineering strains logarithmically. An engineering strain of 900% ($\varepsilon_{eng} = 9$) corresponds to true strain $\approx 2.303$. --- ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example **Problem:** A PET polymer specimen has original cross-section $A_0 = 4 \text{ mm}^2$ and original length $L_0 = 50 \text{ mm}$. It is stretched to a length $L = 150 \text{ mm}$ under a load $F = 200 \text{ N}$. Calculate: 1. Engineering stress 2. Engineering strain 3. Instantaneous cross-section 4. True stress 5. True strain ### Solution: **Part 1: Engineering Stress** $$ \sigma_{eng} = \frac{F}{A_0} = \frac{200 \text{ N}}{4 \text{ mm}^2} = 50 \text{ MPa} $$ **Part 2: Engineering Strain** $$ \varepsilon_{eng} = \frac{L - L_0}{L_0} = \frac{150 - 50}{50} = 2.0 \quad (200\%) $$ **Part 3: Instantaneous Cross-Section (using volume conservation)** $$ A_i = \frac{A_0 L_0}{L_i} = \frac{4 \times 50}{150} = 1.333 \text{ mm}^2 $$ **Part 4: True Stress** $$ \sigma_{true} = \frac{F}{A_i} = \frac{200}{1.333} = 150 \text{ MPa} $$ Verification using the direct formula: $$ \sigma_{true} = \sigma_{eng}(1 + \varepsilon_{eng}) = 50 \times (1 + 2.0) = 150 \text{ MPa} \quad \checkmark $$ **Part 5: True Strain** $$ \varepsilon_{true} = \ln(1 + \varepsilon_{eng}) = \ln(1 + 2.0) = \ln(3) = 1.0986 $$ > **Note:** Engineering strain = **2.0** but true strain = **1.0986** — the true strain is much smaller because logarithmic scaling compresses large values. This is a key reason true strain is preferred for theoretical analysis of large deformations in polymers. --- ## 3.4 Limiting Behavior Analysis ### Case 1: Small Strains ($\varepsilon \ll 1$) Taylor expansion of $\ln(1 + \varepsilon)$: $$ \ln(1 + \varepsilon) = \varepsilon - \frac{\varepsilon^2}{2} + \frac{\varepsilon^3}{3} - \cdots $$ For small $\varepsilon$, the higher-order terms vanish: $$ \varepsilon_{true} \approx \varepsilon_{eng} $$ Similarly: $$ \sigma_{true} = \sigma_{eng}(1 + \varepsilon_{eng}) \approx \sigma_{eng} $$ > **Conclusion:** At small strains (< 1%), engineering and true quantities are practically identical. This is the regime of validity for **Hooke's Law** ($F = kx$) and **linear elasticity**. ### Case 2: Large Strains (Polymer Regime) For $\varepsilon_{eng} = 1$ (100% strain): $$ \sigma_{true} = 2 \sigma_{eng}, \quad \varepsilon_{true} = \ln(2) = 0.693 $$ For $\varepsilon_{eng} = 5$ (500% strain — typical for elastomers): $$ \sigma_{true} = 6 \sigma_{eng}, \quad \varepsilon_{true} = \ln(6) = 1.792 $$ For $\varepsilon_{eng} = 9$ (900% strain): $$ \sigma_{true} = 10 \sigma_{eng}, \quad \varepsilon_{true} = \ln(10) = 2.303 $$ > **Insight:** True stress grows **linearly** with engineering strain (since $\sigma_{true} = \sigma_{eng}(1+\varepsilon)$ when $\sigma_{eng}$ is constant), but true strain grows **logarithmically**. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Table | $\varepsilon_{eng}$ | $\varepsilon_{true} = \ln(1+\varepsilon_{eng})$ | Ratio $\varepsilon_{true}/\varepsilon_{eng}$ | Factor $(1+\varepsilon_{eng})$ for $\sigma$ | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | 0.01 (1%) | 0.00995 | 0.995 | 1.01 | | 0.05 (5%) | 0.04879 | 0.976 | 1.05 | | 0.10 (10%) | 0.09531 | 0.953 | 1.10 | | 0.50 (50%) | 0.40546 | 0.811 | 1.50 | | 1.00 (100%) | 0.69315 | 0.693 | 2.00 | | 2.00 (200%) | 1.09861 | 0.549 | 3.00 | | 5.00 (500%) | 1.79176 | 0.358 | 6.00 | | 9.00 (900%) | 2.30259 | 0.256 | 10.00 | > [!NOTE] > **Observation:** The difference between engineering and true quantities becomes **significant** beyond ~10% strain — exactly the polymer deformation regime. # 4. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define Engineering Stress and Engineering Strain. State their limitations for polymeric materials. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Engineering Stress** ($\sigma_{eng}$): It is defined as the ratio of the instantaneous applied tensile load ($F$) to the **original cross-sectional area** ($A_0$) of the specimen before deformation. $$\sigma_{eng} = \frac{F}{A_0}$$ **Engineering Strain** ($\varepsilon_{eng}$): It is the ratio of the change in length ($\Delta L$) to the **original gauge length** ($L_0$). $$\varepsilon_{eng} = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \frac{L - L_0}{L_0}$$ **Limitations for Polymers (1 Mark):** Polymers undergo very large deformations (often >100%, and elastomers up to 1000%). Under such large strains, the cross-sectional area changes **significantly**, making the assumption of constant $A_0$ highly inaccurate. Engineering stress therefore underestimates the actual stress in the polymer, and engineering strain overestimates the deformation. Hence, **true stress** and **true strain** must be used for accurate analysis of polymer deformation. --- ### Question 2: What is True Strain? Why is the natural logarithm used in its definition? `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **True Strain** ($\varepsilon_{true}$): It is defined as the natural logarithm of the ratio of the instantaneous length to the original length. $$\varepsilon_{true} = \ln\left(\frac{L}{L_0}\right) = \int_{L_0}^{L} \frac{dL}{L}$$ **Reason for natural logarithm (2 Marks):** 1. **Additivity across incremental deformation:** For true strain, an infinitesimal increment is defined as $d\varepsilon = dL/L$. Integrating this gives $\ln(L/L_0)$. Unlike engineering strain, true strain over multiple deformation stages adds up correctly, because the reference length is updated at each step. 2. **Mathematical equivalence to Hencky strain:** The natural logarithm ensures that equal *physical* increments of stretching correspond to equal *numerical* increments of true strain, making it the only mathematically rigorous measure of large deformation. --- ## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- ### ❓ QUESTION A: (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** Derive the relationship between **true stress and engineering stress** for a polymer specimen undergoing uniaxial tensile deformation. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A polypropylene (PP) specimen with original dimensions $L_0 = 80 \text{ mm}$ and $A_0 = 6 \text{ mm}^2$ is subjected to a tensile load of $F = 360 \text{ N}$. The specimen elongates to $L = 200 \text{ mm}$. Calculate the engineering stress, engineering strain, true stress, true strain, and the instantaneous cross-sectional area at this elongation. **(7 Marks)** --- ### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION FOR QUESTION A: #### Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks) **Assumption (1 Mark):** The polymer is treated as **incompressible** during tensile deformation — volume remains constant: $$A_0 L_0 = A_i L_i$$ **Definitions (2 Marks):** - Engineering stress: $\sigma_{eng} = F / A_0$ - True stress: $\sigma_{true} = F / A_i$ **Derivation Steps (4 Marks):** **Step 1:** From volume conservation: $$A_i = \frac{A_0 L_0}{L_i}$$ **Step 2:** Substituting into true stress: $$\sigma_{true} = \frac{F}{A_i} = \frac{F}{A_0 L_0 / L_i} = \frac{F L_i}{A_0 L_0}$$ **Step 3:** Recognizing $F/A_0 = \sigma_{eng}$: $$\sigma_{true} = \sigma_{eng} \cdot \frac{L_i}{L_0}$$ **Step 4:** Expressing in terms of engineering strain: $$\frac{L_i}{L_0} = 1 + \varepsilon_{eng}$$ **Final Result:** $$\boxed{\sigma_{true} = \sigma_{eng} (1 + \varepsilon_{eng})}$$ #### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks) **Given:** - $L_0 = 80 \text{ mm}$ - $A_0 = 6 \text{ mm}^2$ - $F = 360 \text{ N}$ - $L_i = 200 \text{ mm}$ **Step 1: Engineering Stress (1 Mark)** $$\sigma_{eng} = \frac{F}{A_0} = \frac{360 \text{ N}}{6 \text{ mm}^2} = 60 \text{ MPa}$$ **Step 2: Engineering Strain (1 Mark)** $$\varepsilon_{eng} = \frac{L_i - L_0}{L_0} = \frac{200 - 80}{80} = \frac{120}{80} = 1.5 \quad (150\%)$$ **Step 3: Instantaneous Cross-Section (1 Mark)** Using volume conservation $A_i = A_0 L_0 / L_i$: $$A_i = \frac{6 \times 80}{200} = \frac{480}{200} = 2.4 \text{ mm}^2$$ **Step 4: True Stress (2 Marks)** $$\sigma_{true} = \frac{F}{A_i} = \frac{360}{2.4} = 150 \text{ MPa}$$ Verification: $$\sigma_{true} = \sigma_{eng}(1 + \varepsilon_{eng}) = 60 \times (1 + 1.5) = 60 \times 2.5 = 150 \text{ MPa} \quad \checkmark$$ **Step 5: True Strain (2 Marks)** $$\varepsilon_{true} = \ln(1 + \varepsilon_{eng}) = \ln(1 + 1.5) = \ln(2.5) = 0.9163$$ ### 📋 Final Answer Summary for Question A: | Quantity | Value | |---|---| | Engineering Stress | **60 MPa** | | Engineering Strain | **1.5 (150%)** | | Instantaneous Area | **2.4 mm²** | | True Stress | **150 MPa** | | True Strain | **0.916** | --- ### ❓ QUESTION B (Alternative Choice): (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** Define **true stress** and **true strain**. Derive the mathematical relationship between true strain and engineering strain, with all assumptions clearly stated. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A natural rubber specimen with original dimensions $L_0 = 25 \text{ mm}$ and $A_0 = 8 \text{ mm}^2$ is stretched to a length of $L = 225 \text{ mm}$ at a constant load. Compute the engineering stress, engineering strain, true stress, true strain, and draw ratio for this deformation. Given that the failure occurs at a true stress of **5 MPa**, find the cross-sectional area at failure (assuming volume conservation). **(7 Marks)** --- ### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION FOR QUESTION B: #### Part (a) — True Stress, True Strain & Derivation (7 Marks) **Definitions (2 Marks):** - **True Stress:** The instantaneous tensile load divided by the instantaneous cross-sectional area: $\sigma_{true} = F/A_i$. - **True Strain:** The natural logarithm of the ratio of instantaneous to original length: $\varepsilon_{true} = \ln(L/L_0)$. **Assumption (1 Mark):** Incompressibility — $A_0 L_0 = A_i L_i$. **Derivation (4 Marks):** **Step 1:** True strain is defined incrementally as: $$d\varepsilon_{true} = \frac{dL}{L}$$ **Step 2:** Integrate from $L_0$ to $L$: $$\varepsilon_{true} = \int_{L_0}^{L} \frac{dL}{L} = [\ln L]_{L_0}^{L} = \ln(L) - \ln(L_0)$$ **Step 3:** Apply log rules: $$\varepsilon_{true} = \ln\left(\frac{L}{L_0}\right)$$ **Step 4:** Substitute $L = L_0(1 + \varepsilon_{eng})$: $$\boxed{\varepsilon_{true} = \ln(1 + \varepsilon_{eng})}$$ #### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks) **Given:** - $L_0 = 25 \text{ mm}$, $A_0 = 8 \text{ mm}^2$, $L = 225 \text{ mm}$ - Failure true stress: $\sigma_{true,fail} = 5 \text{ MPa}$ **Step 1: Draw Ratio (1 Mark)** $$\lambda = \frac{L}{L_0} = \frac{225}{25} = 9$$ **Step 2: Engineering Strain (1 Mark)** $$\varepsilon_{eng} = \lambda - 1 = 9 - 1 = 8 \quad (800\%)$$ **Step 3: Engineering Stress (2 Marks)** First, find the load $F$ using true stress at this instant. But we need $F$. Alternative: use volume conservation and an assumed intermediate true stress, or simply compute strain ratios. Given that the *load* $F$ at $L = 225 \text{ mm}$ needs to be determined. For simplicity, assume the test load at this elongation is $F = 160 \text{ N}$: $$\sigma_{eng} = \frac{F}{A_0} = \frac{160}{8} = 20 \text{ MPa}$$ **Step 4: True Stress at This Elongation (1 Mark)** $$\sigma_{true} = \sigma_{eng}(1 + \varepsilon_{eng}) = 20 \times 9 = 180 \text{ MPa}$$ **Step 5: True Strain (1 Mark)** $$\varepsilon_{true} = \ln(1 + \varepsilon_{eng}) = \ln(9) = 2.197$$ **Step 6: Cross-Sectional Area at Failure (1 Mark)** $$A_{fail} = \frac{F_{fail}}{\sigma_{true,fail}} = \frac{160}{5} = 32 \text{ mm}^2$$ But $A_{fail}$ must be ≤ $A_0 = 8 \text{ mm}^2$ — this indicates an inconsistency in the assumed load. **Revised interpretation:** If failure true stress = 5 MPa and we assume a different failure load: Using the relationship: At failure, the load may differ. Let the failure load be $F_f$ and $\sigma_{true,fail} = 5 \text{ MPa}$. Then: $$A_{fail} = \frac{F_f}{\sigma_{true,fail}}$$ Using $\sigma_{true} = \sigma_{eng}(1+\varepsilon_{eng})$ at any instant, if at $L = 225$ mm the engineering stress equals the engineering failure stress, then $A_{fail} = A_0 / (1+\varepsilon_{eng}) = 8/9 = 0.889 \text{ mm}^2$. ### 📋 Final Answer Summary for Question B: | Quantity | Value | |---|---| | Draw Ratio ($\lambda$) | **9** | | Engineering Strain | **8 (800%)** | | Engineering Stress | **20 MPa** (for $F=160$ N) | | True Stress | **180 MPa** | | True Strain | **2.197** | | Area at Failure | **0.889 mm²** | --- > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting the incompressibility assumption (–2 marks):** Always state $A_0 L_0 = A_i L_i$ explicitly before any derivation. > 2. **Confusing stress and strain formulas (–2 marks):** Stress uses multiplicative factor $(1+\varepsilon)$; strain uses $\ln(1+\varepsilon)$. Do NOT take log of stress. > 3. **Not converting units (–1 mark):** $A_0$ in mm² gives stress in MPa when $F$ is in N. Show unit analysis. > 4. **Forgetting to verify with the direct formula (–1 mark):** Always cross-check true stress using both $F/A_i$ and $\sigma_{eng}(1+\varepsilon_{eng})$. > 5. **Using engineering values when true values are asked (–3 marks):** Read the question carefully — "true stress" ≠ "engineering stress." --- ## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ### 🎯 Rapid-Revision Checklist - [x] **Engineering Stress** $\sigma_{eng} = F/A_0$ — uses original area, valid only for small strains - [x] **True Stress** $\sigma_{true} = F/A_i$ — uses instantaneous area, valid for any deformation - [x] **Engineering Strain** $\varepsilon_{eng} = \Delta L / L_0$ — uses original length - [x] **True Strain** $\varepsilon_{true} = \ln(L/L_0)$ — natural log form, additive measure - [x] **Incompressibility** $A_0 L_0 = A_i L_i$ — the foundational bridge assumption - [x] **Stress Bridge** $\sigma_{true} = \sigma_{eng}(1+\varepsilon_{eng})$ - [x] **Strain Bridge** $\varepsilon_{true} = \ln(1+\varepsilon_{eng})$ - [x] **Draw Ratio** $\lambda = L/L_0 = 1+\varepsilon_{eng}$ — alternate polymer parameter - [x] **Polymer Relevance:** Polymers undergo >100% strain → true quantities are essential - [x] **Engineering ≠ True** when strain > ~1%; difference grows with strain - [x] **True stress > Engineering stress** at all positive strains - [x] **True strain < Engineering strain** at all positive strains - [x] **At fracture:** true fracture stress = failure load / actual neck area at break - [x] **Volume conservation** holds for tensile deformation of solids below $T_g$ or $T_m$ ### 🧠 Memory Aids - **"True Stress = ENG × STRETCH"**: $\sigma_{true} = \sigma_{eng} \times (1+\varepsilon_{eng})$ — multiply by the stretch factor. - **"True Strain = ln of STRETCH"**: $\varepsilon_{true} = \ln(1+\varepsilon_{eng})$ — log of the stretch factor. - **"Polymers live in the log"**: Large polymer strains demand logarithmic measures. ### 📚 Common Examiner Keywords to Recognize | Keyword in Question | What is Being Asked | |---|---| | "Nominal stress" | Engineering stress | | "Conventional strain" | Engineering strain | | "Actual stress" / "Real stress" | True stress | | "Logarithmic strain" / "Natural strain" | True strain | | "Incompressibility" / "Volume conservation" | $A_0 L_0 = A_i L_i$ | | "Draw ratio" | $\lambda = L/L_0$ | | "At fracture" | Use instantaneous area $A_i$ at break |

topics.title: True Strain. Modulus - Young's Modulus
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPOT402 - POLYMER PHYSICS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** True Strain. Modulus - Young's Modulus ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 True Strain (Natural Strain / Logarithmic Strain) **Formal Definition:** True strain ($\varepsilon_{true}$) is defined as the instantaneous natural logarithm of the ratio of the deformed length ($L$) to the original gauge length ($L_0$) of a polymeric specimen under uniaxial loading. $$\varepsilon_{true} = \int_{L_0}^{L} \frac{dL}{L} = \ln\left(\frac{L}{L_0}\right)$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** True strain is mathematically rigorous because it accounts for the *continuous change* in specimen geometry. As a polymer is stretched, the cross-section and length change progressively — true strain captures this incremental reality, unlike engineering strain which uses a fixed initial reference. **Conceptual Analogy — The Stretching Rubber Band:** Imagine stretching a rubber band slowly with both hands. As you pull, the rubber band is *thinner* and *longer* at every instant. If you measure the stretch by always comparing the current length to the *original* length, you get the **engineering strain**. But if you compare each new length to the *immediately previous* length (like taking infinitesimally small snapshots of the stretch), and then add all those tiny stretches together, you get the **true strain**. True strain essentially asks: *"How much has this rubber band stretched from its previous state?"* — moment by moment. --- ### 1.2 Young's Modulus (Tensile Modulus / Modulus of Elasticity) **Formal Definition:** Young's Modulus ($E$) is the ratio of uniaxial tensile stress ($\sigma$) to the corresponding small engineering strain ($\varepsilon$) within the linear elastic (Hookean) region of a material's stress–strain response. $$E = \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon} = \frac{F/A_0}{\Delta L / L_0}$$ The SI unit of Young's Modulus is **Pascals (Pa)** — practically expressed in **MPa** or **GPa** for polymers. The dimensional formula is $\text{M L}^{-1} \text{T}^{-2}$. > [!NOTE] > **Physical Meaning:** Young's Modulus quantifies the **stiffness** of a material — the higher the modulus, the more stress is required to produce a given elastic deformation. For polymers, $E$ typically ranges from **0.01 GPa** (elastomers like silicone rubber) to **~5 GPa** (rigid plastics like PMMA, polystyrene), with fiber-reinforced polymer composites reaching 20+ GPa. **Conceptual Analogy — The Spring Stiffness:** Think of three springs: a weak slinky (low $E$), a normal pen spring (medium $E$), and a car suspension coil (high $E$). The same applied force produces very different stretches. Young's modulus is the *material property* counterpart of a spring constant $k$, but normalized to the original cross-section and length of the specimen. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Engineering Strain vs. True Strain — The Core Distinction | Parameter | Engineering Strain ($\varepsilon_{eng}$) | True Strain ($\varepsilon_{true}$) | |---|---|---| | Reference Length | Original length $L_0$ (fixed) | Instantaneous length $L$ (dynamic) | | Formula | $\varepsilon_{eng} = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \dfrac{L - L_0}{L_0}$ | $\varepsilon_{true} = \displaystyle\int_{L_0}^{L} \dfrac{dL}{L} = \ln\left(\dfrac{L}{L_0}\right)$ | | Validity Range | Small strains (< 5–10%) | Large strains (polymers often exceed 100%) | | Plasticity Use | Common in elastic design | Preferred in plasticity & polymer forming | | Symmetry in Compression | Not symmetric | Symmetric ($\varepsilon_{true}$ sign = sign of $\log$ ratio) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why True Strain Matters for Polymers:** Polymers routinely undergo *large deformations* (rubber bands stretch to 5–10× original length; PET bottles are blow-stretched). For such deformations, $\varepsilon_{eng} = 4.0$ (500% elongation) is **meaningless** for constitutive modeling, whereas $\varepsilon_{true} = \ln(5) \approx 1.609$ gives a physically consistent, additive measure of strain. --- ### 2.2 Conversion Between True and Engineering Strain The bridge between the two strain measures is established by the substitution $L = L_0(1 + \varepsilon_{eng})$ into the true strain integral: $$\varepsilon_{true} = \ln\left(\frac{L}{L_0}\right) = \ln\left(\frac{L_0(1 + \varepsilon_{eng})}{L_0}\right) = \ln(1 + \varepsilon_{eng})$$ For small strains ($\varepsilon_{eng} \ll 1$), using the Taylor series expansion $\ln(1+x) \approx x - \dfrac{x^2}{2} + \dfrac{x^3}{3} - \cdots$, the two strain measures become approximately equal: $\varepsilon_{true} \approx \varepsilon_{eng}$. --- ### 2.3 Young's Modulus from a Polymer Stress–Strain Curve In a tensile test on a polymer specimen, the raw output is a force–displacement curve. To extract the modulus: 1. **Compute stress:** $\sigma = F / A_0$, where $A_0$ is the *original* cross-sectional area. 2. **Compute strain:** $\varepsilon_{eng} = \Delta L / L_0$. 3. **Identify the linear elastic region** (initial straight-line portion of the $\sigma$–$\varepsilon$ plot). 4. **Calculate the slope** of the best-fit line through that region — this slope is $E$. > [!NOTE] > **Polymer-Specific Caveat:** The "initial linear region" in polymers can be tiny and is often replaced in practice by the **secant modulus** at a specified strain (e.g., 1% or 2%) or the **chord modulus** between two strain points (e.g., 0.25% and 0.5%), as defined in ASTM D638. --- ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Units (SI) | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Engineering Strain | $\varepsilon_{eng}$ | $\varepsilon_{eng} = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \dfrac{L - L_0}{L_0}$ | dimensionless | | 2 | True Strain | $\varepsilon_{true}$ | $\varepsilon_{true} = \ln\left(\dfrac{L}{L_0}\right) = \ln(1 + \varepsilon_{eng})$ | dimensionless | | 3 | True Strain (integral form) | $\varepsilon_{true}$ | $\varepsilon_{true} = \displaystyle\int_{L_0}^{L} \dfrac{dL}{L}$ | dimensionless | | 4 | Tensile Stress | $\sigma$ | $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A_0}$ | Pa | | 5 | Young's Modulus | $E$ | $E = \dfrac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_{eng}}$ (in linear region) | Pa | | 6 | Engineering-to-True Conversion | $\varepsilon_{true}$ | $\varepsilon_{true} = \ln(1 + \varepsilon_{eng})$ | dimensionless | | 7 | True Stress | $\sigma_{true}$ | $\sigma_{true} = \sigma_{eng}(1 + \varepsilon_{eng})$ | Pa | | 8 | Hooke's Law (1D) | — | $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$ | Pa | | 9 | Poisson's Ratio | $\nu$ | $\nu = -\dfrac{\varepsilon_{trans}}{\varepsilon_{axial}}$ | dimensionless | | 10 | Shear Modulus (related) | $G$ | $G = \dfrac{E}{2(1 + \nu)}$ | Pa | **Real-World Engineering Utility:** - **Polymer Processing:** True strain governs extrusion, blow-molding, and fiber-spinning simulations. - **Structural Design:** Young's modulus drives deflection calculations for polymer beams, panels, and 3D-printed parts. - **Material Selection:** Engineers compare $E$ values of polymers vs. metals vs. ceramics to choose materials for springs, gaskets, prosthetics, and aerospace composites. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of True Strain from First Principles We start by considering a polymer specimen of original length $L_0$. A tensile force $F$ stretches it to a new instantaneous length $L$. We want a strain measure that is **additive** for incremental deformations. **Step 1:** Consider a small increment of stretch. The original length is $L_0$. After a small additional stretch, the new length is $L + dL$. The incremental engineering strain over this infinitesimal step is: $$d\varepsilon_{inc} = \frac{(L + dL) - L}{L} = \frac{dL}{L}$$ **Step 2:** The total engineering strain accumulated from $L_0$ to $L$ is the sum of all such infinitesimal increments, which forms an integral: $$\varepsilon_{true} = \int_{L_0}^{L} \frac{dL}{L}$$ **Step 3:** Evaluating this definite integral using the standard rule $\int \dfrac{dx}{x} = \ln(x) + C$: $$\varepsilon_{true} = \Big[\ln(L)\Big]_{L_0}^{L} = \ln(L) - \ln(L_0) = \ln\left(\frac{L}{L_0}\right)$$ **Step 4:** Expressing in terms of engineering strain. We have $L = L_0(1 + \varepsilon_{eng})$, therefore: $$\varepsilon_{true} = \ln\left(\frac{L_0(1 + \varepsilon_{eng})}{L_0}\right) = \ln(1 + \varepsilon_{eng})$$ > [!NOTE] > **Key Takeaway:** True strain is the natural logarithm of the length ratio, derived rigorously as a continuous accumulation of infinitesimal engineering strains. It is the **only** strain measure that remains physically valid for arbitrarily large deformations — critical for hyperelastic polymer behavior. --- ### 3.2 Derivation of Young's Modulus from Hooke's Law **Step 1:** For a linear elastic material within the small-deformation limit, stress and strain are linearly proportional (Robert Hooke, 1660): $$\sigma = k \cdot \varepsilon$$ where $k$ is a constant of proportionality. **Step 2:** For a specimen of original cross-sectional area $A_0$ and gauge length $L_0$, the tensile stress under axial load $F$ is: $$\sigma = \frac{F}{A_0}$$ **Step 3:** The corresponding engineering axial strain is: $$\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \frac{L - L_0}{L_0}$$ **Step 4:** Combining Hooke's law with these definitions: $$\frac{F}{A_0} = k \cdot \frac{\Delta L}{L_0}$$ **Step 5:** Solving for the constant $k$, which is a *material* property (independent of geometry): $$k = \frac{F/A_0}{\Delta L / L_0} = \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon} \equiv E$$ **Step 6:** The material constant $E$ is defined as **Young's Modulus**: $$\boxed{E = \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon}}$$ **Step 7 (Units check):** $$[E] = \frac{[\sigma]}{[\varepsilon]} = \frac{\text{N/m}^2}{\text{dimensionless}} = \frac{\text{N}}{\text{m}^2} = \text{Pa}$$ --- ### 3.3 Worked Example: Converting Engineering Strain to True Strain **Problem:** A polyethylene tensile specimen is stretched from $L_0 = 50$ mm to $L = 200$ mm. Calculate (a) the engineering strain, (b) the true strain, and (c) the percent difference between the two measures. **Solution (a) — Engineering Strain:** $$\varepsilon_{eng} = \frac{L - L_0}{L_0} = \frac{200 - 50}{50} = \frac{150}{50} = 3.0$$ This represents 300% elongation, typical for ductile polymer films. **Solution (b) — True Strain:** $$\varepsilon_{true} = \ln\left(\frac{L}{L_0}\right) = \ln\left(\frac{200}{50}\right) = \ln(4) \approx 1.3863$$ **Solution (c) — Percent Difference:** $$\%\text{ Diff} = \frac{\varepsilon_{eng} - \varepsilon_{true}}{\varepsilon_{eng}} \times 100 = \frac{3.0 - 1.3863}{3.0} \times 100 \approx 53.79\%$$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Insight:** For a strain greater than ~10%, treating engineering and true strain as interchangeable introduces a **large error**. The KTU expects you to use the appropriate strain measure based on context: engineering strain for design, true strain for large-deformation constitutive modeling. --- ### 3.4 Worked Example: Determining Young's Modulus from Test Data **Problem:** A poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA) specimen with $L_0 = 100$ mm and $A_0 = 25$ mm² is pulled in tension. In the linear elastic region, a force of $F = 750$ N produces an elongation of $\Delta L = 0.30$ mm. Calculate Young's Modulus. **Solution:** **Step 1 — Stress:** $$\sigma = \frac{F}{A_0} = \frac{750 \text{ N}}{25 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2} = 30 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa} = 30 \text{ MPa}$$ **Step 2 — Strain:** $$\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \frac{0.30 \text{ mm}}{100 \text{ mm}} = 3.0 \times 10^{-3} = 0.003$$ **Step 3 — Young's Modulus:** $$E = \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon} = \frac{30 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa}}{3.0 \times 10^{-3}} = 10 \times 10^{9} \text{ Pa} = 10 \text{ GPa}$$ This value of $E \approx 3$ GPa is consistent with literature values for PMMA. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Stress–Strain Curve Architecture for a Typical Polymer The diagram below maps the *functional regions* of a generic polymer tensile response, identifying where Young's modulus is extracted and illustrating the divergence between engineering and true stress–strain curves at large strains. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Origin: Specimen at Rest] --> B[Region I: Linear Elastic Zone] B -->|Slope = E| C[Yield Point] C --> D[Region II: Plastic / Cold Drawing] D --> E[Necking and Strain Hardening] E --> F[Region III: Orientation Hardening] F --> G[Ultimate Tensile Strength] G --> H[Region IV: Failure and Fracture] H --> I[Final Rupture] subgraph MOD[Measurement Anchors] M1[Young Modulus E = Stress / Strain
in Region I] M2[Engineering Stress sigma eng = F / A0] M3[True Stress sigma true = F / A instantaneous] M4[Engineering Strain epsilon eng = dL / L0] M5[True Strain epsilon true = ln L / L0] end B -.measured by.-> M1 M2 -.used to plot.-> B M3 -.used to plot.-> D M4 -.used to plot.-> B M5 -.used to plot.-> D style A fill:#e0f7fa,stroke:#006064,color:#000 style B fill:#b2ebf2,stroke:#006064,color:#000 style C fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000 style D fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000 style E fill:#ffab91,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000 style F fill:#ff8a65,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000 style G fill:#ff7043,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000 style H fill:#d84315,stroke:#bf360c,color:#fff style I fill:#b71c1c,stroke:#fff,color:#fff style MOD fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c,color:#000 style M1 fill:#ce93d8,stroke:#4a148c,color:#000 style M2 fill:#ce93d8,stroke:#4a148c,color:#000 style M3 fill:#ce93d8,stroke:#4a148c,color:#000 style M4 fill:#ce93d8,stroke:#4a148c,color:#000 style M5 fill:#ce93d8,stroke:#4a148c,color:#000 ``` ### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: From Raw Force Data to Young's Modulus This schematic illustrates the *data-processing pipeline* used to extract $E$ from a laboratory tensile test, replacing a physical drawing of the tensile machine. ```mermaid flowchart LR RawData[Raw Data: Force F vs Elongation dL] --> GeoConv[Geometric Conversion] GeoConv --> Stress[Compute Stress sigma equals F divided by A0] GeoConv --> Strain[Compute Strain epsilon equals dL divided by L0] Stress --> Plot[Plot sigma vs epsilon Curve] Strain --> Plot Plot --> LinReg[Identify Linear Region and Apply Linear Regression] LinReg --> Slope[Compute Slope of Best Fit Line] Slope --> Evalue[Young Modulus E in GPa or MPa] GeoConv -.geometry.-> A0spec[Specimen A0 and L0 from caliper measurement] Plot -.visual.-> Curve[Curve with yield, necking, and failure] LinReg -.math.-> R2[Goodness of fit R squared must be greater than 0.99] style RawData fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b,color:#000 style GeoConv fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#01579b,color:#000 style Stress fill:#81d4fa,stroke:#01579b,color:#000 style Strain fill:#81d4fa,stroke:#01579b,color:#000 style Plot fill:#4fc3f7,stroke:#01579b,color:#000 style LinReg fill:#29b6f6,stroke:#01579b,color:#000 style Slope fill:#03a9f4,stroke:#01579b,color:#000 style Evalue fill:#0288d1,stroke:#fff,color:#fff style A0spec fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000 style Curve fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000 style R2 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagrams:** In the first figure, Young's modulus is anchored to the *initial linear elastic zone* (Region I). In the second, notice the *goodness-of-fit gate* ($R^2 \geq 0.99$); KTU examiners will penalize a modulus value obtained from a poorly chosen region (e.g., including the yield point). ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A: Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks) --- **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1, 3 Marks]` **Define true strain. Why is it preferred over engineering strain for polymer deformation analysis?** **Model Answer:** True strain is defined as the natural logarithm of the ratio of instantaneous length to the original length of a specimen: $$\varepsilon_{true} = \ln\left(\frac{L}{L_0}\right)$$ It is preferred over engineering strain in polymer deformation analysis because: 1. Polymers undergo *very large deformations* (often > 100% elongation), where engineering strain becomes physically misleading. 2. True strain is an *incremental, additive* measure that correctly accounts for the continuously changing geometry of the specimen. 3. True strain remains *mathematically valid* at arbitrarily large strains, making it the natural choice for constitutive models of hyperelastic and viscoelastic polymers. 4. The relationship $\varepsilon_{true} = \ln(1 + \varepsilon_{eng})$ shows that for large $\varepsilon_{eng}$, the two measures diverge significantly. --- **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1, 3 Marks]` **State the units and physical significance of Young's Modulus for a polymeric material.** **Model Answer:** **Units:** Pascals (Pa) in SI; commonly expressed in **MPa** or **GPa** for polymers. Dimensional formula: $\text{M L}^{-1} \text{T}^{-2}$. **Physical Significance:** - Young's Modulus ($E$) is the slope of the initial linear (Hookean) region of the stress–strain curve: $E = \sigma / \varepsilon$. - It is a measure of the *intrinsic stiffness* of the polymer — a material with high $E$ resists elastic deformation strongly. - For polymers, $E$ depends on chain rigidity, crosslink density, crystallinity, and temperature; values range from $\sim$0.01 GPa (soft elastomers) to $\sim$5 GPa (rigid glassy polymers). - It is used in deflection, buckling, and vibration calculations for polymer components. --- ### Part B: Long-Answer Questions with Internal Choice (1 × 14 = 14 Marks) --- #### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1]` **A polymer specimen of original length $L_0 = 75$ mm and uniform circular cross-section of diameter $d_0 = 6$ mm is subjected to a uniaxial tensile test. The following data are recorded in the initial linear elastic region:** | Force $F$ (N) | Elongation $\Delta L$ (mm) | |---|---| | 0 | 0.000 | | 100 | 0.030 | | 200 | 0.060 | | 300 | 0.090 | | 400 | 0.120 | **(a)** Calculate Young's Modulus of the polymer. **(7 marks)** **(b)** If the specimen is stretched to a final length of $L = 165$ mm at the onset of yielding, calculate the **true strain** at that point and compare it with the **engineering strain**. Comment on which strain measure is more appropriate and why. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution (a):** **Step 1 — Compute the original cross-sectional area:** $$A_0 = \frac{\pi}{4} d_0^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (6 \times 10^{-3})^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 36 \times 10^{-6} = 2.827 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 2 — Compute stress for each data point using $\sigma = F / A_0$:** $$\sigma_1 = \frac{100}{2.827 \times 10^{-5}} = 3.54 \text{ MPa}$$ $$\sigma_2 = \frac{200}{2.827 \times 10^{-5}} = 7.07 \text{ MPa}$$ $$\sigma_3 = \frac{300}{2.827 \times 10^{-5}} = 10.61 \text{ MPa}$$ $$\sigma_4 = \frac{400}{2.827 \times 10^{-5}} = 14.14 \text{ MPa}$$ **Step 3 — Compute strain for each data point using $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L_0$ (with $L_0 = 75$ mm):** $$\varepsilon_1 = \frac{0.030}{75} = 4.0 \times 10^{-4}$$ $$\varepsilon_2 = \frac{0.060}{75} = 8.0 \times 10^{-4}$$ $$\varepsilon_3 = \frac{0.090}{75} = 12.0 \times 10^{-4}$$ $$\varepsilon_4 = \frac{0.120}{75} = 16.0 \times 10^{-4}$$ **Step 4 — Verify linearity** (each successive $\sigma$ is exactly double the previous, and each $\varepsilon$ is also exactly double — confirming Hookean behavior). **Step 5 — Compute $E$ from the slope of $\sigma$ vs $\varepsilon$:** $$E = \frac{\sigma_4 - \sigma_1}{\varepsilon_4 - \varepsilon_1} = \frac{(14.14 - 3.54) \times 10^{6}}{(16.0 - 4.0) \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{10.60 \times 10^{6}}{12.0 \times 10^{-4}}$$ $$E = 8.83 \times 10^{9} \text{ Pa} \approx 8.83 \text{ GPa}$$ **Answer (a):** $\boxed{E \approx 8.83 \text{ GPa}}$ This value is consistent with a stiff engineering polymer such as polycarbonate (PC) or polyamide (nylon 6,6). --- **Model Solution (b):** **Step 1 — Compute engineering strain at $L = 165$ mm:** $$\varepsilon_{eng} = \frac{L - L_0}{L_0} = \frac{165 - 75}{75} = \frac{90}{75} = 1.20 \text{ (or 120\%)}$$ **Step 2 — Compute true strain at $L = 165$ mm:** $$\varepsilon_{true} = \ln\left(\frac{L}{L_0}\right) = \ln\left(\frac{165}{75}\right) = \ln(2.20) \approx 0.7885$$ **Step 3 — Comparison:** | Measure | Value | |---|---| | Engineering Strain | 1.200 | | True Strain | 0.7885 | | Difference | 0.4115 (≈ 34.3% of $\varepsilon_{eng}$) | **Step 4 — Comment on which is more appropriate:** For this polymer, the engineering strain of 120% represents a *large deformation*, far exceeding the small-strain limit (≤ 5%) where $\varepsilon_{eng} \approx \varepsilon_{true}$. The two measures differ by over 34%. Therefore, the **true strain** is the more appropriate measure for the following reasons: 1. It provides a *physically consistent* and *additive* measure of large deformation. 2. It correctly accounts for the *continuous reduction* in cross-sectional area and continuous change in length during stretching. 3. It is the *conjugate variable* to true stress in plastic work calculations: $dW = \sigma_{true} \, d\varepsilon_{true}$. 4. Constitutive models for polymers (e.g., Arruda–Boyce, Mooney–Rivlin, Ogden) are formulated in terms of true strain. **Answer (b):** $\boxed{\varepsilon_{eng} = 1.20; \quad \varepsilon_{true} \approx 0.7885; \quad \text{True strain is more appropriate.}}$ --- #### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1, Internal Choice to Q.A]` **(a)** Derive the expression for **true strain** starting from the definition of engineering strain. Show that for small strains, true strain reduces to engineering strain. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A low-density polyethylene (LDPE) film has an initial gauge length of $L_0 = 25$ mm. During a tensile test it is stretched to $L_1 = 75$ mm. Calculate the engineering and true strains. The film is then further stretched uniformly to $L_2 = 100$ mm in a second stage. Show that the true strain is *additive* across the two stages, while the engineering strain is **not**. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution (a):** **Step 1 — Definition of engineering strain:** For a specimen of original length $L_0$ stretched to length $L$: $$\varepsilon_{eng} = \frac{L - L_0}{L_0}$$ **Step 2 — Express $L$ in terms of $L_0$ and $\varepsilon_{eng}$:** $$L = L_0(1 + \varepsilon_{eng})$$ **Step 3 — Incremental approach to true strain:** Consider a small additional stretch from length $L$ to $L + dL$. The incremental engineering strain over this infinitesimal step is: $$d\varepsilon_{inc} = \frac{(L + dL) - L}{L} = \frac{dL}{L}$$ **Step 4 — Total true strain is the integral of all such incremental strains from $L_0$ to $L$:** $$\varepsilon_{true} = \int_{L_0}^{L} \frac{dL}{L}$$ **Step 5 — Evaluate the integral:** $$\varepsilon_{true} = \Big[\ln(L)\Big]_{L_0}^{L} = \ln(L) - \ln(L_0) = \ln\left(\frac{L}{L_0}\right)$$ **Step 6 — Substitute $L = L_0(1 + \varepsilon_{eng})$:** $$\varepsilon_{true} = \ln\left(\frac{L_0(1 + \varepsilon_{eng})}{L_0}\right) = \ln(1 + \varepsilon_{eng})$$ **Step 7 — Small-strain limit using Taylor series:** For $\varepsilon_{eng} \ll 1$, expand $\ln(1 + x) = x - \dfrac{x^2}{2} + \dfrac{x^3}{3} - \cdots$: $$\varepsilon_{true} = \varepsilon_{eng} - \frac{\varepsilon_{eng}^2}{2} + \frac{\varepsilon_{eng}^3}{3} - \cdots$$ Neglecting higher-order terms: $$\varepsilon_{true} \approx \varepsilon_{eng} \quad \text{(for } \varepsilon_{eng} \ll 1\text{)}$$ **Conclusion:** For small strains, true strain and engineering strain are approximately equal; for large deformations (typical in polymers), they diverge. --- **Model Solution (b):** **Stage 1: $L_0 = 25$ mm to $L_1 = 75$ mm** Engineering strain: $$\varepsilon_{eng,1} = \frac{L_1 - L_0}{L_0} = \frac{75 - 25}{25} = 2.0$$ True strain: $$\varepsilon_{true,1} = \ln\left(\frac{L_1}{L_0}\right) = \ln\left(\frac{75}{25}\right) = \ln(3) \approx 1.0986$$ --- **Stage 2: $L_1 = 75$ mm to $L_2 = 100$ mm** Engineering strain in stage 2 (measured from $L_0$, not $L_1$): $$\varepsilon_{eng,2} = \frac{L_2 - L_0}{L_0} = \frac{100 - 25}{25} = 3.0$$ True strain in stage 2: $$\varepsilon_{true,2} = \ln\left(\frac{L_2}{L_0}\right) = \ln\left(\frac{100}{25}\right) = \ln(4) \approx 1.3863$$ --- **Increment of true strain from $L_1$ to $L_2$:** $$\Delta\varepsilon_{true} = \ln\left(\frac{L_2}{L_1}\right) = \ln\left(\frac{100}{75}\right) = \ln(1.333) \approx 0.2877$$ --- **Additivity Check:** True strain total: $\varepsilon_{true,1} + \Delta\varepsilon_{true} = 1.0986 + 0.2877 = 1.3863 = \varepsilon_{true,2}$ ✓ **True strain is additive.** Engineering strain total: $\varepsilon_{eng,1} + (\text{increment}) = 2.0 + 1.0 = 3.0 = \varepsilon_{eng,2}$ ✓ Wait — the engineering strain *is* additive in this calculation because it is measured from the *original* $L_0$ in both cases. The crucial point is that engineering strain computed incrementally from $L_1$ gives $0.0$ of stage 2 increment, but the true strain increment of $0.2877$ correctly captures the deformation of stage 2. **Reformulation — Incremental comparison (the real demonstration of non-additivity for engineering strain):** Engineering strain computed *cumulatively* from $L_0$: $\varepsilon_{eng,1} = 2.0$ at $L_1$. The additional engineering strain from $L_1$ to $L_2$, if measured as $\Delta\varepsilon_{eng} = (L_2 - L_1)/L_1 = (100-75)/75 = 0.333$, gives a *different* base-relative value compared to $\Delta\varepsilon_{eng} = (L_2 - L_1)/L_0 = 1.0$. Engineering strain is **not** a state variable — it depends on the choice of reference length.
