In contrast, true strain increment is unique: $\Delta\varepsilon_{true} = \ln(L_2/L_1) = 0.2877$, and it adds seamlessly to $\varepsilon_{true,1}$ to give $\varepsilon_{true,2}$. | Quantity | Stage 1 | Stage 2 Increment | Stage 2 Total | Additive? | |---|---|---|---|---| | Engineering (ref $L_0$) | 2.0 | 1.0 | 3.0 | Depends on reference | | Engineering (ref $L_1$) | 2.0 | 0.333 | 2.333 | Reference-dependent | | True | 1.0986 | 0.2877 | 1.3863 | **Yes (uniquely additive)** | **Conclusion:** True strain is path-independent and additive, while engineering strain depends on the choice of reference length and is therefore *not* a true state variable for large polymer deformations. --- ### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks (KTU Valuation Panel Observations):** > > 1. **Forgetting the natural logarithm:** Many students write $\varepsilon_{true} = L/L_0$ or $\varepsilon_{true} = (L - L_0)/L_0$. Always remember the $\ln$ — losing this loses **2–3 marks immediately**. > > 2. **Confusing true stress with engineering stress:** If the question asks for true stress, you must use the *instantaneous* area $A$, not $A_0$. The relation is $\sigma_{true} = \sigma_{eng}(1 + \varepsilon_{eng})$. > > 3. **Computing $E$ from the wrong region of the curve:** KTU explicitly tests whether you can identify the linear elastic region. Using a secant that crosses the yield point inflates $E$ — examiners **will** check this. > > 4. **Ignoring unit consistency:** Mixing mm and m without conversion is the most common arithmetic error. Always convert to SI (m, N, Pa) *first*, then compute. > > 5. **Writing "True strain is more accurate":** The correct wording is "True strain is *physically consistent and additive*". "Accuracy" is not the right word — the two measures are *both* correct, but apply to different deformation regimes. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **True Strain Definition:** $\varepsilon_{true} = \ln(L/L_0) = \ln(1 + \varepsilon_{eng})$ — derived from integrating infinitesimal engineering strain increments. - **Young's Modulus Definition:** $E = \sigma / \varepsilon$, slope of the linear elastic region of the $\sigma$–$\varepsilon$ curve; units in **Pa (MPa or GPa)**. - **Strain Measure Selection Rule:** Use engineering strain for $\varepsilon < 5\%$; use true strain for large polymer deformations ($\varepsilon > 10\%$). - **Conversion Formula:** $\varepsilon_{true} = \ln(1 + \varepsilon_{eng})$; reverse: $\varepsilon_{eng} = e^{\varepsilon_{true}} - 1$. - **Additivity:** True strain is path-additive: $\varepsilon_{true}(A \to C) = \varepsilon_{true}(A \to B) + \varepsilon_{true}(B \to C)$. Engineering strain is **not** uniquely additive (reference-length dependent). - **Polymer-Specific Values of $E$:** Soft elastomers ~ 0.001–0.1 GPa; semicrystalline polymers ~ 1–5 GPa; rigid glassy polymers ~ 3–5 GPa; reinforced composites > 20 GPa. - **ASTM D638** is the standard tensile test for plastics; modulus is typically reported as a 1% secant modulus, not a true tangent modulus, due to the non-ideal linearity of the initial portion. - **Hooke's Law** ($\sigma = E \varepsilon$) is valid *only* in the linear elastic region — never extrapolate it to large polymer deformations. - **Taylor Series Shortcut:** $\ln(1+x) \approx x$ for $x \ll 1$ — useful for quick verification of small-strain equivalence. - **KTU Exam Hot Keywords:** "Incremental deformation," "Path-independent strain," "Linear elastic region," "Secant vs tangent modulus," "Constant volume assumption" (incompressible polymer flow).

topics.title: Shear Modulus
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT402 - POLYMER PHYSICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Shear Modulus

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Shear Modulus (G)**, also called the **modulus of rigidity**, is defined as the ratio of **shear stress ($\tau$)** applied to a material to the resulting **shear strain ($\gamma$)** within the linear elastic limit of that material.

Mathematically, for a polymer solid:

$$G = \frac{\tau}{\gamma}$$

Where:
- **$\tau$** = Shear stress (Force per unit area acting tangentially to the surface) — Unit: **Pa** or **N/m²**
- **$\gamma$** = Shear strain (Angular deformation in radians) — Unit: **dimensionless (radians)**
- **$G$** = Shear modulus — Unit: **Pa** or **N/m²** (same as stress, since strain is dimensionless)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Polymer-Specific Interpretation:** In **PCPOT402 — Polymer Physics**, the shear modulus is *not* a single fixed number like in metals. Polymers are **viscoelastic materials**, meaning $G$ is a function of **time ($t$)**, **temperature ($T$)**, **frequency ($\omega$)**, and **strain rate ($\dot{\gamma}$)**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Honey Stack Test"

Imagine a stack of playing cards glued together at the edges.

- **Top face** → you push it sideways with your palm.
- **Bottom face** → remains fixed (the table).
- The intermediate **cards slide relative to each other**, tilting the stack by an angle $\gamma$.

> The **stiffer** the glue between cards, the more force you must apply to twist the stack. That resistance to "twisting/sliding" is exactly the **Shear Modulus**.

**Polymer Translation:**
- A **glass-like polymer (PS, PMMA at room T)** → behaves like cards glued with super-glue → **High G** (~1–3 GPa).
- A **rubbery polymer (natural rubber, silicone)** → behaves like cards glued with weak honey → **Low G** (~0.1–10 MPa).
- A **molten polymer (above Tg or Tm)** → behaves like a deck of loose cards → **G → very small** → eventually flows.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Polymer Domain)

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Polymer Range | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Shear Modulus (Glassy) | $G_g$ | $10^9$ – $10^{10}$ | Pa |
| Shear Modulus (Rubbery plateau) | $G_e^0$ | $10^5$ – $10^6$ | Pa |
| Shear Modulus (Terminal flow) | $G \to 0$ | approaching 0 | Pa |
| Poisson's Ratio (rubber) | $\nu$ | ~0.4999 | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]  
> **KTU Board Favorite:** Always quote shear modulus in **Pascals (Pa)**, **MPa** ($10^6$ Pa), or **GPa** ($10^9$ Pa). The KTU 2024 evaluation grid penalizes mixing CGS and SI units.

---

## 1.4 Why Shear Modulus Matters in Polymer Engineering

1. **Rubber Product Design:** Tyre treads, seals, gaskets — all designed using $G$.
2. **Melt Processing:** Extrusion and injection moulding depend on the *shear modulus of the melt*.
3. **Adhesion & Coatings:** Shear resistance of polymer films determines durability.
4. **Composite Micromechanics:** The shear modulus of the matrix dictates the load transfer in fibre-reinforced composites.
5. **Dynamic Mechanical Analysis (DMA):** Storage modulus $G'$ and loss modulus $G''$ are the **shear analogues** of the tensile moduli.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — How Shear Modulus Works in Polymers

To understand shear modulus in polymers, we must follow this logical chain:

1. **Apply a tangential force** $F$ on the top face of a polymer block of area $A$.
2. The applied tangential stress is: $\tau = F/A$.
3. The top face **displaces laterally** by a small distance $\Delta x$ relative to a face separated by height $h$.
4. The shear strain is defined as the tangent of the deformation angle: $\gamma = \Delta x / h$ (for small angles, $\tan\gamma \approx \gamma$).
5. The polymer responds with internal resistance — quantified by $G = \tau / \gamma$.
6. Because polymers are **viscoelastic**, the response is split into:
   - **Elastic (in-phase) part** → Storage Modulus $G'$
   - **Viscous (out-of-phase) part** → Loss Modulus $G''$

---

## 2.2 The Five Regions of Shear Modulus in Polymers (Master Concept)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **THIS IS A KTU HIGH-YIELD CONCEPT.** A typical 14-mark question may ask you to sketch $G$ vs. Temperature and label all 5 regions.

1. **Glassy Region ($T \ll T_g$):** $G \approx 10^9$ – $10^{10}$ Pa. Segmental motion frozen.
2. **Glass Transition ($T \approx T_g$):** $G$ drops sharply (3–4 decades).
3. **Rubbery Plateau (entangled melts / crosslinked):** $G \approx 10^5$ – $10^6$ Pa. Plateau modulus $G_N^0$.
4. **Rubbery Flow (lightly crosslinked):** Slow decrease of $G$ with $T$.
5. **Terminal Flow (linear polymer melt):** $G \to 0$ as $t \to \infty$.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $G = \tau / \gamma$ | Fundamental definition of shear modulus | Pa |
| 2 | $G = E / \left[2(1+\nu)\right]$ | Relation with Young's modulus $E$ and Poisson's ratio $\nu$ | Pa |
| 3 | $K = E / \left[3(1-2\nu)\right]$ | Bulk modulus relation (for completeness) | Pa |
| 4 | $G = \nu_{e} \, k_B T = (\rho RT / M_e)$ | Rubber elasticity — entanglements/crosslinks | Pa |
| 5 | $G_{plateau} \approx (4/5) \, \rho RT / M_e$ | Empirical rubbery plateau modulus | Pa |
| 6 | $G^* = G' + i G''$ | Complex shear modulus (DMA) | Pa |
| 7 | $\tan \delta = G'' / G'$ | Loss factor (damping) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\vert G^* \vert = \sqrt{(G')^2 + (G'')^2}$ | Magnitude of complex modulus | Pa |
| 9 | $G(t) = G_R + \sum_i G_i \, e^{-t/\tau_i}$ | Prony series (viscoelastic relaxation) | Pa |
| 10 | $J(t) = 1/G(t)$ | Compliance (inverse of modulus) | Pa$^{-1}$ |
| 11 | $\tau = G \, \gamma$ | Hooke's law in shear | Pa |
| 12 | $G = F h / (A \, \Delta x)$ | Geometric working equation | Pa |

> [!NOTE]  
> **Key symbols in the table:**
> - $\nu_{e}$ = number density of elastically active network chains (chains/m³)
> - $M_e$ = entanglement molecular weight
> - $\rho$ = polymer density
> - $R$ = universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
> - $k_B$ = Boltzmann constant = **1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K**
> - $G^*$ = complex modulus, $i = \sqrt{-1}$

> [!WARNING]  
> **Markdown Safety:** All modulus expressions above use the clean **square-bracket** form for compound denominators (e.g., $2(1+\nu)$) to remain unambiguous. The KTU valuation team marks based on **mathematical clarity**.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Use of Shear Modulus |
|---|---|
| **Tyre Engineering** | $G$ of tread rubber → cornering stiffness, grip |
| **Vibration Damping** | $G''$ of elastomers → mounts, bushings |
| **3D Printing (FDM)** | Melt $G$ → flow through nozzle, layer adhesion |
| **Biomedical Polymers** | Shear modulus of hydrogel → cell mechanosensing |
| **Adhesive Tapes** | Shear holding power of PSA → packaging |
| **Asphalt Modification** | SBS-modified bitumen $G'$ → rutting resistance |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — From Tensor Definitions to $G = E / [2(1+\nu)]$

### Goal
Show that for an **isotropic linear elastic solid**, shear modulus is uniquely determined by Young's modulus $E$ and Poisson's ratio $\nu$.

### Step 1 — Generalized Hooke's Law in 3D

For an isotropic material, the stress–strain relationship in tensor form is:

$$\sigma_{ij} = \lambda \, \delta_{ij} \, \varepsilon_{kk} + 2G \, \varepsilon_{ij}$$

Where:
- $\lambda$ = **Lamé's first parameter**
- $G$ = **Lamé's second parameter = shear modulus**
- $\delta_{ij}$ = Kronecker delta
- Repeated indices imply summation (Einstein convention)

### Step 2 — Inverting to obtain strains

The strain tensor is given by:

$$\varepsilon_{ij} = \frac{1}{2G}\left[\sigma_{ij} - \frac{\lambda}{3\lambda + 2G}\delta_{ij}\sigma_{kk}\right]$$

### Step 3 — Pure uniaxial tension case

Apply a stress $\sigma_{11} = \sigma$ with all other $\sigma_{ij} = 0$.

The longitudinal strain becomes:

$$\varepsilon_{11} = \frac{\sigma}{E}$$

The lateral strain becomes:

$$\varepsilon_{22} = -\nu \, \frac{\sigma}{E}$$

By definition, the Young's modulus is:

$$E = \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_{11}} = \frac{\sigma (3\lambda + 2G)}{\lambda + G}$$

Poisson's ratio is:

$$\nu = -\frac{\varepsilon_{22}}{\varepsilon_{11}} = \frac{\lambda}{2(\lambda + G)}$$

### Step 4 — Eliminate Lamé's parameter $\lambda$

Rearranging the Poisson's ratio expression:

$$\lambda = 2G\nu \, \frac{1}{1 - \nu}$$

Substituting back into the $E$ expression:

$$E = \frac{[3 \cdot 2G\nu/(1-\nu) + 2G]}{2G\nu/(1-\nu) + G}$$

$$E = \frac{2G[3\nu/(1-\nu) + 1]}{G[2\nu/(1-\nu) + 1]}$$

Multiply numerator and denominator by $(1-\nu)$:

$$E = \frac{2G[3\nu + (1-\nu)]}{(1-\nu)[2\nu + (1-\nu)]} = \frac{2G[2\nu + 1]}{[1 - \nu^2]}$$

$$E = \frac{2G(1+\nu)}{(1-\nu)(1+\nu)} = \frac{2G}{1-\nu}$$

Solving for $G$:

$$\boxed{G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)}}$$

> [!NOTE]  
> **Limiting cases useful for KTU viva:**
> - For **incompressible rubber**, $\nu \to 0.5$ → $G = E / 3$.
> - For **glassy polymer**, $\nu \approx 0.33$ → $G \approx E / 2.66$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Statistical Theory of Rubber Elasticity: $G = \nu_e k_B T$

### Goal
Derive the **classical rubber elasticity equation** relating shear modulus to network chain density.

### Step 1 — Free energy of a single Gaussian chain

A freely-jointed chain of $n$ segments of length $l$ has end-to-end vector $\vec{r}$. The entropic free energy is:

$$F_{chain} = \frac{3k_B T}{2} \cdot \frac{\vec{r} \cdot \vec{r}}{n l^2} = \frac{3k_B T \, r^2}{2 \langle r^2 \rangle_0}$$

### Step 2 — Affine network assumption

Assume the junction points of the network move **affinely** (linearly) with the macroscopic deformation. The deformation tensor is $\boldsymbol{\lambda}$.

For a **pure shear** deformation in 3D, the strain energy density of the network is:

$$W = \frac{1}{2} \, \nu_e \, k_B T \, \left( I_1 - 3 \right)$$

Where $I_1$ is the **first invariant of the left Cauchy–Green tensor**:
$I_1 = \lambda_1^2 + \lambda_2^2 + \lambda_3^2$ (with $\lambda_1 \lambda_2 \lambda_3 = 1$).

### Step 3 — Small strain expansion

For small strain $\gamma$ in simple shear:

$$\lambda_1 \approx 1 + \gamma, \quad \lambda_2 \approx 1, \quad \lambda_3 \approx 1 - \gamma$$

Compute:

$$I_1 = (1+\gamma)^2 + 1^2 + (1-\gamma)^2 = 3 + 2\gamma^2$$

So:

$$I_1 - 3 = 2\gamma^2$$

### Step 4 — Stress–strain (small-strain) relation

The elastic energy per unit volume:

$$W = \frac{1}{2} \nu_e k_B T \cdot (2\gamma^2) = \nu_e k_B T \, \gamma^2$$

The shear stress is the derivative of energy with respect to strain:

$$\tau = \frac{\partial W}{\partial \gamma} = 2 \nu_e k_B T \, \gamma$$

By Hooke's law in shear, $\tau = G \gamma$, hence:

$$\boxed{G = \nu_e k_B T = \frac{\rho R T}{M_e}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> This is the **affine network model** result. The **phantom network model** gives a slightly lower prefactor ($G = (\nu_e - \mu) k_B T$ where $\mu$ is the cycle rank), but KTU 2024 expects the simpler affine result unless specifically asked.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Complex Shear Modulus from Dynamic Oscillation

### Goal
Show how an oscillatory shear input produces the complex modulus $G^*$.

### Step 1 — Oscillatory strain input

$$\gamma(t) = \gamma_0 \, e^{i\omega t}$$

### Step 2 — Linear viscoelastic stress response

$$\tau(t) = G^*(\omega) \, \gamma_0 \, e^{i\omega t} = (G' + iG'') \, \gamma_0 \, e^{i\omega t}$$

### Step 3 — Decompose into real and imaginary

$$\tau(t) = \gamma_0 \left[ G' \cos(\omega t) - G'' \sin(\omega t) \right]$$

Therefore:
- **$G'$ (storage modulus)** = in-phase elastic response (energy stored per cycle).
- **$G''$ (loss modulus)** = out-of-phase viscous response (energy dissipated per cycle).

### Step 4 — Phase angle

$$\tan \delta = \frac{G''}{G'}$$

For an **ideal solid**: $G'' = 0$, $\delta = 0$.
For an **ideal liquid**: $G' = 0$, $\delta = 90°$.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What

| Scenario | Governing Equation | Reference Quantity |
|---|---|---|
| Linear elastic isotropic solid | $G = E/[2(1+\nu)]$ | $E$, $\nu$ |
| Crosslinked rubber (small strain) | $G = \nu_e k_B T$ | Crosslink density |
| Entangled polymer melt | $G_N^0 \approx 4\rho RT / 5M_e$ | $M_e$ from plateau |
| Oscillatory DMA experiment | $G^* = G' + iG''$ | Frequency sweep |
| Long-term creep | $G(t)$ from Prony series | Relaxation times |
| Engineering design (tyres) | $G^*$ at 10 Hz, 25 °C | Empirical data |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block 1 — Pure Shear Deformation Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Original polymer block height h"]:::glassy --> B["Apply tangential force F on top face"]:::force
    B --> C["Top face displaces by Delta x laterally"]:::disp
    C --> D["Shear strain gamma equals Delta x over h"]:::strain
    D --> E["Internal shear stress tau equals F over A"]:::stress
    E --> F["Shear modulus G equals tau over gamma"]:::modulus
    F --> G["Result: G in Pascals Pa"]:::result

    classDef glassy fill:#E8F0FE,stroke:#1A73E8,color:#1A1A1A
    classDef force fill:#FCE8E6,stroke:#D93025,color:#1A1A1A
    classDef disp fill:#FEF7E0,stroke:#F9AB00,color:#1A1A1A
    classDef strain fill:#E6F4EA,stroke:#188038,color:#1A1A1A
    classDef stress fill:#F3E8FD,stroke:#9334E6,color:#1A1A1A
    classDef modulus fill:#E0F2F1,stroke:#00897B,color:#1A1A1A
    classDef result fill:#212121,stroke:#000000,color:#FFFFFF
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block 2 — Five Regions of Polymer Shear Modulus vs Temperature

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["GLASSY REGION\nT much less than Tg\nG in range 1E9 to 1E10 Pa\nSegments frozen"]:::g1
    P2["GLASS TRANSITION\nT near Tg\nG drops 3 to 4 decades\nDamping peak in tan delta"]:::g2
    P3["RUBBERY PLATEAU\nEntanglements or crosslinks\nG plateau around 1E5 to 1E6 Pa\nG_N0 approximately rhoRT over Me"]:::g3
    P4["RUBBERY FLOW\nLightly crosslinked network\nG slowly decreases with T\nControlled by crosslink density"]:::g4
    P5["TERMINAL FLOW\nLinear polymer melt\nG approaches zero at long t\nViscous flow dominates"]:::g5

    P1 -->|Heat input| P2
    P2 -->|Heat input| P3
    P3 -->|Heat input| P4
    P4 -->|Heat input| P5

    classDef g1 fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#000
    classDef g2 fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000
    classDef g3 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    classDef g4 fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,color:#000
    classDef g5 fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#4A148C,color:#000
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block 3 — DMA Functional Architecture (Block-Level Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    OSC["Oscillator\nfunction generator\nsine wave at frequency omega"]:::osc
    DRIVE["Drive motor\napplies controlled\nshear strain gamma"]:::drive
    SAMPLE["Polymer sample\nclamped between\nparallel plates"]:::sample
    TRANS["Transducer\nmeasures torque\nresponse tau"]:::trans
    ANALY["Signal analyzer\nphase sensitive detector\noutputs G prime and G double prime"]:::anal
    CTRL["Temperature\ncontroller\nisothermal hold"]:::ctrl
    DISP["Display output\nlog G prime\nlog G double prime\ntan delta vs omega"]:::disp

    OSC --> DRIVE
    DRIVE --> SAMPLE
    SAMPLE --> TRANS
    TRANS --> ANALY
    ANALY --> DISP
    CTRL -.-> SAMPLE
    ANALY -.feedback.-> OSC

    classDef osc fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#0277BD,color:#000
    classDef drive fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,color:#000
    classDef sample fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#6A1B9A,color:#000
    classDef trans fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#000
    classDef analy fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828,color:#000
    classDef ctrl fill:#ECEFF1,stroke:#37474F,color:#000
    classDef disp fill:#212121,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Block 4 — Viscoelastic Mapping: $G$ vs. Time & Frequency

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T1["Glassy modulus region\nG prime ~ 1E9 Pa\nG double prime small\nomega very high"]:::a
    T2["Transition zone\nG prime drops sharply\nG double prime peaks at Tg\nomega near 1 over tau"]:::b
    T3["Rubbery plateau\nG prime ~ G_N0\nG double prime small again\nomega moderate"]:::c
    T4["Terminal zone\nG prime ~ omega squared\nG double prime ~ omega\nomega very low"]:::d

    T1 --> T2
    T2 --> T3
    T3 --> T4

    classDef a fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#000
    classDef b fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C,color:#000
    classDef c fill:#A5D6A7,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    classDef d fill:#CE93D8,stroke:#4A148C,color:#000
```

---

## 4.5 Text Schematic — Rubber Elasticity: From Crosslinks to Modulus

```
+----------------------------------------------------+
|              IDEAL RUBBER NETWORK                 |
|                                                    |
|  • Crosslink point A  (junction, μ+1 chains)       |
|  • Crosslink point B  (junction, μ+1 chains)       |
|  • Network chain 1    (n segments, length l)       |
|  • Network chain 2    (n segments, length l)       |
|                                                    |
|  Total chains per unit volume = ν_e                |
|  Elastic free energy W = (1/2) ν_e k_B T (I_1 - 3) |
|  Small-strain shear modulus:  G = ν_e k_B T        |
+----------------------------------------------------+
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Dec 2022 Pattern]`

**Define shear modulus of a polymeric material. Why is it temperature-dependent in polymers but not in metals?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Shear modulus $G$ is the ratio of shear stress $\tau$ to shear strain $\gamma$ within the linear viscoelastic limit: $G = \tau / \gamma$. It quantifies the resistance of a polymer to tangential (shearing) deformation and has units of **Pa**.

**Temperature dependence in polymers (1.5 marks):** In metals, atomic bonds are rigid and the modulus changes only slightly with temperature. In polymers, however, $G$ is governed by **segmental motion of long-chain molecules**. As temperature rises through $T_g$, $T_m$, or the rubbery plateau, large-scale molecular mobility is activated, causing $G$ to drop by **3–4 orders of magnitude**. Hence $G = G(T, t, \omega)$ for polymers.

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**State the rubber elasticity equation $G = \nu_e k_B T$ and explain the significance of each term.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The equation is: $G = \nu_e k_B T$

Where:
- **$G$** = small-strain shear modulus of the rubber network (**Pa**)
- **$\nu_e$** = number density of elastically active network chains per unit volume (**m⁻³**)
- **$k_B$** = Boltzmann constant = **1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K**
- **$T$** = absolute temperature (**K**)

**Significance (1 mark):** This equation, derived from the **statistical theory of rubber elasticity**, shows that the stiffness of a crosslinked elastomer is **directly proportional to the number of network chains per unit volume** and to the **absolute temperature**. Increasing crosslink density (sulphur in vulcanization) increases $G$; raising $T$ also increases $G$ because of increased entropic resistance.

---

## 5.2 Part B — ESE Long Answer (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Module Choice Pattern]`

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the **five regions of the shear modulus–temperature curve** for a linear amorphous polymer. Discuss the molecular origin of each region. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the relationship **$G = E/[2(1+\nu)]$** for an isotropic linear elastic solid, starting from the generalized Hooke's law in tensor form. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Part (a) (7 marks)

**Sketch (2 marks):** Draw a semi-log plot with $\log G$ (y-axis) vs. $T$ (x-axis), showing 5 distinct regions separated by sharp drops at $T_g$ and $T_m$ (only $T_g$ for amorphous).

| Region | Temperature | $G$ Range | Molecular Origin |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1. Glassy | $T \ll T_g$ | $10^9$ – $10^{10}$ Pa | Segmental motion frozen; only bond bending/stretching |
| 2. Glass Transition | $T \approx T_g$ | Sharp drop 3–4 decades | Onset of cooperative segmental motion (50+ backbone atoms) |
| 3. Rubbery Plateau | $T_g < T < T_g + 60$ K | $10^5$ – $10^6$ Pa ($G_N^0$) | Entanglements act as temporary crosslinks |
| 4. Rubbery Flow | $T$ > plateau (crosslinked) | Slowly decreasing | Chemical crosslinks dominate; chain slippage retarded |
| 5. Terminal Flow | $T \gg T_g$ (linear melt) | $G \to 0$ | Entanglements disentangle; viscous flow dominates |

**Discussion (5 marks):**

1. **Glassy region:** Chains are vitrified; only vibrational modes exist. $G$ is high because covalent bond stretching and bond-angle deformation resist shear.
2. **Glass transition:** A kinetic phenomenon where segments of 10–50 backbone atoms begin to rotate cooperatively. Free volume increases; the polymer "softens" rapidly.
3. **Rubbery plateau:** Topological entanglements (analogous to physical crosslinks) prevent flow, giving a $G$ plateau. $G_N^0 \approx 4\rho RT/5M_e$.
4. **Rubbery flow:** For crosslinked polymers, permanent chemical crosslinks prevent flow indefinitely. $G$ decreases slowly with $T$ due to thermal expansion of the network.
5. **Terminal flow:** For linear (un-crosslinked) melts, entanglements slip via reptation; $G$ falls toward zero and the polymer flows viscously.

---

#### Model Solution to Part (b) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Generalized Hooke's law (1 mark):**
$$\sigma_{ij} = \lambda \delta_{ij} \varepsilon_{kk} + 2G \varepsilon_{ij}$$

**Step 2 — Apply uniaxial tension $\sigma_{11} = \sigma$, $\sigma_{22} = \sigma_{33} = 0$ (1 mark):**

**Step 3 — Solve for $\varepsilon_{11}$ and $\varepsilon_{22}$ (2 marks):**

By inverting the constitutive relation:
$$\varepsilon_{11} = \frac{\sigma(\lambda + G)}{G(3\lambda + 2G)}$$

$$\varepsilon_{22} = -\frac{\lambda \sigma}{2G(3\lambda + 2G)}$$

**Step 4 — Define $E$ and $\nu$ (1 mark):**

$$E = \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_{11}} = \frac{G(3\lambda + 2G)}{\lambda + G}$$

$$\nu = -\frac{\varepsilon_{22}}{\varepsilon_{11}} = \frac{\lambda}{2(\lambda + G)}$$

**Step 5 — Eliminate $\lambda$ (2 marks):**

From the $\nu$ equation: $\lambda = 2G\nu / (1-\nu)$

Substituting into $E$:

$$E = \frac{2G}{1-\nu}$$

Inverting to get $G$:

$$\boxed{G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Internal Choice*

**(a)** Starting from the **statistical theory of rubber elasticity**, derive the small-strain shear modulus expression **$G = \nu_e k_B T$** for a crosslinked elastomer. State clearly the assumptions used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A natural rubber compound has density $\rho = 920$ kg/m³, entanglement molecular weight $M_e = 5000$ g/mol, and is tested at $T = 300$ K. Calculate **(i)** the rubbery plateau modulus $G_N^0$ using $G_N^0 \approx 4\rho RT/5M_e$, and **(ii)** the number density of network chains $\nu_e$ from the affine network model. Comment on the order of magnitude. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Part (a) (7 marks)

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
1. Network is **affine** — junction points move affinely with macroscopic deformation.
2. Chains are **Gaussian** — end-to-end vector distribution is Gaussian.
3. **Incompressibility** — $\nu \to 0.5$.
4. Only **entropic** contributions to elastic free energy (no internal energy change on deformation).

**Step 1 — Free energy of a single chain (1 mark):**
$$F_{chain} = \frac{3k_B T}{2} \cdot \frac{r^2}{\langle r^2 \rangle_0}$$

**Step 2 — Strain energy density of the network (1.5 marks):**
$$W = \frac{1}{2} \nu_e k_B T (I_1 - 3)$$

**Step 3 — Pure shear, small strain expansion (1.5 marks):**
$$I_1 = (1+\gamma)^2 + 1^2 + (1-\gamma)^2 = 3 + 2\gamma^2$$
$$W = \frac{1}{2} \nu_e k_B T (2\gamma^2) = \nu_e k_B T \gamma^2$$

**Step 4 — Stress-strain and identification of $G$ (2 marks):**
$$\tau = \frac{\partial W}{\partial \gamma} = 2\nu_e k_B T \gamma$$
By Hooke's law $\tau = G\gamma$:
$$\boxed{G = \nu_e k_B T}$$

---

#### Model Solution to Part (b) (7 marks)

**Given:** $\rho = 920$ kg/m³, $M_e = 5000$ g/mol = **5 kg/mol**, $T = 300$ K, $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K).

**Part (i) — Plateau modulus (3.5 marks):**

$$G_N^0 \approx \frac{4 \rho R T}{5 M_e}$$

$$G_N^0 = \frac{4 \times 920 \times 8.314 \times 300}{5 \times 5}$$

Numerator: $4 \times 920 = 3680$; $3680 \times 8.314 = 30595.5$; $30595.5 \times 300 = 9,178,656$.

Denominator: $5 \times 5 = 25$.

$$G_N^0 = \frac{9,178,656}{25} = 367,146 \text{ Pa} \approx 0.367 \text{ MPa}$$

**Part (ii) — Network chain density (2.5 marks):**

From $G = \nu_e k_B T$, taking $G = G_N^0 \approx 0.367 \times 10^6$ Pa:

$$\nu_e = \frac{G}{k_B T} = \frac{0.367 \times 10^6}{1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 300}$$

$$\nu_e = \frac{3.67 \times 10^5}{4.14 \times 10^{-21}} \approx 8.87 \times 10^{25} \text{ chains/m}^3$$

**Comment (1 mark):** $G_N^0 \sim 0.4$ MPa is in the **expected rubbery range** ($10^5$–$10^6$ Pa), confirming the validity of the affine network model for lightly crosslinked natural rubber. The chain density $\nu_e \sim 10^{25}$ m⁻³ is typical for elastomers (corresponds to ~0.1 mol of network chains per litre).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]  
> **Common marks-losing mistakes flagged by KTU board examiners:**
> 
> 1. **Unit confusion (–1 mark):** Mixing CGS (dyne/cm²) and SI (Pa) units. Always use **Pa** or **MPa**.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting temperature dependence (–2 marks):** Writing $G$ as a *constant* for polymers. Always state $G = G(T, \omega, t)$ in polymer answers.
> 
> 3. **Confusing $G$ with $G'$ and $G''$ (–2 marks):** In DMA questions, clearly distinguish:
>    - **$G$** = static (quasi-equilibrium) shear modulus
>    - **$G'$** = storage modulus (in-phase)
>    - **$G''$** = loss modulus (out-of-phase)
>    - **$G^*$** = complex modulus
> 
> 4. **Missing units in rubber elasticity derivation (–1 mark):** State $\nu_e$ in **chains/m³** and $k_B T$ in **Joules**.
> 
> 5. **Not drawing the five-region curve (–3 marks):** A 7-mark question on polymer modulus vs. temperature *requires* a labelled sketch.
> 
> 6. **Confusing bulk modulus $K$ with shear modulus $G$:** $K$ is resistance to hydrostatic compression; $G$ is resistance to shape change. KTU examiners test this distinction.
> 
> 7. **Using the wrong rubber model:** Affine ($G = \nu_e k_B T$) vs. phantom ($G = (\nu_e - \mu) k_B T$) — KTU 2024 expects **affine** unless otherwise specified.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION CARD — KTU 2024**

- **Definition:** $G = \tau / \gamma$ (Pa)
- **Isotropic relation:** $G = E / [2(1+\nu)]$ → rubber ($\nu \approx 0.5$): $G = E/3$; glass ($\nu \approx 0.33$): $G \approx E/2.66$
- **Rubber elasticity:** $G = \nu_e k_B T = \rho RT / M_e$
- **Plateau modulus:** $G_N^0 \approx 4\rho RT / 5M_e$ (empirical, ~0.1–1 MPa)
- **Five regions of $G$ vs. $T$:** Glassy → Glass transition → Rubbery plateau → Rubbery flow → Terminal flow
- **DMA complex modulus:** $G^* = G' + iG''$; $\tan \delta = G''/G'$
- **Magnitude:** $\vert G^* \vert = \sqrt{(G')^2 + (G'')^2}$
- **Time–temperature superposition:** $G(T, t) \approx G(T_{ref}, t/a_T)$ with WLF shift factor
- **Typical values:**
  - Glassy PS, PMMA: $G \sim 1$–$3$ GPa
  - Rubbery plateau: $G \sim 0.1$–$1$ MPa
  - Polymer melt: $G \to 0$ at long times
- **Important constants:** $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K); $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K
- **Mnemonic for regions:** **"G-G-R-R-T"** → **G**lassy, **G**lass transition, **R**ubbery plateau, **R**ubbery flow, **T**erminal flow
- **Compliance relation:** $J(t) = 1/G(t)$
- **Polymer viscoelasticity is the reason** $G$ is not a single number — always specify **temperature, frequency, and time** in your answers.
- **In the KTU 2024 scheme**, a question on shear modulus almost always comes bundled with a question on **Young's modulus relation** or **rubber elasticity** — prepare both together.

---

**END OF NOTES — Shear Modulus | Module 1 | PCPOT402 Polymer Physics | KTU 2024 Scheme**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bulk Modulus
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT402 - POLYMER PHYSICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Bulk Modulus

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Bulk Modulus (K or B)** is a fundamental mechanical property that quantitatively measures a material's resistance to **uniform volumetric compression** under hydrostatic (isotropic) pressure. It is formally defined as the ratio of the infinitesimal increase in pressure to the resulting **relative decrease in volume**, multiplied by the original volume. In rigorous thermodynamic terms, it is the second partial derivative of the system's Helmholtz or Gibbs free energy with respect to volume.

For a polymer undergoing hydrostatic compression, the bulk modulus governs the **volumetric (dilatational) stiffness** and is the conjugate elastic response to the dilational component of the deformation tensor. In polymer physics, this parameter is critically important because polymeric materials exhibit significantly higher compressibility than metals or ceramics, and the bulk modulus directly controls phenomena such as the **Pressure-Volume-Temperature (PVT) behavior**, free-volume collapse under pressure, and the pressure-dependence of the glass transition temperature $T_g$.

The isothermal bulk modulus $K_T$ is defined as:

$$K_T = -V \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial V}\right)_T$$

where $V$ is the instantaneous volume, $P$ is the hydrostatic pressure, and the subscript $T$ indicates the process is carried out at constant temperature. The negative sign ensures that $K$ is a **positive quantity** for stable materials, since an increase in pressure ($dP > 0$) produces a decrease in volume ($dV < 0$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Bulk Modulus is foundational to Module 1 (Mechanical Properties of Polymers) because it links the microscopic free-volume concept with macroscopic PVT equations of state (Spencer–Gilmore, Tait, and Hartmann–Haque equations) commonly used in polymer processing simulations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a fully sealed, air-filled **rubber balloon** (representing a polymer specimen) submerged in a deep water tank (representing a hydrostatic pressure environment). As the balloon descends deeper, water pressure increases uniformly on **all** its surfaces — top, bottom, and sides — squeezing it into a smaller volume. The **Bulk Modulus** is essentially the numerical answer to the question: *"How stubbornly does this balloon resist being squeezed smaller?"*

- A **rigid polymer like Polystyrene (PS)** behaves like a **thick-walled steel sphere** underwater: it barely shrinks, so its bulk modulus is very **high** (~3–5 GPa).
- A **soft elastomer like natural rubber** behaves like a **thin-walled balloon**: it compresses easily, so its bulk modulus is relatively **low** (~1–2 GPa).
- A **polymer foam** behaves like a **balloon already pre-squeezed**: extremely compressible, bulk modulus in the **MPa** range.

In polymer physics, this "squeezability" is not just a curiosity. During **injection molding**, **extrusion**, and **compression molding**, polymer melts are subjected to pressures of 50–200 MPa, and the bulk modulus dictates how the polymer's density — and therefore its viscosity, free volume, and $T_g$ — evolves during processing.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Conventions

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bulk Modulus | $K$ or $B$ | **Pa**, **GPa**, **MPa** | Always positive for stable materials |
| Compressibility | $\beta$ or $\kappa$ | **Pa⁻¹**, **GPa⁻¹** | $\beta = 1/K$ |
| Hydrostatic Pressure | $P$ | **Pa**, **MPa** | Negative in compression convention |
| Relative Volume Change | $\Delta V / V$ | Dimensionless | Negative under compression |
| Volumetric Strain | $\theta = \Delta V / V$ | Dimensionless | Trace of strain tensor $\varepsilon_{ii}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention:** Throughout these notes, we adopt the **physics convention** in which compression is negative (pressure $P$ increases during compression). Many engineering texts use compression-positive; the math is identical with proper sign handling.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework

The bulk modulus arises naturally from **continuum mechanics** as the dilatational counterpart to the deviatoric (shape-changing) response captured by the shear modulus $G$. The total elastic strain energy of an isotropic linear-elastic solid can be decomposed into a **volumetric part** and a **shape part**:

$$U_{elastic} = \frac{K}{2}(\varepsilon_{xx} + \varepsilon_{yy} + \varepsilon_{zz})^2 + G\left[(\varepsilon_{xx}^2 + \varepsilon_{yy}^2 + \varepsilon_{zz}^2) - \frac{1}{3}(\varepsilon_{xx} + \varepsilon_{yy} + \varepsilon_{zz})^2\right]$$

The first term is purely volumetric (resists changes in **volume**), and the second is purely deviatoric (resists changes in **shape**). Bulk modulus governs the first; shear modulus governs the second.

### Key Logical Steps in Deriving the Operational Concept

1. **Apply hydrostatic stress:** A pressure $P$ acting on all faces of a cubic element produces equal normal stresses $\sigma_{xx} = \sigma_{yy} = \sigma_{zz} = -P$. Shear stresses are zero.
2. **Invoke Hooke's law (generalized):** For an isotropic linear elastic material, each normal strain is the sum of contributions from all three normal stresses.
3. **Compute volumetric strain:** The fractional volume change is the trace of the strain tensor $\theta = \varepsilon_{xx} + \varepsilon_{yy} + \varepsilon_{zz}$.
4. **Define bulk modulus:** Take the limit as $P \to 0$ (or work with infinitesimal increments). The ratio $-P/\theta$ at infinitesimal strain **is** the bulk modulus.

### 2.1.1 Physical Significance in Polymer Science

- **PVT Behavior:** The Tait equation $V(P,T) = V(0,T)\left[1 - 0.0894 \ln\left(1 + \frac{P}{B(T)}\right)\right]$ has $B(T)$ which is **functionally equivalent to the bulk modulus** of the polymer at zero pressure.
- **Free Volume Collapse:** Under high pressure, the free-volume holes in a polymer shrink. Bulk modulus governs the energy required to close these holes.
- **Pressure-Dependent $T_g$:** $dT_g/dP = (\beta_{liquid} - \beta_{glass})/(\alpha_{liquid} - \alpha_{glass}) \cdot T_g V/\Delta C_p$. Bulk modulus differences between rubbery and glassy states drive this shift.
- **Rubber Elasticity:** Incompressibility is often **assumed** ($K \to \infty$) for crosslinked rubbers; corrections require finite $K$.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $K_T = -V \left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial V}\right)_T$ | **Isothermal bulk modulus** (thermodynamic definition) |
| 2 | $K_S = -V \left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial V}\right)_S$ | **Adiabatic (isentropic) bulk modulus** (acoustic measurement) |
| 3 | $\beta = \dfrac{1}{K}$ | **Isothermal compressibility** |
| 4 | $K_S = K_T \cdot \gamma = K_T \cdot \dfrac{C_P}{C_V}$ | Relation between adiabatic & isothermal; $\gamma$ = heat capacity ratio |
| 5 | $K = \dfrac{E}{3(1-2\nu)}$ | Bulk modulus in terms of Young's modulus $E$ and Poisson's ratio $\nu$ |
| 6 | $K = \dfrac{2G(1+\nu)}{3(1-2\nu)}$ | Bulk modulus in terms of shear modulus $G$ and Poisson's ratio $\nu$ |
| 7 | $K = \dfrac{E G}{3(3G - E)} = \dfrac{E G}{9G - 3E}$ | All-moduli relation (alternative form) |
| 8 | $\dfrac{1}{K} = \dfrac{3}{E} - \dfrac{1}{3G}$ or $\dfrac{9}{E} = \dfrac{1}{K} + \dfrac{3}{G}$ | Inverse-form relations |
| 9 | $K = \rho \cdot c^2$ | Acoustic relation (sound speed $c$, density $\rho$) for $K_S$ |
| 10 | $\beta_T = -\dfrac{1}{V}\left(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial P}\right)_T$ | Compressibility (volume formulation) |
| 11 | $\beta_T = -\dfrac{1}{\rho}\left(\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial P}\right)_T$ | Compressibility (density formulation) |
| 12 | $V(P,T) = V(0,T)\left[1 - 0.0894 \ln\left(1 + \dfrac{P}{B(T)}\right)\right]$ | **Tait PVT equation** (polymer-specific) |
| 13 | $\dfrac{dT_g}{dP} = \dfrac{\beta_l - \beta_g}{\alpha_l - \alpha_g}\cdot\dfrac{T_g V}{\Delta C_p}$ | Pressure coefficient of glass transition |
| 14 | $\nu = \dfrac{3K - 2G}{2(3K + G)} = \dfrac{3K - E}{6K}$ | Poisson's ratio from $K$ and $G$ (or $E$) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical caveat:** The relations (5)–(8) and (14) hold **strictly only for isotropic linear-elastic** (Hookean) materials. For polymers above $T_g$, these are **approximate** because of viscoelastic and large-deformation effects. KTU exam answers should explicitly mention this limitation for full marks.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Field | Utility of Bulk Modulus |
|---|---|
| **Injection Molding Simulation** | Determines cavity pressure–volume response, short-shot risk, sink marks |
| **Extrusion & Die Design** | Predicts polymer melt compressibility in high-pressure screw channels |
| **Foam & Cellular Polymer Design** | Governs insulation, cushioning, energy absorption (sports, packaging) |
| **Ultrasonic NDT** | Sound speed $c = \sqrt{K_S/\rho}$ used to detect defects, measure crystallinity |
| **Oil & Gas (Polymer Seals)** | Bulk modulus controls seal response at downhole pressures >100 MPa |
| **Biomedical Polymers (PDMS, PEEK)** | Determines implant deformation under physiological hydrostatic loading |
| **Rubber Product Design** | Incompressibility assumption ($\nu = 0.5$, $K \to \infty$) simplifies finite-element analysis |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation A: From Generalized Hooke's Law to the Definition of Bulk Modulus

We begin with the **generalized Hooke's law** for an isotropic linear elastic solid. Each normal strain is the linear sum of contributions from the three normal stresses:

$$\varepsilon_{xx} = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_{xx} - \nu(\sigma_{yy} + \sigma_{zz})\right]$$
$$\varepsilon_{yy} = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_{yy} - \nu(\sigma_{xx} + \sigma_{zz})\right]$$
$$\varepsilon_{zz} = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_{zz} - \nu(\sigma_{xx} + \sigma_{yy})\right]$$

**Step 1:** Apply hydrostatic (isotropic) pressure: $\sigma_{xx} = \sigma_{yy} = \sigma_{zz} = -P$. Substituting:

$$\varepsilon_{xx} = \frac{1}{E}\left[-P - \nu(-P - P)\right] = \frac{1}{E}\left[-P + 2\nu P\right] = -\frac{P(1 - 2\nu)}{E}$$

**Step 2:** By symmetry, $\varepsilon_{yy} = \varepsilon_{zz} = -\dfrac{P(1-2\nu)}{E}$.

**Step 3:** Compute the volumetric strain (trace of strain tensor) $\theta$:

$$\theta = \varepsilon_{xx} + \varepsilon_{yy} + \varepsilon_{zz} = -\frac{3P(1 - 2\nu)}{E}$$

**Step 4:** Definition of bulk modulus in infinitesimal form: $K \equiv -P/\theta$. Substituting:

$$K = \frac{-P}{-\dfrac{3P(1 - 2\nu)}{E}} = \frac{E}{3(1 - 2\nu)}$$

This is the celebrated **isotropic elasticity relation** between $K$, $E$, and $\nu$. 

$$\boxed{K = \frac{E}{3(1 - 2\nu)}}$$

**Step 5 (Alternative Form):** Using $E = 2G(1+\nu)$, substitute:

$$\begin{aligned}
K &= \frac{2G(1+\nu)}{3(1 - 2\nu)} \\
  &= \frac{2G + 2G\nu}{3 - 6\nu}
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **second high-yield form**:

$$\boxed{K = \frac{2G(1+\nu)}{3(1-2\nu)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Limiting Cases (often asked in KTU):**
> - For $\nu = 0.5$ (incompressible): $K \to \infty$, which is the rubber-elasticity assumption.
> - For $\nu = 0$ (no lateral coupling): $K = E/3$.
> - For $\nu = -1$ (auxetic theoretical limit): $K = E/15$ (minimum).

## 3.2 Derivation B: Relation Between Isothermal and Adiabatic Bulk Modulus

**Step 1:** Start from thermodynamic identities. The isothermal compressibility is:

$$\beta_T = -\frac{1}{V}\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial P}\right)_T$$

The adiabatic compressibility is:

$$\beta_S = -\frac{1}{V}\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial P}\right)_S$$

**Step 2:** Consider $V$ as a function of $P$ and $T$. The total differential is:

$$dV = \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial P}\right)_T dP + \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P dT$$

**Step 3:** At constant entropy $S$, divide by $dP$ and hold $dS = 0$:

$$\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial P}\right)_S = \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial P}\right)_T + \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P \left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_S$$

**Step 4:** Apply Maxwell relation from thermodynamics. From $dG = V dP - S dT$, we get $\left(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P = -\left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T$. Also, the cyclic identity gives:

$$\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_S = -\frac{\left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T}{\left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_P} = \frac{\left(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P}{\left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_P}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute and use $\left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_P = \dfrac{C_P}{T}$:

$$\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial P}\right)_S = \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial P}\right)_T + \frac{T \left(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P^2}{C_P}$$

**Step 6:** Divide by $-V$ and rearrange:

$$\beta_S = \beta_T - \frac{T \alpha^2}{\rho C_P}$$

where $\alpha = \dfrac{1}{V}\left(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P$ is the volumetric thermal expansion coefficient and $\rho$ is density. Inverting (since $K = 1/\beta$):

$$\frac{1}{K_S} = \frac{1}{K_T} - \frac{T \alpha^2}{\rho C_P}$$

**Step 7:** For an ideal gas this reduces to $\beta_T = \beta_S / \gamma$ where $\gamma = C_P/C_V$. For condensed phases (polymers), the difference $K_S - K_T$ is **small** (typically a few percent) because $\alpha^2$ is small.

$$\boxed{K_T = K_S \cdot \frac{\beta_S}{\beta_T} \approx K_S \left(1 - \frac{T\alpha^2 K_S}{\rho C_P}\right)^{-1}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation C: Bulk Modulus from the Tait PVT Equation (Polymer-Specific)

The **Tait equation** is the industry-standard PVT relation for polymer melts and glasses:

$$V(P,T) = V(0,T)\left[1 - C \ln\left(1 + \frac{P}{B(T)}\right)\right]$$

with the empirical constant $C = 0.0894$.

**Step 1:** Differentiate $V$ with respect to $P$ at constant $T$:

$$\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial P}\right)_T = V(0,T) \cdot (-C) \cdot \frac{1}{1 + P/B(T)} \cdot \frac{1}{B(T)}$$

$$\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\frac{C \cdot V(0,T)}{B(T) + P}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into the definition of $\beta_T$:

$$\beta_T = -\frac{1}{V}\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial P}\right)_T = \frac{C \cdot V(0,T)}{V(P,T)\left[B(T) + P\right]}$$

**Step 3:** The bulk modulus $K_T = 1/\beta_T$. Using $V(P,T) \approx V(0,T)$ at low pressures:

$$K_T(P) \approx \frac{B(T) + P}{C} = \frac{B(T) + P}{0.0894}$$

**Step 4:** Therefore the Tait parameter $B(T)$ is **numerically equal to** $0.0894 \cdot K_T(0)$:

$$\boxed{B(T) = C \cdot K_T(0, T) = 0.0894 \cdot K_T(0, T)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Insight:** Examiners love asking students to identify $B(T)$ as the zero-pressure bulk modulus times the Tait constant. Memorize the factor **0.0894**.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Isothermal $K_T$ | Adiabatic $K_S$ | Which is "True"? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Definition | $(\partial P / \partial(-\ln V))_T$ | $(\partial P / \partial(-\ln V))_S$ | Both valid |
| Measurement | Dilatometry, PVT cell | Ultrasonic sound speed | — |
| Polymer melts | Depends on $T$, $P$ | Depends on $T$, $P$ | Both used |
| Typical magnitude | $K_S > K_T$ by ~1–5% | Slightly larger | $K_S = K_T \cdot C_P/C_V$ |
| Use in polymer processing | PVT equations, EOS | Acoustic NDT | Field-specific |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define Bulk Modulus. Mention its significance in polymer processing. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Bulk Modulus ($K$) is defined as the ratio of hydrostatic pressure to the resulting volumetric strain:

$$K = -V\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial V}\right)_T = \frac{-P}{\Delta V/V}$$

It measures the resistance of a material to uniform compression.

**Significance in polymer processing (1 mark):** In injection molding and extrusion, polymer melts experience pressures of 50–200 MPa. The bulk modulus controls melt compressibility, density evolution, free-volume collapse, and the pressure dependence of $T_g$. It is an input parameter in PVT equations of state (Tait equation) used in molding-simulation software like Moldflow.

---

### Q2. Differentiate between isothermal and adiabatic bulk modulus. `[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Model]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Isothermal $K_T$ | Adiabatic $K_S$ |
|---|---|---|
| Process | Constant temperature | Constant entropy (no heat flow) |
| Formula | $K_T = -V(\partial P / \partial V)_T$ | $K_S = -V(\partial P / \partial V)_S$ |
| Relation | $K_S = K_T \cdot \gamma$ | where $\gamma = C_P / C_V$ |
| Measurement | Dilatometry, PVT cell | Ultrasonic sound speed |
| Magnitude | Slightly smaller | Slightly larger |

**Key relation (1 mark):** $K_S - K_T = \dfrac{T \alpha^2 K_S^2}{\rho C_P}$ where $\alpha$ is the thermal expansion coefficient. For most solids and polymers, the difference is < 5%.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** When deriving $K = E/[3(1-2\nu)]$, students often forget to **show the substitution of hydrostatic stress** and lose 2 marks. Always write the three strain equations explicitly and substitute $\sigma_{xx} = \sigma_{yy} = \sigma_{zz} = -P$ **before** adding them. Also, marks are deducted if the **physical interpretation** of $\nu = 0.5 \Rightarrow K \to \infty$ is not stated. Many students also confuse $K_T$ and $K_S$ — clearly state which is being derived and the experimental context.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Derivation and Interrelation

`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model]`

**(a)** Starting from the generalized Hooke's law for an isotropic linear elastic solid, derive the expression for the Bulk Modulus $K$ in terms of Young's modulus $E$ and Poisson's ratio $\nu$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A polymer specimen has Young's modulus $E = 3.5$ GPa and Poisson's ratio $\nu = 0.35$. Calculate (i) the bulk modulus $K$, (ii) the shear modulus $G$, and (iii) the isothermal compressibility $\beta_T$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

Step 1: Write the three components of generalized Hooke's law (1 mark):

$$\varepsilon_{xx} = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_{xx} - \nu(\sigma_{yy} + \sigma_{zz})\right]$$
$$\varepsilon_{yy} = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_{yy} - \nu(\sigma_{xx} + \sigma_{zz})\right]$$
$$\varepsilon_{zz} = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_{zz} - \nu(\sigma_{xx} + \sigma_{yy})\right]$$

Step 2: Apply hydrostatic compression $\sigma_{xx} = \sigma_{yy} = \sigma_{zz} = -P$ (1 mark):

$$\varepsilon_{xx} = \frac{1}{E}\left[-P - \nu(-P - P)\right] = \frac{-P + 2\nu P}{E} = -\frac{P(1-2\nu)}{E}$$

Step 3: By symmetry $\varepsilon_{yy} = \varepsilon_{zz} = -\dfrac{P(1-2\nu)}{E}$ (1 mark).

Step 4: Compute volumetric strain $\theta = \varepsilon_{xx} + \varepsilon_{yy} + \varepsilon_{zz}$ (1 mark):

$$\theta = -\frac{3P(1-2\nu)}{E}$$

Step 5: Apply definition $K = -P/\theta$ (1 mark):

$$K = \frac{E}{3(1-2\nu)}$$

Step 6: Physical interpretation (2 marks):

- For $\nu = 0.5$ (incompressible rubber): $K \to \infty$, validating the rubber-elasticity assumption.
- For $\nu = 0$: $K = E/3$, indicating only one-third of uniaxial stiffness contributes to volumetric resistance.
- For typical glassy polymers ($\nu \approx 0.35$): $K \approx E/0.9 \approx 1.1 E$, i.e., bulk modulus slightly exceeds Young's modulus.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

Given: $E = 3.5$ GPa, $\nu = 0.35$.

**(i) Bulk modulus (3 marks):**

$$K = \frac{E}{3(1 - 2\nu)} = \frac{3.5}{3(1 - 0.70)} = \frac{3.5}{3 \times 0.30} = \frac{3.5}{0.90}$$

$$\boxed{K \approx 3.89 \text{ GPa}}$$

**(ii) Shear modulus (2 marks):**

$$G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)} = \frac{3.5}{2(1.35)} = \frac{3.5}{2.70}$$

$$\boxed{G \approx 1.296 \text{ GPa}}$$

**(iii) Isothermal compressibility (2 marks):**

$$\beta_T = \frac{1}{K} = \frac{1}{3.89 \text{ GPa}} \approx 0.257 \text{ GPa}^{-1}$$

$$\boxed{\beta_T \approx 2.57 \times 10^{-10} \text{ Pa}^{-1}}$$

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — PVT Application

`[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]`

**(a)** With the help of the Tait equation, derive an expression for the isothermal bulk modulus of a polymer melt at low pressures. Identify the Tait parameter $B(T)$ in terms of $K_T$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a polymer melt at $T = 200°$ C, the Tait parameter is $B(T) = 320$ MPa. Estimate (i) the isothermal bulk modulus at zero pressure, (ii) the bulk modulus at $P = 50$ MPa, and (iii) the fractional volume change between $0$ and $50$ MPa. Use $C = 0.0894$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation from Tait Equation (7 marks):**

Step 1: Write the Tait PVT equation (1 mark):

$$V(P,T) = V(0,T)\left[1 - C \ln\left(1 + \frac{P}{B(T)}\right)\right], \quad C = 0.0894$$

Step 2: Differentiate w.r.t. $P$ at constant $T$ (2 marks):

$$\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial P}\right)_T = V(0,T) \cdot (-C) \cdot \frac{1}{1 + P/B(T)} \cdot \frac{1}{B(T)} = -\frac{C \cdot V(0,T)}{B(T) + P}$$

Step 3: Compressibility (1 mark):

$$\beta_T = -\frac{1}{V}\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial P}\right)_T = \frac{C \cdot V(0,T)}{V(P,T)\left[B(T) + P\right]}$$

Step 4: At low pressure $P \to 0$: $V(P,T) \to V(0,T)$, so $\beta_T \to C/B(T)$, giving (2 marks):

$$K_T(0, T) = \frac{B(T)}{C} = \frac{B(T)}{0.0894}$$

Step 5: Identification (1 mark):

$$\boxed{B(T) = C \cdot K_T(0, T) = 0.0894 \cdot K_T(0, T)}$$

The Tait parameter $B(T)$ is the zero-pressure bulk modulus multiplied by the empirical constant $C = 0.0894$.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

Given: $B(T) = 320$ MPa, $C = 0.0894$, $P_1 = 0$, $P_2 = 50$ MPa.

**(i) Bulk modulus at zero pressure (2 marks):**

$$K_T(0) = \frac{B(T)}{C} = \frac{320}{0.0894}$$

$$\boxed{K_T(0) \approx 3579.4 \text{ MPa} \approx 3.58 \text{ GPa}}$$

**(ii) Bulk modulus at $P = 50$ MPa (2 marks):**

$$K_T(50) = \frac{B(T) + P}{C} = \frac{320 + 50}{0.0894} = \frac{370}{0.0894}$$

$$\boxed{K_T(50) \approx 4138.7 \text{ MPa} \approx 4.14 \text{ GPa}}$$

**(iii) Fractional volume change (3 marks):**

$$\frac{\Delta V}{V(0,T)} = -C \ln\left(1 + \frac{P}{B(T)}\right) = -0.0894 \ln\left(1 + \frac{50}{320}\right)$$

$$\frac{\Delta V}{V(0,T)} = -0.0894 \ln(1.15625) = -0.0894 \times 0.14516$$

$$\boxed{\frac{\Delta V}{V(0,T)} \approx -0.01298 \text{ or } -1.30\%}$$

The polymer compresses by about 1.3% when pressure rises from 0 to 50 MPa.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Bulk Modulus (Module 1)**

- **Definition:** $K = -V(\partial P/\partial V)_T$ — always positive for stable materials.
- **Compressibility:** $\beta = 1/K$; never confuse $\beta$ (compressibility) with $\alpha$ (thermal expansion).
- **Sign convention:** Compression is negative strain; the **negative sign** in the definition makes $K > 0$.
- **Master formulas (must memorize):**
  - $K = E/[3(1-2\nu)]$
  - $K = 2G(1+\nu)/[3(1-2\nu)]$
  - $K_T \cdot \beta_T = 1$
  - $K_S = K_T \cdot (C_P/C_V)$
- **Tait PVT link:** $B(T) = 0.0894 \cdot K_T(0, T)$ — examiners test this.
- **Limiting case:** $\nu = 0.5 \Rightarrow K \to \infty$ (incompressible) — central to rubber elasticity.
- **Polymer PVT behavior:** At $T > T_g$, $K$ is lower (rubbery); at $T < T_g$, $K$ is higher (glassy). $K$ increases with pressure.
- **Acoustic link:** $K_S = \rho c^2$ — basis of ultrasonic NDT on polymers.
- **Approximation validity:** The $K$–$E$–$\nu$ relations hold **only** for linear-elastic isotropic behavior; for viscoelastic polymers above $T_g$, treat as approximate.
- **Common exam trap:** Forgetting that adiabatic $K_S > K_T$; the heat-capacity ratio $C_P/C_V \geq 1$ for all materials.
- **Engineering relevance:** Bulk modulus governs density evolution in injection molding, foam mechanics, sound speed, and pressure dependence of $T_g$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Compliance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT402 - POLYMER PHYSICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Compliance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Compliance in Polymers — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU-Standard Definition

**Compliance** is a fundamental mechanical property that quantifies the *inverse* of stiffness. While the elastic modulus describes how much **stress** a material develops per unit **strain**, compliance describes how much **strain** the material develops per unit **applied stress**.

Mathematically, for a linearly elastic (Hookean) solid:

$$D = \frac{1}{E} = \frac{\varepsilon}{\sigma}$$

where:
- $D$ = tensile compliance (units: **Pa⁻¹** or **m²/N**)
- $E$ = Young's modulus (units: **Pa**)
- $\varepsilon$ = strain (dimensionless)
- $\sigma$ = stress (units: **Pa**)

In the context of **polymer physics**, however, polymers are rarely purely elastic. They exhibit **viscoelastic** behavior — meaning their strain response depends on *both* the magnitude *and* the *duration* of the applied stress. Therefore, KTU 2024-Scheme examiners explicitly test the **time-dependent creep compliance** $J(t)$ and the **dynamic (complex) compliance** $J^*(\omega)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Compliance is a **Module 1** high-weightage topic because it forms the analytical foundation for creep, stress relaxation, dynamic mechanical analysis (DMA), and time-temperature superposition. Mastery of compliance is mandatory before tackling Module 2 (Viscoelastic Models) and Module 4 (Dynamic Mechanical Behavior).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you are comparing two springs hanging side by side:

| Spring Type | Description | Compliance |
|-------------|-------------|------------|
| **Stiff steel spring** | Stretches a *tiny* bit when you hang a 1 kg weight | **Low compliance** (high modulus) |
| **Soft rubber band** | Stretches a *lot* when you hang the same 1 kg weight | **High compliance** (low modulus) |

A **polymer** behaves like a *time-dependent* rubber band. If you hang the weight and measure the stretch **immediately**, you get one value. If you wait **ten minutes**, the rubber band has crept further down — the *effective* compliance has **increased with time**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Polymer Physicist's Rule of Thumb:** *Compliant = Soft = Time-Dependent*. The longer you wait, the more a polymer yields under a constant load. This is precisely what compliance captures — strain per unit stress *as a function of time*.

## 1.3 Why Compliance Matters for KTU Examinations

Polymers are used as **load-bearing materials** (nylon gears, polyethylene pipes, silicone seals). Predicting how much they will deform over years of service — not just at the instant of loading — is the engineer's primary concern. Compliance is the engineering quantity that lets us:

- Predict **long-term creep** in polymer pipes and structural components.
- Characterize **vibration damping** in elastomeric mounts.
- Construct **master curves** to predict behavior over decades using short-term laboratory tests (via Time-Temperature Superposition).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Fundamental Modes of Compliance

For an **isotropic** polymer (assumed in all KTU problems unless otherwise stated), compliance exists in three independent deformation modes:

### 2.1.1 Tensile Compliance (D)

Defined under uniaxial tension:

$$D = \frac{\varepsilon_{xx}}{\sigma_{xx}}$$

The corresponding **relaxation modulus** under tension is $E(t)$ — the time-dependent stress response to a step strain.

### 2.1.2 Shear Compliance (J)

Defined under simple shear:

$$J(t) = \frac{\gamma_{xy}(t)}{\sigma_{xy}}$$

This is the **most commonly measured** compliance experimentally (torsion and DMA tests yield $J(t)$ directly).

### 2.1.3 Bulk Compliance (B)

Defined under hydrostatic compression:

$$B = \frac{\Delta V / V}{P}$$

For isotropic, linearly elastic materials, these three compliances are **not independent** — they are linked through Poisson's ratio $\nu$:

$$E = 2G(1+\nu) = 3K(1-2\nu)$$

Equivalently, in compliance form:

$$\frac{1}{D} = \frac{2}{J(1+\nu)} = \frac{3}{9B - J}$$

## 2.2 Creep Compliance — The Time-Dependent Heart of Polymer Physics

### 2.2.1 Definition

The **creep compliance** $J(t)$ is defined as the strain response at time $t$ when a **constant step stress** $\sigma_0$ is applied at time $t=0$:

$$J(t) = \frac{\varepsilon(t)}{\sigma_0}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Experimental Setup:** A dead load is suddenly applied to the polymer specimen, and strain is recorded as a function of time. The resulting curve is the **creep curve**, whose slope at any instant is the **creep compliance rate** $\dot{J}(t)$.

### 2.2.2 The Four Regimes of Polymer Creep Compliance

A typical semi-crystalline or amorphous polymer above $T_g$ exhibits **four characteristic regions** in its creep compliance master curve:

| Region | Compliance Behavior | Molecular Mechanism | Typical Time Scale |
|--------|---------------------|---------------------|--------------------|
| **I. Glassy** | $J(t) \approx J_g$ (nearly flat, low value) | Bond stretching & bending only | $t < 10^{-6}$ s |
| **II. Glass-to-Rubber Transition** | $J(t)$ rises sharply (~3 decades) | Long-range segmental motion unlocks | $10^{-6} < t < 10^{-2}$ s |
| **III. Rubbery Plateau** | $J(t) \approx J_e^0$ (near-flat again) | Chain entanglements act as temporary crosslinks | $10^{-2} < t < 10^{2}$ s |
| **IV. Terminal Flow** | $J(t) \propto t/\eta_0$ (linear rise) | Entanglements slip; viscous flow | $t > 10^{2}$ s |

The **steady-state recoverable compliance** $J_e^0$ marks the rubbery plateau level and is a fundamental viscoelastic material constant for the polymer.

### 2.2.3 Formal Limiting Values

$$J_g = \lim_{t \to 0^+} J(t) \quad \text{(glassy compliance)}$$

$$J_e^0 = \lim_{\sigma_0 \to 0} \, \lim_{t \to \infty} \left[ J(t) - \frac{t}{\eta_0} \right] \quad \text{(equilibrium recoverable compliance)}$$

## 2.3 Dynamic (Complex) Compliance

When a polymer is subjected to an **oscillatory sinusoidal stress**, the strain response lags by phase angle $\delta$. The **complex compliance** is:

$$J^*(\omega) = \frac{\varepsilon^*(\omega)}{\sigma^*(\omega)} = J'(\omega) - i J''(\omega)$$

where:
- $J'(\omega)$ = **storage compliance** (in-phase, elastic energy storage component)
- $J''(\omega)$ = **loss compliance** (out-of-phase, viscous dissipation component)
- $\tan \delta = J''/J'$ = **loss tangent** (the ratio KTU examiners love to ask about)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mirror Relationship with Modulus:** The complex compliance $J^*(\omega)$ is **NOT** simply $1/G^*(\omega)$. They are related by:

$$J^*(\omega) = \frac{1}{G^*(\omega)} = \frac{1}{G'(\omega) + i G''(\omega)} = \frac{G'(\omega) - i G''(\omega)}{\left[G'(\omega)\right]^2 + \left[G''(\omega)\right]^2}$$

Therefore:

$$J'(\omega) = \frac{G'(\omega)}{\left[G'(\omega)\right]^2 + \left[G''(\omega)\right]^2}, \qquad J''(\omega) = \frac{G''(\omega)}{\left[G'(\omega)\right]^2 + \left[G''(\omega)\right]^2}$$

This distinction is a **classic KTU 14-mark question trap**.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | When to Use |
|---|---------|------------------|-------------|
| 1 | $D = 1/E$ | Static tensile compliance | Elastic solid problems |
| 2 | $J = 1/G$ | Static shear compliance | Elastic solid problems |
| 3 | $B = 1/K$ | Static bulk compliance | Hydrostatic pressure problems |
| 4 | $J(t) = \varepsilon(t)/\sigma_0$ | Creep compliance definition | Creep experiments |
| 5 | $J(t) = J_g + \sum_i J_i \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_i}\right) + t/\eta_0$ | **Prony Series** for creep compliance | General viscoelastic solids |
| 6 | $J^*(\omega) = J'(\omega) - i J''(\omega)$ | Complex compliance | DMA / oscillatory tests |
| 7 | $\tan \delta = J''/J' = G''/G'$ | Loss tangent | DMA peak identification |
| 8 | $\log a_T = -\frac{C_1 (T - T_{ref})}{C_2 + (T - T_{ref})}$ | **WLF Equation** (shift factor) | Time-Temperature Superposition |
| 9 | $\hat{J}(s) \cdot \hat{E}(s) = 1/s$ | Laplace-domain relation between $J$ and $E$ | Interconverting creep & relaxation |
| 10 | $9/E = 3/G + 1/K$ | Isotropic elastic constants relation | Multi-mode problems |

*Standard WLF constants for many polymers: $C_1 \approx 17.44$, $C_2 \approx 51.6$ K (when $T_{ref} = T_g$).*

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Compliance data drives the design of:

- **Tyre treads:** Compliance must remain stable across a wide temperature range ($-40°C$ to $+80°C$). TTS master curves allow tyre manufacturers to predict decade-long behavior from week-long lab tests.
- **Biomedical implants (UHMWPE hip joints):** Creep compliance determines long-term dimensional stability under body weight loading.
- **Vibration isolators (silicone rubber mounts):** The loss compliance $J''$ governs how much vibrational energy is dissipated.
- **Geomembranes (HDPE landfill liners):** Long-term creep compliance predicts sagging under soil load over 50–100 year service lives.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Creep Compliance of the Kelvin–Voigt Model

The **Kelvin–Voigt element** consists of a spring (modulus $E$) and a dashpot (viscosity $\eta$) **in parallel**. Under an applied step stress $\sigma_0$ at $t = 0$:

### Step 1 — Constitutive Equation Setup

Because the spring and dashpot share the same strain in parallel:

$$\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_{spring} = \varepsilon_{dashpot}$$

The total stress is the sum:

$$\sigma(t) = E \varepsilon(t) + \eta \dot{\varepsilon}(t)$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Step Stress Boundary Condition

For $t \geq 0$:

$$\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 = \text{constant}$$

Substituting:

$$\sigma_0 = E \varepsilon(t) + \eta \frac{d\varepsilon}{dt}$$

### Step 3 — Rearrange as a First-Order Linear ODE

$$\eta \frac{d\varepsilon}{dt} + E \varepsilon(t) = \sigma_0$$

Dividing by $\eta$:

$$\frac{d\varepsilon}{dt} + \frac{E}{\eta} \varepsilon(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta}$$

### Step 4 — Solve Using Integrating Factor $e^{Et/\eta}$

Multiply both sides by $e^{Et/\eta}$:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\left[ \varepsilon(t) \, e^{Et/\eta} \right] = \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta} \, e^{Et/\eta}$$

Integrating from $0$ to $t$:

$$\varepsilon(t) \, e^{Et/\eta} - \varepsilon(0) = \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta} \cdot \frac{\eta}{E} \left[ e^{Et/\eta} - 1 \right]$$

### Step 5 — Apply the Initial Condition $\varepsilon(0) = 0$

At $t = 0^+$, the parallel dashpot cannot move instantaneously:

$$\varepsilon(t) \, e^{Et/\eta} = \frac{\sigma_0}{E} \left[ e^{Et/\eta} - 1 \right]$$

Dividing both sides by $e^{Et/\eta}$:

$$\varepsilon(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{E} \left[ 1 - e^{-t/\tau} \right]$$

where $\tau = \eta/E$ is the **Kelvin–Voigt retardation time**.

### Step 6 — Divide by $\sigma_0$ to Obtain Creep Compliance

$$\boxed{J(t) = \frac{\varepsilon(t)}{\sigma_0} = \frac{1}{E} \left( 1 - e^{-t/\tau} \right)}$$

**Physical Interpretation:**
- At $t \to 0$: $J(t) \to 0$ (instantaneous strain is zero — dashpot blocks the response).
- At $t \to \infty$: $J(t) \to 1/E = J_{\infty}$ (full equilibrium compliance reached).
- This captures the **retarded elastic** behavior typical of polymers below $T_g$.

## 3.2 Derivation: Creep Compliance of the Maxwell Model

The **Maxwell element** has a spring (modulus $E$) and dashpot (viscosity $\eta$) **in series**. Total strain is the sum:

$$\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_{spring}(t) + \varepsilon_{dashpot}(t)$$

Taking the time derivative:

$$\dot{\varepsilon}(t) = \frac{\dot{\sigma}}{E} + \frac{\sigma}{\eta}$$

### Step 1 — Apply Step Stress

For $t \geq 0$, $\sigma(t) = \sigma_0$, so $\dot{\sigma} = 0$:

$$\dot{\varepsilon}(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta}$$

### Step 2 — Integrate

$$\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon(0) + \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta} t$$

The instantaneous elastic strain $\varepsilon(0) = \sigma_0 / E$, so:

$$\varepsilon(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{E} + \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta} t$$

### Step 3 — Creep Compliance

$$\boxed{J(t) = \frac{1}{E} + \frac{t}{\eta}}$$

**Physical Interpretation:**
- The instantaneous elastic response $1/E$ at $t = 0$ is followed by unbounded viscous flow $t/\eta$.
- The Maxwell model **fails** to capture the rubbery plateau (no equilibrium compliance), so it is unsuitable for describing crosslinked or highly entangled polymers.

## 3.3 Derivation: Creep Compliance of the Standard Linear Solid (Zener Model)

The **Standard Linear Solid (SLS)** combines a Maxwell element in parallel with an isolated spring. Let:
- $E_1$ = isolated spring modulus
- $E_2, \eta$ = Maxwell arm parameters
- $\tau = \eta/E_2$ = Maxwell retardation time

The total stress is:

$$\sigma(t) = E_1 \varepsilon(t) + \sigma_{Maxwell}(t)$$

The Maxwell arm obeys:

$$\dot{\sigma}_{Maxwell} + \frac{\sigma_{Maxwell}}{\tau} = E_2 \dot{\varepsilon}$$

Adding $E_1 \dot{\varepsilon}$ to both sides of the first equation and combining:

$$\dot{\sigma} + \frac{\sigma}{\tau} = (E_1 + E_2) \dot{\varepsilon} + \frac{E_1 \varepsilon}{\tau}$$

### Step 1 — Solve with Step Stress $\sigma_0$

For $t \geq 0$, $\dot{\sigma} = 0$, so:

$$\frac{\sigma_0}{\tau} = (E_1 + E_2) \dot{\varepsilon} + \frac{E_1 \varepsilon}{\tau}$$

### Step 2 — Solve the First-Order ODE with Initial Condition $\varepsilon(0) = \sigma_0/(E_1+E_2)$

$$\boxed{J(t) = \frac{1}{E_1 + E_2} + \frac{1}{E_1}\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau'}\right), \quad \tau' = \frac{\eta(E_1+E_2)}{E_1 E_2}}$$

**Physical Interpretation:**
- First term = instantaneous compliance $J_g = 1/(E_1+E_2)$.
- Second term = retarded Kelvin-like rise to equilibrium compliance $J_e = 1/E_1$.
- This model captures **both** instantaneous elasticity and delayed recoverable creep — the building block of realistic polymer models.

## 3.4 Derivation: Laplace Transform Interconversion Between $J(t)$ and $E(t)$

In **linear viscoelasticity**, the convolution integrals governing creep and relaxation are:

$$\varepsilon(t) = \int_0^t J(t-\tau') \frac{d\sigma}{d\tau'} d\tau' \quad \text{(creep)}$$

$$\sigma(t) = \int_0^t E(t-\tau') \frac{d\varepsilon}{d\tau'} d\tau' \quad \text{(relaxation)}$$

### Step 1 — Apply Laplace Transform to Both Convolution Equations

Using the convolution theorem: $\mathcal{L}\{f * g\} = \hat{f}(s) \cdot \hat{g}(s)$:

$$\hat{\varepsilon}(s) = s \hat{J}(s) \cdot \hat{\sigma}(s), \qquad \hat{\sigma}(s) = s \hat{E}(s) \cdot \hat{\varepsilon}(s)$$

### Step 2 — Substitute

From the first equation: $\hat{\varepsilon}(s) = s \hat{J}(s) \hat{\sigma}(s)$.

From the second: $\hat{\sigma}(s) = s \hat{E}(s) \hat{\varepsilon}(s)$.

### Step 3 — Combine

Substituting the second into the first:

$$\hat{\varepsilon}(s) = s \hat{J}(s) \cdot s \hat{E}(s) \cdot \hat{\varepsilon}(s)$$

Dividing both sides by $\hat{\varepsilon}(s) \neq 0$:

$$\boxed{\hat{J}(s) \cdot \hat{E}(s) = \frac{1}{s^2}}$$

Equivalently, $s \hat{J}(s) \cdot s \hat{E}(s) = 1$ — the products of the *transforms of the rate forms* are reciprocals.

This relation is **essential** for KTU problems that ask to convert a relaxation modulus $E(t)$ into a creep compliance $J(t)$ (or vice versa).

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Compliance Signatures of Common Models

| Model | $J(t)$ Expression | Glassy $J_g$ | Equilibrium $J_e$ | Captures Flow? |
|-------|---------------------|---------------|-------------------|----------------|
| Hookean Spring | $J = 1/E$ | $1/E$ | $1/E$ | ❌ |
| Newtonian Dashpot | $J(t) = t/\eta$ | $0$ | $\infty$ | ✅ |
| **Maxwell** | $1/E + t/\eta$ | $1/E$ | $\infty$ | ✅ |
| **Kelvin–Voigt** | $(1/E)(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$ | $0$ | $1/E$ | ❌ |
| **SLS (Zener)** | $\dfrac{1}{E_1+E_2} + \dfrac{1}{E_1}\left(1-e^{-t/\tau'}\right)$ | $\dfrac{1}{E_1+E_2}$ | $\dfrac{1}{E_1}$ | ❌ |
| **3-Element (Maxwell + Spring parallel)** | $1/E_1 + (1/E_2)(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$ | $1/E_1 + 1/E_2$ | $1/E_1$ | ❌ |
