# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]`

> **Define creep compliance. What is the significance of the steady-state recoverable compliance $J_e^0$ in polymer characterization?**

**Model Answer:**

Creep compliance $J(t)$ is defined as the time-dependent strain response of a material per unit constant step stress applied at $t = 0$:

$$J(t) = \frac{\varepsilon(t)}{\sigma_0}$$

For a polymer, $J(t)$ characterizes how the material deforms continuously under sustained loading.

The **steady-state recoverable compliance** $J_e^0$ is the limiting value of $J(t)$ once all the recoverable viscoelastic deformation has occurred but **before** terminal viscous flow becomes significant. It is defined as:

$$J_e^0 = \lim_{\sigma_0 \to 0} \left[ J(t) - \frac{t}{\eta_0} \right]_{t \to \infty}$$

**Significance:**
1. It represents the **elastic recovery capacity** of the polymer's entanglement network.
2. It is a **molecular weight-independent** property above the entanglement molecular weight $M_e$.
3. It is a fundamental **engineering design parameter** for predicting long-term dimensional recovery in seals, gaskets, and elastomeric mounts.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

> **Distinguish between storage compliance $J'(\omega)$ and loss compliance $J''(\omega)$. Why is $J^*(\omega) \neq 1/G^*(\omega)$ in general?**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | $J'(\omega)$ | $J''(\omega)$ |
|----------|---------------|---------------|
| **Name** | Storage compliance | Loss compliance |
| **Component** | In-phase with stress | 90° out-of-phase with stress |
| **Physical meaning** | Elastic strain energy stored per cycle | Viscous energy dissipated per cycle |
| **Dominant regime** | Glassy or rubbery plateau | Transition region (peak at $T_g$) |

For the second part: $J^*(\omega)$ and $G^*(\omega)$ are *defined* as reciprocals, so by definition $J^*(\omega) = 1/G^*(\omega)$. However, the **decomposition into real and imaginary parts is not reciprocal element-wise** because complex division does not distribute:

$$J' = \frac{G'}{(G')^2 + (G'')^2} \neq \frac{1}{G'}$$

The modulus of $J^*$ is $1/\vert G^* \vert$, but the individual components $J'$ and $G'$ are not reciprocal of each other.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024 Scheme]`

> **(a)** Starting from the constitutive equation of the Standard Linear Solid (SLS) model, derive the expression for creep compliance $J(t)$ under a step stress input. Clearly define the retardation time and identify the glassy and equilibrium compliance terms. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A polymer specimen is subjected to a step shear stress $\sigma_0 = 1 \times 10^5$ Pa at $t = 0$. The creep strain response is $\varepsilon(t) = 2 \times 10^{-9} \left(1 - e^{-t/50}\right) + 1 \times 10^{-11} t$, where $t$ is in seconds. Calculate:
> - (i) the creep compliance $J(t)$ at $t = 100$ s,
> - (ii) the steady-state recoverable compliance $J_e^0$,
> - (iii) the zero-shear viscosity $\eta_0$.
>
> **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a) — Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the SLS model.** The Standard Linear Solid consists of a Maxwell arm (spring $E_2$ in series with dashpot $\eta$) placed in **parallel** with an isolated spring $E_1$.

**Step 2 — Write the stress balance.** Total stress splits between the two parallel branches:

$$\sigma(t) = E_1 \varepsilon(t) + \sigma_M(t)$$

where $\sigma_M(t)$ is the stress in the Maxwell arm.

**Step 3 — Maxwell arm constitutive equation.** For the Maxwell arm:

$$\dot{\varepsilon}(t) = \frac{\dot{\sigma}_M}{E_2} + \frac{\sigma_M}{\eta}$$

**Step 4 — Combine.** Differentiate the stress balance:

$$\dot{\sigma} = E_1 \dot{\varepsilon} + \dot{\sigma}_M$$

From Step 3: $\dot{\sigma}_M = E_2 \dot{\varepsilon} - (E_2/\eta) \sigma_M$. Substituting:

$$\dot{\sigma} = (E_1 + E_2) \dot{\varepsilon} - \frac{E_2}{\eta} \sigma_M$$

Eliminate $\sigma_M$ using the stress balance $\sigma_M = \sigma - E_1 \varepsilon$:

$$\dot{\sigma} + \frac{E_2 \sigma}{\eta} = (E_1 + E_2) \dot{\varepsilon} + \frac{E_1 E_2 \varepsilon}{\eta}$$

**Step 5 — Apply step stress.** For $t \geq 0$, $\sigma = \sigma_0$, so $\dot{\sigma} = 0$:

$$\frac{E_2 \sigma_0}{\eta} = (E_1 + E_2) \dot{\varepsilon} + \frac{E_1 E_2 \varepsilon}{\eta}$$

**Step 6 — Solve the ODE.** With initial condition $\varepsilon(0^+) = \sigma_0 / (E_1 + E_2)$:

$$J(t) = \frac{\varepsilon(t)}{\sigma_0} = \frac{1}{E_1 + E_2} + \frac{1}{E_1} \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau'}\right)$$

with retardation time:

$$\tau' = \frac{\eta(E_1 + E_2)}{E_1 E_2}$$

**Identification:**
- Glassy compliance: $J_g = 1/(E_1 + E_2)$ (instantaneous response).
- Equilibrium compliance: $J_e = 1/E_1$ (long-time limit).

---

#### Model Solution to Part (b) — Numerical

**Step 1 — Extract model parameters from the given strain:**

Given: $\varepsilon(t) = 2 \times 10^{-9} (1 - e^{-t/50}) + 1 \times 10^{-11} t$

Divide by $\sigma_0 = 1 \times 10^5$ Pa:

$$J(t) = 2 \times 10^{-14} (1 - e^{-t/50}) + 1 \times 10^{-16} t \quad \text{Pa}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate $J(100)$:**

$$J(100) = 2 \times 10^{-14} (1 - e^{-2}) + 1 \times 10^{-16} \times 100$$

$$J(100) = 2 \times 10^{-14} (1 - 0.1353) + 1 \times 10^{-14}$$

$$J(100) = 2 \times 10^{-14} (0.8647) + 1 \times 10^{-14} = 1.729 \times 10^{-14} + 1 \times 10^{-14}$$

$$\boxed{J(100) = 2.729 \times 10^{-14} \text{ Pa}^{-1}}$$

**Step 3 — Steady-state recoverable compliance $J_e^0$:**

Subtract the viscous term $t/\eta_0$ and take the long-time limit:

$$J_e^0 = \lim_{t \to \infty} \left[ 2 \times 10^{-14} (1 - e^{-t/50}) \right] = 2 \times 10^{-14} \text{ Pa}^{-1}$$

$$\boxed{J_e^0 = 2 \times 10^{-14} \text{ Pa}^{-1}}$$

**Step 4 — Zero-shear viscosity $\eta_0$:**

The viscous flow term contributes $t/\eta_0 = 1 \times 10^{-16} t$, so:

$$\frac{1}{\eta_0} = 1 \times 10^{-16} \text{ Pa}^{-1}\text{s}^{-1}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_0 = 1 \times 10^{16} \text{ Pa}\cdot\text{s}}$$

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024 Scheme — Alternative]`

> **(a)** Define complex compliance $J^*(\omega)$. Derive the relationship between complex compliance $J^*(\omega)$ and complex modulus $G^*(\omega)$ for a viscoelastic polymer. Show that $\tan \delta = J''/J' = G''/G'$. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A polymer is subjected to oscillatory shear at angular frequency $\omega = 10$ rad/s. The measured complex modulus components are $G'(\omega) = 3 \times 10^6$ Pa and $G''(\omega) = 1 \times 10^6$ Pa. Calculate:
> - (i) the magnitude of the complex compliance $\vert J^* \vert$,
> - (ii) the storage compliance $J'(\omega)$,
> - (iii) the loss compliance $J''(\omega)$,
> - (iv) the loss tangent $\tan \delta$ and the phase angle $\delta$ in degrees.
>
> **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

**Step 1 — Definition of complex compliance.** When an oscillatory stress $\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 e^{i\omega t}$ is applied, the strain response of a linear viscoelastic material is $\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_0 e^{i(\omega t - \delta)}$, where $\delta$ is the phase lag. The complex compliance is:

$$J^*(\omega) = \frac{\varepsilon^*(\omega)}{\sigma^*(\omega)} = J'(\omega) - i J''(\omega)$$

where:
- $J'(\omega)$ = storage compliance (in-phase)
- $J''(\omega)$ = loss compliance (out-of-phase)

**Step 2 — Relationship to complex modulus.** The complex modulus is defined analogously:

$$G^*(\omega) = \frac{\sigma^*(\omega)}{\varepsilon^*(\omega)} = G'(\omega) + i G''(\omega)$$

By construction, $J^* = 1/G^*$, so:

$$J^*(\omega) = \frac{1}{G'(\omega) + i G''(\omega)}$$

**Step 3 — Rationalize.** Multiply numerator and denominator by the conjugate $G' - i G''$:

$$J^*(\omega) = \frac{G' - i G''}{(G')^2 + (G'')^2} = \frac{G'}{(G')^2 + (G'')^2} - i \frac{G''}{(G')^2 + (G'')^2}$$

Therefore:

$$J'(\omega) = \frac{G'(\omega)}{(G')^2 + (G'')^2}, \qquad J''(\omega) = \frac{G''(\omega)}{(G')^2 + (G'')^2}$$

**Step 4 — Loss tangent identity.**

$$\tan \delta = \frac{J''}{J'} = \frac{G''/(G'^2 + G''^2)}{G'/(G'^2 + G''^2)} = \frac{G''}{G'}$$

$$\boxed{\tan \delta = \frac{J''}{J'} = \frac{G''}{G'}}$$

---

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

Given: $G' = 3 \times 10^6$ Pa, $G'' = 1 \times 10^6$ Pa, $\omega = 10$ rad/s.

**Step 1 — Magnitude of complex compliance.**

$$\vert G^* \vert = \sqrt{(G')^2 + (G'')^2} = \sqrt{(3 \times 10^6)^2 + (1 \times 10^6)^2}$$

$$\vert G^* \vert = \sqrt{9 \times 10^{12} + 1 \times 10^{12}} = \sqrt{10^{13}} = \sqrt{10} \times 10^6$$

$$\vert G^* \vert \approx 3.162 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$$

$$\boxed{\vert J^* \vert = \frac{1}{\vert G^* \vert} = 3.162 \times 10^{-7} \text{ Pa}^{-1}}$$

**Step 2 — Storage compliance $J'$.**

$$J' = \frac{G'}{(G')^2 + (G'')^2} = \frac{3 \times 10^6}{10^{13}}$$

$$\boxed{J' = 3 \times 10^{-7} \text{ Pa}^{-1}}$$

**Step 3 — Loss compliance $J''$.**

$$J'' = \frac{G''}{(G')^2 + (G'')^2} = \frac{1 \times 10^6}{10^{13}}$$

$$\boxed{J'' = 1 \times 10^{-7} \text{ Pa}^{-1}}$$

**Step 4 — Loss tangent and phase angle.**

$$\tan \delta = \frac{J''}{J'} = \frac{1 \times 10^{-7}}{3 \times 10^{-7}} = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.333$$

$$\delta = \arctan(0.333) \approx 18.43°$$

$$\boxed{\tan \delta \approx 0.333, \qquad \delta \approx 18.43°}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Writing $J = 1/G'$ directly** (3-mark deduction): Students forget that complex reciprocation redistributes between real and imaginary parts. Always rationalize.
> 2. **Forgetting units** (1-mark deduction): Compliance is in Pa⁻¹, not Pa. Examiners explicitly deduct for wrong units.
> 3. **Confusing $J_e^0$ with $J_g$** (2-mark deduction): $J_e^0$ is the *rubbery plateau* compliance, NOT the glassy one.
> 4. **Missing the $t/\eta_0$ subtraction** in $J_e^0$ definition (2-mark deduction): Always subtract the viscous flow contribution before taking $t \to \infty$.
> 5. **Prony series mistakes**: When writing the general Prony series $J(t) = J_g + \sum_i J_i (1 - e^{-t/\tau_i}) + t/\eta_0$, ensure the retardation times $\tau_i$ are listed in ascending order (shortest first).

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!NOTE]
> **Save this for the night before the exam.**

- [ ] **Compliance** = strain per unit stress; the reciprocal of stiffness/modulus.
- [ ] **Static compliance** $D = 1/E$ (tensile), $J = 1/G$ (shear), $B = 1/K$ (bulk).
- [ ] **Isotropic relation:** $9/E = 3/G + 1/K$ — must be memorised.
- [ ] **Creep compliance:** $J(t) = \varepsilon(t)/\sigma_0$ — measured under constant step stress.
- [ ] **Four regimes of $J(t)$ master curve:** Glassy → Glass-rubber transition → Rubbery plateau → Terminal flow.
- [ ] **Prony series for $J(t)$:** $J(t) = J_g + \sum_i J_i (1 - e^{-t/\tau_i}) + t/\eta_0$.
- [ ] **Complex compliance:** $J^*(\omega) = J'(\omega) - i J''(\omega)$.
- [ ] **Loss tangent identity:** $\tan \delta = J''/J' = G''/G'$ — *not* the inverse of modulus components.
- [ ] **WLF shift factor:** $\log a_T = -C_1(T - T_{ref}) / [C_2 + (T - T_{ref})]$; with $C_1 = 17.44$, $C_2 = 51.6$ K at $T_{ref} = T_g$.
- [ ] **Laplace interconversion:** $\hat{J}(s) \cdot \hat{E}(s) = 1/s^2$ — used to convert between creep and relaxation data.
- [ ] **Kelvin–Voigt:** $J(t) = (1/E)(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$ — no instantaneous elastic response.
- [ ] **Maxwell:** $J(t) = 1/E + t/\eta$ — captures viscous flow but no equilibrium plateau.
- [ ] **SLS (Zener):** $J(t) = 1/(E_1+E_2) + (1/E_1)(1 - e^{-t/\tau'})$ with $\tau' = \eta(E_1+E_2)/(E_1 E_2)$ — captures both instantaneous and delayed elasticity.
- [ ] **$J_e^0$:** equilibrium recoverable compliance, molecular-weight-independent above $M_e$.
- [ ] **Units:** Compliance in **Pa⁻¹** (or equivalently **m²/N**); modulus in **Pa**.
- [ ] **Engineering applications:** Tyre treads (TTS), hip implants (long-term creep), vibration isolators ($J''$ for damping), landfill liners (50-year creep prediction).

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Poisson's Ratio (ν or μ)** is formally defined as the **negative of the ratio of lateral (transverse) strain to the longitudinal (axial) strain** when a material is subjected to a uniaxial tensile or compressive load within its elastic limit.

Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$\nu = -\frac{\varepsilon_{\text{lateral}}}{\varepsilon_{\text{longitudinal}}} = -\frac{\varepsilon_{\text{transverse}}}{\varepsilon_{\text{axial}}}$$

The **negative sign** is introduced to make Poisson's ratio a **positive quantity**, because when a material is stretched longitudinally ($\varepsilon_{\text{longitudinal}} > 0$), it contracts laterally ($\varepsilon_{\text{lateral}} < 0$), and vice versa.

**Key Terminology Mapping (as per KTU Module 1):**
- **Longitudinal Strain ($\varepsilon_l$)**: Strain produced along the direction of the applied force.
- **Lateral/Transverse Strain ($\varepsilon_t$)**: Strain produced perpendicular to the applied force.
- **Poisson's Ratio (ν)**: A **dimensionless** elastic constant, hence **no units**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in Polymer Physics:** Poisson's ratio is a *fundamental fingerprint* of a polymer's molecular architecture. Cross-linked elastomers approach **ν ≈ 0.5** (incompressible rubbery behavior), while rigid thermosets and glassy polymers exhibit **ν ≈ 0.30 – 0.40**. This single number reveals whether chain segments can rearrange freely under load.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Rubber Band Test**
> Take a rubber band (a classic elastomer) and stretch it horizontally.
> - The band becomes **longer** along the direction of pull (longitudinal extension).
> - Simultaneously, the band becomes **thinner** in the perpendicular directions (lateral contraction).
> - This **"necking-down"** effect is quantified by Poisson's ratio.
> - A perfectly incompressible rubber band has **ν = 0.5**, meaning its *volume barely changes* during deformation.
> - A piece of **cork**, in contrast, has **ν ≈ 0** because its cellular structure resists lateral contraction — this is exactly why cork is used to seal wine bottles!

**Plain-English Summary:**
> *When you pull something, it gets thinner sideways.* The *amount* of sideways shrinkage per unit lengthwise stretch is Poisson's ratio.

**Standard Metrics and Typical Values (Bolded for Quick Recall):**

| Material Class | Typical **ν** Value |
|---|---|
| **Ideal Incompressible Material (Rubber)** | **0.500** |
| **Elastomers (Natural Rubber, SBR)** | **0.490 – 0.499** |
| **Polyethylene (LDPE/HDPE)** | **0.40 – 0.45** |
| **Polystyrene (Glassy)** | **0.33 – 0.38** |
| **Epoxy / Rigid Thermoset** | **0.30 – 0.36** |
| **Cork (Cellular)** | **≈ 0.00** |
| **Steel / Concrete (Engineering Solids)** | **0.25 – 0.33** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Hook:** *Rubber is "lazy" — it hates volume change, so ν → 0.5. Cork is "stubborn" — it refuses to shrink sideways, so ν → 0.*

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The "Why" and "How" of Poisson's Ratio

Poisson's ratio emerges directly from the **generalized Hooke's Law for isotropic materials**. Let us dissect its origin step by step.

**Step 1 — Uniaxial Loading Condition:**
When a tensile stress $\sigma_x$ is applied along the x-axis, the resulting strains in all three orthogonal directions are:

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{\sigma_x}{E}, \qquad \varepsilon_y = \varepsilon_z = -\nu \cdot \frac{\sigma_x}{E}$$

**Step 2 — The "Why" Behind the Negative Sign:**
A positive $\varepsilon_x$ (extension) produces **negative** $\varepsilon_y, \varepsilon_z$ (contraction). To keep $\nu > 0$, the definition carries an explicit negative sign.

**Step 3 — Volume Change Behavior:**
The volumetric strain is the sum of the three orthogonal linear strains:

$$\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z = \frac{\sigma_x}{E}(1 - 2\nu)$$

- If $\nu = 0.5$ → $\varepsilon_v = 0$ → **Incompressible** (volume preserved).
- If $\nu < 0.5$ → $\varepsilon_v > 0$ → **Dilatant under tension**.
- If $\nu > 0.5$ → $\varepsilon_v < 0$ → **Auxetic / Contracting under tension** (rare; engineered metamaterials).

**Step 4 — Physical Bounds on ν:**
Classical elasticity theory imposes thermodynamic limits:

$$-1 \leq \nu \leq 0.5$$

> [!NOTE]
> For **stable, isotropic, linear-elastic materials** under standard conditions, the practical range collapses to **0 ≤ ν ≤ 0.5**.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — it forms the backbone of Module 1 derivations and 14-mark questions.**

| # | Equation | Description | Typical Use in KTU Problems |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\nu = -\varepsilon_t / \varepsilon_l$ | Basic definition | Direct calculation |
| 2 | $E = 2G(1+\nu)$ | E ↔ Shear Modulus relation | Relating elastic constants |
| 3 | $E = 3K(1-2\nu)$ | E ↔ Bulk Modulus relation | Incompressibility analysis |
| 4 | $G = \dfrac{3K(1-2\nu)}{2(1+\nu)}$ | G in terms of K, ν | Cross-conversion |
| 5 | $\varepsilon_v = \dfrac{\sigma}{E}(1-2\nu)$ | Volumetric strain | Polymer compressibility |
| 6 | $\sigma_y = \sigma_x \cdot \dfrac{\nu}{1-\nu}$ (biaxial plane strain) | Induced lateral stress | Thin sheet analysis |
| 7 | $K = \dfrac{E}{3(1-2\nu)}$ | Bulk Modulus formula | Rubbery vs glassy polymers |
| 8 | $\nu = \dfrac{3K - 2G}{2(3K + G)}$ | All-constants relation | Verifying data sets |

> [!WARNING]
> In KTU valuation, **forgetting the negative sign in the definition** of ν is a recurring one-mark deduction. Memorize $\nu = -\varepsilon_t/\varepsilon_l$ exactly.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of Poisson's Ratio |
|---|---|
| **Polymer Processing** | Predicting **die swell** in extrusion of molten polymers (rubbery melts show higher swell due to ν → 0.5). |
| **Biomedical Polymers** | Designing **vascular grafts & stents** — matching ν of the implant to the host tissue prevents stress shielding. |
| **Composite Materials** | Carbon-fiber-reinforced polymers often exhibit **negative ν** in transverse directions (auxetic behavior). |
| **Elastomer Seals & O-rings** | ν ≈ 0.5 is critical for **sealing pressure** without volume loss under compression. |
| **Foam & Cellular Polymers** | PU/PVC foams have ν controlled by cell geometry — affects cushioning performance. |
| **3D Printing of Polymers** | Warpage prediction in FDM/FFF printing uses ν to compute shrinkage anisotropy. |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Relating E, G, and ν

**Starting Premise (from Generalized Hooke's Law):**
For an isotropic linear-elastic solid under pure shear stress $\tau_{xy}$ in the x-y plane, the engineering shear strain $\gamma_{xy}$ is given by:

$$\gamma_{xy} = \frac{\tau_{xy}}{G}$$

**Step 1 — Decompose the shear stress into principal stresses:**
A pure shear state $\tau_{xy}$ is equivalent to a biaxial stress state with principal stresses:

$$\sigma_1 = +\tau_{xy}, \qquad \sigma_2 = -\tau_{xy}, \qquad \sigma_3 = 0$$

**Step 2 — Apply uniaxial Hooke's law in the principal direction (say, x):**
The longitudinal strain along the 1-axis:

$$\varepsilon_1 = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_1 - \nu(\sigma_2 + \sigma_3)\right] = \frac{1}{E}\left[\tau_{xy} - \nu(-\tau_{xy} + 0)\right]$$

$$\varepsilon_1 = \frac{\tau_{xy}}{E}(1 + \nu)$$

**Step 3 — The total shear strain is the engineering difference:**
By definition, engineering shear strain equals the change in angle, which for small deformations is the sum of the principal strains:

$$\gamma_{xy} = \varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_2$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\varepsilon_2$ symmetrically:**

$$\varepsilon_2 = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_2 - \nu(\sigma_1 + \sigma_3)\right] = \frac{1}{E}\left[-\tau_{xy} - \nu(\tau_{xy} + 0)\right]$$

$$\varepsilon_2 = -\frac{\tau_{xy}}{E}(1 + \nu)$$

**Step 5 — Subtract to get shear strain:**

$$\gamma_{xy} = \varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_2 = \frac{\tau_{xy}}{E}(1+\nu) - \left(-\frac{\tau_{xy}}{E}(1+\nu)\right)$$

$$\gamma_{xy} = \frac{2\tau_{xy}(1+\nu)}{E}$$

**Step 6 — Equate to the shear modulus definition:**
Since $\gamma_{xy} = \tau_{xy}/G$, we have:

$$\frac{\tau_{xy}}{G} = \frac{2\tau_{xy}(1+\nu)}{E}$$

**Step 7 — Cancel $\tau_{xy}$ and rearrange:**

$$\boxed{E = 2G(1 + \nu)}$$

> This is the **most tested relationship** in KTU Module 1 derivations.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Relating E, K, and ν

**Starting Premise (Hydrostatic Pressure):**
Apply a uniform hydrostatic pressure $p$ to a cubic element. The induced stress in each direction is:

$$\sigma_x = \sigma_y = \sigma_z = -p$$

**Step 1 — Compute longitudinal strain along x:**

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)\right] = \frac{1}{E}\left[-p - \nu(-p - p)\right]$$

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{-p}{E}(1 - 2\nu)$$

**Step 2 — Volumetric strain = sum of three orthogonal strains (isotropy implies $\varepsilon_x = \varepsilon_y = \varepsilon_z$):**

$$\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z = 3 \cdot \frac{-p}{E}(1 - 2\nu)$$

$$\varepsilon_v = \frac{-3p(1 - 2\nu)}{E}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the definition of Bulk Modulus:**
By definition, $K = -p/\varepsilon_v$, so:

$$K = \frac{-p}{\left[\dfrac{-3p(1-2\nu)}{E}\right]} = \frac{E}{3(1-2\nu)}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the desired boxed relation:**

$$\boxed{E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Insight:** As ν → 0.5, the bulk modulus K → ∞. This means the material **cannot be compressed volumetrically** — a defining property of rubbery polymers and ideal elastomers.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 7-Mark Format)

**Problem:** A polymer rod of length **200 mm** and diameter **20 mm** is subjected to an axial tensile load of **5 kN**. Upon loading, its length increases by **0.50 mm** and its diameter decreases by **0.004 mm**. Determine:
(a) Longitudinal and lateral strains.
(b) Poisson's ratio of the polymer.
(c) Young's modulus if the same material has a shear modulus of **0.8 GPa**.

**Given Data:**

$$L_0 = 200 \text{ mm}, \quad d_0 = 20 \text{ mm}, \quad F = 5 \text{ kN}$$

$$\Delta L = 0.50 \text{ mm}, \quad \Delta d = -0.004 \text{ mm}, \quad G = 0.8 \text{ GPa}$$

**Solution — Part (a): Strains**

$$\varepsilon_l = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \frac{0.50}{200} = 2.5 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\varepsilon_t = \frac{\Delta d}{d_0} = \frac{-0.004}{20} = -2.0 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Solution — Part (b): Poisson's Ratio**

$$\nu = -\frac{\varepsilon_t}{\varepsilon_l} = -\frac{(-2.0 \times 10^{-4})}{2.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.08$$

> Hmm, this very low ν suggests the polymer is highly anisotropic or cellular (e.g., oriented foamed polymer).

**Solution — Part (c): Young's Modulus**

$$E = 2G(1+\nu) = 2 \times 0.8 \text{ GPa} \times (1 + 0.08)$$

$$E = 1.6 \times 1.08 = 1.728 \text{ GPa}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: How Poisson's Ratio Connects Elastic Constants

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Applied Uniaxial Stress sigma_x"] --> B["Longitudinal Strain epsilon_l along x axis"]
    A --> C["Lateral Strain epsilon_t along y and z axes"]
    B --> D["Definition nu equals minus epsilon_t divided by epsilon_l"]
    C --> D
    D --> E["Poisson Ratio nu (dimensionless)"]
    E --> F["Relation E equals 2G times 1 plus nu"]
    E --> G["Relation E equals 3K times 1 minus 2 nu"]
    E --> H["Relation G equals 3K times 1 minus 2 nu over 2 times 1 plus nu"]
    F --> I["Young Modulus E GPa"]
    G --> J["Bulk Modulus K GPa"]
    H --> K["Shear Modulus G GPa"]
    E --> L["Polymer Classification Branch"]
    L --> M["nu greater than 0.45 Elastomers Rubber SBR"]
    L --> N["nu between 0.30 and 0.40 Glassy Polymers PS PMMA"]
    L --> O["nu less than 0.20 Cellular Polymers Cork Foams"]
    L --> P["nu equals 0.50 Ideal Incompressible Limit"]
    I --> Q["Engineering Property Prediction"]
    J --> Q
    K --> Q
    M --> Q
    N --> Q
    O --> Q
    P --> Q
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** Start at the top with the *physical input* (applied stress). Follow the arrows downward to see how a single measurement ($\varepsilon_l$, $\varepsilon_t$) propagates into the **Poisson's ratio**, which then **unlocks all three elastic moduli** and ultimately the **polymer classification**.

---

## 4.2 Schematic Flow: Deformation Pathway of a Polymer Specimen

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["Original Cylinder Length L Diameter D"] --> S2["Apply Tensile Force F along x axis"]
    S2 --> S3["Elongate to L plus delta L"]
    S2 --> S4["Contract to D minus delta D"]
    S3 --> S5["Compute epsilon_l equals delta L over L"]
    S4 --> S6["Compute epsilon_t equals delta D over D"]
    S5 --> S7["Apply nu equals minus epsilon_t over epsilon_l"]
    S6 --> S7
    S7 --> S8["Final Poisson Ratio Value nu"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define Poisson's ratio. State its range of values for a stable, isotropic, linear-elastic material and explain why natural rubber is reported to have a Poisson's ratio very close to 0.5.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Poisson's ratio ($\nu$) is defined as the **negative of the ratio of lateral strain to longitudinal strain** when a material is subjected to a uniaxial load within its elastic limit:

$$\nu = -\frac{\varepsilon_{\text{lateral}}}{\varepsilon_{\text{longitudinal}}}$$

It is a **dimensionless** quantity (no units).

**Range (1 Mark):**
For a stable, isotropic, linear-elastic material, thermodynamics restricts:

$$0 \leq \nu \leq 0.5$$

**Justification for Natural Rubber (1 Mark):**
Natural rubber is a **cross-linked elastomer** whose long-chain molecules undergo conformational rearrangements under load without significant volume change. Since the volumetric strain $\varepsilon_v = \dfrac{\sigma}{E}(1-2\nu) \to 0$, it follows that $\nu \to 0.5$, confirming its **near-incompressibility**.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]**
*A polyethylene sample is stretched, producing a longitudinal strain of 0.004. The resulting lateral strain is –0.0016. Calculate the Poisson's ratio. Comment on the nature of the polymer.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Calculation (2 Marks):**

$$\nu = -\frac{\varepsilon_t}{\varepsilon_l} = -\frac{(-0.0016)}{0.004} = 0.40$$

**Comment (1 Mark):**
A value of **ν = 0.40** is consistent with a **semi-crystalline thermoplastic polymer** such as polyethylene in its glassy/leathery state, where the amorphous regions allow moderate lateral contraction but crystalline domains resist full volume preservation.

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Sign errors** in Poisson's ratio (omitting the negative sign) cost **1 mark** consistently.
> - In derivations, students often **fail to state the assumption of isotropy** — a full 1-mark deduction.
> - Mixing up **E, G, K** symbols in the relation $E = 2G(1+\nu) = 3K(1-2\nu)$ is the most common error. Memorize the **'G-side' and 'K-side' formulas separately**.
> - **Units:** Always report E, G, K in **GPa** (not Pa) when the numerical values are large. KTU examiners expect clean unit consistency.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Define Poisson's ratio and derive the relationship between Young's modulus ($E$), Shear modulus ($G$), and Poisson's ratio ($\nu$).

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (2 Marks):**
Poisson's ratio is the **negative ratio of transverse strain to axial strain** under uniaxial loading:

$$\nu = -\frac{\varepsilon_t}{\varepsilon_l}$$

**Derivation (5 Marks) — Step-by-Step:**

**Step 1:** Consider a 2D element under pure shear stress $\tau_{xy}$. In principal axes, this is equivalent to $\sigma_1 = +\tau_{xy}$ and $\sigma_2 = -\tau_{xy}$.

**Step 2:** Use the generalized Hooke's law along the principal direction:

$$\varepsilon_1 = \frac{1}{E}[\sigma_1 - \nu \sigma_2] = \frac{\tau_{xy}}{E}(1 + \nu)$$

**Step 3:** By symmetry:

$$\varepsilon_2 = \frac{1}{E}[\sigma_2 - \nu \sigma_1] = -\frac{\tau_{xy}}{E}(1 + \nu)$$

**Step 4:** The engineering shear strain is:

$$\gamma_{xy} = \varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_2 = \frac{2\tau_{xy}(1+\nu)}{E}$$

**Step 5:** Equating to $\gamma_{xy} = \tau_{xy}/G$:

$$\frac{1}{G} = \frac{2(1+\nu)}{E} \implies \boxed{E = 2G(1+\nu)}$$

---

**(b)** A polymer rod of circular cross-section has an original length of 100 mm and a diameter of 10 mm. It is subjected to a tensile force of 4 kN. The rod elongates by 0.25 mm and its diameter contracts by 0.0015 mm. Calculate:
(i) Longitudinal strain
(ii) Lateral strain
(iii) Poisson's ratio
(iv) Young's modulus

Given the bulk modulus of the material is 2.5 GPa, also find (v) Shear modulus and (vi) the volumetric strain under this loading.

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $L_0 = 100$ mm, $d_0 = 10$ mm, $F = 4$ kN, $\Delta L = 0.25$ mm, $\Delta d = -0.0015$ mm, $K = 2.5$ GPa.

**(i) Longitudinal strain (1 Mark):**

$$\varepsilon_l = \frac{0.25}{100} = 2.5 \times 10^{-3}$$

**(ii) Lateral strain (1 Mark):**

$$\varepsilon_t = \frac{-0.0015}{10} = -1.5 \times 10^{-4}$$

**(iii) Poisson's ratio (1 Mark):**

$$\nu = -\frac{(-1.5 \times 10^{-4})}{2.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.06$$

> *Note: This very low ν indicates an auxetic/cellular polymer structure.*

**(iv) Young's Modulus (1 Mark):**
Cross-sectional area:

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.010)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2$$

Stress:

$$\sigma = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{4000}{7.854 \times 10^{-5}} = 50.93 \text{ MPa}$$

Young's modulus:

$$E = \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_l} = \frac{50.93 \text{ MPa}}{2.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 20.37 \text{ GPa}$$

**(v) Shear Modulus (1.5 Marks):**
From $E = 3K(1-2\nu)$:

$$G = \frac{3K(1-2\nu)}{2(1+\nu)} = \frac{3 \times 2.5 \times (1-0.12)}{2 \times 1.06} = \frac{6.6}{2.12} = 3.11 \text{ GPa}$$

**(vi) Volumetric strain (1.5 Marks):**

$$\varepsilon_v = \frac{\sigma}{E}(1-2\nu) = \frac{50.93 \times 10^{-3}}{20.37}(1-0.12)$$

$$\varepsilon_v = 2.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.88 = 2.2 \times 10^{-3}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

**(a)** Derive the relation $E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)$ and explain the physical significance of the limit $\nu = 0.5$.

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation (5 Marks):**

**Step 1:** Apply hydrostatic pressure $p$ on a cubic element: $\sigma_x = \sigma_y = \sigma_z = -p$.

**Step 2:** Strain in x-direction:

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)\right] = \frac{-p}{E}(1 - 2\nu)$$

**Step 3:** Volumetric strain (isotropy):

$$\varepsilon_v = 3\varepsilon_x = \frac{-3p(1-2\nu)}{E}$$

**Step 4:** By definition $K = -p/\varepsilon_v$:

$$K = \frac{E}{3(1-2\nu)} \implies \boxed{E = 3K(1-2\nu)}$$

**Physical Significance of $\nu = 0.5$ (2 Marks):**
- Substituting $\nu = 0.5$ gives **K → ∞** (infinite bulk modulus).
- This means the material is **perfectly incompressible** — its volume cannot change under any hydrostatic pressure.
- **Polymer context:** Cross-linked elastomers (e.g., vulcanized rubber, silicone) approximate this behavior because their coiled chains rearrange *without* significant free-volume change. This is the basis for their use in seals, gaskets, and hydraulic mounts.

---

**(b)** For a polymer specimen, the Young's modulus is **2.5 GPa** and the shear modulus is **0.86 GPa**. Determine:
(i) Poisson's ratio
(ii) Bulk modulus
(iii) The percentage change in volume when the specimen is subjected to a uniaxial tensile stress of 10 MPa
(iv) Comment on whether the material is rubbery or glassy based on ν.

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Poisson's ratio (1.5 Marks):**
From $E = 2G(1+\nu)$:

$$2.5 = 2 \times 0.86 \times (1 + \nu)$$

$$1 + \nu = \frac{2.5}{1.72} = 1.4535 \implies \nu = 0.4535 \approx 0.45$$

**(ii) Bulk modulus (1.5 Marks):**
From $E = 3K(1-2\nu)$:

$$K = \frac{E}{3(1-2\nu)} = \frac{2.5}{3(1-0.907)} = \frac{2.5}{0.279} = 8.96 \text{ GPa}$$

**(iii) Percentage volume change (2 Marks):**

$$\varepsilon_v = \frac{\sigma}{E}(1-2\nu) = \frac{10 \times 10^{-3} \text{ GPa}}{2.5 \text{ GPa}} \times (1 - 0.907)$$

$$\varepsilon_v = 4 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.093 = 3.72 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Percentage volume change:**

$$\Delta V / V_0 = 0.0372 \, \% \approx 0.04 \, \%$$

**(iv) Comment on rubbery vs. glassy nature (2 Marks):**
With $\nu = 0.45$, the polymer lies in the **elastomeric/rubbery regime** (rubbery polymers typically exhibit $0.45 \leq \nu \leq 0.499$). The near-zero volumetric strain further confirms that the material is **nearly incompressible**, characteristic of an **elastomer** (e.g., natural rubber or polyurethane elastomer), *not* a glassy polymer (which would have $\nu \approx 0.30$–$0.38$).

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Definition:** $\nu = -\varepsilon_t / \varepsilon_l$ — *negative sign is essential* to keep $\nu$ positive.
- [x] **Dimensionless:** Poisson's ratio has **no units**.
- [x] **Theoretical Range:** $0 \leq \nu \leq 0.5$ for stable isotropic materials; full thermodynamic range is $-1 \leq \nu \leq 0.5$.
- [x] **Three Golden Formulas:**
  - $E = 2G(1+\nu)$
  - $E = 3K(1-2\nu)$
  - $\varepsilon_v = (\sigma/E)(1-2\nu)$
- [x] **Incompressibility limit:** $\nu = 0.5$ ⟹ $K \to \infty$ ⟹ no volume change ⟹ **elastomer fingerprint**.
- [x] **Cork limit:** $\nu \approx 0$ ⟹ *no lateral contraction* ⟹ **cellular/fibrous material fingerprint**.
- [x] **Polymers tend toward high ν:** typical range **0.30 – 0.50**, much higher than metals/concrete (0.20 – 0.33).
- [x] **Volumetric strain sign convention:** Tension → volume increases if $\nu < 0.5$; decreases if $\nu > 0.5$ (auxetic).
- [x] **KTU exam pattern:** Always derive + numerical combo is the **Module 1 standard 14-mark template**.
- [x] **Pitfall to avoid:** Forgetting to convert units of stress/area to **GPa** before plugging into elastic constant relations.
- [x] **Memory hook:** *"G is in (1+ν) — K is in (1−2ν) — E ties them together."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Elastic Solid
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT402 - POLYMER PHYSICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Elastic Solid

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of an Elastic Solid

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Elastic Solid (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):** An **elastic solid** is a macroscopic continuous material body that, when subjected to an externally applied mechanical force (stress) within its elastic limit, undergoes a reversible deformation (strain) and instantaneously recovers its original shape and dimensions upon complete removal of the deforming force. The deformation energy is stored reversibly as **elastic potential energy** within the body and is fully returned when the load is removed.

In the context of **Polymer Physics (PCPOT402)**, the concept of an elastic solid is foundational because polymers exhibit a broad spectrum of elastic behavior — ranging from ideal **Hookean elastic solids** (like lightly cross-linked elastomers and glassy polymers below their glass transition temperature, $T_g$) to **viscoelastic** and **viscoplastic** responses (above $T_g$ or under long loading times).

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Spring Network:** Imagine the elastic solid as a 3D interconnected mesh of microscopic **Hookean springs** (each spring representing the interatomic bond potential, approximated by a quadratic near equilibrium). When you pull the mesh from one end, every individual spring stretches slightly. The macroscopic body deforms uniformly. The moment you release the pull, every spring simultaneously recoils to its natural length, and the mesh returns to its original geometry. This is exactly why an **elastomer rubber band** snaps back to its original size, while **molasses** (a viscous liquid) flows permanently without recovery.

### 1.3 Fundamental Terminology

| Term | Symbol | Definition | Units (SI) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Stress | $\sigma$ | Internal restoring force per unit area | $\text{Pa} = \text{N/m}^2$ |
| Strain | $\varepsilon$ | Relative deformation (dimensionless) | unitless |
| Young's Modulus | $E$ | Resistance to uniaxial tensile/compressive strain | $\text{Pa}$ |
| Shear Modulus | $G$ | Resistance to shear (tangential) deformation | $\text{Pa}$ |
| Bulk Modulus | $K$ | Resistance to volumetric (hydrostatic) compression | $\text{Pa}$ |
| Poisson's Ratio | $\nu$ | Ratio of lateral to axial strain | unitless |

---

> [!TIP]
> **Key Distinction for Polymer Students:** A perfectly elastic solid is an **idealization**. No real material — including polymers — is perfectly elastic over all time scales and temperature ranges. The *degree of elasticity* is what makes polymers special: highly cross-linked rubbers are nearly perfectly elastic, while thermoplastics above $T_g$ behave as viscoelastic liquids.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Principal Types of Stress and Strain

An elastic solid in 3D space can experience three independent modes of deformation:

**(a) Tensile / Compressive (Normal) Stress**
- Force acts **perpendicular** to the surface.
- Produces elongation or contraction along the force direction.

$$\sigma_n = \frac{F_{\perp}}{A}$$

**(b) Shear (Tangential) Stress**
- Force acts **parallel** to the surface.
- Produces a shape distortion (angular deformation).

$$\tau = \frac{F_{\parallel}}{A}$$

**(c) Hydrostatic (Volumetric) Stress**
- Force acts **uniformly from all directions**.
- Produces a change in volume without shape change.

$$p = -\frac{dF}{dA}$$

### 2.2 Hooke's Law — The Governing Principle of Elasticity

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Robert Hooke's Law (1660):** Within the **proportional elastic limit**, the stress in a solid is directly proportional to the strain it produces. The constant of proportionality is the **modulus of elasticity**.

$$\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$$

For shear deformation:

$$\tau = G \cdot \gamma$$

For volumetric deformation:

$$p = -K \cdot \frac{\Delta V}{V}$$

### 2.3 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown of Elastic Response

- **Step 1 — Application of Force:** External load $F$ is applied to the body, generating internal stress $\sigma$.
- **Step 2 — Atomic Bond Distortion:** Interatomic bonds (in polymers, this is a combination of covalent backbone bonds, secondary van der Waals interactions, and entanglements) stretch, bend, or compress.
- **Step 3 — Macroscopic Strain:** The body deforms by a measurable amount $\varepsilon$, proportional to $\sigma$ in the linear regime.
- **Step 4 — Energy Storage:** The work done by the external force is stored as **elastic potential energy** ($U = \frac{1}{2} \sigma \varepsilon V$).
- **Step 5 — Recovery (Unloading):** When the load is removed, the stored elastic energy drives the body back to its equilibrium configuration.
- **Step 6 — Path Independence:** For an ideal elastic solid, the loading and unloading paths are **identical and reversible** (no hysteresis, no energy dissipation).

### 2.4 Poisson's Ratio and Lateral Contraction

When a material is stretched in one direction ($x$), it contracts in the transverse directions ($y, z$). This phenomenon is quantified by **Poisson's Ratio**:

$$\nu = -\frac{\varepsilon_{\text{transverse}}}{\varepsilon_{\text{axial}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For most engineering materials, $\nu$ lies between **0.2 and 0.5**. Cork has $\nu \approx 0$ (does not contract laterally), while ideal rubbers approach $\nu = 0.5$ (volume conservation).

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Polynomial / Polymer Context |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma = E \, \varepsilon$ | Hooke's Law (uniaxial) | Modulus of glassy polymers |
| 2 | $\tau = G \, \gamma$ | Hooke's Law (shear) | Important for melt rheology onset |
| 3 | $p = -K \, (\Delta V / V)$ | Hydrostatic compression | Polymer densification under pressure |
| 4 | $\nu = -\varepsilon_t / \varepsilon_a$ | Poisson's ratio | Typical polymer range: 0.3 – 0.49 |
| 5 | $E = 2G(1+\nu)$ | $E$–$G$–$\nu$ relation | Critical inter-relation |
| 6 | $E = 3K(1-2\nu)$ | $E$–$K$–$\nu$ relation | Critical inter-relation |
| 7 | $G = \frac{3(1-2\nu)}{2(1+\nu)} \, E$ | $G$ from $E$ and $\nu$ | Often used in polymer characterization |
| 8 | $U = \frac{1}{2} \sigma \varepsilon V$ | Elastic strain energy density | Energy stored in stretched rubber |
| 9 | $C_{ij}$ tensor | Generalized Hooke's Law (36 $\to$ 21 constants) | Anisotropic crystalline polymers |

### 2.6 Engineering and Polymer Relevance

- **Rubber Elasticity Theory:** Cross-linked elastomers (e.g., vulcanized natural rubber, silicone) behave as entropic elastic solids. Their elastic response is governed by **entropy reduction** upon chain stretching, not by bond stretching (enthalpic elasticity). This is captured in the **Flory-Huggins** and **affine network models**.
- **Glass Transition Behavior ($T_g$):** Below $T_g$, amorphous polymers (e.g., PS, PMMA) are **glassy elastic solids** with moduli of $\sim 10^9$ Pa. Above $T_g$, they transition to a rubbery/viscoelastic state.
- **Fiber Reinforcement:** High-performance polymer fibers (Kevlar, UHMWPE) achieve $E$ values exceeding **100 GPa** due to oriented crystalline domains.
- **Biomedical Polymers:** Silicones, polyurethanes used in implants rely on elastic-solid mechanics for long-term fatigue resistance.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Generalized Hooke's Law in 3D

For a general anisotropic elastic solid, the stress tensor $\sigma_{ij}$ is related to the strain tensor $\varepsilon_{kl}$ through the **fourth-order stiffness tensor** $C_{ijkl}$:

$$\sigma_{ij} = \sum_{k,l} C_{ijkl} \, \varepsilon_{kl}$$

**Step-by-step derivation of the number of independent elastic constants:**

- **Step 1:** The stress tensor $\sigma_{ij}$ is symmetric, so it has $\frac{6}{1} = 6$ independent components: $\sigma_{11}, \sigma_{22}, \sigma_{33}, \sigma_{12}, \sigma_{13}, \sigma_{23}$.
- **Step 2:** Similarly, the strain tensor $\varepsilon_{kl}$ is symmetric, providing 6 independent components.
- **Step 3:** Therefore, the fourth-order tensor $C_{ijkl}$ originally has $3 \times 3 \times 3 \times 3 = 81$ components.
- **Step 4:** By Voigt symmetry ($C_{ijkl} = C_{jikl} = C_{ijlk}$), this reduces to $6 \times 6 = 36$ components.
- **Step 5:** Due to thermodynamic considerations (existence of elastic strain energy density function), $C_{ijkl} = C_{klij}$, which further reduces the count to $\frac{6 \times 7}{2} = 21$ independent elastic constants.

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** The most general anisotropic elastic solid has **21 independent elastic constants**.

For an **isotropic** elastic solid (which most unfilled amorphous polymers approximate), this reduces to just **2 independent constants** — for example, $E$ and $\nu$, or equivalently $K$ and $G$.

### 3.2 Derivation: Relationship Between Elastic Constants

We will derive the three famous relationships that are **examiner favorites** in KTU exams.

#### 3.2.1 Relation between $E$, $G$, and $\nu$

Consider a cube of side $L$ subjected to pure shear $\gamma$ on its top face. Equivalently, this is a uniaxial tension $\sigma_x$ combined with uniaxial compression $\sigma_y$ at $45^\circ$ to the shear face.

**Step 1:** The principal stresses in the shear configuration are:

$$\sigma_1 = +\tau, \quad \sigma_2 = -\tau$$

**Step 2:** The corresponding principal strains (using 3D Hooke's Law) are:

$$\varepsilon_1 = \frac{1}{E}(\sigma_1 - \nu \sigma_2) = \frac{\tau}{E}(1 + \nu)$$

$$\varepsilon_2 = \frac{1}{E}(\sigma_2 - \nu \sigma_1) = -\frac{\tau}{E}(1 + \nu)$$

**Step 3:** The engineering shear strain $\gamma$ is related to the principal strains by:

$$\gamma = \varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_2 = \frac{2\tau(1 + \nu)}{E}$$

**Step 4:** Substituting $\tau = G \gamma$ (definition of shear modulus):

$$G = \frac{E}{2(1 + \nu)}$$

$$\boxed{E = 2G(1 + \nu)}$$

#### 3.2.2 Relation between $E$, $K$, and $\nu$

**Step 1:** Apply a hydrostatic pressure $p$ to the cube. This creates equal normal stresses on all three axes:

$$\sigma_x = \sigma_y = \sigma_z = -p$$

**Step 2:** The volumetric strain is the sum of the three normal strains:

$$\frac{\Delta V}{V} = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z$$

**Step 3:** Using 3D Hooke's Law:

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)] = \frac{1}{E}[-p - \nu(-2p)] = \frac{-p(1 - 2\nu)}{E}$$

**Step 4:** By symmetry, $\varepsilon_x = \varepsilon_y = \varepsilon_z$, so:

$$\frac{\Delta V}{V} = \frac{-3p(1 - 2\nu)}{E}$$

**Step 5:** Comparing with the definition $p = -K (\Delta V / V)$:

$$K = \frac{E}{3(1 - 2\nu)}$$

$$\boxed{E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)}$$

#### 3.2.3 Relation between $G$, $K$, and $\nu$

Dividing the two boxed equations:

$$\frac{E = 2G(1+\nu)}{E = 3K(1-2\nu)}$$

Solving algebraically:

$$\boxed{G = \frac{3K(1 - 2\nu)}{2(1 + \nu)}}$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Moduli for Selected Polymers

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is a **high-yield reference** for KTU exams. Memorize the orders of magnitude.

| Material | Type | Young's Modulus $E$ (Pa) | Shear Modulus $G$ (Pa) | Poisson's Ratio $\nu$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Natural Rubber (vulcanized) | Elastomer | $\sim 10^6$ | $\sim 4 \times 10^5$ | $\approx 0.49$ |
| Low-Density Polyethylene (LDPE) | Thermoplastic | $\sim 2 \times 10^8$ | $\sim 7 \times 10^7$ | $\approx 0.45$ |
| Polystyrene (PS) | Glassy thermoplastic | $\sim 3 \times 10^9$ | $\sim 1.1 \times 10^9$ | $\approx 0.33$ |
| Poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA) | Glassy thermoplastic | $\sim 3.2 \times 10^9$ | $\sim 1.2 \times 10^9$ | $\approx 0.35$ |
| Nylon 6,6 | Semi-crystalline | $\sim 2.5 \times 10^9$ | $\sim 1.0 \times 10^9$ | $\approx 0.39$ |
| Epoxy Resin (cured) | Thermoset | $\sim 3.5 \times 10^9$ | $\sim 1.3 \times 10^9$ | $\approx 0.38$ |
| Steel (reference) | Metal | $\sim 2 \times 10^{11}$ | $\sim 7.7 \times 10^{10}$ | $\approx 0.30$ |

### 3.4 Strain Energy Density Derivation

**Step 1:** Work done per unit volume by a gradually increasing stress from 0 to $\sigma$ is:

$$dW = \sigma \, d\varepsilon$$

**Step 2:** Substituting Hooke's Law $\sigma = E\varepsilon$:

$$dW = E\varepsilon \, d\varepsilon$$

**Step 3:** Integrating from 0 to final strain $\varepsilon$:

$$W = \int_0^{\varepsilon} E\varepsilon \, d\varepsilon = \frac{1}{2} E \varepsilon^2$$

**Step 4:** Expressing in terms of stress (since $E\varepsilon^2 = \sigma^2 / E$):

$$\boxed{U = \frac{1}{2} \sigma \varepsilon = \frac{\sigma^2}{2E} = \frac{1}{2} E \varepsilon^2}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Elastic-Solid Response

The following Mermaid diagram maps the conceptual flow of elastic response — from atomic-level forces to macroscopic material behavior — which is highly relevant for polymer systems.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[External Mechanical Load F] --> A1[Applied Stress Sigma]
    A1 --> A2{Elastic Limit Check}
    A2 -- Within Limit --> A3[Linear Elastic Region]
    A2 -- Beyond Limit --> A4[Plastic Deformation]
    A3 --> A5[Hookes Law Active]
    A5 --> A6[Strain Epsilon = Sigma E]
    A6 --> A7[Energy Stored as Elastic PE]
    A7 --> A8[Load Removed]
    A8 --> A9[Full Recovery Original Shape]
    A1 --> B1[Polymer Microstructure Response]
    B1 --> B2[Covalent Bond Stretching]
    B1 --> B3[Van der Waals Bond Bending]
    B1 --> B4[Entropic Chain Reorientation]
    B2 --> C1[Enthalpic Elasticity Glasses]
    B3 --> C2[Enthalpic Elasticity Semicrystalline]
    B4 --> C3[Entropic Elasticity Rubbers]
    C1 --> D1[Modulus E ~ 10^9 Pa]
    C2 --> D2[Modulus E ~ 10^9 to 10^10 Pa]
    C3 --> D3[Modulus E ~ 10^6 Pa]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Concept Map — Inter-Relations of Elastic Constants

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[E Young Modulus] --- R1[Relation 1]
    P2[G Shear Modulus] --- R1
    P3[K Bulk Modulus] --- R2
    P4[Nu Poisson Ratio] --- R1
    P4 --- R2
    R1["E = 2G 1 plus Nu"]
    R2["E = 3K 1 minus 2Nu"]
    P2 --- R3
    P3 --- R3
    P4 --- R3
    R3["G = 3K 1 minus 2Nu over 2 1 plus Nu"]
    P5[Sigma Stress] --> P6[Epsilon Strain]
    P6 --> P7[Strain Energy U = half Sigma Epsilon V]
```

### 4.3 Textual Schematic — Stress-Strain Curve of a Polymer

> **Description (since 2D physical graphs cannot be drawn in Mermaid):**
>
> The stress-strain ($\sigma$ vs $\varepsilon$) curve for a typical ductile polymer (e.g., LDPE) under uniaxial tension shows the following regions in sequence:
>
> ```
>  σ │              ┌─────────────┐
>    │             /              │  Fracture
>    │            /  Yield Point  │
>    │           /   ●            │
>    │          /                 │
>    │         /  Plastic Region  │
>    │        /                   │
>    │       /                    │
>    │      / Elastic Region      │
>    │     / (Hooke's Law)        │
>    │    /                       │
>    │   /                        │
>    │  /                         │
>    │ /                          │
>    │/                           │
>    └────────────────────────────●──→ ε
>    O   Elastic    Yield   Plastic
>        Limit      Point    Flow
> ```
>
> **Region A → B:** Linear elastic (Hookean) region — slope = $E$.
> **Point B:** Yield point — onset of plastic flow.
> **Region B → C:** Plastic deformation — irreversible.
> **Point C:** Fracture point.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, May 2024]**
Define an elastic solid. List the four fundamental elastic constants used to characterize isotropic elastic materials.

**Model Answer:**
An elastic solid is a material body that recovers its original shape and size completely upon removal of an applied external force, provided the force is within its elastic limit. The deformation is fully reversible and obeys Hooke's Law.

The four fundamental elastic constants for an **isotropic** elastic solid are:

- Young's Modulus ($E$) — uniaxial tensile response
- Shear Modulus ($G$) — shear response
- Bulk Modulus ($K$) — volumetric response
- Poisson's Ratio ($\nu$) — lateral contraction ratio

Out of these, only **two are independent**; the other two can be derived from the standard inter-relations.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2023]**
State and explain Hooke's Law in its generalized 3D form. What is the maximum number of independent elastic constants for a fully anisotropic solid?

**Model Answer:**
**Generalized Hooke's Law:** Each component of the stress tensor is linearly related to all components of the strain tensor through the fourth-order stiffness tensor:

$$\sigma_{ij} = \sum_{k,l} C_{ijkl} \, \varepsilon_{kl}$$

The number of independent components of $C_{ijkl}$ for a fully anisotropic (triclinic) solid is **21**.

For an isotropic solid, this collapses to **2** independent constants (e.g., $E$ and $\nu$).

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** When solving derivations, students commonly lose marks by:
> 1. Skipping intermediate algebraic steps — always write every transition.
> 2. Forgetting to **define all symbols** before using them.
> 3. Not drawing a clear free-body diagram / coordinate system.
> 4. Missing the **units** in the final numerical answers.
> 5. Confusing **engineering shear strain** ($\gamma$) with **tensor shear strain** ($\gamma/2$).

---

#### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – Module 1, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Derive the relationship between Young's Modulus ($E$), Shear Modulus ($G$), and Poisson's Ratio ($\nu$) for an isotropic elastic solid. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A polymer rod of length 2 m and cross-sectional area $25 \text{ cm}^2$ is subjected to an axial tensile load of 5000 N. If the rod elongates by 2 mm and its lateral dimension contracts by 0.04 mm, calculate: (i) Young's Modulus, (ii) Poisson's Ratio, (iii) Shear Modulus, (iv) Bulk Modulus of the polymer. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**
*(Follow the derivation in Section 3.2.1 of these notes.)*

Start with the principal stress configuration for pure shear:

$$\sigma_1 = +\tau, \quad \sigma_2 = -\tau$$

Apply 3D Hooke's Law:

$$\varepsilon_1 = \frac{1}{E}(\sigma_1 - \nu\sigma_2) = \frac{\tau(1+\nu)}{E}$$

$$\varepsilon_2 = -\frac{\tau(1+\nu)}{E}$$

Engineering shear strain:

$$\gamma = \varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_2 = \frac{2\tau(1+\nu)}{E}$$

Since $\tau = G\gamma$:

$$G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)} \Rightarrow \boxed{E = 2G(1+\nu)}$$

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:**
$L = 2 \text{ m}$, $A = 25 \text{ cm}^2 = 25 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$, $F = 5000 \text{ N}$, $\Delta L = 2 \text{ mm} = 2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$, $\Delta d = 0.04 \text{ mm} = 0.04 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$.

**(i) Young's Modulus:**

$$\sigma = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{5000}{25 \times 10^{-4}} = 2 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$$

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = 10^{-3}$$

$$E = \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon} = \frac{2 \times 10^6}{10^{-3}} = 2 \times 10^9 \text{ Pa} = 2 \text{ GPa}$$

**(ii) Poisson's Ratio:**
Assuming initial diameter $d_0 = \sqrt{4A/\pi} = \sqrt{4 \times 25/\pi} \approx 5.64 \text{ cm}$. Lateral strain:

$$\varepsilon_t = \frac{\Delta d}{d_0} = \frac{0.04 \times 10^{-3}}{0.0564} \approx 7.09 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\nu = -\frac{\varepsilon_t}{\varepsilon_a} = \frac{7.09 \times 10^{-4}}{10^{-3}} \approx 0.71$$

*(Note: If the problem expects lateral dimension, take $d_0$ from problem context.)*

**(iii) Shear Modulus:**

$$G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)} = \frac{2 \times 10^9}{2(1+0.71)} \approx 5.85 \times 10^8 \text{ Pa}$$

**(iv) Bulk Modulus:**

$$K = \frac{E}{3(1-2\nu)} = \frac{2 \times 10^9}{3(1-1.42)} \approx \text{(negative, physically impossible if } \nu > 0.5\text{)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** A Poisson's ratio exceeding 0.5 is thermodynamically forbidden for stable isotropic materials. If the student's $\nu > 0.5$, they must identify this as an unphysical result and re-check the inputs.

---

#### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – Module 1, Model Paper — Alternate]**

**(a)** Derive the relationship between Young's Modulus ($E$), Bulk Modulus ($K$), and Poisson's Ratio ($\nu$). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the engineering significance of each of the four elastic constants for polymer materials, with reference to elastomers, glassy polymers, and semi-crystalline polymers. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**
*(Follow the derivation in Section 3.2.2 of these notes.)*

Apply hydrostatic pressure $p$ on a cube — principal stresses are all $-p$:

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}[-p - \nu(-2p)] = -\frac{p(1-2\nu)}{E}$$

Volumetric strain:

$$\frac{\Delta V}{V} = 3\varepsilon_x = -\frac{3p(1-2\nu)}{E}$$

Using $p = -K \Delta V/V$:

$$K = \frac{E}{3(1-2\nu)} \Rightarrow \boxed{E = 3K(1-2\nu)}$$

**Model Solution for (b):**

- **Young's Modulus ($E$):** Governs stiffness under uniaxial tension/compression. Elastomers have $E \sim 10^6$ Pa (soft, flexible), glassy polymers like PS have $E \sim 10^9$ Pa (rigid), semi-crystalline polymers like HDPE have $E \sim 10^9$ Pa (stiff but ductile).
- **Shear Modulus ($G$):** Important for torsional loading and melt processing. Determines how polymers deform under shear — critical for extrusion and injection molding.
- **Bulk Modulus ($K$):** Determines resistance to hydrostatic compression — relevant in polymer processing under high pressure (e.g., compression molding) and in deep-sea polymer applications.
- **Poisson's Ratio ($\nu$):** Indicates volume conservation. Elastomers show $\nu \to 0.5$ (near-incompressible, ideal rubber behavior). Glassy polymers show $\nu \approx 0.33$. Values near 0.5 indicate the material is dominated by entropic elasticity.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **Elastic solid** = reversible, recoverable deformation; obeys Hooke's Law within the elastic limit.
- **Stress** ($\sigma$) = Force / Area (Pa); **Strain** ($\varepsilon$) = Deformation / Original dimension (dimensionless).
- **Three stress modes:** tensile/compressive, shear, hydrostatic.
- **Four elastic constants:** $E$, $G$, $K$, $\nu$ — only **2 are independent** for isotropic solids.
- **Key relations (memorize):**
  - $E = 2G(1+\nu)$
  - $E = 3K(1-2\nu)$
  - $G = \frac{3K(1-2\nu)}{2(1+\nu)}$
- **Poisson's ratio:** typical polymer range = 0.3 – 0.49; **thermodynamic limit**: $-1 < \nu < 0.5$.
- **Strain energy density:** $U = \frac{1}{2}\sigma\varepsilon = \frac{\sigma^2}{2E} = \frac{1}{2}E\varepsilon^2$.
- **Anisotropic limit:** 21 independent constants; **isotropic limit:** 2 independent constants.
- **Polymer context:** Glassy polymers behave as enthalpic elastic solids below $T_g$; elastomers behave as entropic elastic solids above $T_g$ with $\nu \to 0.5$.
- **Polymer moduli orders of magnitude:** Elastomers $\sim 10^6$ Pa, Glassy/Semi-crystalline $\sim 10^9$ Pa, Fibers (Kevlar) $\sim 10^{11}$ Pa.
- **Exam tip:** Always derive the relation requested by stating the principal stress configuration FIRST, then apply 3D Hooke's Law, then identify the relevant strain, then substitute.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hooke's Law
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT402 - POLYMER PHYSICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Hooke's Law

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Hooke's Law** is the fundamental constitutive equation governing the linear elastic behavior of materials, including polymers, within the small-strain regime. Mathematically, it states that the **stress** $(\sigma)$ experienced by a material is directly proportional to the **strain** $(\varepsilon)$ produced, provided the material remains within its elastic limit.

For a one-dimensional case:

$$\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$$

where:
- $\sigma$ = Normal (tensile/compressive) stress, measured in **Pascals (Pa)** or **N/m²**
- $E$ = **Young's Modulus** (Modulus of Elasticity), intrinsic to the material, in **Pa**
- $\varepsilon$ = Dimensionless engineering strain $\left( \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0} \right)$

In the broader context of **Polymer Physics**, Hooke's Law is the foundational relationship describing the elastic response of polymeric chains, cross-linked networks, and elastomers before yielding, plastic flow, or viscoelastic transitions occur.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Hooke's Law is the gateway concept for Module 1 of Polymer Physics. It must be mastered before proceeding to **viscoelasticity, stress-strain behavior of polymers, and rubber elasticity theory**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **slingshot** — the rubber band stretches when you pull it back. The harder you pull (more force), the more it stretches (more strain). Robert Hooke, in 1660, was the first to formalize this simple observation: **"Ut tensio, sic vis"** — *"As the extension, so the force."*

Now, picture a **polymer chain** as a tangled plate of cooked spaghetti. When you gently pull the plate apart, the noodles straighten out a bit and resist — this is the **elastic regime** governed by Hooke's Law. Pull too hard, and the noodles start sliding past each other irreversibly (plastic deformation). The proportionality constant $E$ is essentially the "stiffness fingerprint" of the polymer, telling you how much it resists elastic deformation.

For different polymers, $E$ varies dramatically:
- **Soft elastomers** (natural rubber): $E \approx 0.01 - 0.1$ GPa
- **Flexible plastics** (polyethylene, polypropylene): $E \approx 0.1 - 2$ GPa
- **Stiff engineering plastics** (PMMA, polystyrene): $E \approx 2 - 4$ GPa
- **High-performance polymers** (Kevlar, carbon fiber reinforced): $E \approx 70 - 200$ GPa

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Constants to Remember:**
> - **1 Pa = 1 N/m²**
> - **1 GPa = 10⁹ Pa = 10⁹ N/m²** (commonly used for polymer moduli)
> - **Poisson's Ratio** for most polymers: $\nu \approx 0.3 - 0.5$ (incompressible limit near 0.5 for elastomers)
> - **Elastic Limit:** The maximum stress beyond which Hooke's Law fails and permanent deformation occurs

## 1.4 Hooke's Law in Polymer Context

Polymers exhibit a **time-dependent** mechanical response, but at very short timescales or low temperatures (below $T_g$), they behave **glassy** and approximately obey Hooke's Law. The **modulus $E$** for polymers is governed by:
- Chain stiffness (bond rotation barriers)
- Crosslink density (for thermosets/elastomers)
- Crystallinity (for semi-crystalline polymers)
- Temperature and strain rate
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of Hooke's Law

The principle can be deconstructed into **five logical pillars** that govern its application:

### Pillar 1: Proportionality
Stress and strain are **linearly related** through the elastic modulus. The graph of $\sigma$ vs. $\varepsilon$ is a **straight line** passing through the origin in the elastic region.

### Pillar 2: Why a Constant Modulus?
The constant $E$ emerges from the **interatomic/intermolecular potential**. Near the equilibrium bond length, the potential energy curve is approximately parabolic (Taylor expansion to second order). The second derivative of this potential yields the stiffness.

### Pillar 3: Tensor Generalization (3D)
In three dimensions, Hooke's Law becomes a **fourth-rank tensor** relationship:

$$\sigma_{ij} = \sum_{k,l} C_{ijkl} \cdot \varepsilon_{kl}$$

For isotropic materials, this reduces to just **two independent constants** (e.g., $E$ and $\nu$).

### Pillar 4: Energy Storage
The elastic strain energy density stored in a Hookean solid is:

$$U = \frac{1}{2} \sigma \varepsilon = \frac{1}{2} E \varepsilon^2 = \frac{\sigma^2}{2E}$$

This energy is **fully recoverable** upon unloading — a defining feature of elastic (as opposed to viscoelastic) behavior.

### Pillar 5: Limits of Validity
Hooke's Law holds only when:
- Strain is small (typically $\varepsilon < 0.1\%$ for metals, can be higher for elastomers)
- Temperature is well below the glass transition ($T_g$) or melting point ($T_m$)
- Loading rate is high enough to suppress viscous flow

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For Polymers:** The **"Hookean region"** of the stress-strain curve is much smaller than for metals. Polymers transition rapidly from elastic to viscoelastic/plastic behavior, which is why polymer mechanics deviates from simple Hookean behavior under most practical conditions.

## 2.2 Generalized Hooke's Law: The Complete Isotropic Form

For an isotropic material, the full 3D stress-strain relationships are:

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)\right]$$

$$\varepsilon_y = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_y - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_z)\right]$$

$$\varepsilon_z = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_z - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_y)\right]$$

The shear components obey:

$$\tau_{xy} = G \cdot \gamma_{xy}, \quad \tau_{yz} = G \cdot \gamma_{yz}, \quad \tau_{zx} = G \cdot \gamma_{zx}$$

where $G$ is the **Shear Modulus**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table is your exam-ready cheat sheet for Hooke's Law in Polymer Physics. Memorize these relations!**

| # | Formula | Description | SI Units |
|---|---------|-------------|----------|
| 1 | $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$ | Basic 1D Hooke's Law | Pa |
| 2 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0}$ | Engineering strain definition | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A_0}$ | Engineering stress definition | Pa |
| 4 | $U = \dfrac{1}{2} E \varepsilon^2$ | Elastic strain energy density | J/m³ |
| 5 | $G = \dfrac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$ | Shear modulus relation | Pa |
| 6 | $K = \dfrac{E}{3(1-2\nu)}$ | Bulk modulus relation | Pa |
| 7 | $\lambda = \dfrac{\nu E}{(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)}$ | Lamé's first parameter | Pa |
| 8 | $\sigma_{ij} = \lambda \delta_{ij} \varepsilon_{kk} + 2G \varepsilon_{ij}$ | Generalized Hooke's Law (tensor) | Pa |
| 9 | $E = \dfrac{\sigma}{\varepsilon}\bigg\vert_{\text{linear region}}$ | Definition of Young's modulus | Pa |
| 10 | $E = \dfrac{F L_0}{A_0 \Delta L}$ | Experimental determination | Pa |
| 11 | $F = k \cdot x$ | Force form of Hooke's Law (spring) | N |
| 12 | $k = \dfrac{EA_0}{L_0}$ | Spring constant from material properties | N/m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL Markdown Note:** Notice how the absolute value / condition symbols use $\vert$ instead of the bare pipe character $\vert$ to avoid breaking the markdown table structure.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of Hooke's Law |
|-------|---------------------------|
| **Polymer Processing** | Calculating die swell, extruder design pressures |
| **Biomedical Implants** | Designing artificial tendons, vascular grafts, heart valves |
| **Packaging Industry** | Tensile strength calculations for films, containers |
| **Aerospace Composites** | Predicting deformation under load in polymer-matrix composites |
| **Rubber Technology** | First-principles design of tires, gaskets, seals |
| **3D Printing** | Predicting warping, residual stress in printed polymer parts |
| **Civil Engineering** | Geopolymer concrete, polymer-modified asphalt design |

## 2.5 The Polymer-Specific Significance

In polymer physics, Hooke's Law sets the stage for understanding:

1. **Glass Transition Mechanics** — Below $T_g$, the amorphous polymer is glassy and obeys Hooke's Law with $E \approx 1-5$ GPa.
2. **Rubber Elasticity** — The **entropic** origin of elasticity in elastomers; the modulus arises from chain entropy, not bond stretching.
3. **Viscoelasticity** — The deviation from Hookean behavior over time leads to **creep** and **stress relaxation**.
4. **Reinforcement** — Adding fillers, fibers, or nanoparticles can increase $E$ by orders of magnitude.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: From Interatomic Potential to Hooke's Law

### Starting Point
The interatomic potential energy $U(r)$ between two bonded atoms (e.g., C–C in a polymer backbone) can be approximated by the **Morse potential**, but near the equilibrium bond length $r_0$, a Taylor series expansion is sufficient.

### Step 1: Taylor Series Expansion
Expand $U(r)$ about the equilibrium separation $r_0$:

$$
U(r) = U(r_0) + \left.\frac{dU}{dr}\right|_{r_0} (r - r_0) + \frac{1}{2}\left.\frac{d^2U}{dr^2}\right|_{r_0} (r - r_0)^2 + \frac{1}{6}\left.\frac{d^3U}{dr^3}\right|_{r_0} (r - r_0)^3 + \ldots
$$

### Step 2: Simplify Using Equilibrium Conditions
At equilibrium $r = r_0$:
- $U(r_0)$ is the minimum potential energy (set as reference, $U(r_0) = 0$)
- $\left.\dfrac{dU}{dr}\right|_{r_0} = 0$ (force is zero at minimum)

The first three terms vanish, leaving:

$$
U(r) \approx \frac{1}{2} \left.\frac{d^2U}{dr^2}\right|_{r_0} (r - r_0)^2
$$

### Step 3: Define the Effective Spring Constant
Let:

$$
k = \left.\frac{d^2U}{dr^2}\right|_{r_0}
$$

Then:

$$
U(r) \approx \frac{1}{2} k (r - r_0)^2
$$

### Step 4: Force from Potential
The force is the negative gradient of the potential:

$$
F = -\frac{dU}{dr} = -k(r - r_0)
$$

### Step 5: Express Displacement as Strain
Let $x = r - r_0 = \Delta r$ (displacement from equilibrium). The force becomes:

$$
F = -k \cdot x
$$

The negative sign indicates that the force is **restoring** (acts opposite to displacement). This is the **mechanical form of Hooke's Law**.

### Step 6: Connect to Macroscopic Stress-Strain Form
For a uniform bar of cross-sectional area $A_0$ and original length $L_0$:
- Force on bar: $F$ (N)
- Stress: $\sigma = F / A_0$
- Strain: $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L_0$
- Displacement of one end: $x = \Delta L$
- Spring constant: $k = E A_0 / L_0$

Substituting:

$$
F = \frac{EA_0}{L_0} \Delta L
$$

Dividing both sides by $A_0$:

$$
\frac{F}{A_0} = E \cdot \frac{\Delta L}{L_0}
$$

This yields the macroscopic Hooke's Law:

$$
\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon \quad \blacksquare
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Generalized 3D Hooke's Law from Strain Energy

### Starting Point
For a linear elastic, isotropic material, the **strain energy density** $U$ is a quadratic function of strain:

$$
U = \frac{1}{2} C_1 \varepsilon_{xx}^2 + C_2 \varepsilon_{yy}^2 + C_3 \varepsilon_{zz}^2 + C_4 \varepsilon_{xx}\varepsilon_{yy} + C_5 \varepsilon_{yy}\varepsilon_{zz} + C_6 \varepsilon_{zz}\varepsilon_{xx} + G(\gamma_{xy}^2 + \gamma_{yz}^2 + \gamma_{zx}^2)
$$

### Step 1: Symmetry Reduction
By isotropy, all normal strain coefficients for the squared terms are equal, and all cross terms share a single coefficient. This gives:

$$
U = \frac{\lambda}{2}(\varepsilon_{xx} + \varepsilon_{yy} + \varepsilon_{zz})^2 + G(\varepsilon_{xx}^2 + \varepsilon_{yy}^2 + \varepsilon_{zz}^2 + \gamma_{xy}^2 + \gamma_{yz}^2 + \gamma_{zx}^2)
$$

where $\lambda$ and $G$ are the two **Lamé constants**.

### Step 2: Derive Stress from Energy
By the principle of **thermodynamic conjugation**, stress components are the partial derivatives of the strain energy density with respect to strain:

$$
\sigma_{xx} = \frac{\partial U}{\partial \varepsilon_{xx}}
$$

### Step 3: Compute the Derivative
Differentiating $U$ with respect to $\varepsilon_{xx}$:

$$
\frac{\partial}{\partial \varepsilon_{xx}}\left[\frac{\lambda}{2}(\varepsilon_{xx} + \varepsilon_{yy} + \varepsilon_{zz})^2\right] = \lambda(\varepsilon_{xx} + \varepsilon_{yy} + \varepsilon_{zz})
$$

$$
\frac{\partial}{\partial \varepsilon_{xx}}\left[G \varepsilon_{xx}^2\right] = 2G \varepsilon_{xx}
$$

### Step 4: Assemble the Result

$$
\sigma_{xx} = \lambda(\varepsilon_{xx} + \varepsilon_{yy} + \varepsilon_{zz}) + 2G \varepsilon_{xx}
$$

In tensor (Einstein summation) form:

$$
\sigma_{ij} = \lambda \delta_{ij} \varepsilon_{kk} + 2G \varepsilon_{ij} \quad \blacksquare
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Relationship Between $E$, $G$, $\nu$, and $K$

This is a **KTU favourite** derivation — students are routinely asked to derive the inter-relationships between the four elastic constants.

### Goal
Prove that:

$$
G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)}
$$

### Step 1: Apply Uniaxial Stress
Let $\sigma_{xx} = \sigma$ and $\sigma_{yy} = \sigma_{zz} = 0$.

From the generalized law:

$$
\sigma_{xx} = \lambda(\varepsilon_{xx} + \varepsilon_{yy} + \varepsilon_{zz}) + 2G \varepsilon_{xx}
$$

### Step 2: Set Lateral Stresses to Zero
For $\sigma_{yy} = 0$:

$$
0 = \lambda(\varepsilon_{xx} + \varepsilon_{yy} + \varepsilon_{zz}) + 2G \varepsilon_{yy}
$$

Solving for $\varepsilon_{yy}$:

$$
\varepsilon_{yy} = -\frac{\lambda}{2G + \lambda} (\varepsilon_{xx} + \varepsilon_{zz})
$$

By symmetry, $\varepsilon_{yy} = \varepsilon_{zz}$, so:

$$
\varepsilon_{yy} = -\frac{\lambda}{2G + \lambda} (\varepsilon_{xx} + \varepsilon_{yy})
$$

### Step 3: Solve for Lateral Strain
Rearranging:

$$
\varepsilon_{yy}\left(1 + \frac{\lambda}{2G + \lambda}\right) = -\frac{\lambda \varepsilon_{xx}}{2G + \lambda}
$$

$$
\varepsilon_{yy}\left(\frac{2G + 2\lambda}{2G + \lambda}\right) = -\frac{\lambda \varepsilon_{xx}}{2G + \lambda}
$$

$$
\varepsilon_{yy} = -\frac{\lambda \varepsilon_{xx}}{2G + 2\lambda} = -\frac{\lambda}{2(G + \lambda)} \varepsilon_{xx}
$$

### Step 4: Define Poisson's Ratio
By definition:

$$
\nu = -\frac{\varepsilon_{yy}}{\varepsilon_{xx}} = \frac{\lambda}{2(G + \lambda)}
$$

### Step 5: Express $\lambda$ in terms of $G$ and $\nu$
Solving for $\lambda$:

$$
\nu \cdot 2(G + \lambda) = \lambda
$$

$$
2\nu G + 2\nu \lambda = \lambda
$$

$$
2\nu G = \lambda(1 - 2\nu)
$$

$$
\lambda = \frac{2\nu G}{1 - 2\nu}
$$

### Step 6: Substitute into $\sigma_{xx}$ Equation
For uniaxial loading, $\sigma_{xx} = \sigma$:

$$
\sigma = \lambda(\varepsilon_{xx} + 2\varepsilon_{yy}) + 2G \varepsilon_{xx}
$$

Using $\varepsilon_{yy} = -\nu \varepsilon_{xx}$:

$$
\sigma = \lambda(\varepsilon_{xx} - 2\nu \varepsilon_{xx}) + 2G \varepsilon_{xx} = \lambda \varepsilon_{xx}(1 - 2\nu) + 2G \varepsilon_{xx}
$$

### Step 7: Recognize $E$
Since $\sigma = E \varepsilon_{xx}$:

$$
E = \lambda(1 - 2\nu) + 2G
$$

Substituting $\lambda = \dfrac{2\nu G}{1 - 2\nu}$:

$$
E = \frac{2\nu G}{1 - 2\nu}(1 - 2\nu) + 2G = 2\nu G + 2G = 2G(1 + \nu)
$$

### Final Result

$$
G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)} \quad \blacksquare
$$

Similarly, by applying hydrostatic pressure ($\sigma_{xx} = \sigma_{yy} = \sigma_{zz} = -p$) and tracing through analogous steps, one obtains:

$$
K = \frac{E}{3(1-2\nu)} \quad \blacksquare
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Stress-Strain for Various Polymers

| Polymer | Young's Modulus $E$ (GPa) | Poisson's Ratio $\nu$ | Tensile Strength (MPa) | Elongation at Break (%) |
|---------|---------------------------|----------------------|----------------------|------------------------|
| Natural Rubber | 0.01 – 0.1 | 0.49 | 20 – 30 | 500 – 800 |
| Low-Density Polyethylene (LDPE) | 0.1 – 0.3 | 0.45 | 10 – 20 | 100 – 600 |
| High-Density Polyethylene (HDPE) | 0.5 – 1.0 | 0.42 | 20 – 40 | 100 – 1000 |
| Polypropylene (PP) | 1.0 – 2.0 | 0.40 | 25 – 40 | 100 – 600 |
| Polystyrene (PS) | 3.0 – 3.5 | 0.34 | 30 – 60 | 1 – 4 |
| PMMA (Acrylic) | 2.5 – 3.5 | 0.37 | 50 – 80 | 2 – 10 |
| Nylon 6,6 | 2.0 – 4.0 | 0.39 | 50 – 90 | 20 – 200 |
| Polycarbonate (PC) | 2.0 – 2.5 | 0.38 | 60 – 75 | 80 – 150 |
| Epoxy (cured) | 2.0 – 5.0 | 0.35 | 35 – 100 | 1 – 10 |
| Kevlar (Aramid fiber) | 70 – 130 | 0.36 | 2900 – 3600 | 2 – 4 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Notice that **elastomers** like natural rubber have very low $E$ but enormous elongation — they obey Hooke's Law only in the **initial 1–5% strain region**, after which **strain-induced crystallization** and **finite extensibility of chains** kick in.

## 3.5 Strain Energy Derivation for Hookean Solids

### Work Done to Stretche a Bar
The infinitesimal work done in stretching a bar by an amount $d\varepsilon$ is:

$$
dW = \sigma \cdot d\varepsilon = E \varepsilon \cdot d\varepsilon
$$

### Total Work
Integrating from 0 to $\varepsilon$:

$$
W = \int_0^{\varepsilon} E \varepsilon' \, d\varepsilon' = \frac{1}{2} E \varepsilon^2
$$

This work is stored as **elastic strain energy density**:

$$
U = \frac{1}{2} E \varepsilon^2 = \frac{1}{2} \sigma \varepsilon = \frac{\sigma^2}{2E} \quad \blacksquare
$$

This is highly relevant for polymer physics, where the strain energy function is used to model **hyperelastic** materials (rubber) beyond the Hookean regime.
