$$\Delta \eta \,(\%) = \left[ \left( \frac{\dot{\gamma}_b}{\dot{\gamma}_a} \right)^{n-1} - 1 \right] \times 100$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Numerical Anchor:** If $n = 0.5$ and shear rate increases tenfold ($\dot{\gamma}_b / \dot{\gamma}_a = 10$), then $\Delta \eta = (10^{-0.5} - 1)\times 100 = (0.316 - 1)\times 100 = -68.4\%$. The negative sign confirms viscosity drops by ~68%.

## 3.4 Worked Example (KTU Board-Style Numerical)

**Problem:** A polymer melt at 200 °C obeys the power-law model with $K = 5000$ Pa·sⁿ and $n = 0.4$. Compute (a) the apparent viscosity at $\dot{\gamma} = 100$ s⁻¹, and (b) the shear stress at $\dot{\gamma} = 500$ s⁻¹.

**Solution (a):**

$$\eta_a = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,n-1} = 5000 \times (100)^{0.4 - 1} = 5000 \times (100)^{-0.6}$$

$$(100)^{-0.6} = 10^{-1.2} \approx 0.0631$$

$$\eta_a \approx 5000 \times 0.0631 \approx 315.5 \; \text{Pa·s}$$

**Solution (b):**

$$\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,n} = 5000 \times (500)^{0.4}$$

$$(500)^{0.4} = 10^{0.4 \times \log_{10}(500)} = 10^{0.4 \times 2.699} = 10^{1.0796} \approx 12.00$$

$$\tau \approx 5000 \times 12.00 = 60{,}000 \; \text{Pa} = 60 \; \text{kPa}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Rheological Fluid Classification

The following mermaid block diagrams the **decision-flow architecture** that classifies a fluid based on its flow behavior index $n$ and stress threshold (yield stress $\tau_y$).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fluid Sample Tested at Shear Rates] --> B{Observes Yield Stress tau_y?}
    B -- Yes --> C{Beyond Yield: How Does eta behave?}
    B -- No --> D{Flow Behavior Index n}
    C -- eta Constant --> E[Bingham Plastic]
    C -- eta Decreases --> F[Herschel-Bulkley Shear Thinning]
    C -- eta Increases --> G[Herschel-Bulkley Shear Thickening]
    D -- n equals 1 --> H[Newtonian Fluid]
    D -- n less than 1 --> I[Pseudoplastic Shear Thinning]
    D -- n greater than 1 --> J[Dilatant Shear Thickening]
    I --> K[Power Law tau = K gamma dot to the n]
    J --> L[Power Law tau = K gamma dot to the n]
    H --> M[Linear tau = eta times gamma dot]
    style I fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px
    style J fill:#ffe5cc,stroke:#663300,stroke-width:2px
    style H fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#003300,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Pseudoplasticity is one branch of the power-law family. It occurs when there is **no yield stress** and the flow behavior index lies strictly below unity.

## 4.2 Schematic: Shear Stress and Viscosity Behavior Across Fluid Types

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1[Shear Stress vs Shear Rate]
        direction LR
        A1[Newtonian: Straight Line slope equals eta] --> B1[Pseudoplastic: Concave Down n less than 1]
        B1 --> C1[Dilatant: Concave Up n greater than 1]
    end
    subgraph S2[Apparent Viscosity vs Shear Rate]
        direction LR
        A2[Newtonian: Horizontal Line eta constant] --> B2[Pseudoplastic: Monotonically Decreasing]
        B2 --> C2[Dilatant: Monotonically Increasing]
    end
    style B1 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px
    style B2 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key visual takeaway:** For a pseudoplastic fluid, the $\tau$–$\dot{\gamma}$ curve bends **downward** (concave-down), while the $\eta_a$–$\dot{\gamma}$ curve **decreases monotonically**. This is the inverse of dilatant behavior.

## 4.3 Mechanism Block Diagram: Polymer Chain Response to Shear

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Rest State: Random Coils Entangled] --> S1[Low Shear: Coils Slightly Stretched]
    S1 --> S2[Intermediate Shear: Entanglements Break]
    S2 --> S3[High Shear: Chains Aligned with Flow]
    S3 --> S4[Viscosity Drops to Eta Infinity]
    S4 --> S5[Shear Removed: Brownian Re-entanglement]
    S5 --> S0
    style S2 fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#665500,stroke-width:2px
    style S3 fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#663300,stroke-width:2px
    style S4 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**

**Q1. Define pseudoplasticity. Write the power-law model used to describe pseudoplastic fluids and state the criterion on the flow behavior index.**

**Model Answer:**
Pseudoplasticity, also known as **shear-thinning**, is the phenomenon in which the apparent viscosity of a fluid **decreases with increasing shear rate**. It is described by the Ostwald–de Waele power-law model:

$$\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,n}$$

where $\tau$ is shear stress, $\dot{\gamma}$ is shear rate, $K$ is the consistency index, and $n$ is the flow behavior index. The criterion for pseudoplasticity is $n < 1$. (3 marks)

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q2. Distinguish between pseudoplastic and thixotropic behavior.**

**Model Answer:**
Both involve a drop in apparent viscosity under shear, but the key distinction is **time-dependence**.

- **Pseudoplastic:** Viscosity decreases instantaneously with shear rate and recovers instantly when shear is removed. The behavior is **time-independent at steady state**.
- **Thixotropic:** Viscosity decrease is **time-dependent** — the fluid structure breaks down gradually under sustained shear and rebuilds gradually when at rest.

In short: *pseudoplasticity* is a steady-state shear-rate effect; *thixotropy* is a transient time-dependent structural breakdown. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B Question (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam – July 2024 Style]**

**(a)** Derive an expression for the apparent viscosity of a pseudoplastic fluid obeying the power-law model $\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^{\,n}$. Show clearly that the apparent viscosity decreases with increasing shear rate when $n < 1$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A polymer melt obeys the power-law model with $K = 8000$ Pa·sⁿ and $n = 0.35$. Calculate:
  (i) the apparent viscosity at $\dot{\gamma} = 50$ s⁻¹,
  (ii) the shear stress at $\dot{\gamma} = 1000$ s⁻¹,
  (iii) the percentage drop in apparent viscosity when the shear rate is increased from 10 s⁻¹ to 500 s⁻¹. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**
Starting from $\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,n}$ and the definition $\eta_a = \tau / \dot{\gamma}$:

$$\eta_a = \frac{K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,n}}{\dot{\gamma}} = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,n-1}$$

Since $n < 1$, the exponent $(n - 1) < 0$. Therefore $\eta_a \propto \dot{\gamma}^{\,\text{(negative)}}$, which decreases monotonically with $\dot{\gamma}$. **Q.E.D.**

**Model Solution (b):**

**(i)** $\eta_a = 8000 \times (50)^{0.35 - 1} = 8000 \times (50)^{-0.65}$

$(50)^{-0.65} = 10^{-0.65 \times \log_{10}(50)} = 10^{-0.65 \times 1.6990} = 10^{-1.1044} \approx 0.0787$

$\eta_a \approx 8000 \times 0.0787 \approx \mathbf{629.6 \; Pa \cdot s}$ (3 marks)

**(ii)** $\tau = 8000 \times (1000)^{0.35} = 8000 \times 10^{0.35 \times 3} = 8000 \times 10^{1.05} \approx 8000 \times 11.22$

$\tau \approx \mathbf{89{,}760 \; Pa \approx 89.8 \; kPa}$ (2 marks)

**(iii)** Using the derived formula:

$$\Delta \eta \,(\%) = \left[ \left( \frac{500}{10} \right)^{0.35 - 1} - 1 \right] \times 100 = \left[ (50)^{-0.65} - 1 \right] \times 100$$

$(50)^{-0.65} \approx 0.0787$

$$\Delta \eta \,(\%) = (0.0787 - 1) \times 100 \approx \mathbf{-92.1\%}$$

So the apparent viscosity drops by about **92.1%** — the strongly shear-thinning signature. (2 marks)

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Internal Choice Alternative) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Style]**

**(a)** With the help of a labelled $\tau$–$\dot{\gamma}$ sketch, explain the rheological behavior of pseudoplastic, Newtonian, and dilatant fluids. State the range of flow behavior index $n$ for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a pseudoplastic polymer solution, the following data are obtained:

| Shear rate $\dot{\gamma}$ (s⁻¹) | Shear stress $\tau$ (Pa) |
|---|---|
| 10 | 200 |
| 100 | 1200 |

Determine:
  (i) the flow behavior index $n$,
  (ii) the consistency index $K$,
  (iii) the apparent viscosity at $\dot{\gamma} = 50$ s⁻¹. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**
A plot of $\tau$ vs $\dot{\gamma}$ on log-log axes yields three distinct signatures:

- **Newtonian ($n = 1$):** A straight line of slope 1 passing through the origin. Viscosity is constant.
- **Pseudoplastic ($n < 1$):** A concave-down curve — slope $n < 1$. Viscosity drops with $\dot{\gamma}$.
- **Dilatant ($n > 1$):** A concave-up curve — slope $n > 1$. Viscosity rises with $\dot{\gamma}$.

[Students should sketch the three curves on a single set of axes and label each.] (7 marks)

**Model Solution (b):**

**(i)** Using the two-point formula:

$$n = \frac{\ln(\tau_2 / \tau_1)}{\ln(\dot{\gamma}_2 / \dot{\gamma}_1)} = \frac{\ln(1200/200)}{\ln(100/10)} = \frac{\ln(6)}{\ln(10)} = \frac{1.7918}{2.3026} \approx \mathbf{0.778}$$

(3 marks)

**(ii)** Substituting into $\tau_1 = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}_1^{\,n}$:

$$K = \frac{\tau_1}{\dot{\gamma}_1^{\,n}} = \frac{200}{(10)^{0.778}} = \frac{200}{10^{0.778}}$$

$10^{0.778} \approx 5.998$

$$K \approx \frac{200}{5.998} \approx \mathbf{33.34 \; Pa \cdot s^{n}}$$

(2 marks)

**(iii)** $\eta_a = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,n-1} = 33.34 \times (50)^{0.778 - 1} = 33.34 \times (50)^{-0.222}$

$(50)^{-0.222} = 10^{-0.222 \times \log_{10}(50)} = 10^{-0.222 \times 1.6990} = 10^{-0.3772} \approx 0.4194$

$$\eta_a \approx 33.34 \times 0.4194 \approx \mathbf{13.98 \; Pa \cdot s}$$

(2 marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Pseudoplasticity Problems:**
> 1. **Sign Error on the Exponent:** Students frequently write $\eta_a = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,1-n}$ instead of $\eta_a = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,n-1}$. The correct exponent is $n-1$ (negative for $n<1$). Marks are deducted in Part B numericals for this.
> 2. **Confusing Pseudoplasticity with Thixotropy:** Part A theory answers that lump the two together lose 1–2 marks. Always mention **time-independence at steady state**.
> 3. **Forgetting Units of $K$:** $K$ has units of Pa·sⁿ — not just Pa·s. KTU examiners specifically check unit correctness in numerical problems.
> 4. **Linear Plot Assumption:** Students often plot $\tau$ vs $\dot{\gamma}$ on linear axes and draw a "curve," losing 1 mark. The straight line is obtained only in **log-log** coordinates.
> 5. **Mixing Up $n < 1$ and $\eta_a$ Trend:** Always state *why* $n<1$ leads to a viscosity decrease (because the exponent $n-1$ becomes negative).

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Pseudoplasticity = Shear Thinning.** Viscosity **decreases** as shear rate increases.
- **Mathematical Signature:** Power-law exponent $n < 1$.
- **Constitutive Equation:** $\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,n}$, with $K$ in Pa·sⁿ.
- **Apparent Viscosity:** $\eta_a = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,n-1}$; decreases monotonically for $n < 1$.
- **Log-Log Linearization:** $\log \tau = \log K + n \log \dot{\gamma}$. Slope of the line gives $n$.
- **Physical Cause:** Disentanglement and alignment of polymer chains in the flow direction; reversible on cessation of shear.
- **Distinguish From:** Thixotropy (time-dependent), Dilatancy ($n > 1$), Bingham plastics (yield stress + Newtonian post-yield).
- **Typical Systems:** Polymer melts, polymer solutions, paints, blood, ketchup.
- **Experimental Determination:** $n$ from log-log slope, $K$ from intercept or back-substitution.
- **Engineering Use:** Die design in extrusion, mold filling simulation, coating flow optimization.
- **Limitation:** Power-law model fails at very low ($\dot{\gamma} \to 0$) and very high ($\dot{\gamma} \to \infty$) shear rates — use the Cross or Carreau model for full-range description (advanced topic, beyond Module 1 scope).
- **Units Vigilance:** Always quote $K$ in Pa·sⁿ, $\eta_a$ in Pa·s, $\dot{\gamma}$ in s⁻¹, $\tau$ in Pa.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dilatancy. Time Dependent Fluids - Thixotropy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPOT402 - POLYMER PHYSICS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Dilatancy. Time Dependent Fluids - Thixotropy # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Dilatancy (Shear Thickening) > [!NOTE] > **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):** > *Dilatancy* is a non-Newtonian flow behavior in which the **apparent viscosity of a fluid increases with an increase in shear rate (γ̇)**. Fluids exhibiting this property are called **dilatant** or **shear-thickening fluids**. In rheological terms, a dilatant fluid resists flow more strongly as more stress is applied. The shear stress grows faster than linearly with the shear rate, giving a **power-law index n > 1**. ### Conceptual Analogy — "The Cornstarch and Water Trick" Imagine mixing cornstarch with a small amount of water. If you stir it slowly with a spoon, the mixture flows smoothly and behaves like a thick liquid. However, if you try to stir it rapidly, hit it with a hammer, or try to run across its surface, the material **suddenly hardens and resists deformation**. This is dilatancy in action — the internal particle arrangement is disturbed, jamming the structure and increasing resistance. Other real-world dilatant examples: - **Wet beach sand** — stepping lightly makes the surface firm; stomping causes it to "lock up" and appear dry. - **Concentrated ceramic suspensions** used in slip casting. - **Polymer melts** above a critical concentration of rigid fillers. > [!IMPORTANT] > In dilatant fluids, the **shear stress (τ) is a super-linear function of shear rate (γ̇)**. Unlike shear-thinning fluids where molecules align easily, in dilatant systems the particles are forced into a more disordered state under stress, increasing hydrodynamic resistance. --- ## 1.2 Thixotropy (Time-Dependent Shear Thinning) > [!NOTE] > **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):** > *Thixotropy* is a **time-dependent non-Newtonian behavior** in which the apparent viscosity of a fluid **decreases progressively with time under constant shear rate**, and is **gradually recovered when the fluid is allowed to rest**. The structural breakdown is reversible. Thixotropy is fundamentally a **structural phenomenon**: a thixotropic fluid has a three-dimensional internal structure (e.g., a gel network, flocculated particles, hydrogen-bonded polymer clusters) that gradually breaks down under shear. When shear is removed, the structure reforms over time, restoring the original viscosity. ### Conceptual Analogy — "Ketchup and Paint" Pick up a bottle of ketchup. It sits on the shelf as a thick, gel-like mass. If you shake or stir it vigorously, it suddenly becomes runny and pours easily. Leave it sitting on the counter for 10 minutes, and it thickens back to its original gel consistency. This reversible, time-dependent breakdown and recovery of structure is **thixotropy**. Other real-world thixotropic examples: - **House paints** — must be fluid when brushed (thinned by shear) but stop running on the wall (regain structure). - **Toothpaste** — flows when squeezed, but holds its shape on the brush. - **Polymer gels, drilling muds, printing inks, and cosmetic creams.** > [!IMPORTANT] > The KTU syllabus explicitly classifies thixotropy under **Time-Dependent Fluids**, distinguishing it from purely *shear-dependent* (time-independent) non-Newtonian behaviors like pseudoplasticity and dilatancy. Time is the *third dimension* in thixotropy — the fluid has a "memory" of its shear history. --- ## 1.3 Why These Matter in Polymer Physics | Behavior | Engineering Importance | |---|---| | **Dilatancy** | Critical in polymer composite processing, slip casting of ceramics, impact-resistant materials design. | | **Thixotropy** | Central to coatings, adhesives, polymer gel processing, injection molding of filled polymers, biomedical gels. | > [!TIP] > **Quick Memory Hook:** **"Dilatancy = More stress, More solid"** and **"Thixotropy = Shear liquefies, Rest rebuilds"**. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Dilatancy — Theoretical Framework ### 2.1.1 Mechanism In a dilatant system (typically a *dense suspension of solid particles in a liquid*): - At **rest**, particles are close-packed with just enough liquid to fill the interstitial gaps. - Under **slow shear**, the liquid acts as a lubricant and the system flows easily. - Under **rapid shear**, particles are forced to move past one another; the voids expand, but liquid cannot fill them fast enough. The result is **drying-out at the particle contacts** and a sharp rise in effective viscosity. The Herschel–Bulkley (generalized power-law) model is the standard representation: $$\tau = \tau_y + K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^n$$ For a pure dilatant fluid with no yield stress, $\tau_y = 0$ and the equation reduces to the Ostwald–de Waele power-law form: $$\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^n \quad ; \quad n > 1$$ where the **apparent viscosity** is: $$\eta_{app} = \dfrac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}} = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$$ Because **n > 1**, increasing $\dot{\gamma}$ makes $\eta_{app}$ increase. ### 2.1.2 Flow Curve Behavior ``` τ | / Dilatant (n>1) | / | / Newtonian (n=1) | / | / Pseudoplastic (n<1) | / | / |/____________________ γ̇ ``` ### 2.1.3 Engineering Reality - Dilatancy is generally **undesirable** in pumping/pipeline operations because it requires more power at high flow rates. - However, it is **desirable in body armor** (e.g., shear-thickening fluid impregnated Kevlar) and in some **anti-slip coatings**. --- ## 2.2 Thixotropy — Theoretical Framework ### 2.2.1 Mechanism A thixotropic fluid is a **two-state system**: 1. **Structured state (gel/network)** → high viscosity 2. **De-structured state (sol)** → low viscosity Under constant shear, the structure parameter $\lambda$ evolves according to a **structural kinetic equation** (Moore / Cheng–Evans model): $$\dfrac{d\lambda}{dt} = k_1 (1 - \lambda) - k_2 \lambda \cdot \dot{\gamma}$$ where: - $\lambda$ = structural parameter (1 = fully structured, 0 = fully broken down) - $k_1$ = rate constant for **build-up** (rest) - $k_2$ = rate constant for **breakdown** (shear) The stress is then related to the structure: $$\tau = (\eta_\infty + (\eta_0 - \eta_\infty) \lambda) \cdot \dot{\gamma}$$ where: - $\eta_0$ = zero-shear viscosity (structured) - $\eta_\infty$ = infinite-shear viscosity (broken down) ### 2.2.2 Hysteresis Loop A thixotropic fluid produces a **hysteresis loop** when the shear rate is cycled up and then down — the **up-curve** (increasing γ̇) lies **above** the **down-curve** (decreasing γ̇). The enclosed **area of the loop is a measure of thixotropy**. ### 2.2.3 Distinction from Other Behaviors | Property | Time-Dependence? | Reversible? | |---|---|---| | **Pseudoplastic (shear-thinning)** | NO | Immediate (within flow time) | | **Dilatant (shear-thickening)** | NO | Immediate | | **Thixotropic** | YES (slow, time-dependent) | YES (gradual recovery at rest) | | **Rheopectic** | YES (time-dependent) | YES (structure *builds* under shear) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Negative thixotropy** (= **Rheopexy**) is the opposite: viscosity *increases* with time under constant shear, and decreases upon resting. Less common in polymers. --- ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Newtonian fluid | $\tau = \eta \dot{\gamma}$ | $\tau$ [Pa], $\eta$ [Pa·s], $\dot{\gamma}$ [s⁻¹] | Baseline | | 2 | Power-law fluid | $\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^{n}$ | $K$ [Pa·sⁿ], $n$ dimensionless | Ostwald–de Waele | | 3 | **Dilatant fluid** | $\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^{n}, \quad n > 1$ | $K$, $n$ | Shear-thickening | | 4 | Pseudoplastic fluid | $\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^{n}, \quad n < 1$ | $K$, $n$ | Shear-thinning | | 5 | Apparent viscosity | $\eta_{app} = \tau / \dot{\gamma} = K \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$ | [Pa·s] | Time-independent fluids | | 6 | Herschel–Bulkley | $\tau = \tau_y + K \dot{\gamma}^{n}$ | $\tau_y$ [Pa] yield stress | With yield stress | | 7 | Bingham plastic | $\tau = \tau_y + \eta_p \dot{\gamma}$ | $\eta_p$ plastic viscosity | $n = 1$ | | 8 | **Thixotropy** (Cheng–Evans) | $\dfrac{d\lambda}{dt} = k_1(1-\lambda) - k_2 \lambda \dot{\gamma}$ | $\lambda \in [0,1]$ | Structural kinetic | | 9 | Thixotropic stress | $\tau = \left[ \eta_\infty + (\eta_0 - \eta_\infty)\lambda \right] \dot{\gamma}$ | $\eta_0, \eta_\infty$ [Pa·s] | $\lambda$ time-evolving | | 10 | Hysteresis loop area | $A = \oint \tau \, d\dot{\gamma}$ | [Pa/s] | Quantifies thixotropy | > [!WARNING] > **Use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute value inside LaTeX inside tables to avoid breaking markdown parsing.** Example: $\eta_{app} = \vert \tau / \dot{\gamma} \vert$. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Apparent Viscosity for a Dilatant Fluid (Power-Law Form) We start from the **Ostwald–de Waele** (power-law) constitutive equation: $$\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n}$$ By definition, the **apparent viscosity** is the ratio of shear stress to shear rate: $$\eta_{app} = \dfrac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}}$$ **Step 1:** Substitute the power-law form of $\tau$ into the expression for $\eta_{app}$. $$\eta_{app} = \dfrac{K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n}}{\dot{\gamma}}$$ **Step 2:** Simplify by using the law of exponents, $\dot{\gamma}^{n}/\dot{\gamma} = \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$. $$\eta_{app} = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,n-1}$$ **Step 3:** Take the natural logarithm of both sides (preparatory for $\log$-log analysis). $$\ln(\eta_{app}) = \ln(K) + (n - 1) \ln(\dot{\gamma})$$ **Step 4:** Interpret the slope. On a $\log \eta_{app}$ vs $\log \dot{\gamma}$ plot, the slope is $(n-1)$. - If $n > 1 \implies$ slope is **positive** $\implies$ $\eta_{app}$ **increases** with $\dot{\gamma}$ → **Dilatant**. - If $n < 1 \implies$ slope is **negative** $\implies$ $\eta_{app}$ **decreases** with $\dot{\gamma}$ → **Pseudoplastic**. - If $n = 1 \implies$ slope is **zero** $\implies$ $\eta_{app} = K = \text{const}$ → **Newtonian**. This derivation justifies why a dilatant fluid must have a power-law index $n > 1$ and how a $\log$-$\log$ plot can be used to extract the value of $n$ experimentally. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Steady-State Structure Parameter for a Thixotropic Fluid The Cheng–Evans structural kinetic equation is: $$\dfrac{d\lambda}{dt} = k_1 (1 - \lambda) - k_2 \lambda \dot{\gamma}$$ **Step 1:** At **steady state**, the structure parameter no longer changes, so $d\lambda/dt = 0$. $$0 = k_1 (1 - \lambda_{ss}) - k_2 \lambda_{ss} \dot{\gamma}$$ **Step 2:** Expand the right-hand side. $$0 = k_1 - k_1 \lambda_{ss} - k_2 \lambda_{ss} \dot{\gamma}$$ **Step 3:** Group the $\lambda_{ss}$ terms. $$k_1 = \lambda_{ss} (k_1 + k_2 \dot{\gamma})$$ **Step 4:** Solve for $\lambda_{ss}$. $$\lambda_{ss} = \dfrac{k_1}{k_1 + k_2 \dot{\gamma}}$$ **Step 5:** Interpret the limits. - As $\dot{\gamma} \to 0$ (very slow shear) $\implies \lambda_{ss} \to 1$ (fully structured). - As $\dot{\gamma} \to \infty$ (very rapid shear) $\implies \lambda_{ss} \to 0$ (fully broken down). - This is precisely the desired thixotropic behavior: high viscosity at rest, low viscosity at high shear. **Step 6:** Substitute $\lambda_{ss}$ into the stress equation. $$\tau = \left[ \eta_\infty + (\eta_0 - \eta_\infty) \cdot \dfrac{k_1}{k_1 + k_2 \dot{\gamma}} \right] \dot{\gamma}$$ **Step 7:** Expand to obtain the working rheological equation. $$\tau = \eta_\infty \dot{\gamma} + \dfrac{(\eta_0 - \eta_\infty) \, k_1 \, \dot{\gamma}}{k_1 + k_2 \dot{\gamma}}$$ This equation is the **theoretical rheogram of a thixotropic fluid at steady state** and matches experimental observations: it transitions from $\eta_0 \dot{\gamma}$ at low shear to $\eta_\infty \dot{\gamma}$ at high shear. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Behavior Comparison | Property | Dilatant | Thixotropic | |---|---|---| | **Time dependence** | None (steady-state only) | Yes (explicit time dependence) | | **Shear rate dependence** | Yes ($\eta_{app} \uparrow$ as $\dot{\gamma} \uparrow$) | Yes, mediated by $\lambda(t)$ | | **Yield stress** | Usually none | Often present (Herschel–Bulkley) | | **Mechanism** | Hydrodynamic jamming, particle re-arrangement | Reversible breakdown of network structure | | **Examples** | Wet sand, cornstarch+water, dense slurries | Paint, ketchup, polymer gels, drilling mud | | **Governing parameter** | $n > 1$ | $\lambda(t)$, $k_1$, $k_2$ | | **Hysteresis** | No (single valued flow curve) | Yes (loop area = thixotropic measure) | | **Rheological equation** | $\tau = K\dot{\gamma}^{n}, n>1$ | $\dfrac{d\lambda}{dt} = k_1(1-\lambda) - k_2\lambda\dot{\gamma}$ | | **Engineering use** | Body armor, anti-slip, impact protection | Coatings, adhesives, injection molding | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Conceptual Flow Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Polymer / Suspension System] --> B{Suspension Type} B -- "Loose, lubricated" --> C[Dilatant Fluid n greater than 1] B -- "Structured network" --> D[Thixotropic Fluid] C --> C1[Apply Slow Shear] C --> C2[Apply Fast Shear] C1 --> C3[Particles glide
low viscosity] C2 --> C4[Hydrodynamic jamming
voids expand
liquid cannot refill] C4 --> C5[Apparent viscosity rises sharply] C5 --> C6[Material behaves like solid] D --> D1[At Rest] D --> D2[Under Shear] D1 --> D3[Structure lambda equals 1
network intact
high viscosity eta zero] D2 --> D4[Structure breaks down
lambda decreases over time] D4 --> D5[Apparent viscosity drops to eta infinity] D5 --> D6[Remove Shear] D6 --> D7[Structure rebuilds
lambda recovers to 1
viscosity returns to eta zero] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Rheological Classification Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD R[Fluid Rheology] --> N[Newtonian] R --> NN[Non-Newtonian] N --> N1[tau equals eta gamma dot
constant viscosity] NN --> T[Time-Independent] NN --> TD[Time-Dependent] T --> PS[Pseudoplastic n less than 1] T --> DI[Dilatant n greater than 1] T --> YP[Yield-Stress Fluids
Bingham and Herschel-Bulkley] TD --> TH[Thixotropic
viscosity drops with time under shear] TD --> RH[Rheopectic
viscosity rises with time under shear] DI --> DI1[Wet sand, dense slurries] TH --> TH1[Paints, gels, ketchup] PS --> PS1[Polymer melts, blood] ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Thixotropy Time Evolution ```mermaid flowchart LR T0[Time t equals 0
Fresh at rest] --> T1[Apply Constant Shear] T1 --> T2[Structure breaks
lambda drops] T2 --> T3[Apparent viscosity decreases] T3 --> T4[Steady State reached
lambda equals k1 over k1 plus k2 gamma dot] T4 --> T5[Remove Shear] T5 --> T6[Build-up phase begins] T6 --> T7[lambda recovers to 1] T7 --> T8[Original viscosity restored] ``` > [!TIP] > These block diagrams replace physical drawings of microstructures (which are difficult to render in Mermaid) with a clear **functional architecture** that a KTU examiner will accept as a schematic representation of the flow phenomena. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]` **Define dilatancy. Give two examples of dilatant materials.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Dilatancy is a non-Newtonian flow behavior in which the **apparent viscosity of a fluid increases with increasing shear rate**. The power-law index $n > 1$, indicating that the fluid becomes more resistant to flow as it is sheared more rapidly. (2 marks) **Examples:** (any two, ½ mark each) 1. **Wet sand** on a beach — light footsteps cause it to feel solid, but vigorous stomping makes it appear "dry" as the water between grains cannot flow into the expanding voids. 2. **Concentrated cornstarch-in-water suspension** — flows under slow stirring but solidifies on sudden impact. --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]` **What is thixotropy? Why is it important in paint formulation?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Thixotropy is a **time-dependent, reversible non-Newtonian behavior** in which the apparent viscosity of a fluid **decreases progressively with time under constant shear** and is gradually recovered when the fluid is allowed to rest. The breakdown and rebuilding of an internal three-dimensional structure (gel network, flocs, hydrogen-bonded polymer clusters) is responsible for this behavior. (2 marks) **Importance in paint:** A thixotropic paint is fluid when brushed (shear thins for easy application and leveling) but thickens at rest on the wall, preventing drips and runs. This is achieved by incorporating thixotropic agents such as **fumed silica, organoclays, or modified castor-oil derivatives**. (1 mark) --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - 2024 Scheme Model - ESE Module 1 Choice A]` **(a)** Explain the phenomenon of **dilatancy** in non-Newtonian fluids with a neat labeled flow curve. Derive the expression for the apparent viscosity of a power-law fluid and state the condition for dilatancy. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss the structural-kinetic model of **thixotropy** and derive the expression for the steady-state structure parameter. Comment on the limits at very low and very high shear rates. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution to Q.A(a) — Dilatancy (7 marks)** **Step 1 — Definition (1 mark):** Dilatancy (also called **shear-thickening**) is the non-Newtonian behavior in which the apparent viscosity of a fluid **increases** as the shear rate increases. It is described by the Ostwald–de Waele power-law model with index $n > 1$. **Step 2 — Flow Curve (2 marks):** On a plot of shear stress ($\tau$) versus shear rate ($\dot{\gamma}$): ``` tau | / | / Dilatant n greater than 1 | / | / Newtonian n equals 1 | / | / Pseudoplastic n less than 1 | / | / |________/________________ gamma-dot ``` A dilatant fluid shows a **concave-upward** flow curve — the stress rises *faster* than linearly with shear rate. Compare with pseudoplastic (concave-down) and Newtonian (straight line). **Step 3 — Derivation of Apparent Viscosity (3 marks):** The power-law constitutive equation is: $$\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n}$$ Apparent viscosity is defined as: $$\eta_{app} = \dfrac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}}$$ Substituting: $$\eta_{app} = \dfrac{K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n}}{\dot{\gamma}} = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,n-1}$$ Taking logarithms: $$\ln(\eta_{app}) = \ln(K) + (n-1) \ln(\dot{\gamma})$$ **Step 4 — Condition for Dilatancy (1 mark):** For the apparent viscosity to **increase** with shear rate, the exponent $(n - 1)$ must be positive: $$n - 1 > 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad n > 1$$ Hence, **$n > 1$ is the necessary and sufficient condition for dilatancy**. --- #### **Model Solution to Q.A(b) — Thixotropy Structural Model (7 marks)** **Step 1 — Concept (1.5 marks):** Thixotropy is a **time-dependent, reversible** shear-thinning behavior. The fluid has a 3D internal structure (gel, floc, network) characterized by a scalar **structure parameter $\lambda$**, where $\lambda = 1$ means fully structured and $\lambda = 0$ means fully broken. **Step 2 — Cheng–Evans Model (2 marks):** The structural evolution is governed by: $$\dfrac{d\lambda}{dt} = k_1 (1 - \lambda) - k_2 \lambda \dot{\gamma}$$ - The first term $k_1(1-\lambda)$ represents **structural build-up** (dominates at rest). - The second term $k_2 \lambda \dot{\gamma}$ represents **structural breakdown** (dominates under shear). **Step 3 — Derivation of Steady-State $\lambda_{ss}$ (2.5 marks):** At steady state, $d\lambda/dt = 0$: $$0 = k_1(1 - \lambda_{ss}) - k_2 \lambda_{ss} \dot{\gamma}$$ Expanding: $$0 = k_1 - k_1 \lambda_{ss} - k_2 \lambda_{ss} \dot{\gamma}$$ $$k_1 = \lambda_{ss}(k_1 + k_2 \dot{\gamma})$$ $$\boxed{\lambda_{ss} = \dfrac{k_1}{k_1 + k_2 \dot{\gamma}}}$$ **Step 4 — Limiting Behavior (1 mark):** - **Low shear limit** ($\dot{\gamma} \to 0$): $\lambda_{ss} \to 1$ → fully structured, viscosity $\to \eta_0$. - **High shear limit** ($\dot{\gamma} \to \infty$): $\lambda_{ss} \to 0$ → fully broken, viscosity $\to \eta_\infty$. This captures the essential **time-dependent transition from high to low viscosity under shear** and the **gradual recovery at rest** — the hallmark of a thixotropic material. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Internal Choice Alternative* `[KTU University Exam - 2024 Scheme Model - ESE Module 1 Choice B]` **(a)** Distinguish clearly between **dilatancy and thixotropy** with reference to their (i) time dependence, (ii) underlying mechanism, (iii) governing equation, and (iv) two practical examples each. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A dilatant fluid is described by the power-law model with consistency $K = 12 \, \text{Pa·s}^n$ and power-law index $n = 1.4$. Calculate the **shear stress** and the **apparent viscosity** when the fluid is sheared at $\dot{\gamma} = 50 \, \text{s}^{-1}$. Comment on whether the fluid is dilatant. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution to Q.B(a) — Comparison Table (7 marks)** | Aspect | Dilatancy | Thixotropy | |---|---|---| | **(i) Time dependence** | Time-independent (steady-state flow curve) | Time-dependent (kinetic evolution) | | **(ii) Mechanism** | Hydrodynamic jamming, particle re-arrangement under fast shear; liquid cannot refill expanding voids | Reversible breakdown of a 3D gel/network structure; rebuilds at rest | | **(iii) Governing equation** | $\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^{n}, \ n > 1$ | $d\lambda/dt = k_1(1-\lambda) - k_2 \lambda \dot{\gamma}$ | | **(iv) Examples** (any two) | Wet sand, cornstarch suspension, concentrated ceramic slurries | Paint, ketchup, polymer gels, drilling mud, toothpaste | (1.5 marks per row + 1 mark for stating examples correctly) --- #### **Model Solution to Q.B(b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)** **Given:** $$K = 12 \, \text{Pa·s}^n, \quad n = 1.4, \quad \dot{\gamma} = 50 \, \text{s}^{-1}$$ **Step 1 — Compute $\dot{\gamma}^{\,n}$ (2 marks):** $$\dot{\gamma}^{\,n} = 50^{1.4}$$ We use logarithms to compute this: $$\log_{10}(50^{1.4}) = 1.4 \times \log_{10}(50) = 1.4 \times 1.6990 = 2.3786$$ $$50^{1.4} = 10^{2.3786} \approx 239.0 \, \text{s}^{-1.4}$$ **Step 2 — Compute Shear Stress $\tau$ (2 marks):** $$\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,n} = 12 \times 239.0$$ $$\boxed{\tau \approx 2868 \, \text{Pa} \approx 2.87 \, \text{kPa}}$$ **Step 3 — Compute Apparent Viscosity (2 marks):** $$\eta_{app} = \dfrac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}} = \dfrac{2868}{50} = 57.36 \, \text{Pa·s}$$ Or equivalently: $$\eta_{app} = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,n-1} = 12 \times 50^{0.4}$$ $\log_{10}(50^{0.4}) = 0.4 \times 1.699 = 0.6796 \Rightarrow 50^{0.4} \approx 4.78$ $$\eta_{app} = 12 \times 4.78 \approx 57.4 \, \text{Pa·s} \quad \checkmark$$ **Step 4 — Verify Dilatancy (1 mark):** Since the power-law index $n = 1.4 > 1$, the fluid is **dilatant (shear-thickening)**. The apparent viscosity ($57.4$ Pa·s) is much larger than $K$ ($12$ Pa·s) at this elevated shear rate, confirming the shear-thickening nature. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:** > 1. **Mixing up dilatancy with pseudoplasticity** — Students often confuse shear-thickening with shear-thinning. Remember: dilatancy is **n > 1**, pseudoplasticity is **n < 1**. > 2. **Confusing thixotropy with pseudoplasticity** — Both involve decreasing viscosity, but only thixotropy has *explicit time dependence*. The hysteresis loop is the dead giveaway. > 3. **Forgetting units in power-law $K$** — The unit of $K$ is **Pa·sⁿ**, not Pa·s, because $n \ne 1$ for non-Newtonian fluids. Loss of 1 mark in numerical problems. > 4. **Skipping the steady-state assumption in the thixotropy derivation** — Examiners expect the step "set $d\lambda/dt = 0$" to be explicitly written. > 5. **Drawing flow curves without axes labels and units** — Always label $\tau$ in Pa and $\dot{\gamma}$ in s⁻¹. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Dilatancy** = shear-thickening; $\eta_{app}$ **increases** with $\dot{\gamma}$; characterized by $n > 1$ in power-law. - **Apparent viscosity formula** (power-law): $\eta_{app} = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$. - **Thixotropy** = **time-dependent**, reversible, shear-thinning; structure breaks under shear, rebuilds at rest. - **Thixotropy is NOT pseudoplasticity** — the time dimension is essential. - **Cheng–Evans structural equation**: $\dfrac{d\lambda}{dt} = k_1(1-\lambda) - k_2 \lambda \dot{\gamma}$, with $\lambda \in [0,1]$. - **Steady-state structure parameter**: $\lambda_{ss} = \dfrac{k_1}{k_1 + k_2 \dot{\gamma}}$. - **Hysteresis loop area** quantifies thixotropy: $A = \oint \tau \, d\dot{\gamma}$. - **Examples to remember**: dilatancy → wet sand, cornstarch+water; thixotropy → paint, ketchup, polymer gels. - **Rheopexy** (negative thixotropy) = viscosity *increases* with time under shear; opposite of thixotropy. - **Log-log plot** of $\tau$ vs $\dot{\gamma}$ has slope $n$; intercept gives $\log K$. - **Newtonian fluid**: $n = 1$, $\eta_{app} = K = \text{const}$. - **Yield stress fluids** (Bingham, Herschel–Bulkley) need a critical $\tau_y$ before flow begins. - **Engineering applications** of thixotropy: paints, inks, drilling muds, polymer gels, injection molding. - **Engineering applications** of dilatancy: body armor, anti-slip surfaces, impact-resistant composites. > [!IMPORTANT] > **One-Line Memory Cheat:** > *"Dilatancy — shear makes it harder; Thixotropy — shear unmakes it, rest remakes it."* ---

topics.title: Rheopexy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT402 - POLYMER PHYSICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Rheopexy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Rheopexy** (also known as **anti-thixotropy** or **negative thixotropy**) is a time-dependent rheological phenomenon exhibited by certain non-Newtonian fluids in which the **apparent viscosity increases progressively with time** when the fluid is subjected to a **constant rate of shear** (or constant shear stress). It is fundamentally the temporal mirror image of thixotropy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Standardized):**
> *Rheopexy is the time-dependent increase in viscosity at a constant shear rate, arising from shear-induced structural build-up (rather than breakdown) within the fluid microstructure. Upon cessation of shear, the structure progressively recovers, often with a time constant similar to that observed during the build-up phase.*

Mathematically, if a fluid at rest has an instantaneous viscosity $\eta_0$, and at time $t$ under constant shear rate $\dot{\gamma}$ its viscosity is $\eta(t,\dot{\gamma})$, then for a **rheopexic fluid**:

$$\frac{\partial \eta(t,\dot{\gamma})}{\partial t} \bigg|_{\dot{\gamma} = \text{const}} > 0$$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine a thick cornstarch–water slurry resting in a bowl. If you gently, continuously stir it in **one direction with a slow, steady motion**, the slurry seems to *thicken and stiffen* the longer you stir — eventually resisting your spoon. If you stop and let it rest, it slowly relaxes back to its fluid state. This **shear-induced build-up of structure over time** is exactly rheopexy.

> [!NOTE]
> **Everyday Engineering Examples of Rheopexy:**
> - **Synovial fluid** in human joints (becomes more viscous during sustained joint motion).
> - **Certain drilling muds** that gel under continued low shear in oilfield operations.
> - **Some surfactant solutions** (e.g., CTAB/NaSal wormlike micelles) that align and entangle under gentle shear.
> - **Bentonite clay suspensions** at specific pH and salt concentrations.
> - **Fresh cement paste** during the early stages of hydration under continuous mixing.

### 1.3 Key Distinguishing Metrics

- **Characteristic build-up time ($\tau_b$):** Time required for viscosity to reach ~63% of its steady-state value under constant shear.
- **Equilibrium viscosity ($\eta_\infty$):** Final, time-independent viscosity at long times under sustained shear.
- **Equilibrium structural parameter ($\lambda_\infty$):** Fraction of fully built structure at steady state, where $\lambda_\infty \in [0,1]$.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Microstructural Origin — The "Why" Behind Rheopexy

Rheopexy arises when **applied shear actively promotes the formation of structure**, rather than disrupting it. The dominant microstructural mechanisms are:

1. **Shear-induced entanglement of long polymer/surfactant chains.**
   Gentle, sustained shear brings chains into proximity, increasing physical crosslinks and entanglements.

2. **Shear-induced aggregation of particles.**
   Low-to-moderate shear drives colloidal particles into ordered arrays (e.g., strings, sheets, or 3D networks) — the so-called **orthokinetic aggregation** regime.

3. **Alignment-assisted hydrogen bonding.**
   In systems with reversible associative groups (e.g., H-bonds, ionic associations), shear aligns molecules to optimize inter-chain bonding sites.

4. **Wormlike micellar growth.**
   In certain surfactant systems, gentle shear elongates micelles into longer, more entangled wormlike structures, dramatically increasing viscosity.

### 2.2 Rheopexy vs. Thixotropy vs. Rheopectic vs. Viscoelasticity

| Property | Thixotropy | **Rheopexy** | Viscoelasticity |
|---|---|---|---|
| Viscosity change with time at constant $\dot{\gamma}$ | **Decreases** | **Increases** | Recovers after stress removal |
| Structural response to shear | Break-down | **Build-up** | Stores + dissipates energy |
| Recovery on shear removal | Slow rebuild | Slow decay | Instantaneous + delayed |
| Typical systems | Paint, ketchup, drilling muds | **Synovial fluid, certain micellar gels** | Polymer melts, polymer solutions |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes all critical equations for rheopexy that KTU examiners typically test. All symbols and their **SI units** are documented to prevent any ambiguity in board examinations.

| # | Equation | Description of Variables | Applicability / Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\eta(t,\dot{\gamma}) = \eta_\infty + (\eta_0 - \eta_\infty)\,e^{-t/\tau_b}$ | $\eta_0$: initial viscosity (Pa·s); $\eta_\infty$: equilibrium viscosity (Pa·s); $\tau_b$: build-up time constant (s) | First-order **exponential approach** to equilibrium; valid for **constant $\dot{\gamma}$** in the linear response regime |
| 2 | $\dfrac{d\lambda}{dt} = k_1 (1-\lambda) + k_2 \dot{\gamma}\,\lambda$ | $\lambda$: structural parameter ($\lambda \in [0,1]$); $k_1$: rate constant for spontaneous build-up (s$^{-1}$); $k_2$: shear-induced build-up rate constant (Pa$^{-1}$) | Structural kinetics model for rheopexy (positive sign on shear term differentiates from thixotropy) |
| 3 | $\lambda(t) = \lambda_\infty \bigl(1 - e^{-(k_1+k_2\dot{\gamma})t}\bigr)$ | $\lambda_\infty = \dfrac{k_1}{k_1+k_2\dot{\gamma}}$: equilibrium structure fraction | Solution to Eq. (2) for $\lambda(0)=0$ |
| 4 | $\tau = \bigl[\eta_\infty + (\eta_0 - \eta_\infty)\,e^{-t/\tau_b}\bigr]\,\dot{\gamma}$ | $\tau$: shear stress (Pa); $\dot{\gamma}$: shear rate (s$^{-1}$) | Shear stress response of a rheopexic fluid under constant $\dot{\gamma}$ |
| 5 | $\tau_b = \dfrac{1}{k_1 + k_2 \dot{\gamma}}$ | $\tau_b$: build-up time constant (s) | Inverse of the effective rate constant in the structural kinetic model |
| 6 | $\eta_{\text{rel}} = \dfrac{\eta(t,\dot{\gamma})}{\eta_\infty}$ | $\eta_{\text{rel}}$: relative (dimensionless) viscosity | Used for normalization in oscillatory shear experiments |

> [!WARNING]
> **Units check (mandatory before any numerical problem):**
> - Shear rate $\dot{\gamma}$ → **s$^{-1}$**
> - Shear stress $\tau$ → **Pa = N·m$^{-2}$**
> - Viscosity $\eta$ → **Pa·s**
> - Rate constants: $k_1$ in **s$^{-1}$**, $k_2$ in **Pa$^{-1}$** (so that $k_2\dot{\gamma}$ is dimensionless per unit time)

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Biomedical Engineering:** Designing **synovial fluid mimics** for osteoarthritis treatment and prosthetic joint lubricants requires precise characterization of rheopexy.
- **Petroleum Engineering:** **Drilling fluids** are engineered to be rheopexic so they rapidly gel when circulation stops, preventing wellbore collapse.
- **Food & Pharmaceutical Industry:** Predicting **time-dependent behavior** of sauces, creams, and topical gels during processing and storage.
- **Cosmetics:** Rheopexy governs the "feel" of lotions and conditioners during application — initial fluidity, then thickening upon rubbing.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Structural Parameter Evolution for a Rheopexic Fluid

**Starting Assumption.** Let $\lambda(t)$ denote the fraction of *fully built* structure at time $t$. By definition, $\lambda(t) \in [0,1]$, where $\lambda = 0$ corresponds to a completely broken-down (Newtonian-like) state, and $\lambda = 1$ corresponds to the fully structured state.

**Step 1 — Write the rate of structural build-up.**
The rate of change $d\lambda/dt$ depends on two competing processes:
- **Spontaneous (thermal) build-up** at rate $k_1 (1-\lambda)$: proportional to the *unstructured* fraction $(1-\lambda)$.
- **Shear-induced build-up** at rate $k_2 \dot{\gamma}\,\lambda$ for rheopexy: shear actively *promotes* further structuring, with magnitude proportional to both the applied shear rate $\dot{\gamma}$ and the existing structure $\lambda$.

$$
\frac{d\lambda}{dt} = k_1 (1-\lambda) + k_2\,\dot{\gamma}\,\lambda
$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange into standard linear ODE form.**
Move all $\lambda$ terms to one side:

$$
\frac{d\lambda}{dt} = k_1 + (k_2 \dot{\gamma} - k_1)\,\lambda
$$

Let $a = k_2 \dot{\gamma} - k_1$. Then:

$$
\frac{d\lambda}{dt} - a\,\lambda = k_1
$$

**Step 3 — Identify the integrating factor.**
The integrating factor is $\mu(t) = e^{-a t}$. Multiply both sides:

$$
e^{-a t}\,\frac{d\lambda}{dt} - a\,e^{-a t}\,\lambda = k_1 e^{-a t}
$$

The left-hand side is exactly:

$$
\frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\lambda\,e^{-a t}\right) = k_1 e^{-a t}
$$

**Step 4 — Integrate from $0$ to $t$.**
Applying initial condition $\lambda(0) = \lambda_0$:

$$
\lambda(t)\,e^{-a t} - \lambda_0 = k_1 \int_0^t e^{-a t'}\, dt' = \frac{k_1}{-a}\left(e^{-a t} - 1\right)
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $\lambda(t)$.**
Multiply by $e^{a t}$:

$$
\lambda(t) = \lambda_0\,e^{a t} + \frac{k_1}{a}\left(1 - e^{a t}\right)
$$

Rearrange:

$$
\lambda(t) = \frac{k_1}{a} + \left(\lambda_0 - \frac{k_1}{a}\right) e^{a t}
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute back $a = k_2 \dot{\gamma} - k_1$.**
After simplification (assuming $\lambda_0 = 0$, i.e., a fully broken structure initially):

$$
\lambda(t) = \frac{k_1}{k_1 - k_2 \dot{\gamma}}\left(1 - e^{-(k_1 - k_2\dot{\gamma}) t}\right)
$$

**Step 7 — For rheopexy, the structure must grow, so $k_1 > k_2 \dot{\gamma}$ (small shear limit), giving a real, positive exponent.** This gives:

$$
\boxed{\;\lambda(t) = \frac{k_1}{k_1 - k_2 \dot{\gamma}}\left(1 - e^{-(k_1 - k_2\dot{\gamma}) t}\right)\;}
$$

### 3.2 Worked Example: Build-Up Time Constant

**Problem.** A rheopexic fluid has $k_1 = 0.05$ s$^{-1}$ and $k_2 = 0.001$ Pa$^{-1}$. It is subjected to a constant shear rate of $\dot{\gamma} = 10$ s$^{-1}$. Calculate the build-up time constant $\tau_b$.

**Step 1 — Apply the formula.**

$$
\tau_b = \frac{1}{k_1 - k_2 \dot{\gamma}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**

$$
\tau_b = \frac{1}{0.05 - (0.001)(10)} = \frac{1}{0.05 - 0.01} = \frac{1}{0.04}
$$

**Step 3 — Final numerical result.**

$$
\boxed{\;\tau_b = 25\ \text{s}\;}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** After ~25 s of continuous shearing, the fluid's structure has reached ~63% of its equilibrium level. After ~$5\tau_b$ = 125 s, it has reached >99% — effectively its steady-state viscosity.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Classification of Time-Dependent Fluids

| Phenomenon | Sign of $d\eta/dt$ at const. $\dot{\gamma}$ | Sign of $k_2\dot{\gamma}$ relative to $k_1$ | Equilibrium reached? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Thixotropic | $< 0$ | $k_2\dot{\gamma} < 0$ (effective breakdown) | Yes, at $\eta_\infty < \eta_0$ |
| Rheopexic | $> 0$ | $0 < k_2\dot{\gamma} < k_1$ | Yes, at $\eta_\infty > \eta_0$ |
| Rheopexic (extreme) | $> 0$ (diverging) | $k_2\dot{\gamma} \to k_1$ | $\tau_b \to \infty$ (no equilibrium) |
| Time-independent non-Newtonian | $= 0$ | $k_2 = 0$ | Instantaneous |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: Microstructural Evolution During Rheopexy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Rest State: Lambda equals 0] --> nodeB[Apply Constant Shear Rate]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Shear Induced Particle Alignment]
    nodeB --> nodeD[Shear Induced Chain Entanglement]
    nodeB --> nodeE[Shear Induced Micellar Growth]
    nodeC --> nodeF[Structural Build Up Begins]
    nodeD --> nodeF
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG[Lambda increasing with time]
    nodeG --> nodeH{Equilibrium Reached}
    nodeH -->|Yes| nodeI[Lambda equals Lambda_infty]
    nodeH -->|No| nodeG
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Stop Shear]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Gradual Structural Decay]
    nodeK --> nodeA
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Architecture: Rheopexy as a Functional System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputA[Input: Constant Shear Rate gamma dot] --> proc1[Block A: Shear Field Application]
    proc1 --> proc2[Block B: Microstructural Reorganization]
    proc2 --> proc3[Block C: Structural Parameter Lambda t Computation]
    proc3 --> proc4[Block D: Viscosity Update eta t]
    proc4 --> proc5{Steady State Check}
    proc5 -->|Not reached| proc1
    proc5 -->|Reached| output1[Output: Equilibrium Viscosity eta_infty]
    proc4 --> outA[Output: Time Dependent Viscosity eta t gamma dot]
```

### 4.3 Viscosity–Time Curve (Descriptive Schematic)

```
Viscosity η (Pa·s)
    ▲
    │                              ●●●●●●●  ← η_∞ (equilibrium, rheopexic)
    │                          ●●●
    │                       ●●
    │                    ●●
    │                 ●●
    │              ●●      ← η(t) rising exponentially
    │           ●●
    │         ●
    │       ●
    │     ●
    │   ●
    │ ●  ← η_0 (initial, after pre-shear)
    │
    └────────────────────────────────────────► Time t (s)
              0        τ_b       2τ_b      3τ_b ...
```

**Reading the curve:** The fluid's viscosity rises from $\eta_0$ (the broken-down state at $t=0$) and asymptotically approaches $\eta_\infty > \eta_0$ as time progresses. The constant $\tau_b$ is the e-folding time for build-up.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]**
**Define rheopexy. How is it distinguished from thixotropy at the molecular level?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Rheopexy is the time-dependent **increase** in viscosity of certain non-Newtonian fluids when subjected to a **constant shear rate**, due to shear-induced structural build-up within the fluid. It is also termed *anti-thixotropy* or *negative thixotropy*.

**Molecular distinction (1 mark):** In thixotropy, applied shear progressively **disrupts** the existing microstructure (e.g., breaking flocs, disentangling polymer chains). In rheopexy, gentle sustained shear **promotes** microstructural build-up — through entanglements, orthokinetic aggregation, or wormlike micellar growth — leading to a viscosity increase with time.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary Exam]**
**State the mathematical condition that identifies a fluid as rheopexic under constant shear rate.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

For a fluid subjected to a constant shear rate $\dot{\gamma}$, the fluid is classified as **rheopexic** if and only if its apparent viscosity $\eta(t,\dot{\gamma})$ is a **monotonically increasing function of time**:

$$
\frac{\partial \eta(t,\dot{\gamma})}{\partial t} \bigg|_{\dot{\gamma} = \text{const}} > 0
$$

Equivalently, using the structural parameter $\lambda(t)$:

$$
\frac{d\lambda}{dt} = k_1(1-\lambda) + k_2 \dot{\gamma}\,\lambda \quad \text{with} \quad k_2 > 0
$$

The positive sign on the shear-induced term is the **defining mathematical signature** of rheopexy.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** [7 marks] Derive the time evolution of the structural parameter $\lambda(t)$ for a rheopexic fluid obeying the structural kinetics model:

$$
\frac{d\lambda}{dt} = k_1(1-\lambda) + k_2 \dot{\gamma}\,\lambda
$$

with initial condition $\lambda(0) = 0$. Clearly identify the conditions under which the fluid exhibits finite-time equilibrium.

**(b)** [7 marks] For a rheopexic fluid, the apparent viscosity is related to the structural parameter by:

$$
\eta(t,\dot{\gamma}) = \eta_\infty + (\eta_0 - \eta_\infty)\,e^{-t/\tau_b}
$$

If $\eta_0 = 1.5$ Pa·s, $\eta_\infty = 6.0$ Pa·s, and $\tau_b = 30$ s, calculate (i) the viscosity after 60 s, (ii) the time required for viscosity to reach 5.0 Pa·s, and (iii) the percentage of equilibrium structure built up at $t = \tau_b$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** [7 marks] Compare and contrast rheopexy with thixotropy in terms of: (i) microstructural origin, (ii) hysteresis loop direction in a shear stress vs. shear rate plot, and (iii) typical real-world examples. Provide diagrams where appropriate.

**(b)** [7 marks] The build-up time constant of a rheopexic fluid is given by $\tau_b = 1/(k_1 - k_2\dot{\gamma})$. Given $k_1 = 0.08$ s$^{-1}$, $k_2 = 5 \times 10^{-4}$ Pa$^{-1}$, and $\dot{\gamma} = 50$ s$^{-1}$, determine: (i) $\tau_b$, (ii) the equilibrium structural parameter $\lambda_\infty$, and (iii) the maximum shear rate at which the fluid remains rheopexic (i.e., $\tau_b$ remains finite and positive).

---

### Model Solutions

#### **Question A — Model Solution**

**Part (a) — Full Derivation (7 marks):**

Following the derivation in Section 3.1 above, start with the structural kinetics ODE:

$$
\frac{d\lambda}{dt} = k_1(1-\lambda) + k_2 \dot{\gamma}\,\lambda
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\frac{d\lambda}{dt} = k_1 + (k_2 \dot{\gamma} - k_1)\,\lambda
$$

Define $a = k_2\dot{\gamma} - k_1$. The ODE becomes:

$$
\frac{d\lambda}{dt} - a\lambda = k_1
$$

Solving with integrating factor $e^{-at}$ and initial condition $\lambda(0) = 0$:

$$
\lambda(t) = \frac{k_1}{a}\left(e^{at} - 1\right)
$$

Substituting back $a = k_2\dot{\gamma} - k_1$:

$$
\boxed{\;\lambda(t) = \frac{k_1}{k_2\dot{\gamma} - k_1}\left(e^{(k_2\dot{\gamma} - k_1)t} - 1\right)\;}
$$

For equilibrium (finite $\tau_b$ and bounded $\lambda_\infty$), the exponent must be **negative**, requiring:

$$
k_2 \dot{\gamma} - k_1 < 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \dot{\gamma} < \frac{k_1}{k_2}
$$

This is the **maximum shear rate** for rheopexic behavior. Beyond this, $\tau_b$ becomes negative — unphysical — and the structure diverges (catastrophic gelation).

Equilibrium structural parameter:

$$
\lambda_\infty = \lim_{t \to \infty} \lambda(t) = \frac{k_1}{k_1 - k_2 \dot{\gamma}}
$$

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Viscosity at $t = 60$ s:**

$$
\eta(60) = 6.0 + (1.5 - 6.0)\,e^{-60/30} = 6.0 + (-4.5)\,e^{-2}
$$

Since $e^{-2} \approx 0.1353$:

$$
\eta(60) = 6.0 - 4.5 \times 0.1353 = 6.0 - 0.609 = \boxed{5.391\ \text{Pa·s}}
$$

**(ii) Time to reach $\eta = 5.0$ Pa·s:**

$$
5.0 = 6.0 - 4.5\,e^{-t/30}
$$

$$
4.5\,e^{-t/30} = 1.0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad e^{-t/30} = \frac{1}{4.5}
$$

$$
t = -30 \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{4.5}\right) = 30 \ln(4.5) = 30 \times 1.5041 = \boxed{45.12\ \text{s}}
$$

**(iii) Percentage built up at $t = \tau_b$:**

At $t = \tau_b$, the exponential factor is $e^{-1} \approx 0.3679$. The fractional build-up:

$$
\frac{\eta(\tau_b) - \eta_0}{\eta_\infty - \eta_0} = \frac{6.0 - 4.5\,(0.1353) - 1.5}{6.0 - 1.5} = \frac{4.5 - 0.609}{4.5} = \frac{3.891}{4.5}
$$

$$
\boxed{\approx 86.5\%}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. **Sign error in the exponent:** Students frequently write $e^{+t/\tau_b}$ instead of $e^{-t/\tau_b}$ for a *rising* viscosity. This yields physically wrong *decay* behavior and costs the entire 7 marks.
> 2. **Unit conversion errors:** Forgetting to convert shear rate from rpm to s$^{-1}$ when given in rotational units. Recall: $1\ \text{rpm} = (2\pi/60)\ \text{s}^{-1}$.
> 3. **Confusion between thixotropy and rheopexy equations:** The structural kinetics ODE differs *only* in the sign of the shear term. A sign flip in this term completely changes the physical phenomenon — graders will deduct 2–3 marks for this error.
> 4. **Missing boundary condition:** When solving the ODE, students often forget $\lambda(0) = 0$ and write the *general solution*, losing the integration constant and 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Numerical rounding:** Always retain at least 4 significant figures in intermediate steps to avoid propagation errors in the final answer.

---

#### **Question B — Model Solution**

**Part (a) — Comparative Analysis (7 marks):**

| Feature | Thixotropy | **Rheopexy** |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Microstructural origin | Shear **breaks down** microstructure (flocs, entanglements, aggregates) | Shear **builds up** microstructure (entanglements, orthokinetic aggregation, micellar growth) |
| (ii) Hysteresis loop | Clockwise (up-curve below down-curve) | **Counter-clockwise** (up-curve above down-curve) |
| (iii) Examples | Paint, ketchup, tomato sauce, drilling mud | **Synovial fluid, certain micellar gels, fresh cement paste** |

*Hysteresis loop sketch (descriptive):*

```
Shear Stress τ
    ▲
    │       /●        ← Up-curve (decreasing η)
    │      / ●●       ← Down-curve higher than up-curve
    │     /  ●●       (THIXOTROPY)
    │    /  ●●
    │   / ●●
    │  /●●
    │ /●
    │/________________► Shear Rate γ̇

Shear Stress τ
    ▲
    │      ●●\        ← Down-curve (decreasing η)
    │     ●●  \       ← Up-curve higher than down-curve
    │    ●●    \      (RHEOPEXY)
    │   ●●      \
    │  ●●        \
    │ ●●          \
    │●_____________► Shear Rate γ̇
```

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $k_1 = 0.08$ s$^{-1}$, $k_2 = 5 \times 10^{-4}$ Pa$^{-1}$, $\dot{\gamma} = 50$ s$^{-1}$.

**(i) Build-up time constant:**

$$
\tau_b = \frac{1}{k_1 - k_2 \dot{\gamma}} = \frac{1}{0.08 - (5 \times 10^{-4})(50)} = \frac{1}{0.08 - 0.025} = \frac{1}{0.055}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\tau_b \approx 18.18\ \text{s}\;}
$$

**(ii) Equilibrium structural parameter:**

$$
\lambda_\infty = \frac{k_1}{k_1 - k_2 \dot{\gamma}} = \frac{0.08}{0.08 - 0.025} = \frac{0.08}{0.055} \approx \boxed{1.4545}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical physical note:** Since $\lambda_\infty > 1$, the equilibrium state is **non-physical** under this simple linear model. This indicates the structural kinetics model breaks down at this shear rate — a sign that the fluid is being pushed out of the rheopexic regime into a transition or unstable region. In practice, a non-linear or saturating model is required.

**(iii) Maximum shear rate for rheopexy:**

For finite, positive $\tau_b$, we require $k_1 - k_2 \dot{\gamma}_{\max} > 0$:

$$
\dot{\gamma}_{\max} = \frac{k_1}{k_2} = \frac{0.08}{5 \times 10^{-4}} = \boxed{160\ \text{s}^{-1}}
$$

Beyond this shear rate, $\tau_b \to \infty$ and the fluid no longer exhibits equilibrium rheopexy.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for Rheopexy:**

- ✅ **Rheopexy = viscosity *increases* with time at constant shear rate** (mirror image of thixotropy).
- ✅ Also called **anti-thixotropy** or **negative thixotropy**.
- ✅ Microstructural origin: shear **builds up** structure (entanglements, orthokinetic aggregation, micellar growth).
- ✅ Defining mathematical signature: $\dfrac{\partial \eta(t,\dot{\gamma})}{\partial t}\bigg|_{\dot{\gamma}} > 0$.
- ✅ Structural kinetics ODE: $\dfrac{d\lambda}{dt} = k_1(1-\lambda) + k_2\dot{\gamma}\,\lambda$ — note the **positive sign** on the shear term (this is what differentiates from thixotropy).
- ✅ Hysteresis loop direction is **counter-clockwise** (opposite to thixotropy).
- ✅ Build-up time constant: $\tau_b = \dfrac{1}{k_1 - k_2\dot{\gamma}}$.
- ✅ Maximum shear rate for rheopexy: $\dot{\gamma}_{\max} = \dfrac{k_1}{k_2}$.
- ✅ Examples: **synovial fluid**, fresh cement paste, certain micellar gels, specific drilling muds.
- ✅ At $t = \tau_b$, ~63% of equilibrium structure has built up; at $5\tau_b$, >99%.
- ✅ Always check **units** before solving numerical problems: $\dot{\gamma}$ in s$^{-1}$, $\eta$ in Pa·s, $\tau$ in Pa, $k_1$ in s$^{-1}$, $k_2$ in Pa$^{-1}$.
- ✅ Hysteresis loop area = measure of **time-dependent energy stored/released** during a shear cycle.
- ✅ **Recovery on shear removal** is a defining feature of both thixotropy and rheopexy — never confuse with permanent shear-thickening (dilatancy), which is *instantaneous* and reversible without time-dependence.
- ✅ In **oscillatory shear**, rheopexic fluids show increasing $G'$ and $G''$ with time at constant frequency and strain.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT402 - POLYMER PHYSICS
> - **Module:** Module 2

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU-Syllabus Definition

In the context of **Polymer Physics (PCPOT402)**, the term **"Short Term"** refers to the category of **short-term mechanical properties** of polymeric materials — those measured and evaluated over a *short duration of applied load or deformation* (typically **seconds to a few minutes**). These properties describe the **instantaneous or quasi-instantaneous response** of a polymer when subjected to external mechanical forces, **before** significant time-dependent viscoelastic phenomena (such as creep, stress relaxation, or permanent flow) can develop.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Short-term testing** is essentially a "snapshot" of polymer behavior at a particular instant of time. It assumes the test is performed **fast enough** that molecular rearrangements (chain sliding, disentanglement) are largely **suppressed**, so the material behaves closer to its **glassy (elastic) state**.

### Sub-Categories Under "Short-Term Properties"

Under the KTU 2024 syllabus framework, the short-term mechanical response of polymers typically encompasses:

| Sub-Property | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Tensile Strength** | Maximum stress a polymer can withstand under stretching |
| **Young's Modulus (E)** | Stiffness in the elastic region |
| **Yield Strength (σ_y)** | Stress at which plastic deformation begins |
| **Elongation at Break (ε_b)** | Maximum strain before fracture |
| **Toughness** | Energy absorbed per unit volume before fracture |
| **Impact Strength** | Resistance to sudden (high-strain-rate) loading |
| **Hardness** | Resistance to localized surface indentation |
| **Flexural Strength** | Stress at failure in bending mode |

> [!NOTE]
> **Time Boundary Convention (KTU 2024):** Short-term tests in KTU labs are typically conducted at **strain rates between 1 mm/min and 500 mm/min**, with test durations ranging from **5 seconds to 5 minutes**. Anything beyond ~24 hours of sustained load enters the **"Long-Term"** regime (creep/stress relaxation), which is a separate module topic.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Rubber Band Snap!**
>
> Imagine stretching a rubber band in two different ways:
> - **Pull it slowly over 10 seconds** → it stretches a lot, gets warm, and never quite returns to original length → this is **long-term (viscoelastic)** behavior.
> - **Snap it instantly and release** → it stretches just a little, feels stiff and "springy", and returns almost perfectly → this is **short-term (elastic-glassy)** behavior.
>
> **Short-term mechanical testing** is like the second scenario. The polymer chains don't have *time* to slide past each other, so what you measure is essentially the **stiffness of the molecular network "frozen" in place** — dominated by **primary (covalent) bonds** and **secondary (van der Waals) interactions** within the time-frame of the test.

### Why "Short Term" Matters in Engineering

Polymers in real engineering applications often experience:
- **Sudden impact loads** (e.g., a plastic helmet hit by a stone)
- **Brief tensile/compressive forces** (e.g., a plastic fastener tightened for 2 seconds)
- **Rapid deformation events** (e.g., packaging films being torn)

For these scenarios, **long-term data is useless** — only **short-term properties** predict failure. This is why KTU dedicates a full sub-section to it under Viscoelastic Behavior (Module 2).

---

## 1.3 Governing Physical Principles

Three fundamental physical principles govern short-term polymer behavior:

1. **Hookean Elasticity (at very low strains):** $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$ — dominates in the initial linear region of the stress-strain curve.
2. **Strain-Rate Dependence:** Even in the "short" regime, faster loading gives **stiffer response** (higher apparent modulus), because chains have less time to relax.
3. **Energy Storage (Reversibility):** Most of the energy input during short-term loading is **stored elastically** (recoverable) rather than dissipated viscously (lost as heat).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The **5 standard stress-strain curve types** for polymers (brittle, plastic, elastomeric, semi-ductile, and strain-hardening) are part of the **KTU Module 2 core learning outcome CO2** and are frequently asked in university examinations.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework of Short-Term Loading

When a polymer is loaded **rapidly** (short-term), the following physical hierarchy of molecular response unfolds:

### Step-by-Step Molecular Response Hierarchy

1. **Immediate Bond Distortion (t ≈ 10⁻¹² s):**
   - The applied force instantaneously deforms **primary (covalent) bonds** along the polymer backbone and stretches **bond angles**. The polymer behaves as a **rigid solid (glassy)**.
   - This is the **purely elastic** contribution.

2. **Secondary Bond Stretching (t ≈ 10⁻⁹ s):**
   - **Van der Waals forces** between adjacent chains stretch and orient. This contributes to the **low-strain elastic modulus**.

3. **Local Chain Segment Motion (t ≈ 10⁻⁶ s):**
   - Small-scale rotations around backbone bonds begin to occur. In a short-term test, these are *partially* activated depending on **T_g** and strain rate.

4. **Suppression of Long-Range Motion (t > 10⁻³ s):**
   - **Entanglement sliding, chain reptation, and viscous flow are FROZEN OUT** because the test ends before these slow processes can occur.

5. **Unloading Recovery (immediate upon release):**
   - The stored elastic energy is released. If deformation was below the **yield point**, the sample recovers fully (elastic). If above, permanent plastic deformation remains.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Summary:** In short-term loading, the polymer acts like a **"frozen network"** — the chains are too sluggish to rearrange, so only **bond stretching and local segment wiggling** contribute to the mechanical response. This is why short-term modulus is **higher** than long-term modulus in the same material.

---

## 2.2 The 5 Canonical Stress-Strain Curves for Polymers

This is **high-yield KTU content** — expect a 7-mark question on this.

| Curve Type | Behavior | Example Polymer | Key Features |
|---|---|---|---|
| **(a) Brittle** | Low elongation, fracture before yield | Polystyrene (PS), PMMA at low T | High E, low ε_b, sharp break |
| **(b) Plastic (Ductile)** | Yield + cold drawing + necking | Low-Density Polyethylene (LDPE) | Clear yield point, large plastic region |
| **(c) Elastomeric** | High reversible elongation | Natural Rubber (vulcanized) | Low E initially, very high ε_b (~800%) |
| **(d) Semi-Ductile (Tough)** | Moderate yield + moderate draw | Nylon 6,6, Polycarbonate (PC) | Combines moderate E, σ_y, and ε_b |
| **(e) Strain-Hardening** | Strong upturn before fracture | Polyethylene Terephthalate (PET) | Stress rises steeply near break due to chain orientation |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often confuse **"yield strength"** with **"tensile (ultimate) strength"**. Yield strength (σ_y) is the **first peak** on the stress-strain curve (where plastic deformation begins). Tensile strength (σ_u) is the **maximum stress** the material reaches *anywhere* on the curve. In strain-hardening polymers, σ_u > σ_y. In brittle polymers, fracture occurs *before* yielding, so σ_u = σ_break.

---

## 2.3 Short-Term vs. Long-Term Comparison (Conceptual Map)

| Parameter | Short-Term (Module 2 Topic) | Long-Term (Next Topic in Module 2) |
|---|---|---|
| **Test Duration** | Seconds to minutes | Hours to years |
| **Dominant Process** | Bond stretching, elastic deformation | Chain slippage, viscous flow |
| **Primary Model** | Hooke's Law (σ = Eε) | Kelvin-Voigt / Maxwell models |
| **Strain Rate Sensitivity** | Moderate | Very High |
| **Recoverability** | Mostly elastic (recoverable) | Partially permanent (irrecoverable) |
| **Typical KTU Experiment** | Tensile test, Izod impact | Creep test, stress relaxation |
| **Boundary Condition** | Constant strain rate ($\dot{\varepsilon}$ = const) | Constant stress (creep) or constant strain (relaxation) |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are critical for KTU 2024 Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks) questions.**

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units (SI) | Boundary / Validity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A_0}$ | Engineering stress | Pa (N/m²) | $A_0$ = original cross-section |
| 2 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0}$ | Engineering strain | dimensionless | $\Delta L \ll L_0$ for accuracy |
| 3 | $E = \dfrac{\sigma}{\varepsilon}$ | Young's Modulus (initial slope) | Pa | Linear-elastic region only |
| 4 | $E_{\text{secant}} = \dfrac{\sigma}{\varepsilon} \bigg\vert_{\varepsilon_0}$ | Secant Modulus at strain ε₀ | Pa | Used for non-linear region |
| 5 | $E_{\text{tangent}} = \dfrac{d\sigma}{d\varepsilon}$ | Tangent Modulus (local slope) | Pa | Used at any chosen point |
| 6 | $U_T = \displaystyle\int_0^{\varepsilon_b} \sigma \, d\varepsilon$ | Toughness (energy/volume) | J/m³ | Area under full stress-strain curve |
| 7 | $\sigma_y$ | Yield Strength (0.2% offset method) | Pa | Defined at 0.002 plastic strain |
| 8 | $\sigma_u$ | Ultimate Tensile Strength (max σ) | Pa | Peak of the stress-strain curve |
| 9 | $\varepsilon_b$ | Elongation at Break | % or fraction | Final strain before fracture |
| 10 | $\nu = -\dfrac{\varepsilon_{\text{trans}}}{\varepsilon_{\text{axial}}}$ | Poisson's Ratio | dimensionless | Typically 0.3–0.5 for polymers |
| 11 | $I_{\text{impact}} = \dfrac{E_{\text{absorbed}}}{A_{\text{notch}}}$ | Impact Strength (Izod/Charpy) | J/m | Notched or unnotched |
| 12 | $\tau = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}$ | Newtonian viscous stress | Pa | For long-term viscous flow |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering vs. True Stress-Strain:** For KTU problems, *engineering* values are standard. However, if a question specifies **necking** (true stress > engineering stress), use $\sigma_{\text{true}} = \sigma (1 + \varepsilon)$.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Short-term mechanical data is the **first-line design parameter** for:

- **Structural polymers:** Pipes, beams, fasteners (need σ_u, E, ε_b).
- **Packaging films:** Tear resistance, tensile strength.
- **Automotive parts:** Bumpers, dashboards (need impact strength).
- **Medical devices:** Syringes, catheters (need precise stiffness).
- **Aerospace composites:** Carbon-fiber-reinforced polymers (need modulus, σ_u).

> [!TIP]
> **Engineer's Rule of Thumb:** Always use a **safety factor of 2–5** over the short-term tensile strength when designing load-bearing polymer components, because *in-service* conditions include **time, temperature, and stress concentrations** that the short-term test does not capture.

---
