# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]**
> **"Distinguish between creep and stress relaxation in polymeric materials. Sketch the typical response curves for a Maxwell element in both cases."** [3 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Creep | Stress Relaxation |
|---|---|---|
| **Applied stimulus** | Constant stress $\sigma_0$ | Constant strain $\varepsilon_0$ |
| **Measured response** | Time-dependent strain $\varepsilon(t)$ | Time-dependent stress $\sigma(t)$ |
| **Maxwell model** | $\varepsilon(t) = \dfrac{\sigma_0}{E} + \dfrac{\sigma_0 t}{\eta}$ (linear, unbounded) | $\sigma(t) = E\varepsilon_0 e^{-t/\tau}$ (exponential decay) |
| **Physical picture** | Material flows continuously under load | Internal stress dissipates with time |

**Sketch description (for exam):**
- **Creep curve**: Vertical jump of $\sigma_0/E$ at $t=0$, then a straight line of slope $\sigma_0/\eta$ going up.
- **Stress relaxation curve**: Starts at $E\varepsilon_0$ at $t=0$, then decays exponentially toward zero with time constant $\tau = \eta/E$.

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]**
> **"Define storage modulus, loss modulus, and loss tangent. What is the physical significance of a peak in $\tan\delta$ vs. temperature plot?"** [3 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

- **Storage Modulus $E'$**: The **in-phase** (real) part of the complex modulus. Represents the **elastic energy stored** per cycle of deformation. Units: Pa.

- **Loss Modulus $E''$**: The **out-of-phase** (imaginary) part. Represents the **energy dissipated as heat** per cycle. Units: Pa.

- **Loss Tangent $\tan\delta$**: Ratio $E''/E'$, where $\delta$ is the phase lag between stress and strain. A dimensionless measure of **damping**.

- **Significance of $\tan\delta$ peak**: A peak in $\tan\delta$ vs. $T$ (or vs. frequency) corresponds to the **glass transition temperature $T_g$**, where molecular segmental motion reaches the timescale of the mechanical deformation. It indicates the temperature of **maximum energy dissipation** — critical for vibration damping applications.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]**
> **(a)** Derive the constitutive equation of the **Maxwell model** starting from its mechanical analog. Use it to obtain expressions for **stress relaxation** and **creep** behavior. Discuss the model's limitations in describing real polymer behavior. [7 Marks]
>
> **(b)** A polymer sample is subjected to a step stress of **2 MPa**. The Maxwell parameters are $E = 100$ MPa and $\eta = 5 \times 10^8$ Pa·s. Calculate: (i) the **characteristic relaxation time** $\tau$, (ii) the **instantaneous strain**, (iii) the **strain after 1000 seconds**. [7 Marks]

#### Part (a) — Model Solution [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Mechanical analog description (1 mark):** A spring of modulus $E$ and a dashpot of viscosity $\eta$ in **series**. Series connection means equal stress through both elements and additive strain.

**Step 2 — Constitutive equation (2 marks):** Differentiating Hooke's and Newton's laws and adding strains:

$$\frac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = \frac{1}{E}\frac{d\sigma}{dt} + \frac{\sigma}{\eta}$$

**Step 3 — Stress relaxation derivation (2 marks):** Setting $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_0$ (constant) so $d\varepsilon/dt = 0$, separating variables, and integrating with initial condition $\sigma(0) = E\varepsilon_0$:

$$\sigma(t) = E\varepsilon_0 \, e^{-t/\tau}, \quad \tau = \frac{\eta}{E}$$

**Step 4 — Creep derivation (1 mark):** Setting $\sigma = \sigma_0$ so $d\sigma/dt = 0$:

$$\varepsilon(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{E} + \frac{\sigma_0 t}{\eta}$$

**Step 5 — Limitations (1 mark):** (i) Predicts **infinite strain at long times** during creep — unrealistic for crosslinked polymers. (ii) Cannot model **delayed elastic recovery**. (iii) Real polymers require the **Standard Linear Solid** for accurate representation.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution [7 Marks]

**Given:** $\sigma_0 = 2 \times 10^6$ Pa, $E = 100 \times 10^6$ Pa, $\eta = 5 \times 10^8$ Pa·s.

**(i) Characteristic relaxation time:**

$$\tau = \frac{\eta}{E} = \frac{5 \times 10^8}{100 \times 10^6} = 5 \text{ seconds}$$

**(ii) Instantaneous strain at $t = 0^+$:**

$$\varepsilon(0) = \frac{\sigma_0}{E} = \frac{2 \times 10^6}{100 \times 10^6} = 0.02 = 2\%$$

**(iii) Strain at $t = 1000$ s:**

$$\varepsilon(1000) = \frac{\sigma_0}{E} + \frac{\sigma_0 t}{\eta} = 0.02 + \frac{(2 \times 10^6)(1000)}{5 \times 10^8}$$

$$\varepsilon(1000) = 0.02 + 0.004 = 0.024 = 2.4\%$$

**Physical interpretation:** The viscous contribution grows linearly with time. After 200 $\tau$ (= 1000 s), the viscous flow is only 0.4%, indicating a stiff, slow-flowing material.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]**
> **(a)** What is the **complex modulus** in dynamic mechanical analysis? Derive the expressions for **storage modulus, loss modulus, and loss tangent** for a **Maxwell element** under oscillatory strain. [7 Marks]
>
> **(b)** A polymer has $T_g = 80°$C. Using the WLF equation with universal constants $C_1 = 17.44$ and $C_2 = 51.6$ K, calculate the **shift factor $a_T$** at $T = 110°$C. Comment on its physical meaning. [7 Marks]

#### Part (a) — Model Solution [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Define complex modulus (1 mark):** When a viscoelastic material is subjected to an oscillatory strain $\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_0 e^{i\omega t}$, the resulting stress is also oscillatory but lags by a phase angle $\delta$. The ratio is the complex modulus:

$$E^*(\omega) = E'(\omega) + iE''(\omega) = \frac{\sigma(t)}{\varepsilon(t)}$$

$E'$ = storage modulus, $E''$ = loss modulus.

**Step 2 — Apply oscillatory strain to Maxwell equation (2 marks):** With $\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_0 e^{i\omega t}$ and $\sigma(t) = \sigma^* e^{i\omega t}$:

$$i\omega\varepsilon = \frac{1}{E}(i\omega\sigma) + \frac{\sigma}{\eta} = \sigma\left(\frac{i\omega}{E} + \frac{1}{\eta}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $E^* = \sigma/\varepsilon$ and introduce $\tau = \eta/E$ (2 marks):**

$$E^* = \frac{i\omega E}{i\omega + E/\eta} = E \cdot \frac{i\omega\tau}{1 + i\omega\tau}$$

**Step 4 — Rationalize and separate real/imaginary parts (2 marks):** Multiplying by $(1 - i\omega\tau)/(1 - i\omega\tau)$:

$$E' = \frac{E(\omega\tau)^2}{1 + (\omega\tau)^2}, \quad E'' = \frac{E\omega\tau}{1 + (\omega\tau)^2}$$

$$\tan\delta = \frac{E''}{E'} = \frac{1}{\omega\tau}$$

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution [7 Marks]

**Given:** $T_g = 80°$C = 353.15 K, $T = 110°$C = 383.15 K, $C_1 = 17.44$, $C_2 = 51.6$ K.

**Temperature difference:**

$$\Delta T = T - T_g = 383.15 - 353.15 = 30 \text{ K}$$

**Apply WLF equation:**

$$\log a_T = -\frac{C_1 (T - T_g)}{C_2 + (T - T_g)} = -\frac{17.44 \times 30}{51.6 + 30} = -\frac{523.2}{81.6} = -6.41$$

$$a_T = 10^{-6.41} \approx 3.89 \times 10^{-7}$$

**Physical meaning (3 marks):**
- The shift factor $a_T$ is **much less than 1**, meaning the polymer at $110°$C behaves as if observed on a **greatly compressed time scale**.
- Specifically, a 1-second experiment at 110°C corresponds to $\sim 2.6 \times 10^6$ seconds (~30 days) at $T_g$.
- This is the essence of **time–temperature superposition (TTS)**: heating the polymer accelerates molecular motion in the same way as waiting longer.
- Practical implication: **Short-term DMA tests at elevated temperature** can predict **long-term creep/relaxation** at service temperature.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls That Cost Marks**
>
> 1. **Series vs. Parallel Confusion**: Many students mix up Maxwell (series) and Kelvin–Voigt (parallel) equations. Memorize: **Maxwell = Max-stress-different-elements** (think "**M**aximum separation"); **Kelvin = Keep-Equal-strain** (parallel means same $\varepsilon$).
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Initial Condition**: In Kelvin–Voigt creep, $\varepsilon(0) = 0$ (NOT $\sigma_0/E$). The dashpot blocks instantaneous motion. **Full mark deduction** if you write the wrong initial condition.
>
> 3. **Creep Slope = $\sigma_0/\eta$, Not $\sigma_0/E$**: The slope of the Maxwell creep curve is the viscous term. Don't confuse this with the elastic spring modulus.
>
> 4. **WLF is valid only between $T_g$ and $T_g + 100$ K**: Outside this range, the **Arrhenius equation** is used. The examiner will deduct marks if you apply WLF to glassy or fully molten states.
>
> 5. **Complex Modulus Units**: Always state $E'$ and $E''$ in **Pa or MPa**, and $\tan\delta$ as **dimensionless**. Numerical answers without units = **automatic 0.5 mark cut**.
>
> 6. **In the SLS Model, Two Different Spring Constants $E_1$ and $E_2$ Exist**: Don't use a single $E$ — the parallel spring and the Maxwell spring are **distinct**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this section and review 30 minutes before your KTU 2024 ESE.**

### Core Definitions
- ☐ **Viscoelasticity** = combination of viscous and elastic response.
- ☐ **Creep** = $\varepsilon(t)$ under constant $\sigma_0$.
- ☐ **Stress relaxation** = $\sigma(t)$ under constant $\varepsilon_0$.
- ☐ **Relaxation time** $\tau = \eta/E$ — the **fingerprint** of a viscoelastic system.

### Maxwell Model (Series: Spring + Dashpot)
- ☐ Constitutive: $\dfrac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = \dfrac{1}{E}\dfrac{d\sigma}{dt} + \dfrac{\sigma}{\eta}$
- ☐ Stress relaxation: $\sigma(t) = E\varepsilon_0 e^{-t/\tau}$
- ☐ Creep: $\varepsilon(t) = \dfrac{\sigma_0}{E} + \dfrac{\sigma_0 t}{\eta}$ (unbounded)
- ☐ Best for: **Stress relaxation** of amorphous polymers; **liquid-like** behavior.
- ☐ Limitation: Predicts **infinite creep strain** at long times.

### Kelvin–Voigt Model (Parallel: Spring + Dashpot)
- ☐ Constitutive: $\sigma = E\varepsilon + \eta\dfrac{d\varepsilon}{dt}$
- ☐ Creep: $\varepsilon(t) = \dfrac{\sigma_0}{E}\left[1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right]$ (bounded)
- ☐ Best for: **Creep** of crosslinked polymers; **delayed elasticity**.
- ☐ Limitation: Cannot describe instantaneous elastic response; poor for stress relaxation.

### Standard Linear Solid (SLS / Zener) Model
- ☐ Combines Maxwell and Kelvin–Voigt to model **realistic** polymer behavior.
- ☐ Stress relaxation: $\sigma(t) = E_2 \varepsilon_0 + E_1 \varepsilon_0 e^{-t/\tau}$ (decays to $E_2 \varepsilon_0$).
- ☐ Provides both **instantaneous** ($E_1 + E_2$) and **equilibrium** ($E_2$) moduli.

### Dynamic Mechanical Analysis (DMA)
- ☐ Complex modulus: $E^* = E' + iE''$
- ☐ Storage modulus $E'$: elastic energy stored per cycle.
- ☐ Loss modulus $E''$: energy dissipated as heat per cycle.
- ☐ Loss tangent: $\tan\delta = E''/E'$ — peak occurs at $T_g$.
- ☐ Maxwell element: $E' = \dfrac{E(\omega\tau)^2}{1 + (\omega\tau)^2}$, $E'' = \dfrac{E\omega\tau}{1 + (\omega\tau)^2}$

### WLF Equation & Time–Temperature Superposition
- ☐ $\log a_T = -\dfrac{C_1 (T - T_g)}{C_2 + (T - T_g)}$
- ☐ Universal constants: $C_1 = 17.44$, $C_2 = 51.6$ K.
- ☐ Valid for $T_g < T < T_g + 100$ K (use Arrhenius outside this range).
- ☐ Higher $T$ → lower $a_T$ → accelerated molecular motion → predicts long-term behavior from short-term tests.

### Boltzmann Superposition Principle (Linear Viscoelasticity)
- ☐ $\sigma(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} E(t - t') \dfrac{d\varepsilon}{dt'}\, dt'$
- ☐ Stress response to complex loading history = sum of responses to infinitesimal step strains.

### Engineering Reality Check
- ☐ Below $T_g$: **glassy, brittle, elastic** → high modulus ($10^9$–$10^{10}$ Pa).
- ☐ Near $T_g$: rapid modulus drop of ~3 decades.
- ☐ Above $T_g$ (amorphous): **rubbery** → modulus $\sim 10^6$ Pa.
- ☐ At $T_m$ or for uncrosslinked polymer: **viscous flow** dominates.
- ☐ Polymers are **never** purely elastic or purely viscous — always viscoelastic.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Strategy Tip:** In long-answer questions, always **draw the mechanical analog** (spring + dashpot diagram) first — it earns you **at least 1 mark** even if your algebra is incomplete. Examiners reward **structured, diagram-supported answers** under the NEP 2020 OBE pattern.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Factors Affecting Viscoelasticity. Short-Term Properties: Stress-Strain Curves
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT402 - POLYMER PHYSICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Factors Affecting Viscoelasticity. Short-Term Properties: Stress-Strain Curves

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Viscoelasticity — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Viscoelasticity** is the property of a material that exhibits both **viscous (liquid-like)** and **elastic (solid-like)** response when subjected to an applied stress or strain. For polymers, this behaviour arises because chain segments can undergo time-dependent rearrangements (viscous flow) superimposed on instantaneous bond deformation (elastic response).

Mathematically, viscoelastic response is a **time- and temperature-dependent** combination of Hookean elasticity and Newtonian viscosity, captured by the classical mechanical analogs — the **Maxwell model** (spring + dashpot in series) and the **Kelvin–Voigt model** (spring + dashpot in parallel).

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Memory-Foam Ball"

Imagine a **memory-foam ball** dropped from your hand:
- The instant it contacts the table, it compresses *elastically* (like a spring).
- But over the next few seconds, the foam slowly *creeps* and redistributes its internal air, recovering its original shape only after a delay.

That two-stage response — **instant bounce + delayed shape change** — is exactly what polymers do. A polymer chain has:
- **Covalent bonds** (provide the "spring" — elastic, instantaneous recovery).
- **Entanglements and segmental motions** (provide the "dashpot" — viscous, time-dependent slippage).

When you **stretch** a polymer, both mechanisms act simultaneously, giving the classic viscoelastic curve.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCPOT402 Module 2):**
> The board examiner expects students to clearly distinguish *short-term* (mechanical) response from *long-term* (time-dependent) response, and to connect molecular structure → segmental mobility → macroscopic mechanical behaviour.

### 1.3 Short-Term Stress–Strain Curves — Definition

> [!NOTE]
> A **stress–strain curve** is a plot of **stress (σ, in MPa or Pa)** versus **strain (ε, dimensionless or %)** obtained from a uniaxial tensile test performed at a **constant strain rate** and (usually) a **constant temperature**. "Short-term" implies the test is completed in seconds to minutes, so that the time-dependent viscous component is small but never zero for any polymer.

The curve reveals four engineering metrics critical for polymer selection:
- **Young's Modulus (E)** — stiffness
- **Yield Strength (σ_y)** — onset of plastic flow
- **Ultimate Tensile Strength (σ_UTS)** — maximum load-bearing capacity
- **Elongation at Break (ε_b)** — ductility

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Factors Affecting Viscoelasticity

The viscoelastic response of a polymer is governed by the interplay of **internal (molecular) factors** and **external (environmental) factors**. Each factor modifies the **relaxation time spectrum** of the material, shifting the material from glassy to rubbery to viscous-flow behaviour.

#### 2.1.1 Internal (Molecular) Factors

- **Molecular Weight (M̄) and Molecular Weight Distribution (MWD)**
  - Higher $\bar{M}_w$ → longer chain → more **entanglements** → longer terminal relaxation time ($\tau$).
  - Broad MWD produces a wider relaxation spectrum, leading to a more gradual transition from glassy to rubbery.

- **Chain Stiffness / Backbone Rigidity**
  - Stiff backbones (e.g., aromatic rings in **polystyrene**, **polycarbonate**) raise the glass transition temperature $T_g$ and restrict segmental motion, increasing elastic modulus.

- **Side Groups and Branching**
  - Bulky side groups (e.g., $-\text{C}(\text{CH}_3)_3$ in PMMA) impede rotation, increasing $T_g$ and modulus.
  - Long-chain branching reduces crystallinity and broadens the relaxation spectrum.

- **Crosslink Density**
  - Higher crosslink density → tighter network → reduced viscous flow → higher rubbery plateau modulus $E_N^0$ and shorter relaxation times (only for sub-network motions).

- **Crystallinity**
  - Crystallites act as **physical crosslinks**. Higher crystallinity → higher modulus, lower creep, sharper yield.

- **Free Volume**
  - Free volume ($v_f$) is the unoccupied space between chains. As $T$ approaches $T_g$, $v_f$ shrinks, segmental mobility falls, and relaxation time diverges:
  
  $$\tau = \tau_0 \exp\left(\frac{B}{v_f}\right)$$

- **Plasticizers and Additives**
  - Small-molecule plasticizers (e.g., DOP in PVC) increase free volume, lower $T_g$, and speed up relaxation.

#### 2.1.2 External (Environmental) Factors

- **Temperature**
  - The single most dominant factor. As $T$ increases, relaxation times decrease roughly following the **Williams–Landel–Ferry (WLF)** equation (above $T_g$):

  $$\log_{10} a_T = -\frac{C_1 (T - T_g)}{C_2 + (T - T_g)}$$
  
  with typical universal constants $C_1 = 17.44$, $C_2 = 51.6$ K. Below $T_g$, the **Arrhenius** equation governs:

  $$\tau(T) = \tau_0 \exp\left(\frac{E_a}{RT}\right)$$

- **Strain Rate ($\dot{\varepsilon}$)**
  - Faster loading → less time for chain rearrangement → material behaves *stiffer and more brittle* (the curve shifts up and to the left). This is the **time–temperature superposition principle** in disguise: rate and temperature are *interchangeable* in their effect on polymer response.

- **Stress/Strain Magnitude**
  - At small strains ($< 1\%$), linear viscoelasticity holds.
  - At large strains (yield region), the response becomes **non-linear** — molecular chains are forced to align and disentangle.

- **Pressure (Hydrostatic)**
  - Increasing pressure compresses free volume, raises $T_g$, and shifts the relaxation spectrum to longer times.

- **Humidity / Moisture**
  - For **hydrophilic** polymers (nylon, PVA, PMMA), absorbed water acts as a plasticizer — lowering $T_g$ and accelerating viscoelastic creep.

- **Cyclic Loading Frequency (ω)**
  - In dynamic mechanical analysis, higher frequency is *equivalent* to lower temperature; the storage modulus $E'$ rises with $\omega$ and falls with $T$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Concept Anchor — Time-Temperature Superposition (TTSP):**
> A very high-yield Module 2 topic. The principle states that *a change in temperature is equivalent to a horizontal shift (along the log-time axis) of the relaxation modulus curve*. This single principle unifies temperature, rate, and frequency effects.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Engineering Stress | $\sigma = F / A_0$ | Pa or MPa; $A_0$ = original cross-section |
| 2 | Engineering Strain | $\varepsilon = (L - L_0) / L_0$ | dimensionless or % |
| 3 | True Stress | $\sigma_{true} = \sigma \cdot (1 + \varepsilon)$ | Pa; accounts for area reduction |
| 4 | True Strain | $\varepsilon_{true} = \ln(1 + \varepsilon)$ | dimensionless |
| 5 | Young's Modulus (linear region) | $E = \sigma / \varepsilon$ | Pa |
| 6 | Toughness (area under σ–ε curve) | $U_T = \int \sigma \, d\varepsilon$ | J/m³ |
| 7 | Maxwell Model Stress Relaxation | $\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \, e^{-t/\tau}$ | $\tau = \eta / E$ |
| 8 | Kelvin–Voigt Model Creep | $\varepsilon(t) = (\sigma_0 / E) [1 - e^{-t/\tau}]$ | retarded elastic strain |
| 9 | WLF Equation (above $T_g$) | $\log_{10} a_T = -C_1 (T - T_g) / [C_2 + (T - T_g)]$ | $C_1 \approx 17.44$, $C_2 \approx 51.6$ K |
| 10 | Arrhenius Equation (below $T_g$) | $\tau(T) = \tau_0 \exp(E_a / RT)$ | $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) |
| 11 | Rubber Elasticity Modulus | $E = 3 \rho R T / M_c$ | $M_c$ = mol. weight between crosslinks |
| 12 | Modulus–MW Relation (entangled melt) | $G_N^0 = (4/5) \rho R T / M_e$ | $M_e$ = entanglement MW |
| 13 | Shift Factor (apparent activation) | $a_T = \tau(T) / \tau(T_{ref})$ | dimensionless |
| 14 | Dynamic Storage Modulus | $E' = (E \omega^2 \tau^2) / (1 + \omega^2 \tau^2)$ | Pa |
| 15 | Dynamic Loss Modulus | $E'' = (E \omega \tau) / (1 + \omega^2 \tau^2)$ | Pa |
| 16 | Loss Tangent | $\tan \delta = E'' / E'$ | peak at $T_g$ or sub-$T_g$ transitions |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Table Safety:** Every vertical bar in the formulas has been written as `\vert` or omitted; raw $\vert$ symbols would break the table parser. KTU students preparing digital notes should adopt the same habit.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Why these factors matter |
|---|---|
| Tyre / Rubber Industry | Designing compounds that resist **creep** and retain **elastic recovery** at elevated road temperatures. |
| Aerospace Composites | Predicting **long-term modulus retention** of epoxy matrices at 120 °C service. |
| Biomedical Implants | Ensuring polymer scaffolds (PLA, PEEK) maintain stiffness during **sterilisation** and physiological loading. |
| Packaging Films | Selecting LDPE/LLDPE grades that balance **toughness** with **tear resistance** at low temperatures. |
| 3D Printing | Tuning **strain rate** to control layer adhesion in FFF (Fused Filament Fabrication). |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Engineering Stress–Strain Relationship from Tensile Load Data

A dog-bone specimen of original gauge length $L_0$ and cross-section $A_0$ is pulled in a tensile testing machine at constant crosshead speed. The recorded load $F$ and extension $\Delta L$ are converted to engineering stress and strain.

**Step 1 — Define engineering stress.**

By definition, engineering stress is the instantaneous load divided by the *original* (undeformed) cross-sectional area:

$$\sigma \equiv \frac{F}{A_0}$$

**Step 2 — Define engineering strain.**

Engineering strain is the change in length divided by the *original* length:

$$\varepsilon \equiv \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \frac{L - L_0}{L_0}$$

**Step 3 — Identify Hooke's law region.**

For small strains, the polymer behaves elastically and obeys Hooke's law:

$$\sigma = E \, \varepsilon \quad \Longrightarrow \quad E = \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon} = \frac{F / A_0}{\Delta L / L_0} = \frac{F L_0}{A_0 \, \Delta L}$$

$E$ is the **slope of the initial linear portion** of the σ–ε curve.

**Step 4 — Compute true stress during necking.**

Once necking begins, the cross-section reduces continuously. Assuming **incompressibility of the polymer volume** (Poisson's ratio $\nu \approx 0.5$ for rubbers, $\approx 0.33$–$0.4$ for plastics), the instantaneous area is:

$$A = \frac{A_0 \, L_0}{L} = \frac{A_0}{1 + \varepsilon}$$

Substituting into the true-stress definition:

$$\sigma_{true} = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{F (1 + \varepsilon)}{A_0} = \sigma (1 + \varepsilon)$$

**Step 5 — Compute true strain.**

Integrating incremental strains from $L_0$ to $L$:

$$\varepsilon_{true} = \int_{L_0}^{L} \frac{dL'}{L'} = \ln\!\left(\frac{L}{L_0}\right) = \ln(1 + \varepsilon)$$

**Step 6 — Relate true stress to true strain in the plastic region (Hollomon equation).**

Many polymers in their plastic region obey a power-law hardening:

$$\sigma_{true} = K \, \varepsilon_{true}^{\, n}$$

where $K$ is the **strength coefficient** (MPa) and $n$ is the **strain-hardening exponent** ($0 \le n \le 1$). For perfectly plastic metals $n = 0$; for perfectly elastic (rubber-like) response $n = 1$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the distinction matters in KTU problems:** Engineering curves show a *fall* in stress beyond the UTS because of geometric necking, even though the material is still strain-hardening. The true-stress curve continues to rise monotonically, revealing the genuine material response. Examiners often ask this as a 3-mark conceptual question.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Maxwell Model Stress Relaxation

A Maxwell element consists of a **spring** (modulus $E$, dashpot (viscosity $\eta$)) connected in series. Under step strain $\varepsilon_0$, the total strain is the sum of the strains in the two elements:

$$\varepsilon_{total} = \varepsilon_{spring} + \varepsilon_{dashpot}$$

**Step 1 — Write constitutive equations.**

For the spring (Hookean):

$$\sigma = E \, \varepsilon_{spring} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \dot{\varepsilon}_{spring} = \frac{\dot{\sigma}}{E}$$

For the dashpot (Newtonian):

$$\sigma = \eta \, \dot{\varepsilon}_{dashpot}$$

**Step 2 — Add the rates.**

$$\dot{\varepsilon}_{total} = \dot{\varepsilon}_{spring} + \dot{\varepsilon}_{dashpot} = \frac{\dot{\sigma}}{E} + \frac{\sigma}{\eta}$$

**Step 3 — Apply step-strain boundary condition.**

For a stress-relaxation test, $\varepsilon_{total} = \varepsilon_0 = \text{const}$, so $\dot{\varepsilon}_{total} = 0$:

$$0 = \frac{\dot{\sigma}}{E} + \frac{\sigma}{\eta}$$

**Step 4 — Separate variables and integrate.**

$$\frac{d\sigma}{\sigma} = -\frac{E}{\eta} \, dt = -\frac{dt}{\tau}, \quad \text{where} \quad \tau = \frac{\eta}{E}$$

Integrating from $\sigma_0$ at $t=0$ to $\sigma(t)$:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{\sigma(t)}{\sigma_0}\right) = -\frac{t}{\tau} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \boxed{\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \, e^{-t/\tau}}$$

$\tau = \eta/E$ is the **characteristic relaxation time** of the material.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Time–Temperature Superposition (TTSP) Master Curve

**Step 1 — Generate isothermal relaxation data.**

Measure $E(t)$ at several temperatures $T_1, T_2, \dots, T_n$.

**Step 2 — Choose a reference temperature** $T_{ref}$ (often $T_g$ or 25 °C).

**Step 3 — Horizontally shift** each isothermal curve along the log-time axis by an amount $\log_{10} a_T$ so that they overlap with the reference curve:

$$\log_{10} t_{reduced} = \log_{10} t - \log_{10} a_T$$

**Step 4 — Use the WLF equation to compute the shift factor** for $T > T_g$:

$$\log_{10} a_T = -\frac{C_1 (T - T_g)}{C_2 + (T - T_g)}$$

with $C_1 = 17.44$, $C_2 = 51.6$ K (universal constants). 

**Step 5 — Superposed curve** $E(t, T_{ref})$ now spans 10–15 decades of time, covering both the glassy ($E \sim 10^9$ Pa) and rubbery ($E \sim 10^6$ Pa) plateaus and the terminal flow region ($E \to 0$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Meaning of $\log_{10} a_T$:**
> If $T > T_{ref}$ and $\log_{10} a_T < 0$, it means the relaxation at the higher temperature occurs *faster*; the isothermal curve must be **shifted left** (to shorter times) to overlap the reference curve.

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Factor for a KTU Question

| Exam Question Stem Keywords | Factor Being Probed | High-Yield Answer Phrase |
|---|---|---|
| "Above $T_g$" / "rubbery plateau" | Temperature | "WLF shift factor applies; relaxation time decreases exponentially with $T$." |
| "Higher strain rate" | Strain Rate | "Equivalent to lowering $T$; material stiffens, yield stress rises." |
| "Higher crosslink density" | Network | "Rubbery modulus $E = 3 \rho R T / M_c$ increases as $M_c$ decreases." |
| "Adding DOP to PVC" | Plasticizer | "Increases free volume, lowers $T_g$, speeds up relaxation." |
| "Nylon in humid environment" | Moisture | "Water acts as plasticizer, drops $T_g$, increases creep." |
| "Higher $\bar{M}_w$" | Molecular Weight | "More entanglements; longer terminal relaxation; higher zero-shear viscosity." |
| "Side groups like phenyl" | Chain Stiffness | "Impedes rotation, raises $T_g$ and modulus." |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, PCPOT402]**
*Define viscoelasticity. Why are polymers considered viscoelastic materials?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Viscoelasticity is the property of a material that exhibits a time-dependent combination of elastic (solid-like, recoverable) and viscous (liquid-like, irrecoverable) response when subjected to an applied stress.
- **Why polymers (2 marks):** Polymers consist of long, entangled chain molecules held together by covalent bonds along the backbone and weak van der Waals forces between chains. When a stress is applied, the covalent bonds deform *instantly and reversibly* (elastic part), while the chain segments must *slip past each other slowly* through reptation and segmental motion (viscous part). The simultaneous occurrence of both mechanisms — over a wide range of temperatures near $T_g$ — makes polymers the prototypical viscoelastic materials.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PCPOT402]**
*Distinguish between engineering stress–strain and true stress–strain curves. Why does the engineering curve show a drop after the ultimate tensile strength while the true curve continues to rise?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Engineering σ–ε (1 mark):** Uses original (undeformed) cross-section $A_0$ and length $L_0$ throughout the test: $\sigma = F/A_0$, $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L_0$.
- **True σ–ε (1 mark):** Uses instantaneous (instantly measured) area $A$ and length $L$: $\sigma_{true} = F/A = \sigma(1+\varepsilon)$, $\varepsilon_{true} = \ln(1+\varepsilon)$.
- **Reason for the drop (1 mark):** In the engineering curve, the apparent stress drops after the UTS because the cross-section *decreases* rapidly during necking, while the load $F$ also decreases faster than the area reduction — even though the material itself is still strain-hardening. The true stress, which uses the *actual* (smaller) area, removes this geometric artefact and correctly shows the material continuing to harden up to fracture.

---

### Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PCPOT402, Module 2]**
> *Explain in detail the various factors affecting the viscoelastic behaviour of polymers. Discuss the effect of temperature and strain rate using the time–temperature superposition principle.* **[14 marks]**

**(a) Factors affecting viscoelasticity (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Molecular Weight and Distribution (1.5 marks):** Increasing $\bar{M}_w$ increases the number of entanglements per chain, lengthens the terminal relaxation time $\tau \propto M^3$ (Rouse–reptation theory), and broadens the rubbery plateau. A broad MWD gives a wider relaxation spectrum, smoothing the glass–rubber transition.

2. **Chain Stiffness (1 mark):** Stiff backbones (aromatic, ring-containing polymers like PS, PC, PEK) raise $T_g$ because higher energy is needed for segmental rotation. Stiff chains yield a higher storage modulus $E'$ in the glassy region.

3. **Crosslink Density (1 mark):** Crosslinks (chemical or physical) restrict chain slippage. The rubbery plateau modulus scales as $E = 3 \rho R T / M_c$, where $M_c$ is the molecular weight between crosslinks. As crosslink density increases, $M_c$ decreases and the rubbery modulus increases.

4. **Crystallinity (0.5 marks):** Crystallites act as physical crosslinks, raising modulus and reducing creep. Higher crystallinity → sharper yield behaviour.

5. **Side Groups, Branching, and Plasticizers (1 mark):** Bulky side groups (PMMA, PS) raise $T_g$ and modulus; long flexible side chains or low-$M_w$ plasticizers (DOP in PVC) lower $T_g$ by increasing free volume.

6. **External Factors (2 marks):** 
   - **Temperature** is the most dominant. As $T$ rises toward $T_g$, relaxation times drop dramatically (WLF equation). 
   - **Strain rate** is the time-equivalent: faster loading = stiffer, more brittle response. 
   - **Pressure** compresses free volume and shifts $T_g$ upward. 
   - **Humidity** plasticises hydrophilic polymers (nylons, PMMA). 
   - **Frequency** in dynamic tests is the dynamic-mechanical equivalent of inverse time.

**(b) Time–Temperature Superposition Principle (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Statement (1 mark):** TTSP states that the effect of changing temperature on the viscoelastic response of a polymer is equivalent to a horizontal shift of the relaxation curve along the logarithmic time axis by a *shift factor* $a_T$.

2. **Master Curve Construction (2 marks):** 
   - Measure stress-relaxation modulus $E(t)$ at several temperatures $T_1 < T_2 < \dots < T_n$ around a chosen reference temperature $T_{ref}$. 
   - Plot $\log E$ versus $\log t$ for each isotherm. 
   - Horizontally shift each isotherm by $\log_{10} a_T$ until the curves superimpose on the reference curve. 
   - The resulting *master curve* spans 10–15 decades of time, revealing the full material response — from glassy to rubbery plateau to terminal flow.

3. **WLF Equation (2 marks):** For $T$ in the range $T_g < T < T_g + 100$ °C, the shift factor obeys:

   $$\log_{10} a_T = -\frac{C_1 (T - T_g)}{C_2 + (T - T_g)}$$
   
   with the "universal" constants $C_1 = 17.44$ and $C_2 = 51.6$ K. The negative sign indicates that raising $T$ above $T_g$ *shortens* relaxation times (curves shift left).

4. **Arrhenius Equation (1 mark):** Below $T_g$, in the sub-glass-transition region, the shift factor follows an Arrhenius form:

   $$\log_{10} a_T = \frac{E_a}{2.303\,R}\!\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_{ref}}\right)$$

5. **Application to Strain Rate (1 mark):** The shift factor $a_T$ is mathematically identical to a *rate* shift factor $a_{\dot\varepsilon} = \dot\varepsilon_{ref} / \dot\varepsilon$. Therefore, increasing the strain rate is equivalent to decreasing the temperature, producing the same stiffening and embrittlement of the stress–strain curve. This is why the polymer appears more glassy and brittle in a high-speed impact test and more rubbery and ductile in a slow tensile test.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, PCPOT402, Module 2]**
> *Describe the various types of stress–strain behaviour exhibited by polymers. With the help of a neat sketch, explain the different regions of a typical engineering stress–strain curve and the engineering parameters obtained from it.* **[14 marks]**

**(a) Types of stress–strain behaviour in polymers (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Polymeric materials show five generic types of tensile behaviour (Sperling's classification):

1. **Brittle (Type 1) — e.g., Polystyrene at 25 °C, PMMA below $T_g$ (1.5 marks):** High Young's modulus ($E \sim 3$ GPa), no yield point, fracture at low strain ($\varepsilon_b < 5\%$). Failure is by crazing followed by brittle crack propagation.

2. **Strong and Tough (Type 2) — e.g., Polycarbonate, Nylon-6,6, PET (1.5 marks):** Moderate-to-high modulus, clear yield, extensive cold drawing (necking), neck propagation along the gauge length, and high elongation at break ($\varepsilon_b \approx 50$–$150\%$). The area under the curve (toughness) is large.

3. **Strong and Brittle (Type 3) — e.g., Epoxy below $T_g$, Highly crosslinked phenolic resin (1 mark):** Very high modulus, high UTS, but no yield and low $\varepsilon_b$. Such polymers are used as rigid structural adhesives and composites.

4. **Plastic / Ductile (Type 4) — e.g., LDPE, HDPE, PTFE (1.5 marks):** Low modulus, gradual yield, very high elongation at break (often $> 500\%$ for LDPE), strain hardening once chains orient. Failure is by ductile tearing.

5. **Elastomeric (Type 5) — e.g., Vulcanised natural rubber, silicone elastomers (1.5 marks):** Very low initial modulus, large reversible elongation (up to 500–1000\%), S-shaped stress–strain curve due to strain-induced crystallisation at high extension. Modulus rises sharply at the end as chains approach full extension.

**(b) Regions of a typical engineering stress–strain curve and engineering parameters (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Region 1 — Hookean Elastic Region (1 mark):** Linear portion near the origin. Slope gives the **Young's Modulus (E)**. Deformation is recoverable; chain bonds stretch, angles bend. Valid for $\varepsilon \le 1$–$2\%$ in glassy polymers.

2. **Region 2 — Yield Point (1 mark):** The stress at which plastic (irrecoverable) deformation begins. **Yield Stress ($\sigma_y$)** and **Yield Strain ($\varepsilon_y$)** mark the onset of chain segment slippage.

3. **Region 3 — Necking and Cold Drawing (1.5 marks):** Cross-section locally reduces ("necks"); polymer chains align with the stretching direction. The neck propagates along the gauge length at roughly constant stress (drawing stress).

4. **Region 4 — Strain Hardening (1 mark):** Once chains are fully aligned, further extension requires stretching of the aligned fibrils. Stress rises steeply. Follows the **Hollomon equation** $\sigma_{true} = K \varepsilon_{true}^{\, n}$.

5. **Region 5 — Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS) (1 mark):** The peak of the engineering stress–strain curve, $\sigma_{UTS} = F_{max} / A_0$. Beyond this point, geometric necking outruns strain hardening.

6. **Region 6 — Fracture (0.5 marks):** Final failure at **Elongation at Break ($\varepsilon_b$)**.

7. **Derived Engineering Parameters (1 mark):**
   - **Toughness** = total energy absorbed per unit volume = $\int_0^{\varepsilon_b} \sigma \, d\varepsilon$ (area under the engineering curve).
   - **Resilience** = $\int_0^{\varepsilon_y} \sigma \, d\varepsilon \approx \sigma_y^2 / (2E)$ (area under the elastic region only).
   - **Modulus of Toughness** = slope in the plastic region.
   - **Poisson's ratio** $\nu$ from lateral strain / axial strain (for polymers $\nu \approx 0.33$–$0.5$).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Where students lose marks:**
> 1. **Confusing engineering and true stress** at the UTS — a 1-mark trap. Always state: "Engineering stress uses the *original* area; true stress uses the *instantaneous* area."
> 2. **Mixing up WLF and Arrhenius regimes.** WLF is valid *only* between $T_g$ and $T_g + 100$ °C; below $T_g$, use Arrhenius.
> 3. **Forgetting units.** $\sigma$ in MPa (not N), $E$ in GPa (not N/m²), and $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) — these slips cost easy marks.
> 4. **Not drawing the diagram.** A question worth 7 marks on stress–strain curves without a sketch *invariably* loses 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Ignoring the "type" prefix in Part (a).** "Strong and tough" (Type 2) and "Strong and brittle" (Type 3) differ only in $\varepsilon_b$ — examiners test whether you know which is which.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- Viscoelasticity = **elastic + viscous** behaviour; springs model the elastic part, dashpots the viscous part.
- The **Maxwell model** describes **stress relaxation** ($\sigma = \sigma_0 e^{-t/\tau}$); the **Kelvin–Voigt model** describes **creep** ($\varepsilon = (\sigma_0/E)(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$).
- The **relaxation time** $\tau = \eta / E$ is the central design parameter; it diverges near $T_g$.
- **Time-Temperature Superposition (TTSP)** is the unifying principle: a change in $T$ is equivalent to a horizontal log-time shift of $a_T$.
- **WLF equation** (above $T_g$): $\log_{10} a_T = -C_1 (T - T_g) / [C_2 + (T - T_g)]$ with $C_1 = 17.44$, $C_2 = 51.6$ K.
- **Arrhenius equation** (below $T_g$): $\log_{10} a_T = (E_a / 2.303 R)(1/T - 1/T_{ref})$.
- **Major factors** raising $T_g$ and stiffening response: high $\bar{M}_w$, stiff backbones, bulky side groups, high crosslink density, high pressure, low plasticiser, low humidity (for non-hydrophilic polymers).
- **Major factors** lowering $T_g$ and accelerating relaxation: small plasticiser molecules, absorbed moisture (nylons), high temperature, high free volume, slow strain rate.
- **Five generic stress–strain types:** Brittle, Strong-Tough, Strong-Brittle, Plastic-Ductile, Elastomeric.
- **Six curve regions:** Hookean elastic → yield → necking/cold-drawing → strain hardening → UTS → fracture.
- **Engineering stress** $\sigma = F / A_0$ drops after UTS; **true stress** $\sigma_{true} = F / A$ keeps rising — the difference is geometric, not material.
- **Engineering parameters** to compute: $E$, $\sigma_y$, $\sigma_{UTS}$, $\varepsilon_b$, **toughness** (area under curve), **resilience** (area under elastic region).
- **Hollomon equation** in the plastic region: $\sigma_{true} = K \varepsilon_{true}^{\, n}$, with $n$ = strain-hardening exponent.
- **Rubber elasticity modulus:** $E = 3 \rho R T / M_c$ — rises linearly with $T$ (entropic elasticity).
- **Dynamic mechanical parameters:** $E'$ (storage), $E''$ (loss), $\tan \delta = E''/E'$, with a $\tan \delta$ peak at $T_g$ and at sub-$T_g$ secondary transitions (β, γ).
- **Strain rate vs. temperature equivalence:** $\uparrow \dot\varepsilon \equiv \downarrow T$ (curve shifts up-left, more brittle). $\downarrow \dot\varepsilon \equiv \uparrow T$ (curve shifts down-right, more ductile).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stress-Strain Curve of a Typical Plastic
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT402 - POLYMER PHYSICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Stress-Strain Curve of a Typical Plastic

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Stress–Strain Curve of a Typical Plastic** is a graphical representation of the mechanical response of a thermoplastic or thermosetting polymer when subjected to a uniaxial tensile load. The curve plots **engineering stress (σ)** on the ordinate (Y-axis) against **engineering strain (ε)** on the abscissa (X-axis), and it consolidates all critical mechanical parameters such as elastic modulus, yield point, ultimate tensile strength, and elongation-at-break into a single diagnostic fingerprint of the material.

Formally, the curve is obtained experimentally by performing a **uniaxial tensile test** (typically per ASTM D638 or ISO 527 standards) on a standardized **dog-bone shaped specimen**, recording the load (F) and elongation (ΔL) continuously until fracture.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The stress-strain curve is a *Module 2* cornerstone. It directly determines whether a given plastic is classified as **Brittle**, **Ductile**, or **Tough** — a classification that dictates its industrial application (e.g., packaging films, automotive dashboards, safety helmets).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Chewing Gum Story"

Imagine stretching a piece of **chewing gum** between your hands, very slowly:

- At first, the gum resists your pull *firmly but slightly* — this is like the **elastic region**.
- As you pull harder, the gum suddenly becomes *much easier to stretch* — this is the **yield point**, where the gum "gives up."
- If you keep pulling, you will see a **thin neck** form in the middle of the gum — this is the **necking region**. The gum stretches at this neck, but the neck itself moves along the gum (called **neck propagation**).
- After the entire gum has been drawn thin, you must pull *extremely hard* again to break it — this is **strain hardening**.
- Finally, the gum snaps — the **fracture point**.

A typical plastic (especially a **semi-crystalline** one like **HDPE** or a **ductile amorphous** one like **PC**) behaves exactly like this chewing gum, but the exact shape of the curve depends on whether the polymer is brittle or ductile at the test temperature.

### 1.3 Key Physical Quantities (Bold Constants and Metrics)

- **Engineering Stress (σ):** $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A_0}$, units: **MPa** or **N/m²**
- **Engineering Strain (ε):** $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0}$, dimensionless (often expressed as %)
- **Young's Modulus (E):** Slope of the initial linear region, units: **MPa** or **GPa**
- **Yield Stress (σ_y):** Stress at the onset of plastic deformation
- **Ultimate Tensile Strength (σ_UTS):** Maximum stress sustained before necking
- **Elongation at Break (ε_b):** Strain at fracture

> [!NOTE]
> **Definitions Box:**
> - **$A_0$** = Original cross-sectional area of the specimen (before loading)
> - **$L_0$** = Original gauge length of the specimen
> - **$F$** = Instantaneous tensile load applied
> - **$\Delta L$** = Instantaneous change in gauge length
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six Distinct Regions of a Typical Plastic's Stress-Strain Curve

A ductile thermoplastic (e.g., low-density polyethylene, polycarbonate, or polyamide) typically exhibits **six well-defined regions** during tensile deformation. Each region corresponds to a unique microstructural mechanism occurring at the molecular level.

#### Region 1 — The Linear Elastic Region
- **Mechanism:** Reversible stretching of covalent bonds and small changes in bond angles along the polymer backbone.
- **Visual Cue:** A *straight-line segment* from the origin O up to point A (the proportional limit).
- **Mathematical Description:** **Hooke's Law** holds — $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$
- **Why it matters:** Defines the **stiffness** of the plastic. A steep slope = stiff material; a shallow slope = compliant material.

#### Region 2 — The Non-Linear Elastic / Yielding Region (A → B)
- **Mechanism:** Cooperative rotation and uncoiling of polymer chains begins. Van der Waals bonds between chains stretch.
- **Visual Cue:** Curve begins to bend away from linearity; the slope decreases continuously.
- **Key Point:** The **Proportional Limit (A)** marks the end of strict Hookean behavior. The **Elastic Limit** marks the maximum stress that can be applied without leaving any *permanent* deformation upon unloading.

#### Region 3 — The Yield Point (B)
- **Mechanism:** A critical microstructural instability — chains begin to slip past one another and align along the tensile axis.
- **Visual Cue:** A local maximum in the curve; sometimes a small "dip" follows (called **yield drop**), caused by localized adiabatic heating or dislocation-like propagation in semi-crystalline polymers.
- **Yield Stress $\sigma_y$:** The stress value at this point. Materials above $\sigma_y$ undergo *permanent* deformation.

#### Region 4 — Necking and Cold Drawing (B → C → D)
- **Mechanism:** A localized **neck** forms at the weakest cross-section. Polymer chains in the neck align parallel to the loading direction — this is called **cold drawing**. As drawing proceeds, the neck propagates along the gauge length (this is **neck propagation**).
- **Visual Cue:** The engineering stress appears to *decrease* even though the material is still intact, because $A_0$ (the original cross-section) is used in the denominator. In reality, the *true* stress is increasing.
- **Engineering Implication:** This is the region responsible for the high **toughness** of ductile plastics — the material absorbs enormous energy via chain alignment.

#### Region 5 — Strain Hardening (D → E)
- **Mechanism:** Once the entire gauge length has been necked (cold-drawn), the now-aligned chains resist further deformation. Covalent bonds along the backbone bear increasing load.
- **Visual Cue:** The curve climbs steeply again, reaching the **Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS)**.

#### Region 6 — Fracture (E → F)
- **Mechanism:** Covalent bond scission occurs; micro-voids coalesce into a crack; the specimen separates.
- **Visual Cue:** The curve terminates abruptly at the **fracture point F**, where the **Elongation-at-Break ($\varepsilon_b$)** is recorded.

### 2.2 Engineering vs. True Stress-Strain Curves

A crucial concept in polymer mechanics is the distinction between **engineering** and **true** quantities.

- **Engineering stress ($\sigma_{eng}$)** uses the *original* cross-section $A_0$. It drops during necking simply because $A_0$ is no longer representative.
- **True stress ($\sigma_{true}$)** uses the *instantaneous* cross-section $A$. It continues to rise monotonically until fracture.

Assuming **volume constancy** during plastic deformation (valid for most plastics at moderate strains):

$$A \cdot L = A_0 \cdot L_0$$

From this, the true stress and true strain are related to engineering values through:

$$\sigma_{true} = \sigma_{eng} (1 + \varepsilon_{eng})$$

$$\varepsilon_{true} = \ln(1 + \varepsilon_{eng})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the distinction matters:** When designing load-bearing plastic parts (gears, structural brackets), engineers must use **true stress** because the actual load-bearing cross-section has shrunk. Using engineering stress would dangerously underestimate the working load.

### 2.3 Classification of Plastics by Stress-Strain Signature

KTU examiners frequently test this classification. Plastics are grouped by the *shape* of their stress-strain curve:

| Class | Curve Shape | Typical ε_b | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Brittle Plastic** | Linear up to fracture; no yield | < 5 % | PS, PMMA, UF resin |
| **Strong & Brittle** | Linear, high σ, low ε | < 5 % | Glass-filled Nylon |
| **Ductile Plastic** | Clear yield + cold drawing | 5 – 50 % | LDPE, PP |
| **Tough Plastic** | Yield + extensive necking + strain hardening | 50 – 1000 % | HDPE, Nylon 6,6 |
| **Elastomeric** | Hyperelastic, J-shaped | > 1000 % | Natural rubber, PU |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Tip:** The temperature and strain rate dramatically shift the curve. Lowering temperature or increasing strain rate makes a *ductile* polymer behave like a *brittle* one. This is known as the **Brittle-Ductile Transition**.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A_0}$ | Engineering stress | MPa |
| 2 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0}$ | Engineering strain | dimensionless |
| 3 | $E = \dfrac{\sigma}{\varepsilon}$ | Young's modulus (Hooke's Law) | MPa |
| 4 | $\sigma_{true} = \sigma_{eng}(1 + \varepsilon_{eng})$ | True stress conversion | MPa |
| 5 | $\varepsilon_{true} = \ln(1 + \varepsilon_{eng})$ | True strain conversion | dimensionless |
| 6 | $U_T = \displaystyle\int_0^{\varepsilon_b} \sigma \, d\varepsilon$ | Tensile toughness (area under curve) | J/m³ |
| 7 | $\sigma_y = \sigma_{UTS} \cdot C$ | Yield-to-UTS ratio (C ≈ 0.5–0.9) | — |
| 8 | $\nu = -\dfrac{\varepsilon_{trans}}{\varepsilon_{axial}}$ | Poisson's ratio | dimensionless |
| 9 | $K = \dfrac{F}{(\Delta L / L_0)}$ | Tensile stiffness (geometric) | N/m |
| 10 | $\sigma_{UTS} = \dfrac{F_{max}}{A_0}$ | Ultimate tensile strength | MPa |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The stress-strain curve is the foundation of **plastic part design**. Every ISO-compliant polymer datasheet published by manufacturers (e.g., Sabic, BASF, DuPont) reports its $\sigma_y$, $\varepsilon_b$, and $E$ from this very curve. Design engineers use:

- $E$ → to predict **deflection** under load (e.g., plastic beam in a washing machine).
- $\sigma_y$ → to define the **maximum allowable service stress** with a safety factor.
- $\varepsilon_b$ → to estimate **formability** in thermoforming, blow molding, and film extrusion.
- Area under curve → to evaluate **energy absorption** in crash-resistant components (e.g., automotive bumpers).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Conversion from Engineering Stress to True Stress

**Given:** A cylindrical plastic tensile specimen of original cross-section $A_0$ and original length $L_0$ is stretched to a new length $L = L_0 + \Delta L$.

**Assumption:** Plastic deformation occurs at **constant volume** (incompressibility of the polymer matrix under uniaxial tension at moderate strains).

**Step 1 — Apply the Incompressibility Constraint**

The volume before deformation equals the volume after deformation:

$$V_0 = A_0 \cdot L_0$$

$$V = A \cdot L$$

Setting $V_0 = V$:

$$A_0 \cdot L_0 = A \cdot L$$

**Step 2 — Express Instantaneous Cross-Section $A$ in Terms of Engineering Strain**

Solve the above equation for $A$:

$$A = \dfrac{A_0 \cdot L_0}{L}$$

Now, recall that engineering strain is $\varepsilon_{eng} = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \dfrac{L - L_0}{L_0}$. Hence:

$$L = L_0 (1 + \varepsilon_{eng})$$

Substituting this expression for $L$ into the equation for $A$:

$$A = \dfrac{A_0 \cdot L_0}{L_0 (1 + \varepsilon_{eng})}$$

$$A = \dfrac{A_0}{1 + \varepsilon_{eng}}$$

**Step 3 — Express True Stress Using the Definition**

By definition, true stress is the instantaneous load divided by the instantaneous cross-section:

$$\sigma_{true} = \dfrac{F}{A}$$

Engineering stress uses the original cross-section:

$$\sigma_{eng} = \dfrac{F}{A_0}$$

**Step 4 — Form the Ratio**

Dividing the two expressions, we note that the load $F$ is common to both:

$$\dfrac{\sigma_{true}}{\sigma_{eng}} = \dfrac{F/A}{F/A_0} = \dfrac{A_0}{A}$$

Substitute the expression for $A$ from Step 2:

$$\dfrac{\sigma_{true}}{\sigma_{eng}} = \dfrac{A_0}{A_0 / (1 + \varepsilon_{eng})}$$

$$\dfrac{\sigma_{true}}{\sigma_{eng}} = (1 + \varepsilon_{eng})$$

**Step 5 — Final Result**

$$\sigma_{true} = \sigma_{eng} \, (1 + \varepsilon_{eng})$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{true} = \sigma_{eng} \, (1 + \varepsilon_{eng})}$$

This relationship is fundamental when the polymer specimen undergoes necking — the true stress continues to rise even though the engineering stress appears to fall.

### 3.2 Derivation: True Strain from Engineering Strain

**Given:** A differential element $dL$ of the specimen.

**Step 1 — Define True Strain Increment**

True strain is the natural logarithm of the length ratio:

$$d\varepsilon_{true} = \dfrac{dL}{L}$$

**Step 2 — Integrate from Initial to Final Length**

$$\varepsilon_{true} = \int_{L_0}^{L} \dfrac{dL}{L} = \ln \left( \dfrac{L}{L_0} \right)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Engineering Strain Relation**

Since $L = L_0 (1 + \varepsilon_{eng})$:

$$\varepsilon_{true} = \ln \left( \dfrac{L_0 (1 + \varepsilon_{eng})}{L_0} \right)$$

**Step 4 — Final Result**

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_{true} = \ln (1 + \varepsilon_{eng})}$$

This expression is critical for large deformations, where engineering strain underestimates the actual material distortion. For example, an engineering strain of 100 % ($\varepsilon_{eng} = 1.0$) corresponds to a true strain of $\ln(2) \approx 0.693$.

### 3.3 Derivation: Modulus of Toughness (Area Under the Stress-Strain Curve)

The **modulus of toughness** $U_T$ represents the energy absorbed per unit volume of the material up to fracture. It is mathematically the definite integral of the stress-strain curve from $0$ to $\varepsilon_b$:

$$U_T = \int_{0}^{\varepsilon_b} \sigma(\varepsilon) \, d\varepsilon$$

**Step 1 — Express as a Riemann Sum**

$$U_T = \lim_{n \to \infty} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sigma(\varepsilon_i) \cdot \Delta\varepsilon_i$$

**Step 2 — Units Verification**

Stress has units of MPa = $\dfrac{N}{m^2}$ = $\dfrac{J}{m^3}$. Strain is dimensionless. Therefore:

$$[U_T] = \dfrac{J}{m^3}$$

This is the **volumetric energy density** — the energy a cubic meter of plastic can absorb before fracturing.

**Step 3 — Approximation Using Trapezoidal Rule for a Real Curve**

For a curve with $n$ sampled points $(\varepsilon_i, \sigma_i)$:

$$U_T \approx \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \dfrac{(\sigma_i + \sigma_{i+1})}{2} (\varepsilon_{i+1} - \varepsilon_i)$$

This approximation is widely used in **finite element analysis (FEA)** software like ANSYS and Abaqus for plastic material models.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** The modulus of toughness is the *quantitative* measure of how "tough" a plastic is. A brittle plastic may have a high $\sigma_{UTS}$ but a tiny area under the curve (small $U_T$), while a ductile plastic like HDPE can absorb ~10× more energy per unit volume.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Region-by-Region Summary

| Region | Stress Range | Strain Range | Slope | Micro-Mechanism | Reversible? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| O → A (Elastic) | 0 → $\sigma_{prop}$ | 0 → $\varepsilon_{prop}$ | $E$ (constant) | Bond stretching | Yes |
| A → B (Yielding) | $\sigma_{prop}$ → $\sigma_y$ | $\varepsilon_{prop}$ → $\varepsilon_y$ | Decreasing | Chain uncoiling | Partially |
| B → C (Necking) | $\sigma_y$ → $\sigma_{neck}$ | $\varepsilon_y$ → $\varepsilon_{neck}$ | Negative | Chain alignment | No |
| C → D (Cold Draw) | $\sigma_{neck}$ → $\sigma_{UTS}$ | $\varepsilon_{neck}$ → $\varepsilon_{UTS}$ | Positive | Neck propagation | No |
| D → E (Hardening) | $\sigma_{UTS}$ (peak) | $\varepsilon_{UTS}$ → $\varepsilon_b$ | Very steep | Bond stretching of aligned chains | No |
| E → F (Fracture) | $\sigma_f$ | $\varepsilon_b$ (final) | Sudden drop | Bond scission, void coalescence | No |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — The Six Regions of the Stress-Strain Curve

The following diagram maps the conceptual flow of a tensile test on a typical ductile plastic, showing each region, the dominant mechanism, and the key stress-strain quantity extracted.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    O["ORIGIN - Specimen Unloaded"]
    A["REGION 1 - LINEAR ELASTIC - Stress proportional to strain"]
    B["REGION 2 - NON LINEAR ELASTIC - Bond angle distortion begins"]
    C["REGION 3 - YIELD POINT - Onset of permanent deformation"]
    D["REGION 4 - NECKING - Localized cross section reduction"]
    E["REGION 5 - COLD DRAWING - Neck propagation along gauge length"]
    F["REGION 6 - STRAIN HARDENING - Aligned chains resist further stretch"]
    G["REGION 7 - FRACTURE - Covalent bond scission and failure"]
    H["OUTPUT - Sigma_y, Sigma_UTS, Epsilon_break, E, U_T"]

    O --> A
    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
    F --> G
    G --> H

    style O fill:#e8f4f8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style A fill:#d4edda,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#d4edda,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#d1ecf1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#f5c6cb,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style H fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Material Classification Logic

The following block diagram shows how the shape of the stress-strain curve is used to classify a plastic into one of four engineering categories.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["START - Tensile Test Performed"]
    Q1{"Is there a clear yield point"}
    Q2{"Does neck propagate along the specimen"}
    Q3{"Is elongation at break greater than 50 percent"}
    Q4{"Is the curve nearly linear up to fracture"}

    BRITTLE["BRITTLE PLASTIC - Example PS or PMMA - High E, low epsilon_b"]
    STRONG_BRITTLE["STRONG AND BRITTLE - Example Glass Filled Nylon - High sigma, low epsilon"]
    DUCTILE["DUCTILE PLASTIC - Example LDPE or PP - Moderate sigma, moderate epsilon"]
    TOUGH["TOUGH PLASTIC - Example HDPE or Nylon 6,6 - Moderate sigma, very high epsilon"]
    ELASTO["ELASTOMERIC - Example Natural Rubber - Hyperelastic J shaped curve"]

    START --> Q1
    Q1 -->|No| Q4
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2
    Q2 -->|No| DUCTILE
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q3
    Q3 -->|Yes| TOUGH
    Q3 -->|No| DUCTILE
    Q4 -->|Yes| BRITTLE
    Q4 -->|No| ELASTO

    style START fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style BRITTLE fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style STRONG_BRITTLE fill:#f5c6cb,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style DUCTILE fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style TOUGH fill:#d4edda,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style ELASTO fill:#d1ecf1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Engineering vs. True Stress Conversion Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Experimental Data - Load F versus Elongation Delta L"]
    B["Step 1 - Compute Engineering Stress Sigma_eng equals F over A_0"]
    C["Step 2 - Compute Engineering Strain Epsilon_eng equals Delta L over L_0"]
    D["Step 3 - Apply Conversion Formulas"]
    E["Sigma_true equals Sigma_eng times bracket 1 plus Epsilon_eng bracket"]
    F["Epsilon_true equals ln bracket 1 plus Epsilon_eng bracket"]
    G["Final True Stress Strain Curve for Necking Analysis"]

    A --> B
    A --> C
    B --> D
    C --> D
    D --> E
    D --> F
    E --> G
    F --> G

    style A fill:#e8f4f8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#d1ecf1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#d4edda,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#d4edda,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper, Dec 2024 Style]**

**Q1.** Define the following terms with reference to the stress-strain curve of a typical plastic:
(a) Yield stress
(b) Ultimate tensile strength
(c) Elongation at break

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**(a) Yield Stress ($\sigma_y$):** It is the maximum stress that a plastic can sustain without undergoing permanent deformation. On the stress-strain curve, it corresponds to the local maximum stress at the onset of the plastic (non-recoverable) region. Beyond this point, polymer chains begin to slip past one another and undergo irreversible alignment.

**(b) Ultimate Tensile Strength ($\sigma_{UTS}$):** It is the maximum engineering stress that the plastic experiences during the entire tensile test, calculated as $\sigma_{UTS} = F_{max} / A_0$. It occurs at the end of the cold-drawing / strain-hardening region, just before the specimen begins to fracture.

**(c) Elongation at Break ($\varepsilon_b$):** It is the engineering strain at which the specimen fractures, expressed as a percentage. It is calculated as $\varepsilon_b = (L_f - L_0)/L_0 \times 100\,\%$. Tough plastics exhibit high $\varepsilon_b$ (often 100–800 %), while brittle plastics show $\varepsilon_b < 5\,\%$.

---

**Q2.** Differentiate between engineering stress and true stress. Why is the distinction important for plastics?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Engineering stress ($\sigma_{eng} = F/A_0$) uses the **original** cross-sectional area, while true stress ($\sigma_{true} = F/A$) uses the **instantaneous** cross-sectional area at the moment of measurement.

The distinction is important for plastics because they undergo **large deformations** and **necking**. During necking, the cross-section at the neck reduces significantly (by 50 % or more). If we use engineering stress, we incorrectly conclude that the material is weakening (because $A_0$ in the denominator is too large). In reality, the *true* stress is increasing as the polymer chains align and bear the load. Therefore, true stress is essential for accurate design calculations of plastic components subjected to large strains.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Module 2 Internal Choice Pattern]**
> *Note: KTU 2024 Scheme mandates an internal choice between two questions in Part B. Both options are provided below.*

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, May 2025 Style]**

**(a)** With the help of a neatly labelled stress-strain curve, describe the various regions a typical ductile thermoplastic passes through during a uniaxial tensile test. Mention the molecular mechanism responsible for each region. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the relationship between true stress and engineering stress for a plastic specimen undergoing large deformation under uniaxial tension. State the assumption clearly. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

A typical ductile thermoplastic (e.g., HDPE, polycarbonate, polypropylene) exhibits **six distinct regions** during a tensile test:

**Region 1 — Linear Elastic Region (Origin to Point A):**
The stress increases linearly with strain, obeying Hooke's law ($\sigma = E\varepsilon$). The molecular mechanism is the *reversible stretching of covalent bonds* along the polymer backbone and minor changes in bond angles. The deformation is fully recoverable upon unloading. The slope of this region gives the **Young's modulus (E)** of the plastic.

**Region 2 — Non-Linear Elastic / Yielding Region (A to B):**
The curve deviates from linearity and the slope progressively decreases. This corresponds to the *cooperative rotation and partial uncoiling* of polymer chains, with stretching of intermolecular van der Waals bonds. At point B (yield point), the stress reaches a maximum known as the **yield stress ($\sigma_y$)**, beyond which permanent plastic deformation occurs.

**Region 3 — Necking Region (B to C):**
A localized reduction in cross-section called a *neck* forms at the weakest point of the specimen. Polymer chains in the neck region begin to align along the tensile axis. The engineering stress appears to *decrease* because the denominator $A_0$ is fixed but the actual load-bearing area $A$ is shrinking.

**Region 4 — Cold Drawing / Neck Propagation (C to D):**
The neck *propagates* along the entire gauge length. During this region, polymer chains become highly oriented parallel to the loading direction. This cold drawing absorbs a large amount of energy, contributing to the **toughness** of the plastic.

**Region 5 — Strain Hardening (D to E):**
Once the entire specimen is cold-drawn, the highly aligned chains resist further deformation. The stress rises sharply, reaching the **Ultimate Tensile Strength ($\sigma_{UTS}$)** at point E. Covalent bonds along the aligned backbones now bear the maximum tensile load.

**Region 6 — Fracture (E to F):**
The specimen fails by *covalent bond scission* and micro-void coalescence. The curve terminates at point F, and the corresponding strain is the **elongation-at-break ($\varepsilon_b$)**.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Assumption:** The polymer is **incompressible** during plastic deformation. Hence volume remains constant.

Let $A_0$ = original cross-section, $L_0$ = original gauge length, and at any instant let $A$ = current cross-section and $L$ = current length.

**Step 1:** Volume constancy gives:
$$A_0 \cdot L_0 = A \cdot L$$

**Step 2:** The current length in terms of engineering strain is:
$$L = L_0 (1 + \varepsilon_{eng})$$

**Step 3:** Substituting and solving for $A$:
$$A = \dfrac{A_0}{1 + \varepsilon_{eng}}$$

**Step 4:** True stress is defined as $\sigma_{true} = F/A$ and engineering stress is $\sigma_{eng} = F/A_0$. Taking the ratio:
$$\dfrac{\sigma_{true}}{\sigma_{eng}} = \dfrac{A_0}{A} = (1 + \varepsilon_{eng})$$

**Step 5:** Therefore:
$$\boxed{\sigma_{true} = \sigma_{eng}(1 + \varepsilon_{eng})}$$

**Verification of units:** Both sides have units of MPa. The factor $(1+\varepsilon_{eng})$ is dimensionless. ∎

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, May 2025 Style]**

**(a)** Explain how the stress-strain behavior of a plastic can be used to classify it as brittle, ductile, or tough. Give one example of each and one typical industrial application. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A polypropylene tensile specimen has an original gauge length of 50 mm and an original cross-sectional area of 20 mm². During the test, when the load reaches 600 N, the gauge length becomes 53 mm and the cross-section reduces to 18.5 mm². Calculate the engineering stress, true stress, engineering strain, and true strain at this point. Comment on why the engineering and true stresses differ. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

The shape of the stress-strain curve is the *primary diagnostic tool* for classifying a plastic's mechanical behavior:

**(i) Brittle Plastic:**
The stress-strain curve is *linear up to fracture* — there is no yield point and negligible plastic deformation. The elongation-at-break is typically less than 5 %.
- **Example:** Polystyrene (PS) or Poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA).
- **Molecular Reason:** The bulky phenyl side groups in PS restrict chain mobility, and the chains fracture before they can reorient.
- **Industrial Application:** Transparent disposable cups, lab ware, optical lenses, light covers.

**(ii) Ductile Plastic:**
The curve shows a clear yield point followed by moderate plastic deformation. The elongation-at-break is in the range of 5 – 50 %.
- **Example:** Low-Density Polyethylene (LDPE) or Polypropylene (PP).
- **Molecular Reason:** Flexible aliphatic backbones (C–C and C–H bonds) and weak intermolecular forces allow chain slippage.
- **Industrial Application:** LDPE is used in plastic bags, squeeze bottles, and cling film due to its formability.

**(iii) Tough Plastic:**
The curve exhibits yielding, extensive cold drawing, and substantial strain hardening before fracture. The elongation-at-break is exceptionally high (50 – 1000 %).
- **Example:** High-Density Polyethylene (HDPE) or Nylon 6,6.
- **Molecular Reason:** Semi-crystalline morphology with strong interlamellar tie molecules allows enormous chain alignment before fracture.
- **Industrial Application:** HDPE is used in chemical storage tanks, gas pipes, and playground equipment due to its high impact resistance.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Students often confuse **strength** with **toughness**. A plastic can be *strong* (high $\sigma_{UTS}$) but *brittle* (low $\varepsilon_b$). Toughness = area under the *entire* stress-strain curve. A high-strength brittle plastic has *low toughness* because there is no plastic region.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given Data:**
- Original gauge length: $L_0 = 50$ mm
- Original cross-section: $A_0 = 20$ mm²
- Applied load: $F = 600$ N
- Current gauge length: $L = 53$ mm
- Current cross-section: $A = 18.5$ mm²

**Step 1: Engineering Stress**
$$\sigma_{eng} = \dfrac{F}{A_0} = \dfrac{600 \,\text{N}}{20 \,\text{mm}^2}$$
$$\boxed{\sigma_{eng} = 30 \,\text{N/mm}^2 = 30 \,\text{MPa}}$$

**Step 2: Engineering Strain**
$$\varepsilon_{eng} = \dfrac{L - L_0}{L_0} = \dfrac{53 - 50}{50} = \dfrac{3}{50}$$
$$\boxed{\varepsilon_{eng} = 0.06 \,\text{or}\, 6\%}$$

**Step 3: True Stress**
$$\sigma_{true} = \dfrac{F}{A} = \dfrac{600 \,\text{N}}{18.5 \,\text{mm}^2}$$
$$\boxed{\sigma_{true} = 32.43 \,\text{MPa}}$$

**Step 4: True Strain**
$$\varepsilon_{true} = \ln\left(\dfrac{L}{L_0}\right) = \ln\left(\dfrac{53}{50}\right)$$
$$\boxed{\varepsilon_{true} = \ln(1.06) = 0.0583}$$

**Step 5: Verification Using Conversion Formulas**
$$\sigma_{true} = \sigma_{eng}(1 + \varepsilon_{eng}) = 30 \times (1 + 0.06) = 30 \times 1.06 = 31.8 \,\text{MPa}$$

(Note: The slight discrepancy between 32.43 and 31.8 MPa arises because the constant-volume assumption is not perfectly exact; in reality, the cross-section changes involve small volumetric effects.)

$$\varepsilon_{true} = \ln(1 + \varepsilon_{eng}) = \ln(1.06) = 0.0583 \,\text{(confirmed)}$$

**Comment on Difference:**

The engineering stress (30 MPa) is **less than** the true stress (32.43 MPa) because the specimen has *necked* — its actual load-bearing cross-section has reduced from 20 mm² to 18.5 mm². Engineering stress uses the *original* cross-section in the denominator, which underestimates the actual stress on the surviving material. As deformation progresses, this gap widens significantly. For accurate design of plastic components experiencing large deformations, engineers must use **true stress** values.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. **Unit Errors (–1 mark):** Students frequently write $N/mm^2$ without converting to MPa. Although numerically equal, the standard KTU convention is to express stress in MPa.
> 2. **Confusing True and Engineering Strain (–2 marks):** Engineering strain is *not* simply $L/L_0$ — it is the *change* in length divided by original length. Many students mistakenly compute $\varepsilon_{true}$ as $L/L_0 - 1$, which is actually engineering strain.
> 3. **Forgetting Volume Constancy Assumption (–1 mark):** In the derivation question, the assumption must be stated explicitly at the start.
> 4. **Mixing up $\sigma_y$ and $\sigma_{UTS}$ (–1 mark):** Yield stress is at the *first* peak (onset of plasticity), while UTS is the *maximum* stress on the engineering curve, which may be lower (during necking) or higher (after strain hardening).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Stress-Strain Curve of a Typical Plastic**

- ✅ The stress-strain curve plots **engineering stress ($\sigma$)** vs. **engineering strain ($\varepsilon$)**.
- ✅ **Six regions** of a ductile plastic: Elastic → Yielding → Necking → Cold Drawing → Strain Hardening → Fracture.
- ✅ **Young's modulus (E)** = slope of the *initial linear region* — measures stiffness.
- ✅ **Yield stress ($\sigma_y$)** = stress at onset of permanent deformation.
- ✅ **Ultimate Tensile Strength ($\sigma_{UTS}$)** = *maximum* stress the engineering curve attains.
- ✅ **Elongation at break ($\varepsilon_b$)** = strain at fracture.
- ✅ **Toughness ($U_T$)** = total area under the engineering stress-strain curve = energy absorbed per unit volume.
- ✅ **Engineering stress** uses original area $A_0$; **True stress** uses instantaneous area $A$.
- ✅ **Conversion formulas** (valid under constant-volume assumption):
  $\sigma_{true} = \sigma_{eng}(1+\varepsilon_{eng})$ and $\varepsilon_{true} = \ln(1+\varepsilon_{eng})$.
- ✅ **Brittle plastics** (PS, PMMA): linear curve, low $\varepsilon_b$, high $E$.
- ✅ **Ductile plastics** (LDPE, PP): clear yield, moderate $\varepsilon_b$.
- ✅ **Tough plastics** (HDPE, Nylon 6,6): extensive cold drawing, very high $\varepsilon_b$.
- ✅ **Lowering temperature** or **increasing strain rate** shifts a plastic from ductile → brittle behavior.
- ✅ **Polymer chains align** along the tensile axis during cold drawing, increasing strength in that direction (this is the basis of **fiber drawing** for Kevlar, PET bottles, etc.).
- ✅ The stress-strain curve is obtained per **ASTM D638** (plastics) or **ISO 527** standards using a *dog-bone* specimen.
- ✅ For large deformations, always use **true stress–true strain** for accurate material modeling.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Comparison of the Stress-Strain Curves for Plastic
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPOT402 - POLYMER PHYSICS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Comparison of the Stress-Strain Curves for Plastic # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In the context of **PCPOT402 – Polymer Physics (Module 2)**, the **Stress–Strain Curve of a Plastic (Polymer)** is a graphical plot of the **nominal (engineering) stress**, $\sigma$ (applied load per unit original cross-sectional area, measured in **MPa** or **N/m²**), on the ordinate (Y-axis) versus the **nominal (engineering) strain**, $\varepsilon$ (elongation per unit original gauge length, dimensionless, often expressed as %), on the abscissa (X-axis). The curve captures the complete **mechanical deformation response** of a polymer solid under uniaxial tensile loading at a defined strain rate and temperature. For a generic **plastic (polymeric) material**, the curve is fundamentally different from that of a metal (which typically shows linear elasticity → yield → plastic flow → necking → fracture). Polymer curves exhibit five canonical archetypes that depend on: - **Degree of crystallinity** ($X_c$), - **Glass transition temperature** ($T_g$) and **melting temperature** ($T_m$), - **Molecular weight** ($M_w$) and **molecular weight distribution** (polydispersity index, PDI), - **Cross-link density** ($\nu_e$), - **Test temperature** relative to $T_g$, and - **Strain rate** ($\dot{\varepsilon}$). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Definition (verbatim style):** "The stress–strain behavior of a polymer is a fingerprint of its internal morphology — rigid/brittle, strong/tough, ductile, elastomeric, or tough — and is governed by the interplay of secondary bond rupture, chain disentanglement, and micro-void formation." ## 1.2 The Five Canonical Polymer Stress–Strain Categories According to the **KTU 2024 Module 2 syllabus**, the comparison is centered on the following five types: | # | Type | Typical Polymer Example | Key Morphology | |---|------|------------------------|----------------| | 1 | **Rigid / Brittle** | Polystyrene (PS), Poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA), Phenol-Formaldehyde (PF) | Highly cross-linked / high $T_g$ amorphous | | 2 | **Strong / Tough** | Polycarbonate (PC), Polyamide (Nylon 6,6), Poly(ethylene terephthalate) (PET) | Moderate crystallinity + chain orientation | | 3 | **Ductile (Cold Drawing)** | Low-Density Polyethylene (LDPE), Polypropylene (PP), Poly(vinyl chloride) (PVC – unplasticized) | Semi-crystalline, low $T_g$ | | 4 | **Elastomeric** | Natural Rubber (NR), Polybutadiene, Silicone Rubber, Polyurethane (PU) | Lightly cross-linked, $T \gg T_g$ | | 5 | **Tough / High Elongation** | Ultra-High Molecular Weight Polyethylene (UHMWPE), Toughened Polystyrene, ABS | Semi-crystalline + rubber-toughened blend | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition) > [!NOTE] > **The "Chewing Gum vs. Nylon Rope" Analogy:** > - **Brittle plastic (PS/PMMA)** is like a **hard candy stick** — pull it, and it snaps almost immediately with no warning. It barely stretches because the molecular chains are *frozen* in a rigid glassy state. > - **Ductile plastic (LDPE)** is like a piece of **soft chewing gum** — pull it, and it stretches enormously, then suddenly *necks* down to a thinner section, then keeps stretching, finally breaking. > - **Elastomeric polymer (rubber)** is like a **bungee cord** — pull it and it stretches to many times its original length, but **snaps back** to its original shape when released (recoverable elastic strain > 200%). > - **Tough polymer (Nylon)** is like a **woven nylon rope** — it is hard to break, absorbs huge amounts of energy (toughness = area under the curve), and combines strength + ductility. > - **Rigid / strong (PC)** is like a **toughened safety glass** — high load-bearing capacity with a moderate amount of give before failure. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight:** The "shape" of the stress–strain curve is a **direct visual map of the molecular mobility** of the polymer chains. A **steep, short curve** = chains are frozen; a **shallow, long curve** = chains can slide past each other freely; a **J-shaped curve** = chains orient and crystallize *during* stretching (strain-induced hardening). ## 1.4 Engineering Stress vs. True Stress — A Critical Distinction $$\sigma_{\text{engineering}} = \frac{F}{A_0} \quad ; \quad \sigma_{\text{true}} = \frac{F}{A_i}$$ $$\varepsilon_{\text{engineering}} = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \frac{L_i - L_0}{L_0} \quad ; \quad \varepsilon_{\text{true}} = \int_{L_0}^{L_i} \frac{dL}{L} = \ln\!\left(\frac{L_i}{L_0}\right)$$ where $A_0$ and $L_0$ are the *original* cross-sectional area and gauge length, and $A_i$, $L_i$ are the instantaneous values. KTU stress–strain diagrams in the syllabus are **engineering (nominal) curves**, unless explicitly stated otherwise. --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Anatomy of a Generic Polymer Stress–Strain Curve Every polymer stress–strain response can be decomposed into **five mechanistic regions** (in the order they typically appear): 1. **Region I — Linear Elastic Deformation (Hookean zone):** -
