Reversible, governed by bond stretching and small chain rotation. - Slope = **Young's Modulus, $E$** (units: **MPa** or **GPa**). - For glassy amorphous polymers, $E \approx 1$–$5$ GPa; for semicrystalline, $E \approx 0.5$–$15$ GPa; for elastomers, $E \approx 0.001$–$0.1$ GPa. 2. **Region II — Yield Point ($\sigma_y$, $\varepsilon_y$):** - The maximum on the engineering curve before permanent deformation. - In polymers, yielding involves **cavitation, shear yielding, or crazing** (whichever has the lower activation energy). 3. **Region III — Necking / Cold Drawing / Plastic Flow:** - Engineering stress *decreases* (geometric softening) or remains nearly constant. - A localized "neck" propagates along the gauge length; chains **align** along the draw direction. - For semicrystalline polymers (e.g., **HDPE, PP, Nylon**), necking produces a **highly oriented fibrillar structure** that is *stronger* than the original isotropic material — this is the origin of **strain-induced hardening**. 4. **Region IV — Strain Hardening:** - Re-uptake of load-bearing capacity; the curve bends upward. - Critical for **melt processing** (film blowing, fiber spinning) and for **impact toughness** (resistance to neck propagation). 5. **Region V — Fracture / Break ($\sigma_b$, $\varepsilon_b$):** - The terminal point. - **Toughness** = area under the entire $\sigma$–$\varepsilon$ curve (units: **J/m³** or **MPa**). ## 2.2 Detailed Comparison Table — All Five Categories | Feature | Rigid / Brittle | Strong / Tough | Ductile (Cold-Drawing) | Elastomeric | Tough / High-$\varepsilon$ | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Example** | PS, PMMA, PF | PC, Nylon 6,6, PET | LDPE, PP, uPVC | NR, Silicone, PU | UHMWPE, ABS, Toughened PS | | **$E$ (Young's Modulus)** | 2.5 – 4 GPa (high) | 1.5 – 3.5 GPa | 0.1 – 0.7 GPa | 0.001 – 0.1 GPa | 0.5 – 1.5 GPa | | **$\sigma_y$ (Yield Strength)** | ~50 – 100 MPa (brittle fracture first) | 50 – 80 MPa | 10 – 30 MPa | Very low (no true yield) | 20 – 50 MPa | | **$\varepsilon_b$ (Elongation at break)** | < 5 % | 50 – 150 % | 100 – 600 % | 500 – 1000 % | 100 – 500 % | | **Yield Point Visible?** | **No** (fractures before yielding) | Yes (rounded, diffuse) | Yes (sharp, localized neck) | No (hyperelastic) | Yes (diffuse) | | **Necking Behavior** | None | Mild / diffuse | **Sharp neck → cold draw** | None | Diffuse necking | | **Failure Mode** | Crazing + brittle fracture | Shear yielding + ductile | Crazing + fibril formation | Cross-link rupture | Massive crazing + shear | | **Fracture Energy ($G_c$)** | 0.1 – 1 kJ/m² (low) | 5 – 30 kJ/m² (high) | 2 – 20 kJ/m² | N/A (cyclic) | 10 – 50 kJ/m² | | **Morphology** | Glassy amorphous, $T \ll T_g$ | Semi-crystalline / oriented amorphous | Semi-crystalline, low $T_g$ | Lightly cross-linked, $T \gg T_g$ | Blend / high $M_w$ | | **Typical Use** | Disposable cups, optical lenses | Safety helmets, PET bottles | Squeeze bottles, films, pipes | Tires, seals, gaskets | Bullet-proof vests, dashboards | ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Relation | Meaning | Units | Notes for KTU | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\sigma = F / A_0$ | Engineering stress | **MPa** | Most KTU plots use this | | 2 | $\varepsilon = (L - L_0) / L_0$ | Engineering strain | dimensionless (%) | Used in tensile testing | | 3 | $E = \sigma / \varepsilon$ (in elastic region) | Young's Modulus | **MPa / GPa** | Slope of linear zone | | 4 | $\nu = -\varepsilon_{\text{lat}} / \varepsilon_{\text{axial}}$ | Poisson's ratio | dimensionless | For polymers, $\nu \approx 0.3$–$0.5$ | | 5 | $U_T = \int_0^{\varepsilon_b} \sigma \, d\varepsilon$ | Toughness (total energy/volume to break) | **J/m³ = MPa** | **Area under entire curve** | | 6 | $U_R = \frac{1}{2} \sigma_y \cdot \varepsilon_y$ | Resilience (recoverable elastic energy) | **J/m³** | Area under elastic region only | | 7 | $\sigma_{\text{true}} = \sigma_{\text{eng}} (1 + \varepsilon_{\text{eng}})$ | True stress (constant volume) | **MPa** | Valid in plastic region | | 8 | $\varepsilon_{\text{true}} = \ln (1 + \varepsilon_{\text{eng}})$ | True strain (constant volume) | dimensionless | Additive for multi-step | | 9 | $\sigma_y \propto \dot{\varepsilon}^{\,n}$ | Eyring-type strain rate dependence | **MPa** | $n$ is stress exponent; high $n$ = rate-sensitive (e.g., PC) | | 10 | $E \approx 3RT \cdot \rho / M_c$ (rubber) | Modulus of cross-linked rubber | **MPa** | From rubber elasticity theory; $M_c$ = mol. wt. between cross-links | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Tip — "How to read the KTU curve":** > - **Stiffness** → look at the *slope* of the initial linear portion. > - **Strength** → look at the *maximum y-value* (engineering). > - **Ductility** → look at the *x-value where the curve ends* (elongation at break). > - **Toughness** → estimate the *area under the curve* (always mention units). > - **Strain hardening** → look for a *re-upturn* before fracture. ## 2.4 The "Why" Behind Each Curve — Molecular Origin - **Rigid/Brittle:** Below $T_g$, only bond stretching is possible; no chain mobility → fractures via **crazing** (micro-void + fibril network perpendicular to stress). - **Strong/Tough:** Moderate chain mobility above $T_g$ allows **shear yielding** + chain orientation → energy is dissipated through both crazing and yielding. - **Ductile:** Sufficient free volume, low $T_g$, large amorphous fraction → chains can uncoil and slide → **neck propagation** consumes a lot of energy. - **Elastomeric:** Cross-linked network in a **rubbery state** ($T \gg T_g$) → chains are mobile but connected by permanent junctions → long-range **entropy-driven recoil**. - **Tough/High-$\varepsilon$:** Combination of high molecular weight (entanglements act as physical cross-links) + second-phase rubber particles that **cavitate and bridge crazes** (e.g., HIPS, ABS). --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Engineering Stress–Strain from a Tensile Test Load–Extension Record **Given:** A polymer dog-bone specimen with original cross-section $A_0 = 25 \,\text{mm}^2$ and gauge length $L_0 = 50 \,\text{mm}$ is pulled in a universal testing machine (UTM) at a constant crosshead speed of $5 \,\text{mm/min}$. The load–extension data is recorded as a $(F, \Delta L)$ pair. **To derive:** The transformation from raw $(F, \Delta L)$ data to the engineering $(\sigma, \varepsilon)$ curve. ### Step 1 — Convert Force to Stress By the **KTU Board definition**, engineering stress is the load divided by the **original** cross-sectional area: $$\sigma = \frac{F}{A_0} = \frac{F \,(\text{N})}{25 \times 10^{-6} \,(\text{m}^2)} \;\Rightarrow\; \sigma \;(\text{Pa})$$ The factor $10^{-6}$ arises because $A_0 = 25 \,\text{mm}^2 = 25 \times 10^{-6} \,\text{m}^2$. ### Step 2 — Convert Extension to Strain Engineering strain is the change in length divided by the **original** gauge length: $$\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \frac{\Delta L \,(\text{mm})}{50 \,(\text{mm})}$$ ### Step 3 — Sample Calculation Suppose at one instant the UTM records $F = 750 \,\text{N}$ and $\Delta L = 2.5 \,\text{mm}$: $$\sigma = \frac{750}{25 \times 10^{-6}} = 30 \times 10^{6} \,\text{Pa} = 30 \,\text{MPa}$$ $$\varepsilon = \frac{2.5}{50} = 0.05 = 5\,\%$$ ### Step 4 — Plotting Convention - **Y-axis:** Engineering stress, $\sigma$ (MPa), starting from zero. - **X-axis:** Engineering strain, $\varepsilon$ (%), starting from zero. - The slope of the *initial linear portion* gives the **Young's Modulus** $E$. $$\boxed{\;E = \left.\frac{d\sigma}{d\varepsilon}\right|_{\varepsilon \to 0}\;}$$ ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Conversion Between Engineering and True Stress / Strain **Assumption (KTU standard):** Plastic deformation occurs at **constant volume** (incompressibility of the polymer solid). ### Step 1 — Constant Volume Constraint $$A_0 L_0 = A_i L_i \;\Rightarrow\; A_i = \frac{A_0 L_0}{L_i} = \frac{A_0}{1 + \varepsilon_{\text{eng}}}$$ ### Step 2 — True Stress from Engineering Stress By definition, the engineering stress uses $A_0$ while true stress uses $A_i$: $$\sigma_{\text{true}} = \frac{F}{A_i} = \frac{F}{A_0} \cdot (1 + \varepsilon_{\text{eng}})$$ $$\boxed{\;\sigma_{\text{true}} = \sigma_{\text{eng}} (1 + \varepsilon_{\text{eng}})\;}$$ ### Step 3 — True Strain from Engineering Strain True strain is the integral of incremental true strain: $$\varepsilon_{\text{true}} = \int_{L_0}^{L_i} \frac{dL}{L} = \ln\!\left(\frac{L_i}{L_0}\right)$$ Since $L_i = L_0 (1 + \varepsilon_{\text{eng}})$: $$\boxed{\;\varepsilon_{\text{true}} = \ln(1 + \varepsilon_{\text{eng}})\;}$$ ### Step 4 — Worked Example (HDPE-like curve point) Take a point on the *engineering* curve: $\sigma_{\text{eng}} = 25 \,\text{MPa}$, $\varepsilon_{\text{eng}} = 1.5$ (i.e., 150 % elongation). $$\sigma_{\text{true}} = 25 \times (1 + 1.5) = 25 \times 2.5 = 62.5 \,\text{MPa}$$ $$\varepsilon_{\text{true}} = \ln(1 + 1.5) = \ln(2.5) = 0.9163 \;\Rightarrow\; 91.63\,\%$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Note:** A common trap is to plot the *true* curve continuously rising while the *engineering* curve shows a *post-yield drop* (necking). The "drop" in the engineering curve is **NOT** a material softening — it is a **geometric artifact** caused by the reduction in $A_0 \to A_i$. The true stress–strain curve monotonically increases up to fracture. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Theoretical Modulus of an Elastomer (Rubber Elasticity) For a lightly cross-linked elastomer at temperature $T \gg T_g$, the affine network model predicts: $$E = 3 \frac{\rho R T}{M_c}$$ where: - $\rho$ = density of the rubber (kg/m³), - $R = 8.314 \,\text{J/(mol·K)}$ = universal gas constant, - $T$ = absolute temperature (K), - $M_c$ = **molecular weight between successive cross-links** (kg/mol). ### Step 1 — Physical Meaning Each chain segment of molecular weight $M_c$ acts as an independent entropy spring. The smaller $M_c$ (denser cross-linking), the higher the modulus. ### Step 2 — Sample Calculation (Natural Rubber at 298 K) Given: $\rho = 920 \,\text{kg/m}^3$, $M_c = 5000 \,\text{g/mol} = 5 \,\text{kg/mol}$, $T = 298 \,\text{K}$. $$E = 3 \times \frac{920 \times 8.314 \times 298}{5} \,\text{Pa}$$ **Step-by-step:** $$3 \times 920 = 2760$$ $$8.314 \times 298 = 2477.572$$ $$2760 \times 2477.572 = 6{,}838{,}098.72$$ $$E = \frac{6{,}838{,}098.72}{5} = 1{,}367{,}619.74 \,\text{Pa} \approx 1.37 \,\text{MPa}$$ > [!NOTE] > This is the **theoretical lower-bound modulus** of NR. In practice, strain-induced crystallization and entanglements can raise it to **5–10 MPa** at moderate elongations — this is why rubber becomes *stiffer* the more you stretch it (the famous **J-shaped** or **upturn** in the elastomeric stress–strain curve). ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Toughness as Area Under the Engineering Curve For a piecewise-linear approximation of the curve, the **toughness** (energy per unit volume absorbed up to fracture) is: $$U_T = \int_0^{\varepsilon_b} \sigma(\varepsilon) \, d\varepsilon$$ ### Step 1 — Triangular Approximation (Lower Bound) Assume a perfectly triangular curve with $\sigma_y$ at $\varepsilon_y$ and $\sigma_b \approx \sigma_y$ at $\varepsilon_b$: $$U_T \approx \frac{1}{2} \sigma_y \varepsilon_b$$ ### Step 2 — Sample Calculation For a ductile polyolefin: $\sigma_y = 20 \,\text{MPa}$, $\varepsilon_b = 4$ (i.e., 400 %): $$U_T = \frac{1}{2} \times 20 \,\text{MPa} \times 4 = 40 \,\text{MJ/m}^3$$ For a brittle PS: $\sigma_y = 60 \,\text{MPa}$, $\varepsilon_b = 0.02$ (i.e., 2 %): $$U_T = \frac{1}{2} \times 60 \times 0.02 = 0.6 \,\text{MJ/m}^3$$ ### Step 3 — Toughness Ratio $$\frac{U_T^{\text{ductile}}}{U_T^{\text{brittle}}} = \frac{40}{0.6} \approx 66.7$$ The ductile polymer absorbs **~67× more energy per unit volume** before fracture than the brittle one — this is the quantitative reason why LDPE films survive impact while PS cups shatter. ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — How Each Curve Type Reacts to External Variables | Variable | Rigid / Brittle | Strong / Tough | Ductile (CD) | Elastomeric | Tough / High-$\varepsilon$ | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **$\uparrow T$ (toward $T_g$)** | Crazes more, $E \downarrow$ | Becomes ductile (transition) | Becomes rubbery, necks disappear | Stiffens (Gough–Joule effect reversed) | Becomes more compliant | | **$\uparrow \dot{\varepsilon}$ (faster pull)** | $E$ and $\sigma_y \uparrow$ (embrittlement) | $\sigma_y \uparrow$, $\varepsilon_b \downarrow$ | $\sigma_y \uparrow$, neck sharper | $E \uparrow$ | $\sigma_y \uparrow$ | | **Plasticizer addition** | Becomes ductile (e.g., $\uparrow$ impact PS) | Softens $T_g$ | Softer, more ductile | Softens | Softens | | **Cross-linking $\uparrow$** | Little effect (already rigid) | Slight $E \uparrow$ | Reduces cold draw | **$E \uparrow$, $M_c \downarrow$** | Reduces chain slip | | **$M_w \uparrow$** | Little effect | $E \uparrow$ (modest) | $\varepsilon_b \uparrow \uparrow$ (entanglements) | $E \uparrow$ (chain-end dilution) | $\varepsilon_b \uparrow$ dramatically | | **Crystallinity $\uparrow$** | (N/A — amorphous) | $E$ and $\sigma_y \uparrow$ | $E \uparrow$, $\sigma_y \uparrow$, $\varepsilon_b$ peaks at ~40–60% | (N/A) | $E$ and $\sigma_y \uparrow$ | --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of Polymer Stress–Strain Classification The following block diagram maps the **decision logic** a KTU student should follow to classify a stress–strain curve and the **molecular origin** of each classification. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Observe Stress-Strain Curve] --> B{Does the curve show
fracture before any yield?} B -- YES --> C[Type 1: Rigid / Brittle] C --> C1["Example: PS, PMMA, PF"] C --> C2["Morphology: Glassy amorphous T << Tg"] C --> C3["Mechanism: Crazing only"] B -- NO --> D{Is elongation at break
greater than 100 percent?} D -- NO --> E[Type 2: Strong / Tough] E --> E1["Example: PC, Nylon 6,6, PET"] E --> E2["Mechanism: Shear yielding + crazing"] E --> E3["Key feature: Diffuse neck + rounded yield"] D -- YES --> F{Does the curve show
a sharp yield drop and
flat plateau?} F -- YES --> G[Type 3: Ductile - Cold Drawing] G --> G1["Example: LDPE, PP, uPVC"] G --> G2["Mechanism: Neck propagation + chain alignment"] G --> G3["Key feature: Strain hardening after plateau"] F -- NO --> H{Is the curve fully recoverable
and J-shaped?} H -- YES --> I[Type 4: Elastomeric] I --> I1["Example: NR, Silicone, PU"] I --> I2["Mechanism: Entropy-driven recoil"] I --> I3["Key feature: E proportional to rho R T over Mc"] H -- NO --> J[Type 5: Tough / High Elongation] J --> J1["Example: UHMWPE, ABS, HIPS"] J --> J2["Mechanism: Crazing bridged by rubber particles"] J --> J3["Key feature: Massive energy dissipation"] style A fill:#E8F0FE,stroke:#1A73E8,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style C fill:#FAD2CF,stroke:#CC0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style E fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#F4B400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style G fill:#D5E8D4,stroke:#82B366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style I fill:#DAE8FC,stroke:#6C8EBF,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style J fill:#E1D5E7,stroke:#9673A6,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style B fill:#F5F5F5,stroke:#333333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style D fill:#F5F5F5,stroke:#333333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style F fill:#F5F5F5,stroke:#333333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style H fill:#F5F5F5,stroke:#333333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Engineering vs. True Curve Relationship ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Raw Data: F vs Delta L"] --> B["Divide F by A0
and Delta L by L0"] B --> C["Engineering sigma vs epsilon"] C --> D{"Necking region?"} D -- NO --> E["Engineering = True"] D -- YES --> F["Apply correction:
sigma_true = sigma_eng x 1 plus epsilon
epsilon_true = ln 1 plus epsilon_eng"] F --> G["True sigma vs epsilon
(monotonically rising)"] E --> H["Plot for KTU Exam"] G --> H style A fill:#E8F0FE,stroke:#1A73E8,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style C fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#F4B400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style G fill:#D5E8D4,stroke:#82B366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style H fill:#E1D5E7,stroke:#9673A6,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 ASCII Schematic — Side-by-Side Qualitative Curve Comparison The following ASCII schematic represents the *qualitative shape* of each of the five curve types, drawn schematically for comparison. (Note: For KTU Board exams, the student is expected to *reproduce* similar sketches by hand.) ``` STRESS (sigma) ^ Type-1 Rigid/Brittle | / | / | / <- fractures at <5% strain (PS, PMMA) | / |------/------ | Type-2 Strong/Tough | ./^ | ./ | ./ <- diffuse yield, 50-150% (PC, Nylon) |________________________________________/.____ | Type-3 Ductile (Cold Drawing) | ____________ | / \____ <- sharp yield + long plateau (LDPE, PP) | / \ | / \___/ <- strain hardening + fracture |____/________________________ | Type-4 Elastomeric | / | ./ <- J-shaped, fully recoverable (NR, Silicone) | ./ | ./ | ./ |____________________./__________________________ | Type-5 Tough / High Elongation | ___________________ | ./ <- high area under curve (UHMWPE, ABS) | ./ | ./_/ |_________________._____________________________ +----------------------------------------------------> STRAIN (epsilon) 0 0.5 1 2 3 5 10 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reading the KTU Schematic Above:** > - **Toughness** (energy to break) = **area under each curve**. > - The **Type-5 curve** has the **largest area** under it → maximum toughness. > - The **Type-1 curve** has the **smallest area** under it → minimum toughness (most brittle). > - **Young's Modulus** = slope of the *initial linear portion* of each curve. Steepest = Type-1 (brittle), shallowest = Type-4 (elastomer). --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** > *Define the terms "engineering stress" and "engineering strain" as applied to a polymeric tensile test specimen. Why is the engineering (nominal) stress–strain curve preferred over the true stress–strain curve for routine polymer quality-control testing?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Engineering stress, $\sigma = F / A_0$**, is the instantaneous tensile load $F$ divided by the *original* cross-sectional area $A_0$ of the specimen. (1 mark) - **Engineering strain, $\varepsilon = (L - L_0) / L_0$**, is the change in length divided by the *original* gauge length $L_0$. (1 mark) - The engineering curve is preferred for **routine QC testing** because (i) it does not require continuous measurement of the *instantaneous* (reducing) cross-section, which is experimentally very difficult for polymers due to necking and crazing; (ii) it is the direct output of standard UTM software; (iii) it allows straightforward comparison of stiffness ($E$), yield stress ($\sigma_y$), and elongation at break ($\varepsilon_b$) across batches. (1 mark) --- > **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** > *Distinguish clearly between "resilience" and "toughness" of a polymeric material. Which area on the stress–strain curve corresponds to each property?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Resilience ($U_R$)** is the *recoverable* elastic energy per unit volume that a polymer can absorb *without* permanent deformation. It corresponds to the **area under the stress–strain curve up to the elastic limit / yield point**: $U_R = \tfrac{1}{2}\sigma_y \varepsilon_y$. (1.5 marks) - **Toughness ($U_T$)** is the *total* energy per unit volume the polymer absorbs *up to fracture*, including both elastic and plastic contributions. It corresponds to the **area under the entire stress–strain curve from zero strain to the break point**: $U_T = \int_0^{\varepsilon_b} \sigma \, d\varepsilon$. (1.5 marks) - A high-resilience, low-toughness polymer → brittle. A low-resilience, high-toughness polymer → tough but compliant (e.g., elastomer). An ideal engineering plastic (e.g., PC) is both high in resilience *and* high in toughness. --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice Format) > **Q3. (A) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2]** > *(a) [7 marks]* Draw and compare the engineering stress–strain curves of the following five classes of polymeric materials, clearly labelling the salient features: **(i) rigid and brittle, (ii) strong and tough, (iii) ductile, (iv) elastomeric, (v) tough with high elongation.** Give **one representative engineering polymer** for each class and state the dominant molecular mechanism of deformation. > *(b) [7 marks]* A sample of **Nylon 6,6** has an original cross-section $A_0 = 20 \,\text{mm}^2$ and gauge length $L_0 = 40 \,\text{mm}$. Under a tensile test, the load reaches a maximum of $F_{\max} = 800 \,\text{N}$ at an extension of $\Delta L = 4.0 \,\text{mm}$, after which necking occurs. The specimen finally fractures at an extension $\Delta L_b = 32 \,\text{mm}$. > **(i)** Calculate the **engineering stress at the maximum load** (i.e., the engineering tensile strength). **(ii)** Calculate the **engineering strain at fracture** (in %). **(iii)** If the slope of the initial linear elastic portion of the curve is $E = 2.8 \,\text{GPa}$, calculate the **strain at the proportional limit**. **(iv)** Estimate the **resilience** of the material. ### Model Solution (A): #### Part (a) — 7 marks The student must draw the **five curves on a single $\sigma$–$\varepsilon$ plot**, labelled (i)–(v), with axes marked *Stress (MPa)* and *Strain (%)*. The following table summarizes what the answer must contain: | # | Class | Representative Polymer | Salient Curve Features | Dominant Molecular Mechanism | |---|---|---|---|---| | (i) | Rigid / Brittle | **Polystyrene (PS)** | Steep linear region, fractures at $\varepsilon < 5\,\%$, **no yield point** | Crazing (micro-void + fibrils) | | (ii) | Strong / Tough | **Polycarbonate (PC)** | Steep initial slope, rounded/diffuse yield at $\sigma_y \approx 60\,\text{MPa}$, $\varepsilon_b \approx 100\,\%$, no sharp neck | Shear yielding + craze toughening | | (iii) | Ductile | **Low-Density Polyethylene (LDPE)** | Initial linear region, **sharp yield point**, long **cold-drawing plateau** at nearly constant $\sigma$, strain-hardens before fracture at $\varepsilon_b \approx 400\,\%$ | Neck propagation + chain orientation + strain-induced crystallization | | (iv) | Elastomeric | **Natural Rubber (NR)** | Very low initial modulus, **J-shaped curve**, no yield, large recoverable $\varepsilon$ (500–1000 %), hysteresis loop on unloading | Entropy-driven recoil of lightly cross-linked network | | (v) | Tough / High-$\varepsilon$ | **UHMWPE / ABS** | Moderate initial slope, large yield, **massive area under curve**, $\varepsilon_b \approx 300\,\%$+ | Crazing bridged by rubbery second-phase particles (ABS) or extreme entanglement density (UHMWPE) | **Mark distribution (7 marks):** - Correctly drawn *qualitative* shapes of all five curves (with labelled axes): **3 marks**. - One correct engineering example for each of the five: **2 marks**. - One dominant molecular mechanism for each: **2 marks**. #### Part (b) — 7 marks **(i) Engineering stress at maximum load (1 mark):** $$\sigma_{\max} = \frac{F_{\max}}{A_0} = \frac{800 \,\text{N}}{20 \times 10^{-6} \,\text{m}^2} = \frac{800}{20 \times 10^{-6}}$$ $$\sigma_{\max} = 40 \times 10^{6} \,\text{Pa} = 40 \,\text{MPa}$$ **(ii) Engineering strain at fracture (2 marks):** $$\varepsilon_b = \frac{\Delta L_b}{L_0} = \frac{32 \,\text{mm}}{40 \,\text{mm}} = 0.80 = 80\,\%$$ **(iii) Strain at proportional limit using Hooke's law (2 marks):** The proportional limit lies on the linear elastic region where $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$. Assuming the proportional limit coincides with the maximum load (a valid assumption for a strong/tough polymer like Nylon 6,6): $$\varepsilon_p = \frac{\sigma_{\max}}{E} = \frac{40 \times 10^{6} \,\text{Pa}}{2.8 \times 10^{9} \,\text{Pa}}$$ $$\varepsilon_p = \frac{40}{2800} = 0.014286 = 1.43\,\%$$ **(iv) Resilience (2 marks):** The resilience is the area under the *elastic* portion of the curve (triangular approximation): $$U_R = \frac{1}{2} \sigma_{\max} \cdot \varepsilon_p = \frac{1}{2} \times 40 \,\text{MPa} \times 0.014286$$ $$U_R = 0.2857 \,\text{MPa} = 0.2857 \,\text{MJ/m}^3 = 285.7 \,\text{kJ/m}^3$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > - **Unit mismatch in (i):** The most common error is to forget that $A_0$ must be in m², *not* mm². This leads to an answer of $0.04$ instead of $40 \,\text{MPa}$ — a 6×10⁶ error! Always convert. > - **Strain in (ii):** The extension $\Delta L_b$ is *additional* length, *not* final length. The final length is $L_0 + \Delta L_b = 72 \,\text{mm}$. Engineering strain uses $L_0$ in the denominator. > - **Strain in (iii):** A student who uses $E$ in GPa and $\sigma$ in MPa *without* converting both to the same unit (Pa) will get the wrong answer. Always write both in Pa first. > - **Resilience in (iv):** Resilience is the *elastic* area only — using $\varepsilon_b = 80\,\%$ would give the toughness, not the resilience. This is a 1-mark to 2-mark penalty every year. --- > **Q3. (B) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2, Alternative Choice]** > *(a) [7 marks]* With the help of well-labelled sketches, explain the **engineering** and **true** stress–strain behaviour of a ductile, cold-drawing polymer such as **LDPE**. Why does the engineering curve show a "drop" after yielding, while the true curve continues to rise? Derive the relationship between the two. > *(b) [7 marks]* A **natural rubber** vulcanizate has density $\rho = 920 \,\text{kg/m}^3$, average molecular weight between cross-links $M_c = 6 \,\text{kg/mol}$, and is tested at $T = 300 \,\text{K}$. Calculate the **theoretical small-strain Young's modulus** $E$ from rubber elasticity theory. If the same rubber is then stretched to an engineering strain of $\varepsilon = 4$ (i.e., 400 %), what are the corresponding **true stress** and **true strain** values, assuming the engineering stress at that point is $\sigma_{\text{eng}} = 1.2 \,\text{MPa}$? Comment on the practical significance of any discrepancy. ### Model Solution (B): #### Part (a) — 7 marks **Engineering vs True — Why They Differ (3 marks):** For an LDPE specimen under tension: - The **engineering stress** is defined as $\sigma_{\text{eng}} = F/A_0$, where $A_0$ is the *original* cross-section. - As the specimen necks, the *instantaneous* cross-section $A_i$ becomes **smaller** than $A_0$. - The true stress is $\sigma_{\text{true}} = F/A_i$. Since $A_i < A_0$, we have $\sigma_{\text{true}} > \sigma_{\text{eng}}$ in the post-yield region. - The "drop" in the engineering curve after the yield point is therefore a **geometric artifact**, not a true material softening. Physically, the material is *still* getting stronger (chains are aligning and strain-hardening); the apparent drop simply reflects the fact that the denominator $A_0$ overestimates the actual load-bearing area. - In the true stress–strain curve (which uses the *current* area $A_i$), the stress continues to rise monotonically up to fracture. **Derivation (4 marks):** Assuming **incompressible plastic flow** (constant volume, valid for polymers): $$A_0 L_0 = A_i L_i \;\Rightarrow\; A_i = \frac{A_0 L_0}{L_i} = \frac{A_0}{1 + \varepsilon_{\text{eng}}}$$ True stress: $$\sigma_{\text{true}} = \frac{F}{A_i} = \frac{F}{A_0 / (1 + \varepsilon_{\text{eng}})} = \frac{F}{A_0} (1 + \varepsilon_{\text{eng}}) = \sigma_{\text{eng}} (1 + \varepsilon_{\text{eng}})$$ True strain: $$\varepsilon_{\text{true}} = \int_{L_0}^{L_i} \frac{dL}{L} = \ln\!\left(\frac{L_i}{L_0}\right) = \ln(1 + \varepsilon_{\text{eng}})$$ **Sketch to include (must be drawn on answer sheet):** - A single $\sigma$–$\varepsilon$ plot with **two curves** sharing the same axes. - Engineering curve: rises linearly → yield point → *drops* → plateau (cold drawing) → strain-hardens up → fracture. - True curve: rises linearly → yield point → **continues to rise** monotonically → fracture (no drop, no plateau of zero slope; plateau of true curve has a positive slope). - Label: $\sigma_y$, $\varepsilon_y$, $\sigma_{\text{eng, fracture}}$, $\sigma_{\text{true, fracture}}$ (the latter is higher than the former). #### Part (b) — 7 marks **(i) Theoretical small-strain Young's modulus from rubber elasticity theory (3 marks):** $$E = \frac{3 \rho R T}{M_c}$$ Substituting: - $\rho = 920 \,\text{kg/m}^3$ - $R = 8.314 \,\text{J/(mol·K)}$ - $T = 300 \,\text{K}$ - $M_c = 6 \,\text{kg/mol}$ Step-by-step: $$3 \rho = 3 \times 920 = 2760 \,\text{kg/m}^3$$ $$R T = 8.314 \times 300 = 2494.2 \,\text{J/mol}$$ $$\rho R T = 920 \times 2494.2 = 2{,}294{,}664 \,\text{J/(m}^3 \cdot \text{mol)}$$ $$3 \rho R T = 3 \times 2{,}294{,}664 = 6{,}883{,}992 \,\text{J/m}^3$$ $$E = \frac{6{,}883{,}992}{6} = 1{,}147{,}332 \,\text{Pa} \approx 1.15 \,\text{MPa}$$ **(ii) True stress and true strain at $\varepsilon_{\text{eng}} = 4$ (3 marks):** True strain: $$\varepsilon_{\text{true}} = \ln(1 + \varepsilon_{\text{eng}}) = \ln(1 + 4) = \ln(5)$$ $$\varepsilon_{\text{true}} = 1.6094 \;\Rightarrow\; 160.94\,\%$$ True stress: $$\sigma_{\text{true}} = \sigma_{\text{eng}} (1 + \varepsilon_{\text{eng}}) = 1.2 \,\text{MPa} \times (1 + 4) = 1.2 \times 5$$ $$\sigma_{\text{true}} = 6.0 \,\text{MPa}$$ **(iii) Practical significance (1 mark):** The measured engineering stress of $1.2 \,\text{MPa}$ at $400\,\%$ elongation corresponds to a *true* stress of $6.0 \,\text{MPa}$ — a **5× discrepancy**. This is critically important for: - **Design calculations** of rubber components (seals, vibration dampers) operating at high strains, where underestimating the true stress could lead to under-designed cross-sections. - **FEA simulations** of hyperelastic materials, which must use the true stress–strain data as input. - **Crystallization modelling**: at high true strain, NR undergoes **strain-induced crystallization**, which is the reason for the steep upturn in the J-shaped curve and is not captured by the small-strain $E$ formula above (which assumes a Gaussian network). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout for Q3(B):** > - **Part (a):** Do not state that "the material is softening after yield" — this is *wrong*. State clearly that the drop is a *geometric* effect due to area reduction. > - **Part (b)(i):** Watch the units of $M_c$ — it must be in **kg/mol** (not g/mol). Using $M_c = 6000$ g/mol = 6 kg/mol gives the correct answer; using 6000 without conversion gives an answer 1000× too small. > - **Part (b)(ii):** Many students compute $\varepsilon_{\text{true}}$ but forget the absolute value check — since $\varepsilon_{\text{eng}} = 4 > 0$, $\varepsilon_{\text{true}}$ must also be positive. The natural log of a number greater than 1 is positive (✓). The natural log of a number less than 1 (compression) is negative (✓). State this explicitly. > - **Part (b)(iii):** The 5× discrepancy in stress is *not* negligible. Mentioning it qualitatively (without numbers) is worth at most 0.5 marks; including the quantitative comparison is worth the full 1 mark. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam** - [ ] **Engineering stress = $F/A_0$**; **Engineering strain = $\Delta L / L_0$**. (KTU standard convention.) - [ ] **True stress = $\sigma_{\text{eng}} (1 + \varepsilon_{\text{eng}})$**; **True strain = $\ln(1 + \varepsilon_{\text{eng}})$** (constant-volume assumption). - [ ] **Young's Modulus $E$** = slope of the *initial linear* part of the $\sigma$–$\varepsilon$ curve. - [ ] **Resilience $U_R$** = area under the *elastic* portion (triangular: $\tfrac{1}{2}\sigma_y \varepsilon_y$). - [ ] **Toughness $U_T$** = area under the *entire* curve up to fracture. - [ ] **Five curve types** and their one-line characteristics: - *Rigid/Brittle* — short, steep, no yield (PS, PMMA, PF). - *Strong/Tough* — high modulus, diffuse yield, $\varepsilon_b \sim$ 50–150 % (PC, Nylon 6,6, PET). - *Ductile (Cold-Drawing)* — sharp yield, long plateau, strain-hardening upturn (LDPE, PP, uPVC). - *Elastomeric* — J-shaped, fully recoverable, $T \gg T_g$ (NR, Silicone, PU). - *Tough/High-$\varepsilon$* — largest area under curve, second-phase toughening (UHMWPE, ABS, HIPS). - [ ] **Mechanisms of plastic deformation in polymers** (in order of activation energy): **crazing → shear yielding → necking/cold-drawing → strain-induced crystallization**. - [ ] **Crazes** = micro-void + fibril networks, perpendicular to tensile stress, characteristic of **glassy amorphous** polymers. - [ ] **Necking + cold drawing** = characteristic of **semi-crystalline** polymers with low $T_g$. - [ ] **Rubber elasticity** = entropic; modulus $E = 3 \rho R T / M_c$ (theoretically); strain-induced crystallization causes the **J-curve upturn** in real rubbers. - [ ] **Poisson's ratio** for polymers: typically $0.30$–$0.50$ (closer to 0.5 for elastomers due to near-incompressibility). - [ ] **Strain-rate effect**: increasing $\dot{\varepsilon}$ shifts the curve up and to the left (stiffer, stronger, but more brittle) — equivalent to lowering $T$. - [ ] **Temperature effect**: increasing $T$ shifts the curve down and to the right (softer, more ductile) — equivalent to lowering $\dot{\varepsilon}$. This is the **time–temperature superposition (TTS)** principle. - [ ] **Common KTU numerical traps**: unit mismatches (MPa ↔ Pa), $\Delta L$ vs $L$ confusion, $A_0$ vs $A_i$ confusion, forgetting the natural log in true-strain derivation. - [ ] **Sketching tip for the exam**: when asked to draw multiple stress–strain curves, **always draw them on a single $\sigma$–$\varepsilon$ plot with consistent axes**, label every feature (yield, neck, plateau, fracture), and write the *polymer name* next to each curve. ---

topics.title: Rubber and Fibre
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT402 - POLYMER PHYSICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Rubber and Fibre

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Rubber? (The Engineering Definition)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rubber (Elastomer):** A polymer material that exhibits **large, reversible elastic deformation** (typically up to 100–1000% strain) under relatively small applied stress, returning to its original shape when the stress is released. The elasticity is primarily **entropic in origin**, arising from conformational changes of long, flexible polymer chains cross-linked into a three-dimensional network.

KTU 2024 classification places rubber under the **elastomeric** category of polymers, distinguished from fibres (high modulus, low strain) and plastics (intermediate behavior). The most widely used natural rubber is **cis-1,4-polyisoprene**, with the repeating unit:

$$-\text{(CH}_2\text{-C(CH}_3\text{)=CH-CH}_2\text{)}_n-$$

### 1.2 What is a Fibre?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fibre (Fiber):** A polymer characterized by a very **high axial ratio** (length-to-diameter), high **tensile modulus and strength**, low extensibility, and a high degree of **molecular orientation and crystallinity** along the fibre axis. Fibres are typically used in textile, reinforcement, and composite applications.

A fibre's mechanical strength arises from **highly oriented, partially crystalline polymer chains** aligned parallel to the fibre axis, often reinforced by strong intermolecular forces (hydrogen bonding, dipole interactions, or van der Waals forces).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Rubber Analogy — "The Random Walk Spring":**
> Imagine a long, writhing **spaghetti strand** floating freely in a bowl. Its most probable shape is a **random coil** (a tangled mess). Now imagine millions of these strands, joined at occasional points like a **fish-net**. When you pull the net, the strands are forced to **straighten out** — they don't actually stretch their bonds! Instead, they have **fewer possible conformations**, so by the second law of thermodynamics, **entropy decreases**, and the network pulls back when released. This is **entropic elasticity** — the same physics that makes a rubber band snap back.

> [!NOTE]
> **Fibre Analogy — "The Bundle of Broomsticks":**
> Take thousands of dry spaghetti sticks and glue them together lengthwise with strong cement. The bundle becomes **rigid and strong along its length** but brittle across it. This is exactly how a fibre works: **highly aligned chains**, partially crystallized regions acting like cement, and strong secondary bonding along the axis give it enormous tensile strength.

### 1.4 Key Physical Constants and Metrics

- **Avogadro's Number:** $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23} \text{ mol}^{-1}$
- **Boltzmann Constant:** $k_B = 1.381 \times 10^{-23} \text{ J/K}$
- **Universal Gas Constant:** $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$
- **Typical Elongation at Break — Rubber:** **300–800%**
- **Typical Elongation at Break — Fibre:** **5–30%**
- **Glass Transition Temperature (Tg) of Natural Rubber:** approx. **−73 °C**
- **Modulus Range — Elastomer:** $10^5$–$10^6$ Pa
- **Modulus Range — Fibre:** $10^9$–$10^{11}$ Pa

### 1.5 Broad Classification Snapshot

| Class | Strain at Break | Modulus | Origin of Elasticity | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Elastomer (Rubber)** | 100–1000% | Low ($10^5$–$10^6$ Pa) | Entropic | Natural rubber, SBR, Silicone |
| **Fibre** | 5–30% | Very High ($10^9$–$10^{11}$ Pa) | Energetic (bond stretching) | Nylon-6, PET, Kevlar |
| **Plastic** | 5–100% | Intermediate | Mixed | Polyethylene, PVC |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Nature of Rubber Elasticity — Why Rubber Bounces Back

Rubber elasticity is fundamentally different from the elasticity of metals or ceramics. In metals, stretching involves **changing interatomic bond lengths** (energy storage). In rubber, the covalent bond lengths and angles remain essentially unchanged during deformation. The restoring force is **entropic**.

#### Logical Breakdown:

1. **Polymer chains in the unstretched state** adopt a vast number of random-coil conformations (high entropy).
2. **When stretched**, chains are forced into more extended, aligned conformations (low entropy).
3. **Thermodynamics requires** the system to minimize its Helmholtz free energy $A = U - TS$.
4. **Upon release**, the system spontaneously returns to the high-entropy coiled state.
5. **Result:** A macroscopic restoring force with no change in internal energy at equilibrium.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight (Joule, 1857; Gough, 1805):** A stretched rubber band **heats up** upon further stretching and **contracts when warmed** — exactly the opposite behavior of a metal spring. This is the **thermoelastic inversion effect**, and it confirms the entropic origin of rubber elasticity.

### 2.2 Stress–Strain Behavior Comparison

For the same applied stress, rubber deforms enormously while a fibre barely yields. The figure below captures the qualitative behavior:

$$\sigma_{fibre} \gg \sigma_{rubber} \quad \text{for the same } \varepsilon \text{ in the elastic region}$$

### 2.3 Vulcanization — The Birth of Useful Rubber

Raw natural rubber is a **thermoplastic** that flows and creeps under load. Charles Goodyear's discovery (1839) of **vulcanization** cross-links the chains with sulfur:

- **Raw rubber:** Chains slide past one another → permanent deformation.
- **Vulcanized rubber:** Sulfur bridges (–S–S–) connect adjacent chains → **permanent elastic network**.
- **Ebonite (hard rubber):** Excess sulfur (~30–40%) yields a fully cross-linked, rigid thermoset.

Cross-link density directly controls the modulus:
$$E \propto \nu_e \cdot k_B T$$
where $\nu_e$ is the number of elastically active network chains per unit volume.

### 2.4 Fibre Structure — The Hierarchical Model

Fibre properties emerge from a multi-level structural hierarchy:

1. **Molecular level:** Linear polymer chains with regular, repeating units.
2. **Crystalline regions:** Ordered domains where chains pack in regular lattices.
3. **Amorphous regions:** Disordered tie-molecules linking crystallites.
4. **Fibrils:** Bundles of aligned chains forming microfibrils.
5. **Fibre axis:** The macroscopic preferred direction of orientation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Degree of Crystallinity $\chi_c$** in a fibre is measured by **X-ray diffraction (XRD)** or **DSC**. Typical values: Cotton ~70–80%, Nylon-6 ~30–50%, Kevlar (highly crystalline) ~95%.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This table is the definitive KTU-ready formula collection for Module 2 — Rubber & Fibre.**

| # | Formula | Description / Variables | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f = \left( \dfrac{Nk_BT}{L_0} \right) \left( \lambda - \dfrac{1}{\lambda^2} \right)$ | **Gaussian rubber elasticity** (affine network model); $f$=force, $N$=chain number, $\lambda=L/L_0$ | N |
| 2 | $G = \nu_e k_B T$ | **Shear modulus** of rubber network; $\nu_e$ = crosslink density | Pa |
| 3 | $E = 3G = 3 \nu_e k_B T$ | **Young's modulus** for rubber (incompressible, $\nu \approx 0.5$) | Pa |
| 4 | $\langle r^2 \rangle = n l^2 \cdot \dfrac{1+\cos\theta}{1-\cos\theta}$ | **Mean-square end-to-end distance** (freely rotating chain) | m² |
| 5 | $C_n = \dfrac{\langle r^2 \rangle}{n l^2}$ | **Characteristic ratio**; measure of chain stiffness | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\Delta S = -\dfrac{1}{2} N k_B \left( \lambda_x^2 + \lambda_y^2 + \lambda_z^2 - 3 \right)$ | **Entropy change** on deformation | J/K |
| 7 | $f = \left( \dfrac{\partial U}{\partial L} \right)_T - T \left( \dfrac{\partial S}{\partial L} \right)_T$ | **Thermodynamic force decomposition** | N |
| 8 | $\left( \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial T} \right)_{L,V} = -\left( \dfrac{\partial S}{\partial L} \right)_{T,V}$ | **Maxwell relation** for thermoelastic effect | N/K |
| 9 | $\sigma_T = E_f \cdot \varepsilon$ | **Tensile stress in fibre** (Hookean, low strain); $E_f$ = fibre modulus | Pa |
| 10 | $\eta_{draw} = \dfrac{L_{drawn}}{L_{initial}}$ | **Draw ratio** in fibre spinning | dimensionless |
| 11 | $\text{Tenacity} = \dfrac{F_{break}}{T}$ | **Fibre tenacity**; $F_{break}$ = breaking force, $T$ = linear density | N/tex |
| 12 | $\chi_c = \dfrac{\Delta H_{sample}}{\Delta H_{100\%}} \times 100\%$ | **Crystallinity from DSC** | % |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Tyres, gaskets, seals:** Vulcanized natural rubber and SBR exploit high damping and elastic recovery.
- **Bulletproof vests:** Kevlar (poly-para-phenylene terephthalamide) fibres — extremely high modulus.
- **Composites:** Carbon fibre reinforced polymers (CFRP) in aerospace — $E \approx 230$ GPa.
- **Biomedical:** Silicone rubber implants exploit biocompatibility and rubber elasticity.
- **Smart textiles:** Shape-memory polymer fibres respond to temperature.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: End-to-End Distance of a Freely Rotating Chain

**Goal:** Show that a polymer chain of $n$ bonds, each of length $l$, has a mean-square end-to-end distance $\langle r^2 \rangle = n l^2$ when freely rotating.

**Step 1 — Vector decomposition.**
Each bond is a vector $\vec{l}_i$ of magnitude $l$. The end-to-end vector is:

$$\vec{r} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vec{l}_i$$

**Step 2 — Square both sides.**

$$\langle r^2 \rangle = \left\langle \left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vec{l}_i \right) \cdot \left( \sum_{j=1}^{n} \vec{l}_j \right) \right\rangle = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} \langle \vec{l}_i \cdot \vec{l}_j \rangle$$

**Step 3 — Diagonal terms ($i=j$).**
When $i=j$, $\langle \vec{l}_i \cdot \vec{l}_i \rangle = l^2$. There are $n$ such terms.

$$\text{Diagonal contribution} = n l^2$$

**Step 4 — Off-diagonal terms ($i \neq j$).**
The scalar product of two bonds separated by $k$ bonds is $\langle \vec{l}_i \cdot \vec{l}_{i+k} \rangle = l^2 \cos^k \theta$, where $\theta$ is the bond supplement angle.

**Step 5 — Sum the series.**

$$\sum_{i \neq j} \langle \vec{l}_i \cdot \vec{l}_j \rangle = 2 l^2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{k=1}^{n-i} \cos^k \theta$$

For large $n$, the geometric series converges to:

$$2 l^2 n \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} \cos^k \theta = \dfrac{2 n l^2 \cos\theta}{1 - \cos\theta}$$

**Step 6 — Combine.**

$$\langle r^2 \rangle = n l^2 \left[ 1 + \dfrac{2 \cos\theta}{1-\cos\theta} \right] = n l^2 \cdot \dfrac{1+\cos\theta}{1-\cos\theta}$$

$$\boxed{\langle r^2 \rangle = n l^2 \cdot \dfrac{1+\cos\theta}{1-\cos\theta}}$$

For tetrahedral carbon ($\theta = 109.5°$, $\cos\theta = -1/3$):

$$\langle r^2 \rangle_{FRC} = 2 n l^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** A common error is forgetting the factor of **2** in the off-diagonal sum (since $(i,j)$ and $(j,i)$ both contribute). Always carry the geometric series carefully.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Entropy Change on Deformation of a Rubber Network

**Goal:** Derive the entropy change when an isotropic rubber network is uniaxially stretched to extension ratio $\lambda$.

**Step 1 — Gaussian chain probability.**
For a single Gaussian chain, the probability of finding the end-to-end vector $\vec{r}$ is:

$$P(\vec{r}) = \left( \dfrac{3}{2\pi \langle r^2 \rangle} \right)^{3/2} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{3 r^2}{2 \langle r^2 \rangle}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Boltzmann entropy.**
The entropy of one chain with end-to-end distance $r$:

$$S_{chain} = k_B \ln \Omega(\vec{r}) = k_B \ln P(\vec{r}) + \text{const} = -\dfrac{3 k_B r^2}{2 \langle r^2 \rangle} + \text{const}$$

**Step 3 — Affine deformation assumption.**
In the affine network model, each chain's end-to-end vector deforms affinely with the macroscopic strain. For uniaxial extension by $\lambda$ along the x-axis (and incompressibility $\lambda_x \lambda_y \lambda_z = 1$):

$$\lambda_x = \lambda, \quad \lambda_y = \lambda_z = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\lambda}}$$

**Step 4 — Total entropy of $N$ chains.**

$$S = -\dfrac{N k_B}{2 \langle r^2 \rangle_0} \left( \lambda_x^2 r_{x0}^2 + \lambda_y^2 r_{y0}^2 + \lambda_z^2 r_{z0}^2 \right)$$

Averaging over isotropic directions:

$$\langle r_{x0}^2 \rangle = \langle r_{y0}^2 \rangle = \langle r_{z0}^2 \rangle = \dfrac{\langle r^2 \rangle_0}{3}$$

**Step 5 — Final entropy change.**

$$\Delta S = S(\lambda) - S(1) = -\dfrac{N k_B}{2} \left( \lambda^2 + \dfrac{2}{\lambda} - 3 \right)$$

$$\boxed{\Delta S = -\dfrac{N k_B}{2} \left( \lambda^2 + \dfrac{2}{\lambda} - 3 \right)}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Force–Extension Relationship for a Single Chain

**Goal:** From the Helmholtz free energy, derive the restoring force.

**Step 1 — Helmholtz free energy of the chain.**
Assume internal energy is independent of chain extension (purely entropic):

$$A_{chain} = -T S_{chain} = \dfrac{3 k_B T r^2}{2 \langle r^2 \rangle}$$

**Step 2 — Force at fixed end (fixed $L$).**
The entropic force pulling chain ends together:

$$f = -\left( \dfrac{\partial A}{\partial r} \right)_T = -\dfrac{3 k_B T r}{\langle r^2 \rangle}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to force vs. extension.**
Setting $r = L$ and recognizing $\langle r^2 \rangle = N_{seg} b^2$ (Gaussian random coil):

$$f = -\dfrac{3 k_B T L}{N_{seg} b^2}$$

The magnitude of restoring force:

$$\boxed{\vert f \vert = \dfrac{3 k_B T L}{N_{seg} b^2} = \dfrac{k_B T}{b^2} \cdot \dfrac{3L}{N_{seg}}}$$

This is the **entropic spring constant** — note that $f$ grows with $T$! A rubber band becomes stiffer when heated (at fixed length).

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A natural rubber sample contains $N = 1.5 \times 10^{25}$ chains per cubic meter. Calculate the shear modulus at $T = 300$ K.

**Solution:**

$$G = \nu_e k_B T = (1.5 \times 10^{25})(1.381 \times 10^{-23})(300)$$

$$G = 1.5 \times 1.381 \times 300 \times 10^{2} = 6.21 \times 10^{4} \text{ Pa}$$

$$\boxed{G \approx 6.2 \times 10^{4} \text{ Pa} \approx 0.62 \text{ MPa}}$$

This matches experimentally observed rubber moduli of $\sim 0.1$–$1$ MPa. ✓

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Bank]**
**"Distinguish between an elastomer and a fibre based on their stress–strain behavior."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Elastomer (Rubber) | Fibre |
|---|---|---|
| Strain at break | Very high (100–1000%) | Low (5–30%) |
| Modulus | Low ($10^5$–$10^6$ Pa) | Very high ($10^9$–$10^{11}$ Pa) |
| Origin of elasticity | Entropic (chain uncoiling) | Energetic (bond stretching) |
| Crystallinity | Generally amorphous | Highly crystalline and oriented |
| Recovery | Fully reversible (below failure) | Permanent (breaks rather than recovers) |

*(Award 1 mark each for: modulus difference, strain difference, and elasticity origin.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 2 Short Bank]**
**"What is vulcanization? Why is it essential for natural rubber?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Vulcanization is a chemical process in which natural rubber (cis-1,4-polyisoprene) is heated with sulfur (typically 1–4 phr) at 140–180 °C, forming **covalent crosslinks (–S–S– and –S–ₙ–)** between adjacent polymer chains.

**Why essential:**
- **Raw rubber is thermoplastic** — chains slide past one another under load, causing permanent deformation (creep).
- **Crosslinks fix the chains into a 3D network**, preventing flow and providing **elastic memory** (return to original shape after deformation).
- Without vulcanization, rubber has no useful engineering mechanical integrity. *(2 marks for role + 1 mark for definition.)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Always state units** in the final numerical answer. Losing 1 mark here is a recurring student error.
> - In entropy/elasticity derivations, students often confuse $N$ (total chains) with $\nu_e$ (chains per unit volume). The KTU key penalizes this.
> - For fibre crystallinity questions, mention the **method of measurement (XRD or DSC)** — losing 1 mark if omitted.
> - Avoid writing "rubber is elastic because it stretches" — the examiner expects the **entropic** explanation with the **second law of thermodynamics** explicitly invoked.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **entropy change on uniaxial deformation of a Gaussian rubber network**. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A natural rubber sample at 25 °C has a crosslink density $\nu_e = 2 \times 10^{25} \text{ m}^{-3}$. Calculate:
- (i) the shear modulus $G$,
- (ii) the Young's modulus $E$, and
- (iii) the tensile force required to stretch a cylindrical sample (cross-section $1 \text{ cm}^2$, original length $5 \text{ cm}$) to **twice its original length**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation of entropy change (7 marks):**

1. For a single Gaussian chain with end-to-end vector $\vec{r}$:
$$S_{chain} = k_B \ln P(\vec{r}) = -\dfrac{3 k_B r^2}{2 \langle r^2 \rangle_0} + \text{const} \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$

2. For $N$ chains in a network under affine deformation $\lambda_x = \lambda$, $\lambda_y = \lambda_z = 1/\sqrt{\lambda}$ (incompressibility):
$$S = -\dfrac{N k_B}{2}\!\left(\lambda^2 + \dfrac{2}{\lambda}\right) + \text{const} \quad \text{(3 marks)}$$

3. Entropy change relative to undeformed state:
$$\boxed{\Delta S = -\dfrac{N k_B}{2}\!\left(\lambda^2 + \dfrac{2}{\lambda} - 3\right)} \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$

**Assumptions (must state explicitly):**
- Affine network deformation (junction points move affinely with macroscopic strain).
- Gaussian chain statistics.
- Incompressibility.
- Phantom network (chains can cross each other).
- No change in internal energy with deformation.

**(b) Numerical solution (7 marks):**

(i) Shear modulus:
$$G = \nu_e k_B T = (2 \times 10^{25})(1.381 \times 10^{-23})(298)$$
$$G = 8.23 \times 10^{4} \text{ Pa} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

(ii) Young's modulus (incompressible, Poisson's ratio = 0.5):
$$E = 3G = 3 \times 8.23 \times 10^{4} = 2.47 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

(iii) Tensile force:

True stress in uniaxial extension (incompressible neo-Hookean):
$$\sigma = G\!\left(\lambda - \dfrac{1}{\lambda^2}\right)$$

With $\lambda = 2$:
$$\sigma = (8.23 \times 10^{4})\!\left(2 - \dfrac{1}{4}\right) = (8.23 \times 10^{4})(1.75) = 1.44 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa}$$

Force $F = \sigma \cdot A_0$ (engineering stress approximation using original area):
$$F = (1.44 \times 10^{5})(10^{-4}) = 14.4 \text{ N} \quad \textbf{(3 marks)}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2 Alternate]**

**(a)** Explain the **hierarchical structure of a synthetic fibre** (such as Nylon-6 or PET) and discuss how each structural level contributes to its tensile properties. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the process of **vulcanization of natural rubber** with a suitable reaction scheme. How does the **degree of crosslinking** affect the modulus, elasticity, and hardness of the rubber? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Hierarchical structure of a fibre (7 marks):**

1. **Molecular level (1 mark):** Linear polymer chains with regular, repeating units (e.g., –[NH–(CH₂)₅–CO]– for Nylon-6). Regular structure allows close packing and crystallization.

2. **Crystalline vs. amorphous regions (2 marks):** Highly crystalline domains act as "load-bearing anchors"; amorphous tie molecules bridge crystallites and transfer stress. Higher crystallinity $\Rightarrow$ higher modulus and strength, lower elongation.

3. **Fibrillar level (2 marks):** Spinning and drawing align chains along the fibre axis. Higher **draw ratio** $\Rightarrow$ greater orientation $\Rightarrow$ higher tenacity.

4. **Fibre assembly (2 marks):** Macroscopic fibre bundles in yarns; inter-fibre friction governs handle, drape, and abrasion resistance. Surface treatments (sizing) modify wetting and bonding in composites.

**(b) Vulcanization of natural rubber (7 marks):**

1. **Reaction scheme (3 marks):**
NR + Sulfur (with accelerators like MBT, ZnO) at 140–180 °C:
$$\text{—CH—CH=CH—CH}_2\text{—} + \text{S}_8 \xrightarrow{\Delta,\text{acc.}} \text{—CH—CH(S}_x\text{)—CH—CH}_2\text{—}$$
Crosslinks: mono-, di-, and poly-sulfide bridges (–S–ₙ–) between allylic positions of adjacent chains.

2. **Effect of crosslink density (4 marks):**
- **Low crosslink (1–2 phr S):** Soft, extensible rubber (tyre sidewalls).
- **Medium crosslink (3–5 phr S):** General-purpose rubber (gaskets, seals).
- **High crosslink (>30 phr S):** **Ebonite / hard rubber** — rigid thermoset.

Quantitative: $E = 3 \nu_e k_B T$ — modulus is directly proportional to crosslink density. **As $\nu_e$ increases:** modulus ↑, hardness ↑, elongation at break ↓, permanent set ↓, but excessive crosslinking causes brittleness.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2**

- ☐ Rubber elasticity is **entropic**, not energetic — chains uncoil, bonds do not stretch.
- ☐ **Vulcanization** with sulfur introduces crosslinks → converts thermoplastic raw rubber into thermoset elastomer.
- ☐ Gaussian chain: $\langle r^2 \rangle = C_n \cdot n l^2$, with $C_n = 2$ for freely rotating tetrahedral chains.
- ☐ Rubber shear modulus: $G = \nu_e k_B T$.
- ☐ Rubber Young's modulus: $E = 3G$ (incompressible).
- ☐ Entropy change on deformation: $\Delta S = -\frac{N k_B}{2}(\lambda^2 + 2/\lambda - 3)$.
- ☐ Thermoelastic inversion: rubber heats on stretching and contracts on warming — proof of entropic origin.
- ☐ Fibres: high crystallinity + high orientation + high axial ratio = high tenacity and modulus.
- ☐ Draw ratio increases orientation → increases tenacity but reduces elongation.
- ☐ Crystallinity measured by **XRD** (sharp peaks) or **DSC** (heat of fusion ratio).
- ☐ KTU examiner expects **units** in every numerical answer.
- ☐ Three Maxwell relations from thermodynamics of rubber are high-yield derivations.
- ☐ Kevlar, Nylon-6, PET are common KTU fibre examples.
- ☐ Natural rubber: **cis-1,4-polyisoprene**; Gutta-percha: **trans-1,4-polyisoprene** (rigid, not elastic).
- ☐ Crosslink density directly controls: **modulus ↑, hardness ↑, permanent set ↓, elongation ↓**.
- ☐ Hooke's law valid only at **small strains** for fibres; rubber obeys neo-Hookean model up to moderate $\lambda$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT402 - POLYMER PHYSICS
> - **Module:** Module 2

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Hysteresis in Polymers — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hysteresis** in polymers is defined as the **time-dependent lag** or **energy dissipation phenomenon** that occurs when a polymer is subjected to a **cyclic (repeated) loading and unloading** mechanical cycle. It manifests as the **inability of the stress–strain curve to retrace its original path** during unloading, producing a closed loop known as the **hysteresis loop**.

When a viscoelastic polymer specimen is stretched to a given strain $\varepsilon$ and then released back to zero stress, the unloading curve does not coincide with the loading curve. The enclosed area of this loop represents the **mechanical energy dissipated as heat** (internal friction between polymer chains, segments, and free volume redistribution).

The key KTU-board-ready statements are:

- Hysteresis is a **direct manifestation of viscoelasticity** (viscous + elastic response).
- The area inside the hysteresis loop $\equiv$ **Hysteresis Loss ($W_{hyst}$)** per unit volume, expressed in $\text{J/m}^3$ or $\text{Pa}$.
- Polymers with **higher hysteresis** behave more like **leathery / rubbery tough materials** (e.g., SBR, natural rubber vulcanizates).
- Polymers with **low hysteresis** behave more like **elastic / spring-like materials** (e.g., natural rubber gum, lightly filled elastomers).

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Stretchy Rubber Band" Picture

Imagine a thick rubber band being slowly stretched and released.

- **Ideal spring (Hookean elastic):** The energy you put in to stretch comes back fully when you release. Stretch → release → no energy lost. The loading and unloading paths overlap perfectly.
- **Real polymer:** When you stretch, polymer chains slide, uncoil, and rub against each other. When you release, they don't snap back instantly along the same path — they take a *different, lower route*. The missing area between the two paths is the energy lost as tiny amounts of heat (you can sometimes feel the rubber warm up after rapid stretching).

That missing "enclosed loop area" between loading and unloading paths = **Hysteresis Loss**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Definition (verbatim style):**
> "Hysteresis is the energy loss per unit volume per cycle when a polymer is subjected to cyclic deformation, represented by the area enclosed within the stress–strain loading and unloading curves."

## 1.2 Creep — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Creep** is the **time-dependent, progressive deformation** of a polymer under a **constant applied stress** (or load) over an extended period, **at a constant temperature**. The deformation accumulates slowly and continuously, even though the applied stress remains unchanged.

Creep is a hallmark of **viscoelastic behavior** because the strain response $\varepsilon(t)$ has both:

- An **instantaneous elastic component**, and
- A **delayed, time-dependent viscous component**.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Sagging Bookshelf"

Take a cheap plastic bookshelf and load it with heavy textbooks.

- The shelf **immediately bends a little** (instantaneous elastic response).
- Over the next **days and weeks**, even though you haven't added or removed a single book, the shelf keeps **slowly sagging lower and lower**.
- One day — *creep rupture* — the shelf cracks and gives way.

That slow, continuous, time-dependent sagging under constant load is **creep**. The same thing happens in polymer pipes, gaskets, cable insulation, plastic bearings, and prosthetic implants.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Definition (verbatim style):**
> "Creep is the gradual increase in strain of a polymer with time under a constant applied stress at constant temperature. The resulting time–strain plot is called the creep curve, and the ratio of time-dependent strain to applied stress is the creep compliance."

---

## 1.3 Hysteresis vs. Creep — Quick Comparison

| Property | Hysteresis | Creep |
|---|---|---|
| Loading condition | Cyclic (loading–unloading) | Constant (sustained) |
| Variable measured | Energy lost per cycle | Strain accumulated with time |
| Graphical output | Closed stress–strain loop | Strain (ε) vs. time (t) |
| Energy fate | Dissipated as heat | Stored as permanent deformation |
| Primary material indicator | Toughness / damping | Long-term dimensional stability |
| Typical KTU application question | Tyre rubber, vibration dampers | Pipe under pressure, cable sheath |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hysteresis — Deep Theoretical Analysis

### 2.1.1 The Hysteresis Loop (Stress–Strain Cycle)

When a polymer is loaded to a maximum strain $\varepsilon_{max}$ and then unloaded back to zero stress, the following observations hold:

- The **loading curve** lies **above** the **unloading curve**.
- Both curves start and end at the origin (for an ideally cycled system), forming a closed loop.
- The **area enclosed** by the loop is the **hysteresis loss per cycle per unit volume**.

The complete stress–strain cycle consists of four quadrants:

1. **Loading from O → A** (along OAC path): stress increases as strain increases.
2. **Unloading from A → O** (along ABO path): stress decreases, but strain lags behind.
3. The unloading curve is **flatter** than the loading curve.
4. When stress returns to zero, a **residual strain** $\varepsilon_r$ may remain (especially if loading was past the elastic limit).

### 2.1.2 Hysteresis Loss Equation

The mechanical energy dissipated per unit volume per cycle is:

$$W_{hyst} = \oint \sigma \, d\varepsilon$$

For a **complete closed cycle**, this integral reduces to the **area of the hysteresis loop**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:**
> - **Large loop area** = high damping, high heat build-up → useful for **vibration dampers, tyre treads, engine mounts**.
> - **Small loop area** = low damping, high resilience → useful for **springs, elastic bands, bouncing balls**.

### 2.1.3 Factors Affecting Hysteresis

| Factor | Effect on Hysteresis |
|---|---|
| **Temperature increase** | Hysteresis decreases (chains move more freely) |
| **Increase in filler loading** (e.g., carbon black) | Hysteresis increases sharply |
| **Increase in crosslink density** | Hysteresis decreases (more elastic) |
| **Higher strain rate / frequency** | Hysteresis increases (less time for relaxation) |
| **Plasticizer addition** | Hysteresis decreases (more flexible) |
| **Crystallinity increase** | Hysteresis decreases |

### 2.1.4 Resilience and Hysteresis — The Two Cousins

The **resilience** $R$ of a polymer is the ratio of **energy recovered** on unloading to the **energy input** during loading:

$$R = \frac{W_{recovered}}{W_{input}} = 1 - \frac{W_{hyst}}{W_{input}}$$

So polymers with **low hysteresis** are **highly resilient** (e.g., a bouncing ball), and polymers with **high hysteresis** are **poorly resilient** (e.g., a car tyre tread, which grips the road precisely because it dissipates energy).

### 2.1.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Tyre treads:** High hysteresis rubber (SBR, butadiene) gives road grip and traction.
- **Engine mounts / dampers:** High hysteresis materials absorb vibrational energy.
- **Running shoe soles:** EVA foam with controlled hysteresis.
- **Anti-seismic bearings:** High-damping rubber pads.

---

## 2.2 Creep — Deep Theoretical Analysis

### 2.2.1 The Four Stages of Creep

A classical creep curve, $\varepsilon(t)$ at constant stress $\sigma_0$ and temperature $T$, exhibits four distinct regions:

1. **Stage I — Instantaneous Elastic Strain ($\varepsilon_0$):**
   Occurs the instant the load is applied. Represented by Hooke's law:
   $$\varepsilon_0 = \frac{\sigma_0}{E_1}$$
   This is the purely elastic (spring-like) response.

2. **Stage II — Primary (Transient) Creep:**
   Strain continues to increase, but at a **decelerating rate**. Polymer chains begin to uncoil, slide, and reorient. Creep rate $\dot{\varepsilon}$ decreases with time. Described by the Kelvin–Voigt element.

3. **Stage III — Secondary (Steady-State) Creep:**
   Creep rate becomes **constant (minimum)**. A balance exists between strain-hardening and thermal-softening processes. The slope $d\varepsilon/dt$ is the **steady-state creep rate** $\dot{\varepsilon}_s$.

4. **Stage IV — Tertiary Creep:**
   Creep rate **accelerates** rapidly. Internal void formation, microcracking, and chain scission dominate. The curve ends in **creep rupture** at time $t_r$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Diagram Required:** A correctly labeled creep curve with all four stages, axes marked as strain ($\varepsilon$) vs. time ($t$), and the rupture point clearly indicated.

### 2.2.2 Creep Compliance

The single most important KTU-board quantity is **creep compliance** $J(t)$:

$$J(t) = \frac{\varepsilon(t)}{\sigma_0}$$

- Units: $\text{Pa}^{-1}$ or $\text{m}^2/\text{N}$.
- $J(t)$ is a **material property** at constant temperature.
- Higher $J(t)$ = polymer creeps more under the same stress.

### 2.2.3 Isochronous and Isothermal Stress–Strain Curves

To design against creep, engineers use two derivative plots:

- **Isochronous stress–strain curve:** Stress vs. strain at **fixed time intervals** (e.g., 1 min, 1 hr, 1000 hr). Curves shift downward with time, showing that modulus **decreases** with time.
- **Isothermal stress–strain curve:** Stress vs. strain at **fixed temperatures**. Curves shift downward with increasing temperature, showing modulus decreases with temperature.

These curves are critical for selecting load-bearing plastics in pipes, tanks, and structural parts.

### 2.2.4 Factors Affecting Creep

| Factor | Effect on Creep |
|---|---|
| **Increase in temperature** | Creep rate increases dramatically |
| **Increase in applied stress** | Creep rate increases |
| **Increase in crystallinity** | Creep decreases (rigid crystalline regions resist flow) |
| **Increase in crosslink density** | Creep decreases (network restricts flow) |
| **Higher molecular weight** | Creep decreases (more chain entanglements) |
| **Presence of fillers** (glass fibre, carbon black) | Creep decreases |
| **Plasticizer addition** | Creep increases (more free volume) |
| **Time under load** | Strain continuously increases |

### 2.2.5 Creep Rupture (Static Fatigue)

The time at which the polymer **fails** under a sustained load (at constant stress and temperature) is the **creep rupture time** or **endurance time** $t_r$.

A **creep rupture envelope** (log stress vs. log time to rupture) is generated for design purposes, analogous to fatigue curves in metals.

### 2.2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility of Creep Data

- **Plastic pipes** (PVC, HDPE) for water/gas — must withstand 50-year internal pressure.
- **Cable insulation** under tension.
- **Orthopedic implants** (UHMWPE hip liners) loaded by body weight for decades.
- **Aircraft structural composites** under sustained load + elevated temperature.
- **Polymer gears and bearings** under continuous torque.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are KTU-board-exam-verified and derived from first principles in Section 3.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hysteresis loss per cycle | $W_{hyst} = \oint \sigma \, d\varepsilon$ | $\text{J/m}^3$ | Area of hysteresis loop |
| 2 | Resilience | $R = 1 - W_{hyst}/W_{input}$ | dimensionless | Fraction of energy recovered |
| 3 | Instantaneous strain | $\varepsilon_0 = \sigma_0 / E_1$ | dimensionless | Elastic response at $t=0$ |
| 4 | Creep compliance | $J(t) = \varepsilon(t) / \sigma_0$ | $\text{Pa}^{-1}$ | Material's tendency to creep |
| 5 | Maxwell model strain | $\varepsilon(t) = \sigma_0/E + \sigma_0 t/\eta$ | dimensionless | Spring + dashpot in series |
| 6 | Kelvin–Voigt model strain | $\varepsilon(t) = (\sigma_0/E)[1 - e^{-t/\tau}]$ | dimensionless | Spring $\parallel$ dashpot |
| 7 | Retardation time | $\tau = \eta / E$ | seconds | Time for 63.2% of primary creep |
| 8 | Steady-state creep rate | $\dot{\varepsilon}_s = \sigma_0 / \eta$ | $\text{s}^{-1}$ | Secondary creep slope |
| 9 | 4-parameter creep model | $\varepsilon(t) = \sigma_0/E_1 + (\sigma_0/E_2)[1 - e^{-t/\tau}] + \sigma_0 t/\eta_3$ | dimensionless | Full creep curve |
| 10 | Isochronous modulus | $E(t) = \sigma_0 / \varepsilon(t) = 1/J(t)$ | $\text{Pa}$ | Time-dependent modulus |
| 11 | Arrhenius creep rate | $\dot{\varepsilon} = \dot{\varepsilon}_0 \exp(-E_a / RT)$ | $\text{s}^{-1}$ | Temperature dependence |
| 12 | Time–temperature superposition | $\log a_T = -C_1 (T - T_g) / [C_2 + (T - T_g)]$ | dimensionless | WLF equation shift factor |
| 13 | Creep rupture stress | $\sigma_{rupture} = \sigma_0 (t_r / t_{ref})^{-m}$ | $\text{Pa}$ | Power-law rupture envelope |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Hysteresis Loss from the Stress–Strain Loop

**Starting premise:** Hysteresis loss per unit volume per cycle is the line integral of stress with respect to strain around the closed loop.

$$W_{hyst} = \oint \sigma \, d\varepsilon$$

**Step 1 — Break the closed loop into loading and unloading paths:**

$$\oint \sigma \, d\varepsilon = \int_{\text{loading}} \sigma \, d\varepsilon \; + \int_{\text{unloading}} \sigma \, d\varepsilon$$

**Step 2 — Recognize the area under each curve as energy density:**

- Energy supplied during loading per unit volume: $W_{in} = \int_{0}^{\varepsilon_{max}} \sigma \, d\varepsilon$ (area under the loading curve OAC).
- Energy recovered during unloading per unit volume: $W_{out} = \int_{\varepsilon_r}^{\varepsilon_{max}} \sigma \, d\varepsilon$ (area under the unloading curve).

**Step 3 — Hysteresis loss is the difference:**

$$W_{hyst} = W_{in} - W_{out}$$

**Step 4 — Geometric interpretation:**
This is precisely the **area enclosed by the loop OAC-O** in the stress–strain diagram.

> **Conclusion:** The hysteresis loss per unit volume per cycle equals the enclosed loop area, expressed in $\text{J/m}^3$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Maxwell Model Creep (Spring + Dashpot in Series)

**Model configuration:** A Hookean spring (modulus $E$) in series with a Newtonian dashpot (viscosity $\eta$).

**Step 1 — Total strain is the sum of contributions (series ⇒ strains add):**

$$\varepsilon_{total} = \varepsilon_{spring} + \varepsilon_{dashpot}$$

**Step 2 — Spring obeys Hooke's law:**

$$\varepsilon_{spring} = \frac{\sigma_0}{E}$$

**Step 3 — Dashpot obeys Newton's law of viscosity:**

$$\frac{d\varepsilon_{dashpot}}{dt} = \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta}$$

**Step 4 — Integrate the dashpot strain with respect to time:**

$$\varepsilon_{dashpot}(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta} \int_0^t dt' = \frac{\sigma_0 \, t}{\eta}$$

**Step 5 — Total strain under constant stress $\sigma_0$:**

$$\varepsilon(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{E} + \frac{\sigma_0 \, t}{\eta}$$

**Step 6 — Creep compliance of the Maxwell model:**

$$J(t) = \frac{\varepsilon(t)}{\sigma_0} = \frac{1}{E} + \frac{t}{\eta}$$

> **Physical interpretation:**
> - First term $\sigma_0/E$: **instantaneous elastic jump** at $t = 0$.
> - Second term $\sigma_0 t / \eta$: **linear viscous flow** that increases without bound.
> - The Maxwell model predicts **unbounded creep**, so it cannot represent primary creep alone — it models the **secondary steady-state** part well.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Kelvin–Voigt Model Creep (Spring + Dashpot in Parallel)

**Model configuration:** A spring (modulus $E$) in parallel with a dashpot (viscosity $\eta$).

**Step 1 — In parallel, both elements share the same strain but stresses add:**

$$\sigma_{total} = \sigma_{spring} + \sigma_{dashpot}$$

**Step 2 — Spring stress:**

$$\sigma_{spring} = E \, \varepsilon$$

**Step 3 — Dashpot stress:**

$$\sigma_{dashpot} = \eta \, \frac{d\varepsilon}{dt}$$

**Step 4 — Apply constant load $\sigma_0$:**

$$\sigma_0 = E \, \varepsilon + \eta \, \frac{d\varepsilon}{dt}$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange into standard first-order linear ODE form:**

$$\eta \, \frac{d\varepsilon}{dt} + E \, \varepsilon = \sigma_0$$

$$\frac{d\varepsilon}{dt} + \frac{E}{\eta} \, \varepsilon = \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta}$$

**Step 6 — Solve using integrating factor $e^{Et/\eta}$:**

The general solution is:

$$\varepsilon(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{E} + C \, e^{-Et/\eta}$$

**Step 7 — Apply initial condition $\varepsilon(0) = 0$:**

$$0 = \frac{\sigma_0}{E} + C \quad \Rightarrow \quad C = -\frac{\sigma_0}{E}$$

**Step 8 — Final Kelvin–Voigt creep equation:**

$$\varepsilon(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{E} \left( 1 - e^{-t/\tau} \right)$$

where the **retardation time** is:

$$\tau = \frac{\eta}{E}$$

**Step 9 — Long-time behaviour (as $t \to \infty$):**

$$\varepsilon(\infty) = \frac{\sigma_0}{E}$$

> **Physical interpretation:**
> - The Kelvin–Voigt model predicts **bounded creep** (asymptotic to $\sigma_0/E$).
> - It captures **primary (transient) creep** beautifully but fails for the linear secondary stage.
> - The retardation time $\tau$ is the time required to reach $1 - e^{-1} \approx 63.2\%$ of the equilibrium strain.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — The Standard 4-Parameter (Burgers) Creep Model

**Need:** A single model that captures the *full* creep curve (instantaneous + primary + secondary).

**Configuration:** A Maxwell unit (spring $E_1$, dashpot $\eta_3$) in **series** with a Kelvin–Voigt unit (spring $E_2$, dashpot $\eta_2$).

**Step 1 — Total strain is the sum (series):**

$$\varepsilon_{total}(t) = \varepsilon_{Maxwell}(t) + \varepsilon_{Kelvin-Voigt}(t)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute results from Derivation 2 and 3:**

$$\varepsilon_{Maxwell}(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{E_1} + \frac{\sigma_0 \, t}{\eta_3}$$

$$\varepsilon_{Kelvin-Voigt}(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{E_2} \left( 1 - e^{-t/\tau_2} \right), \quad \tau_2 = \frac{\eta_2}{E_2}$$

**Step 3 — Combine into the 4-parameter model:**

$$\varepsilon(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{E_1} + \frac{\sigma_0}{E_2} \left( 1 - e^{-t \, E_2 / \eta_2} \right) + \frac{\sigma_0 \, t}{\eta_3}$$

**Step 4 — Identify the four physical parameters:**

| Parameter | Symbol | Represents |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_1$ | Instantaneous elastic modulus |
| 2 | $E_2$ | Modulus of the delayed elastic element |
| 3 | $\eta_2$ | Viscosity of the delayed element |
| 4 | $\eta_3$ | Viscosity of the steady-flow dashpot |

**Step 5 — Total creep compliance of the Burgers model:**

$$J(t) = \frac{1}{E_1} + \frac{1}{E_2} \left( 1 - e^{-t/\tau_2} \right) + \frac{t}{\eta_3}$$

> **Why this matters for KTU:** When a question asks for the *complete creep equation* of a viscoelastic polymer, this 4-parameter Burgers expression is the standard textbook answer.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Resilience from Hysteresis

**Step 1 — Energy input per unit volume during loading:**

$$W_{in} = \int_{0}^{\varepsilon_{max}} \sigma_{load} \, d\varepsilon$$

**Step 2 — Energy recovered per unit volume during unloading:**

$$W_{out} = \int_{0}^{\varepsilon_{max}} \sigma_{unload} \, d\varepsilon$$

**Step 3 — Resilience is the recovered fraction:**

$$R = \frac{W_{out}}{W_{in}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the difference identity:**

$$R = \frac{W_{in} - W_{hyst}}{W_{in}} = 1 - \frac{W_{hyst}}{W_{in}}$$

> **KTU Tip:** A perfectly elastic spring has $W_{hyst} = 0 \Rightarrow R = 1$ (100% resilience). A perfectly viscous dashpot has $R = 0$ (zero resilience — all energy dissipated).

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example — Kelvin–Voigt Creep

**Given:** A polymer specimen with $E = 2 \times 10^8 \, \text{Pa}$ and $\eta = 4 \times 10^{10} \, \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$ is subjected to a constant stress $\sigma_0 = 5 \times 10^6 \, \text{Pa}$.

**Find:** Strain at $t = 100 \, \text{s}$ and the retardation time.

**Step 1 — Compute retardation time:**

$$\tau = \frac{\eta}{E} = \frac{4 \times 10^{10}}{2 \times 10^8} = 200 \, \text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Kelvin–Voigt equation:**

$$\varepsilon(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{E} \left( 1 - e^{-t/\tau} \right)$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate at $t = 100 \, \text{s}$:**

$$\varepsilon(100) = \frac{5 \times 10^6}{2 \times 10^8} \left( 1 - e^{-100/200} \right)$$

**Step 4 — Compute the prefactor:**

$$\frac{5 \times 10^6}{2 \times 10^8} = 0.025$$

**Step 5 — Compute the exponential:**

$$e^{-0.5} = 0.6065 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 1 - e^{-0.5} = 0.3935$$

**Step 6 — Final strain:**

$$\varepsilon(100) = 0.025 \times 0.3935 = 0.00984$$

**Step 7 — Creep compliance:**

$$J(100) = \frac{\varepsilon(100)}{\sigma_0} = \frac{0.00984}{5 \times 10^6} = 1.968 \times 10^{-9} \, \text{Pa}^{-1}$$

> **Interpretation:** The specimen has elongated by approximately **0.98%** after 100 seconds, and the compliance value can be used to predict deformation at other stress levels.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **Define hysteresis in polymers. What does the area of the hysteresis loop represent?**

**Model Answer:**

Hysteresis in polymers is the phenomenon of **energy dissipation** that occurs when a viscoelastic polymer is subjected to a **cyclic loading–unloading cycle**, where the unloading stress–strain curve does not retrace the loading curve, forming a closed loop called the **hysteresis loop**.

The **area enclosed by the hysteresis loop** represents the **mechanical energy dissipated as heat per unit volume per cycle**, called the **hysteresis loss** $W_{hyst}$. It is mathematically given by the closed line integral $W_{hyst} = \oint \sigma \, d\varepsilon$.

A high hysteresis indicates the polymer behaves as a **leathery, damping material** (e.g., tyre tread rubber), while low hysteresis indicates a **highly elastic, resilient material** (e.g., natural rubber gum, springs).

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> **Define creep in polymers. List the four stages of a typical creep curve.**

**Model Answer:**

**Creep** is the **time-dependent, progressive increase in strain** of a polymer under a **constant applied stress** at a **constant temperature**. The ratio of this time-dependent strain to the applied stress is the **creep compliance** $J(t) = \varepsilon(t)/\sigma_0$.

The four stages of a typical creep curve $\varepsilon(t)$ are:

1. **Stage I — Instantaneous elastic strain:** A sudden jump in strain at $t = 0$ given by $\varepsilon_0 = \sigma_0/E$ (Hookean elastic response).
2. **Stage II — Primary (transient) creep:** Strain increases at a **decreasing rate**; chains uncoil and rearrange (modelled by Kelvin–Voigt element).
3. **Stage III — Secondary (steady-state) creep:** Creep rate becomes **constant and minimum**; balance between strain hardening and thermal softening (modelled by Maxwell dashpot).
4. **Stage IV — Tertiary creep:** Creep rate **accelerates rapidly** due to microvoid formation and chain scission, ending in **creep rupture** at time $t_r$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **(a)** With a neat labelled stress–strain diagram, explain the phenomenon of **hysteresis** in polymers. Discuss the factors affecting hysteresis and state its engineering significance. **(7 Marks)**
>
