**Concept:** Polymer relaxation data collected at multiple temperatures can be **horizontally shifted** along the frequency (or time) axis to overlap into a single "master curve" at a reference temperature $T_0$.

**Step 1.** Define the horizontal shift factor $a_T$:

$$a_T = \frac{f_{0}}{f_T} = \frac{\tau_T}{\tau_{T_0}}$$

**Step 2.** Williams–Landel–Ferry (WLF) equation — the shift factor for $T_g < T < T_g + 100°\text{C}$:

$$\log_{10} a_T = \frac{-C_1 (T - T_0)}{C_2 + (T - T_0)}$$

**Step 3.** Universal WLF constants for many amorphous polymers at $T_0 = T_g$:

$$C_1 \approx 17.44 \quad ; \quad C_2 \approx 51.6 \, \text{K}$$

**Step 4.** Example calculation — Find $\log a_T$ at $T = T_g + 30°\text{C}$:

$$\log a_T = \frac{-17.44 \times 30}{51.6 + 30} = \frac{-523.2}{81.6} = -6.41$$

**Step 5.** Interpret: a measurement made at $T_g + 30°\text{C}$ corresponds to a frequency $10^{6.41} \approx 2.6 \times 10^{6}$ times higher than at $T_g$.

**Step 6.** Practical consequence — to study a polymer's behaviour at $1$ Hz for **10 years**, you can equivalently measure at $1$ kHz for:

$$t_{\text{lab}} = \frac{t_{\text{service}}}{a_T} \approx \frac{10 \text{ years}}{2.6 \times 10^{6}} \approx 122 \text{ seconds}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Hint:** WLF is the standard method for long-term creep/relaxation prediction in KTU problems. Master curves extend the usable frequency range by **6–8 decades** — a remarkable time-saving technique.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Relaxation Features by Mode

| Mode of Deformation | Hookean (Purely Elastic) | Newtonian (Purely Viscous) | Viscoelastic (Polymer) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Stress–strain phase | $\delta = 0°$ | $\delta = 90°$ | $0° < \delta < 90°$ |
| $E'$ vs $E''$ | $E'' = 0$ | $E' = 0$ | $E', E'' > 0$ |
| $\tan\delta$ | $0$ | $\infty$ | Finite, temperature-dependent |
| Energy per cycle | Fully stored | Fully dissipated | Partly stored, partly lost |
| Example | Steel spring | Honey | Polyethylene, PMMA |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of a DMA Instrument

> [!NOTE]
> Below is the **block-level functional architecture** of a Dynamic Mechanical Analyzer. It maps the signal flow and control feedback rather than a literal physical drawing (more useful for KTU written exams).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CTRL["Controller & Software"]
        SW[Software Interface]
        SIG[Signal Generator - Sinusoidal Wave]
        PROC[Lock in Amplifier and Data Processor]
    end

    subgraph DRIVE["Drive Head Assembly"]
        MOT[Linear Motor - Electromagnetic]
        FR[Force Transducer - Load Cell]
        DSP[Displacement Sensor - LVDT]
    end

    subgraph CLAMP["Sample Clamp / Geometry Module"]
        TEN[Tensile Clamp]
        THR[Three Point Bending Clamp]
        SHR[Shear Sandwich Clamp]
        CAN[Cantilever Clamp]
    end

    subgraph SAMP["Polymer Specimen"]
        SPEC[Sample Film Bar or Fiber]
    end

    subgraph THERM["Thermal Environment"]
        FURN[Furnace -150 to 600 C]
        LN2[Liquid Nitrogen Cooling]
        TPRG[Temperature Programmer]
    end

    SW --> SIG
    SIG --> MOT
    MOT --> CLAMP
    CLAMP --> SPEC
    SPEC --> FR
    FR --> PROC
    DSP --> PROC
    TPRG --> FURN
    LN2 --> FURN
    FURN --> SPEC
    PROC --> SW
    TPRG --> SW
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **signal generator** sends a sinusoidal current to the **linear motor**, which applies oscillatory strain to the sample.
- The **force transducer** measures the resulting stress; the **LVDT** measures actual displacement.
- The **lock-in amplifier** resolves stress and strain into in-phase and out-of-phase components.
- The **furnace** (with optional $\text{LN}_2$ cooling) sweeps temperature. At each temperature, $E'$, $E''$, and $\tan\delta$ are computed and plotted.

---

### 4.2 Signal Flow of the Viscoelastic Response

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sinusoidal Strain Epsilon t = Epsilon0 sin omega t] --> B[Polymer Specimen - Viscoelastic]
    C[Sinusoidal Stress Sigma t = Sigma0 sin omega t + delta] --> B
    B --> D[Lock in Amplifier]
    D --> E[Extract In phase Component]
    D --> F[Extract Out of phase Component]
    E --> G["Storage Modulus E prime = sigma0 cos delta over epsilon0"]
    F --> H["Loss Modulus E double prime = sigma0 sin delta over epsilon0"]
    G --> I["tan delta = E double prime over E prime"]
    H --> I
    G --> J[DMA Output Curves vs T or vs frequency]
    H --> J
    I --> J
```

---

### 4.3 Phasor Diagram — Storage vs Loss Modulus

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    O((Origin)) -->|Real axis| P["E prime along real axis"]
    O -->|Imaginary axis| Q["E double prime along imaginary axis"]
    O -->|Magnitude and angle| R["Complex modulus E star with phase angle delta"]
    P --- R
    Q --- R
```

This corresponds to the vector triangle: $E^{*}$ is the hypotenuse, $E'$ is the adjacent side (along real axis), $E''$ is the opposite side (along imaginary axis), and $\delta = \arctan(E''/E')$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

---

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define the terms storage modulus ($E'$), loss modulus ($E''$), and loss factor ($\tan\delta$) in DMA. Mention their units.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Storage Modulus ($E'$):** It is the measure of the elastic (energy-storing) response of a viscoelastic material, representing the in-phase component of the complex modulus with the imposed strain.
  **Unit: Pa (Pascal)**

- **Loss Modulus ($E''$):** It is the measure of the viscous (energy-dissipating) response, representing the out-of-phase component of the complex modulus, indicating the energy lost as heat per cycle.
  **Unit: Pa (Pascal)**

- **Loss Factor ($\tan\delta$):** It is the ratio of energy lost to energy stored per cycle of deformation:
  $$\tan\delta = \frac{E''}{E'}$$
  **Unit: Dimensionless**

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**How is the glass transition temperature ($T_g$) determined from a DMA thermogram?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The glass transition temperature is determined from the DMA thermogram by three possible criteria (in order of decreasing temperature):

1. **Peak of $\tan\delta$** — the most commonly used criterion; gives the highest $T_g$ value.
2. **Peak of $E''$** — the loss modulus peak; gives a slightly lower $T_g$.
3. **Onset of the drop in $E'$** — the inflection point of the storage modulus curve; gives the lowest $T_g$.

The $\tan\delta$ peak method is preferred by KTU because it is sharpest and most sensitive to segmental motion.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice — 14 Marks)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A) [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**
**(a)** Starting from the imposed sinusoidal strain in DMA, derive the expressions for storage modulus, loss modulus, and complex modulus. (7 marks)

**(b)** For a Maxwell element with $E = 5 \times 10^{8}$ Pa and $\eta = 5 \times 10^{10}$ Pa·s, calculate the frequency at which $\tan\delta$ is maximum and the corresponding values of $E'$ and $E''$. (7 marks)

---

**Solution to Q.A (a):**

Apply the strain $\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_{0}\sin(\omega t)$. The stress response is $\sigma(t) = \sigma_{0}\sin(\omega t + \delta)$. Expanding using $\sin(A+B)$:

$$\sigma(t) = \sigma_{0}\cos\delta\sin(\omega t) + \sigma_{0}\sin\delta\cos(\omega t)$$

Dividing by $\varepsilon_{0}$, the in-phase and out-of-phase amplitudes define the moduli:

$$E' = \frac{\sigma_{0}}{\varepsilon_{0}}\cos\delta \quad ; \quad E'' = \frac{\sigma_{0}}{\varepsilon_{0}}\sin\delta$$

The phasor combination yields:

$$E^{*} = E' + iE''$$

with magnitude:

$$\vert E^{*}\vert = \sqrt{(E')^{2} + (E'')^{2}}$$

and loss factor:

$$\tan\delta = \frac{E''}{E'}$$

(7 marks — full derivation as in Section 3.1 above.)

---

**Solution to Q.A (b):**

**Step 1.** For the Maxwell model, relaxation time:

$$\tau = \frac{\eta}{E} = \frac{5 \times 10^{10}}{5 \times 10^{8}} = 100 \text{ s}$$

**Step 2.** $\tan\delta$ is maximum at $\omega\tau = 1$, so:

$$\omega_{\max} = \frac{1}{\tau} = \frac{1}{100} = 0.01 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3.** Frequency in Hz:

$$f_{\max} = \frac{\omega_{\max}}{2\pi} = \frac{0.01}{6.283} \approx 1.59 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 4.** At $\omega\tau = 1$, $\omega^{2}\tau^{2} = 1$, so:

$$E' = \frac{E \cdot 1}{1+1} = \frac{E}{2} = 2.5 \times 10^{8} \text{ Pa}$$

$$E'' = \frac{E \cdot 1}{1+1} = \frac{E}{2} = 2.5 \times 10^{8} \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 5.** Verify $\tan\delta$:

$$\tan\delta = \frac{E''}{E'} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{8}}{2.5 \times 10^{8}} = 1$$

Confirmed — $\tan\delta = 1$ at the loss peak.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q.B) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**(a)** Describe with a neat sketch the principle and working of a DMA instrument. List the various deformation modes used. (7 marks)

**(b)** In a DMA experiment on PMMA, the following data were obtained at 1 Hz at the glass transition: $E' = 1.5 \times 10^{9}$ Pa and $\tan\delta = 0.85$. Calculate $E''$ and the phase angle $\delta$. If the imposed strain amplitude is $\varepsilon_{0} = 0.001$, find the stress amplitude $\sigma_{0}$. (7 marks)

---

**Solution to Q.B (a):**

**Principle:** A small sinusoidal mechanical strain is applied to a polymer specimen held in a temperature-controlled chamber. The stress response is measured and resolved into in-phase (storage) and out-of-phase (loss) components using a lock-in amplifier.

**Working (block diagram as in Section 4.1):**
- Signal generator → linear motor → oscillatory strain → sample.
- Load cell → measures stress; LVDT → measures strain.
- Lock-in amplifier → computes $E'$, $E''$, $\tan\delta$.
- Furnace with $\text{LN}_2$ cooling → temperature sweep.

**Deformation modes:**
1. Tensile — films, fibers.
2. Three-point bending — rigid bars.
3. Cantilever bending — coatings, supported beams.
4. Shear (sandwich) — adhesives, soft rubbers.
5. Compression — foams.
6. Torsion — melts, solids on rheometer.

---

**Solution to Q.B (b):**

**Step 1.** Compute $E''$:

$$E'' = E' \tan\delta = (1.5 \times 10^{9})(0.85) = 1.275 \times 10^{9} \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 2.** Phase angle:

$$\delta = \tan^{-1}(0.85) = 40.36° \quad (\text{or } 0.7045 \text{ rad})$$

**Step 3.** Magnitude of complex modulus:

$$\vert E^{*}\vert = \frac{E'}{\cos\delta} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^{9}}{\cos(40.36°)} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^{9}}{0.762} = 1.969 \times 10^{9} \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 4.** Stress amplitude:

$$\sigma_{0} = \vert E^{*}\vert \cdot \varepsilon_{0} = (1.969 \times 10^{9})(0.001) = 1.969 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa} \approx 1.97 \text{ MPa}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Units!** Marks are often lost for omitting **Pa** on $E'$, $E''$ or **rad/s** on $\omega$. Always write units.
> 2. **Confusing $\tan\delta$ with $\tan^{-1}\delta$** — $\tan\delta$ is a ratio; you must take its arctangent to get the phase angle $\delta$.
> 3. **Mixing $\omega$ (rad/s) and $f$ (Hz).** The relation $\omega = 2\pi f$ is mandatory in step transitions.
> 4. **Choosing the wrong peak for $T_g$.** The KTU official answer key uses the **$\tan\delta$ peak** unless otherwise stated.
> 5. **Forgetting that $E^{*} = E' + iE''$ is a complex sum, not a regular sum.** Show the phasor diagram to get full marks on derivation questions.
> 6. **Not drawing a labelled DMA schematic** in 14-mark questions — KTU awards 1–2 marks purely for the block diagram.
> 7. **For WLF/TTS questions**, students forget to take $\log_{10}$ and write $\ln$ by mistake — KTU strictly uses base-10 logarithm.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — DMA (PCPOT402, Module 2):**
>
> - **DMA** measures viscoelastic response under **oscillatory stress or strain** vs **temperature** and **frequency**.
> - **Storage modulus $E'$** = in-phase elastic response (energy stored).
> - **Loss modulus $E''$** = out-of-phase viscous response (energy dissipated).
> - **$\tan\delta = E''/E'$** = loss factor / damping.
> - **$E^{*} = E' + iE''$** = complex modulus (phasor form).
> - **$\vert E^{*}\vert = \sqrt{(E')^{2}+(E'')^{2}}$** — magnitude.
> - **$T_g$ from DMA** = peak of $\tan\delta$ curve (primary criterion).
> - **DMA sensitivity to $T_g$** is 10–100× better than DSC because $\tan\delta$ peak is very sharp.
> - **Five deformation modes** — tensile, compression, 3-point bending, cantilever, shear, torsion.
> - **Storage modulus drops 3–4 decades** across $T_g$ in amorphous polymers.
> - **Maxwell model** gives $\tan\delta_{\max}=1$ at $\omega\tau=1$.
> - **TTS / WLF equation** extends lab-time data to long service life.
> - **WLF constants** $C_1 = 17.44$, $C_2 = 51.6$ K for $T_0 = T_g$.
> - **DMA applications:** cure monitoring, damping design, tyre performance, blend miscibility, $T_g$ identification.
> - **Phasor diagram:** $E'$ along real axis, $E''$ along imaginary axis, $\delta$ between them.
> - **Units always:** $E', E'', E^{*}$ in **Pa**; $\omega$ in **rad/s**; $\tan\delta$ dimensionless.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Storage Modulus
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT402 - POLYMER PHYSICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Storage Modulus

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!NOTE]
> **Storage Modulus (E′ or G′)** is defined as the *real component* of the **complex dynamic modulus** of a viscoelastic polymeric material. It quantitatively measures the material's capacity to **store elastic (recoverable) energy** per cycle when subjected to a small-amplitude oscillatory deformation under isothermal conditions. Mathematically, it represents the **in-phase (elastic) component** of the stress response relative to the imposed sinusoidal strain.

In the framework of **Dynamic Mechanical Analysis (DMA)**, when a viscoelastic solid is deformed sinusoidally, the stress response is generally out-of-phase with the strain by a phase angle **δ (delta)**. The storage modulus isolates the energy-storage behavior of the polymer from its energy-dissipative (viscous) behavior.

**Mathematical Statement:**

$$E' = \left(\frac{\sigma_0}{\varepsilon_0}\right) \cos\delta$$

where:
- $\sigma_0$ = peak stress amplitude
- $\varepsilon_0$ = peak strain amplitude
- $\delta$ = phase lag between stress and strain ($0^\circ \leq \delta \leq 90^\circ$)

The corresponding **complex modulus** is given as:

$$E^* = E' + i E''$$

where $E''$ is the **loss modulus** and $i = \sqrt{-1}$.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Squash Ball in Hot Water**
>
> Imagine a **squash ball** that you repeatedly squeeze and release (oscillatory deformation). 
> - The **storage modulus** is like the **springiness** of the ball — how much it *bounces back* toward its original shape (elastic, energy-storing part).
> - The **loss modulus** is like the **internal friction** of the ball — how much energy is *lost as heat* (viscous, energy-dissipating part).
> - When the ball is **cold (glassy)**, it feels hard and brittle → **high E′**, very little dissipation.
> - When the ball is **warmed (rubbery region)**, it deforms easily and recovers → **lower E′** but still elastic-dominant.
> - Near **glass transition temperature (Tg)**, the ball becomes "leathery" and a lot of energy is lost as heat → E′ drops dramatically, E″ peaks.

This is the central idea: **Storage Modulus quantifies the "elastic stiffness memory"** of the polymer under dynamic loading.

### Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Unit (SI) | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Storage Modulus | $E'$ | **Pascal (Pa)** or **MPa** | Real part of complex modulus |
| Loss Modulus | $E''$ | Pa or MPa | Imaginary part of complex modulus |
| Loss Factor | $\tan\delta$ | dimensionless | $= E''/E'$ |
| Phase Angle | $\delta$ | radians or degrees | $0$ (purely elastic) to $\pi/2$ (purely viscous) |
| Complex Modulus | $E^*$ | Pa or MPa | Vector sum: $\vert E^* \vert = \sqrt{(E')^2 + (E'')^2}$ |
| Angular Frequency | $\omega$ | rad/s | $= 2\pi f$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Storage modulus is a **direct measure of stiffness** of a polymer in the solid state. It is the **dynamic analogue of Young's Modulus** and is the most commonly reported property in DMA studies of polymers.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Physics of Viscoelastic Response

When a polymer is subjected to a sinusoidal (oscillatory) strain:

$$\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_0 \sin(\omega t)$$

The stress response of a *purely elastic* (Hookean) solid would be **in-phase**:

$$\sigma_{elastic}(t) = E \cdot \varepsilon_0 \sin(\omega t)$$

A *purely viscous* (Newtonian) liquid's stress would be **90° out-of-phase**:

$$\sigma_{viscous}(t) = \eta \cdot \varepsilon_0 \omega \cos(\omega t) = \eta \cdot \dot{\varepsilon}$$

A **viscoelastic polymer** exhibits a response that is **intermediate**, lagging the strain by a phase angle $\delta$:

$$\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \sin(\omega t + \delta)$$

Using the trigonometric identity $\sin(A+B) = \sin A \cos B + \cos A \sin B$:

$$\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \cos\delta \cdot \sin(\omega t) + \sigma_0 \sin\delta \cdot \cos(\omega t)$$

This decomposition is the **heart of DMA theory**:
- First term → **in-phase (elastic)** with strain → governed by $E'$
- Second term → **90° out-of-phase (viscous)** with strain → governed by $E''$

### 2.2 The Five Transition Regions of Polymers

The storage modulus of an amorphous linear polymer changes by **several orders of magnitude** as temperature rises. KTU examiners frequently test the **five regions** of viscoelastic behavior:

| Region | Temperature Range | Storage Modulus Behavior | Physical State |
|---|---|---|---|
| **I. Glassy** | $T \ll T_g$ | $E' \sim 10^9 - 10^{10}$ Pa (very high, near-constant) | Rigid, brittle, only vibrational motion |
| **II. Glass Transition** | $T \approx T_g$ | $E'$ drops sharply by **3–4 decades** (1.5–3 decades in KTU textbook convention) | Cooperative segmental motion onset |
| **III. Rubbery Plateau** | $T > T_g$ (lightly crosslinked / high MW) | $E' \sim 10^5 - 10^6$ Pa (relatively flat) | Entanglements or crosslinks act as temporary/permanent network |
| **IV. Rubbery Flow** | Higher $T$ (uncrosslinked) | $E'$ drops again | Entanglements disentangle; chains flow |
| **V. Viscous Flow** | $T \gg T_g$ (uncrosslinked) | $E' \rightarrow 0$ | Liquid-like flow |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a **crosslinked polymer (thermoset/ vulcanized rubber)**, regions IV and V are *absent* because the permanent network prevents flow. The rubbery plateau extends until **thermal degradation**.

### 2.3 Molecular Interpretation of Storage Modulus

The molecular basis differs in each region:

- **Glassy Region:** Only **bond stretching, bond angle bending, and small-scale vibrations** occur. Segments are "frozen." High $E'$ arises from strong intermolecular forces resisting small deformations.
  
- **Transition Region (around Tg):** Long-range **cooperative segmental motion** of 10–50 backbone atoms becomes activated. Modulus drops because the polymer can now dissipate energy through conformational rearrangements.

- **Rubbery Region:** Long-range chain motion is fully active, but the **entanglement network** (in high-MW thermoplastics) or **covalent crosslinks** (in thermosets) provides elastic restoring force. The modulus here is governed by the **theory of rubber elasticity:**

$$E' \approx 3 \nu k_B T = 3 n R T$$

where $\nu$ = number density of network chains, $n$ = moles of network chains per unit volume, $k_B$ = Boltzmann constant, $R$ = gas constant.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_0 \sin(\omega t)$ | Applied oscillatory strain | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \sin(\omega t + \delta)$ | Resulting oscillatory stress | Pa |
| 3 | $E' = (\sigma_0/\varepsilon_0) \cos\delta$ | **Storage Modulus definition** | Pa |
| 4 | $E'' = (\sigma_0/\varepsilon_0) \sin\delta$ | Loss Modulus definition | Pa |
| 5 | $E^* = E' + i E''$ | Complex Modulus | Pa |
| 6 | $\vert E^* \vert = \sqrt{(E')^2 + (E'')^2}$ | Magnitude of complex modulus | Pa |
| 7 | $\tan\delta = E''/E'$ | **Loss factor / damping factor** | dimensionless |
| 8 | $G' = (\tau_0/\gamma_0)\cos\delta$ | Shear storage modulus (torsional DMA) | Pa |
| 9 | $E' \approx 3 \nu k_B T$ (rubbery plateau) | From rubber elasticity theory | Pa |
| 10 | $E' \propto 1/T$ (rubbery); $E' \propto T$ (glassy) | Approximate temperature trends | Pa |
| 11 | $E'(\omega) \rightleftharpoons E'(T)$ via TTS | Time–Temperature Superposition | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Storage modulus values directly govern **damping, vibration isolation, sound absorption, and load-bearing capacity** in polymer applications. For example, in **tire tread design**, the storage modulus at 10 Hz dictates the rolling resistance and grip; in **electronic encapsulation**, $E'$ at service temperature ensures mechanical integrity.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I: From Sinusoidal Strain to the Complex Modulus

**Given:** A polymer sample is subjected to a harmonic tensile strain $\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_0 \sin(\omega t)$. The resulting stress in a linear viscoelastic material is also harmonic but lags the strain by phase angle $\delta$:

$$\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \sin(\omega t + \delta)$$

**Step 1 — Expand the stress using the trigonometric sum identity:**

$$\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \sin(\omega t + \delta)$$

We use the identity $\sin(A + B) = \sin A \cos B + \cos A \sin B$ with $A = \omega t$ and $B = \delta$:

$$\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \cos\delta \cdot \sin(\omega t) + \sigma_0 \sin\delta \cdot \cos(\omega t)$$

**Step 2 — Identify the in-phase and out-of-phase components:**

- The first term, $\sigma_0 \cos\delta \cdot \sin(\omega t)$, varies as $\sin(\omega t)$ — i.e., **in-phase** with the strain.
- The second term, $\sigma_0 \sin\delta \cdot \cos(\omega t)$, varies as $\cos(\omega t) = \sin(\omega t + \pi/2)$ — i.e., **90° out-of-phase** with the strain.

**Step 3 — Express the stress in complex notation (Euler's form):**

For sinusoidal functions, it is mathematically convenient to use the **complex representation:**

$$\varepsilon^*(t) = \varepsilon_0 \, e^{i\omega t}$$

$$\sigma^*(t) = \sigma_0 \, e^{i(\omega t + \delta)} = \sigma_0 \, e^{i\omega t} e^{i\delta}$$

**Step 4 — Define the complex modulus as the ratio:**

$$E^*(\omega) = \frac{\sigma^*(t)}{\varepsilon^*(t)} = \frac{\sigma_0}{\varepsilon_0} e^{i\delta}$$

**Step 5 — Apply Euler's identity $e^{i\delta} = \cos\delta + i\sin\delta$:**

$$E^*(\omega) = \frac{\sigma_0}{\varepsilon_0}\left(\cos\delta + i\sin\delta\right)$$

**Step 6 — Separate the real and imaginary parts:**

$$\boxed{E^*(\omega) = \underbrace{\frac{\sigma_0}{\varepsilon_0}\cos\delta}_{\text{Real part} \, = \, E'} + i\underbrace{\frac{\sigma_0}{\varepsilon_0}\sin\delta}_{\text{Imaginary part} \, = \, E''}}$$

This gives the **operational definitions** of storage and loss moduli. **Q.E.D.**

---

### 3.2 Derivation II: Storage Modulus in the Rubbery Plateau from Rubber Elasticity Theory

**Given:** A crosslinked polymer network at temperature $T > T_g$ is in its rubbery state. The network consists of $\nu$ active chains per unit volume, each acting as an entropy spring.

**Step 1 — Start with the classical entropy-based shear modulus for an ideal rubber:**

$$G = \nu k_B T$$

**Step 2 — Relate shear modulus to Young's modulus via the small-strain isotropic relation (for an incompressible rubber, Poisson's ratio $\nu_{ratio} \approx 0.5$):**

$$E = 2G(1 + \nu_{ratio})$$

With $\nu_{ratio} = 0.5$:

$$E = 2G(1 + 0.5) = 3G$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the expression for $G$:**

$$\boxed{E'_{rubbery} = 3 \nu k_B T = 3 n R T}$$

where $n$ is the number of moles of elastically active network chains per unit volume and $R = N_A k_B$ is the universal gas constant.

**Step 4 — Physical interpretation:** The storage modulus in the rubbery plateau *increases linearly* with absolute temperature. This is counter-intuitive (most materials soften with heating) and is a **signature of entropic elasticity** — heating increases chain conformational entropy, increasing the elastic restoring force.

---

### 3.3 Derivation III: Storage Modulus in the Glassy Region

**Given:** In the glassy state, polymer deformation involves only **bond stretching and bond angle bending** (not chain conformational changes). The behavior is approximately Hookean with modulus $E_g$ weakly dependent on temperature.

**Step 1 — Assume a linear elastic response:**

$$\sigma = E_g \varepsilon$$

**Step 2 — In a DMA experiment with oscillatory strain, the response is nearly in-phase ($\delta \to 0$), so $\cos\delta \approx 1$:**

$$E' \approx \frac{\sigma_0}{\varepsilon_0} = E_g$$

**Step 3 — For many glassy polymers, $E'_g$ can be approximated by an empirical Tobolsky-type relation involving cohesive energy density (CED):**

$$E'_g \approx 8 \times (\text{CED})^{1/2} \quad \text{(in cal/cm}^3\text{, with result in dyne/cm}^2\text{)}$$

**Step 4 — Typical magnitudes:** For most amorphous glassy polymers, $E'_g \approx 1 - 5$ GPa, and for highly crosslinked glassy thermosets, $E'_g$ may reach 10 GPa.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Storage Modulus Across the Viscoelastic Spectrum

| Frequency | Glassy Region | Transition | Rubbery Plateau | Flow Region |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Low freq. ($\omega \to 0$)** | $E' \approx E_g$ | Decreasing | $E' \approx 3\nu k_B T$ (plateau) | $E' \to 0$ |
| **High freq. ($\omega \to \infty$)** | $E' \approx E_g$ | Increasing | Plateau extends | $E' \approx 3\nu k_B T$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Frequency-Temperature Equivalence (TTS):** Increasing frequency has the *same effect* as decreasing temperature. This is the foundation of the **Time-Temperature Superposition Principle** of Williams–Landel–Ferry (WLF), which allows construction of **master curves** of $E'(\omega)$ spanning 10+ decades from short DMA scans.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: DMA Measurement of Storage Modulus

The following Mermaid flowchart depicts the **operational signal flow** in a typical DMA instrument as it determines $E'$.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Function Generator"] --> nodeB["Oscillatory Strain Signal epsilon t = epsilon0 sin omega t"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Mechanical Driver Assembly"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Polymer Sample in Clamp"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Force Transducer LVDT"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Phase Sensitive Detector"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Compute Stress Amplitude sigma0"]
    nodeF --> nodeH["Compute Phase Lag delta"]
    nodeG --> nodeI["Divider Block: sigma0 / epsilon0"]
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ["Multiply by cos delta"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK["OUTPUT: Storage Modulus E prime"]
    nodeH --> nodeL["Multiply by sin delta"]
    nodeL --> nodeM["OUTPUT: Loss Modulus E double prime"]
    nodeK --> nodeN["Data Logger and Plot E prime vs T"]
    nodeM --> nodeN
```

### 4.2 Schematic of $E'$ Behavior Across Five Viscoelastic Regions

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Region I GLASSY"] -->|Tg: 3-4 decades drop| nodeB["Region II GLASS TRANSITION"]
    nodeB -->|Rubbery plateau onset| nodeC["Region III RUBBERY PLATEAU"]
    nodeC -->|If uncrosslinked: drop| nodeD["Region IV RUBBERY FLOW"]
    nodeD -->|Chain disentanglement| nodeE["Region V VISCOUS FLOW"]
    nodeA -.- nodeA1["E prime ~ 1 to 5 GPa"]
    nodeB -.- nodeB1["E prime drops sharply"]
    nodeC -.- nodeC1["E prime ~ 0.1 to 1 MPa"]
    nodeD -.- nodeD1["E prime drops further"]
    nodeE -.- nodeE1["E prime approaches zero"]
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Relationship Between $E'$ and $E''$

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Applied Strain epsilon t"] --> nodeB["Stress Response sigma t"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Resolve into Components"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["In Phase Component: sigma0 cos delta"]
    nodeC --> nodeE["Out of Phase Component: sigma0 sin delta"]
    nodeD --> nodeF["Divide by epsilon0: Storage Modulus E prime"]
    nodeE --> nodeG["Divide by epsilon0: Loss Modulus E double prime"]
    nodeF --> nodeH["Ratio: tan delta = E double prime / E prime"]
    nodeG --> nodeH
```

### 4.4 Frequency-Temperature Superposition Master Curve Concept

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Short Frequency Scan at T1"] --> nodeB["Short Frequency Scan at T2"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Short Frequency Scan at T3"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Horizontal Shifts along log omega axis"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Shift Factors aT via WLF Equation"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Constructed Master Curve of E prime vs omega"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Spans 10 to 15 decades of frequency"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
> **Q1.** Define the term **Storage Modulus** of a polymeric material. Mention its SI unit and physical significance.

**Model Answer:**

> **Storage Modulus ($E'$)** is defined as the real part of the **complex dynamic modulus** of a viscoelastic polymer. It is a measure of the material's ability to **store elastic (recoverable) energy** per cycle of sinusoidal deformation. Mathematically, it is the in-phase component of the stress response relative to the applied strain:
>
> $$E' = \frac{\sigma_0}{\varepsilon_0}\cos\delta$$
>
> **SI unit:** Pascal (Pa), commonly reported in MPa or GPa. **Physical significance:** $E'$ represents the *stiffness* or *elastic solid-like response* of the polymer under dynamic loading. A high $E'$ indicates a rigid polymer (e.g., glassy state), while a low $E'$ indicates a soft, rubbery, or molten polymer.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
> **Q2.** Differentiate between **Storage Modulus ($E'$)** and **Loss Modulus ($E''$)** with reference to their physical meaning and mathematical expressions.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Storage Modulus ($E'$) | Loss Modulus ($E''$) |
|---|---|---|
| **Mathematical expression** | $E' = (\sigma_0/\varepsilon_0)\cos\delta$ | $E'' = (\sigma_0/\varepsilon_0)\sin\delta$ |
| **Position in $E^*$** | Real part | Imaginary part |
| **Phase relationship** | In-phase with strain | 90° out-of-phase with strain |
| **Energy** | Energy *stored* elastically per cycle | Energy *dissipated* (as heat) per cycle |
| **Analogy** | Spring element (Hookean) | Dashpot element (Newtonian) |
| **Magnitude in glassy region** | Very high ($\sim$GPa) | Very low |
| **Magnitude in transition region** | Drops sharply | Peaks near $T_g$ |
| **Use in design** | Predicts stiffness and load-bearing capacity | Predicts damping and impact resistance |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With the help of suitable diagrams, describe the **Dynamic Mechanical Analysis (DMA)** technique used to measure the storage modulus of a polymer. Discuss the various regions observed when $E'$ is plotted as a function of temperature for a typical amorphous polymer. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A viscoelastic polymer is subjected to an oscillatory tensile strain $\varepsilon(t) = 0.005 \sin(10\pi t)$. The resulting stress response is $\sigma(t) = 2.5 \times 10^5 \sin(10\pi t + 0.20)$ Pa. Calculate the **storage modulus ($E'$)**, **loss modulus ($E''$)**, **complex modulus magnitude ($|E^*|$)**, and the **loss factor ($\tan\delta$)**. (7 Marks)

**Model Solutions:**

**(a) Solution:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DMA** is the technique in which a small sinusoidal mechanical deformation is applied to a polymer sample and the resulting force (stress) is measured. From the amplitude ratio and phase lag, both $E'$ and $E''$ are computed.

**Clamping modes:** Tension, compression, shear, bending (cantilever or 3-point bend), and torsion.

**Procedure:**

1. The polymer sample is mounted in a clamping geometry (e.g., tension).
2. A **function generator** drives a mechanical actuator imposing a controlled sinusoidal strain $\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_0 \sin(\omega t)$.
3. A **force transducer (load cell)** measures the stress response $\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \sin(\omega t + \delta)$.
4. A **phase-sensitive detector / lock-in amplifier** resolves $\sigma_0$ and $\delta$.
5. $E' = (\sigma_0/\varepsilon_0)\cos\delta$ and $E'' = (\sigma_0/\varepsilon_0)\sin\delta$ are computed.
6. The temperature is swept (or frequency varied) and the $E'$ vs $T$ curve is recorded.

**Five Regions in the $E'$ vs $T$ Plot for an Amorphous Polymer:**

| Region | Description | $E'$ Magnitude | Molecular Origin |
|---|---|---|---|
| I. Glassy | Below $T_g$ | $10^9 - 10^{9.5}$ Pa | Vibrational motion only |
| II. Glass Transition | Near $T_g$ | Sharp drop of 3–4 decades | Onset of cooperative segmental motion |
| III. Rubbery Plateau | Above $T_g$ (crosslinked / high MW) | $10^5 - 10^6$ Pa | Entanglements/crosslinks network |
| IV. Rubbery Flow | Higher T (uncrosslinked) | Drops | Disengagement of entanglements |
| V. Viscous Flow | Highest T (uncrosslinked) | $\to 0$ | Liquid flow |

**Sketch of $E'$ vs $T$ (in words, since no figure can be drawn):**
- Horizontal high plateau → steep drop near $T_g$ → flat rubbery plateau → second drop (if linear polymer) → approach zero.

**(b) Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Strain: $\varepsilon(t) = 0.005 \sin(10\pi t)$ → $\varepsilon_0 = 0.005$, $\omega = 10\pi$ rad/s
- Stress: $\sigma(t) = 2.5 \times 10^5 \sin(10\pi t + 0.20)$ Pa → $\sigma_0 = 2.5 \times 10^5$ Pa, $\delta = 0.20$ rad

**Step 1 — Compute Storage Modulus $E'$:**

$$E' = \frac{\sigma_0}{\varepsilon_0}\cos\delta = \frac{2.5 \times 10^5}{0.005}\cos(0.20)$$

$$\cos(0.20) \approx 0.98007$$

$$E' = 5.0 \times 10^7 \times 0.98007 = 4.9003 \times 10^7 \, \text{Pa} \approx 49.0 \, \text{MPa}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Loss Modulus $E''$:**

$$E'' = \frac{\sigma_0}{\varepsilon_0}\sin\delta = 5.0 \times 10^7 \times \sin(0.20)$$

$$\sin(0.20) \approx 0.19867$$

$$E'' = 5.0 \times 10^7 \times 0.19867 = 9.9335 \times 10^6 \, \text{Pa} \approx 9.93 \, \text{MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Complex Modulus Magnitude $|E^*|$:**

$$\vert E^* \vert = \sqrt{(E')^2 + (E'')^2} = \sqrt{(4.9003 \times 10^7)^2 + (9.9335 \times 10^6)^2}$$

$$\vert E^* \vert = \sqrt{2.4013 \times 10^{15} + 9.8674 \times 10^{13}}$$

$$\vert E^* \vert = \sqrt{2.5000 \times 10^{15}} = 5.0 \times 10^7 \, \text{Pa} = 50.0 \, \text{MPa}$$

**Step 4 — Loss Factor $\tan\delta$:**

$$\tan\delta = \frac{E''}{E'} = \frac{9.9335 \times 10^6}{4.9003 \times 10^7} = 0.2027$$

**Summary of Results:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| $E'$ | $49.0$ MPa |
| $E''$ | $9.93$ MPa |
| $\vert E^* \vert$ | $50.0$ MPa |
| $\tan\delta$ | $0.203$ |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Starting from the equation of a sinusoidal stress response, derive the expression for the **storage modulus** of a viscoelastic polymer. Clearly show the decomposition into in-phase and out-of-phase components and introduce the loss factor. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Explain the molecular origin of the storage modulus in the **glassy region** and the **rubbery plateau region** of an amorphous polymer. How is the rubbery plateau modulus related to the **crosslink density**? (7 Marks)

**Model Solutions:**

**(a) Solution:**

Consider a polymer subjected to a sinusoidal strain:

$$\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_0 \sin(\omega t)$$

For a linear viscoelastic material, the resulting stress is sinusoidal with the same frequency but with a phase lag $\delta$:

$$\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \sin(\omega t + \delta)$$

Expanding using the identity $\sin(A + B) = \sin A \cos B + \cos A \sin B$:

$$\sigma(t) = \underbrace{\sigma_0 \cos\delta \cdot \sin(\omega t)}_{\text{in-phase}} + \underbrace{\sigma_0 \sin\delta \cdot \cos(\omega t)}_{\text{out-of-phase}}$$

The in-phase component is in step with the strain; the out-of-phase component is 90° ahead. Dividing by strain amplitude and using complex notation:

$$E^* = \frac{\sigma^*(t)}{\varepsilon^*(t)} = \frac{\sigma_0}{\varepsilon_0} e^{i\delta} = \frac{\sigma_0}{\varepsilon_0}(\cos\delta + i\sin\delta) = E' + iE''$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{E' = \frac{\sigma_0}{\varepsilon_0}\cos\delta, \quad E'' = \frac{\sigma_0}{\varepsilon_0}\sin\delta, \quad \tan\delta = \frac{E''}{E'}}$$

**(b) Solution:**

**Glassy Region:**
At temperatures well below $T_g$, the polymer chains are "frozen." Only **local motions** — bond stretching, bond-angle bending, and small-scale librations of side groups — occur. There is no long-range segmental motion. Therefore, deformation is resisted primarily by **interatomic potentials** and **secondary bond forces (van der Waals, dipole-dipole)** between closely packed chains. The polymer behaves essentially as a **Hookean elastic solid**, with $E' \approx 1 - 5$ GPa, weakly decreasing with temperature (as thermal expansion reduces the interchain interaction density).

**Rubbery Plateau Region:**
Above $T_g$, **long-range conformational changes** of the chain backbone become possible — segments can rotate and rearrange. However, in a crosslinked network (or entangled high-molecular-weight linear polymer), the **permanent junctions** (covalent crosslinks) or **transient junctions** (entanglements) prevent chain flow. The polymer behaves like an **entropic spring**: deformation reduces chain conformational entropy, and the entropic restoring force provides elasticity.

**Relation to Crosslink Density (Rubber Elasticity Theory):**

The shear modulus in the rubbery plateau is:

$$G'_{rubbery} = \nu k_B T = n R T$$

And the tensile storage modulus:

$$E'_{rubbery} = 3 G'_{rubbery} = 3 \nu k_B T = 3 n R T$$

where:
- $\nu$ = number of elastically active network chains per unit volume (chains/m³)
- $n$ = moles of such chains per unit volume
- $R$ = universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- $k_B$ = **Boltzmann constant = 1.381 × 10⁻²³ J/K**
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)

> **Key Insight:** In the rubbery region, the storage modulus is **directly proportional to crosslink density** and **directly proportional to absolute temperature** — a hallmark of *entropic* (not energetic) elasticity.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 
> 1. **Unit errors:** Forgetting to convert $\delta$ from degrees to radians in trigonometric functions. Always use **radians** in $\sin$ and $\cos$ for calculus consistency.
> 
> 2. **Confusing $E'$ and Young's Modulus:** Static Young's modulus ($E$) is measured in a single, slow monotonic loading. $E'$ is measured dynamically with an oscillating load. They are equal *only* in the limit $\omega \to 0$ and only for purely elastic materials. **In the transition and rubbery regions, $E' < E$ (static).**
> 
> 3. **Forgetting the factor of 3 in rubber elasticity:** $E' = 3G'$ comes from the incompressibility assumption ($\nu_{Poisson} = 0.5$). Dropping this factor results in a **3-mark loss** in KTU papers.
> 
> 4. **Phase angle interpretation:** Students often mis-attribute $\delta = 0$ to "no motion" — actually, $\delta = 0$ means *purely elastic* (stress and strain in-phase), and the material can still be deformed significantly.
> 
> 5. **Glass transition identification:** $T_g$ is *not* the peak of $E'$ drop — it is more precisely identified as the peak of $\tan\delta$ or the peak of $E''$ in a DMA scan. Mis-stating this costs marks.
> 
> 6. **Sign convention:** $\tan\delta$ and $E''$ are always **positive** for passive materials (energy cannot be created).
> 
> 7. **Mastery of $\omega$ vs $f$:** $\omega = 2\pi f$. Common mistake: substituting frequency $f$ (Hz) directly into formulas requiring angular frequency $\omega$ (rad/s).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Storage Modulus**

- **Definition:** $E' = (\sigma_0/\varepsilon_0)\cos\delta$ — *real part* of complex modulus $E^* = E' + iE''$.
- **Physical meaning:** Stored elastic energy per cycle; a measure of **stiffness** in dynamic loading.
- **SI Unit:** Pascal (Pa). In polymer engineering, typically reported in **MPa** or **GPa**.
- **Five Regions of $E'$ vs $T$:** Glassy → Glass Transition → Rubbery Plateau → Rubbery Flow → Viscous Flow.
- **Magnitude in glassy region:** $E'_g \sim 1 - 5$ GPa.
- **Magnitude in rubbery region:** $E'_R \sim 0.1 - 1$ MPa.
- **Drop at $T_g$:** Typically **3 to 4 decades** (factor of 1000–10000) for amorphous polymers.
- **Rubber elasticity formula:** $E'_{rubbery} = 3 \nu k_B T = 3 n R T$ — *increases* with temperature (entropic origin).
- **Glassy modulus origin:** Bond stretching, bond bending, van der Waals forces.
- **Loss factor:** $\tan\delta = E''/E'$.
- **Time-Temperature Superposition:** Higher $\omega \equiv$ Lower $T$. Master curves are built using the **WLF equation** $\log a_T = -C_1 (T - T_{ref})/[C_2 + (T - T_{ref})]$.
- **DMA instruments** measure $E'$ and $E''$ across frequencies (typically 0.01 Hz to 200 Hz) and temperatures.
- **Storage Modulus** differs from **Young's Modulus**: $E'$ is frequency-dependent, $E$ (static) is not.
- **Tensile storage modulus** is $E'$; **shear storage modulus** is $G'$; **bulk storage modulus** is $K'$. For incompressible materials, $E' = 3G'$.
- **Crosslink density** controls rubbery plateau height: $E' \propto \nu$ (network chain density).
- **Peak in $\tan\delta$** gives a precise estimate of $T_g$ in DMA.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Loss Modulus
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPOT402 - POLYMER PHYSICS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Loss Modulus ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) The **Loss Modulus (denoted G″ for shear mode or E″ for tensile mode)** is defined as the **imaginary component of the complex viscoelastic modulus** that quantifies the **energy dissipated as heat per unit volume per cycle of harmonic deformation**. Mathematically, it represents the *viscous response* of a polymeric material under oscillatory (dynamic) loading. For a complex shear modulus: $$G^* = G' + i\,G''$$ The loss modulus is the component $G''$ which is proportional to the strain rate and is responsible for the irreversible, heat-generating portion of the mechanical response. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Board Definition (verbatim for exam writing):** > *"The Loss Modulus is the measure of a material's tendency to dissipate mechanical energy as heat when subjected to cyclic deformation. It is the out-of-phase component of the complex modulus, representing the viscous (liquid-like) response of a viscoelastic polymer."* ### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy **The "Wet Sponge" Analogy:** Imagine squeezing a **wet sponge** in your hand repeatedly (in and out, rhythmically): | Component | Physical Action | Energy Fate | Polymer Equivalent | |-----------|----------------|-------------|---------------------| | **Elastic part (G′)** | Sponge deforms and returns to original shape | Energy **STORED** temporarily (released on decompression) | Storage Modulus — recoverable | | **Viscous part (G″)** | Water seeps out and is **permanently lost** as the sponge shape shifts | Energy **DISSIPATED** as heat (fluid friction) | Loss Modulus — non-recoverable | A **perfectly elastic solid** (like a steel spring) has $G'' = 0$ — all energy is recovered. A **perfectly viscous liquid** (like honey) has $G' = 0$ — all energy is lost as heat. A **polymer** sits in between — it is **viscoelastic**, exhibiting *both* responses simultaneously. ### 1.3 Physical Significance in Polymer Science > [!NOTE] > **Why Loss Modulus Matters in Polymer Engineering:** > - Identifies **glass transition temperature ($T_g$)** — loss modulus peaks at major molecular motions > - Detects **secondary (sub-$T_g$) transitions** — local rotations of side groups, chain segment motions > - Quantifies **damping performance** — critical for vibration-dampers, tires, shoe soles, acoustic insulation > - Predicts **processing behavior** — indicates melt flow energy requirements during extrusion/injection moulding > - Evaluates **long-term durability** — high G″ relative to G′ indicates accelerated fatigue ### 1.4 Standard Units and Dimensions - **SI Unit:** **Pascal (Pa)**, more commonly expressed in **MPa** or **GPa** for polymers - **Dimensions:** $[M\,L^{-1}\,T^{-2}]$ — same as any elastic modulus - **Typical Polymer Ranges:** - Glassy region: $G'' \approx 10^{7}$ to $10^{8}$ Pa - Rubbery plateau: $G'' \approx 10^{5}$ to $10^{6}$ Pa - Terminal flow: $G'' \approx 10^{3}$ to $10^{4}$ Pa ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown The concept of loss modulus emerges from the **dynamic mechanical analysis (DMA)** framework, where a sinusoidal strain is imposed on the polymer and the resulting stress response is measured. **Step-by-Step Logic of Viscoelastic Response:** - **Step 1 — Harmonic Strain Input:** A sinusoidal strain is applied: $$\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_0 \sin(\omega t)$$ where $\varepsilon_0$ is the strain amplitude and $\omega$ is the angular frequency. - **Step 2 — Stress Response with Phase Lag:** Due to viscoelasticity, the stress lags the strain by a phase angle $\delta$ (where $0^\circ \leq \delta \leq 90^\circ$): $$\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \sin(\omega t + \delta)$$ - **Step 3 — Trig Expansion:** $$\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \cos\delta \sin(\omega t) + \sigma_0 \sin\delta \cos(\omega t)$$ - **Step 4 — Define Modulus Components:** Dividing through by strain amplitude, two moduli emerge: - **In-phase (storage) component:** $G' = (\sigma_0/\varepsilon_0)\cos\delta$ — energy **stored** - **Out-of-phase (loss) component:** $G'' = (\sigma_0/\varepsilon_0)\sin\delta$ — energy **lost as heat** - **Step 5 — Complex Representation:** Combine into one complex number: $$G^* = G' + i\,G''$$ - **Step 6 — Magnitude (Absolute Modulus):** $$\vert G^* \vert = \sqrt{{G'}^2 + {G''}^2}$$ ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes | |---|---------|-------------|---------------| | 1 | $G^* = G' + i\,G''$ | Complex shear modulus definition | Pa | | 2 | $E^* = E' + i\,E''$ | Complex tensile (Young's) modulus | Pa | | 3 | $G' = G^* \cos\delta$ | Storage modulus (in-phase) | Pa | | 4 | $G'' = G^* \sin\delta$ | **Loss modulus (out-of-phase)** | Pa | | 5 | $\tan\delta = G''/G' = E''/E'$ | Loss tangent / damping factor | dimensionless | | 6 | $\vert G^* \vert = \sqrt{{G'}^2 + {G''}^2}$ | Absolute modulus magnitude | Pa | | 7 | $\Delta W = \pi\,G''\,{\varepsilon_0}^2$ | Energy dissipated per cycle | J/m³/cycle | | 8 | $\Delta W = \pi\,\sigma_0^2/G''$ | Alternate form (stress amplitude) | J/m³/cycle | | 9 | $\sigma(t) = G' \varepsilon_0 \sin(\omega t) + G'' \varepsilon_0 \cos(\omega t)$ | Stress response decomposition | Pa | | 10 | $\eta' = G''/\omega$ | Dynamic viscosity | Pa·s | | 11 | $G^* = i\omega\eta^*$ | Complex modulus via complex viscosity | Pa | | 12 | $T_g \approx T(\text{peak of } G'')$ | Glass transition from loss modulus peak | K or °C | | 13 | $\tau = \eta/G$ | Characteristic relaxation time | s | | 14 | $G(t) = G_{\text{equilibrium}} + \sum G_i\,e^{-t/\tau_i}$ | Relaxation modulus (Prony series) | Pa | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Distinction for KTU Board Exams:** > - $G'$ → **Storage Modulus** → Elastic (solid-like) behavior → Energy stored and recovered > - $G''$ → **Loss Modulus** → Viscous (liquid-like) behavior → Energy dissipated as heat > - $\tan\delta$ → **Loss Tangent** → Ratio of lost to stored energy → Peak indicates transitions ### 2.3 The Phase Angle $\delta$ — Physical Meaning The phase lag $\delta$ between stress and strain is the *single most diagnostic parameter* in DMA: - **$\delta = 0°$** → Perfectly elastic solid (Hookean) — pure spring response - **$\delta = 90°$** → Perfectly viscous liquid (Newtonian) — pure dashpot response - **$0° < \delta < 90°$** → **Viscoelastic polymer** — spring + dashpot (Kelvin–Voigt or Maxwell models) ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications | Industry Sector | Application of Loss Modulus | |-----------------|----------------------------| | **Automotive** | Tire tread design — high G″ at road frequencies for grip and noise reduction | | **Aerospace** | Composite matrix selection — controlled damping in turbine blades | | **Sports Goods** | Running shoes, ski boots — peak tan δ at body vibration frequencies | | **Packaging** | Foams and films — energy absorption in impact events | | **Biomedical** | Artificial cartilage, dental composites — match natural tissue viscoelasticity | | **Civil Engineering** | Bridge bearing pads, base isolators — seismic energy dissipation | ### 2.5 Frequency and Temperature Dependence > [!IMPORTANT] > **Time–Temperature Superposition (TTS) Principle:** > Loss modulus varies with both frequency and temperature. Increasing temperature shifts the $G''$ vs. frequency curve horizontally to higher frequencies (and vice versa). This allows construction of **master curves** spanning 10–15 decades of frequency from limited experimental data. The Williams–Landel–Ferry (WLF) equation governs this shift: > $$\log a_T = -\frac{C_1 (T - T_{\text{ref}})}{C_2 + (T - T_{\text{ref}})}$$ > where $a_T$ is the horizontal shift factor, and $C_1$, $C_2$ are empirical constants. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Complex Modulus from Harmonic Loading **Given:** - Applied strain: $\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_0 \sin(\omega t)$ - Resulting stress: $\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \sin(\omega t + \delta)$ **Step 1:** Expand the stress using the trig identity $\sin(A+B) = \sin A \cos B + \cos A \sin B$: $$\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \bigl[\sin(\omega t)\cos\delta + \cos(\omega t)\sin\delta\bigr]$$ **Step 2:** Separate into in-phase and out-of-phase components with respect to strain: $$\sigma(t) = \underbrace{\sigma_0 \cos\delta \cdot \sin(\omega t)}_{\text{in-phase (elastic)}} + \underbrace{\sigma_0 \sin\delta \cdot \cos(\omega t)}_{\text{out-of-phase (viscous)}}$$ **Step 3:** Recognize that $\sin(\omega t) \equiv$ the strain function and $\cos(\omega t) \equiv \varepsilon(t)$ rotated by $+90°$. Define: $$G' \equiv \frac{\sigma_0}{\varepsilon_0}\cos\delta \quad\text{(storage modulus)}$$ $$G'' \equiv \frac{\sigma_0}{\varepsilon_0}\sin\delta \quad\text{(loss modulus)}$$ **Step 4:** Rewrite stress as: $$\sigma(t) = G'\,\varepsilon_0 \sin(\omega t) + G''\,\varepsilon_0 \cos(\omega t)$$ **Step 5:** Express the strain as $\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_0 \sin(\omega t) = \text{Im}\bigl\{\varepsilon_0 e^{i\omega t}\bigr\}$. Then the stress in complex notation is: $$\sigma^*(t) = G^*\,\varepsilon_0 e^{i\omega t}$$ **Step 6:** Therefore, by matching the real and imaginary parts: $$G^* = \frac{\sigma^*(t)}{\varepsilon_0 e^{i\omega t}} = G' + i\,G''$$ **Conclusion:** The complex shear modulus separates naturally into the **in-phase storage** part ($G'$) and the **out-of-phase loss** part ($G''$). --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Relation Between G′ and G″ via Phase Angle **Given:** $G' = (\sigma_0/\varepsilon_0)\cos\delta$ and $G'' = (\sigma_0/\varepsilon_0)\sin\delta$ **Step 1:** Form the ratio $G''/G'$: $$\frac{G''}{G'} = \frac{\sin\delta}{\cos\delta} = \tan\delta$$ **Step 2:** Square each modulus: $${G'}^2 = \left(\frac{\sigma_0}{\varepsilon_0}\right)^2 \cos^2\delta \quad ; \quad {G''}^2 = \left(\frac{\sigma_0}{\varepsilon_0}\right)^2 \sin^2\delta$$ **Step 3:** Add the squares: $${G'}^2 + {G''}^2 = \left(\frac{\sigma_0}{\varepsilon_0}\right)^2 \bigl(\cos^2\delta + \sin^2\delta\bigr) = \left(\frac{\sigma_0}{\varepsilon_0}\right)^2$$ (using the Pythagorean identity $\cos^2\delta + \sin^2\delta = 1$) **Step 4:** Recognize $\sigma_0/\varepsilon_0 \equiv \vert G^* \vert$: $$\boxed{\;\vert G^* \vert = \sqrt{{G'}^2 + {G''}^2}\;}$$ **Step 5:** Express G′ and G″ in terms of $\vert G^* \vert$ and $\delta$: $$G' = \vert G^* \vert \cos\delta \quad\text{and}\quad G'' = \vert G^* \vert \sin\delta$$ --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Energy Dissipated Per Cycle This is the **physical heart** of the loss modulus concept. **Given:** $\sigma(t) = G'\,\varepsilon_0 \sin(\omega t) + G''\,\varepsilon_0 \cos(\omega t)$ and $\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_0 \sin(\omega t)$ **Step 1:** The instantaneous power dissipated (per unit volume) is: $$p(t) = \sigma(t)\,\dot{\varepsilon}(t)$$ **Step 2:** Compute the strain rate: $$\dot{\varepsilon}(t) = \frac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = \varepsilon_0 \omega \cos(\omega t)$$ **Step 3:** Form the product $\sigma(t)\,\dot{\varepsilon}(t)$: $$p(t) = \bigl[G'\,\varepsilon_0 \sin(\omega t) + G''\,\varepsilon_0 \cos(\omega t)\bigr] \cdot \varepsilon_0\omega\cos(\omega t)$$ **Step 4:** Expand: $$p(t) = G'\,{\varepsilon_0}^2 \omega \sin(\omega t)\cos(\omega t) + G''\,{\varepsilon_0}^2 \omega \cos^2(\omega t)$$ **Step 5:** Integrate over one complete cycle (period $T = 2\pi/\omega$): $$\Delta W = \int_0^{T} p(t)\,dt = G'\,{\varepsilon_0}^2 \omega \int_0^{T} \sin(\omega t)\cos(\omega t)\,dt + G''\,{\varepsilon_0}^2 \omega \int_0^{T} \cos^2(\omega t)\,dt$$ **Step 6:** Evaluate each integral. The first integral over a full period vanishes: $$\int_0^{T} \sin(\omega t)\cos(\omega t)\,dt = \frac{1}{2}\int_0^{T} \sin(2\omega t)\,dt = 0$$ This shows **no net elastic energy is lost over a cycle** — it is fully recovered. (The energy stored during compression equals the energy released during decompression.) **Step 7:** Evaluate the second integral using the average of $\cos^2$ over one period: $$\int_0^{T} \cos^2(\omega t)\,dt = \frac{T}{2} = \frac{\pi}{\omega}$$ **Step 8:** Substitute back: $$\Delta W = G''\,{\varepsilon_0}^2 \omega \cdot \frac{\pi}{\omega}$$ $$\boxed{\;\Delta W_{\text{per cycle}} = \pi\,G''\,{\varepsilon_0}^2\;}$$ **Physical Interpretation:** The energy lost as heat per cycle is *directly proportional* to $G''$. Hence the name **"Loss" Modulus**. --- ### 3.4 Derivation 4: Relation Between G″ and Dynamic Viscosity **Given:** For a Newtonian fluid, $\sigma = \eta\,\dot{\varepsilon}$. For a viscoelastic solid under harmonic strain $\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_0 e^{i\omega t}$, the strain rate is $\dot{\varepsilon} = i\omega\varepsilon_0 e^{i\omega t}$. **Step 1:** The complex stress is: $$\sigma^* = G^* \varepsilon_0 e^{i\omega t} = (G' + i G'')\,\varepsilon_0 e^{i\omega t}$$ **Step 2:** The complex viscosity is defined as $\eta^* = \sigma^*/\dot{\varepsilon}^*$: $$\eta^* = \frac{(G' + i G'')\varepsilon_0 e^{i\omega t}}{i\omega\varepsilon_0 e^{i\omega t}} = \frac{G' + i G''}{i\omega}$$ **Step 3:** Multiply numerator and denominator by $-i$: $$\eta^* = \frac{-i(G' + i G'')}{\omega} = \frac{G'' - i G'}{\omega}$$ **Step 4:** Identify real and imaginary parts: $$\eta' = \frac{G''}{\omega} \quad\text{(dynamic viscosity, real part — dissipative)}$$ $$\eta'' = \frac{G'}{\omega} \quad\text{(imaginary part — elastic contribution)}$$ **Conclusion:** $\boxed{\,G'' = \omega\,\eta'\,}$ — this connects the loss modulus directly to the rate-dependent viscous behavior. --- ### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style) **Problem:** A polymer sample is subjected to DMA at frequency $f = 1$ Hz. The measured stress amplitude is $\sigma_0 = 1.5 \times 10^5$ Pa, strain amplitude is $\varepsilon_0 = 0.01$, and the phase angle is $\delta = 25°$. Calculate the loss modulus and the energy dissipated per cycle. **Solution:** **Step 1:** Compute the angular frequency: $$\omega = 2\pi f = 2\pi(1) = 6.283 \text{ rad/s}$$ **Step 2:** Compute the complex modulus magnitude: $$\vert G^* \vert = \frac{\sigma_0}{\varepsilon_0} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^5}{0.01} = 1.5 \times 10^7 \text{ Pa}$$ **Step 3:** Compute the loss modulus using $G'' = \vert G^* \vert \sin\delta$: $$G'' = 1.5 \times 10^7 \times \sin(25°) = 1.5 \times 10^7 \times 0.4226$$ $$\boxed{G'' = 6.34 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 6.34 \text{ MPa}}$$ **Step 4:** Compute the storage modulus for verification: $$G' = 1.5 \times 10^7 \times \cos(25°) = 1.5 \times 10^7 \times 0.9063 = 1.36 \times 10^7 \text{ Pa}$$ **Step 5:** Compute the loss tangent: $$\tan\delta = \frac{G''}{G'} = \tan(25°) = 0.466$$ **Step 6:** Compute energy dissipated per cycle: $$\Delta W = \pi\,G''\,{\varepsilon_0}^2 = \pi \times 6.34 \times 10^6 \times (0.01)^2$$ $$\Delta W = \pi \times 6.34 \times 10^6 \times 10^{-4} = 1991 \text{ J/m}^3$$ $$\boxed{\Delta W \approx 1.99 \text{ kJ/m}^3 \text{ per cycle}}$$ ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Viscoelastic Response Decomposition Flow The following block diagram maps how an applied oscillatory strain is decomposed into the elastic (storage) and viscous (loss) components that define the loss modulus concept. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Harmonic Strain Input
epsilon of t equals epsilon zero times sin of omega t] --> B[Viscoelastic Polymer Sample
Complex Modulus G Star] B --> C{In-Phase Response
G prime} B --> D{Out-of-Phase Response
G double prime} C --> E[Storage Modulus G prime
Elastic Component
Energy Stored and Recovered] D --> F[Loss Modulus G double prime
Viscous Component
Energy Dissipated as Heat] E --> G[Mechanical Analog: Spring
Hookean Behavior] F --> H[Mechanical Analog: Dashpot
Newtonian Behavior] F --> I[Energy Lost per Cycle
Delta W equals pi times G double prime times epsilon zero squared] F --> J[Glass Transition Detection
Peak in G double prime at T sub g] F --> K[Damping Factor
tan delta equals G double prime over G prime] H --> L[Dynamic Viscosity
eta prime equals G double prime over omega] ``` ### 4.2 Stress–Strain Phase Relationship Schematic ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Strain Curve
epsilon of t] -->|Leads by delta degrees| P2[Stress Curve
sigma of t] P2 --> P3[Decompose into:
G prime times sin of omega t
plus
G double prime times cos of omega t] P3 --> Q1[In-Phase Component
Storage G prime] P3 --> Q2[Out-of-Phase Component
Loss G double prime] Q1 --> R1[Energy Stored
Recovers Each Cycle] Q2 --> R2[Energy Lost as Heat
Irreversible] R2 --> S1[Delta W equals pi G double prime epsilon zero squared] ``` ### 4.3 Modulus vs Temperature Curve — Loss Modulus Features ```mermaid flowchart TD T[Temperature Axis T increasing] --> Z1[Region 1: Glassy
Low T - High G prime - Low G double prime] T --> Z2[Region 2: Glass Transition
Peak in G double prime
Peak in tan delta] T --> Z3[Region 3: Rubbery Plateau
Moderate G prime - Declining G double prime] T --> Z4[Region 4: Rubbery Flow
Falling G prime - Local G double prime Peak] T --> Z5[Region 5: Viscous Flow
Very Low G prime - Steady G double prime] Z2 --> X1[Used to Identify T sub g] Z3 --> X2[Used to Assess Entanglement
Network Density] ``` ### 4.4 Mechanical Model Analogues ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[Maxwell Model
Spring and Dashpot in Series] --> A2[Predicts Stress Relaxation
and Viscous Flow] A3[Kelvin Voigt Model
Spring and Dashpot in Parallel] --> A4[Predicts Creep Behavior
and Elastic Recovery] A5[Standard Linear Solid
Maxwell plus Parallel Spring] --> A6[Predicts Full Viscoelastic Response
Including G prime and G double prime] A1 --> B1[For Pure Viscous Loss
Use Dashpot Element] A3 --> B2[For Elastic Recovery
Use Spring Element] A5 --> B3[Combined Model
G star equals G prime plus i G double prime] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]** **Define loss modulus. How is it related to the complex modulus and phase angle?** **Model Answer:** The **loss modulus ($G''$)** is the imaginary component of the complex shear modulus $G^*$ of a viscoelastic polymer, representing the energy dissipated as heat per cycle of deformation. It quantifies the viscous (liquid-like) response of the material. It is related to the complex modulus and phase angle $\delta$ as: $$G'' = G^* \sin\delta \quad\text{and}\quad \tan\delta = \frac{G''}{G'}$$ where $G'$ is the storage modulus and $\delta$ is the phase lag between applied stress and resulting strain. The SI unit is Pascal (Pa). --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Series Question Bank]** **Distinguish between storage modulus and loss modulus.** **Model Answer:** | Property | Storage Modulus $G'$ | Loss Modulus $G''$ | |----------|---------------------|---------------------| | **Component** | Real part of $G^*$ | Imaginary part of $G^*$ | | **Physical meaning** | Elastic (solid-like) response | Viscous (liquid-like) response | | **Phase relation** | In-phase with strain | $90°$ out-of-phase with strain | | **Energy behavior** | Energy stored and recovered | Energy dissipated as heat | | **Formula** | $G' = G^* \cos\delta$ | $G'' = G^* \sin\delta$ | | **Analogy** | Spring | Dashpot | | **Peak at $T_g$?** | Sharp drop at $T_g$ | Distinct peak at $T_g$ | --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]** **(a)** Derive the expression for complex shear modulus starting from harmonic strain input. Clearly show the decomposition into storage and loss modulus components. **(b)** A polymer specimen is tested in DMA at 10 Hz. The storage modulus is measured as $G' = 2.5 \times 10^8$ Pa and the loss modulus as $G'' = 8.5 \times 10^7$ Pa. Calculate (i) the loss tangent, (ii) the absolute complex modulus, (iii) the phase angle, and (iv) the energy dissipated per cubic metre per cycle if the strain amplitude is $\varepsilon_0 = 0.005$. --- **Model Solution:** **Part (a): Derivation (7 marks)** Given: Harmonic strain input $$\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_0 \sin(\omega t)$$ For a viscoelastic material, stress lags strain by phase angle $\delta$: $$\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \sin(\omega t + \delta)$$ Using the identity $\sin(A+B) = \sin A \cos B + \cos A \sin B$: $$\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \cos\delta \cdot \sin(\omega t) + \sigma_0 \sin\delta \cdot \cos(\omega t)$$ Dividing by strain amplitude $\varepsilon_0$: $$\frac{\sigma(t)}{\varepsilon_0} = \frac{\sigma_0\cos\delta}{\varepsilon_0}\sin(\omega t) + \frac{\sigma_0\sin\delta}{\varepsilon_0}\cos(\omega t)$$ Define: $$G' \equiv \frac{\sigma_0}{\varepsilon_0}\cos\delta \quad\text{(storage modulus)}$$ $$G'' \equiv \frac{\sigma_0}{\varepsilon_0}\sin\delta \quad\text{(loss modulus)}$$ Therefore: $$\sigma(t) = G'\,\varepsilon_0 \sin(\omega t) + G''\,\varepsilon_0 \cos(\omega t)$$ Expressing strain as the imaginary part of $\varepsilon_0 e^{i\omega t}$ and combining using Euler's relation $e^{i\theta} = \cos\theta + i\sin\theta$: $$\sigma^*(t) = (G' + i\,G'')\,\varepsilon_0 e^{i\omega t} = G^* \varepsilon_0 e^{i\omega t}$$ where $G^* = G' + i\,G''$ is the **complex shear modulus**, with $G'$ being the in-phase (energy storing) and $G''$ being the out-of-phase (energy dissipating) component. **Part (b): Numerical Problem (7 marks)** **Given:** $G' = 2.5 \times 10^8$ Pa, $G'' = 8.5 \times 10^7$ Pa, $f = 10$ Hz, $\varepsilon_0 = 0.005$ **(i) Loss tangent:** $$\tan\delta = \frac{G''}{G'} = \frac{8.5 \times 10^7}{2.5 \times 10^8} = 0.34$$ **(ii) Absolute complex modulus:** $$\vert G^* \vert = \sqrt{{G'}^2 + {G''}^2} = \sqrt{(2.5 \times 10^8)^2 + (8.5 \times 10^7)^2}$$ $$= \sqrt{6.25 \times 10^{16} + 7.225 \times 10^{15}} = \sqrt{6.9725 \times 10^{16}}$$ $$\boxed{\vert G^* \vert = 2.64 \times 10^8 \text{ Pa} = 264 \text{ MPa}}$$ **(iii) Phase angle:** $$\delta = \tan^{-1}(0.34) = \boxed{18.78°}$$ **(iv) Energy dissipated per cycle:** $$\Delta W = \pi\,G''\,{\varepsilon_0}^2 = \pi \times 8.5 \times 10^7 \times (0.005)^2$$ $$= \pi \times 8.5 \times 10^7 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-5}$$ $$\boxed{\Delta W \approx 6676 \text{ J/m}^3 \text{ per cycle} = 6.68 \text{ kJ/m}^3}$$ --- #### **Question B: [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Alternative Choice]** **(a)** Derive an expression for the energy dissipated per cycle in a viscoelastic polymer in terms of the loss modulus. Explain how this justifies calling $G''$ the "loss" modulus. **(b)** With the help of a labelled diagram, explain the variation of loss modulus with temperature for an amorphous polymer, identifying key regions (glassy, glass transition, rubbery, terminal flow). Why is the peak in $G''$ at $T_g$ diagnostic? --- **Model Solution:** **Part (a): Derivation (7 marks)** Consider a viscoelastic material subjected to oscillatory strain: $$\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_0 \sin(\omega t)$$ The strain rate is: $$\dot{\varepsilon}(t) = \varepsilon_0 \omega \cos(\omega t)$$ The stress response from the complex modulus decomposition is: $$\sigma(t) = G'\,\varepsilon_0 \sin(\omega t) + G''\,\varepsilon_0 \cos(\omega t)$$ Power dissipated per unit volume: $$p(t) = \sigma(t)\,\dot{\varepsilon}(t) = G'\,{\varepsilon_0}^2\omega\sin(\omega t)\cos(\omega t) + G''\,{\varepsilon_0}^2\omega\cos^2(\omega t)$$ Energy dissipated per cycle (integrate over period $T = 2\pi/\omega$): $$\Delta W = \int_0^{T} p(t)\,dt$$ The first integral vanishes (storage term cancels over full cycle): $$\int_0^{T} \sin(\omega t)\cos(\omega t)\,dt = 0$$ The second integral yields: $$\int_0^{T} \cos^2(\omega t)\,dt = \frac{T}{2} = \frac{\pi}{\omega}$$ Therefore: $$\boxed{\Delta W_{\text{cycle}} = \pi\,G''\,{\varepsilon_0}^2}$$ Since $\Delta W \propto G''$ directly, $G''$ is rightfully termed the **loss modulus** — it governs the irreversible, heat-generating energy loss per cycle. A larger $G''$ means more energy dissipated (more damping, more heat generation, more internal friction between polymer chains). **Part (b): Loss Modulus vs Temperature for Amorphous Polymer (7 marks)** The loss modulus $G''$ varies dramatically with temperature, revealing five characteristic regions: **Region 1 — Glassy State (Low T, well below $T_g$):** - Molecular motions are frozen (only vibrational modes active). - $G''$ is small ($\sim 10^6$–$10^7$ Pa). - Small sub-$T_g$ peaks may appear due to **secondary transitions** (local rotations of side groups like $-\text{CH}_3$, phenyl flips). **Region 2 — Glass Transition Region (around $T_g$):** - Large-scale cooperative segmental motions "unfreeze" (typically 10–50 backbone atoms move together). - $G''$ shows a **distinct, sharp peak** ($\sim 10^7$–$10^8$ Pa). - This peak location is the operational definition of $T_g$ via DMA. - Diagnostic because $T(\text{peak } G'')$ corresponds to the temperature at which segmental relaxation time $\tau \approx 1/\omega$ — i.e., the polymer's molecular motion matches the test frequency. **Region 3 — Rubbery Plateau (above $T_g$, before flow):** - For entangled or cross-linked polymers, $G''$ drops to an intermediate value and remains relatively constant. - Plateau reflects the elastic network of entanglements/crosslinks. **Region 4 — Rubbery Flow Region:** - Entanglements begin to disentangle; $G''$ shows a smaller local peak before terminal flow. **Region 5 — Terminal Flow Region (high T):** - Polymer flows like a viscous liquid. - $G'' \approx \omega \eta_0$ (linear with frequency), $G' \to 0$. **Why is the $G''$ peak at $T_g$ diagnostic?** At the $G''$ peak, the relaxation time $\tau$ equals the inverse of the test angular frequency ($\tau \approx 1/\omega$). This is the condition for **maximum energy dissipation per cycle** — polymer segments are just mobile enough to lag behind the applied oscillation, absorbing the maximum energy as internal friction (heat). Below $T_g$, segments are frozen (no motion, no dissipation). Above $T_g$, segments move freely (no resistance, no dissipation). The peak represents the resonance between molecular mobility and measurement timescale, making it the gold-standard signature of the glass transition. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > - **Do not confuse** $G'$ (storage) with $G''$ (loss) in numerical problems — examiners frequently test this. > - **Always state the units** ($G''$ is in Pa/MPa/GPa — same dimension as any modulus). > - **Mention the phase angle explicitly** in definition questions ($G''$ is the **out-of-phase** component). > - In derivation of $\Delta W = \pi G'' \varepsilon_0^2$, students often forget to show **why the storage term integral vanishes** — losing 2 marks. > - When computing $\tan\delta$, present it as the **ratio $G''/G'$, not the ratio of loss to complex modulus**. > - Always identify the **glass transition** as the most important application of loss modulus measurement. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Loss Modulus** **Core Definition:** - ☐ Loss modulus $G''$ = imaginary part of complex modulus $G^*$ - ☐ Represents the **viscous** (energy-dissipating) response of a polymer - ☐ Symbol: $G''$ for shear, $E''$ for tensile mode - ☐ Units: Pa (or MPa, GPa) **Essential Formulas (Memorize):** - ☐ $G^* = G' + i\,G''$ - ☐ $G'' = G^* \sin\delta$ and $G' = G^* \cos\delta$ - ☐ $\tan\delta = G''/G'$ (loss tangent / damping factor) - ☐ $\vert G^* \vert = \sqrt{G'^2 + G''^2}$ - ☐ $\Delta W_{\text{cycle}} = \pi G'' \varepsilon_0^2$ (energy dissipated per cycle) - ☐ $G'' = \omega \eta'$ (link to dynamic viscosity) **Physical Interpretation:** - ☐ $G'' = 0$ → perfectly elastic (energy fully recovered) - ☐ $G''$ maximum → purely viscous (energy fully dissipated) - ☐ Polymer = $0 < G'' < G^*$, **viscoelastic** **Key Signatures in DMA:** - ☐ **Sharp peak in $G''$** at glass transition $T_g$ - ☐ **Smaller peaks** at secondary transitions (sub-$T_g$ motions) - ☐ Frequency-dependent (TTS principle) **Phase Angle Limits:** - ☐ $\delta = 0°$ → Hookean elastic solid - ☐ $\delta = 90°$ → Newtonian viscous liquid - ☐ Polymer: $0° < \delta < 90°$ (viscoelastic) **Engineering Applications:** - ☐ Vibration dampers, acoustic insulation, tires, shoe soles - ☐ $T_g$ determination (most accurate DMA method) - ☐ Quality control in polymer processing - ☐ Predicting long-term fatigue and creep **Exam Strategy:** - ☐ Always draw a stress–strain phase diagram for full marks - ☐ Show all derivation steps — partial credit for incomplete derivations - ☐ Include units in numerical answers - ☐ Mention that $G''$ peak identifies $T_g$ — high-yield application question - ☐ Know the difference: $G'$ stores energy, $G''$ loses it as heat

topics.title: tan δ
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT402 - POLYMER PHYSICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** tan δ

# tan δ — The Loss Tangent in Polymer Viscoelasticity

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

In the viscoelastic characterization of polymers, the symbol **tan δ** (read as "tan delta" or "loss tangent") denotes the **ratio of the loss modulus to the storage modulus** of a polymeric material subjected to a small-amplitude oscillatory deformation. Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$\tan \delta = \frac{E''}{E'} = \frac{G''}{G'}$$

where **E'** is the tensile storage modulus, **E''** is the tensile loss modulus, **G'** is the shear storage modulus, and **G''** is the shear loss modulus. The angle **δ** is the **phase lag** between the applied sinusoidal stress and the resulting sinusoidal strain. A perfectly elastic (Hookean) solid exhibits **δ = 0° (tan δ = 0)**, while a perfectly viscous (Newtonian) liquid exhibits **δ = 90° (tan δ → ∞)**. Polymeric solids typically fall in the intermediate range **0 < δ < 90°**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The loss tangent tan δ is the single most reported parameter extracted from Dynamic Mechanical Analysis (DMA). It is used as the primary diagnostic for locating the **glass transition temperature (Tg)**, identifying secondary relaxations (β, γ transitions), and rating materials for damping, vibration, and acoustic applications.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Bouncy vs Sticky" Rubber Ball

Imagine pushing a playground ball repeatedly against a wall while your friend measures how fast and how hard it pushes back:

- If the ball is a **super-ball** (purely elastic), it pushes back at the exact instant you compress it. There is **no delay** — all the energy you put in comes back to you. This corresponds to **tan δ → 0** (no energy lost, δ = 0°).

- If the ball is **wet clay** (purely viscous), it simply oozes; the pushing force and the deformation happen at completely different times — they are 90° out of phase. Almost no energy is returned. This corresponds to **tan δ → ∞** (δ = 90°).

- A **polymer above Tg but not yet molten** (a leathery rubber) behaves like a partially-bouncy, partially-sticky ball: it pushes back mostly in time, but a fraction of the energy you input is converted into **heat** through internal molecular friction (chain segment rearrangements). The **mismatch in timing** is **δ**, and the **fraction of energy lost per cycle is approximately tan δ** (for small δ, tan δ ≈ sin δ ≈ δ in radians).

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants / Standard Metrics Highlighted:**
> - **tan δ is dimensionless** (a ratio of two moduli).
> - At the glass transition (Tg), tan δ typically reaches a **maximum value between 0.1 and 3.0** for most engineering polymers.
> - The phase angle δ is reported in **degrees** or **radians**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Origin of the Phase Lag in Polymeric Materials

When a sinusoidal oscillatory stress **σ(t) = σ₀ sin(ωt)** is applied to a viscoelastic polymer, the resulting strain response is also sinusoidal but **lags** behind the stress by an angle δ:

$$\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_0 \sin(\omega t - \delta)$$

Using the complex modulus formalism, this is elegantly captured by writing:

$$E^*(\omega) = E'(\omega) + i E''(\omega)$$

where:
- **E'(ω) = (σ₀/ε₀) cos δ** → **Storage Modulus** (energy stored elastically per cycle, in phase with strain)
- **E''(ω) = (σ₀/ε₀) sin δ** → **Loss Modulus** (energy dissipated as heat per cycle, in phase with strain rate)

The angle δ between E* and E' is the **loss angle**, and the tangent of this angle quantifies the **internal friction** of the polymer.

### 2.2 Why tan δ Matters — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Define** the viscoelastic response as a complex number E*.
2. **Decompose** E* into real (E') and imaginary (E'') components.
3. **Compute** the ratio E''/E' = tan δ.
4. **Interpret** physically: a small tan δ means the polymer is **elastic-dominant** (spring-like); a large tan δ means the polymer is **viscous-dominant** (dashpot-like, energy-absorbing).
5. **Apply** to engineering: at the Tg peak of tan δ vs temperature, the polymer is in its **maximum energy-dissipating state** — useful for damping, undesirable for load-bearing structural parts.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Physical Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|--------------------------------|-------|
| 1 | $\tan \delta = \dfrac{E''}{E'} = \dfrac{G''}{G'}$ | Fundamental definition of loss tangent | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $E^* = E' + i\,E''$ | Complex tensile modulus | Pa |
| 3 | $G^* = G' + i\,G''$ | Complex shear modulus | Pa |
| 4 | $E' = \vert E^* \vert \cos\delta$ | Storage modulus (in-phase component) | Pa |
| 5 | $E'' = \vert E^* \vert \sin\delta$ | Loss modulus (out-of-phase component) | Pa |
| 6 | $\tan \delta = \dfrac{\sin\delta}{\cos\delta}$ | Trigonometric form | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $\text{Dissipated Energy per cycle} = \pi\,E''\,\varepsilon_0^2$ | Hysteresis loss per unit volume | J/m³ |
| 8 | $\text{Stored Energy per cycle} = \dfrac{1}{2}\,E'\,\varepsilon_0^2$ | Elastic energy storage per unit volume | J/m³ |
| 9 | $\dfrac{\Delta W}{W} \approx 2\pi \tan \delta$ | Fractional energy loss per cycle (small δ) | Dimensionless |
| 10 | $T_g \approx T_{\text{peak of }\tan\delta}$ | Glass transition from DMA tan δ peak | K or °C |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields:**
> - **Automotive & Aerospace:** Selection of elastomers for engine mounts, bushings, and vibration isolators. Low tan δ → tires (rolling resistance); high tan δ → dash panels (NVH damping).
> - **Electronics:** PCB substrates (low tan δ at GHz frequencies for low dielectric loss); encapsulation resins.
> - **Sports Goods:** Shoe midsoles (EVA, PU) tuned for high tan δ to absorb impact energy.
> - **Biomedical:** Hydrogels and tissue-mimicking materials where damping behavior is critical.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of tan δ from the Complex Modulus

**Starting Point — Applied Sinusoidal Stress:**

A viscoelastic polymer sample is subjected to an oscillatory tensile stress in a DMA tester. The stress varies as:

$$\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \sin(\omega t)$$

Here, **σ₀** is the stress amplitude and **ω** is the angular frequency (rad/s).

---

**Step 1 — Express the strain with a phase lag:**

Because the polymer is viscoelastic, the strain response lags the stress by angle δ:

$$\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_0 \sin(\omega t - \delta)$$

*Conversion logic:* A purely elastic solid would have δ = 0; a purely viscous fluid would have δ = 90°. Polymers are intermediate, so 0 < δ < 90°.

---

**Step 2 — Expand the sine using the identity:**

Using $\sin(A - B) = \sin A \cos B - \cos A \sin B$:

$$\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_0 [\sin(\omega t)\cos\delta - \cos(\omega t)\sin\delta]$$

*Conversion logic:* This splits the strain into two orthogonal components — one in-phase with stress (sin ωt term) and one 90° out-of-phase (cos ωt term).

---

**Step 3 — Define the complex strain:**

Using Euler's relation, $e^{i\omega t} = \cos(\omega t) + i\sin(\omega t)$, we can write the complex strain as:

$$\varepsilon^*(t) = \varepsilon_0\, e^{i(\omega t - \delta)} = \varepsilon_0\, e^{-i\delta}\, e^{i\omega t}$$

---

**Step 4 — Define the complex stress analogously:**

$$\sigma^*(t) = \sigma_0\, e^{i\omega t}$$

---

**Step 5 — Compute the complex modulus:**

The complex modulus is the ratio of complex stress to complex strain:

$$E^*(\omega) = \frac{\sigma^*(t)}{\varepsilon^*(t)} = \frac{\sigma_0}{\varepsilon_0}\, e^{+i\delta}$$

*Conversion logic:* The complex exponential $e^{i\delta}$ encodes both the magnitude ratio and the phase information.

---

**Step 6 — Expand using Euler's formula:**

$$E^*(\omega) = \frac{\sigma_0}{\varepsilon_0}\, [\cos\delta + i\sin\delta]$$

---

**Step 7 — Separate real and imaginary parts:**

$$E^*(\omega) = \underbrace{\frac{\sigma_0}{\varepsilon_0}\cos\delta}_{\text{Real part } = E'} + i\underbrace{\frac{\sigma_0}{\varepsilon_0}\sin\delta}_{\text{Imaginary part } = E''}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{E' = \frac{\sigma_0}{\varepsilon_0}\cos\delta \quad ; \quad E'' = \frac{\sigma_0}{\varepsilon_0}\sin\delta}$$

---

**Step 8 — Form the ratio E''/E':**

$$\tan \delta = \frac{E''}{E'} = \frac{\dfrac{\sigma_0}{\varepsilon_0}\sin\delta}{\dfrac{\sigma_0}{\varepsilon_0}\cos\delta} = \frac{\sin\delta}{\cos\delta}$$

$$\boxed{\tan \delta = \frac{E''}{E'}}$$

This is the defining relation of the loss tangent.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Fractional Energy Dissipated per Cycle

**Step 1 — Energy density in one loading half-cycle:**

The instantaneous power per unit volume dissipated as heat is:

$$\dot{w}(t) = \sigma(t)\,\dot{\varepsilon}(t)$$
