With $\dot{\varepsilon}(t) = \varepsilon_0\,\omega\,\cos(\omega t - \delta)$:

$$\dot{w}(t) = \sigma_0 \sin(\omega t) \cdot \varepsilon_0\,\omega\,\cos(\omega t - \delta)$$

---

**Step 2 — Integrate over one full cycle (T = 2π/ω):**

$$\Delta W = \int_0^{T} \sigma(t)\,\dot{\varepsilon}(t)\,dt$$

Using trigonometric product-to-sum identities and integrating over a complete period, only the cross-product survives, yielding:

$$\Delta W = \pi\,\sigma_0\,\varepsilon_0\,\sin\delta$$

Substituting $\sigma_0 = E'\varepsilon_0/\cos\delta$ from Step 7 of the prior derivation:

$$\Delta W = \pi\,E''\,\varepsilon_0^2$$

*Conversion logic:* This proves that the **loss modulus E''** controls the **energy dissipated per cycle**, and confirms that **tan δ is the figure of merit for damping**.

---

**Step 3 — Maximum stored elastic energy:**

The peak elastic energy density stored in the cycle is:

$$W_{max} = \frac{1}{2}\,E'\,\varepsilon_0^2$$

---

**Step 4 — Fractional energy loss (specific damping capacity):**

$$\Psi = \frac{\Delta W}{W_{max}} = \frac{\pi\,E''\,\varepsilon_0^2}{\frac{1}{2}E'\,\varepsilon_0^2} = 2\pi\,\frac{E''}{E'} = 2\pi\tan\delta$$

$$\boxed{\Psi \approx 2\pi\tan\delta \quad \text{(for small to moderate }\delta)}$$

*Conversion logic:* A tan δ of 0.01 corresponds to ~6.3% energy dissipation per cycle; a tan δ of 0.5 corresponds to ~314% (over one full radian's worth, but the small-angle approximation breaks down).

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — How tan δ Varies Across Polymer States

| Physical State of Polymer | Approximate E' | Approximate tan δ | Molecular Interpretation |
|---------------------------|----------------|-------------------|---------------------------|
| Glassy (T << Tg) | $10^9 - 10^{10}$ Pa | 0.001 – 0.01 | Only local bond vibrations; chain segments frozen |
| Leathery / Glass-Rubber Transition (T ≈ Tg) | $10^6 - 10^8$ Pa (rapidly dropping) | 0.1 – 3.0 (PEAK) | Onset of long-range cooperative segmental motion |
| Rubbery (T > Tg, below Tm for amorphous) | $10^5 - 10^6$ Pa | 0.1 – 1.0 | Large-scale chain mobility; entropic elasticity active |
| Viscous Flow (T >> Tg for amorphous) | < $10^5$ Pa | > 1.0 | Whole-chain sliding past neighbors |
| Cross-linked Elastomer (rubber at room T) | $10^6 - 10^7$ Pa | 0.05 – 0.3 | Permanent network; only moderate internal friction |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Stress-Strain Phase Relationship and tan δ

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["Applied Sinusoidal Stress"]
        A1["σ(t) = σ₀ sin(ωt)"]
    end

    subgraph PHASE["Phase Relationship in Viscoelastic Polymer"]
        direction TB
        B1["Purely Elastic Hookean Spring"]
        B2["δ = 0°"]
        B3["tan δ = 0"]
        B4["All energy stored, none lost"]

        B5["Viscoelastic Polymer"]
        B6["0 < δ < 90°"]
        B7["tan δ = E''/E' > 0"]
        B8["Partial energy dissipation as heat"]

        B9["Purely Viscous Newtonian Dashpot"]
        B10["δ = 90°"]
        B11["tan δ → ∞"]
        B12["All energy dissipated, none stored"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["Measurement & Interpretation"]
        C1["E' (Storage Modulus) = Real part"]
        C2["E'' (Loss Modulus) = Imaginary part"]
        C3["tan δ = E'' / E' = sin δ / cos δ"]
        C4["Peak of tan δ vs T → Tg of polymer"]
        C5["Damping, vibration, acoustic applications"]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A1 --> B5
    A1 --> B9
    B1 --> B2 --> B3 --> B4
    B5 --> B6 --> B7 --> B8
    B9 --> B10 --> B11 --> B12
    B5 --> C1
    B5 --> C2
    C1 --> C3
    C2 --> C3
    C3 --> C4
    C3 --> C5
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Determination of tan δ from a DMA Experiment

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Step 1: Mount polymer sample in DMA clamp (tension / bending / shear)"] --> S2["Step 2: Apply small-amplitude oscillatory strain ε(t) = ε₀ sin(ωt)"]
    S2 --> S3["Step 3: Measure resulting stress σ(t) with phase-sensitive detector"]
    S3 --> S4["Step 4: Extract stress amplitude σ₀ and phase lag δ"]
    S4 --> S5["Step 5: Compute E' = σ₀/ε₀ × cos δ"]
    S4 --> S6["Step 6: Compute E'' = σ₀/ε₀ × sin δ"]
    S5 --> S7["Step 7: Compute tan δ = E'' / E'"]
    S6 --> S7
    S7 --> S8["Step 8: Sweep temperature → plot tan δ vs T → locate peak → identify Tg"]
    S7 --> S9["Step 9: For damping materials → select polymer with target tan δ at operating frequency"]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Energy Partitioning in a Viscoelastic Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IN["Energy Input per Cycle"]
        W["W_in = (1/2) σ₀ ε₀"]
    end

    subgraph STORED["Stored Elastic Energy"]
        Wst["W_stored = (1/2) E' ε₀²"]
    end

    subgraph LOST["Dissipated Energy as Heat"]
        Wd["W_dissipated = π E'' ε₀²"]
    end

    W --> Wst
    W --> Wd
    Wst --> R1["Returned to next cycle (spring-like)"]
    Wd --> R2["Converted to internal friction heat (dashpot-like)"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** The three blocks above jointly illustrate that tan δ is fundamentally a **partition coefficient** for input mechanical energy — splitting it into a recoverable elastic part (controlled by E') and an irrecoverable heat part (controlled by E'').

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question, Module 2]**

**Q1. Define the loss tangent (tan δ) of a viscoelastic polymer. What is its physical significance?**

**Model Answer:**
The loss tangent tan δ is defined as the ratio of the loss modulus (E'') to the storage modulus (E') of a viscoelastic polymer, where δ is the phase lag between applied oscillatory stress and resulting strain: **tan δ = E''/E' = sin δ / cos δ**. Physically, it represents the ratio of energy dissipated as heat to energy stored elastically per deformation cycle. A small tan δ indicates an elastic-dominant (spring-like) response, while a large tan δ indicates a viscous-dominant (dashpot-like) response. The peak in a tan δ vs. temperature plot corresponds to the glass transition temperature Tg of the polymer. **(3 Marks)**

---

**Q2. Why does the tan δ vs. temperature curve of an amorphous polymer exhibit a peak near Tg?**

**Model Answer:**
Near the glass transition temperature Tg, the onset of long-range cooperative segmental motion in the polymer chains produces maximum internal molecular friction. This maximizes the ratio of energy dissipated (E'') to energy stored (E') per cycle, resulting in a sharp peak in tan δ. Below Tg, only localized bond vibrations occur (low E'', low tan δ). Above Tg, chain segments move freely; E' drops sharply and although E'' may remain moderate, the ratio E''/E' decreases, leading to a fall in tan δ. Hence, the peak identifies Tg. **(3 Marks)**

---

### Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]

**(a)** Derive the expression for tan δ in terms of the storage and loss moduli from the complex modulus formalism, clearly stating the meaning of each term. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A DMA experiment on an amorphous polymer at 1 Hz gives the following data at four temperatures. Compute tan δ at each temperature and identify the glass transition temperature (Tg).

| Temperature (°C) | E' (MPa) | E'' (MPa) |
|------------------|----------|-----------|
| 80  | 3200 | 35 |
| 100 | 2900 | 110 |
| 120 | 850  | 240 |
| 140 | 12   | 8 |

**(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

1. Begin with applied oscillatory stress $\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \sin(\omega t)$.
2. Strain response in a viscoelastic solid lags by δ: $\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_0 \sin(\omega t - \delta)$.
3. Express using complex exponentials: $\sigma^* = \sigma_0 e^{i\omega t}$, $\varepsilon^* = \varepsilon_0 e^{i(\omega t - \delta)}$.
4. Complex modulus: $E^* = \sigma^*/\varepsilon^* = (\sigma_0/\varepsilon_0) e^{i\delta} = (\sigma_0/\varepsilon_0)[\cos\delta + i\sin\delta]$.
5. Real part: $E' = (\sigma_0/\varepsilon_0)\cos\delta$ — storage modulus, in-phase energy storage.
6. Imaginary part: $E'' = (\sigma_0/\varepsilon_0)\sin\delta$ — loss modulus, out-of-phase dissipation.
7. Therefore $\tan\delta = E''/E'$ — dimensionless ratio of dissipated-to-stored energy per cycle. **(7 Marks — full step-by-step derivation as shown in Section 3.1 above)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

Compute tan δ = E''/E' at each temperature:

**At 80°C:**
$$\tan\delta = \frac{35}{3200} = 0.0109$$

**At 100°C:**
$$\tan\delta = \frac{110}{2900} = 0.0379$$

**At 120°C:**
$$\tan\delta = \frac{240}{850} = 0.2824$$

**At 140°C:**
$$\tan\delta = \frac{8}{12} = 0.6667$$

**Identification of Tg:**
A clear, sharp jump in tan δ occurs between 100°C and 140°C, with the largest value at 140°C indicating the polymer has crossed Tg into the rubbery/leathery region. Given the steep rise from a near-zero value at 100°C (still glassy) to 0.28 at 120°C (rubbery transition onset), **Tg ≈ 120°C** is the closest data point to the inflection. (In a continuous DMA scan, Tg is taken at the **peak of tan δ**, which would lie between 120°C and 140°C; here, **Tg ≈ 125 ± 5°C**.)

**Conclusion:** The polymer has **Tg ≈ 120–125°C**. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks** [Alternative Choice]

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch (described) how a Dynamic Mechanical Analyzer (DMA) measures tan δ of a polymer. What information can be obtained from a tan δ vs. temperature plot? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare the tan δ behavior of: (i) a cross-linked rubber at room temperature, (ii) an amorphous glassy polymer at Tg, (iii) a molten amorphous polymer well above Tg. Justify your answers using molecular motion arguments. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**DMA Measurement of tan δ:**

A DMA instrument clamps a polymer specimen (in tension, bending, shear, or compression mode). A linear actuator imposes a small sinusoidal strain $\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_0 \sin(\omega t)$, typically with strain amplitude ε₀ ≈ 0.1–1% to stay in the linear viscoelastic regime. A force transducer measures the resulting stress. Because the sample is viscoelastic, the stress response is:

$$\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \sin(\omega t + \delta)$$

where δ is the **phase lag** (a positive value meaning stress lags strain). A phase-sensitive detector (lock-in amplifier) decomposes σ(t) into components in-phase and out-of-phase with ε(t):

- **In-phase component** (multiplied by sin ωt) → gives **E'**.
- **Out-of-phase component** (multiplied by cos ωt) → gives **E''**.
- The instrument's onboard processor then computes **tan δ = E''/E'**.

**Information from tan δ vs. T plot:**

1. **Peak temperature = Tg** of the polymer.
2. **Height of peak** indicates amorphous phase content — higher peak → more amorphous; semi-crystalline polymers show low, broad tan δ peaks.
3. **Secondary peaks** at lower temperatures correspond to β (side-group rotations) and γ (local bond vibrations) transitions.
4. **Magnitude of tan δ** indicates damping performance — useful for selecting vibration-damping materials.
5. **Breadth of peak** relates to the heterogeneity of segmental environments. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**(i) Cross-linked rubber at room temperature:**
A cross-linked elastomer (e.g., natural rubber, polybutadiene) has long, flexible chains tied together by permanent covalent cross-links. At room temperature it is well above its Tg. Only **long-range chain uncoiling** is possible — segments are mobile, but flow is prevented by the network. The internal friction is moderate because chains are lubricated but cannot slip past each other. **Typical tan δ ≈ 0.05–0.30.** Polymer behaves predominantly elastically (entropic spring).

**(ii) Amorphous glassy polymer at Tg:**
At Tg, the **onset of cooperative segmental motion** is in full swing. Chain segments are just becoming free to rearrange, but the rearrangement is sluggish and dissipative. Energy input is heavily converted to heat through internal friction. **tan δ is at its maximum, typically 0.5–3.0.** This is the classic tan δ peak.

**(iii) Molten amorphous polymer well above Tg:**
Whole chains can slide past each other; the material flows like a viscous liquid. The elastic (entropic) restoring force is minimal because chains are completely relaxed. The response is dominated by viscous flow, so **tan δ is very large, often > 1.0 (and E' becomes very small).** Polymer behaves like a viscous fluid. **(7 Marks)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the phase lag interpretation:** Many students write tan δ = E''/E' without explaining what δ represents. Always state that **δ is the phase angle between stress and strain** — failing to do so costs at least 1 mark.
> 2. **Units confusion:** tan δ is **dimensionless**, not in Pa or rad. Do not report it with units.
> 3. **Confusing the location of Tg:** Tg from DMA is taken at the **peak of tan δ**, not at the inflection of E'. (The inflection of E' gives the **onset of Tg**, while the **E'' peak** gives a value intermediate between the two.)
> 4. **Neglecting frequency dependence:** tan δ is strongly frequency-dependent. Always specify the **test frequency** (e.g., 1 Hz) when reporting numerical values.
> 5. **Mixing tensile and shear notations:** Use E' and E'' for tensile, G' and G'' for shear. Do not interchange unless the problem explicitly converts (G = E/2(1+ν)).

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **tan δ = E''/E' = G''/G'** — ratio of loss modulus to storage modulus; **dimensionless**.
- ✅ **δ = phase lag** between applied oscillatory stress and resulting strain.
- ✅ For a **purely elastic** solid → **δ = 0°, tan δ = 0** (no energy lost).
- ✅ For a **purely viscous** fluid → **δ = 90°, tan δ → ∞** (all energy dissipated).
- ✅ **Glass transition Tg** is identified as the **peak of tan δ vs. T** plot from DMA.
- ✅ **Storage modulus E'** measures **elastic energy stored** per cycle (in-phase).
- ✅ **Loss modulus E''** measures **viscous energy dissipated** as heat per cycle (out-of-phase).
- ✅ **Energy dissipated per cycle:** $\Delta W = \pi E'' \varepsilon_0^2$.
- ✅ **Specific damping capacity:** $\Psi = \Delta W / W_{max} \approx 2\pi \tan\delta$.
- ✅ **Order of magnitude of tan δ** by state: glassy ~0.001–0.01, at Tg peak ~0.5–3.0, rubbery ~0.1–1.0, molten > 1.0.
- ✅ **Higher tan δ** = better **damping / vibration absorption** (good for mounts, shoe soles, acoustic panels).
- ✅ **Lower tan δ** = better **elastic recovery / low rolling resistance / low dielectric loss** (good for tires, PCB substrates, springs).
- ✅ tan δ is **frequency-dependent**: peak Tg shifts to higher T with higher frequency (WLF / Arrhenius behaviour).
- ✅ Always report **test frequency** (typically 1 Hz) along with any numerical tan δ value.
- ✅ DMA = Dynamic Mechanical Analyzer — primary instrument for measuring tan δ experimentally.
- ✅ **Secondary relaxations** (β, γ) appear as smaller tan δ peaks at temperatures below Tg.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Damping
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT402 - POLYMER PHYSICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Damping

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] 
> **Damping** in polymer physics is defined as the inherent ability of a polymeric material to **dissipate mechanical energy (vibrational or oscillatory energy) as heat** when subjected to cyclic deformation (dynamic stress or strain). It is quantitatively measured by the **loss modulus ($E''$)** and the **loss tangent ($\tan \delta$)** of the material.

In the framework of **viscoelasticity**, polymers exhibit time-dependent mechanical response that lies between an ideal elastic solid (Hookean) and an ideal viscous liquid (Newtonian). When a sinusoidal stress is applied, the resulting strain lags behind the stress by a phase angle $\delta$. The portion of energy dissipated per cycle is directly proportional to $\sin \delta$, while the stored energy is proportional to $\cos \delta$. This phase lag is the microscopic origin of damping.

The **complex modulus** $E^*$ characterizes this behavior:

$$E^{*} = E' + iE''$$

where:
- $E'$ = **Storage Modulus** (elastic, energy-storing component) in **Pa** or **MPa**
- $E''$ = **Loss Modulus** (viscous, energy-dissipating component) in **Pa** or **MPa**
- $i = \sqrt{-1}$ (imaginary unit)

The ratio is the **loss tangent**, also called the **damping factor**:

$$\tan \delta = \frac{E''}{E'}$$

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — A Bouncing Rubber Ball on Different Surfaces**
>
> Imagine dropping a rubber ball on three surfaces: (1) a hard marble floor, (2) a rubber gym mat, and (3) a thick foam cushion.
>
> - On the **marble floor**, the ball bounces back almost to its original height. Very little energy is lost — the material is **elastic** with low damping.
> - On the **rubber gym mat**, the ball bounces a few times before stopping. Some energy is converted to heat in the mat — moderate damping.
> - On the **foam cushion**, the ball barely bounces and just deforms the foam. Most energy is dissipated as heat and internal friction — **high damping**.
>
> Polymers exhibit this entire range of behavior depending on their temperature relative to the glass transition temperature ($T_g$). A polymer **below $T_g$** behaves like the marble floor (glassy, elastic), while a polymer **near or above $T_g$** behaves like the foam (rubbery, highly damped). The maximum damping occurs in the **glass transition region** itself.

## 1.3 Importance in Engineering

Damping is critical in engineering applications such as:
- **Automotive**: Engine mounts, bushing materials to reduce NVH (Noise, Vibration, Harshness)
- **Aerospace**: Vibration-control mounts in helicopters and aircraft
- **Civil Engineering**: Base isolators for buildings in seismic zones
- **Electronics**: Damping layers in speakers, headphones, and hard disk drives
- **Sports**: Shoe soles, tennis rackets, and helmets for shock absorption
- **Acoustic**: Sound-deadening panels and quiet enclosures

A polymer with high $\tan \delta$ (typically 0.5 to 2.0) in the operating temperature/frequency range is chosen for damping applications. Standard materials are tabulated:

| Polymer | $\tan \delta$ (peak) | Typical $T_g$ ($^\circ$C) |
|---|---|---|
| Polyisobutylene (PIB) | 1.5 – 2.0 | -70 |
| Polyurethane (PU) | 0.8 – 1.2 | -50 to 50 |
| Butyl Rubber (IIR) | 1.0 – 1.5 | -65 |
| Polymethyl methacrylate (PMMA) | 0.6 – 0.8 | 105 |
| Polystyrene (PS) | 0.2 – 0.3 | 100 |
| Polyethylene (PE) | 0.3 – 0.5 | -120 |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanics of Damping in Polymers

When a sinusoidal stress $\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \sin(\omega t)$ is applied to a viscoelastic polymer, the strain response lags behind by a phase angle $\delta$:

$$\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_0 \sin(\omega t - \delta)$$

The work done **per unit volume per cycle** is given by:

$$W_{dissipated} = \pi \sigma_0 \varepsilon_0 \sin \delta = \pi E'' \varepsilon_0^{2}$$

The energy **stored per unit volume per cycle** is:

$$W_{stored} = \frac{1}{2} \sigma_0 \varepsilon_0 \cos \delta = \frac{1}{2} E' \varepsilon_0^{2}$$

The **fractional energy loss per cycle** (specific damping capacity, $\psi$) is:

$$\psi = \frac{W_{dissipated}}{W_{stored}} = 2\pi \tan \delta$$

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Energy Dissipation Logic

The energy dissipation in polymers arises from the following microscopic and macroscopic mechanisms:

1. **Segmental Motion in the Amorphous Phase**: At temperatures near $T_g$, coordinated long-range segmental motions (20–50 backbone atoms) become activated. These motions lag behind the applied stress, producing large $\tan \delta$ peaks.
2. **Side-Group Rotation**: Rotation of bulky side groups (e.g., $-\text{COOCH}_3$ in PMMA, phenyl in PS) produces secondary ($\beta$) relaxation peaks at temperatures below $T_g$.
3. **Interfacial Slipping in Filled/Composite Systems**: At the interface between filler particles and the polymer matrix, mechanical energy is lost through friction (interfacial damping).
4. **Crystalline Phase Effects**: In semi-crystalline polymers, slippage of lamellae, motion within crystalline regions, and crystal–amorphous interfacial motion contribute to damping.
5. **Internal Friction (Hysteresis)**: When a polymer is deformed, internal friction between molecular chains converts mechanical energy to heat.
6. **Thermoelastic Damping**: Even in purely elastic materials, the small temperature change under adiabatic compression produces heat flow that dissipates energy.

## 2.3 The Five Regions of Viscoelastic Behavior

The damping behavior of an amorphous polymer is mapped onto **five regions** as a function of temperature:

| Region | State | $E'$ Behavior | $\tan \delta$ Behavior | Molecular Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Glassy | $\sim 10^9$ Pa (high plateau) | Very low ($\sim 0.01$) | Bond stretching, bond angle deformation |
| 2 | Glass Transition | Sharp drop (3–4 decades) | **Peak** ($\tan \delta$ max) | Long-range segmental motion |
| 3 | Rubbery Plateau | $\sim 10^6$ Pa | Low ($\sim 0.1$–0.3) | Entanglements restrict flow |
| 4 | Rubbery Flow | Gradual decrease | Moderate | Entanglement slippage |
| 5 | Viscous Flow | Low, decreases with time | Increases | Translational chain flow |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note**: The **maximum damping** ($\tan \delta$ peak) ALWAYS occurs in **Region 2 (Glass Transition)**. This is the single most tested concept on damping.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All critical equations for solving damping problems in KTU examinations:

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E^{*} = E' + iE''$ | Complex modulus | Pa |
| 2 | $\tan \delta = \dfrac{E''}{E'}$ | Loss tangent (damping factor) | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $\eta^{*} = \dfrac{E^{*}}{i\omega} = \eta' - i\eta''$ | Complex viscosity | Pa·s |
| 4 | $W_{diss} = \pi \sigma_0 \varepsilon_0 \sin \delta$ | Energy dissipated per cycle | J/m³ |
| 5 | $W_{stored} = \dfrac{1}{2} \sigma_0 \varepsilon_0 \cos \delta$ | Energy stored per cycle | J/m³ |
| 6 | $\psi = 2\pi \tan \delta$ | Specific damping capacity | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $\zeta = \dfrac{\tan \delta_{\text{peak}}}{\sqrt{4 - \tan^{2}\delta_{\text{peak}}}}$ | Damping ratio (mech. engineering) | Dimensionless |
| 8 | $\text{Loss} \,\% = \left(1 - 10^{-\Lambda/20}\right) \times 100$ | Transmission loss in dB | dB |
| 9 | $\Lambda = 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{A_0}{A_n}\right)$ | Logarithmic decrement | dB |
| 10 | $Q^{-1} = \tan \delta$ | Inverse quality factor | Dimensionless |

**CRITICAL: Use `\vert` instead of `|` inside table rows to avoid breaking table syntax. For example, use $\vert E' \vert$ instead of $\vert E' \vert$.**

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Damping measurements via **Dynamic Mechanical Analysis (DMA)** allow engineers to:
- Determine $T_g$ with 10× greater sensitivity than DSC
- Predict the long-term creep and stress relaxation behavior
- Evaluate cure cycles for thermoset resins
- Assess material performance for service temperature ranges
- Design constrained-layer damping treatments (CLD) and free-layer damping (FLD) for vibration control

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Complex Modulus from Sinusoidal Loading

**Given:** A viscoelastic polymer is subjected to a sinusoidal stress:
$$\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \, e^{i\omega t}$$

**Step 1**: The corresponding strain response in a viscoelastic material is also sinusoidal but phase-shifted by $\delta$:
$$\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_0 \, e^{i(\omega t - \delta)}$$

**Step 2**: By the definition of the complex modulus, stress is related to strain by:
$$E^{*} = \frac{\sigma(t)}{\varepsilon(t)}$$

**Step 3**: Substituting the expressions for $\sigma(t)$ and $\varepsilon(t)$:
$$E^{*} = \frac{\sigma_0 \, e^{i\omega t}}{\varepsilon_0 \, e^{i(\omega t - \delta)}}$$

**Step 4**: Simplifying the exponentials by canceling $e^{i\omega t}$:
$$E^{*} = \frac{\sigma_0}{\varepsilon_0} \, e^{i\delta}$$

**Step 5**: Using Euler's identity $e^{i\delta} = \cos \delta + i \sin \delta$:
$$E^{*} = \frac{\sigma_0}{\varepsilon_0} \left(\cos \delta + i \sin \delta\right)$$

**Step 6**: Separating into real and imaginary components:
$$E^{*} = \underbrace{\frac{\sigma_0}{\varepsilon_0}\cos \delta}_{E'} + i \underbrace{\frac{\sigma_0}{\varepsilon_0}\sin \delta}_{E''}$$

**Step 7**: Therefore the storage and loss moduli are:
$$\boxed{E' = \frac{\sigma_0}{\varepsilon_0}\cos \delta \quad \text{and} \quad E'' = \frac{\sigma_0}{\varepsilon_0}\sin \delta}$$

**Step 8**: The ratio gives the loss tangent:
$$\tan \delta = \frac{E''}{E'} = \frac{\sin \delta}{\cos \delta}$$

This is the fundamental definition used in all KTU derivations. (Q.E.D.)

## 3.2 Derivation of Energy Dissipated Per Cycle

**Given:** Energy density in a stressed polymer is $U = \int \sigma \, d\varepsilon$.

**Step 1**: Express the strain as a function of time:
$$\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_0 \sin(\omega t - \delta)$$

**Step 2**: Compute the differential strain:
$$d\varepsilon = \varepsilon_0 \omega \cos(\omega t - \delta) \, dt$$

**Step 3**: The stress is:
$$\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \sin(\omega t)$$

**Step 4**: Substituting into the energy integral over one full cycle of period $T = 2\pi/\omega$:
$$W = \int_{0}^{T} \sigma(t) \, \frac{d\varepsilon}{dt} \, dt$$

**Step 5**: Express the energy density explicitly:
$$W = \int_{0}^{2\pi/\omega} \sigma_0 \sin(\omega t) \cdot \varepsilon_0 \omega \cos(\omega t - \delta) \, dt$$

**Step 6**: Expand the cosine term using the identity $\cos(A-B) = \cos A \cos B + \sin A \sin B$:
$$\cos(\omega t - \delta) = \cos(\omega t)\cos \delta + \sin(\omega t)\sin \delta$$

**Step 7**: The product becomes:
$$\sin(\omega t) \cdot \left[\cos(\omega t)\cos \delta + \sin(\omega t)\sin \delta\right] = \sin(\omega t)\cos(\omega t)\cos \delta + \sin^{2}(\omega t)\sin \delta$$

**Step 8**: The first term $\sin(\omega t)\cos(\omega t) = \tfrac{1}{2}\sin(2\omega t)$ integrates to **zero** over a full cycle.

**Step 9**: The second term gives:
$$W = \sigma_0 \varepsilon_0 \omega \sin \delta \int_{0}^{2\pi/\omega} \sin^{2}(\omega t) \, dt$$

**Step 10**: Evaluate the integral:
$$\int_{0}^{2\pi/\omega} \sin^{2}(\omega t) \, dt = \frac{\pi}{\omega}$$

**Step 11**: Substituting back:
$$\boxed{W_{dissipated} = \pi \sigma_0 \varepsilon_0 \sin \delta = \pi E'' \varepsilon_0^{2}}$$

(Q.E.D.)

## 3.3 Derivation of Damping Ratio from Loss Tangent

In mechanical vibration engineering, the **damping ratio** $\zeta$ is related to $\tan \delta$ through:

**Step 1**: The amplitude of a damped oscillator decays as:
$$A_n = A_0 \, e^{-\zeta \omega_n t}$$

**Step 2**: The logarithmic decrement is:
$$\Lambda = \ln\left(\frac{A_0}{A_n}\right) = \zeta \omega_n T_d = \frac{2\pi \zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}}$$

**Step 3**: The energy ratio between successive cycles is:
$$\frac{U_n}{U_{n+1}} = e^{2\Lambda}$$

**Step 4**: In viscoelastic materials, the specific damping capacity gives:
$$\psi = 2\pi \tan \delta$$

**Step 5**: Equating the energy ratio expressions and solving for $\zeta$ in terms of $\tan \delta$:
$$\boxed{\zeta = \frac{\tan \delta}{\sqrt{4 + \tan^{2}\delta}} \approx \frac{\tan \delta}{2} \quad \text{(for small } \tan\delta\text{)}}$$

This is often used in KTU problems on vibration-damped composites.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Master Equation Reference

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Hot List — Equations to memorize for ESE (End Semester Examination):**

| Concept | Master Equation | Variables Explained | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Complex modulus | $E^{*} = E' + iE''$ | $E'$ (Pa), $E''$ (Pa) | Viscoelastic response |
| Loss tangent | $\tan\delta = E''/E'$ | dimensionless | Damping measurement |
| Damping capacity | $\psi = 2\pi\tan\delta$ | dimensionless | Energy dissipation |
| Frequency-temperature shift | $\log a_T = -\dfrac{C_1 (T-T_g)}{C_2 + (T-T_g)}$ | WLF constants $C_1, C_2$ | Master curve construction |
| Arrhenius for $\beta$-relaxation | $\tau = \tau_0 e^{E_a/RT}$ | $E_a$ activation energy | Sub-$T_g$ damping |
| Damping ratio | $\zeta = \tan\delta / \sqrt{4+\tan^{2}\delta}$ | dimensionless | Vibration engineering |

## 4.3 Schematic Description — Hysteresis Loop in a Viscoelastic Polymer

When a polymer is subjected to one complete cycle of sinusoidal loading (stress–strain), it traces out a closed loop called the **hysteresis loop**:

- The **loading curve** (going up) lies above the **unloading curve** (coming down).
- The **area enclosed** by this loop equals the energy dissipated as heat per unit volume per cycle: $W_{diss} = \oint \sigma \, d\varepsilon$.
- The **slope of the line connecting the origin to the peak** gives the apparent modulus.
- A material with high damping (large $\tan \delta$) exhibits a **wide, fat hysteresis loop**.
- A purely elastic material exhibits a **thin, straight line** (zero area).

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q1.** Define **damping** in polymers. What is the significance of the **loss tangent ($\tan \delta$)**?

**Model Answer:**
Damping in polymers is the dissipation of mechanical (vibrational) energy as heat when the polymer undergoes cyclic deformation. The loss tangent $\tan \delta$ is the ratio of energy dissipated to energy stored per cycle, defined as $\tan \delta = E''/E'$. A higher $\tan \delta$ indicates better damping ability. Values of $\tan \delta$ between 0.5 and 2.0 are considered excellent for engineering damping applications. (3 marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q2.** Why does the **maximum damping peak** occur in the **glass transition region** of an amorphous polymer?

**Model Answer:**
Near the glass transition temperature ($T_g$), long-range coordinated segmental motion of polymer chains (involving 20–50 backbone atoms) becomes activated on a timescale comparable to the experimental deformation frequency. The lag between applied stress and resulting strain is maximized, producing the peak in $E''$ and $\tan \delta$. Below $T_g$ (glassy state), molecular motion is frozen — low damping. Above $T_g$ (rubbery state), chains respond rapidly — also low damping. Hence, the transition region uniquely exhibits maximum damping. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Module 2]**

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **complex modulus** $E^{*}$ of a viscoelastic polymer subjected to sinusoidal stress, and clearly distinguish between the **storage modulus** $E'$ and the **loss modulus** $E''$. (7 marks)

**(b)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **five regions of viscoelastic behavior** as a function of temperature and identify the region of maximum damping. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a)

**Given**: Sinusoidal stress $\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 e^{i\omega t}$ and lagging strain $\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_0 e^{i(\omega t - \delta)}$.

**Step 1**: Define complex modulus as the ratio of stress to strain:
$$E^{*} = \frac{\sigma(t)}{\varepsilon(t)} = \frac{\sigma_0 \, e^{i\omega t}}{\varepsilon_0 \, e^{i(\omega t - \delta)}} = \frac{\sigma_0}{\varepsilon_0} e^{i\delta}$$

**Step 2**: Apply Euler's identity $e^{i\delta} = \cos \delta + i \sin \delta$:
$$E^{*} = \frac{\sigma_0}{\varepsilon_0}\cos \delta + i \frac{\sigma_0}{\varepsilon_0}\sin \delta$$

**Step 3**: Compare with $E^{*} = E' + iE''$:
$$E' = \frac{\sigma_0}{\varepsilon_0}\cos \delta \quad ; \quad E'' = \frac{\sigma_0}{\varepsilon_0}\sin \delta$$

**Step 4 — Distinction**:
- **$E'$ (Storage Modulus)**: The in-phase component representing the elastic, energy-storing (recoverable) response. It is a measure of stiffness.
- **$E''$ (Loss Modulus)**: The out-of-phase component representing the viscous, energy-dissipating response. It is a measure of energy lost as heat.

**Step 5 — Loss Tangent**:
$$\tan \delta = \frac{E''}{E'} = \frac{\sin \delta}{\cos \delta}$$

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b)

**The Five Regions of Viscoelastic Behavior:**

| Region | State | $E'$ | $\tan\delta$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Glassy | High ($\sim 10^9$ Pa) | Very low |
| 2 | Glass Transition | Sharp drop | **Peak** |
| 3 | Rubbery Plateau | $\sim 10^6$ Pa | Low |
| 4 | Rubbery Flow | Decreasing | Moderate |
| 5 | Viscous Flow | Very low | Increasing |

**Region of Maximum Damping**: **Region 2 (Glass Transition Region)**, where segmental motion is activated and phase lag between stress and strain is maximum.

(Sketch: A semi-log plot of $E'$ (left axis, decreasing stepwise curve) and $\tan\delta$ (right axis, showing a sharp peak in Region 2), with temperature $T$ on the x-axis. Annotate each region.)

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — *ALTERNATIVE*

**(a)** Discuss the various **molecular mechanisms** responsible for damping in polymers, with emphasis on **glassy-state**, **glass-transition**, and **rubbery-state** mechanisms. (7 marks)

**(b)** A polymer sample is subjected to a cyclic tensile stress of amplitude $\sigma_0 = 5 \text{ MPa}$ at a frequency of 10 Hz. The resulting strain amplitude is $\varepsilon_0 = 0.005$, and the phase lag is $\delta = 12°$. Calculate:
  (i) The complex modulus $E^*$
  (ii) The storage modulus $E'$
  (iii) The loss modulus $E''$
  (iv) The loss tangent $\tan\delta$
  (v) The energy dissipated per cycle per unit volume
  (vi) The specific damping capacity $\psi$ (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a)

**Molecular Mechanisms of Damping:**

1. **Glassy-State Mechanisms (Below $T_g$)**: Limited to small-scale motions — bond stretching, bond-angle bending, and rotation of small side groups (methyl, phenyl). These produce small $\beta$ and $\gamma$ relaxation peaks.

2. **Glass-Transition Mechanisms (Near $T_g$)**: The CRITICAL mechanism. Coordinated long-range segmental motion of 20–50 backbone atoms. This is the primary damping peak ($\alpha$-relaxation). Energy is dissipated through viscous flow of chain segments lagging behind the applied stress.

3. **Rubbery-State Mechanisms (Above $T_g$)**: Entanglements restrict chain flow, producing the rubbery plateau. Damping is moderate. At higher temperatures, chain disentanglement produces additional flow-related damping.

4. **Crystalline-Phase Effects**: In semi-crystalline polymers, lamellar slip, grain boundary friction, and crystal–amorphous interfacial motion contribute.

5. **Compositional Effects**: In polymer blends and composites, interfacial friction at phase boundaries adds substantial damping.

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b)

**Given**: $\sigma_0 = 5 \text{ MPa}$, $\varepsilon_0 = 0.005$, $\delta = 12°$, $f = 10 \text{ Hz}$.

**Convert phase to radians**: $\delta = 12° \times \dfrac{\pi}{180°} = 0.2094$ rad.

**(i) Complex modulus magnitude**:
$$\vert E^{*} \vert = \frac{\sigma_0}{\varepsilon_0} = \frac{5 \times 10^{6}}{0.005} = 1.0 \times 10^{9} \text{ Pa} = 1.0 \text{ GPa}$$

**(ii) Storage modulus**:
$$E' = \vert E^{*} \vert \cos \delta = (1.0 \times 10^{9}) \cos(12°) = (1.0 \times 10^{9})(0.9781) = 9.781 \times 10^{8} \text{ Pa} \approx 978 \text{ MPa}$$

**(iii) Loss modulus**:
$$E'' = \vert E^{*} \vert \sin \delta = (1.0 \times 10^{9}) \sin(12°) = (1.0 \times 10^{9})(0.2079) = 2.079 \times 10^{8} \text{ Pa} \approx 208 \text{ MPa}$$

**(iv) Loss tangent**:
$$\tan \delta = \frac{E''}{E'} = \frac{2.079 \times 10^{8}}{9.781 \times 10^{8}} = 0.2126 \approx 0.213$$

**Verification**: $\tan(12°) = 0.2126$ ✓

**(v) Energy dissipated per cycle per unit volume**:
$$W_{diss} = \pi \sigma_0 \varepsilon_0 \sin \delta = \pi (5 \times 10^{6})(0.005)(0.2079)$$
$$W_{diss} = \pi (1.0395 \times 10^{4}) = 3.265 \times 10^{4} \text{ J/m}^{3} = 32.65 \text{ kJ/m}^{3}$$

**(vi) Specific damping capacity**:
$$\psi = 2\pi \tan \delta = 2\pi (0.2126) = 1.335$$

**Interpretation**: The material has moderate damping ($\tan\delta \approx 0.21$); suitable for general vibration control but not for high-damping acoustic applications (which require $\tan\delta > 0.5$).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Callout**
>
> **Top 5 ways students LOSE marks on damping questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert degrees to radians** when computing $\sin\delta$ or $\cos\delta$. Always state the conversion: $\delta(\text{rad}) = \delta(°) \times \pi/180$.
> 2. **Mixing up $E'$ and $E''$**: $E' \propto \cos\delta$ (in-phase) and $E'' \propto \sin\delta$ (out-of-phase). Confusing these reverses the answer.
> 3. **Not drawing/labeling the DMA plot properly**: When asked for the five regions, students forget to mark $T_g$ on the temperature axis and the peak position of $\tan\delta$.
> 4. **Ignoring units**: $E'$ and $E''$ must be in Pa (or MPa/GPa), $W_{diss}$ in J/m³. Missing units cost easy marks.
> 5. **Calculating $\psi$ instead of $\tan\delta$** (or vice versa) when the question specifically asks for the damping factor.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Damping in Polymers**

- ✅ **Damping** = Energy dissipation (as heat) during cyclic mechanical deformation.
- ✅ The governing equation is $E^{*} = E' + iE''$ where $E'$ = storage, $E''$ = loss modulus.
- ✅ **Loss tangent** $\tan\delta = E''/E'$ — dimensionless indicator of damping capacity.
- ✅ **Energy dissipated per cycle**: $W_{diss} = \pi \sigma_0 \varepsilon_0 \sin\delta = \pi E'' \varepsilon_0^{2}$.
- ✅ **Energy stored per cycle**: $W_{stored} = \tfrac{1}{2} \sigma_0 \varepsilon_0 \cos\delta = \tfrac{1}{2} E' \varepsilon_0^{2}$.
- ✅ **Specific damping capacity**: $\psi = 2\pi \tan\delta$.
- ✅ **Maximum damping occurs at $T_g$** due to long-range segmental motion activation.
- ✅ **Five regions**: Glassy → Glass transition → Rubbery plateau → Rubbery flow → Viscous flow.
- ✅ **DMA (Dynamic Mechanical Analysis)** is the standard experimental technique.
- ✅ **High-damping polymers**: PIB, butyl rubber, polyurethane ($\tan\delta > 1$).
- ✅ **Low-damping polymers**: Polystyrene, crosslinked epoxies ($\tan\delta < 0.1$).
- ✅ **Engineering rule of thumb**: For damping applications, choose a polymer whose $T_g$ is near the **service temperature** of the application.
- ✅ The **WLF equation** $\log a_T = -C_1(T-T_g)/[C_2 + (T-T_g)]$ allows shifting DMA curves to construct master curves.
- ✅ **Interfacial damping** in filled composites arises from friction at filler–matrix boundaries.
- ✅ **Hysteresis loop area** = $W_{diss}$ per cycle per unit volume.
- ✅ **Damping ratio** $\zeta = \tan\delta / \sqrt{4 + \tan^{2}\delta}$ connects viscoelastic damping to mechanical vibration theory.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Spring
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT402 - POLYMER PHYSICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Spring

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In **Polymer Physics** (KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 3 – *Mechanical Models of Viscoelastic Behavior*), the term **"Spring"** refers to the **Hookean Spring Element** — an *ideal, purely elastic* mechanical analog used to model the **instantaneous elastic response** of a polymeric material.

It obeys **Hooke's Law** of linear elasticity, where the applied stress $\sigma$ is directly proportional to the resulting strain $\varepsilon$, with the proportionality constant being the **Young's Modulus ($E$)** of the material.

Mathematically, the spring element is described by:

$$\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$$

Where:
- $\sigma$ = Applied stress (in **Pa** or **N/m²**)
- $E$ = Young's Modulus (in **Pa**) — material stiffness constant
- $\varepsilon$ = Resulting strain (dimensionless)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The spring element represents the **fully recoverable, time-independent** elastic component of a polymer. It is the building block of all mechanical analog models (Maxwell, Kelvin-Voigt, Standard Linear Solid) used to describe **viscoelasticity** in polymers.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine stretching a **steel spring** in a ballpoint pen clicker:
- You pull it → it stretches *immediately* by a fixed amount.
- You hold it → it stays at the *same* stretched length (no further creep).
- You release it → it snaps back *instantly* to its original length.
- All the energy you put in comes back out — **no energy is lost**.

This is *exactly* how the **Hookean spring** behaves in polymer physics:
- **Instant** response (no time delay).
- **Linear** relationship between force and deformation.
- **No energy dissipation** (purely conservative / elastic).
- Acts as the **"memory-less elastic"** building block of polymer mechanical models.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world polymer example:** A lightly cross-linked rubber band at *very small strains* (<5%) behaves almost like a pure spring — it obeys Hooke's law and shows negligible viscous flow.

---

## 1.3 Why the Spring Element Matters in Polymer Physics

Polymers are **viscoelastic** — they behave partly like an elastic solid (spring) and partly like a viscous fluid (dashpot). To mathematically describe this dual nature, we model the polymer as a combination of:

| Element | Symbol | Behavior | Energy Fate |
|---------|--------|----------|-------------|
| **Spring** | ⓢ | Elastic (Hookean) | Stored & recovered |
| **Dashpot** | Ⓓ | Viscous (Newtonian) | Dissipated as heat |

> [!TIP]
> The **spring is the "elastic personality"** of the polymer. Without it, the model would have no memory of its original shape and would permanently deform.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | SI Unit | Typical Polymer Range |
|----------|--------|---------|------------------------|
| Young's Modulus | $E$ | **Pa (N/m²)** | $10^5$ – $10^9$ Pa |
| Stress | $\sigma$ | **Pa** | $10^3$ – $10^7$ Pa |
| Strain | $\varepsilon$ | dimensionless | $0.001$ – $1.0$ |
| Spring Constant | $k$ | **N/m** | $10^{-3}$ – $10^3$ N/m |
| Compliance | $J$ | **Pa⁻¹** | $10^{-10}$ – $10^{-5}$ Pa⁻¹ |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Logic of a Hookean Spring

The spring element is governed by **four fundamental behavioral rules** that every KTU student must internalize:

### Step 1: Instantaneous Response
The moment a stress $\sigma$ is applied, the strain $\varepsilon$ jumps immediately to its equilibrium value $\varepsilon_0 = \sigma / E$. There is **no time delay** — this is the defining feature of a pure spring.

### Step 2: Constant Strain Under Constant Stress (Creep)
If a constant stress $\sigma_0$ is applied and held, the strain remains **constant** at $\varepsilon_0 = \sigma_0 / E$. The spring does **not** continue to deform over time. This is fundamentally different from a dashpot, which flows continuously.

### Step 3: Instantaneous Stress Relaxation
If a constant strain $\varepsilon_0$ is imposed, the stress instantly reaches $\sigma_0 = E \cdot \varepsilon_0$ and remains **constant** indefinitely. There is **no decay** — pure springs do not relax.

### Step 4: Energy Storage (Conservative Element)
The energy stored per unit volume in a Hookean spring is:

$$U = \frac{1}{2} \, \sigma \, \varepsilon = \frac{1}{2} \, E \, \varepsilon^{2} = \frac{\sigma^{2}}{2E}$$

This energy is **fully recovered** upon unloading — no energy is lost to heat.

---

## 2.2 Why This Matters: The "Why" Behind the Spring

| Question | Answer (KTU Board Perspective) |
|----------|---------------------------------|
| *Why does the spring respond instantly?* | Hooke's law is a **time-independent constitutive equation** — there is no $\partial / \partial t$ term. |
| *Why no creep in a pure spring?* | Creep requires a **time-dependent** deformation mechanism. Hookean elasticity is **memoryless** — $\varepsilon$ depends only on the *current* $\sigma$. |
| *Why no stress relaxation?* | Stress relaxation requires a **dissipative** mechanism. A spring has no dashpot, so it cannot bleed off stress. |
| *Why is $U = \frac{1}{2}E\varepsilon^2$?* | Integration of $\sigma = E\varepsilon$ from 0 to $\varepsilon$ gives the area under the stress-strain curve (work done = energy stored). |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table.** Every KTU question on spring elements uses one or more of these equations.

| # | Formula | Name | Units | Application |
|---|---------|------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | $\sigma = E \, \varepsilon$ | Hooke's Law | Pa | Fundamental spring relation |
| 2 | $\varepsilon = \sigma / E$ | Strain (compliance form) | dimensionless | Solving for deformation |
| 3 | $E = \sigma / \varepsilon$ | Young's Modulus | Pa | Material stiffness |
| 4 | $J(t) = 1/E$ | Creep compliance (spring) | Pa⁻¹ | Constant for pure spring |
| 5 | $G(t) = E$ | Relaxation modulus (spring) | Pa | Constant for pure spring |
| 6 | $U = \frac{1}{2}\sigma\varepsilon$ | Strain energy density | J/m³ | Energy stored in spring |
| 7 | $U = \frac{1}{2}E\varepsilon^{2}$ | Energy (strain form) | J/m³ | Useful in cyclic loading |
| 8 | $U = \sigma^{2}/(2E)$ | Energy (stress form) | J/m³ | Useful in stress-controlled tests |
| 9 | $F = k \, x$ | Spring force (1D) | N | Linear spring analog |
| 10 | $k = E \cdot A / L_0$ | Spring constant ↔ Modulus | N/m | Geometry conversion |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU tip:** The creep compliance $J(t)$ and relaxation modulus $G(t)$ of a **pure spring** are **constants in time** — they do *not* depend on $t$. This is the key contrast with dashpots and combined models.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of Spring Model |
|-------|------------------------------|
| **Rubber Product Design** | Initial elastic response of elastomers in seals & gaskets |
| **Composite Manufacturing** | Modeling the elastic matrix phase in fiber-reinforced polymers |
| **Biomedical Polymers** | Modeling the elastic response of arterial walls, cartilage, tendons |
| **3D Printing (FDM)** | Predicting instantaneous recovery of printed polymer parts |
| **Vibration Damping** | Pure spring = undamped oscillator — baseline for polymer damping studies |
| **Adhesive Science** | Elastic recovery component of pressure-sensitive adhesives |

---

## 2.5 Spring in Series vs. Spring in Parallel

When KTU questions ask about *multiple springs*, the following rules apply:

**Springs in Series (same force, deformations add):**

$$\frac{1}{E_{eq}} = \frac{1}{E_1} + \frac{1}{E_2} + \cdots + \frac{1}{E_n} \quad \Rightarrow \quad E_{eq} = \left( \sum_{i} \frac{1}{E_i} \right)^{-1}$$

**Springs in Parallel (same deformation, forces add):**

$$E_{eq} = E_1 + E_2 + \cdots + E_n$$

> [!TIP]
> **Series = compliance adds; Parallel = stiffness adds.** This is the direct analog of electrical resistors (series vs. parallel) with the $1/E$ playing the role of resistance.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Creep Response of a Pure Spring

**Problem Setup:** A constant stress $\sigma_0$ is suddenly applied at $t = 0$ to a Hookean spring element. Derive the strain response $\varepsilon(t)$.

**Step 1: Write the constitutive equation.**

For a Hookean spring, the governing equation is:

$$\sigma(t) = E \cdot \varepsilon(t)$$

This is a **time-independent** algebraic relation — there are no derivatives with respect to $t$.

**Step 2: Apply the constant stress condition.**

For $t \geq 0$, we impose:

$$\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 = \text{constant}$$

**Step 3: Solve for the strain.**

Rearranging the Hookean relation:

$$\varepsilon(t) = \frac{\sigma(t)}{E} = \frac{\sigma_0}{E}$$

**Step 4: Express the creep compliance.**

The **creep compliance** $J(t)$ is defined as the ratio of strain to applied constant stress:

$$J(t) \; \equiv \; \frac{\varepsilon(t)}{\sigma_0} \; = \; \frac{1}{E}$$

**Step 5: State the final result.**

$$\boxed{\varepsilon(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{E} \quad \text{(constant for all } t \geq 0\text{)}}$$

**Interpretation:** The spring extends *instantaneously* to $\sigma_0/E$ at $t = 0$ and then **stays there forever**. The creep compliance $J(t) = 1/E$ is a **horizontal line** when plotted versus $t$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical meaning:** A pure spring shows **zero creep** — this is its signature. Compare with a dashpot (which gives $J(t) = t/\eta$, an ever-rising line).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Stress Relaxation of a Pure Spring

**Problem Setup:** A constant strain $\varepsilon_0$ is suddenly applied at $t = 0$. Derive the stress response $\sigma(t)$.

**Step 1: Write the constitutive equation.**

$$\sigma(t) = E \cdot \varepsilon(t)$$

**Step 2: Apply the constant strain condition.**

For $t \geq 0$:

$$\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_0 = \text{constant}$$

**Step 3: Solve for the stress.**

$$\sigma(t) = E \cdot \varepsilon_0 = \text{constant}$$

**Step 4: Express the relaxation modulus.**

The **relaxation modulus** $G(t)$ is defined as:

$$G(t) \; \equiv \; \frac{\sigma(t)}{\varepsilon_0} \; = \; E$$

**Step 5: State the final result.**

$$\boxed{\sigma(t) = E \, \varepsilon_0 \quad \text{(constant for all } t \geq 0\text{)}}$$

**Interpretation:** The stress jumps instantly to $E\varepsilon_0$ at $t = 0$ and remains constant. The relaxation modulus $G(t) = E$ is a **horizontal line** versus $t$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU contrast:** Compare with a **Maxwell model** (spring + dashpot in series), where $G(t) = E \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$ — the stress *decays* exponentially. The pure spring alone shows **no decay**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Strain Energy Stored in a Spring

**Problem Setup:** Derive the elastic energy per unit volume stored in a spring strained to $\varepsilon$.

**Step 1: Start from the definition of strain energy density.**

The strain energy density $U$ is the work done per unit volume to deform the material from $\varepsilon = 0$ to a final strain $\varepsilon$:

$$U = \int_{0}^{\varepsilon} \sigma(\varepsilon') \, d\varepsilon'$$

**Step 2: Substitute Hooke's law.**

Using $\sigma = E\varepsilon'$:

$$U = \int_{0}^{\varepsilon} E \, \varepsilon' \, d\varepsilon'$$

**Step 3: Evaluate the integral.**

$$U = E \left[ \frac{(\varepsilon')^{2}}{2} \right]_{0}^{\varepsilon} = E \cdot \frac{\varepsilon^{2}}{2}$$

**Step 4: Final result.**

$$\boxed{U = \frac{1}{2} \, E \, \varepsilon^{2}}$$

**Step 5: Alternative forms.**

Since $\sigma = E\varepsilon$, we can also write:

$$U = \frac{1}{2} \, \sigma \, \varepsilon \quad \text{or} \quad U = \frac{\sigma^{2}}{2E}$$

**Step 6: Physical interpretation.**

This energy is the area under the **linear stress-strain curve** (a triangle of base $\varepsilon$ and height $\sigma$):

$$U = \frac{1}{2} \times \text{base} \times \text{height} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \varepsilon \cdot \sigma$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU trick:** All three forms $\left(\frac{1}{2}E\varepsilon^2,\; \frac{1}{2}\sigma\varepsilon,\; \frac{\sigma^2}{2E}\right)$ are equivalent. Choose the one that matches the quantities given in the problem.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Spring Constant vs. Young's Modulus

**Problem Setup:** A cylindrical polymer specimen of length $L_0$ and cross-sectional area $A$ is modeled as a spring. Relate the mechanical spring constant $k$ to the Young's modulus $E$.

**Step 1: Hooke's law in force-displacement form.**

For a linear spring, the applied force $F$ produces a displacement $x$:

$$F = k \cdot x$$

**Step 2: Express force and displacement in terms of stress and strain.**

Engineering stress: $\sigma = F / A$, so $F = \sigma A$.
Engineering strain: $\varepsilon = x / L_0$, so $x = \varepsilon L_0$.

**Step 3: Substitute into Hooke's law.**

$$\sigma A = k \cdot \varepsilon L_0$$

**Step 4: Rearrange.**

$$k = \frac{\sigma A}{\varepsilon L_0} = \left(\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon}\right) \cdot \frac{A}{L_0} = E \cdot \frac{A}{L_0}$$

**Step 5: Final result.**

$$\boxed{k = \frac{E \, A}{L_0}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Stiffer material** (larger $E$) → larger $k$. **Thicker specimen** (larger $A$) → larger $k$. **Longer specimen** (larger $L_0$) → smaller $k$ (more compliant).

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Equivalent Spring Constant — Series Combination

**Problem Setup:** Two Hookean springs of moduli $E_1$ and $E_2$ (same area $A$, lengths $L_1$ and $L_2$) are connected **in series**. Find the equivalent modulus $E_{eq}$.

**Step 1: In series, the same force is transmitted through both springs.**

$$F = F_1 = F_2$$

**Step 2: Total strain is the sum of individual strains.**

$$\varepsilon_{total} = \varepsilon_1 + \varepsilon_2$$

**Step 3: Express individual strains using Hooke's law.**

$$\varepsilon_1 = \frac{\sigma}{E_1} = \frac{F}{A \, E_1}, \quad \varepsilon_2 = \frac{F}{A \, E_2}$$

**Step 4: Total strain.**

$$\varepsilon_{total} = \frac{F}{A} \left( \frac{1}{E_1} + \frac{1}{E_2} \right)$$

**Step 5: Define equivalent modulus.**

$$\varepsilon_{total} = \frac{F}{A \, E_{eq}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{E_{eq}} = \frac{1}{E_1} + \frac{1}{E_2}$$

**Step 6: Final result.**

$$\boxed{\frac{1}{E_{eq}} = \frac{1}{E_1} + \frac{1}{E_2} + \cdots + \frac{1}{E_n}}$$

---

## 3.6 Derivation 6: Equivalent Spring Constant — Parallel Combination

**Problem Setup:** Two Hookean springs (same length, areas $A_1$ and $A_2$, moduli $E_1$ and $E_2$) are connected **in parallel**. Find the equivalent modulus $E_{eq}$.

**Step 1: In parallel, the same strain is experienced by both springs.**

$$\varepsilon = \varepsilon_1 = \varepsilon_2$$

**Step 2: Total force is the sum of individual forces.**

$$F_{total} = F_1 + F_2$$

**Step 3: Express individual forces.**

$$F_1 = E_1 A_1 \varepsilon, \quad F_2 = E_2 A_2 \varepsilon$$

**Step 4: Total force.**

$$F_{total} = (E_1 A_1 + E_2 A_2) \varepsilon$$

**Step 5: Equivalent modulus (assuming equal areas, $A_1 = A_2 = A$):**

$$E_{eq} \, A = E_1 A + E_2 A \quad \Rightarrow \quad E_{eq} = E_1 + E_2$$

**Step 6: Final result.**

$$\boxed{E_{eq} = E_1 + E_2 + \cdots + E_n}$$

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define a Hookean spring element. Write its constitutive equation and state the significance of Young's modulus in the context of polymer viscoelasticity.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Hookean spring** is an ideal, purely elastic mechanical element used to model the time-independent elastic response of polymeric materials.

Its constitutive equation is:

$$\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$$

Where $\sigma$ is the applied stress, $E$ is the Young's modulus, and $\varepsilon$ is the resulting strain.

**Significance of $E$:** The Young's modulus quantifies the **stiffness** of the polymer. A high $E$ indicates a rigid polymer (e.g., polystyrene, $E \sim 3$ GPa), while a low $E$ indicates a soft, flexible polymer (e.g., rubber, $E \sim 0.01$ GPa). In viscoelastic modeling, $E$ sets the **scale of elastic response** and appears as the instantaneous modulus in Maxwell, Kelvin-Voigt, and Standard Linear Solid models.

*(Award full marks for: definition + equation + brief significance.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**A pure Hookean spring shows neither creep nor stress relaxation. Justify this statement with appropriate equations.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Hookean spring obeys $\sigma = E\varepsilon$, a **time-independent** relation.

**No creep:** Under a constant stress $\sigma_0$, the strain is:

$$\varepsilon(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{E} = \text{constant}$$

The creep compliance $J(t) = 1/E$ is independent of $t$, so the spring does **not** deform progressively — no creep.

**No stress relaxation:** Under a constant strain $\varepsilon_0$, the stress is:

$$\sigma(t) = E \, \varepsilon_0 = \text{constant}$$

The relaxation modulus $G(t) = E$ is independent of $t$, so the spring does **not** bleed off stress — no relaxation.

Thus, a pure spring exhibits **neither** phenomenon. *(3 marks: 1.5 for creep justification + 1.5 for relaxation justification.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

---

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]` — **14 Marks**

**(a)** Derive the creep compliance $J(t)$ and the relaxation modulus $G(t)$ for a Hookean spring element. Sketch both response curves. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A polymer specimen of length $L_0 = 0.1$ m and cross-sectional area $A = 10^{-4}$ m² is modeled as a Hookean spring with Young's modulus $E = 2 \times 10^8$ Pa. Calculate:
  (i) The spring constant $k$ of the specimen.
  (ii) The strain produced when a stress of $5 \times 10^6$ Pa is applied.
  (iii) The elastic energy stored per unit volume at this strain.
  (iv) The total elastic energy stored in the specimen. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Question A

**(a) Derivation of $J(t)$ and $G(t)$:**

**Creep Compliance $J(t)$:**

The constitutive equation of a Hookean spring is:

$$\sigma(t) = E \cdot \varepsilon(t)$$

Under a constant applied stress $\sigma_0$ for $t \geq 0$:

$$\varepsilon(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{E}$$

The creep compliance is defined as $J(t) = \varepsilon(t)/\sigma_0$:

$$\boxed{J(t) = \frac{1}{E} \quad \text{(constant, independent of time)}}$$

**Relaxation Modulus $G(t)$:**

Under a constant imposed strain $\varepsilon_0$ for $t \geq 0$:

$$\sigma(t) = E \cdot \varepsilon_0$$

The relaxation modulus is defined as $G(t) = \sigma(t)/\varepsilon_0$:

$$\boxed{G(t) = E \quad \text{(constant, independent of time)}}$$

**Sketches:**
- $J(t)$ vs. $t$: Horizontal line at height $1/E$ from $t = 0$ onward.
- $G(t)$ vs. $t$: Horizontal line at height $E$ from $t = 0$ onward.

*(7 marks: 2 for $J(t)$ derivation, 2 for $G(t)$ derivation, 2 for sketches, 1 for stating independence of time.)*

---

**(b) Numerical Calculations:**

Given: $L_0 = 0.1$ m, $A = 10^{-4}$ m², $E = 2 \times 10^8$ Pa, $\sigma = 5 \times 10^6$ Pa.

**(i) Spring constant $k$:**

$$k = \frac{E \, A}{L_0} = \frac{(2 \times 10^{8}) \times (10^{-4})}{0.1}$$

$$k = \frac{2 \times 10^{4}}{0.1} = 2 \times 10^{5} \; \text{N/m}$$

$$\boxed{k = 2 \times 10^{5} \; \text{N/m}}$$

**(ii) Strain $\varepsilon$:**

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{5 \times 10^{6}}{2 \times 10^{8}} = 2.5 \times 10^{-2}$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon = 0.025 \quad \text{(dimensionless)}}$$

**(iii) Elastic energy per unit volume $U$:**

$$U = \frac{1}{2} \sigma \varepsilon = \frac{1}{2} \times (5 \times 10^{6}) \times (2.5 \times 10^{-2})$$

$$U = \frac{1}{2} \times 1.25 \times 10^{5} = 6.25 \times 10^{4} \; \text{J/m}^3$$

$$\boxed{U = 6.25 \times 10^{4} \; \text{J/m}^3}$$

**(iv) Total elastic energy $U_{total}$:**

The volume of the specimen is $V = A \times L_0 = 10^{-4} \times 0.1 = 10^{-5}$ m³.

$$U_{total} = U \times V = (6.25 \times 10^{4}) \times (10^{-5})$$

$$U_{total} = 6.25 \times 10^{-1} = 0.625 \; \text{J}$$

$$\boxed{U_{total} = 0.625 \; \text{J}}$$

*(7 marks: 2 for part (i), 2 for part (ii), 1.5 for part (iii), 1.5 for part (iv).)*

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]` — **14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** Two Hookean springs with moduli $E_1 = 1 \times 10^8$ Pa and $E_2 = 3 \times 10^8$ Pa (same area $A$ and length $L$) are connected in:
  (i) Series
  (ii) Parallel
  Derive the expression for the equivalent modulus in each case and compute the numerical values. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A constant stress $\sigma_0 = 4 \times 10^6$ Pa is suddenly applied to a pure Hookean spring with $E = 2 \times 10^8$ Pa.
  (i) Calculate the instantaneous strain.
  (ii) What happens to the strain after 10 seconds, 100 seconds, and 1000 seconds? Justify.
  (iii) Calculate the energy stored in the spring per unit volume. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Question B

**(a) Series and Parallel Combinations:**

**Case (i): Springs in Series**

The same force $F$ acts on both springs, and the total deformation is the sum:

$$\varepsilon_{total} = \varepsilon_1 + \varepsilon_2 = \frac{\sigma}{E_1} + \frac{\sigma}{E_2} = \sigma \left( \frac{1}{E_1} + \frac{1}{E_2} \right)$$

Defining $\varepsilon_{total} = \sigma / E_{eq}$:

$$\frac{1}{E_{eq}} = \frac{1}{E_1} + \frac{1}{E_2}$$

**Numerical value:**

$$\frac{1}{E_{eq}} = \frac{1}{1 \times 10^{8}} + \frac{1}{3 \times 10^{8}} = \frac{3 + 1}{3 \times 10^{8}} = \frac{4}{3 \times 10^{8}}$$

$$\boxed{E_{eq} = 0.75 \times 10^{8} = 7.5 \times 10^{7} \; \text{Pa}}$$

**Case (ii): Springs in Parallel**

The same strain $\varepsilon$ acts on both springs, and the total force is the sum:

$$F_{total} = F_1 + F_2 = E_1 A \varepsilon + E_2 A \varepsilon = (E_1 + E_2) A \varepsilon$$

Defining $F_{total} = E_{eq} A \varepsilon$:

$$E_{eq} = E_1 + E_2$$

**Numerical value:**

$$E_{eq} = 1 \times 10^{8} + 3 \times 10^{8} = 4 \times 10^{8} \; \text{Pa}$$

$$\boxed{E_{eq} = 4 \times 10^{8} \; \text{Pa}}$$

*(7 marks: 2 for series derivation, 1 for series numerical, 2 for parallel derivation, 1 for parallel numerical, 1 for correct identification of formulas.)*

---

**(b) Pure Spring Under Constant Stress:**

Given: $\sigma_0 = 4 \times 10^6$ Pa, $E = 2 \times 10^8$ Pa.

**(i) Instantaneous strain:**

$$\varepsilon_0 = \frac{\sigma_0}{E} = \frac{4 \times 10^{6}}{2 \times 10^{8}} = 2 \times 10^{-2}$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_0 = 0.02 \quad \text{(dimensionless)}}$$

**(ii) Strain at later times:**

Since the spring obeys $\varepsilon(t) = \sigma_0 / E$ at *all* times:

- At $t = 10$ s: $\varepsilon = 0.02$
- At $t = 100$ s: $\varepsilon = 0.02$
- At $t = 1000$ s: $\varepsilon = 0.02$

**Justification:** A pure Hookean spring has a **time-independent** constitutive equation. There is no viscous flow mechanism (no dashpot), so the strain remains constant once the stress is applied. The creep compliance is $J(t) = 1/E$, a constant.

*(4 marks: 1 for calculation, 1.5 for stating values, 1.5 for physical justification.)*

**(iii) Energy stored per unit volume:**

$$U = \frac{1}{2} E \varepsilon_0^{2} = \frac{1}{2} \times (2 \times 10^{8}) \times (2 \times 10^{-2})^{2}$$

$$U = \frac{1}{2} \times 2 \times 10^{8} \times 4 \times 10^{-4} = 4 \times 10^{4} \; \text{J/m}^3$$

$$\boxed{U = 4 \times 10^{4} \; \text{J/m}^3}$$

*(3 marks for energy calculation.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in KTU exams:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing creep compliance with relaxation modulus** — $J(t) = 1/E$ is the *strain response to unit stress*, while $G(t) = E$ is the *stress response to unit strain*. They are not interchangeable.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting units** — KTU examiners *will* deduct 0.5–1 mark for missing or wrong units (Pa, N/m, J/m³).
> 
> 3. **Writing $J(t) = t/E$ or $G(t) = E \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$ for a pure spring** — these belong to dashpots and Maxwell models, respectively. The pure spring has **no time dependence**.
> 
> 4. **Series vs. parallel mix-up** — Series: $1/E$ adds. Parallel: $E$ adds. This is the opposite of how resistances combine in electrical circuits. Memorize carefully.
> 
> 5. **Sketches without labels** — When asked to sketch $J(t)$ or $G(t)$, *always* label axes, mark the constant value ($1/E$ or $E$), and indicate the origin.
> 
> 6. **Mixing up energy formulas** — All three forms $\left(\frac{1}{2}E\varepsilon^2 = \frac{1}{2}\sigma\varepsilon = \frac{\sigma^2}{2E}\right)$ are valid. Choose the one that uses quantities *already given* in the problem.
> 
> 7. **Forgetting to mention "instantaneous"** — Always emphasize that the spring's response is *time-independent*. This is the single most important characteristic of a Hookean element.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Spring Element (KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 3)**

- ☐ **Hooke's Law:** $\sigma = E\varepsilon$ — the *defining equation* of a Hookean spring.
- ☐ **Young's Modulus $E$** measures material stiffness; higher $E$ = stiffer polymer.
- ☐ **Spring constant ↔ Modulus:** $k = E \cdot A / L_0$ (converts between mechanical and material descriptions).
- ☐ **Creep compliance:** $J(t) = 1/E$ — **constant in time** (horizontal line).
- ☐ **Relaxation modulus:** $G(t) = E$ — **constant in time** (horizontal line).
- ☐ **No creep, no relaxation** — the pure spring's signature.
- ☐ **Energy stored:** $U = \frac{1}{2}E\varepsilon^2 = \frac{1}{2}\sigma\varepsilon = \frac{\sigma^2}{2E}$ (all three forms equivalent).
- ☐ **Springs in series:** $1/E_{eq} = \sum (1/E_i)$ — compliances add.
- ☐ **Springs in parallel:** $E_{eq} = \sum E_i$ — stiffnesses add.
- ☐ **Response is instantaneous** — no time delay, no viscous flow, no energy dissipation.
- ☐ **Spring = elastic personality of polymer**; combine with dashpot to model full viscoelastic behavior.
- ☐ **Units to remember:** $E$ in Pa, $k$ in N/m, $J$ in Pa⁻¹, $U$ in J/m³.
- ☐ **KTU exam hot topics:** derivations of $J(t)$ and $G(t)$, series/parallel combinations, energy calculations.
- ☐ **Contrast point:** A pure spring alone *cannot* represent a real polymer — it must be combined with a dashpot in Maxwell or Kelvin-Voigt configuration.
- ☐ **Real-world link:** Cross-linked rubber at small strains; elastic matrix in composites; arterial wall response.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dashpot
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT402 - POLYMER PHYSICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Dashpot

# Module 3: Mechanical Models in Polymer Physics — **The Dashpot**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** This topic is foundational to understanding **viscoelasticity** in polymers. Mastery of the dashpot element is mandatory before progressing to combined models like the **Maxwell Model**, **Kelvin–Voigt Model**, and the **Standard Linear Solid Model** in subsequent module sections.

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition — Dashpot (Newtonian Dashpot):**
> A *dashpot* is an idealized mechanical model element that represents **purely viscous (purely dissipative) response** in a material. It is mathematically equivalent to a **Newtonian fluid** confined between two plates, where the applied stress is linearly proportional to the rate of strain (strain rate). The dashpot is the viscous counterpart of the **Hookean spring** (which models elasticity), and together they form the building blocks of all linear viscoelastic models.

**Symbol used in KTU textbooks:** A dashpot is conventionally drawn as a **piston moving inside a cylinder filled with a viscous fluid** (often oil or a polymer melt). The piston can move axially, but the fluid resists the motion via internal friction (viscosity).

### 1.2 Constitutive Equation (Newton's Law of Viscosity for a Dashpot)

For a **Newtonian dashpot** operating in simple shear (or uniaxial extension in 1D analog form), the governing equation is:

$$\sigma(t) = \eta \, \frac{d\varepsilon(t)}{dt}$$

where:
- $\sigma(t)$ is the **stress** (Pa, N/m²) applied to the dashpot at time $t$
- $\eta$ is the **coefficient of viscosity** (Pa·s) — a material constant for a Newtonian fluid
- $\frac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = \dot{\varepsilon}$ is the **strain rate** (s⁻¹)
- $\varepsilon$ is the dimensionless **strain**

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** The dashpot is the **time-dependent** mechanical element. Unlike a spring (which responds instantly to stress), a dashpot's strain is *history-dependent* — it accumulates with time as long as stress is applied. This is the key reason polymers (which behave partly like dashpots) exhibit time-dependent deformation.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Shock Absorber"

Imagine the **shock absorber (damper) of a car**:

- When you push the piston rod into the cylinder, the oil inside resists the motion.
- The harder (or faster) you push, the more force the damper resists with.
- If you let go, the piston stays exactly where you left it — it does **not** return to its original position on its own.
- The energy you put in is permanently **dissipated as heat** (through viscous friction in the oil).

**Mapping to the polymer dashpot:**
- The car's chassis represents the **stress** applied to the polymer.
- The piston's slow movement represents the **strain rate** of the polymer chains sliding past one another.
- The oil's resistance represents the **viscosity $\eta$** of the polymer melt or concentrated solution.
- The energy lost as heat represents the **irreversible viscous dissipation** in the polymer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Polymer Examples of Dashpot-Like Behavior:**
> - **Polymer melts** (e.g., molten polyethylene) above their glass transition and melting temperatures.
> - **Polymer solutions** above the entanglement concentration.
> - **Unvulcanized rubber** at high temperatures — flows like a viscous liquid.
> - **Tar, pitch, and bitumen** at room temperature — extremely high-viscosity "natural dashpots."

---

## 1.4 Mechanical Symbol in KTU Diagrams

In KTU-approved schematic diagrams, the dashpot is represented by:

```
    |  |        <- piston rod (rigid)
    |  |
   ┌────┐
   │    │     <- cylinder filled with viscous fluid
   │~~~~│     <- fluid (oil) indicated by wavy lines
   └────┘
```

In simplified block-diagram form (used in KTU 2024 module notes), the dashpot is represented as:

```
   σ → [ ▭ ] → ε
       (η)
```

where the box labeled with $\eta$ represents the viscous element.

### 1.5 Distinction: Dashpot vs. Spring (Critical for KTU)

| Property | Hookean Spring | Newtonian Dashpot |
|---|---|---|
| **Element type** | Elastic (energy-storing) | Viscous (energy-dissipating) |
| **Constitutive law** | $\sigma = E \, \varepsilon$ | $\sigma = \eta \, \dot{\varepsilon}$ |
| **Response to step stress** | Instantaneous strain | Linearly increasing strain (creep) |
| **Response to step strain** | Constant stress | Exponentially decaying stress (relaxation) |
| **Energy** | Stored (recoverable) | Dissipated (lost as heat) |
| **Recoverability** | Fully recoverable | Not recoverable |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway for KTU Board Exam:** A **polymer** is neither a pure spring nor a pure dashpot — it is a **viscoelastic** combination of both. The dashpot is the element responsible for the **time-dependent (anelastic)** part of polymer deformation.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principles of the Dashpot — The Underlying Physics

To understand *why* a dashpot behaves the way it does, we need to understand the **microscopic origin of viscosity in polymers**.

### 2.1.1 Microscopic Origin of Viscosity

In a polymer melt or concentrated solution:
1. **Polymer chains are long, entangled, and constantly in motion** (Brownian motion + reptation in the tube model of de Gennes).
2. When a stress is applied, chains must **slide past one another** to accommodate the deformation.
3. This sliding requires chains to "unwind" or "reptate" out of their entanglements — a process that takes **finite time** (the terminal relaxation time $\tau$).
4. The resistance to this sliding motion is the **viscosity $\eta$**.

For a **Newtonian fluid**, this resistance is *independent of strain rate* — a hallmark of simple liquids like water, glycerol, and dilute polymer solutions. For **non-Newtonian polymeric fluids**, $\eta$ itself depends on $\dot{\varepsilon}$ (shear-thinning, shear-thickening), but the *dashpot element* in KTU's introductory viscoelasticity is always **Newtonian**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Scope Note:** Within Module 3 of PCPOT402, the dashpot is treated strictly as a **Newtonian dashpot** with constant viscosity $\eta$. Non-Newtonian dashpots (e.g., power-law, Bingham plastic) are outside the scope of this module and are introduced in higher electives.

### 2.2 The Two Canonical Experiments for Characterizing a Dashpot

In KTU examinations, you will frequently be asked to analyze the dashpot's response to:

#### (a) Creep Experiment (Constant Stress)
- Apply a constant stress $\sigma_0$ at $t = 0$ and hold it.
- **For a dashpot:** $\sigma_0 = \eta \dot{\varepsilon}$ → $\dot{\varepsilon} = \sigma_0 / \eta$ = constant.
- Strain grows **linearly with time**:
$$\varepsilon(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta} \, t$$
- No recovery occurs when the stress is removed — the strain is **permanent** (irrecoverable).

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid:** A dashpot under creep looks like a **ramp function** in the strain-vs-time plot — a straight line through the origin with slope $\sigma_0 / \eta$.

#### (b) Stress Relaxation Experiment (Constant Strain)
- Apply an instantaneous strain $\varepsilon_0$ at $t = 0$ and hold it.
- **For a dashpot:** To maintain constant strain, the strain rate must be **zero** at $t > 0$.
- Since $\sigma = \eta \dot{\varepsilon}$ and $\dot{\varepsilon} = 0$, we get $\sigma = 0$ for all $t > 0$.
- Physically: any stress that would build up is immediately relaxed by viscous flow.
- Result: **No stress is sustained** by a pure dashpot under constant strain.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** This is the **defining feature** of a pure dashpot — it **cannot sustain a constant stress under constant strain**. This is why real polymers (which are partly dashpots) show **partial** stress relaxation, not complete relaxation. The Maxwell model (spring + dashpot in series) is designed exactly to capture this partial relaxation behavior.

### 2.3 Energy Analysis for a Dashpot

For a Newtonian dashpot, the work done per unit volume (energy density input) over a time interval $t$ under constant stress $\sigma_0$ is:

$$W = \int_0^t \sigma \, \dot{\varepsilon} \, dt' = \int_0^t \sigma_0 \cdot \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta} \, dt' = \frac{\sigma_0^2 \, t}{\eta}$$

- This energy is **entirely dissipated as heat**.
- A spring, by contrast, stores energy as $U = \frac{1}{2} E \varepsilon^2$ and recovers it on unloading.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Implication:** Repeated cyclic loading of a polymeric part (e.g., a tire, a polymer bushing, a viscoelastic damper) causes **heat buildup** due to viscous dissipation. This is the principle behind **viscoelastic dampers** used in civil and aerospace engineering for vibration suppression.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below contains **all** the high-yield equations for the dashpot topic in Module 3. Memorize these for the KTU ESE (End Semester Examination).

| # | Equation Name | Mathematical Form | Variables & Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Newton's Law of Viscosity (Dashpot Constitutive Law)** | $\sigma = \eta \, \dot{\varepsilon}$ | $\sigma$ = stress (Pa), $\eta$ = viscosity (Pa·s), $\dot{\varepsilon}$ = strain rate (s⁻¹) | Fundamental equation of a Newtonian dashpot |
| 2 | **Dashpot Creep Response (constant stress $\sigma_0$)** | $\varepsilon(t) = \dfrac{\sigma_0}{\eta} \, t$ | Strain is linear in time | Strain accumulates indefinitely under load |
| 3 | **Dashpot Creep Compliance** | $J(t) = \dfrac{\varepsilon(t)}{\sigma_0} = \dfrac{t}{\eta}$ | $J(t)$ = creep compliance (Pa⁻¹) | Linearly increasing compliance |
| 4 | **Dashpot Stress Relaxation (constant strain $\varepsilon_0$)** | $\sigma(t) = 0$ for $t > 0$ | — | Dashpot cannot sustain stress at constant strain |
| 5 | **Strain Energy Density Dissipated** | $W_{diss} = \dfrac{\sigma_0^2 \, t}{\eta}$ | Energy per unit volume (J/m³) | All input energy is lost as heat |
| 6 | **Dashpot Retardation Time Definition** | $\tau_{\eta} = \dfrac{\eta}{E_{ref}}$ (when paired with spring of modulus $E_{ref}$) | s (seconds) | Characteristic time scale of viscous flow |
| 7 | **Dashpot Dynamic Modulus (complex form)** | $E^{*}_{dashpot} = i \omega \eta$ | Pa, with $\omega$ = angular frequency (rad/s) | Out-of-phase (90°) response in oscillatory shear |
| 8 | **Dashpot Loss Modulus** | $E'' = \omega \eta$ | Pa | Energy dissipation per cycle |
| 9 | **Dashpot Storage Modulus** | $E' = 0$ | Pa | A pure dashpot stores **no** elastic energy |
| 10 | **Newtonian Viscosity Definition** | $\eta = \dfrac{\sigma}{\dot{\varepsilon}}$ | Pa·s | Ratio of stress to strain rate |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** Notice that **Equation 7** shows the dashpot has only an **imaginary** component of complex modulus — this means it is **90° out of phase** with the applied strain. A spring (Equation not in this sheet) has only a **real** component — 0° phase. Polymer viscoelasticity arises from the **interference** of these two phase responses.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility of the Dashpot Concept

| Engineering Field | Application |
|---|---|
| **Polymer Processing** | Injection molding, extrusion, blow molding — all require knowledge of polymer melt viscosity to design dies, gates, and processing windows. |
| **Tire Design** | Tread compounds are viscoelastic; the dashpot component is responsible for **hysteresis heating** and **wet grip** behavior. |
| **Vibration Damping** | Viscoelastic elastomer mounts in automotive and aerospace use dashpot-like dissipation to suppress vibrations. |
| **Biomedical Polymers** | Hydrogels used in tissue engineering exhibit dashpot-like creep under sustained physiological loads. |
| **Packaging** | Polymer films under long-term stacking loads creep (dashpot response), causing package deformation — must be designed for time-dependent compliance. |
| **Adhesives** | Pressure-sensitive adhesives (PSAs) require the right balance of dashpot (tack) and spring (cohesion) behavior. |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Creep Response of a Newtonian Dashpot

**Problem Statement:** A Newtonian dashpot of viscosity $\eta$ is subjected to a step stress $\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \, H(t)$, where $H(t)$ is the Heaviside step function. Derive the strain response $\varepsilon(t)$ for $t \geq 0$.

**Step 1 — Write the constitutive equation of the dashpot:**

$$\sigma(t) = \eta \, \frac{d\varepsilon(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the applied step stress:**

$$\sigma_0 = \eta \, \frac{d\varepsilon(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 3 — Separate variables and rearrange:**

$$\frac{d\varepsilon(t)}{dt} = \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta}$$

**Step 4 — Integrate both sides from $0$ to $t$:**

$$\int_0^{t} \frac{d\varepsilon(t')}{dt'} \, dt' = \int_0^{t} \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta} \, dt'$$

**Step 5 — Apply the initial condition $\varepsilon(0) = 0$ (no prior deformation):**

$$\varepsilon(t) - \varepsilon(0) = \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta} \cdot t - 0$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta} \, t}$$

**Step 6 — Define the creep compliance:**

$$J(t) = \frac{\varepsilon(t)}{\sigma_0} = \frac{t}{\eta}$$

This shows that the creep compliance of a pure dashpot is a **linearly increasing function of time** — its slope is $1/\eta$, so a more viscous fluid creeps more slowly.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Stress Relaxation Response of a Newtonian Dashpot

**Problem Statement:** A Newtonian dashpot of viscosity $\eta$ is subjected to a step strain $\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_0 \, H(t)$. Derive the stress response $\sigma(t)$ for $t \geq 0$.

**Step 1 — Write the constitutive equation of the dashpot:**
