$$\sigma(t) = \eta \, \frac{d\varepsilon(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the applied step strain:**

For $t < 0$: $\varepsilon(t) = 0$, so $\dot{\varepsilon}(t) = 0$, and $\sigma(t) = 0$.

For $t = 0^+$: The strain jumps from 0 to $\varepsilon_0$ instantaneously, so $\dot{\varepsilon}(0^+) = \varepsilon_0 \, \delta(t)$ (a Dirac delta spike).

For $t > 0$: The strain is held constant at $\varepsilon_0$, so:

$$\frac{d\varepsilon(t)}{dt} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Apply the constitutive law for $t > 0$:**

$$\boxed{\sigma(t) = \eta \cdot 0 = 0 \quad \text{for } t > 0}$$

**Step 4 — Physical interpretation:**

A pure Newtonian dashpot **cannot sustain any stress** under a constant applied strain. The instant the strain is imposed, viscous flow immediately relaxes any internal stress. This is the **defining behavioral signature** of a viscous element.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mathematical subtlety:** Technically, an impulsive stress of magnitude $\eta \, \varepsilon_0 \, \delta(t)$ is required at $t = 0$ to instantaneously impose the strain — this is the "impulse" that initiates the viscous flow. For $t > 0$, however, the stress is identically zero.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Dynamic (Oscillatory) Response of a Newtonian Dashpot

**Problem Statement:** A Newtonian dashpot is subjected to an oscillatory strain $\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_0 \, e^{i \omega t}$, where $\omega$ is the angular frequency. Derive the complex modulus $E^* = \sigma(t)/\varepsilon(t)$.

**Step 1 — Compute the strain rate:**

$$\dot{\varepsilon}(t) = \frac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = i \omega \, \varepsilon_0 \, e^{i \omega t} = i \omega \, \varepsilon(t)$$

**Step 2 — Apply the constitutive law:**

$$\sigma(t) = \eta \, \dot{\varepsilon}(t) = \eta \cdot i \omega \, \varepsilon(t) = i \omega \eta \, \varepsilon(t)$$

**Step 3 — Identify the complex modulus:**

$$E^*_{dashpot} = \frac{\sigma(t)}{\varepsilon(t)} = i \omega \eta$$

**Step 4 — Decompose into storage and loss moduli:**

$$E^* = E' + i E'' = 0 + i \omega \eta$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{E' = 0, \quad E'' = \omega \eta, \quad \tan \delta = \frac{E''}{E'} = \infty}$$

**Step 5 — Physical interpretation:**

- The **storage modulus $E' = 0$**: a pure dashpot stores **no elastic energy** (purely dissipative).
- The **loss modulus $E'' = \omega \eta$**: energy dissipation increases linearly with frequency.
- The **phase angle $\delta = 90°$**: stress leads strain by 90° (a quarter cycle).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Concept Bridge:** When you later study the Maxwell model (spring + dashpot in series), you will see that $E^* = \frac{i \omega \eta \cdot E}{E + i \omega \eta}$, which has **both** real and imaginary parts. The **fraction** of $E'$ vs. $E''$ determines how "solid-like" or "liquid-like" the polymer is at a given frequency.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Dashpot Response Summary

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Table for KTU Board Exam — Print & Memorize This:**

| Test Type | Input Function | Output Function | Plot Shape | Energy Behavior |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Creep (constant $\sigma_0$)** | $\sigma(t) = \sigma_0$ | $\varepsilon(t) = \dfrac{\sigma_0}{\eta} \, t$ | Linear ramp | All input energy dissipated |
| **Stress Relaxation (constant $\varepsilon_0$)** | $\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_0$ | $\sigma(t) = 0$ for $t > 0$ | Zero (after delta impulse) | No sustained stress |
| **Strain Rate Step (constant $\dot{\varepsilon}_0$)** | $\dot{\varepsilon}(t) = \dot{\varepsilon}_0$ | $\sigma(t) = \eta \, \dot{\varepsilon}_0$ | Constant stress (ramp in strain) | Continuous dissipation |
| **Oscillatory (steady-state)** | $\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_0 e^{i\omega t}$ | $\sigma(t) = i\omega\eta \, \varepsilon_0 e^{i\omega t}$ | Ellipse in $\sigma$–$\varepsilon$ (purely imaginary) | All energy dissipated per cycle |

---

## 3.5 Derivation 4: Comparison of Energy Stored vs. Energy Dissipated in a Loading Cycle

**Problem Statement:** Compare the energy stored per cycle in a Hookean spring (modulus $E$) to the energy dissipated per cycle in a Newtonian dashpot (viscosity $\eta$) when both are subjected to a strain cycle of amplitude $\varepsilon_0$ at angular frequency $\omega$.

**For the spring (energy stored, recoverable):**

$$W_{spring} = \frac{1}{2} E \, \varepsilon_0^2$$

This is independent of frequency.

**For the dashpot (energy dissipated, lost as heat):**

The stress in the dashpot under oscillatory strain is $\sigma(t) = \eta \dot{\varepsilon}(t) = i \omega \eta \, \varepsilon_0 \, e^{i \omega t}$.

The energy dissipated per cycle per unit volume is:

$$W_{dashpot} = \oint \sigma \, d\varepsilon = \int_0^{2\pi/\omega} \sigma(t) \, \dot{\varepsilon}(t) \, dt$$

**Compute the integrand:**

$$\sigma(t) \, \dot{\varepsilon}(t) = (i \omega \eta \, \varepsilon_0 e^{i\omega t}) \cdot (i \omega \, \varepsilon_0 e^{i\omega t}) = -\omega^2 \eta \, \varepsilon_0^2 \, e^{2 i \omega t}$$

**Take the real part (physical energy):**

$$\text{Re}[\sigma \, \dot{\varepsilon}] = -\omega^2 \eta \, \varepsilon_0^2 \cos(2\omega t)$$

**Integrate over one cycle $T = 2\pi/\omega$:**

$$W_{dashpot} = \int_0^{2\pi/\omega} -\omega^2 \eta \, \varepsilon_0^2 \cos(2\omega t) \, dt$$

$$W_{dashpot} = -\omega^2 \eta \, \varepsilon_0^2 \cdot \left[\frac{\sin(2\omega t)}{2\omega}\right]_0^{2\pi/\omega}$$

$$W_{dashpot} = -\omega^2 \eta \, \varepsilon_0^2 \cdot \frac{\sin(4\pi) - \sin(0)}{2\omega} = 0$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Wait — this gives zero?** The integral of $\cos(2\omega t)$ over a full cycle is indeed zero. This is because we computed the *net* work, which oscillates. The correct quantity is the **average power dissipated per cycle**, which equals the area of the **stress–strain hysteresis loop**.

**Correct computation — area of the elliptical hysteresis loop:**

For a dashpot with complex modulus $E^* = i\omega\eta$, the loss modulus is $E'' = \omega\eta$. The energy dissipated per cycle is:

$$\boxed{W_{diss} = \pi \, E'' \, \varepsilon_0^2 = \pi \, \omega \eta \, \varepsilon_0^2}$$

This is the **standard formula** used in polymer rheology to quantify viscous heating per cycle.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight for KTU 2024 Board Exam:** In a **viscoelastic polymer** (modeled by combining springs and dashpots), the energy dissipated per cycle is $\pi E'' \varepsilon_0^2$ and the energy stored is $\frac{1}{2} E' \varepsilon_0^2$. The ratio is the **loss tangent**: $\tan \delta = E''/E'$. This single number characterizes how "rubbery" or "glassy" a polymer is at a given frequency.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: The Dashpot in a Functional Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional architecture of the dashpot** in the context of polymer mechanical modeling, showing how it interfaces with inputs, outputs, and the broader viscoelastic model hierarchy.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inputA[Applied Stress sigma] --> nodeDisp[Displacement Input]
    inputB[Applied Strain epsilon] --> nodeStrain[Strain Input]
    nodeDisp --> dashpotCore
    nodeStrain --> dashpotCore
    dashpotCore{{Dashpot Element viscosity eta}}
    dashpotCore --> branch1{Constitutive Law}
    branch1 --> path1[sigma equals eta times d epsilon by dt]
    branch1 --> path2[Creep epsilon linear in t]
    branch1 --> path3[Stress relaxation sigma equals 0]
    dashpotCore --> branch2{Energy Behavior}
    branch2 --> path4[Energy Dissipated as Heat]
    branch2 --> path5[No Energy Stored]
    dashpotCore --> branch3{Phase Response}
    branch3 --> path6[Oscillatory 90 degree phase lag]
    branch3 --> path7[Storage Modulus E prime equals 0]
    branch3 --> path8[Loss Modulus E double prime equals omega eta]
    dashpotCore --> combineA[Series Combination Maxwell Model]
    dashpotCore --> combineB[Parallel Combination Kelvin Voigt Model]
    combineA --> outputA[Stress Relaxation Modulus]
    combineB --> outputB[Creep Compliance]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Guide:** The central diamond `{{Dashpot Element}}` is the single canonical element. Its three branches (Constitutive Law, Energy Behavior, Phase Response) capture the three essential aspects tested in KTU examinations. The bottom two output nodes (Maxwell, Kelvin–Voigt) are previews of the combined models you will study in the next sub-sections of Module 3.

---

## 4.2 Schematic: Dashpot Symbol vs. Spring Symbol (Side-by-Side)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HookeanSpring[Hookean Spring Element]
        springInput[Stress sigma] --> springBox[Spring constant E]
        springBox --> springOutput[Strain epsilon equals sigma by E]
    end
    subgraph NewtonianDashpot[Newtonian Dashpot Element]
        dashpotInput[Stress sigma] --> dashpotBox[Viscosity eta]
        dashpotBox --> dashpotOutput[Strain rate d epsilon by dt equals sigma by eta]
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Side-by-Side Comparison:** The spring and dashpot are the two "atomic" elements of linear viscoelasticity. Every linear viscoelastic model (Maxwell, Kelvin–Voigt, Standard Linear Solid, Burgers, Generalized Maxwell) is built by connecting one or more springs and dashpots in series and/or parallel.

---

## 4.3 Functional Block Diagram: Dashpot in a Creep Experiment

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepInput[Step Stress sigma naught at t equals 0] --> dashpotNode[Dashpot with viscosity eta]
    dashpotNode --> derivBlock[Strain rate d epsilon by dt equals sigma naught by eta]
    derivBlock --> integrator[Integrate over time t]
    integrator --> rampOut[Strain epsilon of t equals sigma naught times t divided by eta]
    rampOut --> plotNode[Ramp Function Linear in Time]
    plotNode --> outputA[No Recovery on Unloading]
    plotNode --> outputB[Permanent Viscous Flow]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Reading:** This is the **block-diagram representation of a creep test** on a Newtonian dashpot. The integrator block is the **mathematical core** that converts strain rate into cumulative strain. The output (ramp function) is the **signature** of pure viscous behavior — if you see a linear ramp in a creep experiment, the material is behaving as a pure dashpot in that time window.

---

## 4.4 Schematic: Combined Model Preview (For Module 3 Continuity)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MaxwellModel[Maxwell Model Spring and Dashpot in Series]
        m1[Spring with E] --- m2[Dashpot with eta]
        m2 --> mOut[Common Strain epsilon]
    end
    subgraph KelvinVoigtModel[Kelvin Voigt Model Spring and Dashpot in Parallel]
        k1[Spring with E] --- k2[Dashpot with eta]
        k1 --> kOut[Common Stress sigma]
        k2 --> kOut
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Preview Note:** In the **Maxwell model**, the spring and dashpot share the **same stress** (series connection), but their strains add. In the **Kelvin–Voigt model**, they share the **same strain** (parallel connection), but their stresses add. These are the two foundational combined models of linear viscoelasticity that you will study in the next sub-sections of Module 3.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls for the Dashpot Topic:**
> 1. **Confusing $\eta$ with $E$:** Students often write the dashpot law as $\sigma = E \dot{\varepsilon}$ (mixing up the spring and dashpot). **Correct form:** $\sigma = \eta \dot{\varepsilon}$.
> 2. **Forgetting the time-dependence:** A dashpot's strain is *always* time-dependent. Writing $\varepsilon = \sigma/\eta$ (without the $t$) is a major error.
> 3. **Assuming the dashpot recovers elastically:** It does **not**. A pure dashpot shows **permanent deformation** — no recovery upon unloading.
> 4. **Mixing up creep and stress relaxation:** Creep = constant stress → ramp strain. Stress relaxation = constant strain → decaying stress. For a *pure dashpot*, the stress relaxation response is identically zero (for $t > 0$).
> 5. **Ignoring the constitutive equation derivation:** KTU ESE 14-mark questions often ask you to *derive* the creep/relaxation response. Skipping integration steps costs 3–5 marks.

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**"Define a Newtonian dashpot. Write its constitutive equation and explain the physical significance of viscosity in the context of polymer flow."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A Newtonian dashpot is an idealized mechanical element that represents purely viscous behavior in a material, equivalent to a Newtonian fluid confined between two plates.

**Constitutive equation:**

$$\sigma(t) = \eta \, \frac{d\varepsilon(t)}{dt}$$

where $\sigma$ is the applied stress, $\eta$ is the coefficient of viscosity (Pa·s), and $\dot{\varepsilon}$ is the strain rate.

**Physical significance of viscosity in polymers:**
Viscosity $\eta$ quantifies the **internal resistance of a polymer to flow**. In a polymer melt, chain entanglements and intermolecular forces resist the sliding of chains past one another, giving rise to macroscopic viscosity. A higher $\eta$ means the polymer flows more slowly under the same stress. It governs polymer processing behavior in extrusion, injection molding, and blow molding, and determines the polymer's response time in creep and stress relaxation experiments. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]
**"Compare the responses of a Hookean spring and a Newtonian dashpot to a creep test (constant applied stress)."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Hookean Spring | Newtonian Dashpot |
|---|---|---|
| **Constitutive law** | $\sigma = E \varepsilon$ | $\sigma = \eta \dot{\varepsilon}$ |
| **Creep strain response** | $\varepsilon(t) = \sigma_0 / E$ (instantaneous, constant) | $\varepsilon(t) = (\sigma_0 / \eta) \, t$ (linear in time) |
| **Time-dependence** | Time-independent | Time-dependent |
| **Recovery on unloading** | Fully recovers (elastic) | No recovery (permanent viscous flow) |
| **Energy** | Stored elastically, fully recovered | Dissipated as heat, not recovered |

**Conclusion:** The spring responds instantaneously and recovers fully; the dashpot responds continuously (linearly) with time and shows no recovery. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — Model Paper]

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
**(i)** Derive the **creep compliance** of a Newtonian dashpot subjected to a step stress $\sigma_0$.

**(ii)** A polymer sample behaves as a Newtonian dashpot with viscosity $\eta = 5 \times 10^{4}$ Pa·s. If a constant stress of $\sigma_0 = 10^{5}$ Pa is applied, calculate the strain after $t = 100$ s.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
**(i)** Show that a Newtonian dashpot **cannot sustain a constant stress** under an applied constant strain. Derive the stress relaxation response.

**(ii)** Calculate the **energy dissipated per unit volume** by this dashpot during a creep test of duration $t = 60$ s under the stress from Part (a)(ii). Comment on the fate of this energy.

---

**Model Solution — Question A:**

#### Part (a)(i) — Creep Compliance Derivation (5 Marks)

**Step 1:** Write the dashpot constitutive law:
$$\sigma(t) = \eta \, \frac{d\varepsilon(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the step stress $\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \, H(t)$. For $t > 0$:
$$\sigma_0 = \eta \, \frac{d\varepsilon}{dt}$$

**Step 3:** Separate and integrate:
$$\frac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \varepsilon(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta} \, t + C$$

**Step 4:** Apply initial condition $\varepsilon(0) = 0$, giving $C = 0$:
$$\varepsilon(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta} \, t$$

**Step 5:** Creep compliance is strain divided by applied stress:
$$J(t) = \frac{\varepsilon(t)}{\sigma_0} = \frac{t}{\eta}$$

This is a **linearly increasing function of time** with slope $1/\eta$. **(5 marks)**

#### Part (a)(ii) — Numerical Calculation (2 Marks)

$$\varepsilon(100) = \frac{10^5}{5 \times 10^4} \times 100 = 2 \times 100 = 200$$

Wait — this gives a strain of 200, which is unphysically large. The problem implies a creep test that has not yet reached steady-state (which for a dashpot is never). Let me recompute:

$$\varepsilon(100\,\text{s}) = \frac{\sigma_0 \, t}{\eta} = \frac{(10^5\,\text{Pa}) \times (100\,\text{s})}{5 \times 10^4\,\text{Pa·s}} = \frac{10^7}{5 \times 10^4} = 200$$

The strain is **dimensionless = 200** (i.e., 20,000%). This is mathematically correct but physically unrealizable — a real polymer would either flow catastrophically or transition to non-Newtonian behavior. The point is to illustrate that a pure dashpot has **no limiting strain**. **(2 marks)**

#### Part (b)(i) — Stress Relaxation Derivation (5 Marks)

**Step 1:** Apply step strain $\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_0 \, H(t)$.

**Step 2:** Compute the strain rate:
- For $t < 0$: $\dot{\varepsilon} = 0$
- For $t = 0$: $\dot{\varepsilon} = \varepsilon_0 \, \delta(t)$ (impulsive)
- For $t > 0$: $\dot{\varepsilon} = 0$ (strain held constant)

**Step 3:** Apply the dashpot constitutive law for $t > 0$:
$$\sigma(t) = \eta \cdot \dot{\varepsilon} = \eta \cdot 0 = 0$$

**Conclusion:** A pure Newtonian dashpot **cannot sustain any stress** at constant strain — for $t > 0$, the stress is identically zero. The viscous flow immediately relaxes any internal stress the moment the strain is held constant. **(5 marks)**

#### Part (b)(ii) — Energy Dissipated Calculation (2 Marks)

Energy dissipated per unit volume during the creep test:
$$W_{diss} = \frac{\sigma_0^2 \, t}{\eta} = \frac{(10^5)^2 \times 60}{5 \times 10^4} = \frac{10^{10} \times 60}{5 \times 10^4} = \frac{6 \times 10^{11}}{5 \times 10^4} = 1.2 \times 10^{7}\,\text{J/m}^3$$

**Fate of energy:** The energy is **entirely dissipated as heat** through viscous friction between the polymer chains as they slide past one another. In a real polymer processing scenario, this heat would raise the temperature of the polymer, potentially lowering its viscosity (non-Newtonian effect) or causing thermal degradation. **(2 marks)**

**Total for Question A: 14 marks**

---

### Question B — 14 Marks [Alternative Module Internal Choice]

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
**(i)** A Newtonian dashpot is subjected to an **oscillatory strain** $\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_0 \cos(\omega t)$. Derive the **complex modulus** $E^* = \sigma(t)/\varepsilon(t)$ and identify the storage modulus $E'$ and loss modulus $E''$.

**(ii)** Calculate the **energy dissipated per cycle** for $\varepsilon_0 = 0.01$, $\omega = 10$ rad/s, and $\eta = 10^{3}$ Pa·s.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
**(i)** Explain the **microscopic origin of viscosity** in a polymer melt, with reference to chain entanglements and reptation (de Gennes tube model).

**(ii)** A polymer melt has viscosity $\eta = 10^{4}$ Pa·s at 200°C. A capillary rheometer applies a constant stress of $\sigma_0 = 10^{3}$ Pa. Calculate the **volumetric flow rate** $Q$ in a capillary of radius $R = 1$ mm and length $L = 10$ cm, assuming laminar (Hagen–Poiseuille) flow.

---

**Model Solution — Question B:**

#### Part (a)(i) — Complex Modulus Derivation (5 Marks)

**Step 1:** Express the oscillatory strain in complex form:
$$\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_0 \, e^{i \omega t}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the strain rate:
$$\dot{\varepsilon}(t) = i \omega \, \varepsilon_0 \, e^{i \omega t} = i \omega \, \varepsilon(t)$$

**Step 3:** Apply the dashpot constitutive law:
$$\sigma(t) = \eta \, \dot{\varepsilon}(t) = i \omega \eta \, \varepsilon(t)$$

**Step 4:** Define the complex modulus:
$$E^* = \frac{\sigma(t)}{\varepsilon(t)} = i \omega \eta$$

**Step 5:** Decompose into real and imaginary parts:
$$E^* = \underbrace{0}_{E'} + i \underbrace{\omega \eta}_{E''}$$

**Conclusion:**
- **Storage modulus** $E' = 0$ — a pure dashpot stores no elastic energy.
- **Loss modulus** $E'' = \omega \eta$ — energy dissipation per cycle scales linearly with frequency.
- **Phase angle** $\delta = \tan^{-1}(E''/E') = 90°$ — stress leads strain by a quarter cycle. **(5 marks)**

#### Part (a)(ii) — Energy Dissipated Per Cycle (2 Marks)

For a pure dashpot, the energy dissipated per cycle is:
$$W_{diss} = \pi \, E'' \, \varepsilon_0^2 = \pi \, \omega \eta \, \varepsilon_0^2$$

$$W_{diss} = \pi \times 10 \times 10^3 \times (0.01)^2 = \pi \times 10^4 \times 10^{-4} = \pi \approx 3.14\,\text{J/m}^3$$

**Interpretation:** This is the energy converted to heat per cubic meter of polymer per oscillation cycle. At higher frequencies, viscous heating increases linearly. **(2 marks)**

#### Part (b)(i) — Microscopic Origin of Viscosity (4 Marks)

In a polymer melt or concentrated solution, **long-chain molecules are entangled** like a bowl of spaghetti. When stress is applied, chains must slide past one another for the polymer to flow. This sliding is hindered by:

1. **Entanglements:** Physical "knots" between chains that must be worked around. Each chain is confined to a **tube** (de Gennes reptation model) formed by the surrounding chains.

2. **Reptation:** The chain escapes its tube by **snakelike diffusion** along its own contour (de Gennes, 1971). This process is **thermally activated** and has a characteristic time scale (the **terminal reptation time $\tau_d$**).

3. **Intermolecular forces:** Van der Waals interactions, entanglements, and (in polar polymers) hydrogen bonds or dipole-dipole interactions resist chain motion.

The macroscopic viscosity $\eta$ is related to the reptation time by:
$$\eta \propto G_N^0 \, \tau_d$$
where $G_N^0$ is the **plateau modulus** (a measure of entanglement density). Polymers with longer chains have longer $\tau_d$ and hence higher $\eta$. **(4 marks)**

#### Part (b)(ii) — Hagen–Poiseuille Flow Calculation (3 Marks)

For laminar flow of a Newtonian fluid in a circular capillary under a pressure gradient, the **Hagen–Poiseuille equation** gives the volumetric flow rate:

$$Q = \frac{\pi R^4 \, \Delta P}{8 \eta L}$$

**Step 1:** Compute the pressure drop from the applied wall stress:
$$\sigma_0 = \frac{\Delta P \cdot R}{2L} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Delta P = \frac{2 \sigma_0 L}{R} = \frac{2 \times 10^3 \times 0.1}{10^{-3}} = 2 \times 10^5\,\text{Pa}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into the Hagen–Poiseuille equation:
$$Q = \frac{\pi (10^{-3})^4 \times 2 \times 10^5}{8 \times 10^4 \times 0.1}$$

$$Q = \frac{\pi \times 10^{-12} \times 2 \times 10^5}{8 \times 10^3} = \frac{2\pi \times 10^{-7}}{8 \times 10^3} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 10^{-10} \approx 7.85 \times 10^{-11}\,\text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Result:** $Q \approx 7.85 \times 10^{-11}$ m³/s $\approx 78.5\,\text{nL/s}$.

**Engineering note:** This is the basis of **capillary rheometry** used in polymer melt characterization — by measuring $Q$ and $\Delta P$ in a die of known $R$ and $L$, one can extract the viscosity $\eta$. **(3 marks)**

**Total for Question B: 14 marks**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — The Dashpot (Module 3)**

### 🎯 Core Conceptual Points
- ☐ A dashpot is a **purely viscous** mechanical element that models Newtonian fluid behavior.
- ☐ **Constitutive law:** $\sigma = \eta \, \dot{\varepsilon}$ (memorize verbatim).
- ☐ **Viscosity $\eta$** has units of **Pa·s** (or equivalently, kg·m⁻¹·s⁻¹ or Poiseuille = 10 Pa·s in SI nomenclature).
- ☐ The dashpot is the **time-dependent** element; the spring is the **time-independent** element.

### 🔬 Behavioral Signatures
- ☐ **Creep (constant stress):** Strain grows **linearly with time**, $\varepsilon(t) = (\sigma_0 / \eta) t$.
- ☐ **Stress relaxation (constant strain):** Stress is **identically zero** for $t > 0$ — dashpot cannot sustain constant strain.
- ☐ **Oscillatory response:** $E^* = i\omega\eta$, $E' = 0$, $E'' = \omega\eta$, phase angle $\delta = 90°$.
- ☐ **Energy:** All input energy is **dissipated as heat**; none is stored elastically.
- ☐ **Recovery:** **None** — deformation is permanent (viscous flow).

### 🧮 Must-Know Formulas (KTU High-Yield)
- ☐ $\sigma = \eta \, \dot{\varepsilon}$ — *the* dashpot equation.
- ☐ $\varepsilon(t) = \sigma_0 t / \eta$ — creep response.
- ☐ $J(t) = t / \eta$ — creep compliance.
- ☐ $W_{diss} = \pi \omega \eta \varepsilon_0^2$ — energy dissipated per cycle.
- ☐ $E'' = \omega \eta$, $E' = 0$ — dynamic moduli.

### 🔗 Role in Larger Viscoelastic Models
- ☐ **Maxwell Model** = Spring (E) + Dashpot (η) **in series** → models stress relaxation.
- ☐ **Kelvin–Voigt Model** = Spring (E) + Dashpot (η) **in parallel** → models creep.
- ☐ **Standard Linear Solid (Zener)** = Maxwell element in parallel with a spring (or Kelvin–Voigt in series with a spring) → models both creep and stress relaxation.
- ☐ Pure dashpots are **rare in isolation**; they are the **building blocks** of all linear viscoelastic models.

### ⚠️ Common KTU Board Exam Traps
- ☐ Don't confuse **dashpot viscosity $\eta$** with **spring modulus $E$** — they have different units (Pa·s vs. Pa).
- ☐ A pure dashpot does **not** exhibit stress relaxation (stress = 0) — only viscoelastic models do.
- ☐ "Newtonian" means $\eta$ is **constant** (independent of $\dot{\varepsilon}$) — non-Newtonian dashpots are out of scope for this module.
- ☐ The creep strain of a pure dashpot is **unbounded** — it grows indefinitely. There is no equilibrium strain.
- ☐ In oscillatory tests, $\tan \delta = E''/E' = \infty$ for a pure dashpot — perfect "lossy" behavior.

### 🏭 Engineering Relevance
- ☐ Polymer melt viscosity governs **processing** (extrusion, injection molding).
- ☐ Viscoelastic heating in tires and dampers is governed by the **dashpot dissipation** $E''$.
- ☐ Long-term creep in polymer parts (pipes, load-bearing members) is the dashpot contribution.
- ☐ Pressure-sensitive adhesives (tape, labels) rely on dashpot-like tack behavior.

---

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Board Exam Strategy:** When a question asks about the dashpot, **always start with the constitutive law** $\sigma = \eta \dot{\varepsilon}$, then identify the input (stress or strain), then derive the output by integration. The **delta impulse** at $t = 0$ in stress relaxation and the **ramp function** in creep are the two iconic responses you must be able to sketch from memory.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Maxwell Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPOT402 - POLYMER PHYSICS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Maxwell Model ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Maxwell Model (Standard Linear Viscoelastic Element — Series Form)** > The **Maxwell Model** is the simplest mechanical analog used to describe the **viscoelastic response of a polymer**. It represents the material as a **linear spring (Hookean element)** and a **linear dashpot (Newtonian viscous element)** connected **in series**. Under an applied load, the **stress is identical in both elements**, while the **total strain is the algebraic sum of the individual strains** contributed by the spring and the dashpot. Where: - The **spring** obeys **Hooke's Law**: $$\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon_{\text{spring}}$$ - The **dashpot** obeys **Newton's Law of viscosity**: $$\sigma = \eta \cdot \frac{d\varepsilon_{\text{dashpot}}}{dt}$$ - The combination is governed by the **Maxwell Differential Equation**: $$\frac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = \frac{1}{E}\frac{d\sigma}{dt} + \frac{\sigma}{\eta}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — Module 3):** The Maxwell Model is one of the two **foundational viscoelastic models** (the other being the Voigt/Kelvin–Voigt Model). It successfully predicts **stress relaxation** but **fails to capture creep recovery**, which is a classic board-exam discussion point. --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Sponge and the Syringe" Imagine a **shock absorber** in an old automobile: - The **spring** is like a **stiff sponge** — when you push it, it instantly deforms in proportion to the force, and it returns to its original shape as soon as you release the force. *(Elastic, instantaneous, recoverable.)* - The **dashpot** is like a **syringe filled with thick oil** — when you push it, the piston moves **slowly and continuously**, and the deformation is **never recovered**. *(Viscous, time-dependent, permanent.)* - The Maxwell element is the **two connected in series**: pull the sponge and the syringe simultaneously. The instantaneous snap-back is from the sponge; the slow, oozing flow is from the syringe. Together they model how a polymer relaxes internal stress over time. > [!TIP] > **Mental Hook:** "**Spring snaps, dashpot slides — Maxwell adds them in series.**" If you remember this single line, you will never confuse Maxwell with the Voigt Model (which uses **parallel** connection). --- ### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics | Symbol | Quantity | Standard SI Unit | Typical Polymer Range | |:------:|:---------|:----------------:|:---------------------:| | $E$ | Young's Modulus (Spring) | $\text{Pa}$ | $10^{6}$ – $10^{9}$ | | $\eta$ | Viscosity (Dashpot) | $\text{Pa} \cdot \text{s}$ | $10^{3}$ – $10^{12}$ | | $\tau$ | Relaxation Time | $\text{s}$ | $10^{-6}$ – $10^{6}$ | | $\sigma_{0}$ | Initial Applied Stress | $\text{Pa}$ | problem-specific | | $\varepsilon_{0}$ | Applied Step Strain | dimensionless | $0.01$ – $0.10$ | > **Critical relationship:** The single most important quantity in the Maxwell Model is the **relaxation time** $\tau = \dfrac{\eta}{E}$, which is a **characteristic fingerprint of the polymer**. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Mechanics **Step 1 — Series Connection Law.** Because the spring and dashpot are in **series**, the same **tensile force** passes through both. This gives: $$\sigma_{\text{total}} = \sigma_{\text{spring}} = \sigma_{\text{dashpot}}$$ **Step 2 — Strain Additivity.** The **total displacement** of the Maxwell element is the **sum of the individual displacements** of the spring and the dashpot: $$\varepsilon_{\text{total}} = \varepsilon_{\text{spring}} + \varepsilon_{\text{dashpot}}$$ **Step 3 — Constitutive Substitution.** From Hooke's Law: $\varepsilon_{\text{spring}} = \dfrac{\sigma}{E}$ From Newton's Law: $\dfrac{d\varepsilon_{\text{dashpot}}}{dt} = \dfrac{\sigma}{\eta}$ **Step 4 — Differentiate the Strain Equation.** Differentiating Step 2 with respect to time and substituting the constitutive relations yields the **Maxwell Constitutive Equation**: $$\frac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = \frac{1}{E}\frac{d\sigma}{dt} + \frac{\sigma}{\eta}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why this equation matters:** This is the **master equation** from which *every* Maxwell Model prediction (stress relaxation, creep, dynamic response) is derived. In the KTU exam, deriving or applying this equation is worth **5–7 marks** on its own. --- ### 2.2 Limiting Behaviour Analysis | Limit | Physical Condition | Material Behaviour | |:-----:|:-------------------|:-------------------| | $\eta \to \infty$ | Dashpot becomes rigid | Pure elastic solid (Hookean) | | $E \to \infty$ | Spring becomes infinitely stiff | Pure viscous fluid (Newtonian) | | $t \ll \tau$ | Short time scale | Glassy / elastic response dominates | | $t \gg \tau$ | Long time scale | Liquid-like viscous flow dominates | | $\sigma = \text{const.}$ | Stress held constant | Creep test (linear strain increase) | | $\varepsilon = \text{const.}$ | Strain held constant | Stress relaxation test (exponential decay) | --- ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master Maxwell Equation Sheet — Memorize for ESE** | # | Test / Scenario | Governing Equation | Key Parameter | |:-:|:---------------|:-------------------|:--------------| | 1 | Constitutive (general) | $\dfrac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = \dfrac{1}{E}\dfrac{d\sigma}{dt} + \dfrac{\sigma}{\eta}$ | — | | 2 | Relaxation Time | $\tau = \dfrac{\eta}{E}$ | $\tau$ [s] | | 3 | Stress Relaxation (step strain $\varepsilon_{0}$) | $\sigma(t) = \sigma_{0} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{t}{\tau}\right)$ | $\tau = \eta / E$ | | 4 | Creep Compliance (step stress $\sigma_{0}$) | $J(t) = \dfrac{1}{E} + \dfrac{t}{\eta}$ | slope $= 1/\eta$ | | 5 | Strain under constant stress | $\varepsilon(t) = \dfrac{\sigma_{0}}{E} + \dfrac{\sigma_{0}}{\eta}\,t$ | — | | 6 | Dynamic Modulus (sinusoidal $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_{0} e^{i\omega t}$) | $E^{\ast} = \dfrac{E\,i\omega\tau}{1 + i\omega\tau}$ | — | | 7 | Storage Modulus | $E^{\prime}(\omega) = \dfrac{E\,\omega^{2}\tau^{2}}{1 + \omega^{2}\tau^{2}}$ | — | | 8 | Loss Modulus | $E^{\prime\prime}(\omega) = \dfrac{E\,\omega\tau}{1 + \omega^{2}\tau^{2}}$ | — | | 9 | Loss Tangent | $\tan\delta = \dfrac{E^{\prime\prime}}{E^{\prime}} = \dfrac{1}{\omega\tau}$ | — | | 10 | Permanent Set after Stress Removal | $\varepsilon_{\text{perm}} = \dfrac{\sigma_{0}}{\eta}\,t_{0}$ | never recovers | > **Tip for tables:** Where absolute-value bars would normally appear in formulas above (e.g. $\vert t \vert$), they have been written as $\omega\tau$ or contextually absorbed. Use the symbol pair **$\mid$** or **$\vert$** in your written scripts if you must denote magnitude, **never the bare pipe symbol inside a markdown table row**. --- ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Polymer Processing:** Predicting how a melt relaxes residual stresses during injection molding and extrusion. - **Vibration Damping:** Designing elastomeric mounts for engines and machinery using $\tan\delta$ analysis. - **Tire Engineering:** Hysteresis loss and rolling resistance prediction in elastomer compounds. - **Biomedical Polymers:** Long-term stress decay in sutures, stents, and orthopedic implants. - **Coatings and Adhesives:** Predicting the lifetime of bonded joints under sustained load. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Maxwell Constitutive Equation (from First Principles) **Starting point — strain additivity:** $$\varepsilon = \varepsilon_{\text{spring}} + \varepsilon_{\text{dashpot}}$$ **Substitute Hooke's Law** $\varepsilon_{\text{spring}} = \sigma / E$: $$\varepsilon = \frac{\sigma}{E} + \varepsilon_{\text{dashpot}}$$ **Differentiate with respect to time:** $$\frac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = \frac{1}{E}\frac{d\sigma}{dt} + \frac{d\varepsilon_{\text{dashpot}}}{dt}$$ **Substitute Newton's viscous law** $d\varepsilon_{\text{dashpot}}/dt = \sigma/\eta$: $$\boxed{\;\frac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = \frac{1}{E}\frac{d\sigma}{dt} + \frac{\sigma}{\eta}\;}$$ This is the **Maxwell Constitutive Equation**. ✅ --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Stress Relaxation Under a Step Strain **Experimental Setup:** At time $t = 0$, the sample is **instantly strained** to a fixed value $\varepsilon_{0}$ and held there. For $t > 0$: $$\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_{0} = \text{constant}$$ Therefore: $$\frac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = 0$$ **Substitute into the Maxwell equation:** $$0 = \frac{1}{E}\frac{d\sigma}{dt} + \frac{\sigma}{\eta}$$ **Rearrange to isolate the derivative:** $$\frac{d\sigma}{dt} = -\frac{E}{\eta}\sigma$$ **Recognize this is a first-order separable ODE:** $$\frac{d\sigma}{\sigma} = -\frac{E}{\eta}\,dt$$ **Integrate both sides** (using initial condition $\sigma(0) = \sigma_{0} = E\,\varepsilon_{0}$): $$\int_{\sigma_{0}}^{\sigma(t)}\frac{d\sigma}{\sigma} = -\frac{E}{\eta}\int_{0}^{t} dt$$ $$\ln\!\left(\frac{\sigma(t)}{\sigma_{0}}\right) = -\frac{E}{\eta}t$$ **Define the relaxation time** $\tau \equiv \eta / E$: $$\ln\!\left(\frac{\sigma(t)}{\sigma_{0}}\right) = -\frac{t}{\tau}$$ **Exponentiate both sides:** $$\boxed{\;\sigma(t) = \sigma_{0}\exp\!\left(-\frac{t}{\tau}\right)\;}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Physical interpretation:** The internal stress decays **exponentially** with a characteristic time constant $\tau$. After one relaxation time ($\tau$), the stress drops to $\approx 36.8\%$ of its initial value; after $5\tau$, it is essentially zero ($\approx 0.67\%$). --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Creep Under a Step Stress **Experimental Setup:** At $t = 0$, a **constant stress** $\sigma_{0}$ is applied and held. For $t > 0$: $$\sigma(t) = \sigma_{0} = \text{constant} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \frac{d\sigma}{dt} = 0$$ **Substitute into the Maxwell equation:** $$\frac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = \frac{1}{E}(0) + \frac{\sigma_{0}}{\eta}$$ $$\frac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = \frac{\sigma_{0}}{\eta}$$ **Integrate** with $\varepsilon(0) = \sigma_{0}/E$ (instantaneous elastic strain at $t=0$): $$\int_{\varepsilon(0)}^{\varepsilon(t)} d\varepsilon = \frac{\sigma_{0}}{\eta}\int_{0}^{t} dt$$ $$\varepsilon(t) - \frac{\sigma_{0}}{E} = \frac{\sigma_{0}}{\eta}\,t$$ $$\boxed{\;\varepsilon(t) = \frac{\sigma_{0}}{E} + \frac{\sigma_{0}}{\eta}\,t\;}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Critical Board-Exam Trap:** The Maxwell model predicts **unbounded linear creep** — the strain grows forever. This is physically unrealistic for a solid polymer. The model therefore **fails for creep testing of solids**, even though it works perfectly for stress relaxation. Always mention this limitation in your answer. --- ### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Dynamic (Oscillatory) Mechanical Response Apply a sinusoidal strain: $$\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_{0}\,e^{i\omega t}$$ Compute the time derivatives: $$\frac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = i\omega\,\varepsilon_{0}\,e^{i\omega t}, \qquad \frac{d\sigma}{dt} = i\omega\,\sigma$$ **Substitute into the Maxwell equation:** $$i\omega\,\varepsilon_{0}\,e^{i\omega t} = \frac{1}{E}\,i\omega\,\sigma + \frac{\sigma}{\eta}$$ Factor out $\sigma$ on the right: $$i\omega\,\varepsilon = \sigma\!\left(\frac{i\omega}{E} + \frac{1}{\eta}\right)$$ Define the **complex dynamic modulus** $E^{\ast} = \sigma/\varepsilon$: $$E^{\ast} = \frac{i\omega}{\dfrac{i\omega}{E} + \dfrac{1}{\eta}}$$ Multiply numerator and denominator by $E\eta$: $$E^{\ast} = \frac{i\omega E\eta}{i\omega\eta + E} = \frac{E\,i\omega\tau}{1 + i\omega\tau}$$ **Rationalize** by multiplying numerator and denominator by the complex conjugate $(1 - i\omega\tau)$: $$E^{\ast} = \frac{E\,i\omega\tau(1 - i\omega\tau)}{1 + \omega^{2}\tau^{2}}$$ **Expand the numerator:** $$E^{\ast} = \frac{E(\omega^{2}\tau^{2} + i\omega\tau)}{1 + \omega^{2}\tau^{2}}$$ **Equate real and imaginary parts:** $$\boxed{\;E^{\prime}(\omega) = \frac{E\,\omega^{2}\tau^{2}}{1 + \omega^{2}\tau^{2}}\;}$$ $$\boxed{\;E^{\prime\prime}(\omega) = \frac{E\,\omega\tau}{1 + \omega^{2}\tau^{2}}\;}$$ These are the **storage modulus** (elastic energy stored) and **loss modulus** (energy dissipated as heat). --- ### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) > **Problem:** A Maxwell element has $E = 5 \times 10^{7}\ \text{Pa}$ and $\eta = 5 \times 10^{8}\ \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$. If a step strain of $\varepsilon_{0} = 0.02$ is applied, calculate: > (a) The relaxation time $\tau$. > (b) The stress after $5$ seconds. > (c) The time required for the stress to drop to $1\%$ of its initial value. **Solution:** **(a)** Relaxation time: $$\tau = \frac{\eta}{E} = \frac{5 \times 10^{8}}{5 \times 10^{7}} = 10\ \text{s}$$ **(b)** Initial stress: $$\sigma_{0} = E\,\varepsilon_{0} = (5 \times 10^{7})(0.02) = 1 \times 10^{6}\ \text{Pa}$$ Stress at $t = 5$ s: $$\sigma(5) = 10^{6}\,\exp(-5/10) = 10^{6}\,e^{-0.5} = 10^{6}(0.6065)$$ $$\sigma(5) \approx 6.065 \times 10^{5}\ \text{Pa}$$ **(c)** For $\sigma(t)/\sigma_{0} = 0.01$: $$\exp(-t/\tau) = 0.01 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad -t/\tau = \ln(0.01) = -4.605$$ $$t = 4.605\,\tau = 4.605 \times 10 \approx 46.05\ \text{s}$$ ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mechanical Analog Architecture (Mermaid Flowchart) ```mermaid flowchart LR A["LOAD sigma"] --> B["MAXWELL ELEMENT"] subgraph B [" "] direction LR S1["SPRING
Hookean
E = const"] -- "sigma same" --> D1["DASHPOT
Newtonian
eta = const"] end B --> C["RESPONSES"] subgraph C [" "] direction LR R1["Stress Relaxation
sigma = sigma0 exp(-t/tau)"] R2["Creep
eps = sigma0/E + sigma0 t / eta"] R3["Dynamic Modulus
E* = E iw tau / 1 + iw tau"] end style B fill:#fdf6b2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style C fill:#d9f0ff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style S1 fill:#b6f5b6,stroke:#1a1 style D1 fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#a11 ``` ### 4.2 Decision Flow — When to Apply the Maxwell Model ```mermaid flowchart TD P["Start: Viscoelastic Problem"] --> Q{"Type of test?"} Q -- "Step STRAIN held constant" --> R["Use STRESS RELAXATION
sigma = sigma0 exp(-t/tau)"] Q -- "Step STRESS held constant" --> S["Use CREEP EQUATION
eps = sigma0/E + sigma0 t/eta"] Q -- "Sinusoidal strain" --> T["Use DYNAMIC MODULI
E' and E'' formulas"] R --> U["Cross-check: tau = eta / E"] S --> U T --> V["Cross-check: tan delta = 1 / omega tau"] U --> W["Predict polymer behaviour"] V --> W ``` ### 4.3 Response Curves (Schematic Overview) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Stress_Relax ["Stress Relaxation sigma vs t"] direction LR SP1["sigma = sigma0 at t=0"] --> SP2["Exponential decay"] SP2 --> SP3["sigma approx 0 at t = 5 tau"] end subgraph Creep_Behav ["Creep Strain eps vs t"] direction LR CP1["Instant jump sigma0/E at t=0"] --> CP2["Linear viscous increase"] CP2 --> CP3["Unbounded flow (permanent set)"] end style Stress_Relax fill:#fff4e6,stroke:#a60 style Creep_Behav fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#060 ``` ### 4.4 Real-World System Mapping (Engineering Block Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TB M["Macroscopic Polymer Specimen"] --> T1["Apply Step Strain"] M --> T2["Apply Step Stress"] M --> T3["Apply Oscillatory Strain"] T1 --> MT1["Maxwell Element
Stress Relaxation Test"] T2 --> MT1b["Maxwell Element
Creep Test (predicted, but inaccurate for solids)"] T3 --> MT1c["Maxwell Element
DMA Frequency Sweep"] MT1 --> O1["Output: E and eta from log sigma vs t"] MT1b --> O2["Output: J(t) compliance curve"] MT1c --> O3["Output: E' and E'' vs frequency"] O1 --> APP["Application: Mould design, Residual stress"] O2 --> APP O3 --> APP2["Application: Damping, Tire engineering"] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Define the Maxwell Model for a viscoelastic polymer. State the governing differential equation.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Definition (1 mark):** The Maxwell Model represents a polymer as a **spring (Hookean element, modulus $E$)** and a **dashpot (Newtonian element, viscosity $\eta$) connected in series**. - **Governing equation (2 marks):** Because stress is equal in both elements and strains add, we obtain: $$\frac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = \frac{1}{E}\frac{d\sigma}{dt} + \frac{\sigma}{\eta}$$ --- **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **What is the relaxation time in the Maxwell Model? On what factors does it depend?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Definition (1 mark):** The relaxation time $\tau$ is the characteristic time required for the stress in a Maxwell element under step strain to decay to $1/e \ (\approx 36.8\%)$ of its initial value. - **Formula (1 mark):** $$\tau = \frac{\eta}{E}$$ - **Factors (1 mark):** It depends on the **viscosity of the dashpot** ($\eta$) and the **modulus of the spring** ($E$), which in turn depend on **temperature, molecular weight, and chemical structure** of the polymer. --- ### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > 1. **Forgetting the initial condition** $\sigma(0) = E\varepsilon_{0}$ during stress relaxation derivation → lose **2 marks**. > 2. **Confusing Maxwell (series) with Voigt (parallel)** — a single wrong sketch costs **3 marks**. > 3. **Writing $\sigma/E$ as strain of dashpot** instead of $\varepsilon_{d} = \sigma/\eta \cdot t$ → **1 mark** deducted per occurrence. > 4. **Not stating units** of $E$, $\eta$, and $\tau$ → lose **1 mark** in numerical problems. > 5. **Omitting the limitation** that the Maxwell model fails for creep in solids → **1 mark** deducted. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]` **(a)** Derive the expression for **stress relaxation** in a Maxwell element subjected to a sudden step strain $\varepsilon_{0}$ applied at $t = 0$. **\[7 Marks\]** **(b)** A Maxwell element has $E = 2 \times 10^{7}\ \text{Pa}$ and $\eta = 4 \times 10^{8}\ \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$. A step strain $\varepsilon_{0} = 0.05$ is applied. Calculate: - (i) The relaxation time $\tau$. - (ii) The stress at $t = 0$, $t = \tau$, and $t = 3\tau$. - (iii) The time for the stress to decay to $10\%$ of its initial value. **\[7 Marks\]** --- **Model Solution — Part (a):** *(7 marks distributed as: setup 2 marks, ODE 2 marks, integration 2 marks, final expression 1 mark)* **Setup (2 marks):** A step strain is applied: $\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_{0}$ for all $t \ge 0$. Differentiating: $$\frac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = 0$$ **Governing Equation (2 marks):** The Maxwell constitutive equation is: $$\frac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = \frac{1}{E}\frac{d\sigma}{dt} + \frac{\sigma}{\eta}$$ **Solving the ODE (2 marks):** Substituting $d\varepsilon/dt = 0$: $$0 = \frac{1}{E}\frac{d\sigma}{dt} + \frac{\sigma}{\eta}$$ $$\frac{d\sigma}{\sigma} = -\frac{E}{\eta}\,dt$$ Integrating from $0$ to $t$: $$\ln\!\left(\frac{\sigma(t)}{\sigma(0)}\right) = -\frac{E}{\eta}t$$ **Final Expression (1 mark):** With initial stress $\sigma(0) = E\varepsilon_{0}$ and $\tau = \eta/E$: $$\boxed{\;\sigma(t) = E\varepsilon_{0}\,\exp\!\left(-\frac{t}{\tau}\right)\;}$$ --- **Model Solution — Part (b):** *(7 marks distributed as: part i 2 marks, part ii 3 marks, part iii 2 marks)* **(i) Relaxation time:** $$\tau = \frac{\eta}{E} = \frac{4 \times 10^{8}}{2 \times 10^{7}} = 20\ \text{s}$$ **\[2 marks\]** **(ii) Initial stress:** $$\sigma(0) = E\varepsilon_{0} = (2 \times 10^{7})(0.05) = 1 \times 10^{6}\ \text{Pa}$$ - At $t = \tau = 20$ s: $$\sigma(\tau) = 10^{6}\,e^{-1} \approx 3.68 \times 10^{5}\ \text{Pa}$$ - At $t = 3\tau = 60$ s: $$\sigma(3\tau) = 10^{6}\,e^{-3} \approx 4.98 \times 10^{4}\ \text{Pa}$$ **\[3 marks\]** **(iii) Time to decay to 10 %:** $$0.10 = \exp(-t/\tau) \quad \Longrightarrow \quad t/\tau = \ln(10) = 2.3026$$ $$t = 2.3026 \times 20 \approx 46.05\ \text{s}$$ **\[2 marks\]** --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]` **(a)** Derive the expression for **creep strain** in a Maxwell element under a constant applied stress $\sigma_{0}$. **\[7 Marks\]** **(b)** Explain **two major limitations** of the Maxwell Model. Compare its predictions with the **Voigt (Kelvin–Voigt) Model** in a tabular form. **\[7 Marks\]** --- **Model Solution — Part (a):** *(7 marks distributed as: setup 1 mark, governing eqn 2 marks, integration 2 marks, final form 2 marks)* **Setup (1 mark):** A constant stress $\sigma(t) = \sigma_{0}$ is applied at $t = 0$, hence $d\sigma/dt = 0$. **Governing Equation (2 marks):** Maxwell equation: $$\frac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = \frac{1}{E}\frac{d\sigma}{dt} + \frac{\sigma}{\eta}$$ **Integration (2 marks):** With $d\sigma/dt = 0$: $$\frac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = \frac{\sigma_{0}}{\eta}$$ $$\int d\varepsilon = \frac{\sigma_{0}}{\eta}\int dt \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \varepsilon(t) = \frac{\sigma_{0}}{\eta}t + C$$ **Final Form (2 marks):** Using initial condition $\varepsilon(0) = \sigma_{0}/E$ (instantaneous spring response): $$\boxed{\;\varepsilon(t) = \frac{\sigma_{0}}{E} + \frac{\sigma_{0}}{\eta}\,t\;}$$ --- **Model Solution — Part (b):** *(7 marks distributed as: 2 limitations = 4 marks, table comparison = 3 marks)* **Limitation 1 (2 marks):** The Maxwell model predicts **unbounded linear creep** — strain grows indefinitely as long as stress is applied. Real polymer solids do not flow indefinitely; they approach an equilibrium strain. **Limitation 2 (2 marks):** Upon **removal of load**, the Maxwell model predicts an **instantaneous elastic recovery of $\sigma_{0}/E$**, followed by **permanent viscous deformation** $\sigma_{0}t_{0}/\eta$. It cannot model **delayed elastic recovery** (which the Voigt model captures). **Comparison Table (3 marks):** | Feature | Maxwell Model | Voigt (Kelvin–Voigt) Model | |:--------|:-------------|:---------------------------| | Configuration | Spring **in series** with dashpot | Spring **in parallel** with dashpot | | Stress | Same in both: $\sigma = \sigma_{s} = \sigma_{d}$ | Sum: $\sigma = \sigma_{s} + \sigma_{d}$ | | Strain | Sum: $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_{s} + \varepsilon_{d}$ | Same in both: $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_{s} = \varepsilon_{d}$ | | Stress Relaxation | Predicts well ($\sigma = \sigma_{0}e^{-t/\tau}$) | Predicts no decay (constant residual stress) | | Creep Behaviour | Fails — unbounded linear flow | Predicts well — bounded approach to $\sigma_{0}/E$ | | Recovery | Partial; permanent set remains | Complete; strain decays to zero | --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist** - ☐ Maxwell = **spring + dashpot in SERIES** (Voigt = parallel). - ☐ **Same stress** in both elements; **strains ADD**. - ☐ Master equation: $\dfrac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = \dfrac{1}{E}\dfrac{d\sigma}{dt} + \dfrac{\sigma}{\eta}$. - ☐ Relaxation time: $\tau = \eta / E$ — the **fingerprint** of the polymer. - ☐ Stress relaxation (step strain): $\sigma(t) = \sigma_{0}\exp(-t/\tau)$. - ☐ Creep (step stress): $\varepsilon(t) = \sigma_{0}/E + \sigma_{0}t/\eta$ — **unbounded** → main flaw. - ☐ Dynamic moduli: $E^{\prime} = E\omega^{2}\tau^{2}/(1+\omega^{2}\tau^{2})$, $E^{\prime\prime} = E\omega\tau/(1+\omega^{2}\tau^{2})$. - ☐ Loss tangent: $\tan\delta = 1/(\omega\tau)$. - ☐ Maxwell works for **liquids/melts** but fails for **cross-linked elastomers** in creep. - ☐ Always state **units** ($E$ in Pa, $\eta$ in Pa·s, $\tau$ in s) in numerical answers. - ☐ In KTU exams, **draw the schematic** of the mechanical analog — earns **easy 2 marks**. - ☐ Mention **at least one limitation** of the model — examiners reward critical awareness.

topics.title: Voigt Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT402 - POLYMER PHYSICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Voigt Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Voigt Model** (also historically referred to as the **Kelvin-Voigt Model**) is a fundamental linear viscoelastic constitutive model used in polymer physics to describe the time-dependent mechanical response of viscoelastic materials. It represents a mechanical analog in which a **Hookean spring** (representing the elastic component) and a **Newtonian dashpot** (representing the viscous component) are connected in **parallel**.

In this configuration, both elements experience the **same strain** ($\varepsilon$) at any instant of time, while the **total stress** ($\sigma$) borne by the system is the **arithmetic sum** of the individual stresses carried by the spring and the dashpot.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Voigt Model is one of the two foundational two-element mechanical analogs (the other being the Maxwell Model) used to represent the viscoelastic behavior of polymeric materials under load. Mastery of this model is essential for Module 3 (Viscoelastic Behavior of Polymers).

## 1.2 Constitutive Equation (Formal Statement)

The Voigt Model is mathematically expressed by the following differential constitutive equation:

$$\sigma(t) = E \cdot \varepsilon(t) + \eta \cdot \frac{d\varepsilon(t)}{dt}$$

Where:
- $\sigma(t)$ = Total stress at time $t$ (in **Pascals, Pa**)
- $E$ = Elastic modulus of the spring element (in **Pa**)
- $\eta$ = Coefficient of viscosity of the dashpot (in **Pa·s**)
- $\varepsilon(t)$ = Strain at time $t$ (dimensionless)
- $\dfrac{d\varepsilon(t)}{dt}$ = Strain rate with respect to time (in **s⁻¹**)

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Explanation

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Damped Shock Absorber"**
>
> Imagine you are sitting on an old-style sofa cushion that contains both **springs** and a **viscous fluid damper** (like a shock absorber) working **side-by-side** (parallel). When you suddenly sit down:
>
> 1. The **spring** wants to push back instantly (elastic response).
> 2. The **dashpot** resists motion slowly due to fluid friction (viscous response).
> 3. Because they work in parallel, the cushion compresses **slowly and gradually** rather than instantly.
>
> This delayed, smooth compression is exactly the **creep behavior** predicted by the Voigt Model. The more viscous the dashpot, the slower the cushion compresses under your weight. When you stand up and remove the load, the cushion **slowly recovers** to its original shape — it does not snap back instantly. This gradual recovery is also a unique hallmark of the Voigt Model.

**Alternative Analogy — Silicone Putty:** Take a piece of silicone putty. If you press it quickly with your finger and release it quickly, it does not fully recover instantly. The spring in the putty tries to restore shape, but the dashpot (internal friction) slows the recovery. This is a **Voigt-type** response.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

| Symbol | Parameter | Typical Unit | Physical Meaning |
|--------|-----------|--------------|------------------|
| $E$ | Elastic Modulus | **Pa (N/m²)** | Stiffness of spring element |
| $\eta$ | Viscosity Coefficient | **Pa·s** | Internal friction of dashpot |
| $\tau$ | Retardation Time | **s (seconds)** | Characteristic time of response |
| $J(t)$ | Creep Compliance | **Pa⁻¹** | Strain per unit applied stress |
| $\varepsilon_0$ | Instantaneous Strain | dimensionless | Strain at $t = 0$ |

The **retardation time** $\tau$ is defined as:

$$\tau = \frac{\eta}{E}$$

This represents the time required for the strain to reach approximately **63.2%** of its ultimate equilibrium value under a step-applied constant stress.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanical Construction — The Parallel Arrangement

The defining feature of the Voigt Model is its **parallel topology**. To understand its mechanical behavior, recall the following:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Parallel Mechanical Elements:**
> - **Strain** in each parallel element is **IDENTICAL** at all times.
> - **Stress** in the system is the **SUM** of stresses in each individual element.
> - The total system behaves as the **stiffer** combination of both elements.

In contrast, the Maxwell Model (covered in another module) uses a **series** arrangement, which yields fundamentally different physical responses (such as stress relaxation under constant strain — a behavior the Voigt Model **cannot** reproduce).

## 2.2 Stress Decomposition Logic

For the Voigt Model, the total stress is the sum of contributions from two distinct physical mechanisms:

### 2.2.1 Stress in the Spring (Hooke's Law)
The spring obeys ideal elastic behavior:

$$\sigma_{\text{spring}} = E \cdot \varepsilon(t)$$

This stress is **instantaneous** — the spring responds immediately to any strain.

### 2.2.2 Stress in the Dashpot (Newton's Law of Viscosity)
The dashpot obeys ideal viscous flow:

$$\sigma_{\text{dashpot}} = \eta \cdot \frac{d\varepsilon(t)}{dt}$$

This stress depends on the **rate of deformation**, not the magnitude of strain itself.

### 2.2.3 Total Stress (Parallel Superposition)

$$\sigma_{\text{total}} = \sigma_{\text{spring}} + \sigma_{\text{dashpot}} = E \cdot \varepsilon(t) + \eta \cdot \frac{d\varepsilon(t)}{dt}$$

## 2.3 Three Classical Experiments in Viscoelasticity

To fully characterize a viscoelastic model, KTU examiners expect you to analyze three fundamental experiments:

### 2.3.1 Creep Experiment (Constant Stress, Observe Strain)
- **Input:** A step stress $\sigma_0$ is applied at $t = 0$ and held constant.
- **Output Observed:** The strain $\varepsilon(t)$ evolves with time.

For the Voigt Model, the strain does **not** jump instantly. Instead, it grows **gradually** from zero (assuming zero initial strain) and asymptotically approaches a steady-state value:

$$\varepsilon_{\infty} = \frac{\sigma_0}{E}$$

This is because the dashpot initially resists rapid motion, forcing the strain to develop over time.

### 2.3.2 Stress Relaxation Experiment (Constant Strain, Observe Stress)
- **Input:** A step strain $\varepsilon_0$ is applied instantaneously at $t = 0$ and held constant.
- **Output Observed:** The stress $\sigma(t)$ evolves with time.

For the Voigt Model, since the strain is held constant, $\dfrac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = 0$, and the stress response is:

$$\sigma(t) = E \cdot \varepsilon_0$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** The Voigt Model **CANNOT** represent stress relaxation! The stress remains **constant and equal to $E\varepsilon_0$** indefinitely. This is a major limitation that distinguishes it from the Maxwell Model. Examiners frequently test this distinction.

### 2.3.3 Recovery Experiment (Stress Removed, Observe Strain Decay)
- **Input:** After the creep experiment, the stress is suddenly removed at $t = t_1$.
- **Output Observed:** The strain $\varepsilon(t)$ decays back toward zero.

The Voigt Model exhibits **complete and gradual recovery** — a hallmark feature distinguishing it from purely plastic or purely viscous behavior.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Phenomenon | Mathematical Expression | Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|-----------|------------------------|-------|------------------|
| 1 | Constitutive Equation | $\sigma = E\varepsilon + \eta\dfrac{d\varepsilon}{dt}$ | Pa | Fundamental stress-strain-rate relationship |
| 2 | Retardation Time | $\tau = \dfrac{\eta}{E}$ | s (seconds) | Characteristic time scale of viscous response |
| 3 | Creep Strain Response | $\varepsilon(t) = \dfrac{\sigma_0}{E}\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$ | dimensionless | Strain evolution under step stress $\sigma_0$ |
| 4 | Steady-State Strain (Creep) | $\varepsilon_{\infty} = \dfrac{\sigma_0}{E}$ | dimensionless | Maximum strain reached at $t \to \infty$ |
| 5 | Creep Compliance | $J(t) = \dfrac{1}{E}\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$ | Pa⁻¹ | Strain per unit applied stress |
| 6 | Stress Relaxation Response | $\sigma(t) = E\varepsilon_0$ (constant) | Pa | No stress relaxation — a key limitation |
| 7 | Recovery Response | $\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_1 \cdot e^{-(t-t_1)/\tau}$ | dimensionless | Exponential strain decay after unloading |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Applications in Polymer Engineering:**
>
> 1. **Polymer Foams and Cushions:** The slow compression under sustained load (e.g., a mattress under a sleeper) follows Voigt-type behavior. Engineers use this model to predict long-term indentation.
> 2. **Creep in Plastic Pipes:** Underground PVC or HDPE pipes under constant soil pressure creep slowly — the Voigt model provides first-order predictions.
> 3. **Vibration Damping in Rubber Mounts:** Automotive engine mounts made of rubber exhibit Voigt-type damping — the spring stores energy while the dashpot dissipates it, reducing transmitted vibrations.
> 4. **Biological Tissue Mechanics:** Cartilage and tendon (which are polymeric in nature) show Voigt-type creep behavior under sustained physiological loads.
> 5. **Creep Recovery in Polymers Used in Construction:** Polymer-modified concrete and structural polymer components use Voigt-based predictions for long-term deformation.

The **WHY** behind the Voigt Model's relevance: Polymers are neither purely elastic (like metals at low temperature) nor purely viscous (like fluids). They exhibit both behaviors simultaneously, and the Voigt Model captures this duality in its simplest mathematical form.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation #1: Creep Response (Strain vs. Time under Constant Stress)

This is the most frequently asked derivation in KTU exams on the Voigt Model.

### Given Information
- At $t = 0$, a step stress $\sigma_0$ is applied.
- The stress is held constant for $t > 0$: $\sigma(t) = \sigma_0$.
- Initial strain is zero: $\varepsilon(0) = 0$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Begin with the Voigt constitutive equation:

$$\sigma(t) = E \cdot \varepsilon(t) + \eta \cdot \frac{d\varepsilon(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the constant stress condition $\sigma(t) = \sigma_0$:

$$\sigma_0 = E \cdot \varepsilon(t) + \eta \cdot \frac{d\varepsilon(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 3:** Rearrange to isolate the strain rate term on one side of the equation:

$$\eta \cdot \frac{d\varepsilon(t)}{dt} = \sigma_0 - E \cdot \varepsilon(t)$$

**Step 4:** Divide both sides by $\eta$ to obtain standard first-order linear ODE form:

$$\frac{d\varepsilon(t)}{dt} = \frac{\sigma_0 - E \cdot \varepsilon(t)}{\eta}$$

**Step 5:** Separate variables (grouping all $\varepsilon$ terms on one side, $t$ on the other):

$$\frac{d\varepsilon(t)}{\sigma_0 - E \cdot \varepsilon(t)} = \frac{dt}{\eta}$$

**Step 6:** Substitute $u = \sigma_0 - E\varepsilon$, then $du = -E \, d\varepsilon$, so $d\varepsilon = -\dfrac{du}{E}$. The integral becomes:

$$\int \frac{-du/E}{u} = \int \frac{dt}{\eta}$$

$$-\frac{1}{E} \int \frac{du}{u} = \frac{1}{\eta} \int dt$$

**Step 7:** Perform the integration:

$$-\frac{1}{E} \ln \vert u \vert = \frac{t}{\eta} + C$$

where $C$ is the constant of integration.

**Step 8:** Substitute back $u = \sigma_0 - E\varepsilon$:

$$-\frac{1}{E} \ln \vert \sigma_0 - E\varepsilon \vert = \frac{t}{\eta} + C$$

**Step 9:** Multiply both sides by $-E$:

$$\ln \vert \sigma_0 - E\varepsilon \vert = -\frac{Et}{\eta} - CE$$

**Step 10:** Define a new constant $C' = -CE$ (just a renaming):

$$\ln \vert \sigma_0 - E\varepsilon \vert = -\frac{Et}{\eta} + C'$$

**Step 11:** Exponentiate both sides:

$$\sigma_0 - E\varepsilon = e^{C'} \cdot e^{-Et/\eta}$$

**Step 12:** Let $A = e^{C'}$ (a new constant):

$$\sigma_0 - E\varepsilon = A \cdot e^{-Et/\eta}$$

**Step 13:** Solve for $\varepsilon$:

$$E\varepsilon = \sigma_0 - A \cdot e^{-Et/\eta}$$

$$\varepsilon(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{E} - \frac{A}{E} \cdot e^{-Et/\eta}$$

**Step 14:** Apply the initial condition $\varepsilon(0) = 0$:

$$0 = \frac{\sigma_0}{E} - \frac{A}{E} \cdot e^{0}$$

$$0 = \frac{\sigma_0}{E} - \frac{A}{E}$$

Solving for $A$:

$$A = \sigma_0$$

**Step 15:** Substitute $A = \sigma_0$ back into the general solution:

$$\varepsilon(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{E} - \frac{\sigma_0}{E} \cdot e^{-Et/\eta}$$

**Step 16:** Factor out $\sigma_0 / E$:

$$\boxed{\varepsilon(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{E} \left(1 - e^{-Et/\eta}\right) = \frac{\sigma_0}{E} \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)}$$

where the **retardation time** is $\tau = \eta / E$.

### Physical Interpretation of the Result
- At $t = 0$: $\varepsilon(0) = 0$ (no instantaneous strain — Voigt does not predict an initial jump)
- As $t \to \infty$: $\varepsilon(\infty) = \sigma_0 / E$ (asymptotic equilibrium strain)
- At $t = \tau$: $\varepsilon(\tau) = (\sigma_0/E)(1 - e^{-1}) \approx 0.632 \cdot (\sigma_0/E)$ — strain reaches 63.2% of maximum

---

## 3.2 Derivation #2: Creep Compliance $J(t)$

The creep compliance is defined as the strain per unit applied stress:

$$J(t) = \frac{\varepsilon(t)}{\sigma_0}$$

Using the derived creep response from Section 3.1:

$$\boxed{J(t) = \frac{1}{E}\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)}$$

### Limiting Behavior
- At $t = 0$: $J(0) = 0$ (zero instantaneous compliance — the Voigt model predicts a **rigid** initial response)
- As $t \to \infty$: $J(\infty) = 1/E$ (the equilibrium compliance is purely elastic)

---

## 3.3 Derivation #3: Stress Relaxation Response (Important Limitation)

### Given Information
- At $t = 0$, a step strain $\varepsilon_0$ is applied and held constant.
- Therefore: $\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_0$ for all $t \geq 0$, and $\dfrac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = 0$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Begin with the Voigt constitutive equation:

$$\sigma(t) = E \cdot \varepsilon(t) + \eta \cdot \frac{d\varepsilon(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_0$ (constant) and $\dfrac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = 0$:

$$\sigma(t) = E \cdot \varepsilon_0 + \eta \cdot (0)$$

**Step 3:** Simplify:

$$\boxed{\sigma(t) = E \cdot \varepsilon_0 = \text{constant}}$$

### Physical Interpretation
The stress remains **constant and equal to $E\varepsilon_0$** at all times. This means:
- The Voigt Model **does not predict stress relaxation**.
- It behaves as a perfectly elastic solid under constant strain conditions.
- This is a major limitation — real polymers do show stress relaxation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does this happen?** Under constant strain, the dashpot element cannot deform (it is "frozen" at a fixed displacement), so the strain rate is zero. The spring bears the entire mechanical load alone, leading to a constant stress equal to $E\varepsilon_0$.

---

## 3.4 Derivation #4: Recovery Response (Strain Decay after Unloading)

### Given Information
- A stress $\sigma_0$ was applied from $t = 0$ to $t = t_1$, producing a strain $\varepsilon_1 = (\sigma_0/E)(1 - e^{-t_1/\tau})$.
- At $t = t_1$, the stress is suddenly removed: $\sigma(t) = 0$ for $t \geq t_1$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** With $\sigma(t) = 0$ for $t \geq t_1$, the constitutive equation becomes:

$$0 = E \cdot \varepsilon(t) + \eta \cdot \frac{d\varepsilon(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 2:** Rearrange:

$$\eta \cdot \frac{d\varepsilon(t)}{dt} = -E \cdot \varepsilon(t)$$

**Step 3:** Separate variables:

$$\frac{d\varepsilon(t)}{\varepsilon(t)} = -\frac{E}{\eta} \, dt = -\frac{1}{\tau} \, dt$$

**Step 4:** Integrate both sides from $t_1$ to $t$:

$$\int_{\varepsilon_1}^{\varepsilon(t)} \frac{d\varepsilon'}{\varepsilon'} = -\frac{1}{\tau} \int_{t_1}^{t} dt'$$

$$\ln \left(\frac{\varepsilon(t)}{\varepsilon_1}\right) = -\frac{(t - t_1)}{\tau}$$

**Step 5:** Exponentiate both sides:

$$\frac{\varepsilon(t)}{\varepsilon_1} = e^{-(t - t_1)/\tau}$$

**Step 6:** Final form of the recovery response:

$$\boxed{\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_1 \cdot e^{-(t - t_1)/\tau} \quad \text{for } t \geq t_1}$$

### Physical Interpretation
- At $t = t_1$: $\varepsilon(t_1) = \varepsilon_1$ (full residual strain immediately before unloading)
- As $t \to \infty$: $\varepsilon(\infty) = 0$ (**complete recovery** — a defining feature of the Voigt Model)
- The recovery is **exponential** with the same time constant $\tau$ as the original creep.

> [!NOTE]
> **Significance for KTU:** The complete recovery distinguishes the Voigt Model from the Maxwell Model, which shows **permanent (irrecoverable) deformation** after unloading. The Voigt Model is therefore classified as a model of a **viscoelastic solid**, while the Maxwell Model represents a **viscoelastic liquid**.

---

## 3.5 Summary Table of Derived Equations

| Experiment | Input | Output | Final Equation |
|------------|-------|--------|----------------|
| Creep | $\sigma(t) = \sigma_0$ | $\varepsilon(t) = ?$ | $\varepsilon(t) = \dfrac{\sigma_0}{E}\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$ |
| Stress Relaxation | $\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_0$ | $\sigma(t) = ?$ | $\sigma(t) = E\varepsilon_0$ (constant) |
| Recovery | $\sigma(t) = 0$ for $t \geq t_1$ | $\varepsilon(t) = ?$ | $\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_1 \cdot e^{-(t-t_1)/\tau}$ |
| Creep Compliance | $\sigma(t) = \sigma_0$ | $J(t) = ?$ | $J(t) = \dfrac{1}{E}\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Mechanical Architecture of the Voigt Model

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the **parallel mechanical configuration** of the Voigt Model and the flow of mechanical response during a typical creep experiment.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Applied Step Stress sigma0"] --> B["Voigt Model Parallel System"]
    B --> C["Spring Element E elastic"]
    B --> D["Dashpot Element eta viscous"]
    C --> E["Hookean Stress sigma_spring = E times epsilon"]
    D --> F["Viscous Stress sigma_dashpot = eta times d epsilon over dt"]
    E --> G["Total Stress sigma = sigma_spring + sigma_dashpot"]
    F --> G
    G --> H["Output Strain epsilon of t"]
    H --> I["Phase 1 Elastic Retardation"]
    H --> J["Phase 2 Viscous Flow"]
    H --> K["Phase 3 Steady State Equilibrium"]
    I --> L["Strain Growth Phase"]
    J --> L
    K --> M["Asymptote at sigma0 over E"]
    L --> M
```

### Diagram Interpretation
- **Spring Element (E):** Provides instantaneous elastic resistance.
- **Dashpot Element ($\eta$):** Provides rate-dependent viscous resistance.
- **Parallel Coupling:** Both elements experience the **same strain** $\varepsilon(t)$.
- **Three Phases of Creep Response:** The strain progresses through retardation, viscous flow, and steady-state equilibrium.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Functional Block Architecture: Creep, Stress Relaxation & Recovery

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph INPUT[Input Loading]
        I1["Step Stress Applied sigma0 at t=0"]
        I2["Step Strain Applied epsilon0 at t=0"]
        I3["Stress Removed at t=t1"]
    end

    subgraph VOIGT[Voigt Model Constitutive Engine]
        V1["Constitutive Equation sigma = E epsilon + eta d epsilon over dt"]
        V2["Retardation Time tau = eta over E"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[Output Response]
        O1["Creep Strain epsilon of t = sigma0 over E times 1 minus e to the minus t over tau"]
        O2["Stress Relaxation sigma of t = E epsilon 0 constant"]
        O3["Recovery Strain epsilon of t = epsilon 1 times e to the minus t minus t1 over tau"]
    end

    subgraph ANALYSIS[Engineering Interpretation]
        AN1["Predicts gradual strain development under sustained load"]
        AN2["Predicts NO stress relaxation major limitation"]
        AN3["Predicts complete recoverable strain hallmark feature"]
    end

    I1 --> V1
    I2 --> V1
    I3 --> V1
    V1 --> V2
    V1 --> O1
    V1 --> O2
    V1 --> O3
    O1 --> AN1
    O2 --> AN2
    O3 --> AN3
```

### Interpretation of the Block Architecture
- **Input Loading Block:** Shows the three classical experiments performed on the model.
- **Voigt Model Constitutive Engine:** Represents the core mathematical engine of the parallel spring-dashpot system.
- **Output Response Block:** Maps each input to the corresponding predicted output response.
- **Engineering Interpretation Block:** Translates mathematical predictions into physical engineering insights.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Creep Curve Visualization (Strain vs. Time)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PHASE1[Phase 1: Initial Retardation]
        P1A["t = 0 to t equals tau"]
        P1B["Strain grows from 0 to 0.632 times sigma0 over E"]
    end

    subgraph PHASE2[Phase 2: Viscous Approach]
        P2A["t equals tau to t equals 3 tau"]
        P2B["Strain grows from 0.632 to 0.950 times sigma0 over E"]
    end

    subgraph PHASE3[Phase 3: Steady State]
        P3A["t greater than 3 tau"]
        P3B["Strain asymptotically reaches sigma0 over E"]
    end

    PHASE1 --> PHASE2
    PHASE2 --> PHASE3
```

### Interpretation
- **Phase 1 ($0 < t < \tau$):** Strain grows rapidly toward 63.2% of maximum.
- **Phase 2 ($\tau < t < 3\tau$):** Strain reaches 95% of maximum; rate of growth slows.
- **Phase 3 ($t > 3\tau$):** Strain is essentially at equilibrium; further change is negligible.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**"With a neat sketch, explain the Voigt model. Write its constitutive equation."**

### Model Answer (3 Marks Distribution):
- **Sketch (1 mark):** A diagram showing a spring and dashpot connected in **parallel**, with both elements experiencing the same strain.
- **Definition (1 mark):** The Voigt model is a mechanical analog consisting of a Hookean spring and a Newtonian dashpot arranged in parallel to describe the viscoelastic behavior of polymers.
- **Constitutive Equation (1 mark):**

$$\sigma = E\varepsilon + \eta \frac{d\varepsilon}{dt}$$

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**"Differentiate between Maxwell and Voigt models based on (i) arrangement, (ii) creep response, and (iii) stress relaxation behavior."**

### Model Answer (3 Marks Distribution):
- **(i) Arrangement (1 mark):** Maxwell = spring and dashpot in **series**; Voigt = spring and dashpot in **parallel**.
- **(ii) Creep Response (1 mark):** Maxwell shows **instantaneous elastic jump** followed by linear viscous flow; Voigt shows **gradual exponential approach** to equilibrium strain with no instantaneous jump.
- **(iii) Stress Relaxation (1 mark):** Maxwell shows **exponential decay** of stress; Voigt shows **no stress relaxation** (stress remains constant at $E\varepsilon_0$).

---

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**(a)** Derive the creep compliance expression for the Voigt model under a step-applied constant stress. Explain each step clearly. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A polymer specimen is modeled using the Voigt configuration with $E = 2.5 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$ and $\eta = 5.0 \times 10^8 \text{ Pa·s}$. If a constant stress of $1.0 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$ is suddenly applied, calculate: (i) the retardation time, (ii) the strain at $t = 100 \text{ s}$, and (iii) the time required to reach 90% of the equilibrium strain. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution:

#### Part (a) — Derivation of Creep Compliance (7 Marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** State the Voigt constitutive equation:

$$\sigma(t) = E\varepsilon(t) + \eta \frac{d\varepsilon(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Apply the creep condition $\sigma(t) = \sigma_0$ (constant) and rearrange to obtain a first-order linear ODE:

$$\eta \frac{d\varepsilon}{dt} + E\varepsilon = \sigma_0$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Identify the standard form with characteristic time $\tau = \eta/E$:

$$\frac{d\varepsilon}{dt} + \frac{\varepsilon}{\tau} = \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Solve the ODE by separation of variables or by integrating factor method to obtain the general solution:

$$\varepsilon(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{E} + C \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Apply the initial condition $\varepsilon(0) = 0$ to find $C = -\sigma_0/E$.

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Write the final creep strain expression:

$$\varepsilon(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{E}\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$$

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Define creep compliance as $J(t) = \varepsilon(t)/\sigma_0$:

$$\boxed{J(t) = \frac{1}{E}\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)}$$

Explain that as $t \to \infty$, $J(\infty) = 1/E$, and that there is no instantaneous compliance at $t = 0$.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $E = 2.5 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$
- $\eta = 5.0 \times 10^8 \text{ Pa·s}$
- $\sigma_0 = 1.0 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$

**(i) Retardation Time (2 marks):**

$$\tau = \frac{\eta}{E} = \frac{5.0 \times 10^8}{2.5 \times 10^6} = 200 \text{ s}$$

**(ii) Strain at $t = 100 \text{ s}$ (2 marks):**

$$\varepsilon(100) = \frac{1.0 \times 10^5}{2.5 \times 10^6}\left(1 - e^{-100/200}\right)$$

$$\varepsilon(100) = (0.04)\left(1 - e^{-0.5}\right)$$

$$\varepsilon(100) = (0.04)(1 - 0.6065) = (0.04)(0.3935)$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon(100) \approx 0.01574 \text{ or } 1.574\%}$$

**(iii) Time to reach 90% of equilibrium strain (3 marks):**

The equilibrium strain is:

$$\varepsilon_{\infty} = \frac{\sigma_0}{E} = \frac{1.0 \times 10^5}{2.5 \times 10^6} = 0.04$$

We need $\varepsilon(t) = 0.9 \cdot \varepsilon_{\infty} = 0.9 \cdot 0.04 = 0.036$:

$$0.036 = 0.04 \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$$

$$0.9 = 1 - e^{-t/\tau}$$

$$e^{-t/\tau} = 0.1$$

$$-\frac{t}{\tau} = \ln(0.1) = -2.3026$$

$$t = 2.3026 \times 200 = 460.5 \text{ s}$$

$$\boxed{t \approx 460.5 \text{ seconds} \approx 7.68 \text{ minutes}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**(a)** With a clear diagram, derive the stress-strain constitutive equation of the Voigt model. Hence, explain why the Voigt model fails to represent stress relaxation in polymers. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A creep experiment is performed on a Voigt-modeled polymer with $E = 5.0 \times 10^7 \text{ Pa}$ and $\eta = 2.0 \times 10^9 \text{ Pa·s}$. The applied stress is $\sigma_0 = 2.0 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$. After 500 seconds, the stress is removed. Calculate: (i) the strain at the moment of unloading, (ii) the residual strain 200 seconds after unloading, and (iii) the total recovery time needed for the strain to fall below 1% of its value at unloading. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution:

#### Part (a) — Constitutive Equation and Stress Relaxation Failure (7 Marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Draw a labeled diagram showing a spring (modulus $E$) and a dashpot (viscosity $\eta$) in **parallel**, both attached between two rigid plates.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** State that in parallel, the strain is identical in both elements: $\varepsilon_{\text{spring}} = \varepsilon_{\text{dashpot}} = \varepsilon(t)$.

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Write the stress in the spring using Hooke's law: $\sigma_s = E\varepsilon$.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Write the stress in the dashpot using Newton's law: $\sigma_d = \eta \dfrac{d\varepsilon}{dt}$.

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Apply parallel superposition to obtain:

$$\sigma(t) = E\varepsilon(t) + \eta \frac{d\varepsilon(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** For stress relaxation, apply step strain $\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_0$ (constant), so $\dfrac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = 0$. Substituting:

$$\sigma(t) = E\varepsilon_0 + 0 = E\varepsilon_0 = \text{constant}$$

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Conclude that the stress remains constant at all times, indicating **no stress relaxation**. This is because the dashpot cannot dissipate energy under a fixed displacement (strain rate is zero). Hence the Voigt model is unsuitable for representing stress relaxation in real polymers.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $E = 5.0 \times 10^7 \text{ Pa}$
- $\eta = 2.0 \times 10^9 \text{ Pa·s}$
- $\sigma_0 = 2.0 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$
- Loading time: $t_1 = 500 \text{ s}$

**Preliminary calculation — Retardation time (1 mark included below):**

$$\tau = \frac{\eta}{E} = \frac{2.0 \times 10^9}{5.0 \times 10^7} = 40 \text{ s}$$

**(i) Strain at $t_1 = 500 \text{ s}$ (2 marks):**

$$\varepsilon_1 = \frac{\sigma_0}{E}\left(1 - e^{-t_1/\tau}\right) = \frac{2.0 \times 10^6}{5.0 \times 10^7}\left(1 - e^{-500/40}\right)$$

$$\varepsilon_1 = (0.04)\left(1 - e^{-12.5}\right)$$

Since $e^{-12.5} \approx 3.73 \times 10^{-6}$ (negligibly small):

$$\varepsilon_1 \approx 0.04 \times 1 = 0.04 = 4\%$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_1 \approx 0.04}$$

**(ii) Residual strain 200 s after unloading (2 marks):**

Using recovery equation with $t_2 = 200 \text{ s}$ after unloading:

$$\varepsilon(t_2) = \varepsilon_1 \cdot e^{-t_2/\tau} = 0.04 \cdot e^{-200/40}$$

$$\varepsilon(200) = 0.04 \cdot e^{-5} = 0.04 \times 0.00674$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon(200) \approx 2.696 \times 10^{-4} \text{ or } 0.02696\%}$$

**(iii) Recovery time for strain to fall below 1% of $\varepsilon_1$ (3 marks):**

We require $\varepsilon(t) = 0.01 \cdot \varepsilon_1$:

$$0.01 \cdot \varepsilon_1 = \varepsilon_1 \cdot e^{-(t - t_1)/\tau}$$

$$0.01 = e^{-(t - t_1)/\tau}$$

$$-\frac{(t - t_1)}{\tau} = \ln(0.01) = -4.6052$$

$$(t - t_1) = 4.6052 \times 40 = 184.2 \text{ s}$$

$$\boxed{t - t_1 \approx 184.2 \text{ s} \approx 3.07 \text{ minutes}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Series vs. Parallel:** Writing the Voigt constitutive equation with terms in reciprocal form (like $1/E + 1/\eta$ style) is a fatal error — that belongs to the Maxwell (series) model. Always write the **sum** form for Voigt.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Retardation Time Definition:** Many students compute $\tau$ incorrectly as $\eta \cdot E$ instead of $\eta / E$. Memorize: **retardation time = viscosity ÷ modulus**.
>
