> 3. **Claiming Voigt Can Represent Stress Relaxation:** This is a conceptual error. The Voigt model **CANNOT** show stress relaxation. If an exam question asks "explain why," students who write "the stress decays exponentially" will lose full marks.
>
> 4. **Missing the Initial Condition:** In creep derivations, students often forget to apply $\varepsilon(0) = 0$, leading to an undetermined constant of integration.
>
> 5. **Unit Errors:** Viscosity has units of **Pa·s**, not Pa. Stress and modulus use **Pa**. Strain is dimensionless.
>
> 6. **Mixing Maxwell and Voigt Behaviors:** The Maxwell model shows **permanent deformation** after unloading, while Voigt shows **complete recovery**. Mixing these up is a common 2–3 mark loss.
>
> 7. **Numerical Calculation Errors with Exponentials:** For large $t/\tau$ (e.g., $t = 500$ s, $\tau = 40$ s), the exponential $e^{-12.5}$ is essentially zero. Some students incorrectly retain it as a significant value, leading to wrong final answers.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ⭐ **Voigt Model = Spring $\vert\vert$ Dashpot (parallel configuration).**
- ⭐ **Constitutive Equation:** $\sigma = E\varepsilon + \eta \dfrac{d\varepsilon}{dt}$.
- ⭐ **Retardation Time:** $\tau = \dfrac{\eta}{E}$ — characteristic time scale of viscous response.
- ⭐ **Creep Response:** $\varepsilon(t) = \dfrac{\sigma_0}{E}\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$ — exponential growth toward equilibrium.
- ⭐ **Equilibrium Strain:** $\varepsilon_{\infty} = \dfrac{\sigma_0}{E}$.
- ⭐ **Creep Compliance:** $J(t) = \dfrac{1}{E}\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$.
- ⭐ **Stress Relaxation:** Voigt model predicts **NO relaxation** — $\sigma(t) = E\varepsilon_0$ (constant). This is a key limitation.
- ⭐ **Recovery Response:** $\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_1 e^{-(t-t_1)/\tau}$ — **complete and gradual recovery** to zero.
- ⭐ **Classification:** Voigt model represents a **viscoelastic solid** (recovers fully) vs. Maxwell's **viscoelastic liquid** (permanent deformation).
- ⭐ **Physical Interpretation:** Under load, strain grows slowly; after unloading, strain decays back to zero exponentially.
- ⭐ **Engineering Relevance:** Used for polymer foams, rubber dampers, plastic pipe creep, and biological tissue mechanics.
- ⭐ **Units to Memorize:** $E$ in Pa, $\eta$ in Pa·s, $\tau$ in seconds, $J(t)$ in Pa⁻¹, strain is dimensionless.
- ⭐ **Common Mistake in Exams:** Never confuse the Voigt (parallel) with Maxwell (series) — the constitutive equations, creep curves, and relaxation behaviors are fundamentally different.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Derivation of the Following Equations: Governing Equation for Maxwell Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT402 - POLYMER PHYSICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Derivation of the Following Equations: Governing Equation for Maxwell Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Maxwell Model** (proposed by James Clerk Maxwell in 1867) is a fundamental **viscoelastic constitutive model** that describes the time-dependent mechanical behavior of polymeric materials by combining a perfectly elastic element (Hookean spring) and a perfectly viscous element (Newtonian dashpot) arranged in **series**. The governing equation of the Maxwell model mathematically couples the elastic and viscous response of a material, expressing the total strain rate as the sum of the elastic strain rate and the viscous strain rate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3 – Viscoelastic Behavior):**
> The Maxwell model represents the **simplest linear viscoelastic model** capable of qualitatively predicting **stress relaxation**, but it **fails to describe creep** (since it predicts unbounded linear flow under sustained load). For creep, the **Kelvin–Voigt model** is used, and for both behaviors, the **Standard Linear Solid (SLS) / Zener model** is required.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine pushing a **shock absorber** (used in motorcycles) with your hand:

- The **spring** inside it is stiff — it tries to bounce back instantly, representing the **elastic (instant, recoverable)** part of a polymer.
- The **dashpot** (oil-filled piston) resists motion in a time-dependent, fluid-like manner — it represents the **viscous (slow, irrecoverable)** part.

When you push once, the spring responds first, then the dashpot slowly dissipates the energy. In a **Maxwell model**, the spring and dashpot are connected in **series** (one after the other), so the **same stress** passes through both, but the **strains add up**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world polymer analogy:** A silicone rubber band (polydimethylsiloxane) held stretched at fixed length will gradually lose its internal stress — the molecules slowly rearrange (viscous flow) while the chains remain stretched (elastic). This is exactly what the Maxwell model describes mathematically.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Unit (SI) |
|---|---|---|
| **σ** (sigma) | Stress | **Pa (N/m²)** |
| **ε** (epsilon) | Strain | **dimensionless** |
| **E** | Elastic Modulus (Young's Modulus) | **Pa (N/m²)** |
| **η** (eta) | Coefficient of Viscosity | **Pa·s** |
| **τ** (tau) | Relaxation Time | **seconds (s)** |
| **dε/dt** | Strain Rate | **s⁻¹** |
| **dσ/dt** | Stress Rate | **Pa/s** |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Relaxation Time (τ = η/E)** is the **most important polymer-physics parameter** of the Maxwell model. It represents the characteristic time required for the stress in a deformed polymer to decay to **1/e (~36.8%)** of its original value. For polymers, τ typically ranges from **10⁻⁸ s (rubbery state)** to **10⁶ s (glassy state)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown of the Maxwell Model

The Maxwell model is built on **two fundamental physical laws** combined by the **series mechanical analogy**.

#### Step 1 — Identify the Two Mechanical Elements

- **Spring (Hookean Solid):** Obeys **Hooke's Law** — stress is directly proportional to strain.
- **Dashpot (Newtonian Fluid):** Obeys **Newton's Law of Viscosity** — stress is directly proportional to strain rate.

#### Step 2 — Apply the Series Combination Rules

For elements connected in **series**, the fundamental rule is:

> **Stress in series elements is the SAME; Total strain is the SUM of individual strains.**

Mathematically:
$$\sigma_{spring} = \sigma_{dashpot} = \sigma$$
$$\varepsilon_{total} = \varepsilon_{spring} + \varepsilon_{dashpot}$$

#### Step 3 — Write the Constitutive Law for Each Element

- For the spring: $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon_{spring}$
- For the dashpot: $\sigma = \eta \cdot \dfrac{d\varepsilon_{dashpot}}{dt}$

#### Step 4 — Combine the Two Laws

Differentiate the strain additivity condition with respect to time, and substitute the spring/dashpot laws to obtain the **single governing differential equation**.

#### Step 5 — Define the Relaxation Time

The relaxation time is the ratio of viscosity to modulus:
$$\tau = \dfrac{\eta}{E}$$

This converts the governing equation into a **standard first-order linear ODE** with constant coefficients, easily solvable for stress relaxation, creep, and dynamic mechanical analysis problems.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This table is the absolute minimum a student must memorize for KTU exams on the Maxwell model.**

| Equation No. | Formula | Physical Meaning | Key Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| (1) | $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon_{spring}$ | Hooke's Law (elastic element) | Spring strain calculation |
| (2) | $\sigma = \eta \cdot \dfrac{d\varepsilon_{dashpot}}{dt}$ | Newton's viscosity law (dashpot) | Dashpot strain rate |
| (3) | $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_{spring} + \varepsilon_{dashpot}$ | Series strain additivity | Combination rule |
| (4) | $\dfrac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = \dfrac{1}{E}\dfrac{d\sigma}{dt} + \dfrac{\sigma}{\eta}$ | **Governing equation (general form)** | Master equation of Maxwell model |
| (5) | $\dfrac{d\sigma}{dt} + \dfrac{\sigma}{\tau} = E \cdot \dfrac{d\varepsilon}{dt}$ | **Governing equation (standard form)** | Standard ODE form for solving |
| (6) | $\tau = \dfrac{\eta}{E}$ | Relaxation time definition | Polymer-state identification |
| (7) | $\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$ | Stress relaxation under constant strain | Stress-decay problems |
| (8) | $\varepsilon(t) = \dfrac{\sigma_0}{E} + \dfrac{\sigma_0}{\eta} \cdot t$ | Creep under constant stress | Linear unbounded creep |
| (9) | $E^*(\omega) = \dfrac{E \cdot \omega^2 \tau^2}{1 + \omega^2 \tau^2} + i\dfrac{E\omega\tau}{1+\omega^2\tau^2}$ | Complex dynamic modulus | DMA analysis |
| (10) | $\tan \delta = \dfrac{1}{\omega\tau}$ | Loss tangent | Viscoelastic damping |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Field | Application of Maxwell Model |
|---|---|
| **Polymer Processing** | Predicting die-swell, melt flow, extrusion behavior |
| **Tire Design (Rubber)** | Stress relaxation during rolling contact |
| **Biomedical Polymers** | Predicting long-term behavior of implants (PMMA bone cement) |
| **Packaging Industry** | Stress decay in stretched PET/PP films |
| **Adhesives & Coatings** | Time-dependent bond strength of pressure-sensitive adhesives |
| **3D Printing Polymers** | Predicting warping and residual stress in PLA/ABS parts |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Setup of the Maxwell Model (Series Mechanical Analogy)

The Maxwell element consists of a **spring (modulus E)** and a **dashpot (viscosity η)** connected end-to-end in **series**. When an external stress **σ** is applied to the assembly, the **same stress** transmits through both elements, and the **total strain ε** is the sum of the individual strains.

$$
\sigma_{spring} = \sigma_{dashpot} = \sigma
$$
$$
\varepsilon = \varepsilon_{spring} + \varepsilon_{dashpot}
$$

Let us denote:
- $\varepsilon_1$ = strain in the spring
- $\varepsilon_2$ = strain in the dashpot
- $\varepsilon$ = total strain of the assembly

Therefore:
$$
\varepsilon = \varepsilon_1 + \varepsilon_2 \quad \text{...(i)}
$$

### 3.2 Constitutive Law of the Spring (Hooke's Law)

For a perfectly Hookean spring, stress is directly proportional to strain:
$$
\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon_1
$$

Rearranging to express strain:
$$
\varepsilon_1 = \dfrac{\sigma}{E} \quad \text{...(ii)}
$$

Differentiating equation (ii) with respect to time **t**:
$$
\dfrac{d\varepsilon_1}{dt} = \dfrac{1}{E} \cdot \dfrac{d\sigma}{dt} \quad \text{...(iii)}
$$

This equation (iii) represents the **rate of elastic strain** in the spring. It is non-zero **only when stress is changing with time**.

### 3.3 Constitutive Law of the Dashpot (Newton's Viscosity Law)

For a perfectly Newtonian dashpot, stress is directly proportional to strain rate:
$$
\sigma = \eta \cdot \dfrac{d\varepsilon_2}{dt}
$$

Rearranging to express the strain rate:
$$
\dfrac{d\varepsilon_2}{dt} = \dfrac{\sigma}{\eta} \quad \text{...(iv)}
$$

This equation (iv) represents the **rate of viscous strain** in the dashpot. It is non-zero whenever there is an applied stress, regardless of whether the stress is constant or changing.

### 3.4 Differentiate the Strain Additivity Equation

Differentiating the series combination equation (i) with respect to time:
$$
\dfrac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = \dfrac{d\varepsilon_1}{dt} + \dfrac{d\varepsilon_2}{dt} \quad \text{...(v)}
$$

### 3.5 Substitute the Constitutive Relations

Substituting equations (iii) and (iv) into equation (v):

$$
\dfrac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = \dfrac{1}{E} \cdot \dfrac{d\sigma}{dt} + \dfrac{\sigma}{\eta}
$$

This is the **Governing Equation of the Maxwell Model** in its **general form**:
$$
\boxed{\dfrac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = \dfrac{1}{E}\dfrac{d\sigma}{dt} + \dfrac{\sigma}{\eta}} \quad \text{...(vi)}
$$

### 3.6 Standard Form of the Governing Equation

Multiplying both sides of equation (vi) by **E**:
$$
E \cdot \dfrac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = \dfrac{d\sigma}{dt} + \dfrac{E}{\eta} \cdot \sigma
$$

Defining the **relaxation time** as:
$$
\tau = \dfrac{\eta}{E} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dfrac{E}{\eta} = \dfrac{1}{\tau}
$$

Substituting this into the multiplied equation:
$$
E \cdot \dfrac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = \dfrac{d\sigma}{dt} + \dfrac{\sigma}{\tau}
$$

Rearranging into the standard first-order linear ODE form:
$$
\boxed{\dfrac{d\sigma}{dt} + \dfrac{\sigma}{\tau} = E \cdot \dfrac{d\varepsilon}{dt}} \quad \text{...(vii)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Equation (vii) is the **canonical form** expected in KTU examination answers. A student writing the answer in this form is marked higher because it is directly integrable for various boundary conditions (stress relaxation, creep, dynamic loading).

### 3.7 Verification by Limiting Cases (Analytical Matrix)

The validity of the governing equation can be cross-checked by examining the two limiting physical cases:

| Limiting Condition | Resulting Behavior | Physical Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| **No dashpot** (η → ∞) | $\dfrac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = \dfrac{1}{E}\dfrac{d\sigma}{dt}$ | Pure elastic solid (Hookean) ✓ |
| **No spring** (E → ∞) | $\dfrac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = \dfrac{\sigma}{\eta}$ | Pure viscous fluid (Newtonian) ✓ |
| **Constant stress** (dσ/dt = 0) | $\dfrac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = \dfrac{\sigma}{\eta}$ | Linear creep (steady flow) ✓ |
| **Constant strain** (dε/dt = 0) | $\dfrac{d\sigma}{dt} = -\dfrac{\sigma}{\tau}$ | Exponential stress relaxation ✓ |

### 3.8 Solving the Governing Equation for Stress Relaxation (Bonus)

For a **stress relaxation experiment**, a constant strain ε₀ is applied instantaneously at t = 0. This means dε/dt = 0 for t > 0.

Substituting dε/dt = 0 into equation (vii):
$$
\dfrac{d\sigma}{dt} + \dfrac{\sigma}{\tau} = 0
$$

This is a first-order linear ODE with the well-known exponential solution:
$$
\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \cdot e^{-t/\tau}
$$

where **σ₀ = E·ε₀** is the initial stress at t = 0.

This confirms that the Maxwell model **exponentially relaxes** stress under a held strain — a hallmark of viscoelastic polymer behavior.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mechanical Analogy Diagram — Maxwell Model (Series Spring–Dashpot)

The following Mermaid block renders a **functional block-level architecture** of the Maxwell Model, showing the series mechanical connection, the stress-strain distribution, and the governing equation generation flow.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[APPLIED STRESS sigma] --> B[SPRING ELEMENT]
    A --> C[DASHPOT ELEMENT]
    B --> D1[Hookes Law: sigma = E times epsilon1]
    C --> D2[Newton Viscosity: sigma = eta times depsilon2 by dt]
    D1 --> E[TIME DIFFERENTIATION]
    D2 --> E
    E --> F[STRAIN RATE ADDITIVITY]
    F --> G[GOVERNING EQUATION]
    G --> H[General Form: deps_dt = 1 by E dsigma_dt + sigma by eta]
    G --> I[Standard Form: dsigma_dt + sigma by tau = E deps_dt]
    G --> J[Relaxation Time: tau = eta by E]
```

### 4.2 Logical Flow Diagram — Derivation Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Step 1: Define spring and dashpot in SERIES] --> P2[Step 2: sigma_spring = sigma_dashpot = sigma]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3: epsilon_total = epsilon1 + epsilon2]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4: Differentiate: deps_dt = deps1_dt + deps2_dt]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5: Spring law: deps1_dt = 1 by E dsigma_dt]
    P4 --> P6[Step 6: Dashpot law: deps2_dt = sigma by eta]
    P5 --> P7[Step 7: Substitute both into strain rate equation]
    P6 --> P7
    P7 --> P8[Step 8: Governing Equation: deps_dt = 1 by E dsigma_dt + sigma by eta]
    P8 --> P9[Step 9: Multiply by E and substitute tau = eta by E]
    P9 --> P10[Step 10: Final Standard Form: dsigma_dt + sigma by tau = E deps_dt]
```

### 4.3 Physical Visualization — Stress Transmission in Series Combination

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Fixed Wall] --- S1[Spring E]
    S1 --- D1[Dashpot eta]
    D1 --- L2[Free End with applied stress sigma]
    N1[Note: Same stress sigma passes through spring and dashpot]
    N2[Note: Total strain epsilon = epsilon1 + epsilon2]
    S1 -.-> N1
    D1 -.-> N2
```

### 4.4 Limiting Behavior Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M[Maxwell Governing Equation] --> M1[If eta to infinity]
    M --> M2[If E to infinity]
    M --> M3[If dsigma_dt = 0]
    M --> M4[If deps_dt = 0]
    M1 --> R1[Pure Elastic Hookean Solid]
    M2 --> R2[Pure Viscous Newtonian Fluid]
    M3 --> R3[Linear Steady Creep]
    M4 --> R4[Exponential Stress Relaxation]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**State the governing equation of the Maxwell model and define the term "relaxation time" with its physical significance.**

**Model Answer:**

The governing equation of the Maxwell model in its standard form is:

$$
\dfrac{d\sigma}{dt} + \dfrac{\sigma}{\tau} = E \cdot \dfrac{d\varepsilon}{dt}
$$

where **E** is the elastic modulus of the spring element and **η** is the viscosity of the dashpot element.

The **relaxation time (τ)** is defined as the ratio of viscosity to modulus:

$$
\tau = \dfrac{\eta}{E}
$$

**Physical Significance:** The relaxation time represents the **characteristic time** required for the internal stress in a deformed polymer sample to decay to **1/e (~36.8%)** of its initial value when the sample is held at constant strain. It is the bridge between the **elastic** and **viscous** response times of the material and serves as a **direct measure of the polymer's physical state** — a glassy polymer has very large τ (molecular motion frozen), whereas a rubbery polymer has small τ (chains can rearrange quickly).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**For a Maxwell element subjected to constant stress σ₀, derive the expression for creep strain as a function of time.**

**Model Answer:**

For **constant stress** σ = σ₀, we have dσ/dt = 0. Substituting into the governing equation:

$$
\dfrac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = \dfrac{1}{E} \cdot 0 + \dfrac{\sigma_0}{\eta} = \dfrac{\sigma_0}{\eta}
$$

Integrating both sides with respect to time:

$$
\varepsilon(t) = \dfrac{\sigma_0}{E} + \dfrac{\sigma_0}{\eta} \cdot t
$$

This is the **creep equation** of the Maxwell model. The first term **σ₀/E** is the **instantaneous elastic strain** (from the spring), and the second term **σ₀t/η** is the **linear viscous flow** (from the dashpot). Because the strain grows **unboundedly and linearly with time**, the Maxwell model is **not suitable for describing long-term creep** of solid polymers — it overpredicts deformation. This is a well-known **limitation of the Maxwell model** in KTU-style conceptual questions.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **Maxwell model** for viscoelastic behavior of polymers. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Starting from the constitutive laws of the spring and dashpot, **derive the governing equation** of the Maxwell model in both general and standard forms. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Definition:**
The Maxwell model represents a polymer as a **spring (Hookean element)** and a **dashpot (Newtonian element)** connected in **series**. It qualitatively captures the **stress relaxation** behavior of amorphous polymers above their glass transition temperature.

**Schematic:**

```
   ┌──── Fixed Wall ────┐        ┌──── Free End (Stress σ applied) ────┐
   │                    │        │                                      │
   │        SPRING      │ ─── ── │        DASHPOT                       │
   │      (Modulus E)   │        │      (Viscosity η)                   │
   │       /\/\/\       │        │       | | |                          │
   │       \  /         │        │      ─█─█─█─                         │
   │        \/          │        │                                      │
   └────────────────────┘        └──────────────────────────────────────┘
              ε₁                              ε₂
   ←──────────────── Total strain ε = ε₁ + ε₂ ──────────────────→
   ←────── Same stress σ passes through both elements ──────────→
```

**Key Features:**

- Same stress σ acts on both spring and dashpot (series connection).
- Total strain ε is the sum of spring strain ε₁ and dashpot strain ε₂.
- Spring: obeys Hooke's law — σ = E·ε₁ → elastic, instantaneous.
- Dashpot: obeys Newton's viscosity — σ = η·(dε₂/dt) → viscous, time-dependent.
- **Limitation:** Predicts unbounded creep → not suitable for long-term creep of solid polymers.

#### Model Solution for (b):

Let σ = applied stress, ε = total strain, ε₁ = spring strain, ε₂ = dashpot strain.

**For the spring (Hooke's law):**
$$
\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon_1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \varepsilon_1 = \dfrac{\sigma}{E}
$$

Differentiating with respect to time:
$$
\dfrac{d\varepsilon_1}{dt} = \dfrac{1}{E} \cdot \dfrac{d\sigma}{dt} \quad \text{...(1)}
$$

**For the dashpot (Newton's viscosity law):**
$$
\sigma = \eta \cdot \dfrac{d\varepsilon_2}{dt} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dfrac{d\varepsilon_2}{dt} = \dfrac{\sigma}{\eta} \quad \text{...(2)}
$$

**Series combination (strain additivity):**
$$
\varepsilon = \varepsilon_1 + \varepsilon_2
$$

Differentiating with respect to time:
$$
\dfrac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = \dfrac{d\varepsilon_1}{dt} + \dfrac{d\varepsilon_2}{dt} \quad \text{...(3)}
$$

**Substituting (1) and (2) into (3):**
$$
\dfrac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = \dfrac{1}{E} \cdot \dfrac{d\sigma}{dt} + \dfrac{\sigma}{\eta}
$$

This is the **General Form** of the governing equation.

**Standard form** — multiply by E and substitute τ = η/E:
$$
\dfrac{d\sigma}{dt} + \dfrac{\sigma}{\tau} = E \cdot \dfrac{d\varepsilon}{dt}
$$

This is the **Standard Form** of the Maxwell model governing equation, which is a **first-order linear ODE** suitable for direct integration under various boundary conditions.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model Paper]

**(a)** What is the **relaxation time** in the Maxwell model? Show that the stress in a Maxwell element under constant strain decays exponentially with time. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Maxwell element has a spring modulus **E = 5 × 10⁶ Pa** and a dashpot viscosity **η = 1 × 10⁸ Pa·s**. Calculate the **relaxation time**. If the element is suddenly strained to **ε₀ = 0.02** and held, find the **stress at t = 10 s** and the **time required for the stress to fall to 10% of its initial value**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Definition of Relaxation Time:**
The relaxation time **τ** is defined as the ratio of the dashpot viscosity (η) to the spring modulus (E):
$$
\tau = \dfrac{\eta}{E}
$$

It has units of **seconds** and represents the characteristic time scale of the viscoelastic material.

**Stress Relaxation Derivation:**

For a Maxwell element held at **constant strain** ε = ε₀, we have dε/dt = 0 for all t > 0.

Substituting dε/dt = 0 into the standard governing equation:
$$
\dfrac{d\sigma}{dt} + \dfrac{\sigma}{\tau} = E \cdot 0 = 0
$$

This gives a first-order ODE:
$$
\dfrac{d\sigma}{dt} = -\dfrac{\sigma}{\tau}
$$

Separating variables and integrating:
$$
\int_{\sigma_0}^{\sigma(t)} \dfrac{d\sigma}{\sigma} = -\dfrac{1}{\tau} \int_{0}^{t} dt
$$

$$
\ln\left(\dfrac{\sigma(t)}{\sigma_0}\right) = -\dfrac{t}{\tau}
$$

$$
\boxed{\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \cdot e^{-t/\tau}}
$$

This is the **exponential stress relaxation** of the Maxwell model. The initial stress σ₀ = E·ε₀ decays to **1/e (~36.8%)** of its value in time τ.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:**
- E = 5 × 10⁶ Pa
- η = 1 × 10⁸ Pa·s
- ε₀ = 0.02
- t = 10 s

**Step 1: Calculate the relaxation time**
$$
\tau = \dfrac{\eta}{E} = \dfrac{1 \times 10^{8}}{5 \times 10^{6}} = 20 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate the initial stress**
$$
\sigma_0 = E \cdot \varepsilon_0 = (5 \times 10^{6}) \times 0.02 = 1 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa} = 100 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate the stress at t = 10 s**
$$
\sigma(10) = \sigma_0 \cdot e^{-t/\tau} = 1 \times 10^{5} \cdot e^{-10/20}
$$
$$
\sigma(10) = 1 \times 10^{5} \cdot e^{-0.5} = 1 \times 10^{5} \times 0.6065
$$
$$
\boxed{\sigma(10) \approx 6.065 \times 10^{4} \text{ Pa} \approx 60.65 \text{ kPa}}
$$

**Step 4: Time for stress to fall to 10% of initial value**

We need σ(t) = 0.10·σ₀:
$$
0.10 \cdot \sigma_0 = \sigma_0 \cdot e^{-t/\tau}
$$
$$
0.10 = e^{-t/\tau}
$$
$$
\ln(0.10) = -\dfrac{t}{\tau}
$$
$$
t = -\tau \cdot \ln(0.10) = \tau \cdot \ln(10) = 20 \times 2.3026
$$
$$
\boxed{t \approx 46.05 \text{ s}}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mixing up series vs. parallel rules:** Many students wrongly apply parallel-element rules (strain equality, stress additivity) to the Maxwell model. **Remember: Maxwell = SERIES → Stress equal, Strain additive.**
> 2. **Forgetting the differentiation step:** Equation (iii) and (iv) require **time-differentiation** of the spring law. Writing the final equation without showing this step costs 2 marks.
> 3. **Wrong units for τ:** Students sometimes write τ = E/η instead of η/E. **Relaxation time = viscosity ÷ modulus.**
> 4. **Not mentioning the limitation:** A 7-mark answer that does not state the **failure of the Maxwell model to predict bounded creep** is considered incomplete by KTU examiners.
> 5. **Skipping the series rule statement:** Always begin the derivation with: *"For elements in series, the stress is the same in each element, and the strains are additive."* This is the **first line of credit** in the marking scheme.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ The **Maxwell model** = **Spring (E) + Dashpot (η) in SERIES**.
- ✅ Series rule → **σ same in both elements**, **ε = ε₁ + ε₂**.
- ✅ **General form** of governing equation: $\dfrac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = \dfrac{1}{E}\dfrac{d\sigma}{dt} + \dfrac{\sigma}{\eta}$
- ✅ **Standard form** of governing equation: $\dfrac{d\sigma}{dt} + \dfrac{\sigma}{\tau} = E\dfrac{d\varepsilon}{dt}$
- ✅ **Relaxation time** $\tau = \dfrac{\eta}{E}$ — most important polymer parameter.
- ✅ **Stress relaxation** (constant strain): $\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 e^{-t/\tau}$ — exponential decay.
- ✅ **Creep** (constant stress): $\varepsilon(t) = \dfrac{\sigma_0}{E} + \dfrac{\sigma_0}{\eta} t$ — **linear unbounded**, hence a **limitation**.
- ✅ **Limiting cases:** η → ∞ → elastic solid; E → ∞ → viscous fluid.
- ✅ **Key limitation:** Fails for creep → use **Kelvin–Voigt (parallel)** or **Standard Linear Solid (SLS)** model.
- ✅ **Always quote units:** E in Pa, η in Pa·s, τ in seconds, σ in Pa.
- ✅ **Dynamic behavior:** $\tan \delta = \dfrac{1}{\omega \tau}$ — high at low frequency (rubbery), low at high frequency (glassy).
- ✅ **Memory of exam:** Begin every Maxwell derivation with the **series combination rule** statement — examiners allocate the first 1–2 marks specifically for this.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Equation for Maxwell Model Under Creep
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT402 - POLYMER PHYSICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Equation for Maxwell Model Under Creep

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition
The **Maxwell Model** is the simplest mathematical representation of linear viscoelasticity, conceptualized as a purely elastic spring (obeying Hooke's Law) connected in **series** with a purely viscous dashpot (obeying Newton's Law of Viscosity). The **Equation for the Maxwell Model Under Creep** describes the time-dependent strain response of the material when a **constant stress (σ₀)** is suddenly applied and held constant over time. 

In a **creep experiment**, the model responds with an instantaneous elastic deformation from the spring, followed by a continuous, unbounded viscous flow from the dashpot. The resulting strain equation is:

$$\varepsilon(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{E} + \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta} \cdot t$$

Where:
- $\sigma_0$ = Constant applied stress (**Pa** or **N/m²**)
- $E$ = Modulus of elasticity of the spring (**Pa**)
- $\eta$ = Coefficient of viscosity of the dashpot (**Pa·s** or **Poiseuille (Pl)**)
- $t$ = Time elapsed since the application of stress (**seconds**)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Maxwell Model in series is the foundational mechanical analogue for predicting long-term flow behavior in polymers, especially thermoplastics above their glass transition temperature.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine pushing a **shock absorber (dashpot)** connected to a **coil spring**. If you apply a constant force:
- The **spring** instantly stretches by a fixed amount (like stepping on a car's bumper — it compresses and stops).
- The **dashpot** (oil-filled piston) starts to slowly ooze out and keeps extending forever as long as you hold the force, like honey slowly dripping down a spoon.

A polymer acts similarly: the **elastic part** represents the recoverable, energy-storing bonds in the polymer chains, while the **viscous part** represents the irreversible slippage of the long molecular chains past one another under load.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The Maxwell Model predicts a **linear, unbounded** creep response. It cannot model recovery or finite strain limits, which is why it works well for polymer melts but poorly for cross-linked rubbers.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Mechanical Representation (Series Combination)
In the Maxwell Model, the **spring** and the **dashpot** are arranged **end-to-end (in series)**. This arrangement imposes two critical physical conditions:

1. **Stress Equivalence (Iso-stress condition):** The same stress acts on both elements at all times.
$$\sigma_{total} = \sigma_{spring} = \sigma_{dashpot} = \sigma_0$$
2. **Strain Additivity (Iso-strain condition):** The total deformation is the sum of individual deformations.
$$\varepsilon_{total} = \varepsilon_{spring} + \varepsilon_{dashpot}$$

### Constitutive Equations of the Individual Elements
- **Spring (Hookean Solid):** $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon_{spring}$
  - Differentiating: $\frac{d\sigma}{dt} = E \cdot \frac{d\varepsilon_{spring}}{dt}$
- **Dashpot (Newtonian Fluid):** $\sigma = \eta \cdot \frac{d\varepsilon_{dashpot}}{dt}$

### Creep Logic Step-by-Step
- **Step 1:** A constant stress $\sigma_0$ is applied at $t = 0$ and held steady.
- **Step 2:** Because stress is constant, $\frac{d\sigma}{dt} = 0$.
- **Step 3:** The spring reaches an equilibrium elastic strain instantly: $\varepsilon_{spring} = \frac{\sigma_0}{E}$.
- **Step 4:** The dashpot undergoes continuous viscous flow at a constant rate: $\frac{d\varepsilon_{dashpot}}{dt} = \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta}$.
- **Step 5:** Integrating the dashpot strain over time: $\varepsilon_{dashpot}(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta} \cdot t$.
- **Step 6:** Summing the two contributions yields the Maxwell creep equation.

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter / Formula** | **Symbol / Expression** | **Units** | **Physical Meaning** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Total Creep Strain | $\varepsilon(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{E} + \frac{\sigma_0 t}{\eta}$ | Dimensionless | Time-dependent deformation under constant load |
| Instantaneous Elastic Strain | $\varepsilon_{spring} = \frac{\sigma_0}{E}$ | Dimensionless | Recoverable strain (spring response) |
| Viscous Flow Strain | $\varepsilon_{dashpot} = \frac{\sigma_0 t}{\eta}$ | Dimensionless | Irreversible, time-dependent flow |
| Creep Compliance | $J(t) = \frac{\varepsilon(t)}{\sigma_0} = \frac{1}{E} + \frac{t}{\eta}$ | Pa⁻¹ | Material's tendency to deform under load |
| Retardation Time | $\tau = \frac{\eta}{E}$ | seconds | Characteristic time for viscous flow to dominate |
| Slope of Creep Curve | $\frac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta}$ | s⁻¹ | Constant steady-state creep rate |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** In the plastics and rubber industry, this model is used to predict the **long-term dimensional stability** of plastic pipes, geotextiles, and hot-melt adhesives. A high $\eta$ (viscosity) means the polymer resists flow, ensuring durability in load-bearing applications.

### Limitations of the Maxwell Model in Creep
- Predicts **unbounded linear flow** — in reality, polymers show a saturation/steady-state creep rate.
- Cannot model **instantaneous recovery** when the stress is removed (no spring-only recovery branch).
- Works best for **polymer melts and dilute solutions**, not for highly cross-linked or crystalline solids.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation 1: The General Maxwell Constitutive Equation (Background)
Starting from the iso-strain condition and differentiating with respect to time:

$$\varepsilon_{total} = \varepsilon_{spring} + \varepsilon_{dashpot}$$

Differentiating both sides with respect to time $t$:

$$\frac{d\varepsilon_{total}}{dt} = \frac{d\varepsilon_{spring}}{dt} + \frac{d\varepsilon_{dashpot}}{dt}$$

Substituting the constitutive laws for the spring and dashpot:

$$\frac{d\varepsilon_{total}}{dt} = \frac{1}{E} \cdot \frac{d\sigma}{dt} + \frac{\sigma}{\eta}$$

This is the **generalized Maxwell equation** for arbitrary stress histories.

---

### Derivation 2: The Creep-Specific Equation (Constant Stress Condition)

**Given:** A constant stress $\sigma = \sigma_0$ is applied at time $t = 0$ and maintained indefinitely.

**Step 1 — Condition of constant stress:**
Since the stress does not vary with time:

$$\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{d\sigma}{dt} = 0$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the generalized Maxwell equation:**
The first term vanishes because $\frac{d\sigma}{dt} = 0$:

$$\frac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = \frac{1}{E}(0) + \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the strain rate:**
This leaves a constant strain rate:

$$\frac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta}$$

**Step 4 — Integrate with respect to time:**
Integrate both sides from $0$ to $t$, applying the initial condition $\varepsilon(0) = \varepsilon_0$:

$$\int_{\varepsilon_0}^{\varepsilon(t)} d\varepsilon = \int_0^t \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta} \, dt'$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the integrals:**

$$\varepsilon(t) - \varepsilon_0 = \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta} \cdot t$$

**Step 6 — Define the initial elastic strain:**
At $t = 0$, the dashpot has had no time to deform, so the entire initial strain is borne by the spring:

$$\varepsilon_0 = \varepsilon_{spring}(0) = \frac{\sigma_0}{E}$$

**Step 7 — Combine the two contributions to obtain the final creep equation:**

$$\boxed{\varepsilon(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{E} + \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta} \cdot t}$$

---

### Derivation 3: Creep Compliance Form

Dividing the strain equation by the applied stress $\sigma_0$ yields the **creep compliance** $J(t)$, a material property independent of load magnitude:

$$J(t) = \frac{\varepsilon(t)}{\sigma_0} = \frac{1}{E} + \frac{t}{\eta}$$

This form is particularly useful in experimental rheology because it allows comparison between different polymers tested at different stress levels.

---

### Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A Maxwell element has a spring modulus $E = 5 \times 10^8 \text{ Pa}$ and dashpot viscosity $\eta = 1 \times 10^{10} \text{ Pa}\cdot\text{s}$. A constant stress of $\sigma_0 = 2 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$ is applied. Calculate: (a) the instantaneous strain, (b) the strain after 100 seconds, and (c) the retardation time.

**Solution:**

**(a) Instantaneous Strain (at $t = 0$):**
$$\varepsilon(0) = \frac{\sigma_0}{E} = \frac{2 \times 10^6}{5 \times 10^8} = 4 \times 10^{-3} = 0.4\%$$

**(b) Strain at $t = 100$ s:**
$$\varepsilon(100) = \frac{2 \times 10^6}{5 \times 10^8} + \frac{(2 \times 10^6)(100)}{1 \times 10^{10}}$$
$$\varepsilon(100) = 0.004 + 0.02 = 0.024 = 2.4\%$$

**(c) Retardation Time:**
$$\tau = \frac{\eta}{E} = \frac{1 \times 10^{10}}{5 \times 10^8} = 20 \text{ s}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram 1: Maxwell Model Physical Layout

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Fixed Wall] -->|Connection| B((Spring: E))
    B -->|Connection| C((Dashpot: eta))
    C -->|Connection| D[Movable Plate]
    E[Constant Force sigma0] -.->|Applied| D
    
    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#ff9900,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#009933,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px
```

**Description:** A spring and dashpot are connected end-to-end between a fixed wall and a movable plate. A constant force $\sigma_0$ pulls the plate, causing simultaneous spring extension and dashpot displacement.

---

### Diagram 2: Block-Level Functional Architecture of Creep Response

```mermaid
graph TD
    input[Input: Constant Stress sigma0] --> stepA[Step A: Spring Element]
    input --> stepB[Step B: Dashpot Element]
    
    stepA --> outputA[Elastic Strain: sigma0 / E]
    stepB --> outputB[Viscous Strain: sigma0 times t / eta]
    
    outputA --> sum[Superposition Block]
    outputB --> sum
    
    sum --> final[Output: Total Strain epsilon of t]
    final --> graph[Creep Curve: Linear with Positive Slope]
    
    style input fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#ff9900,stroke-width:2px
    style stepA fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px
    style stepB fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#009933,stroke-width:2px
    style sum fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px
    style final fill:#f0e1ff,stroke:#6600cc,stroke-width:2px
    style graph fill:#e1e1ff,stroke:#3333cc,stroke-width:2px
```

**Description:** The applied constant stress splits into two parallel computational pathways — the spring branch (instantaneous elastic response) and the dashpot branch (time-dependent viscous flow). These are mathematically summed (superposition principle) to yield the total strain, which manifests as a linearly increasing creep curve on a strain-vs-time plot.

---

### Diagram 3: Maxwell Creep Curve Schematic

```mermaid
graph TD
    axis[Plot: Strain vs Time] --> point0[Origin: 0 comma 0]
    point0 -->|Jump at t=0| point1[Point: sigma0 over E, Instantaneous Elastic Strain]
    point1 -->|Linear Slope: sigma0 over eta| point2[Point: Strain at time t1]
    point2 -->|Continuous Linear Flow| point3[Point: Unbounded Viscous Flow]
    
    style axis fill:#f5f5f5,stroke:#333333,stroke-width:2px
    style point0 fill:#000000,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:3px
    style point1 fill:#ff9900,stroke:#ff9900,stroke-width:2px
    style point2 fill:#009933,stroke:#009933,stroke-width:2px
    style point3 fill:#cc0000,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px
```

**Description:** The Maxwell creep curve has two distinct regions: (1) a vertical jump at $t = 0$ representing the spring's elastic response, and (2) a straight line with constant positive slope $\sigma_0 / \eta$ representing the dashpot's continuous viscous flow.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`**
**State and explain the Maxwell Model of linear viscoelasticity. How does it represent the creep behavior of a polymer?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Maxwell Model represents a viscoelastic material as a **spring (elastic element)** and a **dashpot (viscous element)** connected in **series**. Under a constant applied stress, the model shows an **instantaneous elastic deformation** equal to $\sigma_0 / E$ (spring response), followed by a **linear, time-dependent viscous flow** equal to $\sigma_0 t / \eta$ (dashpot response). The total strain is the sum of these two components:
$$\varepsilon(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{E} + \frac{\sigma_0 t}{\eta}$$
This model is suitable for representing **polymer melts and thermoplastics above $T_g$** that exhibit continuous flow under load.

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`**
**Distinguish between the Maxwell and Kelvin-Voigt models in terms of their mechanical arrangement and creep response.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **Feature** | **Maxwell Model** | **Kelvin-Voigt Model** |
|---|---|---|
| Arrangement | Spring and dashpot in **series** | Spring and dashpot in **parallel** |
| Stress condition | Iso-stress: same stress in both elements | Iso-strain: same strain in both elements |
| Creep response | Instantaneous jump + linear flow | Delayed exponential approach to equilibrium |
| Recovery | Only partial (no spring-only path) | Complete recovery upon stress removal |

---

### Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** Derive the **constitutive equation** for the Maxwell Model and show that under a constant applied stress $\sigma_0$, the strain response is given by $\varepsilon(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{E} + \frac{\sigma_0 t}{\eta}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Maxwell element has $E = 2 \times 10^9 \text{ Pa}$ and $\eta = 5 \times 10^{11} \text{ Pa}\cdot\text{s}$. If a constant stress of $1 \times 10^7 \text{ Pa}$ is applied for 50 seconds, calculate: (i) the instantaneous elastic strain, (ii) the total strain at $t = 50$ s, and (iii) the retardation time. Comment on the dominant deformation mode. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**
For a Maxwell element with spring (modulus $E$) and dashpot (viscosity $\eta$) in series:
$$\varepsilon = \varepsilon_{spring} + \varepsilon_{dashpot}$$
Differentiating: $\frac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = \frac{1}{E}\frac{d\sigma}{dt} + \frac{\sigma}{\eta}$
Under constant stress: $\frac{d\sigma}{dt} = 0$ and $\sigma = \sigma_0$.
Therefore: $\frac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta}$
Integrating with initial condition $\varepsilon(0) = \sigma_0 / E$:
$$\varepsilon(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{E} + \frac{\sigma_0 t}{\eta}$$

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**
(i) Instantaneous strain: $\varepsilon(0) = \frac{10^7}{2 \times 10^9} = 5 \times 10^{-3} = 0.5\%$
(ii) Total strain: $\varepsilon(50) = 5 \times 10^{-3} + \frac{(10^7)(50)}{5 \times 10^{11}} = 5 \times 10^{-3} + 10^{-3} = 6 \times 10^{-3} = 0.6\%$
(iii) Retardation time: $\tau = \frac{5 \times 10^{11}}{2 \times 10^9} = 250 \text{ s}$
Since the observation time (50 s) is much less than $\tau$ (250 s), the **elastic (spring) response dominates** in this short interval.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **mechanical analogue of the Maxwell Model**. Define **creep compliance** and derive its expression for the Maxwell element. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A polymer sample behaves as a Maxwell element with $E = 1 \times 10^8 \text{ Pa}$ and $\eta = 1 \times 10^9 \text{ Pa}\cdot\text{s}$. The sample is subjected to a constant stress of $5 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$. Plot the **qualitative nature of the creep curve** and determine the time at which the viscous strain equals the elastic strain. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Sketch and Compliance (7 marks):**
The Maxwell Model consists of a spring and dashpot in series, sharing a common stress. The **creep compliance** $J(t)$ is defined as the ratio of time-dependent strain to the constant applied stress:
$$J(t) = \frac{\varepsilon(t)}{\sigma_0} = \frac{1}{E} + \frac{t}{\eta}$$
It has units of Pa⁻¹ and is a fundamental material property used to compare viscoelastic responses independent of load magnitude.

**(b) Numerical Analysis (7 marks):**
Setting viscous strain equal to elastic strain:
$$\frac{\sigma_0 t}{\eta} = \frac{\sigma_0}{E}$$
$$t = \frac{\eta}{E} = \frac{10^9}{10^8} = 10 \text{ s}$$
The creep curve shows an instantaneous jump of $5 \times 10^6 / 10^8 = 0.05$ (or 5%) at $t = 0$, followed by a linear increase at a rate of $\sigma_0 / \eta = 5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}^{-1}$. At $t = 10$ s, total strain = 0.10 (10%).

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Leading to Mark Deduction:**
> 1. **Forgetting units of $\eta$:** Students often write $\eta$ in wrong units (e.g., Pa instead of Pa·s). Always include seconds in the viscosity unit to avoid dimensional inconsistency.
> 2. **Mixing Maxwell with Kelvin-Voigt:** The series arrangement (Maxwell) and parallel arrangement (Kelvin-Voigt) yield **opposite** equations. Confusing them is the #1 reason for losing full marks.
> 3. **Skipping the initial condition:** Always state that $\varepsilon(0) = \sigma_0 / E$ before integrating. Without it, the integration constant is undefined.
> 4. **Assuming recovery exists in Maxwell:** The Maxwell model has **no instantaneous recovery** when stress is removed — only the elastic portion recovers. Do not claim complete recovery.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Maxwell Model = Spring (E) + Dashpot (η) in SERIES**
- **Creep Equation:** $\varepsilon(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{E} + \frac{\sigma_0 t}{\eta}$ (linear in time)
- **Creep Compliance:** $J(t) = \frac{1}{E} + \frac{t}{\eta}$ (material property)
- **Retardation Time:** $\tau = \frac{\eta}{E}$ — the time at which elastic and viscous strains are equal
- **Iso-stress condition:** $\sigma_{spring} = \sigma_{dashpot} = \sigma_0$
- **Iso-strain condition:** $\varepsilon_{total} = \varepsilon_{spring} + \varepsilon_{dashpot}$
- The Maxwell Model predicts **unbounded viscous flow** — it represents polymer melts and dilute solutions well, but not cross-linked rubbers.
- For $t \ll \tau$: **elastic behavior dominates** (spring-like).
- For $t \gg \tau$: **viscous flow dominates** (dashpot-like, liquid-like).
- Always write the **generalized constitutive equation first** before applying the creep-specific simplification.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Equation for Maxwell Model Under Stress Relaxation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT402 - POLYMER PHYSICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Equation for Maxwell Model Under Stress Relaxation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU-Standard Definition

The **Maxwell Model** is the simplest viscoelastic constitutive model that captures **stress relaxation** behavior in a polymer. It represents the material as a **linear spring (Hookean element, modulus $E$)** and a **linear dashpot (Newtonian element, viscosity $\eta$)** connected **in series**.

In a **stress relaxation experiment**, a **constant strain $\varepsilon_0$** is imposed instantaneously at $t = 0$ and held fixed thereafter. The stress required to maintain this deformation is not constant — it **decays exponentially with time**. The equation governing this decay is the **Maxwell stress relaxation equation**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCPOT402 – Module 3):**
> Under a constant applied strain, the stress in a Maxwell element relaxes as:
> $$\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \, e^{-t/\tau}$$
> where $\tau = \eta / E$ is the **characteristic relaxation time** of the material.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine stretching a **rubber band with a viscous honey blob glued to it**:

- The **spring** is the rubber band — it wants to **snap back** immediately when pulled.
- The **dashpot** is the honey blob — it **flows slowly** under load and has no memory of being stretched.
- Connect them **end-to-end (series)**, and you get the Maxwell element.

When you suddenly pull the combined element to a fixed length and hold it there:
- At $t = 0$, **the spring carries the full load** (it resists instantly).
- Over time, the **dashpot gradually takes over** the deformation, easing the load on the spring.
- The **stress you must apply to keep the length constant** therefore **drops progressively**, eventually approaching zero.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world example:** A polymer chain under sudden stretching. The covalent bonds (spring-like) react instantly, but the chain segments rearrange via rotation around bonds (dashpot-like). The internal stress you measure drops over time — this is stress relaxation.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Spring modulus ($E$)**: Measured in **Pa** (or **MPa**). Represents **elastic stiffness**.
- **Dashpot viscosity ($\eta$)**: Measured in **Pa·s** (or **Poise**, where $1 \text{ P} = 0.1 \text{ Pa·s}$). Represents **flow resistance**.
- **Relaxation time ($\tau = \eta / E$)**: Measured in **seconds (s)**. The time required for the stress to decay to $1/e \approx 36.8\%$ of its initial value.
- **Initial stress ($\sigma_0 = E \, \varepsilon_0$)**: The instantaneous stress at $t = 0$ carried entirely by the spring.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Tip:** A common KTU pitfall is confusing **relaxation time** (Maxwell, $\eta/E$) with **retardation time** (Kelvin–Voigt, $\eta/E$ but under creep, not relaxation). Remember: **relaxation = stress decay; retardation = strain growth**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Elements and Their Governing Laws

The Maxwell model is built from two ideal mechanical elements:

**(a) The Linear Spring (Hookean Solid)**

- **Stress–strain law:** $\sigma = E \, \varepsilon_s$
- Differentiated with respect to time: $\dfrac{d\sigma}{dt} = E \dfrac{d\varepsilon_s}{dt}$

**(b) The Linear Dashpot (Newtonian Fluid)**

- **Stress–strain-rate law:** $\sigma = \eta \dfrac{d\varepsilon_d}{dt}$
- Rearranged: $\dfrac{d\varepsilon_d}{dt} = \dfrac{\sigma}{\eta}$

> [!NOTE]
> Both elements carry the **same stress** because they are connected in series. The **strains add up**:
> $$\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_s(t) + \varepsilon_d(t)$$

### 2.2 The Stress Relaxation Condition

In a stress relaxation test:
- A **step strain** $\varepsilon_0$ is applied at $t = 0$.
- The strain is then **held constant**: $\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_0$ for $t \geq 0$.
- Therefore: $\dfrac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = 0$ for $t > 0$.

Differentiating the additive strain law:

$$\frac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = \frac{d\varepsilon_s}{dt} + \frac{d\varepsilon_d}{dt} = 0$$

Substituting the element laws:

$$\frac{1}{E} \frac{d\sigma}{dt} + \frac{\sigma}{\eta} = 0$$

This is a **first-order linear ordinary differential equation** in $\sigma(t)$, whose solution gives the **Maxwell stress relaxation equation**.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Symbol** | **Quantity** | **Formula / Definition** | **SI Unit** |
|---|---|---|---|
| $E$ | Spring modulus (elastic) | Material property | $\text{Pa}$ |
| $\eta$ | Dashpot viscosity | Material property | $\text{Pa·s}$ |
| $\sigma_0$ | Initial stress at $t = 0$ | $\sigma_0 = E \, \varepsilon_0$ | $\text{Pa}$ |
| $\tau$ | Relaxation time | $\tau = \eta / E$ | $\text{s}$ |
| $\sigma(t)$ | Stress at time $t$ | $\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \, e^{-t/\tau}$ | $\text{Pa}$ |
| $G(t)$ | Relaxation modulus | $G(t) = E \, e^{-t/\tau}$ | $\text{Pa}$ |
| $\eta_{\text{app}}(t)$ | Apparent viscosity during relaxation | $\eta_{\text{app}} = \sigma(t) / \dot{\varepsilon} \to \infty$ (since $\dot{\varepsilon} = 0$) | — |
| $E_{\text{rel}}$ | Relaxed modulus (at $t \to \infty$) | $0$ (Maxwell has no equilibrium modulus) | $\text{Pa}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-Mandated Boundary Conditions for the ODE:**
> - Initial condition: $\sigma(0) = \sigma_0 = E \, \varepsilon_0$
> - Final condition: $\sigma(\infty) = 0$

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Polymer Processing:** Predicting how molded plastic parts relieve internal stresses after cooling — critical for dimensional stability.
- **Rubber Industry:** Characterizing elastomer behavior under sustained deformation (e.g., gaskets, O-rings).
- **Biomedical Polymers:** Designing surgical sutures and stents that must relax stress to avoid tissue damage.
- **Adhesives & Coatings:** Estimating long-term holding force in pressure-sensitive tapes.
- **Geophysical Materials:** Modeling stress decay in ice, bitumen, and rock salt.

> [!TIP]
> **Industrial Note:** A stress-relaxation experiment is the **standard ASTM D1646 / ISO 6721** test for determining $\eta$ and $E$ of a viscoelastic solid polymer at a given temperature.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Full Derivation of the Maxwell Stress Relaxation Equation

**Step 1 — Set up the model.**

The Maxwell element is a **spring (modulus $E$)** and a **dashpot (viscosity $\eta$)** in **series**. The total strain $\varepsilon$ is the sum of the individual element strains:

$$\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_s(t) + \varepsilon_d(t)$$

This is the **additive strain (series) condition**.

**Step 2 — Apply the stress relaxation boundary condition.**

The experiment imposes a **step strain** of magnitude $\varepsilon_0$ at $t = 0$ and holds it constant:

$$\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_0 \quad \text{for all} \quad t \geq 0$$

Differentiating with respect to time:

$$\frac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = 0 \quad \text{for} \quad t > 0$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate the additive strain law.**

$$\frac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = \frac{d\varepsilon_s}{dt} + \frac{d\varepsilon_d}{dt} = 0$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the constitutive laws of each element.**

For the **spring** (Hooke's law differentiated):

$$\frac{d\varepsilon_s}{dt} = \frac{1}{E} \frac{d\sigma}{dt}$$

For the **dashpot** (Newton's law rearranged):

$$\frac{d\varepsilon_d}{dt} = \frac{\sigma}{\eta}$$

Substituting both into the differentiated strain equation:

$$\frac{1}{E} \frac{d\sigma}{dt} + \frac{\sigma}{\eta} = 0$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange into a standard first-order linear ODE.**

$$\frac{d\sigma}{dt} + \frac{E}{\eta} \sigma = 0$$

This is a **homogeneous, first-order, linear ODE** with constant coefficients.

**Step 6 — Separate variables and integrate.**

Rearranging:

$$\frac{d\sigma}{\sigma} = -\frac{E}{\eta} \, dt$$

Integrating both sides from the initial state $(0, \sigma_0)$ to a general state $(t, \sigma(t))$:

$$\int_{\sigma_0}^{\sigma(t)} \frac{d\sigma'}{\sigma'} = -\frac{E}{\eta} \int_0^t dt'$$

**Step 7 — Evaluate the integrals.**

$$\left[ \ln \sigma' \right]_{\sigma_0}^{\sigma(t)} = -\frac{E}{\eta} \, t$$

$$\ln \sigma(t) - \ln \sigma_0 = -\frac{E}{\eta} \, t$$

**Step 8 — Exponentiate both sides.**

$$\ln \left( \frac{\sigma(t)}{\sigma_0} \right) = -\frac{E}{\eta} \, t$$

$$\frac{\sigma(t)}{\sigma_0} = e^{-(E/\eta) \, t}$$

**Step 9 — Define the relaxation time and write the final equation.**

Define the **characteristic relaxation time** $\tau$:

$$\tau \equiv \frac{\eta}{E}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \, e^{-t/\tau}}$$

This is the **Maxwell stress relaxation equation**, the most important result in this module.

**Step 10 — Verify the boundary conditions.**

- At $t = 0$: $\sigma(0) = \sigma_0 \, e^{0} = \sigma_0$ ✓ (matches $E \, \varepsilon_0$)
- At $t = \tau$: $\sigma(\tau) = \sigma_0 \, e^{-1} \approx 0.368 \, \sigma_0$ ✓ (stress drops to ~36.8%)
- At $t \to \infty$: $\sigma(\infty) = \sigma_0 \, e^{-\infty} = 0$ ✓ (stress fully relaxes — Maxwell model has **no equilibrium modulus**)

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Special Time Points

| **Time $t$** | **Stress $\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 e^{-t/\tau}$** | **Fraction of $\sigma_0$** | **Physical Meaning** |
|---|---|---|---|
| $0$ | $\sigma_0$ | $100\%$ | All load on the spring |
| $\tau$ | $\sigma_0 / e$ | $36.79\%$ | **Definition of relaxation time** |
| $2\tau$ | $\sigma_0 / e^2$ | $13.53\%$ | Stress nearly dissipated |
| $3\tau$ | $\sigma_0 / e^3$ | $4.98\%$ | Stress essentially zero |
| $5\tau$ | $\sigma_0 / e^5$ | $0.674\%$ | Practically complete relaxation |
| $\infty$ | $0$ | $0\%$ | Full stress decay (no equilibrium) |

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A Maxwell element has $E = 2 \times 10^6$ Pa and $\eta = 4 \times 10^8$ Pa·s. A step strain of $\varepsilon_0 = 0.01$ is applied. Calculate the stress at $t = 100$ s.

**Solution:**

$$\tau = \frac{\eta}{E} = \frac{4 \times 10^8}{2 \times 10^6} = 200 \text{ s}$$

$$\sigma_0 = E \, \varepsilon_0 = (2 \times 10^6)(0.01) = 2 \times 10^4 \text{ Pa} = 20 \text{ kPa}$$

$$\sigma(100) = (2 \times 10^4) \, e^{-100/200} = (2 \times 10^4) \, e^{-0.5}$$

$$e^{-0.5} \approx 0.6065$$

$$\sigma(100) \approx (2 \times 10^4)(0.6065) = 1.213 \times 10^4 \text{ Pa} = 12.13 \text{ kPa}$$

**Answer:** The stress has relaxed from **20 kPa to ~12.13 kPa** in 100 seconds.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Maxwell Element — Physical Series Configuration

The Maxwell model is a **spring and dashpot connected end-to-end**. The following Mermaid block-diagram illustrates the **functional architecture** of the element and the **flow of strain / stress** during a stress relaxation test.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[STEP STRAIN APPLIED epsilon0] --> B[Fixed Platen - Constant Displacement]
    B --> C{Maxwell Element - Series}
    C --> D[Spring E - Carries Load Instantly]
    C --> E[Dashpot eta - Dissipates Strain Slowly]
    D --> F[Total Stress sigma t]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Sensor Records Decay]
    G --> H[sigma t equals sigma0 exp negative t over tau]
    H --> I[Stress approaches zero as t increases]
```

### 4.2 Stress Decay Curve (Schematic Block Layout)

The following flowchart shows the **time evolution of the normalized stress** $\sigma(t)/\sigma_0$ at the critical instants used in KTU exam problems.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Time t equals 0] --> V0[Stress ratio equals 1.00]
    P1[Time t equals tau] --> V1[Stress ratio equals 0.368]
    P2[Time t equals 2 tau] --> V2[Stress ratio equals 0.135]
    P3[Time t equals 3 tau] --> V3[Stress ratio equals 0.050]
    P4[Time t equals infinity] --> V4[Stress ratio equals 0.000]
    V0 --> V1 --> V2 --> V3 --> V4
```

### 4.3 State Diagram — How Stress Evolves in Each Element

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1[State at t equals 0]
        SA[Spring is Stretched]
        SB[Dashpot has not moved yet]
    end
    subgraph S2[State at t equals tau]
        SC[Spring is partially relaxed]
        SD[Dashpot has flowed visibly]
    end
    subgraph S3[State at t large]
        SE[Spring is almost fully relaxed]
        SF[Dashpot has absorbed most strain]
    end
    S1 --> S2 --> S3
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Exam-Oriented)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    IN[Input - Step Strain epsilon0 at t equals 0] --> M1[Constitutive Equation - 1 over E d sigma dt plus sigma over eta equals 0]
    M1 --> M2[Define relaxation time tau equals eta over E]
    M2 --> M3[Apply Initial Condition sigma 0 equals E epsilon 0]
    M3 --> OUT[Output - sigma t equals sigma 0 exp negative t over tau]
    OUT --> V1[Verify sigma 0 at t equals 0]
    OUT --> V2[Verify sigma tau equals sigma 0 over e]
    OUT --> V3[Verify sigma infinity equals 0]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> Define the **Maxwell model** for a viscoelastic material. Sketch the model and write down the governing equation for **stress relaxation**.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **(1 mark)** The Maxwell model is the simplest representation of a viscoelastic material, consisting of a **linear spring (modulus $E$)** and a **linear dashpot (viscosity $\eta$)** connected **in series**.
- **(1 mark)** Under a constant applied strain $\varepsilon_0$, the stress in the element **decays exponentially** with time as the dashpot gradually flows and relieves the spring.
- **(1 mark)** The governing equation is:
$$\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \, e^{-t/\tau}, \quad \text{where} \quad \tau = \eta / E$$

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> What is **relaxation time** in a Maxwell element? What is its physical significance?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **(1 mark)** The relaxation time is defined as:
$$\tau = \frac{\eta}{E}$$
- **(1 mark)** It has units of **seconds** and represents the time required for the stress to decay to $1/e \approx 36.8\%$ of its initial value $\sigma_0$.
- **(1 mark)** Physically, $\tau$ is the characteristic time for **molecular rearrangement** in the polymer. If the experimental timescale $t \gg \tau$, the material behaves like a **viscous fluid**; if $t \ll \tau$, it behaves like an **elastic solid**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **Q3(a). [KTU University Exam – May 2024 – Module 3, 14 marks]**
> **(a)** Derive the equation for stress relaxation in a **Maxwell model** under a constant imposed strain. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** For a Maxwell element with $E = 5 \times 10^5$ Pa and $\eta = 1 \times 10^8$ Pa·s, a step strain $\varepsilon_0 = 0.05$ is applied at $t = 0$. Calculate (i) the relaxation time, (ii) the initial stress, and (iii) the stress remaining at $t = 300$ s. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Define the Maxwell element. Spring and dashpot in series:

$$\varepsilon = \varepsilon_s + \varepsilon_d$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** State the stress relaxation condition: $\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_0$, so $d\varepsilon/dt = 0$.

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Differentiate the strain law:

$$\frac{d\varepsilon_s}{dt} + \frac{d\varepsilon_d}{dt} = 0$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Substitute element laws:

$$\frac{1}{E} \frac{d\sigma}{dt} + \frac{\sigma}{\eta} = 0$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Rearrange and integrate:

$$\int_{\sigma_0}^{\sigma(t)} \frac{d\sigma'}{\sigma'} = -\frac{E}{\eta} \int_0^t dt'$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Solve and exponentiate:

$$\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \, e^{-(E/\eta) t}$$

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Define $\tau = \eta / E$ to obtain the final boxed result:

$$\boxed{\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \, e^{-t/\tau}}$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Calculate relaxation time:

$$\tau = \frac{\eta}{E} = \frac{1 \times 10^8}{5 \times 10^5} = 200 \text{ s}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Calculate initial stress:

$$\sigma_0 = E \, \varepsilon_0 = (5 \times 10^5)(0.05) = 2.5 \times 10^4 \text{ Pa} = 25 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 3 (3 marks):** Calculate stress at $t = 300$ s:

$$\sigma(300) = (2.5 \times 10^4) \, e^{-300/200} = (2.5 \times 10^4) \, e^{-1.5}$$

$$e^{-1.5} \approx 0.2231$$

$$\sigma(300) \approx (2.5 \times 10^4)(0.2231) \approx 5578 \text{ Pa} \approx 5.58 \text{ kPa}$$

**Final Answer:** $\tau = 200$ s, $\sigma_0 = 25$ kPa, $\sigma(300) \approx 5.58$ kPa.

---

> **Q3(b). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 – Module 3, 14 marks — OR Question]**
> **(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **Maxwell model** for a viscoelastic polymer. Define **relaxation time** and discuss its significance. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Derive the **relaxation modulus** $G(t)$ for the Maxwell element and show that it decays exponentially. A polymer has $G(0) = 10^7$ Pa and $\tau = 50$ s. Find the stress after 75 s under a step strain of 0.01. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Sketch: spring and dashpot in series (describe verbally since sketches cannot be auto-rendered here).

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Define Maxwell model: series combination of Hookean spring (modulus $E$) and Newtonian dashpot (viscosity $\eta$); carries same stress, strains add.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Define relaxation time $\tau = \eta / E$ — time for stress to decay to $1/e$ of $\sigma_0$.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Significance: $\tau$ sets the boundary between elastic-like ($t \ll \tau$) and viscous-like ($t \gg \tau$) response. Determines polymer behavior in processing and end-use.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Step 1 (2 marks):** The relaxation modulus is defined as:

$$G(t) = \frac{\sigma(t)}{\varepsilon_0} = E \, e^{-t/\tau}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Starting from the derived stress relation $\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 e^{-t/\tau} = E \varepsilon_0 e^{-t/\tau}$, divide by $\varepsilon_0$ to get $G(t)$.

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Identify $G(0) = E = 10^7$ Pa and $\tau = 50$ s.

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Compute the stress at $t = 75$ s, $\varepsilon_0 = 0.01$:

$$\sigma(75) = G(75) \cdot \varepsilon_0 = (10^7) \, e^{-75/50} \cdot 0.01$$

$$= (10^7)(e^{-1.5})(0.01) = (10^7)(0.2231)(0.01)$$

$$\sigma(75) \approx 2.231 \times 10^4 \text{ Pa} = 22.31 \text{ kPa}$$

**Final Answer:** $\sigma(75) \approx 22.31$ kPa.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mixing up series vs. parallel (Kelvin–Voigt):** In Maxwell, strains ADD; in Kelvin–Voigt, stresses ADD. Confusing the two will cost **all 7 marks** in derivation questions.
> 2. **Forgetting the initial condition:** Many students write $\sigma(t) = A e^{-t/\tau}$ but forget to determine the constant $A = \sigma_0$ from $\sigma(0) = E \varepsilon_0$. This loses **1–2 marks**.
> 3. **Unit errors in $\tau$:** Writing $\tau = E/\eta$ (inverted!) is a classic blunder. Always cross-check: $\text{Pa·s} / \text{Pa} = \text{s}$ ✓.
> 4. **Treating Maxwell as a solid:** The Maxwell model **cannot support a sustained load** ($\sigma \to 0$ as $t \to \infty$); it is **not** suitable for long-term creep analysis. Use the **standard linear solid (Zener) model** for that.
> 5. **Missing the definition of $G(t)$:** Always state the relaxation modulus as $G(t) = \sigma(t)/\varepsilon_0$ before using it.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ The **Maxwell model** is a **spring + dashpot in series**.
- ✅ **Stress relaxation equation:** $\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \, e^{-t/\tau}$
- ✅ **Initial stress:** $\sigma_0 = E \, \varepsilon_0$ (carried entirely by the spring at $t = 0$).
- ✅ **Relaxation time:** $\tau = \eta / E$ — time for stress to drop to $36.8\%$ of $\sigma_0$.
- ✅ **Relaxation modulus:** $G(t) = E \, e^{-t/\tau}$.
- ✅ **Series condition:** $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_s + \varepsilon_d$ (strains add, stresses are equal).
- ✅ **Governing ODE:** $\dfrac{1}{E} \dfrac{d\sigma}{dt} + \dfrac{\sigma}{\eta} = 0$
- ✅ **Boundary conditions:** $\sigma(0) = \sigma_0$, $\sigma(\infty) = 0$.
- ✅ **At $t = \tau$:** stress is $0.368 \, \sigma_0$. **At $t = 3\tau$:** stress is ~$5\%$ of $\sigma_0$ (practically zero).
- ✅ Maxwell model is a **fluid-like** viscoelastic model — it flows completely under long-term load.
- ✅ For **creep** (constant stress, growing strain), use the **Kelvin–Voigt model** instead.
- ✅ For **real polymers** that show both an equilibrium modulus and viscous flow, use the **standard linear solid (Zener)** model.
- ✅ **Units check:** $\tau$ in seconds, $E$ in Pa, $\eta$ in Pa·s.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Governing Equation for Voigt Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT402 - POLYMER PHYSICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Governing Equation for Voigt Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Voigt Model** (also known as the **Kelvin–Voigt Model** or **Kelvin Solid**) is a fundamental linear viscoelastic model that represents the mechanical behavior of a polymer by combining an **ideal Hookean spring** (elastic element, modulus $E$) and a **Newtonian dashpot** (viscous element, viscosity $\eta$) connected mechanically in **parallel** (side-by-side configuration).

In this parallel arrangement, both elements experience the **same strain** $\varepsilon(t)$ at every instant, while the total stress borne by the system is the **algebraic sum** of the individual stresses in each branch.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Tone):**
> The Voigt (Kelvin) model consists of a spring and a dashpot arranged in parallel. It is used to model the *creep* behavior of polymers, where the deformation under a constant load is time-dependent but fully recoverable upon removal of the load.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Dashpot + Spring Braking System"

Imagine a car's suspension system:
- The **spring** (shock absorber coil) is the elastic element — it wants to push back instantly.
- The **oil-filled shock absorber** (dashpot) is the viscous element — it damps motion slowly.

When you sit on the car (apply a constant load), the car does **not** drop instantly to the bottom. Instead, it sinks **gradually** and asymptotes to an equilibrium position. The spring alone would react instantly; the dashpot alone would sink forever. Together in parallel, the **dashpot slows the spring's response**, and the **spring prevents permanent deformation** — the car eventually returns to its original height when you step out. This is *delayed elasticity* — the essence of the Voigt model.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from Maxwell Model:**
> - **Maxwell Model** → Spring and Dashpot in **SERIES** → Models **Stress Relaxation**.
> - **Voigt Model** → Spring and Dashpot in **PARALLEL** → Models **Creep (Delayed Elasticity)**.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit (SI) | Physical Meaning |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| Elastic Modulus | $E$ | $\text{Pa}$ (Pascal) | Stiffness of the spring element |
| Coefficient of Viscosity | $\eta$ | $\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$ | Internal friction of the dashpot |
| Retardation Time | $\tau$ | $\text{s}$ | Characteristic time scale $\eta/E$ |
| Strain | $\varepsilon$ | dimensionless | Relative deformation |
| Stress | $\sigma$ | $\text{Pa}$ | Force per unit area |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — How the Voigt Model Works

The Voigt model operates on three fundamental constitutive principles:

- **Step 1 — Kinematic Constraint (Same Strain):**  
  Because the spring and dashpot are mechanically clamped together in parallel, they must deform **identically** at all times:
  $$\varepsilon_{\text{spring}}(t) = \varepsilon_{\text{dashpot}}(t) = \varepsilon(t)$$

- **Step 2 — Stress Additivity (Different Stresses):**  
  The total load is shared between the two branches. Each branch carries a portion of the applied stress independently:
  $$\sigma(t) = \sigma_{\text{spring}}(t) + \sigma_{\text{dashpot}}(t)$$

- **Step 3 — Constitutive Laws of Each Element:**  
  The spring obeys **Hooke's Law** (instantaneous elastic response):
  $$\sigma_{\text{spring}} = E\,\varepsilon$$
  The dashpot obeys **Newton's Law of Viscosity** (rate-dependent response):
  $$\sigma_{\text{dashpot}} = \eta\,\dfrac{d\varepsilon}{dt}$$

- **Step 4 — Assembly into Governing Equation:**  
  Adding the two contributions yields the **first-order linear differential equation**:
  $$\sigma(t) = E\,\varepsilon(t) + \eta\,\dfrac{d\varepsilon}{dt}$$

- **Step 5 — Why It Models Creep:**  
  When a constant stress $\sigma_0$ is applied, the dashpot's resistance ($\eta\,\dot{\varepsilon}$) gradually yields, allowing the spring to stretch progressively. When the load is removed, the spring *pulls back* through the dashpot, producing **delayed recovery** rather than permanent set.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** The Voigt model accurately predicts the **short-term creep compliance** of cross-linked polymers, amorphous glassy polymers below $T_g$, and elastomers. It is also the building block of the more sophisticated **Standard Linear Solid (Zener)** model when combined with a Maxwell branch.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Physical Interpretation | Conditions |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\sigma = E\varepsilon + \eta\,\dot{\varepsilon}$ | **Governing Equation** (Differential Form) | General viscoelastic loading |
| 2 | $\tau = \dfrac{\eta}{E}$ | **Retardation Time** (Characteristic Time) | Material constant |
| 3 | $\varepsilon(t) = \dfrac{\sigma_0}{E}\left[1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right]$ | **Creep Strain** under step stress $\sigma_0$ | $\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 H(t)$ |
| 4 | $\varepsilon(t) = \dfrac{\sigma_0}{E}\left[e^{-(t-t_1)/\tau} - e^{-t/\tau}\right]$ | **Creep Recovery** after unloading at $t_1$ | $\sigma = 0$ for $t > t_1$ |
| 5 | $\sigma(t) = E\varepsilon_0$ | **Stress Response** to step strain $\varepsilon_0$ | $\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_0 H(t)$ (no relaxation) |
| 6 | $E^{*}(\omega) = E' + iE'' = E + i\omega\eta$ | **Complex Modulus** in DMA | Oscillatory shear |
| 7 | $E' = E$ | **Storage Modulus** (Elastic Part) | Frequency independent |
| 8 | $E'' = \omega\eta$ | **Loss Modulus** (Viscous Part) | Linear in $\omega$ |
| 9 | $\tan\delta = \dfrac{E''}{E'} = \omega\tau$ | **Loss Tangent** (Damping Factor) | Energy dissipation ratio |
| 10 | $J(t) = \dfrac{1}{E}\left[1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right]$ | **Creep Compliance** function | $J(t) = \varepsilon(t)/\sigma_0$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Note on Absolute Values:** The retardation time symbol $\tau$ is *not* an absolute value operator. It is the Greek letter tau. The modulus $\vert E^{*} \vert$ uses vertical bars — in LaTeX tables, prefer `\vert E^{*} \vert` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Polymer Processing:** Predicting sag and drape in extruded thermoplastics.
- **Biomedical Devices:** Modeling the time-dependent compliance of silicone implants and hydrogels.
- **Civil/Structural Engineering:** Long-term deflection of polymer-based structural bearings.
- **Rubber Industry:** Capturing delayed recovery in elastomeric seals and gaskets.
- **DMA Testing:** Provides the theoretical basis for low-frequency loss modulus measurements in polymer characterization.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Governing Equation (Constitutive Form)

The derivation proceeds from the parallel mechanical assembly of a Hookean spring and a Newtonian dashpot.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Apply the parallel mechanical constraint.} \\
& \varepsilon_{\text{total}}(t) = \varepsilon_{\text{spring}}(t) = \varepsilon_{\text{dashpot}}(t) = \varepsilon(t) \\
\\
\textbf{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Apply Newton's equilibrium — stresses add in parallel branches.} \\
& \sigma_{\text{total}}(t) = \sigma_{\text{spring}}(t) + \sigma_{\text{dashpot}}(t) \\
\\
\textbf{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Substitute Hooke's Law for the spring element.} \\
& \sigma_{\text{spring}} = E \cdot \varepsilon(t) \\
\\
\textbf{Step 4:} \quad & \text{Substitute Newton's Law of Viscosity for the dashpot element.} \\
& \sigma_{\text{dashpot}} = \eta \cdot \frac{d\varepsilon(t)}{dt} \\
\\
\textbf{Step 5:} \quad & \text{Combine to obtain the Voigt Governing Equation.} \\
& \sigma(t) = E\,\varepsilon(t) + \eta\,\frac{d\varepsilon(t)}{dt}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is a **first-order linear ordinary differential equation (ODE)** with constant coefficients, governing the entire viscoelastic response of the Voigt solid.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Creep Response $\varepsilon(t)$ under Step Stress

**Problem Setup:** A constant stress $\sigma_0$ is suddenly applied at $t = 0$ and held constant thereafter. Mathematically, $\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \cdot H(t)$, where $H(t)$ is the Heaviside step function.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Substitute } \sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \text{ into the governing equation for } t > 0. \\
& \sigma_0 = E\,\varepsilon(t) + \eta\,\frac{d\varepsilon}{dt} \\
\\
\textbf{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Rearrange into standard linear ODE form.} \\
& \eta\,\frac{d\varepsilon}{dt} + E\,\varepsilon = \sigma_0 \\
\\
\textbf{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Divide through by } \eta \text{ to isolate the derivative.} \\
& \frac{d\varepsilon}{dt} + \frac{E}{\eta}\,\varepsilon = \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta} \\
\\
\textbf{Step 4:} \quad & \text{Identify the integrating factor. The characteristic time is } \tau = \eta/E. \\
& \frac{d\varepsilon}{dt} + \frac{1}{\tau}\,\varepsilon = \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta} \\
\\
\textbf{Step 5:} \quad & \text{Apply the integrating factor method. Multiply both sides by } e^{t/\tau}. \\
& e^{t/\tau}\frac{d\varepsilon}{dt} + \frac{1}{\tau}e^{t/\tau}\varepsilon = \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta}e^{t/\tau} \\
\\
\textbf{Step 6:} \quad & \text{Recognize the left-hand side as the derivative of a product.} \\
& \frac{d}{dt}\!\left[\varepsilon(t)\cdot e^{t/\tau}\right] = \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta}e^{t/\tau} \\
\\
\textbf{Step 7:} \quad & \text{Integrate both sides with respect to } t. \\
& \varepsilon(t)\cdot e^{t/\tau} = \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta}\int e^{t/\tau}\,dt = \frac{\sigma_0\,\tau}{\eta}\,e^{t/\tau} + C_1 \\
\\
\textbf{Step 8:} \quad & \text{Recall that } \tau/\eta = 1/E, \text{ hence } \frac{\sigma_0\,\tau}{\eta} = \frac{\sigma_0}{E}. \\
& \varepsilon(t)\cdot e^{t/\tau} = \frac{\sigma_0}{E}\,e^{t/\tau} + C_1 \\
\\
\textbf{Step 9:} \quad & \text{Solve for } \varepsilon(t). \\
& \varepsilon(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{E} + C_1\,e^{-t/\tau} \\
\\
\textbf{Step 10:} \quad & \text{Apply the initial condition } \varepsilon(0) = 0 \text{ (no instantaneous strain in Voigt).} \\
& 0 = \frac{\sigma_0}{E} + C_1 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad C_1 = -\frac{\sigma_0}{E} \\
\\
\textbf{Step 11:} \quad & \text{Write the final creep response.} \\
& \varepsilon(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{E}\left[1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Physical Interpretation:**  
- At $t = 0$: $\varepsilon = 0$ (no instantaneous jump — the dashpot blocks it).  
- At $t \to \infty$: $\varepsilon \to \sigma_0/E$ (the spring fully stretches, dashpot fully yields).  
- The transition occurs over the characteristic retardation time $\tau = \eta/E$.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Dynamic Mechanical Response (Complex Modulus)

**Problem Setup:** Apply a small-amplitude oscillatory strain $\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_0 e^{i\omega t}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Compute the strain rate.} \\
& \frac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = i\omega\,\varepsilon_0\,e^{i\omega t} = i\omega\,\varepsilon(t) \\
\\
\textbf{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Substitute into the governing Voigt equation.} \\
& \sigma(t) = E\,\varepsilon(t) + \eta\,(i\omega\,\varepsilon(t)) \\
\\
\textbf{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Factor out } \varepsilon(t). \\
& \sigma(t) = \left[E + i\omega\eta\right]\,\varepsilon(t) \\
\\
\textbf{Step 4:} \quad & \text{Identify the complex dynamic modulus } E^{*}(\omega). \\
& E^{*}(\omega) = \frac{\sigma(t)}{\varepsilon(t)} = E + i\omega\eta \\
\\
\textbf{Step 5:} \quad & \text{Separate the real (storage) and imaginary (loss) components.} \\
& E' = E \quad \text{(storage modulus — frequency independent)} \\
& E'' = \omega\eta \quad \text{(loss modulus — linear in frequency)} \\
\\
\textbf{Step 6:} \quad & \text{Compute the loss tangent.} \\
& \tan\delta = \frac{E''}{E'} = \frac{\omega\eta}{E} = \omega\tau
\end{aligned}
$$

**Physical Interpretation:**  
- The Voigt solid is **purely elastic in storage** (no frequency dependence of $E'$).  
- The **loss modulus grows linearly** with frequency.  
- Unlike real polymers, the model has **no peak** in $E''$ — it cannot capture the glass transition $\alpha$-relaxation by itself. This motivates the Standard Linear Solid Model.

---

### 3.4 Derivation of the Stress Response to Step Strain

**Problem Setup:** Apply a constant strain $\varepsilon_0$ at $t = 0$, i.e., $\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_0 H(t)$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Step 1:} \quad & \text{For } t > 0, \text{ the strain is constant: } \frac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = 0. \\
\\
\textbf{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Substitute into the governing equation.} \\
