& \sigma(t) = E\,\varepsilon_0 + \eta\,(0) = E\,\varepsilon_0 \\
\\
\textbf{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Conclusion: The Voigt model does NOT exhibit stress relaxation.} \\
& \sigma(t) = E\,\varepsilon_0 = \text{constant for all } t > 0
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Exam Insight:** The Voigt model **cannot** describe stress relaxation because the parallel dashpot cannot independently relax the stress — the spring maintains it indefinitely. For stress relaxation, use the **Maxwell model**.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Loading Mode | Input Function | Output Response | Equation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Creep | $\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 H(t)$ | Delayed strain growth | $\varepsilon(t) = \dfrac{\sigma_0}{E}\left[1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right]$ |
| Recovery | $\sigma(t) = 0$ for $t > t_1$ | Delayed strain decay | $\varepsilon(t) = \dfrac{\sigma_0}{E}\left[e^{-(t-t_1)/\tau} - e^{-t/\tau}\right]$ |
| Stress Relaxation | $\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_0 H(t)$ | **No relaxation** | $\sigma(t) = E\,\varepsilon_0$ (constant) |
| Dynamic Oscillation | $\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_0 e^{i\omega t}$ | Frequency-dependent stress | $E^{*}(\omega) = E + i\omega\eta$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Physical Assembly Diagram (Block-Level Architecture)

The following Mermaid block represents the parallel mechanical coupling of the spring and dashpot. Note that physical drawings (springs, dashpots) are adapted as a functional block diagram, since Mermaid cannot render photorealistic mechanical schematics.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["Applied Stress sigma"] --> B["Top Clamp Plate"]
    B --> C["Spring Branch - E"]
    B --> D["Dashpot Branch - eta"]
    C --> E["Bottom Clamp Plate"]
    D --> E
    E --> F["Strain Response epsilon t"]

    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style B fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style C fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style D fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style E fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style F fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The applied stress $\sigma$ enters the top clamp plate.
- It splits into two parallel branches: the **spring** (stiffness $E$) and the **dashpot** (viscosity $\eta$).
- Both branches rejoin at the bottom clamp plate.
- The common displacement of the plates is the output strain $\varepsilon(t)$.

---

### 4.2 Creep Response Visualization (Functional Flow)

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1["Time t = 0: Apply constant stress sigma 0"] --> P2["Differential equation: sigma 0 = E epsilon + eta d epsilon dt"]
    P2 --> P3["Integrate ODE with IC epsilon 0 = 0"]
    P3 --> P4["Creep equation: epsilon t = sigma 0 over E times 1 minus e to the minus t over tau"]
    P4 --> P5["As t approaches infinity, epsilon asymptotes to sigma 0 over E"]
    P4 --> P6["At t equals tau, epsilon reaches 63.2 percent of equilibrium"]

    style P1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style P2 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style P3 fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style P4 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style P5 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style P6 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

---

### 4.3 Comparative Schematic — Maxwell vs Voigt (KTU High-Yield Distinction)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph S1["Maxwell Model - Series"]
        M1["sigma same in both"] --> M2["Spring E"]
        M2 --> M3["Dashpot eta"]
        M3 --> M4["Strain ADDS: epsilon total = epsilon spring plus epsilon dashpot"]
    end
    subgraph S2["Voigt Model - Parallel"]
        V1["Strain same in both"] --> V2["Spring E"]
        V1 --> V3["Dashpot eta"]
        V2 --> V4["Stress ADDS: sigma total = sigma spring plus sigma dashpot"]
        V3 --> V4
    end

    style M1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style M2 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style M3 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style M4 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style V1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style V2 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style V3 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style V4 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> This side-by-side schematic is a frequently-tested KTU question (often as a 3-mark short note or a comparison table).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**  
**State and derive the governing equation for the Voigt (Kelvin) model of viscoelasticity.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The Voigt model consists of a Hookean spring (modulus $E$) and a Newtonian dashpot (viscosity $\eta$) connected in **parallel**. In a parallel arrangement, the strain is the same in both elements, while the stresses add. Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_{\text{spring}}(t) &= \varepsilon_{\text{dashpot}}(t) = \varepsilon(t) \\
\sigma(t) &= \sigma_{\text{spring}} + \sigma_{\text{dashpot}} = E\varepsilon + \eta\frac{d\varepsilon}{dt}
\end{aligned}
$$

This first-order linear differential equation, $\sigma(t) = E\varepsilon + \eta\dot{\varepsilon}$, is the **governing equation** of the Voigt model and is used to model **delayed elastic creep** in polymers. *(3 marks — 1 for diagram description, 1 for constitutive law, 1 for final equation)*

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
**Define retardation time. What is its physical significance in the Voigt model?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **retardation time** $\tau$ is defined as the ratio of viscosity to elastic modulus:

$$
\tau = \frac{\eta}{E}
$$

**Physical Significance:**  
- It represents the characteristic time required for the polymer to attain $\approx 63.2\%$ of its equilibrium strain under a constant applied stress (i.e., $1 - e^{-1} \approx 0.632$).  
- A **large** $\tau$ indicates slow creep (e.g., cross-linked rubber or high-viscosity polymer melts).  
- A **small** $\tau$ indicates fast response (e.g., glassy polymers).  
- It governs the rate of delayed elastic recovery when the load is removed. *(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for formula, 1 for physical significance)*

---

### **PART B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

#### **(a) Derive the creep compliance expression $\varepsilon(t)$ for a Voigt model subjected to a step stress $\sigma_0$ at $t = 0$. Sketch the creep and recovery curves. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Governing Equation:**  
For the Voigt model in parallel:
$$\sigma(t) = E\varepsilon(t) + \eta\frac{d\varepsilon}{dt}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Step Stress:**  
For $t > 0$, $\sigma(t) = \sigma_0$:
$$\sigma_0 = E\varepsilon + \eta\frac{d\varepsilon}{dt}$$

**Step 3 — Solve the ODE:**  
Rearranging and using $\tau = \eta/E$:
$$\frac{d\varepsilon}{dt} + \frac{1}{\tau}\varepsilon = \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta}$$

Using the integrating factor $e^{t/\tau}$ and applying the initial condition $\varepsilon(0) = 0$:
$$\varepsilon(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{E}\left[1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right]$$

**Step 4 — Sketch the Curves:**

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1["t = 0: epsilon = 0"] --> A2["t = tau: epsilon = 0.632 sigma 0 over E"]
    A2 --> A3["t to infinity: epsilon equals sigma 0 over E horizontal asymptote"]

    style A1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style A2 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style A3 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

**Creep Phase:** $\varepsilon$ rises exponentially from $0$ to $\sigma_0/E$ as $t \to \infty$.

**Recovery Phase (after unloading at $t = t_1$):**  
$$\varepsilon_{\text{recovery}}(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{E}\left[e^{-(t-t_1)/\tau} - e^{-t/\tau}\right]$$
The strain decays back to **zero** (fully recoverable — no permanent set). *(7 marks: 2 for ODE setup, 3 for derivation, 2 for sketch and recovery)*

---

#### **(b) For a Voigt solid with $E = 5 \times 10^8$ Pa and $\eta = 5 \times 10^{10}$ Pa·s, calculate the retardation time and the strain after 200 seconds if a step stress of $2 \times 10^6$ Pa is applied. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute Retardation Time:**
$$\tau = \frac{\eta}{E} = \frac{5 \times 10^{10}}{5 \times 10^8} = 100 \text{ s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Equilibrium Strain:**
$$\varepsilon_{\infty} = \frac{\sigma_0}{E} = \frac{2 \times 10^6}{5 \times 10^8} = 4 \times 10^{-3} = 0.004$$

**Step 3 — Apply Creep Equation at $t = 200$ s:**
$$\varepsilon(200) = \frac{\sigma_0}{E}\left[1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right] = 4 \times 10^{-3}\left[1 - e^{-200/100}\right]$$
$$\varepsilon(200) = 4 \times 10^{-3}\left[1 - e^{-2}\right] = 4 \times 10^{-3}\left[1 - 0.1353\right]$$
$$\boxed{\varepsilon(200) = 4 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.8647 = 3.459 \times 10^{-3}}$$

**Step 4 — Percentage of Equilibrium Reached:**
$$\frac{\varepsilon(200)}{\varepsilon_{\infty}} \times 100\% = 86.47\%$$

> The polymer has reached ~86.5% of its equilibrium creep strain in 200 seconds (i.e., 2 retardation times). *(7 marks: 2 for τ, 2 for ε∞, 3 for time-dependent strain)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

#### **(a) Derive the expressions for the complex modulus $E^{*}(\omega)$, storage modulus $E'$, loss modulus $E''$, and loss tangent $\tan\delta$ for a Voigt model under oscillatory strain. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Apply Oscillatory Strain:**  
Let $\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_0 e^{i\omega t}$. Then:
$$\frac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = i\omega\varepsilon_0 e^{i\omega t} = i\omega\varepsilon(t)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into Governing Equation:**
$$\sigma(t) = E\varepsilon(t) + \eta(i\omega)\varepsilon(t) = (E + i\omega\eta)\varepsilon(t)$$

**Step 3 — Identify Complex Modulus:**
$$E^{*}(\omega) = \frac{\sigma(t)}{\varepsilon(t)} = E + i\omega\eta$$

**Step 4 — Separate Real and Imaginary Parts:**
$$E'(\omega) = E \quad (\text{Storage Modulus — constant})$$
$$E''(\omega) = \omega\eta \quad (\text{Loss Modulus — linear in } \omega)$$

**Step 5 — Loss Tangent:**
$$\tan\delta = \frac{E''}{E'} = \frac{\omega\eta}{E} = \omega\tau$$

**Interpretation:**  
- $E'$ is **frequency independent** — the Voigt model predicts a perfectly elastic solid in storage.  
- $E''$ grows linearly with $\omega$ — the model has no peak in loss modulus (cannot capture glass transition). *(7 marks: 2 for substitution, 2 for complex modulus, 3 for moduli and tan δ)*

---

#### **(b) A polymer sample exhibits Voigt behavior with $E = 10^9$ Pa and $\eta = 10^8$ Pa·s. Determine (i) the loss modulus at a frequency of 10 rad/s, (ii) the loss tangent, and (iii) the storage modulus. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Storage Modulus $E'$:**  
For the Voigt model, $E' = E$ regardless of frequency:
$$\boxed{E' = 10^9 \text{ Pa}}$$

**Step 2 — Loss Modulus $E''$ at $\omega = 10$ rad/s:**
$$E'' = \omega\eta = (10)(10^8) = 10^9 \text{ Pa}$$

$$\boxed{E'' = 10^9 \text{ Pa}}$$

**Step 3 — Loss Tangent:**
$$\tan\delta = \frac{E''}{E'} = \frac{10^9}{10^9} = 1$$

$$\boxed{\tan\delta = 1 \quad \text{i.e., } \delta = 45°}$$

**Step 4 — Retardation Time Check:**
$$\tau = \frac{\eta}{E} = \frac{10^8}{10^9} = 0.1 \text{ s}, \quad \omega\tau = (10)(0.1) = 1 \checkmark$$

> The frequency $\omega = 10$ rad/s is exactly equal to $1/\tau$, meaning the polymer is in the **transition region** of its viscoelastic response (equal storage and loss components). *(7 marks: 2 for E', 2 for E'', 3 for tan δ and physical interpretation)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 
> 1. **Drawing the wrong circuit:** Students often draw the **Maxwell model** (series) when asked about the Voigt model (parallel), or vice versa. **Always specify "parallel" in the diagram.**
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the initial condition:** In the creep derivation, the condition $\varepsilon(0) = 0$ is **mandatory**. Skipping it leads to a wrong integration constant and a missed 2-mark deduction.
> 
> 3. **Confusing retardation with relaxation:** Voigt uses **retardation time** $\tau = \eta/E$. Maxwell uses **relaxation time** $\lambda = \eta/E$ (same formula, different context). Mixing them loses marks.
> 
> 4. **Wrong separation of $E'$ and $E''$:** The real part of $E^{*}$ is $E'$, and the imaginary part (coefficient of $i$) is $E''$. Reversing them is a frequent error.
> 
> 5. **Neglecting units:** Always carry Pascal (Pa) and seconds (s) explicitly in numerical problems. Marks are reserved for correct unit handling.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)**

- ✅ The **Voigt Model** = Spring ($E$) ∥ Dashpot ($\eta$) in **parallel**.
- ✅ Governing Equation: $\sigma(t) = E\varepsilon(t) + \eta\,\dfrac{d\varepsilon}{dt}$.
- ✅ Retardation Time: $\tau = \dfrac{\eta}{E}$ — characteristic timescale of creep.
- ✅ Creep Response: $\varepsilon(t) = \dfrac{\sigma_0}{E}\left[1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right]$ — exponential approach to equilibrium.
- ✅ Recovery is **fully reversible** (no permanent deformation) — the Voigt model is a *delayed elastic solid*.
- ✅ The Voigt model **does NOT predict stress relaxation** — $\sigma = E\varepsilon_0$ stays constant under step strain.
- ✅ Dynamic Response: $E' = E$ (constant), $E'' = \omega\eta$ (linear in $\omega$), $\tan\delta = \omega\tau$.
- ✅ Primary Use: Modeling **short-term creep** in cross-linked polymers and glassy amorphous polymers below $T_g$.
- ✅ Limitation: Cannot capture the **peak in $E''$** at the glass transition — needs an extended model (e.g., Standard Linear Solid) for that.
- ✅ **Maxwell vs Voigt:** Maxwell → Series → Stress Relaxation. Voigt → Parallel → Creep. Always state the configuration explicitly in board answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Equation for Voigt Model Under Creep
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT402 - POLYMER PHYSICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Equation for Voigt Model Under Creep

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Voigt Model** (also called the **Kelvin–Voigt Model**) is a fundamental linear viscoelastic model used to describe the time-dependent mechanical behavior of polymeric materials. It consists of a **Hookean spring** (modulus **E**) and a **Newtonian dashpot** (viscosity **η**) connected in **parallel**.

Under **creep loading**, a constant stress $\sigma_0$ is instantaneously applied at time $t = 0$ and maintained indefinitely. The model predicts a **time-dependent, delayed elastic strain** $\varepsilon(t)$ that asymptotically approaches the equilibrium value $\sigma_0 / E$. Unlike the Maxwell model, the Voigt element exhibits **no instantaneous elastic deformation** because the dashpot (in parallel) initially restrains the spring.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Voigt model is a "viscoelastic solid" — it exhibits **delayed elasticity** and **complete recovery** upon unloading. There is **no permanent (irrecoverable) flow** component.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine pressing your finger slowly into a thick silicone gel pad (similar to a viscoelastic polymer):

- The **spring** represents the **instantaneous elastic backbone** of the polymer network — the entanglements and crosslinks that want to spring back.
- The **dashpot** represents the **time-dependent viscous resistance** — the slow uncoiling, reorientation, and slippage of polymer chains past each other.
- Because they are in **parallel**, when you press (apply stress), the spring *cannot* instantly stretch — the dashpot *holds it back*, forcing the deformation to build up **gradually**.
- When you **release** your finger (remove the stress), the spring slowly pulls the dashpot back, and the silicone pad **fully recovers** its original shape — though it takes time.

This is exactly the **delayed elastic response** predicted by the Voigt model under creep.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** Voigt → "Solid-like" behavior. It deforms slowly under load, but **recovers fully** when unloaded. Permanent flow is **zero**.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Units (SI) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\sigma_0$ | Applied constant creep stress | **Pa** (or **N/m²**) |
| $E$ | Elastic modulus of spring | **Pa** |
| $\eta$ | Viscosity coefficient of dashpot | **Pa·s** |
| $\tau$ | **Retardation time** = $\eta / E$ | **s** |
| $\varepsilon(t)$ | Time-dependent strain | **dimensionless** |

The **retardation time** $\tau$ is the single most important characteristic parameter of the Voigt element. It sets the natural timescale over which creep deformation develops.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanical Setup — Why Parallel?

In a **parallel arrangement**, both elements experience the **same strain** (they share the same displacement), but the **stresses add up**:

$$
\varepsilon_{\text{spring}}(t) = \varepsilon_{\text{dashpot}}(t) = \varepsilon(t)
$$

$$
\sigma_{\text{total}}(t) = \sigma_{\text{spring}}(t) + \sigma_{\text{dashpot}}(t)
$$

This additive stress rule is the mathematical signature of the parallel topology.

## 2.2 Constitutive Equations of the Two Elements

**Hookean Spring (elastic element):**

$$
\sigma_{\text{spring}} = E \cdot \varepsilon
$$

**Newtonian Dashpot (viscous element):**

$$
\sigma_{\text{dashpot}} = \eta \cdot \frac{d\varepsilon}{dt}
$$

## 2.3 Combining into the Voigt Constitutive Equation

Summing the stresses:

$$
\sigma(t) = E \cdot \varepsilon(t) + \eta \cdot \frac{d\varepsilon}{dt}
$$

This first-order linear ODE is the **master equation** of the Voigt model.

## 2.4 Creep Loading Condition

Under **creep**, a step stress $\sigma_0$ is applied instantaneously at $t = 0$:

$$
\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \cdot H(t)
$$

where $H(t)$ is the **Heaviside step function**.

Substituting:

$$
\sigma_0 = E \cdot \varepsilon(t) + \eta \cdot \frac{d\varepsilon}{dt}
$$

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Equation | Physical Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\sigma(t) = E\,\varepsilon(t) + \eta\,\dfrac{d\varepsilon}{dt}$ | **Voigt constitutive equation** (general) |
| 2 | $\tau = \dfrac{\eta}{E}$ | **Retardation time** (characteristic timescale) |
| 3 | $\varepsilon(t) = \dfrac{\sigma_0}{E}\left[1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right]$ | **Creep strain response** (Voigt model) |
| 4 | $\varepsilon(\infty) = \dfrac{\sigma_0}{E}$ | **Equilibrium (asymptotic) creep strain** |
| 5 | $\varepsilon(0^+) = 0$ | **Initial strain is zero** (no instantaneous jump) |
| 6 | $\varepsilon_{\text{rec}}(t) = \varepsilon(t_1)\,e^{-(t-t_1)/\tau}$ | **Recovery strain** after unloading at $t = t_1$ |
| 7 | $\varepsilon_{\text{rec}}(\infty) = 0$ | **Full recovery** — confirms solid-like behavior |
| 8 | $J(t) = \dfrac{\varepsilon(t)}{\sigma_0} = \dfrac{1}{E}\left[1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right]$ | **Creep compliance** $J(t)$ |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Voigt model is extensively used in:

- **Polymer engineering:** Predicting long-term deformation of plastic components under sustained load (e.g., pipes, structural members).
- **Biomedical devices:** Modeling soft tissue response (skin, cartilage, gels).
- **Civil engineering:** Concrete creep prediction.
- **Textile industry:** Viscoelastic recovery of fibers.
- **3D printing & additive manufacturing:** Predicting shape retention of printed polymers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** When $\tau$ is very small, the Voigt element behaves almost like a pure spring. When $\tau$ is very large, it behaves like a viscous fluid (deformation accumulates very slowly). The dimensionless ratio $t/\tau$ is therefore the central design parameter.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Voigt Creep Equation

**Step 1 — Write the constitutive equation for the parallel combination.**

The total stress is the sum of spring and dashpot stresses (parallel topology), and the strain is common:

$$
\sigma(t) = E\,\varepsilon(t) + \eta\,\frac{d\varepsilon(t)}{dt}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the creep loading condition.**

For a creep test, a constant stress $\sigma_0$ is applied at $t = 0$:

$$
\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \quad \text{for} \quad t \geq 0
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the constant stress into the constitutive equation.**

$$
\sigma_0 = E\,\varepsilon(t) + \eta\,\frac{d\varepsilon(t)}{dt}
$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange into the standard linear ODE form.**

Divide both sides by $\eta$:

$$
\frac{d\varepsilon(t)}{dt} + \frac{E}{\eta}\,\varepsilon(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta}
$$

This is a **first-order linear ODE** with constant coefficients.

**Step 5 — Identify the integrating factor.**

The integrating factor is:

$$
\mu(t) = e^{\int (E/\eta)\,dt} = e^{Et/\eta} = e^{t/\tau}
$$

where the **retardation time** is defined as:

$$
\tau \equiv \frac{\eta}{E}
$$

**Step 6 — Multiply the ODE by the integrating factor.**

$$
e^{t/\tau}\,\frac{d\varepsilon}{dt} + \frac{1}{\tau}\,e^{t/\tau}\,\varepsilon = \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta}\,e^{t/\tau}
$$

The left-hand side is now an exact derivative:

$$
\frac{d}{dt}\left[\varepsilon(t)\cdot e^{t/\tau}\right] = \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta}\,e^{t/\tau}
$$

**Step 7 — Integrate both sides with respect to time.**

$$
\varepsilon(t)\,e^{t/\tau} = \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta}\int e^{t/\tau}\,dt + C
$$

Evaluating the integral (using $\int e^{t/\tau}dt = \tau\,e^{t/\tau}$):

$$
\varepsilon(t)\,e^{t/\tau} = \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta}\cdot \tau\,e^{t/\tau} + C
$$

Substituting $\tau = \eta / E$, so $\sigma_0 \tau / \eta = \sigma_0 / E$:

$$
\varepsilon(t)\,e^{t/\tau} = \frac{\sigma_0}{E}\,e^{t/\tau} + C
$$

**Step 8 — Apply the initial condition.**

At $t = 0^+$, the strain in a Voigt element is **zero** (the dashpot prevents any instantaneous deformation):

$$
\varepsilon(0^+) = 0
$$

Substituting $t = 0$:

$$
0 = \frac{\sigma_0}{E} + C \quad \Longrightarrow \quad C = -\frac{\sigma_0}{E}
$$

**Step 9 — Solve for $\varepsilon(t)$.**

$$
\varepsilon(t)\,e^{t/\tau} = \frac{\sigma_0}{E}\,e^{t/\tau} - \frac{\sigma_0}{E}
$$

Divide through by $e^{t/\tau}$:

$$
\varepsilon(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{E} - \frac{\sigma_0}{E}\,e^{-t/\tau}
$$

**Step 10 — Final standard form.**

$$
\boxed{\;\varepsilon(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{E}\left[1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right]\;}
$$

This is the **canonical creep equation of the Voigt model**.

## 3.2 Limiting Case Analysis (Sanity Check)

**Case A — At $t = 0$:**

$$
\varepsilon(0) = \frac{\sigma_0}{E}\left[1 - 1\right] = 0
$$

Confirms **no instantaneous elastic strain** — characteristic of the Voigt (solid) model.

**Case B — At $t \to \infty$:**

$$
\varepsilon(\infty) = \frac{\sigma_0}{E}\left[1 - 0\right] = \frac{\sigma_0}{E}
$$

Confirms the **equilibrium elastic strain** — all deformation is recoverable.

**Case C — At $t = \tau$:**

$$
\varepsilon(\tau) = \frac{\sigma_0}{E}\left[1 - e^{-1}\right] \approx 0.632\,\frac{\sigma_0}{E}
$$

Exactly **63.2%** of the equilibrium strain is reached in one retardation time. This is the standard first-order time constant interpretation.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Recovery Equation (Unloading Case)

Suppose the creep stress is **removed** at time $t = t_1$. The strain at that instant is:

$$
\varepsilon(t_1) = \frac{\sigma_0}{E}\left[1 - e^{-t_1/\tau}\right]
$$

For $t > t_1$, the applied stress is zero, so the constitutive equation becomes:

$$
0 = E\,\varepsilon(t) + \eta\,\frac{d\varepsilon(t)}{dt}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\frac{d\varepsilon}{\varepsilon} = -\frac{E}{\eta}\,dt = -\frac{dt}{\tau}
$$

Integrating from $t_1$ to $t$:

$$
\ln\varepsilon(t) - \ln\varepsilon(t_1) = -\frac{t - t_1}{\tau}
$$

Therefore the **recovery strain** is:

$$
\boxed{\;\varepsilon_{\text{rec}}(t) = \varepsilon(t_1)\,e^{-(t-t_1)/\tau}\;}
$$

As $t \to \infty$, $\varepsilon_{\text{rec}} \to 0$ — **full recovery**, confirming solid-like behavior.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Voigt Model Topology & Creep Loading

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Applied Stress Sigma t = Sigma0 constant"] --> B["Parallel Combination"]
    B --> C["Spring Element Modulus E"]
    B --> D["Dashpot Element Viscosity eta"]
    C --> E["Common Strain epsilon t"]
    D --> E
    E --> F["Creep Response epsilon t = Sigma0 over E times 1 minus e to minus t over tau"]
    F --> G["Equilibrium Strain Sigma0 over E"]
    F --> H["Initial Strain at t = 0 is Zero"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Schematic — Voigt Creep & Recovery Curve Timeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["Time Axis t = 0"] --> P2["Stress applied Sigma0"]
    P2 --> P3["Phase 1 CREEP: epsilon t increases as 1 minus e to minus t over tau"]
    P3 --> P4["Strain approaches Sigma0 over E asymptotically"]
    P4 --> P5["Time t = t1: Stress removed Sigma = 0"]
    P5 --> P6["Phase 2 RECOVERY: epsilon decreases as e to minus t minus t1 over tau"]
    P6 --> P7["Strain returns fully to 0 as t approaches infinity"]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Architecture — Mechanical Analogy Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1["Silicone Gel Pad"] --> R2["Apply Finger Pressure Sigma0"]
    R2 --> R3["Spring represents elastic polymer network"]
    R2 --> R4["Dashpot represents viscous chain motion"]
    R3 --> R5["Combined Delayed Elastic Response"]
    R4 --> R5
    R5 --> R6["Equilibrium deformation Sigma0 over E"]
    R6 --> R7["Remove finger: full recovery back to zero"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The two branches (spring + dashpot) in the first diagram represent the **parallel mechanical coupling**. The second diagram illustrates the **two-phase experimental response**: creep loading followed by unloading and recovery. The third diagram connects the abstract math to a tangible engineering analogy.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PCPOT402]**

**Q: Define the Voigt model. State its key constitutive equation.**

**Model Answer:**

The Voigt (Kelvin–Voigt) model is a linear viscoelastic model consisting of a Hookean spring (modulus $E$) and a Newtonian dashpot (viscosity $\eta$) connected **in parallel**. It describes **delayed elastic** behavior of polymeric solids.

The constitutive equation is:

$$
\sigma(t) = E\,\varepsilon(t) + \eta\,\frac{d\varepsilon(t)}{dt}
$$

The strain response under constant creep stress $\sigma_0$ is:

$$
\varepsilon(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{E}\left[1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right], \quad \tau = \frac{\eta}{E}
$$

### Question A2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Similar Pattern]**

**Q: What is retardation time? What is its physical significance in the Voigt model?**

**Model Answer:**

The **retardation time** is defined as:

$$
\tau = \frac{\eta}{E}
$$

**Physical significance:**
- It is the **characteristic time** required for the Voigt element to reach $\approx 63.2\%$ of its equilibrium strain under a step stress.
- It quantifies the **relative dominance** of viscous vs. elastic response.
- A **large $\tau$** indicates slow (viscous-dominated) deformation; a **small $\tau$** indicates fast (elastic-dominated) response.
- It governs the **rate of delayed elastic recovery** upon unloading.

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PCPOT402, Module 3 Choice A]**

**Q (a) [7 Marks]:** With a neat sketch, explain the Voigt model. Derive the equation for strain under creep loading.

**Q (b) [7 Marks]:** A polymer element modeled by a Voigt element has $E = 5 \times 10^6$ Pa and $\eta = 2.5 \times 10^8$ Pa·s. A constant creep stress of $\sigma_0 = 1 \times 10^5$ Pa is applied at $t = 0$. Calculate (i) the retardation time, (ii) the strain at $t = 100$ s, and (iii) the equilibrium strain.

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Step 1 — Sketch:** (See Section 4.1 of these notes for the Mermaid diagram.)

The Voigt model: spring (modulus $E$) **in parallel** with dashpot (viscosity $\eta$). Both share the same strain; stresses add.

**Step 2 — Constitutive equation:**

$$
\sigma(t) = E\,\varepsilon(t) + \eta\,\frac{d\varepsilon(t)}{dt}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply creep condition** $\sigma(t) = \sigma_0$:

$$
\sigma_0 = E\,\varepsilon + \eta\,\frac{d\varepsilon}{dt}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve the linear ODE** with $\varepsilon(0^+) = 0$:

$$
\boxed{\;\varepsilon(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{E}\left[1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right]\;}
$$

where $\tau = \eta / E$.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Given:** $E = 5 \times 10^6$ Pa, $\eta = 2.5 \times 10^8$ Pa·s, $\sigma_0 = 1 \times 10^5$ Pa.

**(i) Retardation time:**

$$
\tau = \frac{\eta}{E} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^8}{5 \times 10^6} = 50 \text{ s}
$$

**(ii) Strain at $t = 100$ s:**

$$
\varepsilon(100) = \frac{\sigma_0}{E}\left[1 - e^{-100/50}\right] = \frac{1 \times 10^5}{5 \times 10^6}\left[1 - e^{-2}\right]
$$

$$
\varepsilon(100) = 0.02 \times [1 - 0.1353] = 0.02 \times 0.8647 = 0.01729
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\varepsilon(100\text{ s}) \approx 0.01729 \quad (\text{i.e., } 1.729\%)\;}
$$

**(iii) Equilibrium strain** (as $t \to \infty$):

$$
\varepsilon(\infty) = \frac{\sigma_0}{E} = \frac{1 \times 10^5}{5 \times 10^6} = 0.02
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\varepsilon_{\text{eq}} = 0.02 \quad (\text{i.e., } 2\%)\;}
$$

**Observation:** At $t = 2\tau$, the strain is $\approx 86.5\%$ of the equilibrium value, consistent with first-order exponential growth.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PCPOT402, Module 3 Choice B]**

**Q (a) [7 Marks]:** Discuss the creep and recovery behavior of a Voigt element. Derive the recovery equation when the stress is removed at time $t = t_1$.

**Q (b) [7 Marks]:** For a polymer following the Voigt model, the strain at equilibrium under a creep stress of $2 \times 10^5$ Pa is 0.04. The retardation time is 80 s. Determine (i) the modulus $E$, (ii) the dashpot viscosity $\eta$, and (iii) the time required for the strain to reach 75% of its equilibrium value.

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Step 1 — Creep phase ($0 \leq t \leq t_1$):**

Under constant stress $\sigma_0$:

$$
\varepsilon(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{E}\left[1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right]
$$

**Step 2 — Strain at the instant of unloading $t = t_1$:**

$$
\varepsilon(t_1) = \frac{\sigma_0}{E}\left[1 - e^{-t_1/\tau}\right]
$$

**Step 3 — Recovery phase ($t > t_1$):** With $\sigma = 0$:

$$
0 = E\,\varepsilon + \eta\,\frac{d\varepsilon}{dt}
$$

Rearranging and solving with initial condition $\varepsilon(t_1)$:

$$
\boxed{\;\varepsilon_{\text{rec}}(t) = \varepsilon(t_1)\,e^{-(t-t_1)/\tau}\;}
$$

**Step 4 — Limiting behavior:**

As $t \to \infty$, $\varepsilon_{\text{rec}} \to 0$ — **complete recovery** (no permanent deformation), confirming the **solid-like** nature of the Voigt model.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Given:** $\sigma_0 = 2 \times 10^5$ Pa, $\varepsilon(\infty) = 0.04$, $\tau = 80$ s.

**(i) Modulus $E$:**

$$
\varepsilon(\infty) = \frac{\sigma_0}{E} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad E = \frac{\sigma_0}{\varepsilon(\infty)} = \frac{2 \times 10^5}{0.04}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;E = 5 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}\;}
$$

**(ii) Dashpot viscosity $\eta$:**

$$
\eta = E\,\tau = (5 \times 10^6)(80) = 4 \times 10^8 \text{ Pa·s}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\eta = 4 \times 10^8 \text{ Pa·s}\;}
$$

**(iii) Time to reach 75% of equilibrium strain:**

Set $\varepsilon(t) = 0.75\,\varepsilon(\infty)$:

$$
0.75 = 1 - e^{-t/\tau} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad e^{-t/\tau} = 0.25
$$

$$
-\frac{t}{\tau} = \ln(0.25) = -1.3863
$$

$$
t = 1.3863 \times \tau = 1.3863 \times 80
$$

$$
\boxed{\;t \approx 110.9 \text{ s}\;}
$$

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the initial condition $\varepsilon(0) = 0$** — The integration constant $C$ MUST be evaluated. Omitting this step loses 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing Voigt with Maxwell** — Maxwell has spring and dashpot in **series** (stress same, strain adds). Voigt has them in **parallel** (strain same, stress adds). This is the single most common conceptual error.
> 3. **Writing $\varepsilon(0) = \sigma_0/E$** — That would be the Maxwell model behavior. In Voigt, $\varepsilon(0^+) = 0$ because the dashpot restrains instantaneous deformation.
> 4. **Missing units in numerical answers** — Always state units: Pa for $E$, Pa·s for $\eta$, seconds for $\tau$.
> 5. **Forgetting the "complete recovery" comment** — Examiners reward the insight that Voigt = solid-like (no permanent flow).
> 6. **Writing the retardation time formula wrong** — It is $\tau = \eta / E$, **not** $E/\eta$.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Voigt model** = spring $E$ **in parallel** with dashpot $\eta$.
- ✅ **Strain is common** in parallel; **stress adds**.
- ✅ **Constitutive equation:** $\sigma = E\varepsilon + \eta\,\dot{\varepsilon}$.
- ✅ **Retardation time:** $\tau = \eta / E$ — sets the timescale of delayed response.
- ✅ **Creep response:** $\varepsilon(t) = \dfrac{\sigma_0}{E}\left[1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right]$.
- ✅ **Initial strain $\varepsilon(0^+) = 0$** (no instantaneous jump — dashpot restrains).
- ✅ **Equilibrium strain:** $\varepsilon(\infty) = \sigma_0/E$.
- ✅ **At $t = \tau$:** strain = $63.2\%$ of equilibrium value.
- ✅ **Recovery:** $\varepsilon_{\text{rec}}(t) = \varepsilon(t_1)\,e^{-(t-t_1)/\tau}$.
- ✅ **Full recovery** as $t \to \infty$ — solid-like behavior.
- ✅ **NO permanent flow** in Voigt — this is the key distinction from Maxwell.
- ✅ **Real-world analogy:** viscoelastic polymer gel / silicone pad / soft tissue.
- ✅ **Engineering use:** long-term deformation prediction under sustained loads.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Equation for Voigt Model Under Stress Relaxation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT402 - POLYMER PHYSICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Equation for Voigt Model Under Stress Relaxation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

The **Voigt Model** (also known as the **Kelvin–Voigt Model**) is a fundamental viscoelastic mechanical analog representing the combined elastic and viscous behavior of polymeric solids. It consists of a **Hookean spring** (representing ideal elastic response governed by Young's modulus **E**) and a **Newtonian dashpot** (representing ideal viscous flow governed by viscosity **η**), connected in **parallel** arrangement.

In a **Stress Relaxation** experiment, a constant strain **ε₀** is imposed instantaneously at time **t = 0** and held constant. The resulting stress **σ(t)** is then monitored as a function of time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Conceptual Point for KTU 2024 Scheme:** Unlike the Maxwell model, the Voigt (Kelvin–Voigt) model **does NOT exhibit traditional stress relaxation**. Because the dashpot is connected in parallel with the spring, both elements must undergo the **same strain**. When strain is held constant, the dashpot cannot flow (since flow requires a *changing* strain), and the entire load is carried by the spring instantaneously. Therefore, the stress remains **time-independent** at a value of **Eε₀**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Explanation

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Sponge in Honey" System**

Imagine a **spring** (think of a rubber band) placed **in parallel** with a **dashpot** (a piston moving through thick honey). The spring can stretch instantly, but the dashpot resists any *sudden* motion.

- When you **suddenly stretch** the combined system and hold it at a fixed length (constant strain):
  - The **spring stretches immediately** and develops a force.
  - The **dashpot cannot respond instantaneously** to a sudden displacement — it requires time to flow.
  - Since both elements share the same displacement, the dashpot simply "locks" at the new position.
  - The total force you feel is therefore **constant** — it is simply the spring force **Eε₀**.

- This is fundamentally different from a **Maxwell model** (spring and dashpot in series), where the dashpot can keep flowing even when the total strain is held constant, allowing the spring to gradually relax.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Parameter | Standard Unit (SI) |
|:------:|-----------|:------------------:|
| **E** | Young's Modulus of the spring element | **Pa (N/m²)** |
| **η** | Viscosity of the dashpot element | **Pa·s** |
| **τ** | Retardation time (characteristic time of the Voigt model) = **η/E** | **s (seconds)** |
| **ε₀** | Imposed constant strain | **dimensionless** |
| **σ(t)** | Time-dependent stress response | **Pa (N/m²)** |
| **E(t)** | Relaxation modulus | **Pa (N/m²)** |

### 1.4 Pre-requisite Concepts (Mandatory for KTU Module 3)

1. **Hooke's Law** for the spring: $\sigma_E = E\varepsilon$
2. **Newton's Law of Viscosity** for the dashpot: $\sigma_V = \eta \dfrac{d\varepsilon}{dt}$
3. **Parallel combination rule**: strains are equal; stresses add.
4. **Definition of Stress Relaxation Modulus**: $E(t) = \dfrac{\sigma(t)}{\varepsilon_0}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Constitutive Equation of the Voigt Model

Because the spring and dashpot are arranged in **parallel**, the **total stress** is the sum of the individual element stresses, while the **strain is common** to both:

$$\varepsilon_{\text{total}} = \varepsilon_{\text{spring}} = \varepsilon_{\text{dashpot}} = \varepsilon$$

$$\sigma_{\text{total}} = \sigma_{\text{spring}} + \sigma_{\text{dashpot}}$$

Substituting the constitutive laws:

$$\sigma(t) = E\varepsilon(t) + \eta \frac{d\varepsilon(t)}{dt}$$

This is the **generalized Voigt constitutive differential equation** (also called the **Kelvin equation**).

### 2.2 Operational Logic — Why Strain Derivatives Vanish

In a **stress relaxation test**:
- A step strain $\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_0 \, H(t)$ is applied, where $H(t)$ is the Heaviside step function.
- This means $\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_0$ for all $t > 0$.
- Therefore, the **time derivative of strain is identically zero**: $\dfrac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = 0$ for $t > 0$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Why Behind the Result:** The dashpot responds to the *rate* of strain, not the strain itself. When strain is held constant, there is no rate, so the dashpot contributes **zero** stress. All the load is borne instantly by the parallel spring. This is the fundamental reason the Voigt model cannot show stress relaxation in its pure form.

### 2.3 Step-by-Step Mathematical Resolution

Step 1: Apply constant strain condition
$$\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_0 \quad \text{(for } t > 0\text{)}$$

Step 2: Compute strain derivative
$$\frac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt}(\varepsilon_0) = 0$$

Step 3: Substitute into the Voigt constitutive equation
$$\sigma(t) = E(\varepsilon_0) + \eta(0)$$

Step 4: Simplify
$$\boxed{\sigma(t) = E\varepsilon_0 = \text{constant}}$$

Step 5: Compute the relaxation modulus
$$E(t) = \frac{\sigma(t)}{\varepsilon_0} = \frac{E\varepsilon_0}{\varepsilon_0} = E$$

The relaxation modulus is **time-invariant** — there is no decay.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Equation | Remarks |
|:--------|:---------|:--------|
| Voigt Constitutive Equation | $\sigma = E\varepsilon + \eta\dfrac{d\varepsilon}{dt}$ | Master differential equation |
| Stress Relaxation Response (Voigt) | $\sigma(t) = E\varepsilon_0$ | **Constant — no decay** |
| Relaxation Modulus (Voigt) | $E(t) = E$ | Time-independent |
| Maxwell Stress Relaxation (for comparison) | $\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \, e^{-t/\tau_M}$ | Exponential decay with $\tau_M = \eta/E$ |
| Retardation Time | $\tau = \dfrac{\eta}{E}$ | Characteristic time of the model |
| Voigt Creep Compliance (for comparison) | $J(t) = \dfrac{1}{E}\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$ | Used in creep test, not stress relaxation |
| Creep Recovery (for comparison) | $\varepsilon(t) = \dfrac{\sigma_0}{E}\,e^{-t/\tau}$ | Strain decays after load removal |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The Voigt model is the workhorse for modeling **creep** in solid polymers (e.g., PVC pipes under sustained pressure, polymer bearings under continuous load). Recognizing that it does *not* predict stress relaxation is essential for KTU board questions and helps students avoid common conceptual pitfalls.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Solid polymer components under sustained deformation** (gaskets, seals, structural adhesives): The Voigt model correctly predicts that the internal stress remains nearly constant — useful for design of bolted joints.
- **Vibration damping in polymers**: The Voigt model captures the **loss modulus** behavior in dynamic mechanical analysis (DMA).
- **Geological materials** (ice, rock salt): Used in geophysics to model delayed elastic response.
- **Biomedical polymers** (artificial cartilage, contact lenses): Predict time-dependent recovery after deformation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Voigt Stress Relaxation Equation

> **Objective:** Starting from the fundamental Voigt constitutive equation, derive the complete stress response under a step-strain input.

**Step 1: Establish the parallel arrangement constraint**

For a Voigt element (spring $\parallel$ dashpot), the kinematic condition is:

$$\varepsilon_{\text{spring}}(t) = \varepsilon_{\text{dashpot}}(t) = \varepsilon(t)$$

The force (stress) balance is:

$$\sigma(t) = \sigma_{\text{spring}}(t) + \sigma_{\text{dashpot}}(t)$$

**Step 2: Write the individual element equations**

For the **Hookean spring** (linear elastic):

$$\sigma_{\text{spring}}(t) = E \, \varepsilon(t)$$

For the **Newtonian dashpot** (linear viscous):

$$\sigma_{\text{dashpot}}(t) = \eta \, \frac{d\varepsilon(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 3: Combine to form the master equation**

Substituting into the force balance:

$$\sigma(t) = E \, \varepsilon(t) + \eta \, \frac{d\varepsilon(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 4: Define the input (stress relaxation boundary condition)**

The stress relaxation experiment imposes a **step strain**:

$$\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_0 \, H(t)$$

where $H(t)$ is the Heaviside step function:

$$H(t) = \begin{cases} 0, & t < 0 \\ 1, & t \geq 0 \end{cases}$$

For $t > 0$:

$$\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_0 = \text{constant}$$

**Step 5: Compute the time derivative of the imposed strain**

$$\frac{d\varepsilon(t)}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt}(\varepsilon_0) = 0 \quad \text{for } t > 0$$

**Step 6: Substitute into the master Voigt equation**

$$\sigma(t) = E(\varepsilon_0) + \eta(0)$$

**Step 7: Simplify**

$$\sigma(t) = E\varepsilon_0 + 0$$

$$\boxed{\sigma(t) = E\varepsilon_0}$$

**Step 8: Calculate the relaxation modulus**

By definition, the stress relaxation modulus is:

$$E(t) \equiv \frac{\sigma(t)}{\varepsilon_0}$$

Substituting the result from Step 7:

$$E(t) = \frac{E\varepsilon_0}{\varepsilon_0} = E = \text{constant}$$

**Step 9: Express in terms of the retardation time**

Since $\tau = \eta/E$, we may equivalently write:

$$E(t) = \frac{\eta}{\tau}$$

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Step-by-Step Summary Table

| Step | Operation | Result | Physical Meaning |
|:----:|:----------|:-------|:-----------------|
| 1 | Apply parallel constraint | $\varepsilon_{\text{spring}} = \varepsilon_{\text{dashpot}} = \varepsilon$ | Strain is shared |
| 2 | Apply force balance | $\sigma = \sigma_{\text{spring}} + \sigma_{\text{dashpot}}$ | Stresses add |
| 3 | Substitute constitutive laws | $\sigma = E\varepsilon + \eta\dfrac{d\varepsilon}{dt}$ | Master Voigt equation |
| 4 | Apply step-strain input | $\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_0$ for $t>0$ | Constant strain |
| 5 | Differentiate input | $\dfrac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = 0$ | No rate of strain |
| 6 | Substitute back | $\sigma(t) = E\varepsilon_0 + 0$ | Spring carries full load |
| 7 | Simplify | $\sigma(t) = E\varepsilon_0$ | **Constant stress** |
| 8 | Compute relaxation modulus | $E(t) = E$ | **Time-invariant** |

### 3.3 Comparative Analytical Derivation — Maxwell Model (for KTU Contextual Understanding)

> Since KTU examiners often ask students to **contrast** Voigt and Maxwell behavior, the Maxwell derivation is included for completeness.

For the **Maxwell model** (spring $\dashv$ dashpot in series):

$$\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_{\text{spring}}(t) + \varepsilon_{\text{dashpot}}(t)$$

$$\frac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = \frac{1}{E}\frac{d\sigma}{dt} + \frac{\sigma}{\eta}$$

Under step strain $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_0$, we have $\dfrac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = 0$:

$$0 = \frac{1}{E}\frac{d\sigma}{dt} + \frac{\sigma}{\eta}$$

$$\frac{d\sigma}{\sigma} = -\frac{E}{\eta}\,dt = -\frac{dt}{\tau_M}$$

Integrating from $0$ to $t$:

$$\ln\left(\frac{\sigma(t)}{\sigma_0}\right) = -\frac{t}{\tau_M}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \, e^{-t/\tau_M}}$$

This is the classic **exponential decay** — exactly what stress relaxation *should* look like. The Voigt model, by contrast, gives a **flat line** at $\sigma = E\varepsilon_0$.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** A Voigt element has $E = 2 \times 10^{6}$ Pa and $\eta = 4 \times 10^{8}$ Pa·s. A constant strain $\varepsilon_0 = 0.005$ is applied at $t = 0$. Calculate:
(a) The retardation time $\tau$
(b) The stress at $t = 10$ s
(c) The relaxation modulus $E(t)$ at $t = 10$ s

**Solution:**

(a) Retardation time:

$$\tau = \frac{\eta}{E} = \frac{4 \times 10^8}{2 \times 10^6} = 200 \text{ s}$$

(b) Stress at $t = 10$ s (using the Voigt stress relaxation result):

$$\sigma(10) = E\varepsilon_0 = (2 \times 10^6)(0.005) = 1 \times 10^{4} \text{ Pa} = 10 \text{ kPa}$$

(c) Relaxation modulus at $t = 10$ s:

$$E(10) = \frac{\sigma(10)}{\varepsilon_0} = \frac{10^4}{0.005} = 2 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa} = E$$

**Verification:** The stress and modulus are **time-independent**, confirming the Voigt model's inability to predict stress relaxation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Voigt Model Mechanical Schematic (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["APPLIED STRAIN epsilon_0"] --> B["VOIGT ELEMENT IN PARALLEL"]
    B --> C["SPRING: sigma_s = E * epsilon"]
    B --> D["DASHPOT: sigma_d = eta * d_epsilon_dt"]
    C --> E["STRESS SUMMATION: sigma_total = sigma_s + sigma_d"]
    D --> E
    E --> F["FINAL OUTPUT: sigma_t = E * epsilon_0  CONSTANT"]
    F --> G["RELAXATION MODULUS: E_t = E  TIME INVARIANT"]
```

### 4.2 Comparative Response Curves (Mermaid Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    H["STRESS RELAXATION TEST INPUT"] --> I["STEP STRAIN epsilon_0 AT t = 0"]
    I --> J["MODEL SELECTION"]
    J --> K["MAXWELL MODEL: SPRING + DASHPOT IN SERIES"]
    J --> L["VOIGT MODEL: SPRING + DASHPOT IN PARALLEL"]
    K --> M["OUTPUT: sigma_t = sigma_0 EXP NEGATIVE t BY tau_M  EXPONENTIAL DECAY"]
    L --> N["OUTPUT: sigma_t = E * epsilon_0  CONSTANT FLAT LINE"]
    M --> O["DECAYS TO ZERO OVER TIME"]
    N --> P["REMAINS CONSTANT  NO STRESS RELAXATION"]
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture — Voigt Element Under Stress Relaxation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1["INPUT LAYER: STEP STRAIN APPLIED epsilon_0"] --> P2["KINEMATIC LAYER: PARALLEL CONSTRAINT epsilon_spring = epsilon_dashpot"]
    P2 --> P3["SPRING BRANCH: sigma_s = E * epsilon_0"]
    P2 --> P4["DASHPOT BRANCH: sigma_d = eta * d_epsilon_dt = 0"]
    P3 --> P5["STRESS ADDITION LAYER: sigma_total = sigma_s + sigma_d"]
    P4 --> P5
    P5 --> P6["OUTPUT LAYER: sigma_t = E * epsilon_0  TIME INDEPENDENT"]
    P6 --> P7["MODULUS LAYER: E_t = E  TIME INVARIANT"]
    P7 --> P8["PHYSICAL INTERPRETATION: NO STRESS RELAXATION OCCURS IN PURE VOIGT MODEL"]
```

### 4.4 Creep vs Stress Relaxation — Voigt Model Response Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q1["TEST TYPE A: CREEP TEST"] --> Q2["INPUT: CONSTANT STRESS sigma_0"]
    Q2 --> Q3["VOIGT RESPONSE: epsilon_t = sigma_0 BY E TIMES 1 MINUS EXP NEGATIVE t BY tau"]
    Q3 --> Q4["OUTPUT: STRAIN GROWS EXPONENTIALLY AND SATURATES"]

    R1["TEST TYPE B: STRESS RELAXATION"] --> R2["INPUT: CONSTANT STRAIN epsilon_0"]
    R2 --> R3["VOIGT RESPONSE: sigma_t = E * epsilon_0"]
    R3 --> R4["OUTPUT: STRESS IS INSTANTANEOUS AND REMAINS CONSTANT"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**
*State the constitutive equation of the Voigt model. Why does this model fail to predict stress relaxation in polymers?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Voigt (Kelvin–Voigt) model consists of a spring and a dashpot connected in **parallel**. Its constitutive equation is:

$$\sigma(t) = E\varepsilon(t) + \eta \frac{d\varepsilon(t)}{dt}$$

The model fails to predict stress relaxation because, in a stress relaxation test, the strain is held constant, i.e., $\dfrac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = 0$. The dashpot stress term vanishes, leaving $\sigma = E\varepsilon_0$, which is **time-independent**. Hence, no decay of stress with time is observed. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
*Define the retardation time for a Voigt element. Mention its physical significance.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The retardation time of a Voigt element is defined as:

$$\tau = \frac{\eta}{E}$$

where $\eta$ is the viscosity of the dashpot and $E$ is the spring modulus. Physically, $\tau$ represents the characteristic time required for the material to develop a delayed elastic strain under a sustained load. It governs the rate at which strain approaches its equilibrium value during creep. A larger $\tau$ indicates a slower, more viscous response. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 ESE Style Question A]**

**Q3A. (a)** Derive the constitutive equation for the Voigt (Kelvin–Voigt) model consisting of a spring and dashpot in parallel. Apply this to a stress relaxation test and obtain the expression for stress as a function of time. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A polymer sample is represented by a Voigt element with $E = 5 \times 10^7$ Pa and $\eta = 2 \times 10^9$ Pa·s. A step strain of magnitude $0.002$ is applied. Compute: (i) the retardation time, (ii) the stress immediately after strain application, and (iii) the relaxation modulus at $t = 50$ s. Comment on the time-dependence of the modulus. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

For the parallel arrangement, the strain is common and stress is additive:

$$\varepsilon_{\text{spring}} = \varepsilon_{\text{dashpot}} = \varepsilon \quad ; \quad \sigma = \sigma_s + \sigma_d$$

Substituting Hooke's law and Newton's law:

$$\sigma(t) = E\varepsilon(t) + \eta\frac{d\varepsilon(t)}{dt}$$

This is the Voigt constitutive equation. **(2 marks)**

In a stress relaxation test, a step strain $\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_0$ is imposed for $t > 0$, so $\dfrac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = 0$. **(2 marks)**

Substituting:

$$\sigma(t) = E\varepsilon_0 + \eta(0) = E\varepsilon_0$$

$$\boxed{\sigma(t) = E\varepsilon_0 = \text{constant}} \quad \textbf{(3 marks)}$$

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

Given: $E = 5 \times 10^7$ Pa, $\eta = 2 \times 10^9$ Pa·s, $\varepsilon_0 = 0.002$.

(i) Retardation time:

$$\tau = \frac{\eta}{E} = \frac{2 \times 10^9}{5 \times 10^7} = 40 \text{ s} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

(ii) Stress immediately after strain application:

$$\sigma(0^+) = E\varepsilon_0 = (5 \times 10^7)(0.002) = 1 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 100 \text{ kPa} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

(iii) Relaxation modulus at $t = 50$ s:

$$E(t) = \frac{\sigma(t)}{\varepsilon_0} = \frac{1 \times 10^5}{0.002} = 5 \times 10^7 \text{ Pa} = E \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**Comment:** The relaxation modulus is **time-independent** and remains equal to $E$ at all times. This confirms that the Voigt model does not exhibit stress relaxation. **(1 mark)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 ESE Style Question B]**

**Q3B. (a)** Explain the arrangement and physical significance of the Voigt model with a neat schematic representation. Compare its stress relaxation behavior with that of the Maxwell model. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Voigt element with $E = 1.5 \times 10^8$ Pa and $\eta = 6 \times 10^9$ Pa·s is subjected to a step strain of $0.004$. Determine: (i) the retardation time, (ii) the stress at $t = 0$ and at $t = 100$ s, and (iii) the relaxation modulus of the system. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Explanation and Comparison (7 marks):**

The Voigt model consists of a Hookean spring (modulus $E$) and a Newtonian dashpot (viscosity $\eta$) connected in **parallel**. In this arrangement, both elements experience the same strain, while the total stress is the sum of the spring stress and dashpot stress. The model is physically significant because it represents **delayed elastic response** — a behavior common in solid polymers where deformation is partly instantaneous and partly time-dependent. **(3 marks)**

**Comparison with Maxwell Model:**

- **Maxwell model** (spring and dashpot in series): Predicts **stress relaxation** — stress decays exponentially as $\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 e^{-t/\tau_M}$.
- **Voigt model** (spring and dashpot in parallel): **Does NOT predict stress relaxation** — stress remains constant at $E\varepsilon_0$ under constant strain.
- The Maxwell model is suitable for modeling **stress relaxation in polymers**, while the Voigt model is suited for **creep behavior**. **(4 marks)**

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

Given: $E = 1.5 \times 10^8$ Pa, $\eta = 6 \times 10^9$ Pa·s, $\varepsilon_0 = 0.004$.

(i) Retardation time:

$$\tau = \frac{\eta}{E} = \frac{6 \times 10^9}{1.5 \times 10^8} = 40 \text{ s} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

(ii) Stress at $t = 0$ and at $t = 100$ s:

$$\sigma(t) = E\varepsilon_0 = (1.5 \times 10^8)(0.004) = 6 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 600 \text{ kPa} \quad \textbf{(3 marks)}$$

Therefore: $\sigma(0) = \sigma(100) = 600$ kPa (stress is constant).

(iii) Relaxation modulus:

$$E(t) = \frac{\sigma(t)}{\varepsilon_0} = \frac{6 \times 10^5}{0.004} = 1.5 \times 10^8 \text{ Pa} = E \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Examinations:**

1. **Writing $\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 e^{-t/\tau}$ for the Voigt model** — This is the **Maxwell** result, not Voigt. Examiners specifically test whether students understand that the Voigt model **does not** show exponential decay of stress.

2. **Forgetting the strain derivative term** — Students often write $\sigma = E\varepsilon$ for the Voigt model, omitting $\eta\dfrac{d\varepsilon}{dt}$. This violates the parallel stress addition rule.

3. **Confusing creep with stress relaxation** — Stress relaxation uses **constant strain**; creep uses **constant stress**. Mixing these is a frequent KTU error.

4. **Incorrectly using the Heaviside function** — For $t > 0$, $H(t) = 1$, so $\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_0$, not $\varepsilon_0 H(t)$ written as if it varies. Ensure clarity that the derivative is zero for $t > 0$.

5. **Units omission** — Always quote stress in **Pa** or **kPa** and retardation time in **seconds**. KTU examiners specifically check for correct unit handling.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 3 – Voigt Stress Relaxation)**

- The **Voigt model** = spring $\parallel$ dashpot; **Maxwell model** = spring $\dashv$ dashpot (series).
- **Constitutive equation (Voigt):** $\sigma = E\varepsilon + \eta\dfrac{d\varepsilon}{dt}$
- **Stress relaxation test input:** constant strain $\varepsilon_0$, hence $\dfrac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = 0$.
- **Voigt stress relaxation result:** $\sigma(t) = E\varepsilon_0$ — **constant, no decay**.
- **Voigt relaxation modulus:** $E(t) = E$ — **time-invariant**.
- **Retardation time:** $\tau = \dfrac{\eta}{E}$ — characteristic time of the Voigt model.
- The Voigt model **cannot predict stress relaxation** in its pure form; it predicts **creep** instead.
- **Voigt creep compliance:** $J(t) = \dfrac{1}{E}\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$.
- **Voigt creep recovery:** $\varepsilon(t) = \dfrac{\sigma_0}{E} e^{-t/\tau}$.
- **Comparison keyword:** Maxwell → exponential decay; Voigt → flat line.
- **Engineering applications:** solid polymer creep, vibration damping, geological materials, biomedical polymers.
- **Units to remember:** $E$ in Pa, $\eta$ in Pa·s, $\tau$ in s, $\sigma$ in Pa, $\varepsilon$ dimensionless.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Maxwell-Wiechert Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT402 - POLYMER PHYSICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Maxwell-Wiechert Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Maxwell-Wiechert Model (Generalized Maxwell Model):** A phenomenological viscoelastic constitutive model representing a linear polymer as a discrete (or continuous) spectrum of **N Maxwell elements arranged in parallel**, where each Maxwell element consists of a Hookean spring (modulus $E_i$) and a Newtonian dashpot (viscosity $\eta_i$) connected in series. It is used to describe the time-dependent stress relaxation and creep behavior of polymers that cannot be captured by a single relaxation time.

The **single Maxwell model** is the building block, representing the simplest viscoelastic element whose constitutive equation is:

$$\sigma + \tau \frac{d\sigma}{dt} = E \tau \frac{d\varepsilon}{dt}$$

where the **relaxation time** $\tau = \eta / E$ characterizes the rate of stress decay.

The **Maxwell-Wiechert (generalized) model** overcomes the limitations of the single Maxwell model by summing multiple relaxation processes:

$$\sigma(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sigma_i(t) = \varepsilon_0 \sum_{i=1}^{N} E_i \exp(-t/\tau_i)$$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition)

Imagine a **bouquet of rubber bands dipped in honey jars**, all tied together at the top and bottom so they all stretch together:

- Each **rubber band** = **spring** (instantaneous elastic response — stores energy).
- Each **honey jar (dashpot)** = **viscous flow** (time-dependent rearrangement — dissipates energy).
- A **single Maxwell element** is ONE rubber band pulling through ONE jar of honey.
- A **polymer** has many molecular segments (long chains, entanglements, side groups) relaxing at *different speeds*. So instead of one honey jar, we have **N jars of different thicknesses in parallel**, each representing a distinct molecular relaxation process.

> [!NOTE]
> Think of polymer molecules like a crowd at a concert: some people (small segments) move quickly to the music, others (large chain segments) respond slowly. The Wiechert model sums all these responses into one macroscopic stress.

**Physical constants and standards used in this module:**

- **Relaxation time** $\tau$: typically $10^{-12}$ to $10^{2}$ seconds for polymers
- **Glass transition temperature** $T_g$: the temperature above which relaxation times drop sharply
- **Time-Temperature Superposition Principle (TTSP)**: shifts curves horizontally along the log-time axis
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Single Maxwell Element — Foundational Logic

A Maxwell element is constructed by placing a **spring (E)** and **dashpot ($\eta$) in series**:

- The same stress $\sigma$ acts on both elements (series connection).
- The total strain is the sum of individual strains: $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_{\text{spring}} + \varepsilon_{\text{dashpot}}$.
- Differentiating with respect to time:

$$\frac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = \frac{1}{E}\frac{d\sigma}{dt} + \frac{\sigma}{\eta}$$

- Rearranging yields the **Maxwell constitutive equation**:

$$\sigma + \tau \frac{d\sigma}{dt} = E\tau \frac{d\varepsilon}{dt} \quad \text{where} \quad \tau = \frac{\eta}{E}$$

### 2.2 Limitations of the Single Maxwell Model

> [!WARNING]
> A single Maxwell element is **NOT** adequate for real polymers because:
> 1. It predicts **zero equilibrium modulus** (predicts fluid-like behavior) — but solid polymers retain stiffness.
> 2. It uses **only one relaxation time** — real polymers exhibit a **spectrum** of relaxation times spanning many decades.
> 3. It cannot describe **transitions** between glassy, rubbery, and terminal flow regions.

### 2.3 The Maxwell-Wiechert Solution — Generalized Form

To overcome these limitations, **N Maxwell elements are placed in parallel**:

- All elements experience the **same strain** $\varepsilon(t)$ (parallel connection).
- The total stress is the **sum** of individual stresses: $\sigma(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sigma_i(t)$.
- Each element has its own modulus $E_i$, viscosity $\eta_i$, and relaxation time $\tau_i = \eta_i / E_i$.

**Stress relaxation at constant strain $\varepsilon_0$:**

$$\sigma(t) = \varepsilon_0 \sum_{i=1}^{N} E_i \exp(-t/\tau_i)$$

The **relaxation modulus** is therefore:

$$E(t) = \frac{\sigma(t)}{\varepsilon_0} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} E_i \exp(-t/\tau_i)$$

**Continuous relaxation spectrum form** (when N becomes a continuum):

$$E(t) = E_e + \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} H(\tau) \exp(-t/\tau)\, d(\ln \tau)$$

where $H(\tau)$ is the **relaxation spectrum** and $E_e$ is the equilibrium modulus.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| # | Concept | Equation | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|----------|---------------|
| 1 | Maxwell Constitutive Law | $\sigma + \tau \dot{\sigma} = E\tau \dot{\varepsilon}$ | $\tau = \eta/E$ in **seconds (s)** |
| 2 | Single Maxwell Stress Relaxation | $\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \exp(-t/\tau)$ | $\sigma_0 = E\varepsilon_0$ in **Pa** |
| 3 | Single Maxwell Creep Response | $\varepsilon(t) = \sigma_0/E + (\sigma_0/\eta)t$ | Linear viscous flow term |
| 4 | Wiechert Relaxation Modulus | $E(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} E_i \exp(-t/\tau_i)$ | Discrete spectrum |
| 5 | Wiechert Continuous Form | $E(t) = E_e + \int H(\tau) \exp(-t/\tau)\, d(\ln\tau)$ | $H(\tau)$: relaxation spectrum |
| 6 | Creep Compliance (Wiechert) | $J(t) = \sum (1/E_i)[1 - \exp(-t/\tau_i')] + t/\eta_0$ | $\eta_0$: terminal viscosity |
| 7 | Instantaneous Modulus | $E(0) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} E_i = E_g$ | Glassy modulus (~$10^{9}$ Pa) |
| 8 | Equilibrium Modulus | $E(\infty) = E_e$ | Rubbery plateau modulus |
| 9 | Stress-Strain Superposition | $\sigma(t) = \int_0^t E(t-u)\, d\varepsilon(u)$ | Boltzmann superposition |
| 10 | Relaxation Time Range | $10^{-12}\,\text{s} \leq \tau \leq 10^{3}\,\text{s}$ | Spans many decades for polymers |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Maxwell-Wiechert model is the cornerstone of:

- **Tire design**: predicting rolling resistance and wet grip via the relaxation spectrum of tread compounds.
- **Vibration damping**: selecting polymer mounts with appropriate $H(\tau)$ to damp specific frequency ranges.
- **3D printing**: ensuring that molten polymer chains can flow (low $\eta$) yet rapidly recover stiffness (high $E$) upon cooling.
- **Biomedical devices**: silicone implants and catheters must relax stress without permanent deformation.
- **Packaging films**: optimizing shelf life by tuning barrier properties through controlled relaxation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** This topic integrates mathematical modeling, molecular interpretation, and engineering application — a prime target for **14-mark analytical questions**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Constitutive Equation of a Single Maxwell Element

**Given:** A spring of modulus $E$ in series with a dashpot of viscosity $\eta$.

**Step 1:** The spring obeys Hooke's Law:

$$\sigma = E \varepsilon_{\text{spring}} \implies \varepsilon_{\text{spring}} = \frac{\sigma}{E}$$

**Step 2:** The dashpot obeys Newton's Law of viscosity:

$$\sigma = \eta \frac{d\varepsilon_{\text{dashpot}}}{dt}$$

**Step 3:** For a series arrangement, total strain is additive:

$$\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_{\text{spring}}(t) + \varepsilon_{\text{dashpot}}(t)$$

**Step 4:** Differentiate both sides with respect to time $t$:

$$\frac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = \frac{d\varepsilon_{\text{spring}}}{dt} + \frac{d\varepsilon_{\text{dashpot}}}{dt}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute from Steps 1 and 2:

$$\frac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = \frac{1}{E}\frac{d\sigma}{dt} + \frac{\sigma}{\eta}$$

**Step 6:** Multiply both sides by $\eta$:

$$\eta \frac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = \frac{\eta}{E}\frac{d\sigma}{dt} + \sigma$$

**Step 7:** Substitute $\tau = \eta/E$:

$$\sigma + \tau \frac{d\sigma}{dt} = E\tau \frac{d\varepsilon}{dt}$$

This is the **Maxwell constitutive equation**.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Stress Relaxation in a Single Maxwell Element

**Given:** A constant strain $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_0$ is applied instantaneously at $t = 0$ and held.

**Step 1:** Since strain is constant:

$$\frac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = 0$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into the constitutive equation:

$$\sigma + \tau \frac{d\sigma}{dt} = 0$$

**Step 3:** Rearrange into a separable ODE:

$$\frac{d\sigma}{\sigma} = -\frac{dt}{\tau}$$

**Step 4:** Integrate both sides from $0$ to $t$:

$$\int_{\sigma_0}^{\sigma(t)} \frac{d\sigma'}{\sigma'} = -\frac{1}{\tau}\int_0^t dt'$$

**Step 5:** Evaluate the integrals:

$$\ln\left(\frac{\sigma(t)}{\sigma_0}\right) = -\frac{t}{\tau}$$

**Step 6:** Exponentiate:

$$\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \exp(-t/\tau)$$

where the initial stress $\sigma_0 = E\varepsilon_0$ because the dashpot cannot respond instantaneously — the spring alone takes the initial load.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Creep Response of a Single Maxwell Element

**Given:** A constant stress $\sigma_0$ is applied at $t = 0$ and held.

**Step 1:** Constant stress implies:

$$\frac{d\sigma}{dt} = 0$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into the constitutive equation:

$$\sigma_0 + 0 = E\tau \frac{d\varepsilon}{dt}$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $d\varepsilon/dt$:

$$\frac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = \frac{\sigma_0}{E\tau} = \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta}$$

**Step 4:** Integrate from $0$ to $t$ with initial condition $\varepsilon(0) = \sigma_0 / E$ (instantaneous elastic response):

$$\varepsilon(t) - \frac{\sigma_0}{E} = \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta}t$$

**Step 5:** Final creep equation:

$$\varepsilon(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{E} + \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta}t$$

This shows the **instantaneous elastic jump** plus **linear viscous flow**.

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Maxwell-Wiechert Stress Relaxation Modulus

**Given:** N Maxwell elements in parallel, all sharing strain $\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_0$.

**Step 1:** Each Maxwell element $i$ independently relaxes with its own $\tau_i$:

$$\sigma_i(t) = E_i \varepsilon_0 \exp(-t/\tau_i)$$

**Step 2:** Parallel arrangement implies stress addition:

$$\sigma(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sigma_i(t) = \varepsilon_0 \sum_{i=1}^{N} E_i \exp(-t/\tau_i)$$

**Step 3:** Define the **stress relaxation modulus**:

$$E(t) \equiv \frac{\sigma(t)}{\varepsilon_0} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} E_i \exp(-t/\tau_i)$$

**Step 4:** Limiting cases:

$$E(0) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} E_i = E_g \quad \text{(glassy modulus, instantaneous)}$$

$$E(\infty) = E_e \quad \text{(equilibrium modulus, long-term rubbery plateau if solid)}$$

**Step 5:** For a truly continuous distribution (infinite elements), sum becomes integral:

$$E(t) = E_e + \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} H(\tau) \exp(-t/\tau)\, d(\ln \tau)$$

where $H(\tau)$ is the **relaxation spectrum** density function.

### 3.5 Derivation 5: Creep Compliance for Maxwell-Wiechert Model

The **Kelvin-Voigt representation** (N Kelvin-Voigt elements in series + a free dashpot) gives:

$$J(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} J_i \left[1 - \exp(-t/\tau_i')\right] + \frac{t}{\eta_0}$$

where $J_i = 1/E_i$ and $\tau_i' = \eta_i / E_i$ is the **retardation time**.

At $t \to \infty$: $J(\infty) = \sum J_i + t/\eta_0$ — unbounded for a polymer melt (flow), but finite for a cross-linked rubber.

### 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix

| Step Type | Input Condition | Method to Use | Expected Output |
|-----------|----------------|---------------|-----------------|
| **Stress Relaxation** | Constant strain $\varepsilon_0$ | Use $E(t) = \sum E_i \exp(-t/\tau_i)$ | Decaying stress $\sigma(t)$ |
| **Creep** | Constant stress $\sigma_0$ | Use $J(t)$ form (Kelvin-Voigt) | Growing strain $\varepsilon(t)$ |
| **Dynamic Modulus** | Oscillatory strain $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_0 \sin(\omega t)$ | Substitute into constitutive law | $E^*(\omega) = E'(\omega) + iE''(\omega)$ |
| **Storage Modulus** | $\omega \to \infty$ | Use high-frequency limit | $E'(\infty) = \sum E_i$ |
| **Loss Modulus** | $\omega \to \omega_i = 1/\tau_i$ | Find peak of $E''(\omega)$ | Maximum at $\omega = 1/\tau_i$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** When asked to "derive" in an exam, students often write the final formula without showing the differential equation step. Always show: **(i)** assumption, **(ii)** substitution into the constitutive equation, **(iii)** integration, **(iv)** final result with units.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Single Maxwell Element — Symbolic and Block Representation

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Applied Stress s] --> S((Spring E))
    S --> D([Dashpot eta])
    D --> B[Output Response]
    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style B fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600
    style S fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#009933
    style D fill:#f4d4d4,stroke:#cc0000
```

**Interpretation:** Stress flows sequentially through the spring (instantaneous elastic response) and dashpot (time-delayed viscous response). Same stress, additive strain.

### 4.2 Maxwell-Wiechert Model — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT LAYER"]
        I[Applied Strain e_t at all times]
    end

    subgraph PARALLEL_BANK["PARALLEL MAXWELL ELEMENT BANK"]
        M1[Element 1: E1, eta1, tau1]
        M2[Element 2: E2, eta2, tau2]
        M3[Element i: Ei, etai, taui]
        MN[Element N: EN, etaN, tauN]
    end

    subgraph SUMMER["STRESS SUMMATION"]
        SUM[Sigma t equals sum of sigma i t]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT LAYER"]
        O[Total Stress Response sigma t]
        MOD[Relaxation Modulus E t equals sigma t divided by e0]
    end

    I --> M1
    I --> M2
    I --> M3
    I --> MN
    M1 --> SUM
    M2 --> SUM
    M3 --> SUM
    MN --> SUM
    SUM --> O
    O --> MOD

    style I fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style O fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600
    style MOD fill:#f0e1ff,stroke:#6600cc
    style SUM fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000
```

**Interpretation:** Every Maxwell element experiences the same strain (parallel bus architecture), but contributes its own stress. The summer node combines all contributions into the total macroscopic stress.

### 4.3 Relaxation Modulus Decay Curve (Schematic Behavior)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph TIME_AXIS["Log Time Axis"]
        T1[t equals 0] --> T2[t equals tau1] --> T3[t equals tau2] --> T4[t equals tauN] --> T5[t approaches infinity]
    end

    subgraph MOD_AXIS["Modulus E t on log scale"]
        M1[E t equals Eg glassy] --> M2[E t drops] --> M3[E t at rubbery plateau] --> M4[E t at flow] --> M5[E t approaches Ee]
    end
```

**Interpretation:** Short relaxation times ($\tau$ small) decay first — representing local segmental motions. Long relaxation times dominate the terminal flow region — representing whole-chain reptation.

### 4.4 Mapping the Physical Mechanism to the Model

| Physical Phenomenon | Model Element | Mathematical Signature |
|---------------------|---------------|------------------------|
| Bond stretching (glassy) | Spring with high $E_g$ | Instantaneous elastic response |
| Side-group rotation | Maxwell element with small $\tau$ | Fast exponential decay |
| Entanglement slippage | Maxwell element with medium $\tau$ | Decay in transition zone |
| Whole-chain reptation | Maxwell element with very large $\tau$ | Slow terminal decay |
| Cross-link network (rubber) | Spring in parallel with bank | Finite $E_e$ at $t \to \infty$ |
| Molecular friction | Dashpot viscosity $\eta$ | Linear flow term $t/\eta$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Application:** The spectrum $H(\tau)$ can be extracted experimentally from dynamic mechanical analysis (DMA) data and fed back into the model to predict long-term polymer behavior from short-term tests — a key concept in **Time-Temperature Superposition (TTS)**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3]**  
Define the Maxwell model and write its constitutive equation. What is the physical significance of the relaxation time $\tau$?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Maxwell model** is a mechanical analog representing viscoelastic behavior using a Hookean spring (modulus $E$) and a Newtonian dashpot (viscosity $\eta$) connected **in series**.

**Constitutive equation:**

$$\sigma + \tau \frac{d\sigma}{dt} = E\tau \frac{d\varepsilon}{dt}, \quad \text{where } \tau = \frac{\eta}{E}$$

**Physical significance of $\tau$:** It represents the characteristic time required for stress to decay to $1/e$ ($\approx 36.8\%$) of its initial value under constant strain. It sets the natural timescale separating elastic-dominated and viscous-dominated responses.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern, Module 3]**  
Explain why a single Maxwell element is inadequate to describe polymer viscoelasticity. How does the Maxwell-Wiechert model overcome this limitation?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **single Maxwell element** fails because:

1. It assumes **only one relaxation time**, while real polymers exhibit a broad spectrum of molecular motions spanning many decades ($10^{-12}$ to $10^{3}$ s).
2. It predicts **zero equilibrium modulus** — incompatible with solid polymers that retain stiffness indefinitely.
3. It cannot capture the **glassy, transition, rubbery, and flow** regions of polymer behavior.

The **Maxwell-Wiechert model** places **N Maxwell elements in parallel**, each with its own $E_i$, $\eta_i$, $\tau_i$. The relaxation modulus becomes:

$$E(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} E_i \exp(-t/\tau_i)$$

which can reproduce any experimentally observed decay curve by appropriately choosing the spectrum $\{E_i, \tau_i\}$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks): Comprehensive Derivation**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Style, Module 3]**

**(a)** Derive the stress relaxation equation for a single Maxwell element under constant strain. Show all steps. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the construction of the Maxwell-Wiechert model. Derive the stress relaxation modulus for N elements in parallel and discuss its limiting behavior at $t \to 0$ and $t \to \infty$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Stress Relaxation for Single Maxwell Element (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Write the constitutive equation. For a Maxwell element:

$$\sigma + \tau \frac{d\sigma}{dt} = E\tau \frac{d\varepsilon}{dt}, \quad \tau = \eta/E$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Apply the stress relaxation condition. Under constant strain $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_0$ applied instantaneously:

$$\frac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = 0 \implies \sigma + \tau \frac{d\sigma}{dt} = 0$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Rearrange into separable ODE:

$$\frac{d\sigma}{\sigma} = -\frac{dt}{\tau}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Integrate with limits. At $t = 0$, all strain is borne by the spring:

$$\sigma(0) = E\varepsilon_0 \equiv \sigma_0$$

$$\int_{\sigma_0}^{\sigma(t)} \frac{d\sigma'}{\sigma'} = -\frac{1}{\tau}\int_0^t dt'$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Solve:

$$\ln\frac{\sigma(t)}{\sigma_0} = -\frac{t}{\tau} \implies \sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \exp(-t/\tau)$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Discussion. At $t = \tau$, stress decays to $\sigma_0/e$. As $t \to \infty$, stress vanishes — confirming the **fluid-like** terminal behavior of the single Maxwell model.

**Step 7 (1 mark):** State the final equation with units: $\sigma(t)$ in Pa, $\tau$ in seconds.

**(b) Maxwell-Wiechert Model and Stress Relaxation Modulus (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Construction. Place N Maxwell elements in parallel. Each element has $E_i$, $\eta_i$, $\tau_i$. All share the same strain $\varepsilon(t)$; total stress is the sum:

$$\sigma(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sigma_i(t)$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Derivation. From part (a), for constant strain $\varepsilon_0$, each element contributes:

$$\sigma_i(t) = E_i \varepsilon_0 \exp(-t/\tau_i)$$

Adding all contributions:

$$\sigma(t) = \varepsilon_0 \sum_{i=1}^{N} E_i \exp(-t/\tau_i)$$

The **relaxation modulus** is:

$$E(t) = \frac{\sigma(t)}{\varepsilon_0} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} E_i \exp(-t/\tau_i)$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Limiting cases.

$$E(0) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} E_i = E_g \quad \text{(glassy/instantaneous modulus)}$$

$$E(\infty) = 0 \quad \text{(if no equilibrium spring, fluid-like)}$$

$$E(\infty) = E_e \quad \text{(if a spring is added in parallel, solid-like)}$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Discussion. By choosing $\{E_i, \tau_i\}$ appropriately (or by a continuous spectrum $H(\tau)$), the Wiechert model can reproduce any experimental decay curve of a real polymer across the glassy, transition, rubbery, and flow regions.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks): Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — Supplementary Pattern, Module 3]**

**(a)** Discuss the limitations of the single Maxwell model in describing polymer behavior. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the creep compliance equation for the Maxwell-Wiechert (Kelvin-Voigt series representation) model and explain its physical significance. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Limitations of Single Maxwell Model (7 marks):**

**Point 1 (2 marks):** Single relaxation time. Real polymers have multiple molecular relaxation mechanisms — bond vibrations ($10^{-12}$ s), side-group rotations ($10^{-9}$ s), segmental motions near $T_g$ ($10^{-2}$ s), entanglement disentanglement ($10^{2}$ s), and whole-chain reptation ($10^{4}$ s). One $\tau$ cannot span this range.

**Point 2 (2 marks):** Equilibrium modulus prediction. Single Maxwell predicts $E(\infty) = 0$, meaning the polymer flows indefinitely. But cross-linked rubbers and glasses retain finite stiffness.

**Point 3 (1.5 marks):** No rubbery plateau. The transition from glassy to flow region in real polymers shows a flat plateau where modulus is roughly constant. The Maxwell model gives a continuous exponential decay.

**Point 4 (1.5 marks):** Frequency-domain failure. In DMA experiments, real polymers show broad loss modulus peaks $E''(\omega)$, but the Maxwell model gives only a single Debye peak.

**(b) Creep Compliance Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Kelvin-Voigt series representation. Represent the polymer as N Kelvin-Voigt elements (spring $E_i'$ parallel to dashpot $\eta_i'$) connected **in series**, with a free dashpot $\eta_0$ at the end to represent permanent flow.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** For each Kelvin-Voigt element under constant stress $\sigma_0$:

$$\varepsilon_i(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{E_i'}\left[1 - \exp(-t/\tau_i')\right], \quad \tau_i' = \eta_i'/E_i'$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Total strain from all elements plus the free dashpot:
