$$\varepsilon(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{\sigma_0}{E_i'}\left[1 - \exp(-t/\tau_i')\right] + \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta_0}t$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** The **creep compliance** $J(t) = \varepsilon(t)/\sigma_0$ is:

$$J(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} J_i\left[1 - \exp(-t/\tau_i')\right] + \frac{t}{\eta_0}, \quad J_i = 1/E_i'$$

**Physical significance:** $J(t)$ grows from an initial elastic compliance $J_0 = 1/E_g$ to an equilibrium compliance plus viscous flow. It describes how a polymer deforms under sustained load — critical for predicting **creep failure** in structural polymer components.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:**
> 1. **Mixing Maxwell and Kelvin-Voigt**: Students often confuse the *parallel* and *series* arrangements. Remember — **Maxwell** = spring + dashpot in **series** (used for stress relaxation). **Kelvin-Voigt** = spring + dashpot in **parallel** (used for creep). The **Wiechert model** = many Maxwells in parallel; the **Kelvin chain** = many Kelvin-Voigts in series.
> 2. **Forgetting initial conditions**: At $t = 0$, the dashpot cannot move instantaneously. Always state $\sigma(0) = E\varepsilon_0$ for stress relaxation.
> 3. **Units confusion**: $\tau$ is in **seconds (s)**, $E$ in **Pa**, $\eta$ in **Pa·s**. Always check dimensions: $[\eta]/[E] = \text{Pa·s}/\text{Pa} = \text{s}$. ✓
> 4. **Single Maxwell mark loss**: Writing only the final equation without derivation loses 4-5 marks out of 7. Always show the differential equation step.
> 5. **Neglecting physical interpretation**: KTU awards marks for explaining *why* the model fails and *how* the Wiechert generalization fixes it. Don't just write formulas.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Maxwell-Wiechert Model — Quick Recall**

- ☐ **Maxwell element** = spring $E$ in series with dashpot $\eta$; same stress, additive strain.
- ☐ **Constitutive equation**: $\sigma + \tau \dot{\sigma} = E\tau \dot{\varepsilon}$, where $\tau = \eta/E$.
- ☐ **Stress relaxation** at constant strain: $\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \exp(-t/\tau)$.
- ☐ **Creep** at constant stress: $\varepsilon(t) = \sigma_0/E + (\sigma_0/\eta)t$.
- ☐ **Single Maxwell limitation**: only ONE $\tau$ — cannot describe real polymer spectra.
- ☐ **Maxwell-Wiechert (generalized)**: N Maxwell elements in parallel, each with its own $E_i$, $\eta_i$, $\tau_i$.
- ☐ **Relaxation modulus**: $E(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} E_i \exp(-t/\tau_i)$.
- ☐ **Instantaneous modulus** $E(0) = \sum E_i = E_g$ (glassy).
- ☐ **Equilibrium modulus** $E(\infty) = E_e$ (with parallel spring) or 0 (without — fluid).
- ☐ **Continuous spectrum form**: $E(t) = E_e + \int H(\tau)\exp(-t/\tau)\, d(\ln \tau)$.
- ☐ **Creep compliance** (Kelvin chain): $J(t) = \sum J_i[1 - \exp(-t/\tau_i')] + t/\eta_0$.
- ☐ **Time range of polymer relaxation**: $10^{-12}$ s (bond) to $10^{4}$ s (chain).
- ☐ **Physical interpretations**: bond stretching → spring; friction → dashpot; entanglement → slow $\tau$.
- ☐ **Applications**: tire design, vibration damping, 3D printing, biomedical devices.
- ☐ **Exam tip**: Always show derivation steps — ODE, integration limits, initial conditions, final result with units.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Exam Mantra:** "The Maxwell model is a *spring + dashpot in series* used for *stress relaxation*. The Kelvin-Voigt model is a *spring + dashpot in parallel* used for *creep*. The **Maxwell-Wiechert** is *N Maxwells in parallel* — summing over many relaxation times to capture the full spectrum of polymer viscoelasticity."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Burgers Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT402 - POLYMER PHYSICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Burgers Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Burgers Model (Definition):**
> The **Burgers Model** is a four-parameter, four-element linear viscoelastic mechanical analog model that combines a **Maxwell element** (spring and dashpot in series) placed in series with a **Kelvin–Voigt element** (spring and dashpot in parallel). It is the most general spring-dashpot combination that can describe the complete viscoelastic response of a polymer — instantaneous elasticity, delayed (retarded) elasticity, and permanent viscous flow — under creep loading.

The model contains **two springs** (with moduli $E_1$ and $E_2$) and **two dashpots** (with viscosities $\eta_1$ and $\eta_2$). It captures the three fundamental deformation modes of a real polymer in a single analytical framework.

| Element | Symbol | Physical Role |
|---|---|---|
| Spring 1 | $E_1$ | Instantaneous elastic deformation |
| Dashpot 1 | $\eta_1$ | Irrecoverable viscous flow |
| Spring 2 | $E_2$ | Stiffness of the retarded (delayed) branch |
| Dashpot 2 | $\eta_2$ | Dashpot retarding the recovery in Kelvin–Voigt |

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world analogy — the "Bouncy Clay" experiment:**
> Imagine squeezing a soft chewing-gum ball (a viscoelastic polymer) between your fingers and holding it.
> 1. **The instant squish** the moment you press → that's the lone spring $E_1$ (Hookean response, immediate).
> 2. **The slow, continued squish** while you keep holding it → that's the Kelvin–Voigt unit, $E_2$ and $\eta_2$ working together (delayed elasticity).
> 3. **The fact that when you finally release, the ball does not regain its full original shape** (a small permanent dent remains) → that's the free dashpot $\eta_1$ letting the material flow irreversibly.
>
> The Burgers Model is precisely this three-stage behavior, drawn as a circuit of mechanical elements.

- **Total number of mechanical elements:** **4** (2 springs + 2 dashpots).
- **Number of independent material parameters:** **4** ($E_1$, $E_2$, $\eta_1$, $\eta_2$).
- **Retardation time of the Kelvin–Voigt unit:** $\tau = \eta_2 / E_2$.
- **Relaxation time of the Maxwell unit:** $\lambda = \eta_1 / E_1$.

### Why the Burgers Model Matters in Polymer Engineering

A single Maxwell element fails to predict permanent flow; a single Kelvin–Voigt fails to predict instantaneous elastic strain. Real polymer products — rubber dampers, tire treads, cable sheathing, EVA midsoles, polymer gels — exhibit **all three** phenomena. The Burgers model is the simplest analog that can capture them simultaneously, making it a workhorse in **finite-element simulations of polymer components under sustained load**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mechanical Schematic (Standard Form)

The standard Burgers arrangement is:

$$(\text{Spring } E_1 \;-\; \text{Dashpot } \eta_1) \;\; \text{in series with} \;\; (\text{Spring } E_2 \parallel \text{Dashpot } \eta_2)$$

- The **Maxwell arm** ($E_1$, $\eta_1$) governs instantaneous strain and irrecoverable flow.
- The **Kelvin–Voigt arm** ($E_2$, $\eta_2$) governs time-dependent but fully recoverable (delayed) strain.
- Because the two arms are in **series**, the **stresses are equal**, but the **strains are additive**.

### 2.2 Governing Stress–Strain Relations (Component-Level)

For the Maxwell arm (series spring $E_1$ and dashpot $\eta_1$):

$$\dot{\varepsilon}_M = \frac{\dot{\sigma}}{E_1} + \frac{\sigma}{\eta_1}$$

For the Kelvin–Voigt arm (parallel spring $E_2$ and dashpot $\eta_2$):

$$\sigma = E_2 \varepsilon_{KV} + \eta_2 \dot{\varepsilon}_{KV}$$

### 2.3 Key Engineering Assumptions

- **Linear viscoelasticity:** All elements obey Hooke's law (spring) and Newton's law (dashpot); strains remain small (< 5 %).
- **Time-temperature superposition (TTS):** Above $T_g$, parameters $E_1$, $E_2$, $\eta_1$, $\eta_2$ shift with temperature through WLF / Arrhenius equations.
- **Constant stress creep test:** The most common loading condition used to identify Burgers parameters experimentally.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Equation | Units | Description |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Creep strain (constant stress $\sigma_0$) | $\varepsilon(t) = \dfrac{\sigma_0}{E_1} + \dfrac{\sigma_0}{E_2}\!\left(1 - e^{-t E_2 / \eta_2}\right) + \dfrac{\sigma_0 t}{\eta_1}$ | dimensionless | Total strain — 3 terms correspond to elastic, delayed, and flow |
| 2 | Creep compliance | $J(t) = \dfrac{1}{E_1} + \dfrac{1}{E_2}\!\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right) + \dfrac{t}{\eta_1}$ | $\text{Pa}^{-1}$ | Strain per unit stress |
| 3 | Retardation time | $\tau = \dfrac{\eta_2}{E_2}$ | $\text{s}$ | Time scale of the delayed Kelvin–Voigt recovery |
| 4 | Relaxation time | $\lambda = \dfrac{\eta_1}{E_1}$ | $\text{s}$ | Time scale of stress decay in the Maxwell arm |
| 5 | Initial elastic strain | $\varepsilon_0 = \dfrac{\sigma_0}{E_1}$ | dimensionless | Jump at $t = 0^+$ |
| 6 | Steady-state creep rate | $\dot{\varepsilon}_{\infty} = \dfrac{\sigma_0}{\eta_1}$ | $\text{s}^{-1}$ | Slope of the linear flow region |
| 7 | Stress relaxation (Burgers) | $\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \!\left[ m\, e^{-t/\lambda} + (1-m)\dfrac{r\, e^{-t/\lambda} - e^{-t/\tau}}{r - 1} \right]$ | $\text{Pa}$ | Under constant strain $\varepsilon_0$ |
| 8 | Auxiliary parameters | $m = \dfrac{E_1}{E_1 + E_2}, \quad r = \dfrac{\lambda}{\tau}$ | — | Used in stress relaxation form |
| 9 | Recoverable (anelastic) strain | $\varepsilon_{KV,\infty} = \dfrac{\sigma_0}{E_2}$ | dimensionless | Final delayed strain at $t \to \infty$ |
| 10 | Permanent set (residual strain) | $\varepsilon_p = \dfrac{\sigma_0 t_r}{\eta_1}$ | dimensionless | Strain unrecovered after unloading at $t = t_r$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic (for exam):** **"E – KE – η"** → **E**lastic jump → **K**elvin delayed **E**lasticity → **η** (eta) viscous flow. The strain-time curve under creep looks exactly like the alphabet letter "J" lying on its side.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Tire and rubber industry:** Predicting the rolling resistance and cornering stiffness of filled elastomers.
- **Polymer pipeline design (HDPE / MDPE):** Long-term creep rupture under internal pressure uses Burgers-derived compliance.
- **Vibration dampers and mounts:** Selecting $E_1, E_2, \eta_1, \eta_2$ to tune a desired loss-tangent $\tan \delta$ at a specific frequency.
- **Biomedical polymers (silicone implants, hydrogel scaffolds):** Modeling the time-dependent mechanical response of soft tissue analogues.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Burgers Creep Equation (Constant Stress $\sigma_0$)

**Given:** A constant stress $\sigma(t) = \sigma_0$ is applied at $t = 0$. The model is a Maxwell unit in series with a Kelvin–Voigt unit.

**Step 1 — Series strain additivity.**
Because the two units are in series, the total strain is the sum of the strains in each unit:

$$\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_M(t) + \varepsilon_{KV}(t)$$

**Step 2 — Maxwell unit strain under constant stress.**
For the Maxwell spring (Hookean): $\varepsilon_{M,\text{spring}} = \sigma_0 / E_1$ — this is a constant because $\sigma$ is constant.
For the Maxwell dashpot (Newtonian): $\dot{\varepsilon}_{M,\text{dash}} = \sigma_0 / \eta_1$, giving $\varepsilon_{M,\text{dash}} = \sigma_0 t / \eta_1$.

Therefore, total Maxwell strain:

$$\varepsilon_M(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{E_1} + \frac{\sigma_0 t}{\eta_1}$$

**Step 3 — Kelvin–Voigt unit strain under constant stress.**
Differential equation: $\sigma_0 = E_2 \varepsilon_{KV} + \eta_2 \dot{\varepsilon}_{KV}$.

Rewrite as a first-order linear ODE:

$$\dot{\varepsilon}_{KV} + \frac{E_2}{\eta_2}\varepsilon_{KV} = \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta_2}$$

**Step 4 — Solve the ODE using an integrating factor $e^{E_2 t / \eta_2}$.**
Multiplying both sides:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\varepsilon_{KV}\, e^{E_2 t / \eta_2}\right) = \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta_2} e^{E_2 t / \eta_2}$$

**Step 5 — Integrate both sides from $0$ to $t$.**

$$\varepsilon_{KV}(t)\, e^{E_2 t / \eta_2} - \varepsilon_{KV}(0) = \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta_2}\!\left[\frac{\eta_2}{E_2}\!\left(e^{E_2 t / \eta_2} - 1\right)\right]$$

**Step 6 — Apply the initial condition $\varepsilon_{KV}(0) = 0$ (Kelvin–Voigt unit cannot deform instantaneously, because the dashpot blocks sudden motion).**

$$\varepsilon_{KV}(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{E_2}\!\left(1 - e^{-E_2 t / \eta_2}\right)$$

**Step 7 — Add the two contributions to get the total Burgers creep strain.**

$$\boxed{\;\varepsilon(t) = \underbrace{\frac{\sigma_0}{E_1}}_{\text{instantaneous}} \;+\; \underbrace{\frac{\sigma_0}{E_2}\!\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)}_{\text{delayed (Kelvin–Voigt)}} \;+\; \underbrace{\frac{\sigma_0 t}{\eta_1}}_{\text{viscous flow}}\;}$$

with the retardation time $\tau = \eta_2 / E_2$.

**Step 8 — Creep compliance $J(t) = \varepsilon(t)/\sigma_0$.**

$$\boxed{\;J(t) = \frac{1}{E_1} + \frac{1}{E_2}\!\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right) + \frac{t}{\eta_1}\;}$$

### 3.2 Derivation of the Burgers Stress-Relaxation Equation (Constant Strain $\varepsilon_0$)

**Given:** A constant strain $\varepsilon_0$ is applied instantly at $t = 0$. The model is the same — Maxwell in series with Kelvin–Voigt — but now the total strain is fixed, while the internal stress decays.

**Step 1 — Strain constraint.**

$$\varepsilon_0 = \varepsilon_M(t) + \varepsilon_{KV}(t) \quad (\text{constant})$$

Differentiate with respect to time:

$$0 = \dot{\varepsilon}_M(t) + \dot{\varepsilon}_{KV}(t)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the Maxwell and Kelvin–Voigt constitutive laws.**

For the Maxwell arm: $\dot{\varepsilon}_M = \dot{\sigma}/E_1 + \sigma/\eta_1$.
For the Kelvin–Voigt arm (constant $\varepsilon_0$, varying $\sigma$): $\dot{\varepsilon}_{KV} = \dfrac{\dot{\sigma} - 0}{E_2} \cdot \text{via rearrangement}$ — we use the relation $\varepsilon_{KV} = \varepsilon_0 - \varepsilon_M$ and $\sigma = E_2(\varepsilon_0 - \varepsilon_M) + \eta_2 \dot{\varepsilon}_{KV}$.

A cleaner approach uses the differential operator form. With $\sigma$ common to both arms:

$$\dot{\varepsilon}_M = \frac{\dot{\sigma}}{E_1} + \frac{\sigma}{\eta_1}, \qquad \dot{\varepsilon}_{KV} = \frac{1}{E_2}\!\left(\dot{\sigma} - \frac{d}{dt}[E_2\varepsilon_{KV}]\right)$$

Replacing $\varepsilon_{KV} = \varepsilon_0 - \varepsilon_M$ and using $\sigma = E_2(\varepsilon_0 - \varepsilon_M) + \eta_2 \dot{\varepsilon}_{KV}$:

$$\sigma = E_2(\varepsilon_0 - \varepsilon_M) + \eta_2(-\dot{\varepsilon}_M)$$

Substituting $\dot{\varepsilon}_M$:

$$\sigma + \frac{\eta_1 + \eta_2}{E_1}\dot{\sigma} + \frac{\eta_1 \eta_2}{E_1 E_2}\ddot{\sigma} = \frac{\eta_1}{E_1}\dot{\sigma} + \frac{\eta_1 \eta_2}{E_1 E_2}\ddot{\sigma} + E_2 \varepsilon_0$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the master equation.**

$$\frac{\eta_1}{E_1}\dot{\sigma} + \frac{\eta_1 \eta_2}{E_1 E_2}\ddot{\sigma} + \sigma = E_2 \varepsilon_0$$

Dividing by $\eta_1 \eta_2 / (E_1 E_2)$:

$$\ddot{\sigma} + \left(\frac{1}{\lambda} + \frac{1}{\tau}\right)\dot{\sigma} + \frac{1}{\lambda \tau}\sigma = \frac{E_2}{\lambda \tau}\varepsilon_0$$

where $\lambda = \eta_1/E_1$ and $\tau = \eta_2/E_2$.

**Step 4 — Solve the second-order linear ODE.**
The steady-state (long-time) solution: $\sigma_\infty = 0$ because the Maxwell dashpot flows indefinitely at constant strain.
The transient solution has the form $\sigma(t) = A e^{-t/\lambda} + B e^{-t/\tau}$.

**Step 5 — Apply initial conditions.**
At $t = 0^+$ (instantaneous elastic response, dashpots frozen):

$$\sigma(0^+) = E_1 \varepsilon_0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; A + B = E_1 \varepsilon_0$$

At $t = 0^+$, only the spring $E_1$ in the Maxwell arm can deform suddenly. The Maxwell arm carries the full $\varepsilon_0$ initially (Kelvin dashpot blocks its branch), so $\sigma(0^+) = E_1 \varepsilon_0$.
For $\dot{\sigma}(0^+)$: differentiate the master equation and use $\ddot{\sigma}(0)=0$ initially:

$$\dot{\sigma}(0^+) = -\frac{E_1 \varepsilon_0}{\eta_1} \cdot \frac{r - 1}{r}, \quad \text{where } r = \frac{\lambda}{\tau}$$

**Step 6 — Solve for the constants $A$ and $B$.**

After algebra:

$$A = \frac{m\, E_1 \varepsilon_0 (r - 1)}{r - 1 + m}, \qquad B = \frac{(1 - m) E_1 \varepsilon_0}{1 - m + m/r}$$

with $m = E_1/(E_1 + E_2)$.

**Step 7 — Final stress-relaxation equation.**

$$\boxed{\;\sigma(t) = E_1 \varepsilon_0 \!\left[\, m\, e^{-t/\lambda} + (1 - m)\!\left(\frac{r\, e^{-t/\lambda} - e^{-t/\tau}}{r - 1}\right) \right]\;}$$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A polymer specimen is modeled by a Burgers chain with $E_1 = 5\,\text{MPa}$, $E_2 = 2\,\text{MPa}$, $\eta_1 = 1 \times 10^{12}\,\text{Pa·s}$, $\eta_2 = 4 \times 10^{10}\,\text{Pa·s}$. A constant stress $\sigma_0 = 1\,\text{MPa}$ is applied. Compute (a) the retardation time, (b) the creep strain at $t = 100\,\text{s}$, and (c) the steady-state creep rate.

**Solution:**

(a) Retardation time:

$$\tau = \frac{\eta_2}{E_2} = \frac{4 \times 10^{10}}{2 \times 10^{6}} = 2 \times 10^{4}\,\text{s}$$

(b) Substituting into the creep equation:

$$\varepsilon(100) = \frac{1 \times 10^{6}}{5 \times 10^{6}} + \frac{1 \times 10^{6}}{2 \times 10^{6}}\!\left(1 - e^{-100/2 \times 10^{4}}\right) + \frac{(1 \times 10^{6})(100)}{1 \times 10^{12}}$$

Since $100 / 2 \times 10^{4} = 0.005 \ll 1$, $e^{-0.005} \approx 0.9950$:

$$\varepsilon(100) = 0.200 + 0.500 \times 0.0050 + 0.0001 = 0.2026$$

$$\boxed{\;\varepsilon(100\,\text{s}) \approx 0.2026 \;(\text{or } 20.26\%)\;}$$

(c) Steady-state creep rate:

$$\dot{\varepsilon}_{\infty} = \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta_1} = \frac{1 \times 10^{6}}{1 \times 10^{12}} = 1 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{s}^{-1}$$

This means **the strain keeps increasing linearly** at the rate of one microstrain per second indefinitely.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mechanical Schematic of the Standard Burgers Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Applied Stress sigma_0] --> B[Maxwell Unit: Series]
    B --> C[Spring E1]
    C --> D[Dashpot eta1]
    D --> E[Kelvin-Voigt Unit: Parallel]
    E --> F[Spring E2]
    E --> G[Dashpot eta2]
    F --- E
    G --- E
    H[Total Strain epsilon t] --> I[Output: Three-Stage Deformation]
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture Flow — Burgers Creep Response

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    input[Constant Stress sigma_0 Applied at t=0] --> instantJump
    instantJump[Stage 1: Instantaneous Elastic Jump] --> A1[Strain = sigma_0 / E1]
    A1 --> delayed
    delayed[Stage 2: Delayed Kelvin-Voigt Response] --> A2[Strain grows as sigma_0 / E2 x 1 minus e to power t/tau]
    A2 --> flow
    flow[Stage 3: Irrecoverable Viscous Flow] --> A3[Strain grows linearly as sigma_0 x t over eta1]
    A3 --> output[Total epsilon t = Elastic + Delayed + Flow]
    style instantJump fill:#FFD580,stroke:#FF8C00
    style delayed fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4
    style flow fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#C71585
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Decomposition of the Constitutive Law

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sigma[sigma t] --> M1[Maxwell Branch: epsilon M]
    sigma --> KV1[Kelvin-Voigt Branch: epsilon KV]
    M1 --> sum[Series Sum: epsilon t]
    KV1 --> sum
    sum --> output[Output epsilon t]
    M1 -. constitutive .-> M2[depsilon M by dt = dsigma/dt over E1 + sigma over eta1]
    KV1 -. constitutive .-> KV2[sigma = E2 epsilon KV + eta2 d epsilon KV over dt]
    style sigma fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#BDB76B
    style output fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22
```

### 4.4 Creep Curve — Qualitative Time Response

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    t0[t equals 0] --> S1[Vertical Jump: sigma over E1]
    S1 --> S2[Curved Region: Kelvin-Voigt Recovery]
    S2 --> S3[Linear Tail: Viscous Flow at slope sigma over eta1]
    style t0 fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#8B0000
    style S1 fill:#FFD580,stroke:#FF8C00
    style S2 fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4
    style S3 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#C71585
```

> [!NOTE]
> The three regions in the diagram above correspond to the three additive terms in the Burgers creep equation: the vertical "jump" (instantaneous elasticity), the curved "knee" (delayed Kelvin–Voigt elasticity), and the linear "tail" (viscous flow of the Maxwell dashpot).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the Burgers model. Sketch its mechanical analog and identify each element.**

**Model Answer:**
The Burgers model is a four-element linear viscoelastic model used to describe the time-dependent mechanical behavior of polymers. It consists of:

- A **Maxwell element** (spring $E_1$ and dashpot $\eta_1$ in series) — provides instantaneous elasticity and irrecoverable viscous flow.
- A **Kelvin–Voigt element** (spring $E_2$ in parallel with dashpot $\eta_2$) — provides delayed, fully recoverable elasticity.

These two units are connected **in series**. The model is described by four independent parameters: $E_1, E_2, \eta_1, \eta_2$.

Sketch:

```
 σ ──[E1]──[η1]──┬──[E2]──┐
                 │         ├── ε
                 └──[η2]───┘
```

(2 marks: definition + 1 mark: sketch with labels)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Write the creep compliance expression for a Burgers model and explain the physical significance of each term.**

**Model Answer:**
The creep compliance of a Burgers model is:

$$J(t) = \frac{1}{E_1} + \frac{1}{E_2}\!\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right) + \frac{t}{\eta_1}, \quad \tau = \frac{\eta_2}{E_2}$$

Physical significance of each term:

1. $1/E_1$ — Instantaneous elastic compliance; represents the recoverable Hookean strain produced the moment the load is applied.
2. $(1/E_2)(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$ — Delayed (retarded) compliance from the Kelvin–Voigt unit; the strain grows exponentially with time constant $\tau$ and is **fully recoverable** upon unloading.
3. $t/\eta_1$ — Viscous flow compliance from the Maxwell dashpot; produces an irrecoverable strain that grows linearly with time.

(2 marks: equation + 1 mark: explanation)

---

### Part B Question (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students confuse the *Maxwell* and *Kelvin–Voigt* arrangements in the Burgers model and end up writing the wrong creep equation. **Always draw the schematic first, label the elements, then derive.** Marks are deducted heavily if the schematic is missing or unlabeled. Also, **forgetting the initial condition** $\varepsilon_{KV}(0) = 0$ in the derivation loses 2 marks.

#### Question A (14 Marks) — Creep Analysis

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**(a)** Derive the creep strain expression $\varepsilon(t)$ for a Burgers model subjected to a constant stress $\sigma_0$. Identify and physically interpret each term. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A polymer sample follows Burgers behavior with $E_1 = 4\,\text{MPa}$, $E_2 = 3\,\text{MPa}$, $\eta_1 = 5 \times 10^{12}\,\text{Pa·s}$, $\eta_2 = 6 \times 10^{10}\,\text{Pa·s}$. A constant stress $\sigma_0 = 0.5\,\text{MPa}$ is applied for $5 \times 10^{4}\,\text{s}$ and then removed. Calculate (i) the retardation time, (ii) the strain at the end of loading, and (iii) the permanent set (residual strain) immediately after unloading. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation:** See Section 3.1 (Step-by-Step Derivation). The final expression is:

$$\varepsilon(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{E_1} + \frac{\sigma_0}{E_2}\!\left(1 - e^{-t E_2/\eta_2}\right) + \frac{\sigma_0 t}{\eta_1}$$

**Physical interpretation:**

| Term | Symbol | Origin | Recoverable? |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1st term | $\sigma_0/E_1$ | Spring $E_1$ in Maxwell arm | Yes (instantaneous) |
| 2nd term | $(\sigma_0/E_2)(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$ | Kelvin–Voigt unit | Yes (delayed) |
| 3rd term | $\sigma_0 t/\eta_1$ | Dashpot $\eta_1$ in Maxwell arm | **No** (permanent) |

**(b) Numerical solution:**

(i) Retardation time:

$$\tau = \frac{\eta_2}{E_2} = \frac{6 \times 10^{10}}{3 \times 10^{6}} = 2 \times 10^{4}\,\text{s}$$

(ii) Strain at $t = 5 \times 10^{4}\,\text{s}$:

$$\varepsilon(5 \times 10^{4}) = \frac{0.5 \times 10^{6}}{4 \times 10^{6}} + \frac{0.5 \times 10^{6}}{3 \times 10^{6}}\!\left(1 - e^{-5 \times 10^{4}/2 \times 10^{4}}\right) + \frac{(0.5 \times 10^{6})(5 \times 10^{4})}{5 \times 10^{12}}$$

$$= 0.125 + 0.1667\,(1 - e^{-2.5}) + 5 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$= 0.125 + 0.1667 \times (1 - 0.0821) + 0.005$$

$$= 0.125 + 0.1530 + 0.005 = 0.2830$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon(5 \times 10^{4}\,\text{s}) = 0.283 \;(\text{or } 28.3\%)}$$

(iii) Permanent set immediately after unloading at $t_r = 5 \times 10^{4}\,\text{s}$:
On unloading, the elastic and delayed terms recover, but the viscous flow term is permanent:

$$\varepsilon_p = \frac{\sigma_0 t_r}{\eta_1} = \frac{(0.5 \times 10^{6})(5 \times 10^{4})}{5 \times 10^{12}} = 5 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_p = 5 \times 10^{-3} \;(\text{or } 0.5\%)}$$

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Stress Relaxation Analysis (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**(a)** Derive the stress-relaxation expression $\sigma(t)$ for a Burgers model subjected to a constant strain $\varepsilon_0$. State clearly the assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a Burgers model with $E_1 = 6\,\text{MPa}$, $E_2 = 4\,\text{MPa}$, $\eta_1 = 3 \times 10^{12}\,\text{Pa·s}$, $\eta_2 = 2 \times 10^{10}\,\text{Pa·s}$, compute the relaxation time $\lambda$, retardation time $\tau$, the parameter $m$, and the asymptotic long-time stress under a constant strain $\varepsilon_0 = 0.05$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation:** See Section 3.2 (Step-by-Step Derivation). The final expression is:

$$\sigma(t) = E_1 \varepsilon_0 \!\left[\, m\, e^{-t/\lambda} + (1 - m)\!\left(\frac{r\, e^{-t/\lambda} - e^{-t/\tau}}{r - 1}\right) \right]$$

with $m = E_1/(E_1+E_2)$ and $r = \lambda/\tau$.

**Assumptions:** linear viscoelasticity, time-invariant parameters, small strains, isothermal conditions.

**(b) Numerical solution:**

(i) Relaxation time:

$$\lambda = \frac{\eta_1}{E_1} = \frac{3 \times 10^{12}}{6 \times 10^{6}} = 5 \times 10^{5}\,\text{s}$$

(ii) Retardation time:

$$\tau = \frac{\eta_2}{E_2} = \frac{2 \times 10^{10}}{4 \times 10^{6}} = 5 \times 10^{3}\,\text{s}$$

(iii) Parameter $m$:

$$m = \frac{E_1}{E_1 + E_2} = \frac{6}{6 + 4} = 0.6$$

(iv) Asymptotic long-time stress ($t \to \infty$):

Because the Maxwell dashpot $\eta_1$ allows indefinite flow, the stress eventually **decays to zero**:

$$\boxed{\sigma(\infty) = 0}$$

(If the question asked for the initial stress: $\sigma(0^+) = E_1 \varepsilon_0 = 6 \times 10^{6} \times 0.05 = 3 \times 10^{5}\,\text{Pa} = 0.3\,\text{MPa}$.)

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Burgers Model**

- **Configuration:** Maxwell ($E_1$, $\eta_1$) **in series with** Kelvin–Voigt ($E_2 \parallel \eta_2$). Total elements = **4**; total parameters = **4**.
- **Creep strain** (constant $\sigma_0$): three additive terms → **elastic jump** + **delayed Kelvin recovery** + **linear viscous flow**.
- **Creep compliance:** $J(t) = \dfrac{1}{E_1} + \dfrac{1}{E_2}\!\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right) + \dfrac{t}{\eta_1}$.
- **Retardation time:** $\tau = \eta_2 / E_2$. **Relaxation time:** $\lambda = \eta_1 / E_1$.
- **Creep curve shape:** looks like a "J" rotated 90° (vertical jump → curved knee → straight tail).
- **Stress relaxation** (constant $\varepsilon_0$): $\sigma(t)$ decays from $E_1\varepsilon_0$ toward **0** (because of the free dashpot $\eta_1$).
- **Permanent set** on unloading: $\varepsilon_p = \sigma_0 t_r / \eta_1$ — caused by viscous flow in $\eta_1$.
- **Recoverable strains:** $\sigma_0/E_1$ (instant) + $\sigma_0/E_2$ (delayed) — total anelastic limit.
- **Series additivity:** strains add; stresses are common. **Parallel additivity:** stresses add; strains are common.
- **Limiting cases:** set $\eta_1 \to \infty$ → recovers **Kelvin–Voigt**; set $E_1 \to \infty$ → recovers **Maxwell**; the Burgers model **encloses** both.
- **Common exam trap:** confusing the symbol conventions (Maxwell arm = $E_1,\eta_1$; Kelvin arm = $E_2,\eta_2$). Always label the schematic first.
- **Why it matters in industry:** enables prediction of long-term creep, permanent deformation, and stress decay in rubber products, plastic pipes, gaskets, and biomedical polymers.
- **Time-temperature shift:** above $T_g$, the dashpots $\eta_1, \eta_2$ decrease rapidly; creep rate $\sigma_0/\eta_1$ increases — must be considered in design.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Deborah Number. Basis of Time-Temperature Superposition Principle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT402 - POLYMER PHYSICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Deborah Number. Basis of Time-Temperature Superposition Principle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Deborah Number (Dₑ) — Formal Definition

The **Deborah Number (Dₑ)** is a dimensionless quantity in polymer rheology introduced by Professor **Markus Reiner** in 1964. It is mathematically defined as the ratio of the characteristic **relaxation time (τ)** of a material to the **characteristic observation/processing/experimental time (T)**:

$$D_e = \frac{\tau}{T}$$

where:
- **τ (tau)** = the intrinsic relaxation time of the material (time required for molecular rearrangement).
- **T** = the time scale of the observer or the deformation process (observation time).

> [!NOTE]
> **Origin of the Name:** The name is taken from the Song of Deborah (Judges 5:5) in the Hebrew Bible: *"The mountains flowed before the Lord."* Prophetess Deborah's insight was that even mountains (which appear solid) flow — if observed on a geological time scale. Reiner humorously assigned a Deborah Number of **Dₑ ≈ 100** to the mountains, since for an observer whose time scale is geological, they behave like a fluid. Modern accepted value for geological flow of mountains is debated, but the poetic value of ~100 remains the iconic example.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are holding a **tar pitch droplet** (the famous University of Queensland "Pitch Drop Experiment", one of the world's longest running laboratory experiments, started in 1927).

- If you look at the pitch for **10 seconds** → it behaves like a **solid**. It is hard, brittle, and snaps if you hit it. → **Dₑ >> 1**
- If you observe the same pitch for **10 years** → it flows like a viscous **liquid**, dripping from a funnel. → **Dₑ << 1**

The **same material** can be "solid" or "liquid" depending on **how long you are willing to wait**. This is the central insight of viscoelasticity, and the Deborah Number quantifies exactly this duality.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Deborah Number is the fundamental bridge between **solid-like (elastic)** and **liquid-like (viscous)** behavior of polymers. A polymer behaves:
> - As a **solid** when Dₑ >> 1 (relaxation time ≫ observation time)
> - As a **liquid** when Dₑ << 1 (relaxation time ≪ observation time)
> - In a **viscoelastic transition** region when Dₑ ≈ 1

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Range | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Relaxation time | τ | 10⁻¹² – 10⁶ s | Time scale of molecular motion |
| Observation time | T | 10⁻³ – 10⁹ s | Time scale of experiment/application |
| Deborah Number | Dₑ | 10⁻⁶ – 10⁶ | Dimensionless solid/liquid index |

The Deborah Number is **truly dimensionless** (seconds/seconds), making it a universal measure across all materials — from water (Dₑ ≈ 10⁻¹²) to window glass (Dₑ ≈ 10⁰) to Earth's mantle (Dₑ ≈ 10²²).

---

### 1.4 Time-Temperature Superposition Principle (TTSP) — Formal Definition

The **Time-Temperature Superposition Principle (TTSP)** is the foundational experimental and theoretical statement in polymer viscoelasticity that:

> *The mechanical response of a polymer at one temperature can be related to its response at a different temperature simply by a horizontal shift along the time (or frequency) axis.*

Mathematically, the viscoelastic functions at a reference temperature T₀ are related to those at temperature T by a **shift factor aₜ**:

$$E(T, t) = E(T_0, t \cdot a_T)$$

$$E(T, \omega) = E(T_0, \omega \cdot a_T)$$

This is also called the **Method of Reduced Variables**, and the resulting superposed curve is called the **Master Curve**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Note:** TTSP was first articulated empirically by **Leaderman (1943)** and later formalized by **Williams, Landel, and Ferry (1955)** with the famous WLF equation. It is valid primarily in the **glass transition region** (T_g < T < T_g + 100°C). For very low or very high temperatures, the vertical shift factor bₜ (density correction) is also required.

---

### 1.5 Conceptual Analogy for TTSP

Think of a polymer as a **spring-dashpot network** (a Maxwell or Voigt model). Its response depends on:
- **How long** you pull it (time scale), AND
- **How hot** the polymer is (temperature).

TTSP says these two factors are **interchangeable knobs**. If you cannot perform an experiment spanning 10 decades of time, simply **change the temperature** — a 10°C increase can simulate a 10× change in time scale. You can therefore **build a master curve** that covers time scales impossible to access in any single experiment.

---

### 1.6 Why Are TTSP and Deborah Number Linked?

Both concepts revolve around the **competition between intrinsic time scales (τ) and extrinsic observation time scales (T)**:
- **Dₑ** quantifies *whether a material looks solid or liquid* for a given observer.
- **TTSP** exploits the fact that **τ is strongly temperature-dependent**, so by changing T (or T), we can effectively change the ratio τ/T — and therefore the apparent viscoelastic state.

This deep connection makes the study of the two topics inseparable in the KTU Module 3 syllabus.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Logic Behind the Deborah Number — Step-by-Step

The Deborah Number is built on three logical pillars:

**Step 1 — Every material has a characteristic relaxation time (τ).**
This is the time required for the molecular chains to rearrange, disentangle, or flow. For polymers, τ is governed by chain mobility, free volume, and temperature.

**Step 2 — Every observation has a characteristic time (T).**
Whether you strike the material (T ≈ 10⁻³ s), pull it in a tensile test (T ≈ 1 s), or watch it sag for years (T ≈ 10⁸ s), the observer imposes a "ruler" of time.

**Step 3 — Compare them.**
- If τ ≫ T → the molecules cannot rearrange during the experiment → **elastic / solid-like response** (Dₑ ≫ 1).
- If τ ≪ T → the molecules have plenty of time to flow → **viscous / liquid-like response** (Dₑ ≪ 1).
- If τ ≈ T → we are at the **viscoelastic transition**, where both elastic and viscous contributions are comparable (Dₑ ≈ 1).

### 2.2 The Logic Behind TTSP — Why Does It Work?

The principle rests on a single deep fact: **All molecular relaxation processes in polymers follow a thermally activated Arrhenius-type kinetics** near T_g. Therefore:

- Increasing T (temperature) speeds up molecular motion → equivalent to *decreasing the observation time*.
- Decreasing T slows down molecular motion → equivalent to *increasing the observation time*.

Since the shape of the relaxation spectrum is **thermo-rheologically simple** (a property of the polymer that does not change with T), only a horizontal shift along the log-time axis is required to superpose curves at different temperatures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **"Thermo-rheologically simple" polymers:** Polymers that obey TTSP exactly. Most amorphous, single-phase polymers in the glass-transition region are thermo-rheologically simple. **Semi-crystalline polymers, blends, and filled composites are generally NOT** — they show breakdown of TTSP.

### 2.3 The WLF Equation — Mathematical Heart of TTSP

For temperatures near T_g (specifically T_g < T < T_g + 100°C), the shift factor aₜ follows the **Williams–Landel–Ferry (WLF) equation**:

$$\log_{10}(a_T) = -\frac{C_1 (T - T_g)}{C_2 + (T - T_g)}$$

where:
- **C₁** and **C₂** are the WLF constants (universal for many polymers, sometimes called "universal constants").
- **T_g** is the glass transition temperature.
- The reference temperature is T_g (or sometimes a chosen T₀).

> [!NOTE]
> **Universal WLF Constants** (when T_g is the reference):
> - C₁ ≈ **17.44**
> - C₂ ≈ **51.6 K**
>
> These were shown by Williams, Landel, and Ferry to be remarkably consistent across a wide range of polymers — a striking universality in polymer physics.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Concept | Mathematical Form | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Deborah Number** | $D_e = \tau / T$ | Dimensionless |
| 2 | **Solid-like limit** | $D_e \gg 1$ | $\tau \gg T$ |
| 3 | **Liquid-like limit** | $D_e \ll 1$ | $\tau \ll T$ |
| 4 | **TTSP — Time domain** | $E(T, t) = E(T_0, t \cdot a_T)$ | Master curve construction |
| 5 | **TTSP — Frequency domain** | $E(T, \omega) = E(T_0, \omega \cdot a_T)$ | Dynamic mechanical analysis |
| 6 | **WLF equation** | $\log_{10}(a_T) = -C_1(T-T_g)/[C_2+(T-T_g)]$ | Valid for T_g < T < T_g+100°C |
| 7 | **Universal WLF constants** | $C_1 = 17.44$, $C_2 = 51.6$ K | When T_g is reference |
| 8 | **Arrhenius form (below T_g)** | $\log_{10}(a_T) = E_a/(2.303 R) \cdot (1/T - 1/T_0)$ | Sub-T_g / high-T behavior |
| 9 | **Vertical shift factor** | $b_T = \rho_0 T_0 / (\rho T)$ | Density/temperature correction |
| 10 | **Apparent activation energy from WLF** | $E_a = 2.303 \cdot R \cdot C_1 C_2 T^2 / (C_2 + T - T_g)^2$ | In J/mol |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall — Do NOT confuse the "T" in Deborah Number with temperature.** In Dₑ = τ/T, the denominator T is **observation time (seconds)**, not absolute temperature (Kelvin). Examiners often test this distinction.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Field | Application of Dₑ & TTSP |
|---|---|
| **Polymer Processing (Injection Molding)** | Dₑ determines whether the melt fills the mold (liquid-like) or freezes prematurely. TTSP helps predict mold-filling time at processing T. |
| **Tire Design (Rubber Industry)** | Operating temperatures vary from –40°C to +80°C. TTSP master curves cover 10–15 decades of frequency, predicting wet grip, rolling resistance, wear. |
| **Polymer Recycling** | Reprocessing temperatures are chosen based on WLF shift factors to avoid thermal degradation. |
| **Adhesives & Sealants** | Dₑ indicates whether a hot-melt adhesive will bond instantly (Dₑ small) or remain tacky (Dₑ large). |
| **Geophysics / Geology** | Rocks under geological time scales (Dₑ huge) flow like viscous fluids — earthquakes, mantle convection. |
| **Biomedical Polymers** | Dₑ determines blood-compatibility of vascular grafts; TTSP predicts long-term creep of implants. |
| **3D Printing (FDM)** | Layer bonding depends on whether deposited polymer has time to interdiffuse (WLF-controlled) before cooling. |

### 2.6 The Physical Origin of the WLF Equation — Doolittle Free-Volume Theory

Doolittle (1951) showed that the viscosity η of a polymer is related to its **free volume fraction f** by:

$$\eta = A \exp\left(\frac{B}{f}\right)$$

where A and B are constants (B ≈ 1). Free volume increases linearly with T above T_g:

$$f = f_g + \alpha_f (T - T_g)$$

where **f_g ≈ 0.025** (universal free volume at T_g) and **α_f** is the thermal expansion coefficient of free volume.

Substituting and taking the ratio of viscosities at T and T_g gives the WLF equation with:

$$C_1 = \frac{B}{2.303 \cdot f_g} \approx 17.44$$

$$C_2 = \frac{f_g}{\alpha_f} \approx 51.6 \text{ K}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Conceptual Question Trigger:** *"Why are the WLF constants called 'universal'?"* — Answer: Because they derive from **universal free-volume parameters** at T_g (f_g ≈ 0.025) and the assumption that B ≈ 1, which holds for most amorphous polymers.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Doolittle Equation to WLF Equation

**Starting Point (Doolittle Equation, 1951):**
The viscosity of a polymer melt depends on its free volume fraction **f**:

$$\eta = A \exp\left(\frac{B}{f}\right)$$

**Step 1 — Define the shift factor aₜ.**
By definition, the shift factor relates the relaxation time (or viscosity) at temperature T to that at reference T_g:

$$a_T = \frac{\tau(T)}{\tau(T_g)} = \frac{\eta(T)}{\eta(T_g)}$$

(The assumption η ∝ τ holds for polymers in the terminal/flow region.)

**Step 2 — Take the ratio using the Doolittle equation:**

$$a_T = \frac{A \exp(B/f_T)}{A \exp(B/f_g)} = \exp\left[B \left(\frac{1}{f_T} - \frac{1}{f_g}\right)\right]$$

**Step 3 — Take the base-10 logarithm:**

$$\log_{10}(a_T) = \frac{B}{2.303} \left(\frac{1}{f_T} - \frac{1}{f_g}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Express free volume at T.**
Free volume grows linearly above T_g with thermal expansion coefficient α_f:

$$f_T = f_g + \alpha_f (T - T_g)$$

**Step 5 — Substitute f_T:**

$$\log_{10}(a_T) = \frac{B}{2.303} \left[\frac{1}{f_g + \alpha_f(T - T_g)} - \frac{1}{f_g}\right]$$

**Step 6 — Simplify the bracket:**

$$\frac{1}{f_g + \alpha_f(T - T_g)} - \frac{1}{f_g} = \frac{f_g - [f_g + \alpha_f(T - T_g)]}{f_g[f_g + \alpha_f(T - T_g)]} = \frac{-\alpha_f(T - T_g)}{f_g[f_g + \alpha_f(T - T_g)]}$$

**Step 7 — Factor α_f(T - T_g) and divide numerator & denominator by f_g:**

$$\log_{10}(a_T) = \frac{B}{2.303} \cdot \frac{-(T - T_g)}{f_g} \cdot \frac{1}{[f_g + \alpha_f(T - T_g)]} \cdot f_g$$

Reorganizing:

$$\log_{10}(a_T) = -\frac{(B/2.303)(T - T_g)}{f_g + \alpha_f(T - T_g)}$$

**Step 8 — Define WLF constants:**

$$C_1 \equiv \frac{B}{2.303 \cdot f_g}, \qquad C_2 \equiv \frac{f_g}{\alpha_f}$$

**Step 9 — Final WLF equation:**

$$\boxed{\log_{10}(a_T) = -\frac{C_1 (T - T_g)}{C_2 + (T - T_g)}}$$

**Step 10 — Verify universal constants:**

$$C_1 = \frac{1}{2.303 \times 0.025} \approx 17.44 \quad \checkmark$$

$$C_2 = \frac{0.025}{4.85 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 51.6 \text{ K} \quad \checkmark$$

using B ≈ 1, f_g ≈ 0.025, α_f ≈ 4.85 × 10⁻⁴ K⁻¹.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Apparent Activation Energy from WLF

The **apparent activation energy** of the relaxation process is defined as:

$$E_a = 2.303 \cdot R \cdot \frac{d \log_{10}(a_T)}{d(1/T)}$$

**Step 1 — Differentiate the WLF equation with respect to (1/T).**

Let $x \equiv T - T_g$. Then:

$$\log_{10}(a_T) = -\frac{C_1 x}{C_2 + x}$$

We can rewrite this as:

$$\log_{10}(a_T) = -C_1 + \frac{C_1 C_2}{C_2 + x} = -C_1 + \frac{C_1 C_2}{C_2 + T - T_g}$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate with respect to T:**

$$\frac{d \log_{10}(a_T)}{dT} = -\frac{C_1 C_2}{(C_2 + T - T_g)^2}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to d/d(1/T) using the chain rule:**

$$\frac{d \log_{10}(a_T)}{d(1/T)} = \frac{d \log_{10}(a_T)}{dT} \cdot \frac{dT}{d(1/T)} = -\frac{C_1 C_2}{(C_2 + T - T_g)^2} \cdot (-T^2)$$

$$\frac{d \log_{10}(a_T)}{d(1/T)} = \frac{C_1 C_2 T^2}{(C_2 + T - T_g)^2}$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by 2.303·R to get activation energy:**

$$\boxed{E_a = 2.303 \cdot R \cdot \frac{C_1 C_2 T^2}{(C_2 + T - T_g)^2}}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical check at T = T_g + 50°C ≈ 373 K (using C₁ = 17.44, C₂ = 51.6):**

$$E_a = 2.303 \times 8.314 \times \frac{17.44 \times 51.6 \times (373)^2}{(51.6 + 50)^2}$$

Computing numerator: $17.44 \times 51.6 \times 139129 \approx 1.252 \times 10^8$

Computing denominator: $(101.6)^2 = 10322.6$

Ratio: $\approx 12129$

$E_a \approx 2.303 \times 8.314 \times 12129 \approx 2.32 \times 10^5$ J/mol = **232 kJ/mol**

> [!NOTE]
> This is a typical glass-transition activation energy — very large compared to sub-T_g processes (e.g., 50 kJ/mol). This huge E_a near T_g is the molecular origin of the **strong T-dependence** of polymer rheology and the reason TTSP works so well near T_g.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 1 — Calculating the Shift Factor aₜ

**Problem:** A polymer has T_g = 100°C. Using the universal WLF constants, calculate the shift factor aₜ at T = 130°C relative to T_g.

**Solution:**

Given: $T - T_g = 30$ K, $C_1 = 17.44$, $C_2 = 51.6$ K.

$$\log_{10}(a_T) = -\frac{17.44 \times 30}{51.6 + 30} = -\frac{523.2}{81.6} = -6.412$$

$$a_T = 10^{-6.412} \approx 3.87 \times 10^{-7}$$

**Interpretation:** The polymer is **~2.6 million times faster** at 130°C than at T_g. To superpose the 130°C curve onto the master curve at T_g, we must shift it **6.412 decades to the LEFT** along the log-time axis.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Aid:** Higher T → shorter relaxation time → smaller aₜ → shift curve to the LEFT (faster). Lower T → longer relaxation time → larger aₜ → shift curve to the RIGHT (slower).

---

### 3.4 Worked Example 2 — Estimating Deborah Number in a Process

**Problem:** A polymer melt has a relaxation time τ = 50 s at 200°C. It is being injected into a mold with a typical filling time of 2 s. Calculate the Deborah Number and comment on the flow behavior.

**Solution:**

$$D_e = \frac{\tau}{T} = \frac{50 \text{ s}}{2 \text{ s}} = 25$$

Since $D_e = 25 \gg 1$, the polymer behaves predominantly as a **viscoelastic solid** during mold filling. This means:
- The melt will exhibit **die swell** and **memory effects**.
- It may **freeze prematurely** near cold mold walls.
- Process engineers must reduce T (lower τ) or increase filling pressure (decrease T) to push Dₑ toward 1.

> [!WARNING]
> **Industrial Design Rule of Thumb:** For smooth injection molding, target $D_e \approx 0.1$ to 1 — i.e., the polymer must flow faster than it relaxes.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Curve Construction via TTSP — Concept Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Viscoelastic Data at T1]:::raw
    B[Raw Viscoelastic Data at T2]:::raw
    C[Raw Viscoelastic Data at T3]:::raw
    D[Reference Temperature T0 selected]:::ref
    E[Compute Shift Factors log aT1, log aT2, log aT3]:::calc
    F[Apply WLF Equation to Predict aT]:::calc
    G[Horizontally Shift Each Curve by log aT]:::shift
    H[Superpose All Curves]:::sup
    I[Master Curve at T0]:::master
    J[Covers 10 to 15 Decades of Time]:::master

    A --> D
    B --> D
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
    F --> G
    A --> G
    B --> G
    C --> G
    G --> H
    H --> I
    I --> J

    classDef raw fill:#fde0c5,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    classDef ref fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef calc fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000
    classDef shift fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000
    classDef sup fill:#e2d4f0,stroke:#4a148c,color:#000
    classDef master fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#b30000,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Deborah Number Behavior Map — Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Material with relaxation time tau]:::start
    Choose{Choose observation time T}:::decide
    Tiny[T very small - impact, sonication]:::tiny
    Medium[T medium - tensile test, molding]:::med
    Huge[T very large - geological, creep]:::huge

    Compare1{Compare tau vs T}:::decide
    Case1[tau MUCH GREATER than T]:::case1
    Case2[tau APPROXIMATELY EQUAL to T]:::case2
    Case3[tau MUCH LESS than T]:::case3

    Res1[De MUCH GREATER than 1]:::res1
    Res2[De APPROX 1]:::res2
    Res3[De MUCH LESS than 1]:::res3

    Behavior1[GLASSY or CRYSTALLINE SOLID-like]:::b1
    Behavior2[VISCOELASTIC TRANSITION]:::b2
    Behavior3[VISCOUS LIQUID-like]:::b3

    Example1[Polystyrene at room temp struck by hammer]:::ex1
    Example2[Polymer melt in extruder]:::ex2
    Example3[Honey flowing slowly off a spoon]:::ex3

    Start --> Choose
    Choose --> Tiny
    Choose --> Medium
    Choose --> Huge
    Tiny --> Compare1
    Medium --> Compare1
    Huge --> Compare1
    Compare1 --> Case1
    Compare1 --> Case2
    Compare1 --> Case3
    Case1 --> Res1 --> Behavior1 --> Example1
    Case2 --> Res2 --> Behavior2 --> Example2
    Case3 --> Res3 --> Behavior3 --> Example3

    classDef decide fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#01579b,color:#000
    classDef tiny fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000
    classDef med fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    classDef huge fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#311b92,color:#000
    classDef case1 fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#000
    classDef case2 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
    classDef case3 fill:#b2dfdb,stroke:#004d40,color:#000
    classDef res1 fill:#ff8a65,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000
```
