<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**

Define the Deborah Number. What is its significance in distinguishing solid-like and liquid-like behavior of polymers?

**Model Answer:**

The **Deborah Number (Dₑ)** is a dimensionless ratio defined as:

$$D_e = \frac{\tau}{T}$$

where **τ** is the **characteristic relaxation time** of the material (a measure of how quickly its molecular structure responds to stress) and **T** is the **characteristic observation or process time** (the time scale of the experiment or deformation imposed on the material).

**Significance:**
- When $D_e \gg 1$: τ ≫ T → molecular rearrangement cannot occur during observation → **solid-like (elastic)** response.
- When $D_e \ll 1$: τ ≪ T → molecules have ample time to flow → **liquid-like (viscous)** response.
- When $D_e \approx 1$: **viscoelastic transition** — both elastic and viscous responses are comparable.

The Deborah Number thus unifies solid and liquid behavior into a single continuous spectrum and quantifies the "fluidity" of a material on the time scale of the observer. It highlights that the distinction between solid and liquid is not absolute but **observer-dependent** (e.g., mountains flow on geological time scales — a poetic Dₑ ≈ 100).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**

State the Time-Temperature Superposition Principle (TTSP). Write the WLF equation and name the universal constants.

**Model Answer:**

**Statement of TTSP:** *The viscoelastic response of a thermo-rheologically simple polymer at one temperature can be superposed onto that at another temperature by a horizontal shift along the logarithmic time (or frequency) axis.* The principle allows construction of a single **master curve** covering many decades of time at a reference temperature.

**Mathematically:**

$$E(T, t) = E(T_0, t \cdot a_T)$$

where $a_T$ is the **horizontal shift factor**.

**WLF Equation (Williams–Landel–Ferry, 1955):**

$$\log_{10}(a_T) = -\frac{C_1 (T - T_g)}{C_2 + (T - T_g)}$$

**Universal WLF Constants** (with T_g as reference):
- $C_1 \approx 17.44$
- $C_2 \approx 51.6$ K

The equation is valid in the range $T_g < T < T_g + 100$°C. Outside this range (especially below T_g), an **Arrhenius equation** is more appropriate. The WLF equation derives from **Doolittle's free-volume theory** of polymer viscosity.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]*

**(a)** Derive the WLF equation from the Doolittle free-volume theory, defining each term clearly. Show that the universal WLF constants are $C_1 \approx 17.44$ and $C_2 \approx 51.6$ K. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA) sample has $T_g = 105$°C. Using the universal WLF constants, calculate:
- (i) the shift factor $a_T$ at T = 130°C, and
- (ii) the apparent activation energy of the relaxation process at 130°C.
- (R = 8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹) **(7 Marks)**

---

**MODEL SOLUTION (a):**

**Step 1 — Doolittle Equation (1951):**
Polymer viscosity η depends on the free volume fraction f:

$$\eta = A \exp\left(\frac{B}{f}\right)$$

where A and B are constants, with $B \approx 1$ for most polymers.

**Step 2 — Definition of shift factor:**

$$a_T = \frac{\tau(T)}{\tau(T_g)} = \frac{\eta(T)}{\eta(T_g)} = \exp\left[B \left(\frac{1}{f_T} - \frac{1}{f_g}\right)\right]$$

**Step 3 — Free volume at temperature T (linear thermal expansion above T_g):**

$$f_T = f_g + \alpha_f (T - T_g)$$

where $f_g \approx 0.025$ is the free volume fraction at $T_g$ and $\alpha_f$ is the thermal expansion coefficient of free volume.

**Step 4 — Take log₁₀ and substitute:**

$$\log_{10}(a_T) = \frac{B}{2.303}\left[\frac{1}{f_g + \alpha_f(T-T_g)} - \frac{1}{f_g}\right]$$

**Step 5 — Algebraic simplification:**

$$\log_{10}(a_T) = -\frac{B}{2.303} \cdot \frac{(T - T_g)}{[f_g + \alpha_f(T-T_g)] f_g} \cdot \alpha_f \cdot f_g$$

After algebraic manipulation, this reduces to:

$$\log_{10}(a_T) = -\frac{(B/2.303)(T - T_g)}{f_g + \alpha_f(T - T_g)}$$

**Step 6 — Identify WLF constants:**

$$C_1 = \frac{B}{2.303 \cdot f_g} = \frac{1}{2.303 \times 0.025} \approx 17.44$$

$$C_2 = \frac{f_g}{\alpha_f} = \frac{0.025}{4.85 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 51.6 \text{ K}$$

**Step 7 — Final WLF form:**

$$\boxed{\log_{10}(a_T) = -\frac{C_1 (T - T_g)}{C_2 + (T - T_g)}}$$

**Conclusion:** The "universality" of C₁ and C₂ arises from the universal free-volume parameters at T_g, which are remarkably similar across many amorphous polymers.

---

**MODEL SOLUTION (b):**

**Given:** $T_g = 105$°C, T = 130°C, $C_1 = 17.44$, $C_2 = 51.6$ K, R = 8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹.

**Part (i) — Shift factor:**

$\Delta T = T - T_g = 130 - 105 = 25$ K

$$\log_{10}(a_T) = -\frac{17.44 \times 25}{51.6 + 25} = -\frac{436}{76.6} = -5.692$$

$$a_T = 10^{-5.692} \approx 2.03 \times 10^{-6}$$

**Interpretation:** The polymer relaxes ~5 × 10⁵ times faster at 130°C than at T_g.

**Part (ii) — Apparent activation energy:**

Using T = 130 + 273 = 403 K:

$$E_a = 2.303 \times R \times \frac{C_1 C_2 T^2}{(C_2 + T - T_g)^2}$$

**Numerator (excluding constants):**
$C_1 C_2 T^2 = 17.44 \times 51.6 \times (403)^2$
$= 17.44 \times 51.6 \times 162409$
$= 1.461 \times 10^8$

**Denominator:**
$(C_2 + T - T_g)^2 = (51.6 + 25)^2 = (76.6)^2 = 5867.56$

**Ratio:**
$\frac{1.461 \times 10^8}{5867.56} = 24907$

**Final calculation:**
$E_a = 2.303 \times 8.314 \times 24907 = 4.77 \times 10^5$ J/mol ≈ **477 kJ/mol**

**Comment:** This very high activation energy near T_g is characteristic of cooperative segmental motion in the glass-transition region, far exceeding sub-T_g secondary relaxation processes (typically 30–80 kJ/mol).

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]*

**(a)** Explain the concept of the Deborah Number with a real-world analogy. Discuss how the Deborah Number categorizes material behavior, citing at least three examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A polymer melt at 220°C has a relaxation time $\tau = 100$ s. It is subjected to two different processes:
- (i) High-speed injection molding with filling time T₁ = 0.5 s.
- (ii) Slow extrusion with residence time T₂ = 200 s.

Calculate the Deborah Number for each process. Comment on the rheological behavior and its engineering implications. **(7 Marks)**

---

**MODEL SOLUTION (a):**

**Definition:** The Deborah Number is defined as:

$$D_e = \frac{\tau}{T}$$

where τ is the material's intrinsic relaxation time and T is the observation/processing time. It is **dimensionless** and was introduced by **Markus Reiner (1964)**, named after the biblical prophetess Deborah whose song states *"the mountains flowed before the Lord"* — emphasizing that all materials flow if observed on a sufficiently long time scale.

**Real-world Analogy — The Pitch Drop Experiment:**
The University of Queensland's Pitch Drop Experiment (started 1927) is a famous example. A piece of pitch (a viscoelastic polymer) in a funnel:
- Behaves as a **hard solid** when struck with a hammer (T ≈ 0.01 s, τ ≈ 10⁸ s, Dₑ ≈ 10¹⁰).
- Behaves as a **flowing liquid** over years of observation (T ≈ 10⁸ s, τ ≈ 10⁸ s, Dₑ ≈ 1, and on longer time scales, drips every ~10 years).

**Three Categorical Examples:**

| # | Example | τ (s) | T (s) | Dₑ | Behavior |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Polystyrene at 25°C struck by hammer | 10⁶ | 10⁻³ | 10⁹ | **Glassy solid** (shatters) |
| 2 | Polyethylene melt in extruder | 10⁻² | 1 | 10⁻² | **Viscous liquid** (flows smoothly) |
| 3 | Earth's mantle in geological convection | 10¹¹ | 10¹⁵ | 10⁻⁴ | **Viscous fluid** (mountains "flow") |

**Key Insights:**
- Dₑ > 1 → **solid-like** response.
- Dₑ < 1 → **liquid-like** response.
- Dₑ ≈ 1 → **viscoelastic** — the regime of polymers.

The Deborah Number thus resolves the classical debate "is glass a solid or a very slow liquid?" — glass is a solid on human time scales (Dₑ huge) but a liquid on geological time scales (Dₑ < 1).

---

**MODEL SOLUTION (b):**

**Given:** τ = 100 s, T₁ = 0.5 s, T₂ = 200 s.

**Part (i) — Injection molding:**

$$D_e^{(1)} = \frac{\tau}{T_1} = \frac{100}{0.5} = 200$$

Since $D_e^{(1)} = 200 \gg 1$, the polymer melt is **solid-like (highly elastic)** during high-speed injection. The chains cannot relax fast enough. Engineering implications:
- Severe **die swell** at the mold entry.
- **Frozen-in orientation** and residual stresses in the part.
- Risk of **weld-line weakness** and **warping**.
- High **pressure requirements** to overcome elastic resistance.
- **Surface defects** like jetting.

**Part (ii) — Slow extrusion:**

$$D_e^{(2)} = \frac{\tau}{T_2} = \frac{100}{200} = 0.5$$

Since $D_e^{(2)} = 0.5 < 1$, the polymer melt behaves in the **viscoelastic-to-viscous** regime. The chains have enough time to relax and disentangle. Engineering implications:
- Smooth, **laminar flow** with minimal die swell.
- Good **molecular orientation relaxation** → isotropic properties.
- Lower **extrudate swell** and better dimensional stability.
- Suitable for **profile extrusion** and **sheet forming**.

**Comparative Insight:**

| Process | Dₑ | Regime | Practical Consequence |
|---|---|---|---|
| Injection molding | 200 | Elastic-dominated | High pressure, residual stress |
| Slow extrusion | 0.5 | Viscous-dominated | Smooth flow, low defects |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. **Sign error in WLF log:** Many students forget the **negative sign** in $\log_{10}(a_T) = -C_1(T-T_g)/[C_2+(T-T_g)]$. Always check: aₜ < 1 above T_g, so log(aₜ) must be **negative**.
> 2. **Confusing observation time T with temperature:** In Dₑ = τ/T, T is in **seconds**, NOT Kelvin.
> 3. **Using wrong WLF constants:** Universal C₁ = 17.44, C₂ = 51.6 only when **T_g is the reference temperature**. If the reference is T₀ ≠ T_g, the constants change to:
> $C_1^* = C_1 C_2 / (C_2 + T_0 - T_g)$ and $C_2^* = C_2 + T_0 - T_g$.
> 4. **Forgetting vertical shift factor bₜ for density/temperature correction** in high-precision master curve work.
> 5. **Applying TTSP to semi-crystalline polymers or composites** — it fails for these because they are NOT thermo-rheologically simple.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3**

✅ **Deborah Number:**
- $D_e = \tau / T$ — dimensionless, ratio of relaxation time to observation time.
- $D_e \gg 1$ → solid-like.
- $D_e \ll 1$ → liquid-like.
- $D_e \approx 1$ → viscoelastic transition.
- Pitch Drop Experiment and biblical mountains are canonical examples.
- Reiner (1964) coined the term.

✅ **Time-Temperature Superposition Principle (TTSP):**
- "Change in time scale = change in temperature scale."
- Master curve construction by horizontal shifting: $E(T, t) = E(T_0, t \cdot a_T)$.
- Valid for thermo-rheologically simple amorphous polymers.
- Breaks down for semi-crystalline polymers, blends, and composites.
- Horizontal shift factor = aₜ; vertical shift factor = bₜ (for density correction).

✅ **WLF Equation:**
- $\log_{10}(a_T) = -C_1(T - T_g)/[C_2 + (T - T_g)]$.
- Universal constants: $C_1 = 17.44$, $C_2 = 51.6$ K (T_g as reference).
- Valid range: $T_g$ to $T_g + 100$°C.
- Below T_g → use **Arrhenius** form: $\log_{10}(a_T) = (E_a/2.303R)(1/T - 1/T_0)$.
- Derived from **Doolittle free-volume theory**.
- Universal constants arise from $f_g \approx 0.025$ and B ≈ 1.

✅ **Apparent Activation Energy from WLF:**
- $E_a = 2.303 \cdot R \cdot C_1 C_2 T^2 / (C_2 + T - T_g)^2$.
- At T = T_g + 50 K → Eₐ ≈ 200–500 kJ/mol (cooperative glass transition).
- At T = T_g + 5 K → Eₐ very large (low mobility, near-frozen state).

✅ **Key Connections:**
- Both Dₑ and TTSP revolve around the **competition between intrinsic τ and extrinsic T**.
- TTSP works **because τ is exponentially T-dependent** near T_g.
- Lowering T = shifting the curve to the **RIGHT** (slower).
- Raising T = shifting the curve to the **LEFT** (faster).
- Polymers are **viscoelastic by nature**; Dₑ and TTSP are the two essential tools to characterize and exploit this.

✅ **Industrial Relevance to Remember:**
- Injection molding → require low Dₑ (< 1).
- Rubber tire design → master curves span 10–15 decades of frequency.
- 3D printing → WLF predicts inter-layer welding and crystallization kinetics.
- Adhesives → Dₑ indicates "open time" before setting.

✅ **Common Mistake in Examinations:**
- Mixing the **two T's** in Dₑ = τ/T (time) and WLF (temperature). Always check the units!
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: WLF Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT402 - POLYMER PHYSICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** WLF Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Williams–Landel–Ferry (WLF) Equation** is an empirical constitutive relation that quantitatively describes the temperature dependence of the **shift factor ($a_T$)** — and thereby the mechanical relaxation times, viscosity, and viscoelastic moduli — of amorphous, non-crystallizing polymers in the **supercooled liquid region**, i.e., between the glass transition temperature ($T_g$) and approximately $T_g + 100^{\circ}\text{C}$.

The canonical form of the equation, with $T_g$ as the reference temperature, is:

$$\log_{10} a_T = -\frac{C_1 \, (T - T_g)}{C_2 + (T - T_g)}$$

where:
- $a_T$ is the **time-scale shift factor** (dimensionless) required to horizontally superimpose viscoelastic response curves measured at temperature $T$ onto the master curve built at the reference temperature $T_g$.
- $C_1$ and $C_2$ are **empirically determined constants**.
- The "universal" values reported by Williams, Landel, and Ferry (1955) are $C_1 = 17.44$ and $C_2 = 51.6\ \text{K}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — PCPOT402, Module 3:**
> The WLF equation is one of the *core assessment topics* of Module 3. Students are expected to (i) derive it from the **Doolittle equation**, (ii) interpret it via **free-volume theory**, and (iii) apply it in conjunction with the **Time–Temperature Superposition Principle (TTSP)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are watching a thick honey being poured. At room temperature it flows slowly; when warmed on a stove it flows dramatically faster. The "internal clock" of the honey (i.e., how long its molecular chains take to rearrange) is changing with temperature. 

> **Plain-English Analogy:** Think of $a_T$ as the *time-warp dial* on a movie projector. If you recorded polymer creep at $90\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$ and wanted to compare it directly with data taken at $80\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$, the WLF equation tells you exactly by what factor you must stretch or compress the time axis so that both curves overlap perfectly into one **master curve**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for the universal constants:** *“17.44 — 51.6 — these are the WLF keys to the sky.”* Commit $C_1 = 17.44$ and $C_2 = 51.6\ \text{K}$ to memory; KTU examiners regularly award (or withhold) marks for their recall.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Metrics

| Symbol | Physical Meaning | Standard / Universal Value |
|---|---|---|
| $C_1$ | First WLF constant (dimensionless) | **17.44** |
| $C_2$ | Second WLF constant (temperature) | **51.6 K** |
| $f_g$ | Fractional free volume at $T_g$ | **0.025** |
| $\alpha_f$ | Thermal expansion coeff. of free volume | **$4.8 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{K}^{-1}$** |
| Valid range | Above $T_g$ | $T_g \leq T \leq T_g + 100\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Conceptual Pillars Behind WLF

The WLF equation is not a stand-alone empirical curve fit. It rests on **three theoretical pillars**, and KTU expects each to be discussed.

### Pillar 1 — Free Volume Theory (Fox & Flory)
- Total specific volume of an amorphous polymer $v = v_0 + v_f$, where $v_0$ is the *occupied volume* and $v_f$ is the *free volume*.
- The **fractional free volume** is defined as $f = v_f / v$.
- Above $T_g$, free volume increases linearly with temperature:

$$f = f_g + \alpha_f \, (T - T_g)$$

- Below $T_g$, the polymer is a glass; free volume is essentially frozen at $f_g$.

### Pillar 2 — Doolittle Equation
Doolittle (1951) related viscosity $\eta$ to free volume for simple liquids:

$$\eta = A \, \exp\!\left(\frac{B}{f}\right)$$

or equivalently $\ln \eta = \ln A + B/f$, where $A$ and $B$ are material constants.

### Pillar 3 — Time–Temperature Superposition Principle (TTSP)
- The shape of a viscoelastic response (creep, stress relaxation, $\tan\delta$) is *temperature-invariant*; only the time scale changes.
- Therefore the **shift factor** is:

$$a_T = \frac{\eta(T)}{\eta(T_g)} = \frac{\tau(T)}{\tau(T_g)} = \frac{E(T)}{E(T_g)} \bigg\vert_{\text{at fixed } \omega \text{ or } t \cdot a_T}$$

## 2.2 The Universal WLF Constants — Where do they come from?

Substituting the Doolittle equation into the ratio $\eta(T)/\eta(T_g)$ and applying the linear free-volume expansion yields:

$$\ln a_T = B\!\left(\frac{1}{f} - \frac{1}{f_g}\right) = \frac{B}{f_g} \cdot \frac{(T - T_g)}{(f_g / \alpha_f) + (T - T_g)}$$

Converting $\ln$ to $\log_{10}$ and identifying:

$$C_1 = \frac{B}{2.303 \, f_g}, \qquad C_2 = \frac{f_g}{\alpha_f}$$

For a wide range of polymers, Ferry showed $f_g \approx 0.025$ and $\alpha_f \approx 4.8 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{K}^{-1}$, giving the *universal* constants $C_1 = 17.44$ and $C_2 = 51.6\ \text{K}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why “Universal”?** Despite chemically very different polymers (PS, PMMA, PVAc, natural rubber…), their $f_g$ and $\alpha_f$ cluster around the same values. Hence $C_1$ and $C_2$ are nearly *transferable* across the amorphous-polymer family — a remarkable result that justified WLF as a near-fundamental law rather than a curve fit.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> Use `\vert` (not `|`) inside every row below — KTU Markdown Table Protection Protocol.

| # | Equation | Physical Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\log a_T = -C_1(T - T_g)/[C_2 + (T - T_g)]$ | WLF (reference: $T_g$) | $a_T$ dimensionless, $T$ in K |
| 2 | $\log a_T = -C_1(T - T_s)/[C_2 + (T - T_s)]$ | WLF (reference: $T_s$) | $T_s$ in K |
| 3 | $\eta = A\exp(B/f)$ | Doolittle equation | $\eta$ in Pa·s |
| 4 | $f = f_g + \alpha_f (T - T_g)$ | Linear free-volume expansion | dimensionless |
| 5 | $C_1 = B/(2.303\,f_g)$ | Link to Doolittle $B$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | $C_2 = f_g/\alpha_f$ | Link to free-volume expansion coeff. | K |
| 7 | $f_g = B/(2.303\,C_1)$ | Fractional free volume at $T_g$ | $\approx 0.025$ |
| 8 | $\alpha_f = B/(2.303\,C_1 C_2)$ | Thermal expansion of $v_f$ | $\text{K}^{-1}$ |
| 9 | $W = 1 - f$ | Occupied-volume fraction | dimensionless |
| 10 | $a_T \cdot a_{T'} = a_{T''}$ | Group-shift rule (when $T_g$ same) | dimensionless |

### Validity & Boundary Conditions

- $T_g \leq T \leq T_g + 100\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$ → **WLF valid**.
- $T < T_g$ → WLF fails; use **Arrhenius** equation instead.
- $T \gg T_g + 100$ → WLF deviates because free-volume expansion saturates.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry / Domain | Use of WLF |
|---|---|
| **Rheology of polymer melts** | Predicting viscosity for extrusion & injection-molding design |
| **Rubber compounding** | Designing cure cycles near $T_g + 50$ |
| **Adhesives & coatings** | Determining open time & tack at application temperatures |
| **Aged polymers / physical aging** | Estimating long-term modulus from short-term data |
| **Dynamic Mechanical Analysis (DMA)** | Constructing master curves over wide frequency ranges |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Full Derivation of WLF from the Doolittle Equation

This is the *single most important derivation* in Module 3 — be ready to write every step on the answer sheet.

### Step 1 — Begin with the Doolittle Equation

For a simple liquid (and as an ansatz for polymer melts above $T_g$):

$$\eta = A \, \exp\!\left(\frac{B}{f}\right)$$

where $f$ is the fractional free volume at temperature $T$, and $A$, $B$ are positive material constants.

### Step 2 — Form the Ratio $\eta(T)/\eta(T_g)$

By the time–temperature superposition principle, the shift factor equals the viscosity ratio:

$$a_T = \frac{\eta(T)}{\eta(T_g)}$$

Substituting Doolittle's expression at both temperatures:

$$a_T = \frac{A\,\exp(B/f)}{A\,\exp(B/f_g)} = \exp\!\left[B\!\left(\frac{1}{f} - \frac{1}{f_g}\right)\right]$$

### Step 3 — Apply Free-Volume Linear Expansion Above $T_g$

From Fox–Flory free-volume theory:

$$f = f_g + \alpha_f \, (T - T_g)$$

where $\alpha_f$ is the thermal expansion coefficient of the free volume alone. Substitute into the reciprocal $1/f$:

$$\frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{f_g + \alpha_f (T - T_g)}$$

### Step 4 — Algebraic Manipulation of the Reciprocal Difference

Compute the key difference:

$$\frac{1}{f} - \frac{1}{f_g} = \frac{1}{f_g + \alpha_f (T - T_g)} - \frac{1}{f_g}$$

Combine over a common denominator:

$$\frac{1}{f} - \frac{1}{f_g} = \frac{f_g - [f_g + \alpha_f (T - T_g)]}{f_g [f_g + \alpha_f (T - T_g)]}$$

Simplify the numerator:

$$\frac{1}{f} - \frac{1}{f_g} = \frac{-\alpha_f (T - T_g)}{f_g [f_g + \alpha_f (T - T_g)]}$$

Divide numerator and denominator by $\alpha_f$:

$$\frac{1}{f} - \frac{1}{f_g} = -\frac{(T - T_g)}{f_g \,\bigl[(f_g/\alpha_f) + (T - T_g)\bigr]}$$

### Step 5 — Insert Back into the Shift-Factor Expression

$$a_T = \exp\!\left[ -\,\frac{B (T - T_g)}{f_g \bigl[(f_g/\alpha_f) + (T - T_g)\bigr]} \right]$$

### Step 6 — Convert Natural Logarithm to Base-10

Take $\log_{10}$ of both sides, using $\ln x = 2.303 \log_{10} x$:

$$\log_{10} a_T = -\frac{B}{2.303 \, f_g} \cdot \frac{(T - T_g)}{(f_g/\alpha_f) + (T - T_g)}$$

### Step 7 — Identify the WLF Constants

Define the two empirical constants as:

$$C_1 \equiv \frac{B}{2.303 \, f_g}, \qquad C_2 \equiv \frac{f_g}{\alpha_f}$$

These identifications are what give WLF its *physical meaning*: $C_1$ encodes the magnitude of the free-volume-related energy barrier (through $B$), while $C_2$ encodes the ratio of free volume at $T_g$ to its thermal expansion rate.

### Step 8 — Final Canonical Form

$$\boxed{\log_{10} a_T = -\frac{C_1 (T - T_g)}{C_2 + (T - T_g)}}$$

with the universal numerical values $C_1 = 17.44$, $C_2 = 51.6\ \text{K}$ when $T_g$ is the reference.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem.** The glass transition temperature of polystyrene is $T_g = 100\ ^{\circ}\text{C} = 373\ \text{K}$. Calculate the WLF shift factor $a_T$ at $T = 130\ ^{\circ}\text{C} = 403\ \text{K}$. By what factor does the apparent relaxation time change?

### Solution

**Step 1 — Compute the temperature difference:**

$$\Delta T = T - T_g = 403 - 373 = 30\ \text{K}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into WLF (using universal constants):**

$$\log_{10} a_T = -\frac{17.44 \times 30}{51.6 + 30} = -\frac{523.2}{81.6}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the quotient:**

$$\log_{10} a_T = -6.412$$

**Step 4 — Take the antilog:**

$$a_T = 10^{-6.412} = 3.87 \times 10^{-7}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation.** Because $a_T < 1$, the polymer at $130\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$ relaxes $\mathbf{1/a_T \approx 2.58 \times 10^{6}}$ times *faster* than at $T_g$. This massive acceleration is precisely why extrusion processes work so well just $30\ \text{K}$ above $T_g$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark-Winning Comment for KTU:** Always state the **physical interpretation** of $a_T$ — “$a_T < 1$ means the polymer is in a faster-relaxing (more fluid) state at temperature $T$ compared to the reference.”

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — WLF vs Arrhenius vs Doolittle

| Property | Arrhenius | Doolittle | WLF |
|---|---|---|---|
| Functional form | $\ln \eta \propto 1/T$ | $\ln \eta = \ln A + B/f$ | $\log a_T = -C_1\Delta T/(C_2+\Delta T)$ |
| Underlying mechanism | Activation over energy barrier | Free-volume crowding | Free volume + TTSP |
| Valid range | $T < T_g$ (glassy) | Wide, simple liquids | $T_g$ to $T_g + 100\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$ |
| Parameters | $E_a$ | $A$, $B$ | $C_1$, $C_2$ |
| Universality | Material-specific | Material-specific | Near-universal ($17.44$, $51.6$) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Architecture Flow — From Free Volume to Master Curve

The following Mermaid block diagrams the logical chain that produces a usable master curve from the WLF equation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Amorphous Polymer Sample] --> B0[Identify Tg via DSC or DMA]
    B0 --> C0[Apply Free Volume Theory]
    C0[Apply Fox-Flory Free Volume Theory] --> D0[Doolittle Equation]
    D0[Use Doolittle Equation eta = A exp B over f] --> E0[Form Viscosity Ratio]
    E0 --> F0[WLF Equation log aT]
    F0[WLF Equation with C1 C2] --> G0[Compute Shift Factor at T]
    G0 --> H0[Apply TTSP Horizontally]
    H0[Apply Time Temperature Superposition] --> I0[Construct Master Curve]
    I0 --> J0[Predict Long Term Behaviour]

    K0[Reference Tg] -. universal values .-> F0
    L0[Reference Ts] -. non universal C1 C2 .-> F0
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Mind-Map of WLF Concepts

```mermaid
mindmap
    root((WLF Equation))
        Theoretical Base
            Free Volume Theory
                Fractional Free Volume f
                Thermal Expansion alpha f
            Doolittle Equation
                Material constants A and B
        Core Equation
            log aT equals negative C1 over C2 form
            Universal Constants
                C1 equals 17.44
                C2 equals 51.6 K
            Reference Temperature
                Tg as default
                Arbitrary Ts shift
        Application Domain
            Tg to Tg plus 100 range
            Polymer Melt Rheology
            DMA Master Curve Construction
            Physical Aging Prediction
        Limitations
            Fails Below Tg
            Use Arrhenius Instead
            Deviation Above Tg plus 100
```

## 4.3 Numerical Sensitivity Block — Shift-Factor Behaviour

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T0[T equals Tg] -->|delta T equals 0| S0[aT equals 1]
    T10[T equals Tg plus 10 K] -->|delta T equals 10| S10[log aT equals negative 2.83]
    T50[T equals Tg plus 50 K] -->|delta T equals 50| S50[log aT equals negative 8.57]
    T100[T equals Tg plus 100 K] -->|delta T equals 100| S100[log aT equals negative 11.55]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the schematic:** As $\Delta T$ increases above $T_g$, $\log a_T$ becomes large and negative, meaning $a_T$ collapses to very small values — the polymer “flows like water” deep into the melt regime.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]**
**State the Williams–Landel–Ferry equation and write the universal values of the constants $C_1$ and $C_2$. Mention the temperature range in which it is valid.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The WLF equation expresses the temperature dependence of the shift factor $a_T$ for amorphous polymers:

$$\log_{10} a_T = -\frac{C_1 (T - T_g)}{C_2 + (T - T_g)}$$

The universal constants (Williams, Landel & Ferry, 1955) are $C_1 = 17.44$ and $C_2 = 51.6\ \text{K}$ when the reference temperature is $T_g$. The equation is valid in the range $T_g \leq T \leq T_g + 100\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$, i.e., in the supercooled liquid (rubbery-to-melt) region.

*(Mark split: 1 for equation, 1 for constants, 1 for range.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]**
**What is the physical significance of the shift factor $a_T$ in viscoelasticity? Define the Time–Temperature Superposition Principle.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The shift factor $a_T$ is a dimensionless scalar that quantifies the horizontal translation required (along the logarithmic time or frequency axis) to superimpose a viscoelastic response curve measured at temperature $T$ onto a master curve built at a chosen reference temperature $T_r$.

The Time–Temperature Superposition Principle (TTSP) states that for amorphous thermorheologically simple polymers, the effects of changing temperature and changing time (or frequency) are equivalent: an increase in temperature produces the same viscoelastic response as a decrease in time scale, and vice-versa.

*(Mark split: 1 for $a_T$ definition, 1 for TTSP, 1 for the equivalence statement.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries 14 marks, usually divided into two 7-mark sub-parts. Students must attempt **either Option A or Option B**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Full Derivation + Physical Interpretation

**(a) [7 Marks]** Starting from the **Doolittle equation**, derive the **WLF equation** and clearly identify the constants $C_1$ and $C_2$ in terms of free-volume parameters.

**(b) [7 Marks)** Discuss the *universality* of the WLF constants $C_1$ and $C_2$. Show how the equation changes when the reference temperature is shifted from $T_g$ to an arbitrary temperature $T_s$, and tabulate the resulting constants for $T_s = T_g$, $T_g + 50\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$, and $T_g + 100\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation:**
Follow **Section 3.1** above, Steps 1 → 8, in full. Each algebraic transition must be visible.

- Step 1: Doolittle equation $\eta = A\exp(B/f)$ — **0.5 mark**
- Step 2: Ratio $\eta(T)/\eta(T_g)$ — **0.5 mark**
- Step 3: Free-volume linear expansion $f = f_g + \alpha_f \Delta T$ — **1 mark**
- Step 4: Algebra on $(1/f - 1/f_g)$ — **2 marks**
- Step 5: Substitution back — **0.5 mark**
- Step 6: $\ln \to \log_{10}$ conversion using factor $2.303$ — **0.5 mark**
- Step 7: Identification $C_1 = B/2.303 f_g$ and $C_2 = f_g/\alpha_f$ — **1 mark**
- Step 8: Final boxed WLF form — **1 mark**

**Part (b) — Universality and Reference-Temperature Shift:**

Write the WLF form with reference $T_s$:

$$\log_{10} a_T = -\frac{C_1^{s} (T - T_s)}{C_2^{s} + (T - T_s)}$$

The new constants are related to the $T_g$-referenced values by:

$$C_1^{s} = \frac{C_1 \, C_2}{C_2 + (T_s - T_g)}, \qquad C_2^{s} = C_2 + (T_s - T_g)$$

**Tabulation of Constants for Various Reference Temperatures:**

| Reference Temperature $T_s$ | $C_1^{s}$ | $C_2^{s}$ (K) |
|---|---|---|
| $T_s = T_g$ | **17.44** | **51.6** |
| $T_s = T_g + 50\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$ | **8.86** | **101.6** |
| $T_s = T_g + 100\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$ | **6.18** | **151.6** |

> **Universality statement (1 mark):** When referenced to $T_g$, both $C_1$ and $C_2$ are nearly *constant across a wide variety of amorphous polymers* (PS, PMMA, PVAc, natural rubber, etc.). Ferry attributed this to a *common free-volume state at $T_g$* ($f_g \approx 0.025$) and a *common free-volume expansion rate* ($\alpha_f \approx 4.8 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{K}^{-1}$). When the reference is shifted, the constants change *systematically* but lose universality — they become polymer-specific.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Free-Volume Theory + Numerical Problem

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain **free-volume theory** as proposed by Fox and Flory. Define fractional free volume $f$, the free-volume expansion coefficient $\alpha_f$, and explain why $f$ is *frozen* below $T_g$ but grows linearly above it. Discuss the role of $f_g = 0.025$ and $\alpha_f = 4.8 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{K}^{-1}$ as universal values.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For a polymer with $T_g = 80\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$:
   (i) Calculate the shift factor $a_T$ at $T = 110\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$ using universal WLF constants.
   (ii) If the relaxation time at $T_g$ is $\tau(T_g) = 100\ \text{s}$, what is the relaxation time at $110\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$?
   (iii) Comment on why WLF fails above $T_g + 100\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Free-Volume Theory:**

The total macroscopic volume of a polymer is partitioned into *occupied volume* ($v_0$, the volume intrinsically occupied by molecules themselves) and *free volume* ($v_f$, the small voids and microvoids between segments). The fractional free volume is:

$$f = \frac{v_f}{v_{\text{total}}}$$

Above $T_g$, increasing temperature causes $v_0$ to expand normally (with $\alpha_g$) and $v_f$ to expand *faster* (with $\alpha_f > \alpha_g$). The net effect is:

$$f(T) = f_g + \alpha_f (T - T_g)$$

Below $T_g$, segmental mobility is essentially frozen, so free volume cannot redistribute; $f \approx f_g = \text{const}$.

The values $f_g = 0.025$ and $\alpha_f = 4.8 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{K}^{-1}$ are observed across many polymers because *segmental packing efficiency at the glass transition* and *thermal expansivity of the unoccupied space* are remarkably insensitive to chemical structure.

*(Marks: 1 for defining $f$, 1 for $f_g = 0.025$, 1 for $\alpha_f$ value, 1 for linear growth above $T_g$, 1 for frozen below $T_g$, 1 for universality argument, 1 for relevance to WLF.)*

**Part (b) — Numerical:**

**(i) Compute $a_T$ at $T = 110\ ^{\circ}\text{C} = 383\ \text{K}$, reference $T_g = 80\ ^{\circ}\text{C} = 353\ \text{K}$:**

$$\Delta T = 383 - 353 = 30\ \text{K}$$

$$\log_{10} a_T = -\frac{17.44 \times 30}{51.6 + 30} = -\frac{523.2}{81.6} = -6.412$$

$$a_T = 10^{-6.412} \approx 3.87 \times 10^{-7}$$

**(ii) Relaxation time at $110\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$:**

Since $a_T = \tau(T)/\tau(T_g)$:

$$\tau(110\ ^{\circ}\text{C}) = a_T \cdot \tau(T_g) = (3.87 \times 10^{-7})(100\ \text{s})$$

$$\tau(110\ ^{\circ}\text{C}) \approx 3.87 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{s} = 38.7\ \mu\text{s}$$

**(iii) Why WLF fails above $T_g + 100\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$:**

The free-volume expansion assumed linear in the derivation is only a *first-order approximation*. At temperatures far above $T_g$ (deep melt regime), the polymer chains gain sufficient thermal energy that:
- Free volume no longer scales linearly; cooperative segmental rearrangements begin to dominate.
- The thermodynamic assumption of a *single* relaxation time breaks down — distribution of relaxation times broadens.
- Entropic elasticity (rather than free-volume crowding) becomes the dominant resistance to flow.

Hence the WLF equation systematically over-predicts the temperature sensitivity, and the Arrhenius or Vogel–Fulcher–Tammann (VFT) form may be preferred at very high $T$.

*(Marks: 1 for correct $\Delta T$, 1 for substitution, 1 for $\log a_T$ value, 1 for $a_T$ numeric, 1 for $\tau$ computation, 1 for the multiplication, 1 for the discussion of high-$T$ failure.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps — KTU WLF Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor $2.303$** when converting $\ln \to \log_{10}$ in the derivation. This alone can cost 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Using inconsistent units for $T$ and $T_g$** (e.g., $T$ in $^\circ$C and $T_g$ in K). *Always use the same units, preferably Kelvin.*
> 3. **Citing the universal constants for an arbitrary reference temperature.** $C_1 = 17.44$, $C_2 = 51.6$ only when the reference is $T_g$. If $T_s \neq T_g$, you MUST recompute.
> 4. **Forgetting to state the validity range** $T_g \to T_g + 100\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$. Examiners often award a full mark solely for this line.
> 5. **Writing $T - T_g$ without absolute-value clarity.** Remember $T > T_g$ always in the WLF region, so the negative sign in the equation is *physically meaningful* (it makes $a_T < 1$).
> 6. **Not interpreting $a_T$ physically.** Always close numerical problems with a sentence like: “Since $a_T \ll 1$, the polymer flows far more readily at $T$ than at $T_g$.”

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — WLF Equation, Module 3**

- **Canonical form:** $\log_{10} a_T = -C_1 (T - T_g) / [C_2 + (T - T_g)]$
- **Universal constants (reference $T_g$):** $C_1 = 17.44$, $C_2 = 51.6\ \text{K}$
- **Universal free-volume parameters:** $f_g = 0.025$, $\alpha_f = 4.8 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{K}^{-1}$
- **Validity range:** $T_g \leq T \leq T_g + 100\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$
- **Origin:** Doolittle equation + Fox–Flory free-volume theory + TTSP
- **Constants–parameter links:** $C_1 = B/(2.303 f_g)$, $C_2 = f_g/\alpha_f$
- **Reference-shift rule:** $C_1^{s} = C_1 C_2 / [C_2 + (T_s - T_g)]$, $C_2^{s} = C_2 + (T_s - T_g)$
- **Group property:** $a_T(T_1 \to T_3) = a_T(T_1 \to T_2) \cdot a_T(T_2 \to T_3)$
- **Failure regimes:** below $T_g$ → use Arrhenius; far above $T_g + 100\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$ → use VFT
- **Physical meaning of $a_T < 1$:** polymer flows faster at $T$ than at $T_g$ (high $T$ → low viscosity)
- **Engineering uses:** master-curve construction in DMA, melt-viscosity prediction for extrusion, physical-aging extrapolation, design of cure cycles for elastomers
- **Universal master curve:** built by horizontal shifts $\log a_T$ predicted by WLF
- **Free volume is frozen** below $T_g$ — a key conceptual point for viva voce
- **Doolittle form (recap):** $\eta = A\exp(B/f)$ — must be cited whenever deriving WLF
- **Units discipline:** Use Kelvin consistently in $T - T_g$
- **Common viva question:** *“Why are $C_1$ and $C_2$ universal?”* — Answer: because $f_g$ and $\alpha_f$ are nearly identical across amorphous polymers due to a common segmental-packing state at $T_g$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rubber Elasticity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT402 - POLYMER PHYSICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Rubber Elasticity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — RUBBER ELASTICITY

## 1. Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rubber Elasticity (Elastomeric Elasticity)** is the *entropic* restoring force mechanism exhibited by lightly cross-linked polymer networks (elastomers) when subjected to large, reversible deformations (typically 100 %–1000 % strain) at temperatures **above** their glass transition temperature ($T > T_g$).

Unlike metals or ceramics — where elasticity originates from **enthalpic** bond-stretching — the elasticity of rubber originates almost entirely from a **decrease in configurational entropy** of long, flexible polymer chains when the chains are forced apart. When the deforming force is removed, the chains spontaneously coil back to their most probable (maximum-entropy) random-walk conformations.

### 1.1 KTU 2024 Working Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus-Highlight Definition:**
> *"Rubber elasticity is the reversible, long-range, non-linear elastic response of a polymer network whose restoring force arises primarily from the conformational entropy of the network chains (Gaussian network theory). It is characterized by a low elastic modulus ($E \approx 0.1$–$10 \text{ MPa}$), high extensibility ($\lambda \approx 10$), and near-incompressibility (Poisson's ratio $\nu \approx 0.49$)."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Bowl of Spaghetti That Wants to Coil"

Imagine a bowl of cooked spaghetti, lightly glued at a few junction points (the **cross-links**). Each noodle is free to wiggle between the cross-links, exploring countless random shapes. Now grab the bowl and stretch it:
- The noodles are forced to **straighten** (fewer wiggle-positions).
- They "fight back" not because the bonds stretch, but because they *miss* the freedom of coiling.
- Release the bowl → the noodles spontaneously recoil to a tangled, highly probable state.

This "missing-freedom" tug is the **entropy spring** — the essence of rubber elasticity.

### 1.3 Key Distinguishing Features

| Property | Rubber (Entropic) | Metal (Enthalpic) |
|---|---|---|
| Origin of restoring force | Conformational entropy change | Inter-atomic bond stretching |
| Young's modulus | $0.1$–$10 \text{ MPa}$ | $10^4$–$10^5 \text{ MPa}$ |
| Max reversible strain | $500$–$1000 \text{ %}$ | $< 1 \text{ %}$ |
| Effect of heating | Modulus **increases** | Modulus **decreases** |
| Poisson's ratio | $\approx 0.49$ (incompressible) | $\approx 0.3$ |
| Operative regime | $T > T_g$ | Always |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory hook:** "Hot rubber pulls harder" — the *opposite* of how metals behave. This is the textbook signature of entropic elasticity.

### 1.4 Fundamental Quantities in Rubber Elasticity

- **$N$** — number of network chains (active chains between cross-links) per unit volume $\left[\text{m}^{-3}\right]$
- **$\nu$** — molar concentration of network chains $\left[\text{mol m}^{-3}\right]$
- **$\lambda$** — extension ratio (deformed length / original length) [dimensionless]
- **$k_B$** — Boltzmann constant $= 1.381 \times 10^{-23} \text{ J K}^{-1}$
- **$R$** — universal gas constant $= 8.314 \text{ J mol}^{-1}\text{K}^{-1}$
- **$T$** — absolute temperature in **kelvin (K)**
- **$G$** — shear modulus $[\text{Pa}]$
- **$E$** — Young's modulus $[\text{Pa}]$

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Pillars of Rubber Elasticity Theory

### Pillar I — Thermodynamic (Phenomenological) Approach
The Helmholtz free energy of a deformed network can be decomposed:

$$
A = U - T S
$$

The tensile force on a rubber strip held at length $L$ is:

$$
f = \left( \frac{\partial A}{\partial L} \right)_{T,V} = \left( \frac{\partial U}{\partial L} \right)_{T,V} - T \left( \frac{\partial S}{\partial L} \right)_{T,V}
$$

Experimental measurements show that for an ideal elastomer:

$$
\left( \frac{\partial U}{\partial L} \right)_{T,V} \approx 0
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\,f = -T \left( \frac{\partial S}{\partial L} \right)_{T,V}\,}
$$

This is the *single most important result* in rubber thermodynamics — the force is **purely entropic** in origin.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is $\partial U/\partial L \approx 0$?** Because the C–C and C–H bond energies are so high ($\sim 350 \text{ kJ/mol}$) compared to the thermal energy $k_B T$ that the bond lengths and angles barely change during moderate deformation. Only the *torsional* angles change, and these cost virtually no energy.

### Pillar II — Statistical (Molecular) Approach
Treat each network chain as a **Gaussian chain** with end-to-end vector $\mathbf{r}$. The probability of finding end-to-end distance $r$ is:

$$
P(\mathbf{r}) = \left( \frac{3}{2 \pi \langle r^2 \rangle_0} \right)^{3/2} \exp\!\left( - \frac{3 r^2}{2 \langle r^2 \rangle_0} \right)
$$

where $\langle r^2 \rangle_0 = N_b \, l^2 \, C_\infty$ is the mean-square end-to-end distance of a *free* (unperturbed) chain with $N_b$ backbone bonds of length $l$ and characteristic ratio $C_\infty$.

The entropy of a single Gaussian chain is:

$$
S_{\text{chain}} = k_B \ln P(\mathbf{r}) + \text{const} = k_B \left[ \text{const} - \frac{3 r^2}{2 \langle r^2 \rangle_0} \right]
$$

So the **entropy change** of one chain whose ends are displaced from $\mathbf{0}$ to $\mathbf{r}$:

$$
\Delta S_{\text{chain}} = - \frac{3 k_B}{2 \langle r^2 \rangle_0} \, r^2
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This quadratic dependence on $r$ is the **Gaussian signature**. It immediately yields the famous *quadratic* free-energy form when summed over a 3D network.

---

## 2.2 The Gaussian Network Free Energy

Consider $N$ network chains per unit volume in a macroscopic sample undergoing affine deformation with principal extension ratios $\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \lambda_3$. Affine deformation means that **every chain end moves in proportion to the macroscopic strain**, as if embedded in a continuous elastic continuum.

The entropy change per unit volume is:

$$
\Delta S = - \frac{1}{2} N k_B \left( \lambda_1^2 + \lambda_2^2 + \lambda_3^2 - 3 \right)
$$

For an **incompressible** elastomer ($\lambda_1 \lambda_2 \lambda_3 = 1$):

$$
\Delta A = -T \Delta S = \frac{1}{2} N k_B T \left( \lambda_1^2 + \lambda_2^2 + \lambda_3^2 - 3 \right)
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\Delta A = \frac{1}{2} N k_B T \left( \lambda_1^2 + \lambda_2^2 + \lambda_3^2 - 3 \right)\,}
$$

This is the **fundamental Gaussian network free energy** — the cornerstone of classical rubber elasticity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Where does "3" come from?** It is the isotropic (undeformed) reference state $\lambda_1 = \lambda_2 = \lambda_3 = 1$, ensuring $\Delta A = 0$ at zero deformation. The $\tfrac{1}{2}$ appears because each chain is shared between two cross-links (counted once in the network).

---

## 2.3 Engineering Stress–Strain for Uniaxial Extension

For uniaxial extension $\lambda_1 = \lambda$, $\lambda_2 = \lambda_3 = \lambda^{-1/2}$ (volume conservation):

$$
\sigma = N k_B T \left( \lambda - \lambda^{-2} \right)
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\sigma_{\text{affine}} = \nu R T \left( \lambda - \lambda^{-2} \right)\,}
$$

where $\nu = N / N_A$ is the molar concentration of active chains.

For small strains ($\lambda = 1 + \varepsilon$, $\varepsilon \ll 1$), Taylor-expansion gives:

$$
\sigma \approx 3 N k_B T \, \varepsilon = E \, \varepsilon
$$

Hence the **small-strain Young's modulus**:

$$
\boxed{\,E = 3 N k_B T = 3 \nu R T\,}
$$

And the **shear modulus**:

$$
\boxed{\,G = N k_B T = \nu R T\,}
$$

This famous relation $G = \nu R T$ predicts that the modulus is **proportional to absolute temperature** — exactly the opposite of an enthalpic spring, and a hallmark of entropic elasticity that has been verified experimentally for natural rubber, polyisoprene, polybutadiene, and silicone networks.

---

## 2.4 Affine vs Phantom Network Models

| Feature | **Affine Network Model** | **Phantom Network Model** |
|---|---|---|
| Cross-link motion | Cross-links move **affinely** with macroscopic strain | Cross-links fluctuate around mean positions (Gaussian) |
| $\langle r^2 \rangle$ of chains | Scales as $\lambda^2 \langle r^2 \rangle_0$ | Scales as $\lambda^2 \langle r^2 \rangle_0$ |
| Modulus | $G_A = \nu R T$ | $G_P = (\nu - \mu) R T = \xi \nu R T$ |
| Front factor $\xi$ | $\xi = 1$ | $\xi = 1 - 2/\phi$ ($\phi$ = functionality) |
| Agreement with experiment | Over-predicts modulus at large $\lambda$ | Better at small-to-moderate strains |
| Treats entanglements as | Effective additional cross-links | Neglected (chains fluctuate freely) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a **tetrafunctional** network ($\phi = 4$): $\xi_{\text{phantom}} = 1 - \tfrac{2}{4} = \tfrac{1}{2}$. So phantom modulus is *half* the affine modulus.

---

## 2.5 Real Network Behaviour — Mooney–Rivlin & Frontiers

Real elastomers exhibit deviation from Gaussian theory at high strains (strain-induced crystallization, finite extensibility) and at small strains (chain entanglement contributions). The **Mooney–Rivlin** semi-empirical form is widely used:

$$
\sigma = \left( 2 C_1 + \frac{2 C_2}{\lambda} \right) \left( \lambda - \lambda^{-2} \right)
$$

where $C_1$ and $C_2$ are empirical constants; $2C_1 \approx \tfrac{1}{2} \nu R T$ captures the network contribution, $C_2$ captures loose-chain/entanglement effects.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f = (\partial A / \partial L)_{T,V}$ | Tensile force from free energy | N |
| 2 | $f = -T(\partial S/\partial L)_{T,V}$ | Force is purely entropic | N |
| 3 | $P(\mathbf{r}) = \left(\tfrac{3}{2\pi \langle r^2\rangle_0}\right)^{3/2} e^{-3r^2/2\langle r^2\rangle_0}$ | Gaussian chain end-to-end probability | $\text{m}^{-3}$ |
| 4 | $\Delta S_{\text{chain}} = -\tfrac{3 k_B}{2 \langle r^2\rangle_0} r^2$ | Single-chain entropy loss | J K$^{-1}$ |
| 5 | $\Delta A = \tfrac{1}{2} N k_B T(\lambda_1^2+\lambda_2^2+\lambda_3^2-3)$ | Gaussian network free energy | J m$^{-3}$ |
| 6 | $\sigma = \nu R T (\lambda - \lambda^{-2})$ | Uniaxial engineering stress | Pa |
| 7 | $E = 3 \nu R T$ | Young's modulus (small strain) | Pa |
| 8 | $G = \nu R T$ | Shear modulus | Pa |
| 9 | $G_P = (\nu - \mu) R T = \xi \nu R T$ | Phantom network modulus | Pa |
| 10 | $\sigma = (2C_1 + 2C_2/\lambda)(\lambda-\lambda^{-2})$ | Mooney–Rivlin empirical form | Pa |
| 11 | $f_{\text{elastomeric}} = -T\,\Delta S / \Delta L$ | Force from entropy loss | N |
| 12 | $\lambda_1 \lambda_2 \lambda_3 = 1$ | Incompressibility constraint | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorize formulae 5, 6, 7, 8** — they appear in approximately **80 %** of KTU Module-4 derivations.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Tyre technology** — natural rubber / polybutadiene / SBR blends; modulus tuning via $\nu$ (cross-link density).
- **Vibration dampers & mounts** — viscoelastic but storage modulus governed by $G = \nu R T$.
- **Biomedical elastomers** — silicone rubber, polyurethane for implants, catheters.
- **Soft robotics & dielectric elastomer actuators** — exploit large $\lambda$ with controllable stress.
- **Pressure-sensitive adhesives** — require a precise balance of $E$ and tack.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The relation $G = \nu R T$ is the *engineer's tool*: by measuring $G$ at known $T$, one directly infers the **active network chain concentration** $\nu$, which is otherwise very hard to measure. Conversely, by controlling cross-link density (via sulphur or peroxide cure), the engineer can dial in the desired modulus.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation A — Entropy Loss of a Single Gaussian Chain

**Starting point:** A single polymer chain with $n$ freely-jointed segments of length $l$ has a Gaussian probability distribution for its end-to-end vector $\mathbf{r}$:

$$
P(\mathbf{r}) = \left( \frac{3}{2 \pi \langle r^2 \rangle_0} \right)^{3/2} \exp\!\left( - \frac{3 r^2}{2 \langle r^2 \rangle_0} \right)
$$

where $\langle r^2 \rangle_0 = n l^2$ is the mean-square end-to-end distance for the unperturbed (free) chain.

**Step 1 — Write entropy using Boltzmann's relation.** The entropy associated with finding the chain in state $\mathbf{r}$ is:

$$
S(\mathbf{r}) = k_B \ln \Omega(\mathbf{r}) = k_B \ln P(\mathbf{r}) + \text{const}
$$

Here $k_B = 1.381 \times 10^{-23} \text{ J K}^{-1}$.

**Step 2 — Substitute the Gaussian form.**

$$
S(\mathbf{r}) = k_B \left[ \frac{3}{2} \ln\!\left( \frac{3}{2\pi \langle r^2 \rangle_0} \right) - \frac{3 r^2}{2 \langle r^2 \rangle_0} \right] + \text{const}
$$

**Step 3 — Identify terms independent of $\mathbf{r}$.** The first logarithmic term depends only on $\langle r^2 \rangle_0$, which is itself a property of the free chain and does not change during deformation. Group all $\mathbf{r}$-independent terms into $S_0$:

$$
S(\mathbf{r}) = S_0 - \frac{3 k_B}{2 \langle r^2 \rangle_0} \, r^2
$$

**Step 4 — Compute entropy change relative to the undeformed state ($r = 0$).**

$$
\Delta S_{\text{chain}} = S(\mathbf{r}) - S(0) = \left( S_0 - \frac{3 k_B r^2}{2 \langle r^2 \rangle_0} \right) - S_0
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\Delta S_{\text{chain}} = -\,\frac{3 k_B}{2 \langle r^2 \rangle_0}\, r^2\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The entropy *decreases* (negative sign) as the chain ends are pulled apart. Physically: there are exponentially fewer microstates available for a chain stretched to a large $r$ than for one near the origin.

---

## 3.2 Derivation B — Free Energy of a Deformed Gaussian Network (Affine Model)

**Step 1 — Apply affine deformation.** In a macroscopic sample with principal stretches $\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \lambda_3$, every chain end at position $\mathbf{r}_0$ in the undeformed state is mapped affinely to:

$$
\mathbf{r} = \boldsymbol{\lambda} \cdot \mathbf{r}_0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad r_i^2 = \lambda_i^2 \, r_{0,i}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Sum over all $N$ chains per unit volume.**

$$
\Delta S_{\text{network}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \Delta S_{\text{chain},i} = -\,\frac{3 k_B}{2 \langle r^2 \rangle_0} \sum_{i=1}^{N} r_i^2
$$

**Step 3 — Apply affine mapping and take expectation.** Because the undeformed chain orientations are random (isotropic):

$$
\langle r_{0,1}^2 \rangle = \langle r_{0,2}^2 \rangle = \langle r_{0,3}^2 \rangle = \frac{1}{3} \langle r^2 \rangle_0
$$

Therefore:

$$
\langle r_i^2 \rangle = \lambda_1^2 \langle r_{0,1}^2 \rangle + \lambda_2^2 \langle r_{0,2}^2 \rangle + \lambda_3^2 \langle r_{0,3}^2 \rangle = \frac{\langle r^2 \rangle_0}{3} \left( \lambda_1^2 + \lambda_2^2 + \lambda_3^2 \right)
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the entropy sum.**

$$
\Delta S = -\,\frac{3 k_B}{2 \langle r^2 \rangle_0} \cdot N \cdot \frac{\langle r^2 \rangle_0}{3} \left( \lambda_1^2 + \lambda_2^2 + \lambda_3^2 \right)
$$

The $\langle r^2 \rangle_0$ cancels cleanly:

$$
\Delta S = -\,\frac{1}{2} N k_B \left( \lambda_1^2 + \lambda_2^2 + \lambda_3^2 \right)
$$

**Step 5 — Add isotropic reference term.** At undeformed state $\lambda_i = 1$, we must have $\Delta S = 0$, so subtract the reference:

$$
\Delta S = -\,\frac{1}{2} N k_B \left( \lambda_1^2 + \lambda_2^2 + \lambda_3^2 - 3 \right)
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to Helmholtz free energy** ($\Delta U = 0$ for ideal rubber):

$$
\Delta A = \Delta U - T \Delta S = - T \Delta S
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\Delta A = \frac{1}{2} N k_B T \left( \lambda_1^2 + \lambda_2^2 + \lambda_3^2 - 3 \right)\,}
$$

This is **THE** Gaussian network free energy — central to every rubber-elasticity problem.

---

## 3.3 Derivation C — Uniaxial Engineering Stress $\sigma(\lambda)$

**Step 1 — Apply incompressibility.** Rubber is essentially incompressible ($\nu_{\text{Poisson}} \approx 0.49$):

$$
\lambda_1 \lambda_2 \lambda_3 = 1
$$

For uniaxial extension along axis 1 with stretch $\lambda \equiv \lambda_1$, symmetry gives $\lambda_2 = \lambda_3 = \lambda^{-1/2}$.

**Step 2 — Express $\Delta A$ in terms of $\lambda$ only.**

$$
\lambda_1^2 + \lambda_2^2 + \lambda_3^2 = \lambda^2 + 2 \lambda^{-1}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\Delta A = \frac{1}{2} N k_B T \left( \lambda^2 + 2 \lambda^{-1} - 3 \right)
$$

**Step 3 — Compute nominal (engineering) stress.** True stress is $t = \partial \Delta A / \partial \lambda$ per unit reference volume. Engineering stress is related by $\sigma = t / \lambda$. Differentiating:

$$
\frac{\partial (\Delta A)}{\partial \lambda} = \frac{1}{2} N k_B T \left( 2\lambda - 2\lambda^{-2} \right) = N k_B T \left( \lambda - \lambda^{-2} \right)
$$

This is the **true stress**. Dividing by $\lambda$:

$$
\sigma_{\text{engineering}} = N k_B T \left( 1 - \lambda^{-3} \right)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Convention check for KTU:** Most KTU textbook problems adopt $\sigma = \nu R T (\lambda - \lambda^{-2})$ as the *nominal/engineering stress* directly. Be careful — both forms appear. Always verify whether the textbook defines $\sigma$ as true or engineering.

Sticking with the more common KTU convention:

$$
\boxed{\,\sigma_{\text{nominal}} = \nu R T \left( \lambda - \lambda^{-2} \right)\,}
$$

**Step 4 — Linearize for small strains.** Set $\lambda = 1 + \varepsilon$ with $\varepsilon \ll 1$. Taylor expansion:

$$
\lambda^{-2} = (1 + \varepsilon)^{-2} \approx 1 - 2\varepsilon + 3\varepsilon^2 - \cdots
$$

Hence:

$$
\lambda - \lambda^{-2} \approx (1+\varepsilon) - (1 - 2\varepsilon) = 3\varepsilon
$$

Therefore:

$$
\sigma \approx 3 \nu R T \, \varepsilon
$$

Comparing with Hooke's law $\sigma = E \varepsilon$:

$$
\boxed{\,E = 3 \nu R T\,}
$$

And since $G = E / [2(1+\nu_{\text{Poisson}})] \to E/3$ for incompressible material ($\nu_{\text{Poisson}} = 0.5$):

$$
\boxed{\,G = \nu R T\,}
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation D — Phantom Network Modulus

In the **phantom network model**, cross-links are not pinned to the macroscopic strain but execute thermal fluctuations about their mean (affine) positions. Let $\xi$ be the *front factor* relating the phantom modulus to the affine modulus:

$$
G_{\text{phantom}} = \xi \, G_{\text{affine}} = \xi \, \nu R T
$$

For a network of functionality $\phi$ (number of chains meeting at each cross-link), the James–Guth result gives:

$$
\xi = 1 - \frac{2}{\phi}
$$

Derivation sketch (KTU-friendly):

**Step 1 — Fluctuating cross-links.** In the phantom network, each cross-link moves by $\Delta \mathbf{R}$ around its mean affine position $\bar{\mathbf{R}}$. The mean-square fluctuation is constrained by the chains emanating from it.

**Step 2 — For a tetrafunctional cross-link** ($\phi = 4$): two of the four chains are essentially "absorbed" into constraint fluctuations, contributing a factor of $2/\phi = 1/2$.

**Step 3 — Therefore:**

$$
G_{\text{phantom}} = \left( 1 - \frac{2}{\phi} \right) \nu R T
$$

$$
\boxed{\,G_{\text{phantom}} = \left( 1 - \frac{2}{\phi} \right) \nu R T\,}
$$

For $\phi = 3$ (trifunctional): $\xi = 1/3$; for $\phi = 4$: $\xi = 1/2$; for $\phi \to \infty$: $\xi \to 1$ (recovers affine limit).

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — How to Solve a Typical KTU Numericals

| Given | Find | Route |
|---|---|---|
| $\nu$, $T$, $\lambda$ | $\sigma$ | $\sigma = \nu R T (\lambda - \lambda^{-2})$ |
| $\nu$, $T$ | $E$, $G$ | $E = 3 \nu R T$; $G = \nu R T$ |
| $G$ at known $T$ | $\nu$ | $\nu = G / (R T)$ |
| $M_c$ (chain mol. wt.), $\rho$ | $\nu$ | $\nu = \rho / M_c$ in consistent SI units |
| $\phi$ | $G_{\text{phantom}}$ | $G = (1 - 2/\phi) \nu R T$ |
| $\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \lambda_3$ | $\Delta A$ | Use Gaussian free-energy formula 5 |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Flow — Origin of Rubber Elasticity

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Cross-linked Polymer Network above Tg] --> nodeB[Apply Macroscopic Deformation]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Network Chains Forced to Straighten]
    nodeC --> nodeD[End-to-end Distance r Increases]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Number of Accessible Configurations Decreases Sharply]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Configurational Entropy S Decreases]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Free Energy A = U - TS Increases]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Restoring Force f = -T dS/dL Emerges]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Removal of Force: Chains Recoil to Maximum-Entropy State]
```

## 4.2 Hierarchy of Network Models

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Rubber Elasticity Theories] --> nodeB[Affine Network Model]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Phantom Network Model]
    nodeA --> nodeD[Constrained Junction Model]
    nodeA --> nodeE[Slip-link Model]
    nodeB --> nodeF[Cross-links move with macroscopic strain]
    nodeB --> nodeG[GA = nu RT]
    nodeC --> nodeH[Cross-links fluctuate thermally]
    nodeC --> nodeI[GP = 1 minus 2 over phi times nu RT]
    nodeD --> nodeJ[Includes entanglement constraints]
    nodeD --> nodeK[Real-network behaviour at intermediate strains]
    nodeE --> nodeL[Chains can slide through entanglements]
    nodeE --> nodeM[Predicts large-strain stiffening]
```

## 4.3 Free-Energy Decomposition — Enthalpic vs Entropic Contributions

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Total Restoring Force f] --> nodeB[Enthalpic Component fe]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Entropic Component fs]
    nodeB --> nodeD[Bond stretching and angle bending]
    nodeB --> nodeE[Small for rubber - below 10 percent]
    nodeC --> nodeF[Conformational entropy change]
    nodeC --> nodeG[Greater than 90 percent for ideal rubber]
    nodeD --> nodeH[fe = dU/dL at constant T V]
    nodeF --> nodeI[fs = -T dS/dL at constant T V]
    nodeH --> nodeJ[Total Force f equals fe plus fs]
    nodeI --> nodeJ
```

## 4.4 Block Architecture — Stress Calculation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Inputs: nu, T, lambda1, lambda2, lambda3] --> nodeB[Check Incompressibility]
    nodeB --> nodeC{lambda1 times lambda2 times lambda3 equals 1?}
    nodeC --> nodeD[Yes - Continue]
    nodeC --> nodeE[No - Apply Penalty or Hydrostatic Pressure term]
    nodeD --> nodeF[Compute delta A using Gaussian formula]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Compute Stress Tensor Components]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Uniaxial case: sigma equals nu RT times lambda minus lambda to power minus 2]
    nodeG --> nodeI[Biaxial or Pure Shear: extract respective components]
    nodeH --> nodeJ[Output: Engineering Stress and Modulus]
    nodeI --> nodeJ
```

## 4.5 Cross-link Functionality & Phantom Front Factor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Cross-link functionality phi] --> nodeB{phi value}
    nodeB --> nodeC[phi equals 3]
    nodeB --> nodeD[phi equals 4]
    nodeB --> nodeE[phi approaches infinity]
    nodeC --> nodeF[xi = 1 - 2 over 3 = 1 over 3]
    nodeD --> nodeG[xi = 1 - 2 over 4 = 1 over 2]
    nodeE --> nodeH[xi approaches 1 - affine limit]
    nodeF --> nodeI[GP = xi nu RT]
    nodeG --> nodeI
    nodeH --> nodeI
```
