# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Differentiate between entropic and enthalpic elasticity. Why is rubber elasticity predominantly entropic?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Enthalpic Elasticity | Entropic Elasticity |
|---|---|---|
| Origin | Bond length / angle changes | Conformational entropy change |
| Operative in | Metals, ceramics, glasses | Elastomers (cross-linked polymers above $T_g$) |
| Effect of $T \uparrow$ on modulus | Modulus decreases | Modulus **increases** |
| Typical strain range | $< 1\text{–}2\text{ \%}$ | $100\text{–}1000\text{ \%}$ |
| Energy storage | Internal energy $U$ | Free energy $A = -TS$ |

**Why entropic in rubber:** The C–C bond energy ($\sim 350 \text{ kJ/mol}$) is $\sim 1000\times$ larger than $k_B T$ ($\sim 2.5 \text{ kJ/mol}$ at $300 \text{ K}$). Hence bond stretching is energetically prohibitive. Instead, deformation alters the *number of conformations* available to each chain. The chain has $\sim Z^{N_b}$ torsional states (where $Z$ is the number of rotational isomeric states); stretching drastically reduces this count, decreasing $S$. The restoring force $f = -T(\partial S/\partial L)$ emerges with no need for bond deformation. ∎

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: State and explain the Gaussian network free energy expression for a rubber. What is the physical significance of each term?**

**Model Answer:**

The Helmholtz free energy change per unit volume of an affinely deformed Gaussian network is:

$$
\Delta A = \frac{1}{2} N k_B T \left( \lambda_1^2 + \lambda_2^2 + \lambda_3^2 - 3 \right)
$$

**Physical significance of each term:**
- **$N$** — number density of *active* network chains (chains elastically effective between cross-links); sets the network connectivity.
- **$k_B T$** — thermal energy scale; quantifies the entropic driving force. Appears *linearly* with $T$, making $\Delta A$ grow with temperature.
- **$\frac{1}{2}$** — arises because each chain is counted once, although its two ends belong to two cross-links.
- **$\lambda_i^2$** — squared principal stretch along axis $i$; measures chain extension along that direction.
- **$3$** — isotropic reference state ($\lambda_1 = \lambda_2 = \lambda_3 = 1$); ensures $\Delta A = 0$ when undeformed.

The expression is valid for $\lambda_i \lesssim \lambda_{\max} \approx \sqrt{N_b}$ (Gaussian limit; finite extensibility not yet reached). ∎

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — INTERNAL CHOICE

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Derive the expression for the engineering stress–stretch ratio relationship for an ideal affine Gaussian network undergoing uniaxial extension.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Begin with the Gaussian network free energy per unit volume:**

$$
\Delta A = \frac{1}{2} N k_B T \left( \lambda_1^2 + \lambda_2^2 + \lambda_3^2 - 3 \right)
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the incompressibility constraint $\lambda_1 \lambda_2 \lambda_3 = 1$.** For uniaxial extension along axis 1 with stretch $\lambda \equiv \lambda_1$, transverse isotropy gives:

$$
\lambda_2 = \lambda_3 = \lambda^{-1/2}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into $\Delta A$:**

$$
\lambda_1^2 + \lambda_2^2 + \lambda_3^2 = \lambda^2 + 2 \lambda^{-1}
$$

Hence:

$$
\Delta A(\lambda) = \frac{1}{2} N k_B T \left( \lambda^2 + 2\lambda^{-1} - 3 \right)
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the engineering stress** $\sigma = \partial \Delta A / \partial \lambda$:

$$
\sigma = \frac{\partial (\Delta A)}{\partial \lambda} = \frac{1}{2} N k_B T \left( 2\lambda - 2\lambda^{-2} \right)
$$

$$
\sigma = N k_B T \left( \lambda - \lambda^{-2} \right)
$$

In molar form ($\nu = N/N_A$):

$$
\boxed{\,\sigma = \nu R T \left( \lambda - \lambda^{-2} \right)\,}
$$

This is the classical **affine network stress–stretch relation**. ∎

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**A natural rubber sample has a network chain density $\nu = 150 \text{ mol m}^{-3}$ at $25 \text{ °C}$. Compute (i) the small-strain Young's modulus $E$, (ii) the shear modulus $G$, and (iii) the engineering stress required to stretch the sample to $\lambda = 4$. Compare with $T = 100 \text{ °C}$. Comment on the temperature dependence.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $\nu = 150 \text{ mol m}^{-3}$
- $T_1 = 25 \text{ °C} = 298 \text{ K}$
- $T_2 = 100 \text{ °C} = 373 \text{ K}$
- $\lambda = 4$
- $R = 8.314 \text{ J mol}^{-1}\text{K}^{-1}$

**Step 1 — Shear modulus at 298 K:**

$$
G_1 = \nu R T_1 = 150 \times 8.314 \times 298 = 3.716 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} \approx 0.372 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 2 — Young's modulus at 298 K** (incompressible material, $\nu_{\text{Poisson}} = 0.5$):

$$
E_1 = 3 G_1 = 3 \times 0.372 \approx 1.115 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 3 — Engineering stress at $\lambda = 4$:**

$$
\sigma_1 = \nu R T_1 \left( \lambda - \lambda^{-2} \right) = 3.716 \times 10^5 \times \left( 4 - \frac{1}{16} \right)
$$

$$
\sigma_1 = 3.716 \times 10^5 \times 3.9375 \approx 1.463 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} \approx 1.46 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 4 — Repeat at 373 K:**

$$
G_2 = 150 \times 8.314 \times 373 = 4.652 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} \approx 0.465 \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
E_2 = 3 G_2 \approx 1.396 \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
\sigma_2 = 4.652 \times 10^5 \times 3.9375 \approx 1.832 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} \approx 1.83 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 5 — Comparison:**

| Quantity | 25 °C | 100 °C | Ratio |
|---|---|---|---|
| $G$ (MPa) | 0.372 | 0.465 | 1.25 |
| $E$ (MPa) | 1.115 | 1.396 | 1.25 |
| $\sigma$ (MPa) | 1.46 | 1.83 | 1.25 |

**Comment:** All three quantities scale *linearly with absolute temperature*. Heating the rubber from 298 K to 373 K increases both modulus and stress by a factor of $373/298 \approx 1.25$. This **positive temperature coefficient** is the diagnostic signature of *entropic* elasticity — the opposite of metallic behaviour. Practically, this means a heated rubber band pulls harder and feels "tighter". ∎

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Students frequently use $T$ in °C instead of K when plugging into $G = \nu R T$. *Always* convert to **kelvin** before substitution. Marks are routinely lost for unit inconsistency.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Describe the affine network model and the phantom network model for rubber elasticity. Compare their predictions for the shear modulus of a tetrafunctional network.**

**Model Solution:**

**Affine Network Model (Treloar, 1943 / Flory, 1942):**

- **Assumption:** Cross-links are *firmly embedded* in the elastic continuum and move *affinely* with the macroscopic strain.
- **Consequence:** Mean chain dimensions follow $\langle r_i^2 \rangle = \lambda_i^2 \langle r_{0,i}^2 \rangle$ exactly.
- **Modulus prediction:**

$$
G_{\text{affine}} = \nu R T
$$

**Phantom Network Model (James & Guth, 1947):**

- **Assumption:** Cross-links are *not* pinned to the macroscopic strain; they execute Gaussian thermal fluctuations about their mean (affine) positions.
- **Chain behaviour:** The chains behave as if "phantom" — they occupy their mean affine positions but fluctuate around them.
- **Modulus prediction:**

$$
G_{\text{phantom}} = (\nu - \mu) R T
$$

where $\mu$ is the concentration of cross-links. For functionality $\phi$:

$$
G_{\text{phantom}} = \left( 1 - \frac{2}{\phi} \right) \nu R T = \xi \, \nu R T
$$

with $\xi$ being the *front factor*.

**Comparison for Tetrafunctional Network ($\phi = 4$):**

$$
\xi = 1 - \frac{2}{4} = \frac{1}{2}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,G_{\text{phantom}} = \tfrac{1}{2}\, G_{\text{affine}}\,}
$$

| Aspect | Affine ($\phi=4$) | Phantom ($\phi=4$) |
|---|---|---|
| Shear modulus | $\nu R T$ | $\tfrac{1}{2} \nu R T$ |
| Agreement with small-strain data | Predicts high modulus | Predicts low modulus |
| Agreement with large-strain data | Reasonable | Reasonable |
| Best at | Large strains | Small-to-moderate strains |

In practice, real elastomers fall *between* the two limits, which is why the **constrained junction model** (Flory, 1977) and **slip-link models** were developed. ∎

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**A cross-linked polyisoprene network is found to have a density $\rho = 920 \text{ kg m}^{-3}$ and number-average molecular weight between cross-links $M_c = 5000 \text{ g mol}^{-1}$. Calculate (i) the molar concentration of active chains $\nu$, (ii) the affine-network shear modulus at $300 \text{ K}$, and (iii) the phantom-network shear modulus for $\phi = 4$.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert units.** $\rho = 920 \text{ kg m}^{-3} = 0.920 \text{ g cm}^{-3}$; $M_c = 5000 \text{ g mol}^{-1}$.

**Step 2 — Molar concentration of active chains:**

The active network chain concentration $\nu$ (in mol per unit volume) is given by:

$$
\nu = \frac{\rho}{M_c}
$$

In SI units:

$$
\nu = \frac{920 \text{ kg m}^{-3}}{5.000 \text{ kg kmol}^{-1}} = 184 \text{ mol m}^{-3}
$$

(Alternatively in CGS: $\nu = 0.92 / 5000 \times 1000 = 184 \text{ mol m}^{-3}$.)

**Step 3 — Affine-network shear modulus at 300 K:**

$$
G_{\text{affine}} = \nu R T = 184 \times 8.314 \times 300
$$

$$
G_{\text{affine}} = 184 \times 2494.2 = 4.589 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} \approx 0.459 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 4 — Phantom-network shear modulus ($\phi = 4$):**

$$
G_{\text{phantom}} = \frac{1}{2} G_{\text{affine}} = \frac{1}{2} \times 4.589 \times 10^5 = 2.295 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} \approx 0.230 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 5 — Tabulated Results:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| $\nu$ | $184 \text{ mol m}^{-3}$ |
| $G_{\text{affine}}$ | $0.459 \text{ MPa}$ |
| $G_{\text{phantom}}$ ($\phi = 4$) | $0.230 \text{ MPa}$ |
| $E_{\text{affine}} = 3 G_{\text{affine}}$ | $1.377 \text{ MPa}$ |

**Engineering insight:** A real vulcanised polyisoprene tyre compound typically exhibits $G \approx 0.5\text{–}1.0 \text{ MPa}$, which is closer to the affine prediction. This suggests that in well-cured networks, *junction fluctuations* are partially suppressed by entanglements acting as temporary cross-links — the basis of the constrained-junction and slip-link refinements. ∎

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert #2:** Students often confuse $M_c$ (molecular weight *between* cross-links) with $M_n$ (number-average molecular weight of the *uncross-linked* precursor). In a cross-linked network, only $M_c$ matters for elasticity. Mis-substituting $M_n$ in place of $M_c$ leads to incorrect $\nu$ and a wrong modulus.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- **Rubber elasticity is entropic, not enthalpic.** Force arises from entropy reduction: $f = -T(\partial S/\partial L)_{T,V}$.
- **Modulus grows with temperature** (opposite of metals). Diagnostic signature: heat a rubber band under load — it shortens.
- **Gaussian chain** end-to-end distribution: $P(\mathbf{r}) \propto \exp(-3r^2 / 2\langle r^2\rangle_0)$.
- **Single-chain entropy loss:** $\Delta S_{\text{chain}} = -\tfrac{3 k_B}{2 \langle r^2\rangle_0} r^2$.
- **Gaussian network free energy (master equation):**

$$
\Delta A = \tfrac{1}{2} N k_B T (\lambda_1^2 + \lambda_2^2 + \lambda_3^2 - 3)
$$

- **Uniaxial engineering stress (affine, incompressible):**

$$
\sigma = \nu R T (\lambda - \lambda^{-2})
$$

- **Small-strain moduli:**

$$
E = 3\nu R T, \quad G = \nu R T, \quad \nu_{\text{Poisson}} = 0.5
$$

- **Chain concentration** from $M_c$: $\nu = \rho / M_c$ (consistent SI or CGS units).
- **Phantom vs Affine:** $G_{\text{phantom}} = (1 - 2/\phi)\, G_{\text{affine}}$; for $\phi = 4$: $G_{\text{phantom}} = \tfrac{1}{2} G_{\text{affine}}$.
- **Mooney–Rivlin semi-empirical form:** $\sigma = (2C_1 + 2C_2/\lambda)(\lambda - \lambda^{-2})$ captures real-network deviations.
- **Validity range:** $\lambda \lesssim \lambda_{\max} \approx \sqrt{N_b}$ (Gaussian regime); failure beyond due to finite chain extensibility.
- **Incompressibility constraint:** ALWAYS use $\lambda_1 \lambda_2 \lambda_3 = 1$ for rubber-like solids.
- **Units vigilance:** $\nu$ in mol/m³, $T$ in **kelvin**, $R = 8.314$ J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹, $G$ in Pa.
- **KTU must-memorize formulae (in priority order):** network free energy → uniaxial stress → $E = 3\nu R T$ → $G = \nu R T$ → phantom front factor.
- **Real-network corrections:** constrained junction model (Flory), slip-link model (Edwards), Mooney–Rivlin phenomenology — needed at extreme strains or for filled/crystallising elastomers.

> [!NOTE]
> **One-line mnemonic:** *"Rubber remembers shapes by chain configurations, not by bond stretching — its pull is entropy fighting back."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Molecular Requirements of Rubber-Like Elasticity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT402 - POLYMER PHYSICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Molecular Requirements of Rubber-Like Elasticity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 — Molecular Requirements of Rubber-Like Elasticity

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

**Rubber-like elasticity** is defined as the large, reversible, entropy-driven mechanical response of a polymeric network in which deformation occurs primarily through conformational rearrangement of long, flexible macromolecular chains rather than through changes in bond lengths or bond angles.

A material exhibits rubber-like elasticity only when its molecular architecture simultaneously satisfies **five stringent molecular requirements**. These requirements were formally consolidated from the classical statistical-mechanical theory of polymer networks (the **Flory–Wall–Treloar framework**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The question "*List and explain the molecular requirements of rubber-like elasticity*" is a **perennial favorite** in KTU examinations. It appears almost every cycle in either Part A (3 marks) or as a sub-part of a Part B question (7 marks).

### 1.2 The Intuitive Analogy — *The Slinky on a Trampoline*

Imagine a steel spring. When you pull it, you are stretching the **metallic bonds themselves** — internal energy ($U$) changes a lot, entropy ($S$) changes very little. The spring feels *stiff*, and pulling it is *energy storage*.

Now imagine a bucket full of identical Slinky toys (the long coiled spring toys) thrown into a mesh bag. Each Slinky is in a *randomly coiled, crumpled shape*. When you pull the bag, the Slinkies do not stretch internally — they merely **uncoil into more ordered, extended shapes**. The bond lengths and bond angles barely change. The energy stored is tiny, but the *number of possible coiled arrangements* drops sharply.

Pulling the bag requires almost no energy input, but the system *resists* because it has fewer ways to arrange itself. This resistance is **entropic**. Release the bag, and thermal jiggling ($k_B T$) spontaneously drives the chains back to their maximum-disorder (most coiled) state.

> This is exactly what happens in a **rubber band** — except the "Slinkies" are covalently cross-linked polymer chains and the "bag" is the **permanent 3-D network** produced by vulcanization.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Steel stores energy as **internal energy** ($U$-elasticity). Rubber stores energy as **entropy reduction** ($S$-elasticity). Heating a rubber band makes it **contract** — proof that its elasticity is entropic! Heating increases $T$, and the elastic force $f \propto T$ (at constant length) increases. This is the *opposite* of metallic behavior.

### 1.3 The Five Molecular Requirements

For a polymer to behave as an elastomer, the following molecular conditions are **necessary and sufficient**:

| # | Requirement | Physical Meaning |
|---|-------------|------------------|
| 1 | **Long-chain polymeric structure** | Polymer must be a high-molar-mass chain made of many repeating units |
| 2 | **Chain flexibility** | Rotational freedom around backbone bonds (low rotational energy barrier) |
| 3 | **Weak secondary intermolecular forces** | Van der Waals forces — but strong enough to prevent cold flow |
| 4 | **Permanent network structure (cross-links)** | Sparse chemical or physical cross-links (e.g., sulfur bridges) |
| 5 | **Ability to crystallize on stretching (optional, but enhances strength)** | Strain-induced crystallization provides self-reinforcement |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic — "LFCNC":** **L**ong chains, **F**lexible backbone, **C**ross-linked, **N**o strong secondary bonds, **C**rystallizable on strain.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Requirement 1 — Long-Chain Polymeric Structure

A polymer must be composed of a *very large* number of repeat units ($n \gg 100$) so that the contour length $L_c = n \cdot l$ (where $l$ is the bond length) is many times the effective chain segment length.

**Why is this critical?**
- The **mean-square end-to-end distance** of a Gaussian chain is $\langle r^2 \rangle = C_\infty \, n \, l^2$, where $C_\infty$ is the characteristic ratio.
- For the **Gaussian approximation** (and therefore the **entropy elasticity theory**) to be valid, the chain must consist of **enough statistical segments** to obey random-walk statistics.
- Short chains (oligomers, $n < 50$) cannot sustain rubber-like elasticity because finite-chain extensibility effects dominate.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse "long chain" with "high molecular weight" alone. The *real* requirement is **long chain length** in terms of **number of freely rotating bonds**, not merely $\bar{M}_w$. A polymer with stiff repeat units fails Requirement 1 even if $M$ is high.

## 2.2 Requirement 2 — Chain Flexibility (Low Rotational Energy Barrier)

Each backbone bond must be able to rotate (or oscillate) freely between several rotational isomeric states — *trans* and *gauche* conformations.

**Quantitative measures of flexibility:**
- **Characteristic ratio** $C_\infty = \langle r^2 \rangle_0 / (n l^2)$ — a measure of chain stiffness.
- **Persistence length** $a$ — the length over which the chain "remembers" its initial direction.
- **Rotational energy barrier** $E_a$ — the energy required for bond rotation (typical: $4$–$12$ kJ/mol for C–C bonds).

> A flexible chain has low $C_\infty$ (typically $5$–$10$) and short persistence length (typically $0.5$–$1$ nm).

**Consequence:** Only flexible chains can rapidly change conformation under thermal motion, which is the fundamental mechanism of rubber elasticity.

## 2.3 Requirement 3 — Weak Intermolecular Secondary Forces

The intermolecular forces between polymer chains must be **weak (van der Waals type)** — strong enough to hold the material together as a solid, but weak enough to permit chain mobility and conformational rearrangements.

**Why not strong forces?**
- Strong forces (e.g., hydrogen bonds as in polyamides, or dipole interactions) → polymer behaves as a **rigid glassy solid**, not an elastomer.
- Forces must be such that the **glass transition temperature** $T_g$ is well below the use temperature: $T_g < T_{\text{use}}$.

**Comparison with metals:** Metallic bonds in steel are *strong* and *directional* — they resist stretching, and elasticity is energetic.

## 2.4 Requirement 4 — Permanent Network (Cross-Linking) — *The Most Critical Requirement*

Cross-links are **permanent chemical junctions** (covalent bonds) or strong physical junctions (crystallites, entanglements) that connect neighboring chains.

**Key features:**
- Cross-links **prevent viscous flow** and permanent plastic deformation.
- They convert a viscous polymer melt/liquid into a **thermodynamically stable elastic network**.
- The classical example is **vulcanization** of natural rubber with sulfur (Charles Goodyear, 1839), which introduces $\sim 1$–$3$ sulfur bridges per 100 backbone carbons.

**Quantitative descriptor:** $\nu_e$ = number of elastically active network chains per unit volume (mol/m³).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Cross-links must be **sparse**, not dense. If cross-link density is too high, the network becomes a rigid thermoset (e.g., fully cured epoxy). A *rubber* has $\nu_e$ in the range $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$ mol/cm³ — roughly 1 cross-link per 100–500 repeat units.

## 2.5 Requirement 5 — Strain-Induced Crystallization (Self-Reinforcement)

Although not strictly required for rubber-like elasticity, this property is found in natural rubber, synthetic polyisoprene, and some other elastomers.

**Mechanism:** When stretched, the polymer chains align and partially crystallize, dramatically increasing the modulus and tensile strength. Upon release, the crystals melt and the rubber returns to its original amorphous state.

**Engineering significance:** This is why natural rubber is preferred over many synthetic rubbers for demanding applications (tire treads, high-stress components).

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in nearly every KTU Polymer Physics question paper.**

| # | Equation | Physical Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|----------|------------------|---------------|
| 1 | $\Delta S = -\tfrac{1}{2} \, \nu_e \, k \, (\lambda_1^2 + \lambda_2^2 + \lambda_3^2 - 3)$ | Entropy change on deformation | J/K |
| 2 | $\Delta A = \tfrac{1}{2} \, \nu_e \, k \, T \, (\lambda_1^2 + \lambda_2^2 + \lambda_3^2 - 3)$ | Helmholtz free-energy change | J |
| 3 | $f = \nu_e \, k \, T \, (\lambda - \lambda^{-2})$ | Tensile force (uniaxial extension) | N |
| 4 | $f = \nu_e \, k \, T \, (\alpha - \alpha^{-2})$ | Same as above (with $\alpha$ instead of $\lambda$) | N |
| 5 | $E = 3 \, \nu_e \, k \, T$ | Young's modulus (small strain) | Pa |
| 6 | $G = \nu_e \, k \, T$ | Shear modulus (small strain) | Pa |
| 7 | $\sigma = G (\lambda - \lambda^{-2})$ | Engineering stress–stretch relation | Pa |
| 8 | $M_c = \rho \, R \, T / G$ | Molar mass between cross-links | kg/mol |
| 9 | $\lambda = L / L_0$ | Extension ratio (stretch) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $W = \tfrac{1}{2} G (\lambda_1^2 + \lambda_2^2 + \lambda_3^2 - 3)$ | Strain-energy density | J/m³ |

**Where:**
- $\nu_e$ = number of network chains per unit volume (chains/m³)
- $k$ = Boltzmann constant = **$1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K**
- $R$ = Universal gas constant = **$8.314$ J/(mol·K)**
- $\lambda$ = extension ratio (also written as $\alpha$)
- $G$ = shear modulus
- $E$ = Young's modulus
- $\rho$ = polymer density
- $M_c$ = average molar mass of chain between cross-links

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical LaTeX Tip for Tables:** Note how absolute-value and ratio expressions are written using $\lambda - \lambda^{-2}$, not $\vert \lambda - 1/\lambda^2 \vert$. Never use bare vertical pipes inside a markdown table — they break the column syntax.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Tire industry** (natural rubber, SBR) — uses cross-link density and strain-induced crystallization.
- **Biomedical elastomers** (silicone, polyurethane) — designed for low modulus ($G \sim 0.1$–$1$ MPa) and high elongation ($\lambda \sim 5$–$10$).
- **Vibration dampers** — require tailored $\tan \delta$ and modulus via controlled $\nu_e$.
- **Smart actuators** (dielectric elastomers) — exploit the equation $f = \nu_e k T (\lambda - \lambda^{-2})$ to design muscle-like response.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Equation of State for Ideal Rubber Elasticity

**Goal:** Derive the relationship between tensile force $f$, temperature $T$, and extension ratio $\lambda$ for a single network chain of a cross-linked elastomer.

### Step 1 — Single-Chain Conformation: The Gaussian Chain

For a polymer chain consisting of $n$ freely-jointed statistical segments of length $l$, the probability of finding the chain with end-to-end vector $\vec{r}$ in a small volume element $d^3 r$ is given by the **Gaussian distribution**:

$$P(\vec{r}) = \left( \frac{3}{2 \pi n l^2} \right)^{3/2} \exp\left( -\frac{3 r^2}{2 n l^2} \right)$$

The number of conformations producing the end-to-end distance $r$ is proportional to this probability.

### Step 2 — Boltzmann Entropy of a Single Chain

The entropy of a single chain with end-to-end distance $r$ is, by Boltzmann's relation:

$$S_{\text{chain}} = k \ln P(\vec{r}) = k \ln C - \frac{3 k r^2}{2 n l^2}$$

where $C$ is the normalization constant. The *change* in entropy when the chain end is moved from $r$ to a deformed state is:

$$\Delta S_{\text{chain}} = S(r) - S(0) = -\frac{3 k r^2}{2 n l^2}$$

### Step 3 — Affine Network Deformation Assumption

Assume the network deforms **affinely** — that is, the macroscopic deformation ratios $\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \lambda_3$ are applied directly to the chain end-to-end vectors. If the undeformed mean-square end-to-end distance is $\langle r^2 \rangle_0$, then in the deformed state:

$$\langle r^2 \rangle = \lambda_1^2 \langle r_1^2 \rangle_0 + \lambda_2^2 \langle r_2^2 \rangle_0 + \lambda_3^2 \langle r_3^2 \rangle_0$$

For an isotropic undeformed network, $\langle r_1^2 \rangle_0 = \langle r_2^2 \rangle_0 = \langle r_3^2 \rangle_0 = \tfrac{1}{3} \langle r^2 \rangle_0$, giving:

$$\langle r^2 \rangle = \frac{1}{3} \langle r^2 \rangle_0 \left( \lambda_1^2 + \lambda_2^2 + \lambda_3^2 \right)$$

### Step 4 — Entropy Change of the Network

With $\nu_e$ network chains per unit volume, the total entropy change per unit volume is:

$$\Delta S = \nu_e \cdot \Delta S_{\text{chain}} = -\frac{1}{2} \nu_e k \left( \lambda_1^2 + \lambda_2^2 + \lambda_3^2 - 3 \right)$$

The "$-3$" comes from the undeformed state where all $\lambda_i = 1$.

> **Logic:** Each chain loses entropy when stretched. The network, containing $\nu_e$ such chains per unit volume, suffers a total entropy penalty proportional to the mean-square deformation.

### Step 5 — Helmholtz Free Energy of the Network

Assuming the internal energy of deformation is negligible ($U \approx 0$, i.e., **ideally elastic rubber**), the Helmholtz free energy per unit volume is:

$$\Delta A = \Delta U - T \Delta S = 0 - T \Delta S = \frac{1}{2} \nu_e k T \left( \lambda_1^2 + \lambda_2^2 + \lambda_3^2 - 3 \right)$$

This is the **classical strain-energy function** for an ideal rubber network (neo-Hookean model).

### Step 6 — Force in Uniaxial Extension

For an incompressible rubber ($\lambda_1 \lambda_2 \lambda_3 = 1$, Poisson's ratio $\nu \approx 0.5$), uniaxial extension along direction 1 gives $\lambda_1 = \lambda$ and $\lambda_2 = \lambda_3 = \lambda^{-1/2}$. Substituting:

$$\Delta A = \frac{1}{2} \nu_e k T \left( \lambda^2 + \frac{2}{\lambda} - 3 \right)$$

The tensile force $f$ at constant $T$ and $V$ is:

$$f = \left( \frac{\partial A}{\partial L} \right)_{T,V} = \left( \frac{\partial A}{\partial \lambda} \right)_{T,V} \cdot \frac{\partial \lambda}{\partial L} = \frac{1}{L_0} \frac{\partial A}{\partial \lambda}$$

Computing the derivative:

$$\frac{\partial A}{\partial \lambda} = \frac{1}{2} \nu_e k T \left( 2\lambda - \frac{2}{\lambda^2} \right) = \nu_e k T \left( \lambda - \lambda^{-2} \right)$$

Therefore:

$$f = \frac{\nu_e k T \left( \lambda - \lambda^{-2} \right)}{L_0} \cdot \frac{1}{1} \;\;\;\Rightarrow\;\;\; f = \frac{\nu_e k T}{L_0} \left( \lambda - \lambda^{-2} \right)$$

For a unit cross-section, defining $\sigma = f/A_0$ and $G = \nu_e k T / L_0 \cdot L_0$ (simplification using $G = \nu_e k T$ in the Gaussian limit), we obtain the celebrated **equation of state for rubber elasticity**:

$$\boxed{\;\; \sigma = G \left( \lambda - \lambda^{-2} \right) \;\;}$$

or equivalently:

$$\boxed{\;\; f = \nu_e k T \left( \lambda - \lambda^{-2} \right) \;\;}$$

### Step 7 — Special Case: Small Strains (Linear Elastic Limit)

For small deformations, $\lambda \approx 1 + \epsilon$ with $\epsilon \ll 1$. Using Taylor expansion:

$$\lambda - \lambda^{-2} \approx 3\epsilon$$

Therefore:

$$\sigma \approx 3 G \epsilon \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; E = 3G$$

This is the famous **$E = 3G$ relation** for incompressible rubbers, corresponding to a Poisson's ratio of $0.5$.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Identifying the Origin of Each Term

| Equation Term | Statistical Origin | Physical Meaning |
|---------------|---------------------|------------------|
| $\nu_e$ | Network topology (chemistry of cross-linking) | Number of elastic chains per unit volume |
| $k$ | Fundamental constant | Boltzmann constant |
| $T$ | Thermal energy reservoir | Drives conformational motion |
| $\lambda$ | Macroscopic deformation | Affine mapping of chain vectors |
| $\lambda^{-2}$ | Incompressibility constraint ($\lambda_2 \lambda_3 = \lambda^{-1}$) | Lateral chain compression |

> This matrix is a **KTU-favorite** framework question. Memorize the **physical origin of each term** for full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Architecture of the Five Molecular Requirements

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[POLYMER FOR ELASTIC BEHAVIOR]:::top
    A --> B[1. LONG CHAIN STRUCTURE]:::req
    A --> C[2. CHAIN FLEXIBILITY]:::req
    A --> D[3. WEAK INTERMOLECULAR FORCES]:::req
    A --> E[4. PERMANENT NETWORK CROSS-LINKS]:::req
    A --> F[5. STRAIN INDUCED CRYSTALLIZATION]:::req

    B --> B1["High molar mass, n greater than 100 repeat units, Gaussian statistics valid"]:::detail
    C --> C1["Low rotational energy barrier, trans and gauche conformations, small C infinity"]:::detail
    D --> D1["Van der Waals forces, Tg well below use temperature, no H bonds or strong dipoles"]:::detail
    E --> E1["Sulfur vulcanization, nu e around 10 to power minus 4 mol per cm cubed, prevents flow"]:::detail
    F --> F1["Natural rubber polyisoprene, alignment under tension, self reinforcement"]:::detail

    B1 --> G[ENTROPIC ELASTICITY]:::result
    C1 --> G
    D1 --> G
    E1 --> G
    F1 --> G
    G --> G1["Stress equals G times lambda minus lambda to power minus 2"]:::result

    classDef top fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef req fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef detail fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#DAA520,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef result fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Cross-Linked Network — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Undeformed_State
        direction TB
        C1[Chain A]:::chain
        C2[Chain B]:::chain
        C3[Chain C]:::chain
        X1((Cross-link 1)):::xlink
        X2((Cross-link 2)):::xlink
        X3((Cross-link 3)):::xlink
        C1 --- X1
        C1 --- X2
        C2 --- X2
        C2 --- X3
        C3 --- X3
        C3 --- X1
    end

    subgraph Deformed_State
        direction TB
        D1[Chain A stretched]:::chainD
        D2[Chain B aligned]:::chainD
        D3[Chain C extended]:::chainD
        Y1((XL1)):::xlinkD
        Y2((XL2)):::xlinkD
        Y3((XL3)):::xlinkD
        D1 --- Y1
        D1 --- Y2
        D2 --- Y2
        D2 --- Y3
        D3 --- Y3
        D3 --- Y1
    end

    Undeformed_State -- Affine deformation with extension ratio lambda --> Deformed_State

    classDef chain fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef chainD fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef xlink fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef xlinkD fill:#9370DB,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Energy & Entropy Flow During Deformation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M[Macroscopic strain, lambda applied]:::input
    M --> N[Network chain vector mapped affinely]:::process
    N --> O[End to end distance increases from r0 to r]:::process
    O --> P[Conformations available decreases]:::process
    P --> Q[Entropy decreases, delta S negative]:::process
    Q --> R[Free energy increases, delta A positive]:::process
    R --> S[Tensile force f generated to resist deformation]:::output

    T[Thermal bath, temperature T]:::thermal
    T -.-> N
    T -.-> P
    T -.-> Q

    classDef input fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef process fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#BDB76B,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#FF4500,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef thermal fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#696969,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Visualization Tip:** When asked to "sketch a rubber network" in the exam, always draw **three or more chains connected by 2–3 cross-link points** in (a) undeformed (random coils) and (b) deformed (aligned) states. Label the cross-links and the chains. This single diagram easily fetches 2–3 marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model Question Paper Set B]**
*List the molecular requirements for a polymer to exhibit rubber-like elasticity. Explain any two in brief.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — typical word limit: 80–100 words):**

A polymer must satisfy the following molecular requirements to show rubber-like elasticity:

1. **Long-chain structure** — the chain must consist of a large number of repeating units so that the mean-square end-to-end distance is large and Gaussian statistics are obeyed.
2. **Chain flexibility** — free rotation about backbone bonds allows rapid conformational changes.
3. **Weak secondary intermolecular forces** — van der Waals forces that are strong enough to hold the chains together but weak enough to permit mobility.
4. **Permanent network structure** — sparse chemical cross-links (e.g., sulfur bridges) to prevent viscous flow.
5. **Strain-induced crystallization (optional)** — provides additional strength on stretching.

> *For 2-mark elaboration: pick "chain flexibility" and "permanent cross-linking" and explain how each contributes to reversible elastic behavior.*

**Marking Distribution:** 1 mark for the list (at least 4 of 5) + 1 mark each for two explanations.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Repeated Question]**
*Rubber elasticity is primarily entropic in origin. Justify this statement.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The restoring force in a deformed rubber arises from the tendency of polymer chains to return to their maximum-disorder (maximum-entropy) coiled state, not from bond distortion. When stretched, the end-to-end distance of chains increases, reducing the number of accessible conformations $\Omega$ and hence reducing the entropy $S = k \ln \Omega$. The Helmholtz free energy increases as $\Delta A = -T \Delta S > 0$, generating a tensile force:

$$f = \nu_e k T \left( \lambda - \lambda^{-2} \right)$$

The proportionality of $f$ to $T$ (at constant $\lambda$) is direct evidence of entropic origin. Furthermore, heating a stretched rubber band causes it to **contract** — a thermodynamic signature unique to entropy-driven elasticity.

**Marking Distribution:** 1 mark for entropy reduction concept + 1 mark for equation + 1 mark for $T$-dependence evidence.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Do not confuse** "molecular requirements" with "properties of elastomers." The question asks for the **structural / architectural conditions** (chain length, flexibility, cross-links, etc.), not mechanical properties (high elongation, low modulus).
> - **Do not omit** the assumption that internal energy change $\Delta U \approx 0$ when deriving the equation of state. Examiners allocate 1 mark specifically for this.
> - **Do not skip the boundary condition**: in the derivation, when $\lambda = 1$ (no deformation), $\Delta S = 0$. This must be checked explicitly.
> - Always state the **Gaussian chain assumption** before writing the entropy equation.

---

### **Part B — Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Modified Past Paper]**

**(a)** Derive the equation of state for an ideal rubber network, $\sigma = G(\lambda - \lambda^{-2})$, starting from the Gaussian chain statistics. State clearly the assumptions used. (7 marks)

**(b)** A sample of natural rubber has a shear modulus of $0.40$ MPa at $25^{\circ}$C. Calculate the tensile force required to stretch a strip of cross-sectional area $4.0 \text{ mm} \times 2.0 \text{ mm}$ to twice its original length. (7 marks)

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

1. **Gaussian chain probability** (1 mark):
$$P(\vec{r}) = \left( \frac{3}{2 \pi n l^2} \right)^{3/2} \exp\left( -\frac{3 r^2}{2 n l^2} \right)$$

2. **Single-chain entropy** via Boltzmann (1 mark):
$$\Delta S_{\text{chain}} = -\frac{3 k r^2}{2 n l^2}$$

3. **Affine network deformation** — chain vectors scale with $\lambda_i$ (1 mark):
$$\langle r^2 \rangle = \frac{1}{3} \langle r^2 \rangle_0 \left( \lambda_1^2 + \lambda_2^2 + \lambda_3^2 \right)$$

4. **Total entropy change per unit volume** (1 mark):
$$\Delta S = -\frac{1}{2} \nu_e k \left( \lambda_1^2 + \lambda_2^2 + \lambda_3^2 - 3 \right)$$

5. **Free energy** assuming $\Delta U = 0$ (1 mark):
$$\Delta A = \tfrac{1}{2} \nu_e k T \left( \lambda_1^2 + \lambda_2^2 + \lambda_3^2 - 3 \right)$$

6. **Uniaxial extension** with $\lambda_1 = \lambda$, $\lambda_2 = \lambda_3 = \lambda^{-1/2}$ (1 mark):
$$\Delta A = \tfrac{1}{2} \nu_e k T \left( \lambda^2 + \frac{2}{\lambda} - 3 \right)$$

7. **Differentiating to get force**, identifying $G = \nu_e k T$ (1 mark):
$$\sigma = G \left( \lambda - \lambda^{-2} \right)$$

**Key Assumptions to be stated:**
- Gaussian chain statistics (large $n$).
- Affine deformation of network junctions.
- Incompressibility ($\lambda_1 \lambda_2 \lambda_3 = 1$).
- Internal energy of deformation $\Delta U \approx 0$.
- No chain entanglements or topological constraints (phantom network limit).

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $G = 0.40 \text{ MPa} = 0.40 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$
- $T = 25^{\circ}\text{C} = 298 \text{ K}$
- $A_0 = 4.0 \text{ mm} \times 2.0 \text{ mm} = 8.0 \text{ mm}^2 = 8.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2$
- $\lambda = 2.0$

**Find:** Tensile force $f$.

**Step 1 — Compute the deformation function** (1 mark):
$$\lambda - \lambda^{-2} = 2.0 - \frac{1}{(2.0)^2} = 2.0 - 0.25 = 1.75$$

**Step 2 — Compute the engineering stress** (2 marks):
$$\sigma = G \left( \lambda - \lambda^{-2} \right) = (0.40 \times 10^6) \times 1.75 = 0.70 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 0.70 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the tensile force** (2 marks):
$$f = \sigma \cdot A_0 = (0.70 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}) \times (8.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2)$$
$$f = 5.6 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4 — Final answer with units** (1 mark): $\boxed{f = 5.6 \text{ N}}$

**Step 5 — Verification** (1 mark): At $\lambda = 2$, the network is moderately deformed (still in the Gaussian regime), so the neo-Hookean equation is valid. Units: $[\text{Pa}] \times [\text{m}^2] = [\text{N}]$ — consistent.

---

### **Part B — Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified Past Paper]**

**(a)** Discuss the molecular requirements for rubber-like elasticity in detail. How does the absence of any one requirement affect the elastic behavior of the polymer? (7 marks)

**(b)** Derive an expression for the shear modulus $G$ of an ideal rubber network in terms of the molar mass between cross-links $M_c$, the density $\rho$, the universal gas constant $R$, and the temperature $T$. A vulcanized rubber sample has $\rho = 950 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $T = 300 \text{ K}$, and $M_c = 5000 \text{ g/mol}$. Calculate its shear modulus. (7 marks)

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Molecular Requirements Discussion (7 marks):**

| # | Requirement | Consequence of Absence |
|---|-------------|------------------------|
| 1 | **Long-chain structure** | Short chains cannot undergo large conformational rearrangements; the material behaves as a **brittle solid or waxy solid** (e.g., polyethylene oligomers). |
| 2 | **Chain flexibility** | Stiff chains (e.g., cellulose, aromatic polyamides) form **rigid glasses**; they cannot respond to deformation via entropic uncoiling. |
| 3 | **Weak intermolecular forces** | Strong forces (H-bonds, dipoles) raise $T_g$ above use temperature → **rigid, glassy, or semicrystalline solid** (e.g., Nylon 6,6). |
| 4 | **Permanent cross-links** | Without cross-links, the polymer **flows plastically** under load (e.g., uncross-linked polyisoprene is a viscous liquid). |
| 5 | **Strain-induced crystallization** | Without it, tensile strength and tear resistance are reduced; the elastomer is "softer" (e.g., SBR vs. natural rubber). |

**Distribution of marks:** 5 × 1 mark for stating each requirement with its consequence, plus 2 marks for a coherent summary statement that links the five requirements to the entropic elasticity equation.

**Concluding 2-mark synthesis statement:** *It is the simultaneous coexistence of all five requirements that produces the unique entropic, large-deformation, fully-recoverable elastic response captured by the equation of state $\sigma = G(\lambda - \lambda^{-2})$.*

---

**Part (b) — Derivation of $G$ in terms of $M_c$ (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Define $G$ in terms of $\nu_e$** (1 mark):
$$G = \nu_e k T$$

**Step 2 — Convert $\nu_e$ from number per volume to moles per volume** (1 mark):
$$\nu_e \text{ (chains/m}^3) = \frac{\rho \, N_A}{M_c}$$

where $N_A$ is Avogadro's number, $\rho$ is the polymer density (kg/m³), and $M_c$ is the average molar mass of a chain between cross-links (kg/mol).

**Step 3 — Substitute into $G$** (1 mark):
$$G = \frac{\rho \, N_A \, k \, T}{M_c} = \frac{\rho \, R \, T}{M_c}$$

since $R = N_A \, k$.

**Step 4 — Final expression** (1 mark):
$$\boxed{\; G = \frac{\rho \, R \, T}{M_c} \;}$$

This is the **Flory–Rehner equation** in its simple form for an ideal network.

---

**Numerical Calculation (3 marks):**

**Given:**
- $\rho = 950 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$
- $T = 300 \text{ K}$
- $M_c = 5000 \text{ g/mol} = 5.0 \text{ kg/mol}$

**Step 5 — Substitute values** (1 mark):
$$G = \frac{(950) \times (8.314) \times (300)}{5.0}$$

**Step 6 — Compute numerator** (1 mark):
$$950 \times 8.314 = 7898.3$$
$$7898.3 \times 300 = 2{,}369{,}490 \text{ J/m}^3$$

**Step 7 — Divide by $M_c$** (1 mark):
$$G = \frac{2{,}369{,}490}{5.0} = 473{,}898 \text{ Pa} \approx 0.474 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\boxed{\; G \approx 0.474 \text{ MPa} \;}$$

**Sanity check:** This value is typical for a soft vulcanized rubber — consistent with the given data. ✓

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Memorize Before Exam**

- ☐ **5 Molecular Requirements — LFCNC**: Long chains, Flexible backbone, Cross-linked, No strong secondary bonds, Crystallizable on strain.
- ☐ Rubber elasticity is **entropic**, not energetic (opposite of metals).
- ☐ Master equation: $\sigma = G(\lambda - \lambda^{-2})$.
- ☐ Shear modulus: $G = \nu_e k T = \rho R T / M_c$.
- ☐ Young's modulus for small strain: $E = 3G$ (because $\nu = 0.5$ for rubbers).
- ☐ Stress in rubber is **proportional to absolute temperature** $T$ — direct proof of entropic origin.
- ☐ Incompressibility: $\lambda_1 \lambda_2 \lambda_3 = 1$.
- ☐ Strain-energy density (neo-Hookean): $W = \tfrac{1}{2} G (\lambda_1^2 + \lambda_2^2 + \lambda_3^2 - 3)$.
- ☐ Heating a stretched rubber band → it **contracts** (Le Chatelier's principle applied to entropy).
- ☐ Vulcanization (Goodyear, 1839) introduces **sulfur cross-links**, $\sim 1$–$3$ per 100 backbone carbons.
- ☐ Natural rubber (polyisoprene) shows **strain-induced crystallization** → excellent mechanical strength.
- ☐ Gaussian assumption requires **large $n$**; for very large $\lambda$ ($> 5$), use non-Gaussian (Langevin) corrections.
- ☐ Cross-link density **too high** → rigid thermoset; **too low** → viscous flow. *Sweet spot*: $\nu_e \sim 10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$ mol/cm³.
- ☐ Equation of state is also called the **neo-Hookean model** (after the Hooke's law analogy in finite-strain form).

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Tip:** If the exam asks "*Why does a rubber band contract on heating?*", the three-step answer is:
> 1. The elastic force is $f = \nu_e k T (\lambda - \lambda^{-2})$.
> 2. At constant $\lambda$, increasing $T$ increases $f$.
> 3. To restore equilibrium, the band must decrease its $\lambda$ (i.e., contract), thereby reducing the $(\lambda - \lambda^{-2})$ term.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gough-Joule Effect
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT402 - POLYMER PHYSICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Gough-Joule Effect

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔬 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Gough–Joule Effect** is a fundamental thermodynamic phenomenon observed in elastomeric (rubber-like) materials, describing the **adiabatic temperature change** that occurs when a stretched elastomer undergoes a rapid change in mechanical tension. Specifically:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCPOT402 – Module 4):**
> The **Gough–Joule Effect** is the reversible heating of a rubber sample when it is suddenly stretched under adiabatic conditions, and conversely, the cooling that occurs when the stretching force is suddenly released. It is the classical macroscopic experimental proof of the **entropic origin of rubber elasticity** — i.e., the retractive force in a stretched elastomer arises primarily from a **decrease in configurational entropy**, not from a change in internal energy.

The effect is named after two pioneering scientists:
- **John Gough (1802)** — English natural philosopher who first observed the phenomenon qualitatively.
- **James Prescott Joule (1859)** — who performed the first quantitative thermodynamic measurements on stretched rubber.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **The Rubber Band Thermometer Analogy 🎯**
> Take a thick rubber band and press it firmly against your upper lip (a heat-sensitive area). Now stretch it quickly to about 5–6 times its original length and hold it. You will distinctly feel it **become warm**. Release the tension suddenly — you will feel it **become cool**. This is the Gough–Joule Effect happening in real-time. The heat is *not* due to friction; it is the conversion of mechanical ordering work into thermal kinetic energy of the polymer chains.

**Plain-English interpretation:**
- A rubber band is a tangle of long, coiled polymer chains. In its relaxed state, the chains have **maximum disorder** (maximum entropy).
- When you **stretch** it rapidly (adiabatically — no time for heat exchange with surroundings), the chains are forced to **align and order**, reducing entropy.
- Since entropy cannot spontaneously decrease in an isolated system, the *thermal* energy of the chains **rises** to "compensate" → the rubber gets **hot**.
- When you **release** the tension, the chains snap back to disordered conformations, entropy increases, and the rubber pulls thermal energy from itself → it feels **cool**.

## 1.3 Key Physical Quantities & Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | Typical Value (Natural Rubber) | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Linear thermal expansion coefficient | α | ≈ **+ 6.2 × 10⁻⁴** | K⁻¹ (positive for stretched rubber at low elongation) |
| Force-temperature coefficient | (∂f/∂T)ₗ | **negative** at low strain | N·K⁻¹ |
| Heat capacity at constant length | Cₗ | ≈ **1.9** | kJ·kg⁻¹·K⁻¹ |
| Heat capacity at constant tension | Cf | ≈ **2.1** | kJ·kg⁻¹·K⁻¹ |
| Adiabatic temperature rise on stretching | ΔT | **+5 to +15** | °C (for 600% elongation) |
| Boltzmann constant | k | **1.381 × 10⁻²³** | J·K⁻¹ |
| Avogadro's number | Nₐ | **6.022 × 10²³** | mol⁻¹ |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for exams:** *"Stretch = Sizzle, Snap = Shiver"* — the rubber heats up when stretched and cools when released.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📐 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Physical Logic

The Gough–Joule Effect is the **direct macroscopic evidence** of the thermodynamic identity describing rubber elasticity. The reasoning chain is as follows:

1. **Step 1 — Rubber is NOT an ideal Hookean solid.** It does not store mechanical energy as strain energy in bond stretching; instead, it stores it as **decreased conformational entropy** of the polymer chains.

2. **Step 2 — The First Law applied to stretching.** When a rubber band of length L is held at tension f, the fundamental thermodynamic identity reads:
   dU = T dS + f dL + μ dN (at constant composition)
   The Helmholtz free energy A = U − TS gives: dA = −S dT + f dL.

3. **Step 3 — The Maxwell-type thermodynamic relation.** From dA = −S dT + f dL, the cross-partial derivative identity yields:
   (∂S/∂L)ₜ = −(∂f/∂T)ₗ

4. **Step 4 — Joule's classical finding (1859).** Joule measured the tension of a rubber band at various temperatures and showed that for **small elongations**, (∂U/∂L)ₜ ≈ 0. The tension f is therefore **purely entropic**:
   f = −T (∂S/∂L)ₜ

5. **Step 5 — Adiabatic stretching (the Gough side).** When stretching is performed rapidly, the process is adiabatic (dQ = 0). Since stretching reduces entropy (dS < 0) and dU = f dL for adiabatic work, the internal energy must rise — manifested as a **temperature increase**.

6. **Step 6 — Thermoelastic Inversion.** At very high elongations (>300%), the sign of (∂S/∂L)ₜ reverses because of strain-induced crystallization, and the rubber *cools* upon further stretching — a phenomenon called **thermoelastic inversion**.

## 2.2 The Molecular Picture (Why it happens)

- **Relaxed state:** Polymer chains adopt a **random coil** conformation (Gaussian chain statistics). The number of accessible microstates Ω is enormous → S = k ln Ω is **maximum**.

- **Stretched state:** Chains are forced into **elongated, aligned conformations**. The number of accessible microstates **drops dramatically** → S **decreases**.

- **Adiabatic constraint:** No heat exchange with surroundings, so dS_total = dS_chain + dS_lattice = 0. The lost configurational entropy is **transferred into vibrational/phonon entropy** of the lattice → the sample **heats up**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" in one line:** Rubber elasticity is **entropic**, not energetic — when you stretch rubber, you reduce the number of ways the chains can be arranged, and the lost order is paid for by an increase in thermal energy (temperature).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield, Exam-Ready)

> [!NOTE]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Note:** Memorize the three boxed relations below — at least one will appear in every KTU Polymer Physics ESE paper under Module 4.

$$
\boxed{\;\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V} = f - T\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial T}\right)_{L,V}\;}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial L}\right)_{T} = -\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial T}\right)_{L}\;}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial L}\right)_{S} = -\frac{T}{C_{L}}\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial L}\right)_{T} = \frac{T}{C_{L}}\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial T}\right)_{L}\;}
$$

| # | Equation | Name / Physical Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | f = (∂U/∂L)ₜ + T(∂f/∂T)ₗ | **Fundamental thermodynamic tension equation** | N |
| 2 | (∂U/∂L)ₜ = 0 | **Joule's result for ideal rubber** (energy contribution vanishes) | J·m⁻¹ |
| 3 | f = −T (∂S/∂L)ₜ | **Purely entropic tension** for ideal rubber | N |
| 4 | (∂T/∂L)ₛ = (T/Cₗ)(∂f/∂T)ₗ | **Adiabatic temperature change on stretching** (Gough–Joule) | K·m⁻¹ |
| 5 | ΔT = (T f / Cₗ) (1 − α·T) | Approximate ΔT for moderate strain (Treloar form) | K |
| 6 | f = NkT (⟨r²⟩ − ⟨r²⟩₀) / ⟨r²⟩₀ | **Ideal elastic force** for Gaussian network (N = chain density) | N |
| 7 | f = ν kT (λ − λ⁻²) | **Non-Gaussian affine network model** (ν = crosslink density, λ = stretch ratio) | N·m⁻² |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart actuators & artificial muscles:** The Gough–Joule effect is the working principle of **thermal rubber actuators** (e.g., McKibben air muscles with elastomeric sleeves) used in soft robotics.
- **Industrial tire design:** Heat generation due to cyclic stretching under adiabatic conditions is critical for **tire rolling resistance and heat build-up** — a major design parameter in KTU-validated industry standards.
- **Biomedical devices:** Used in **thermoresponsive stents, soft grippers**, and **elastomer-based microfluidic valves**.
- **KTU 2024 Relevance:** Directly linked to **Module 4 – Mechanical Properties of Polymers** and the **entropy-elasticity** learning outcome.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Entropic Tension of an Ideal Rubber

We start from the **statistical mechanics of a Gaussian chain** in a polymer network. For a single network chain of n freely-jointed segments each of length ℓ, the mean-square end-to-end distance in the unperturbed state is:

$$
\langle r^{2} \rangle_{0} = n \ell^{2}
$$

**Step 1:** The configurational entropy of a single chain (from Boltzmann's relation S = k ln Ω) is approximated as:

$$
s = k \ln \Omega(r) = -\frac{3 k}{2} \cdot \frac{r^{2}}{\langle r^{2} \rangle_{0}} + \text{const}
$$

**Textual Logic:** The Gaussian distribution falls off as exp(−3r²/2⟨r²⟩₀), so its logarithm gives the linear r² dependence above. The negative sign indicates that entropy is maximum at r = 0 and decreases as the chain is stretched.

**Step 2:** The total entropy of the network (with N chains per unit volume) is:

$$
S = -\frac{3Nk}{2}\left(\lambda_{x}^{2}+\lambda_{y}^{2}+\lambda_{z}^{2}-3\right)
$$

where λx, λy, λz are the principal stretch ratios relative to the isotropic reference state.

**Textual Logic:** The factor of N appears because each of the N chains contributes identically; subtracting 3 normalizes S to zero at λ = 1 (undeformed state).

**Step 3:** For **uniaxial extension** along x, with incompressibility λxλyλz = 1, we have λy = λz = λx⁻¹/². Substituting:

$$
S = -\frac{3Nk}{2}\left(\lambda^{2}+\frac{2}{\lambda}-3\right)
$$

**Step 4:** The Helmholtz free energy per unit reference volume A = U − TS. For an ideal rubber with (∂U/∂L)ₜ = 0, A is purely entropic:

$$
A = -TS = \frac{3NkT}{2}\left(\lambda^{2}+\frac{2}{\lambda}-3\right)
$$

**Step 5:** The retractive (tensile) force per unit original cross-section is obtained by differentiation with respect to extension:

$$
f = \frac{\partial A}{\partial \lambda} = NkT\left(\lambda - \frac{1}{\lambda^{2}}\right)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result — The Affine Network Model (Treloar):**
> $$\boxed{\,f = N k T \left(\lambda - \lambda^{-2}\right)\,}$$
> **Interpretation:** The retractive force is directly proportional to **absolute temperature T** — this is the iconic signature of purely entropic elasticity and the theoretical backbone of the Gough–Joule Effect.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — The Adiabatic Temperature Coefficient (∂T/∂L)ₛ

We begin with the **First Law** for a reversible adiabatic process (dQ = 0):

$$
dU = f \, dL
$$

**Step 1:** Express dU in terms of temperature and length:

$$
dU = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_{L} dT + \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial L}\right)_{T} dL
$$

**Step 2:** Identify the heat capacity at constant length Cₗ:

$$
C_{L} = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_{L}
$$

**Step 3:** Substituting and equating with f dL:

$$
C_{L} \, dT + \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial L}\right)_{T} dL = f \, dL
$$

**Step 4:** Solve for dT/dL:

$$
\frac{dT}{dL} = \frac{1}{C_{L}}\left[f - \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial L}\right)_{T}\right]
$$

**Step 5:** Use the **fundamental relation** f = (∂U/∂L)ₜ + T(∂f/∂T)ₗ, hence:

$$
f - \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial L}\right)_{T} = T\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial T}\right)_{L}
$$

**Step 6:** Final adiabatic temperature coefficient:

$$
\boxed{\;\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial L}\right)_{S} = \frac{T}{C_{L}}\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial T}\right)_{L}\;}
$$

**Textual Logic:** Since for an ideal rubber (∂f/∂T)ₗ > 0 (force increases with temperature at fixed length — see Derivation 1), and Cₗ > 0 and T > 0, we get (∂T/∂L)ₛ > 0. **The rubber heats up when stretched adiabatically** — this is the Gough–Joule Effect in equation form.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Joule's Original Experimental Determination of (∂U/∂L)ₜ

**Step 1:** Joule's experiment: measure f at constant L as T varies. Plot f vs T.

**Step 2:** The slope at any point gives (∂f/∂T)ₗ. For an ideal rubber, this slope is positive and equal to f/T.

**Step 3:** Compute (∂U/∂L)ₜ using the thermodynamic identity:

$$
\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial L}\right)_{T} = f - T\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial T}\right)_{L}
$$

**Step 4:** For natural rubber, Joule found this to be **≈ 0** (within experimental error ≈ 0.2 J per meter of elongation), confirming that the elastic force is essentially **entropic**.

**Step 5:** Define the **energy contribution fraction** χ:

$$
\chi = \frac{(\partial U/\partial L)_{T}}{f}
$$

For natural rubber at room temperature, χ ≈ **0.1 – 0.2**, meaning 80–90% of the retractive force is entropic and only 10–20% is energetic (van der Waals + covalent bond deformation).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Problem-Solving Matrix

| Problem Type | Key Equation to Use | Typical KTU Mark Weight |
|---|---|---|
| Prove rubber is entropic | (∂U/∂L)ₜ = 0 from f vs T data | 7 marks |
| Calculate adiabatic ΔT | (∂T/∂L)ₛ = (T/Cₗ)(∂f/∂T)ₗ | 7 marks |
| Compute retractive force | f = NkT(λ − λ⁻²) | 5–7 marks |
| Explain Gough–Joule qualitatively | Entropy + adiabatic reasoning | 3–5 marks |
| Thermoelastic inversion | Sign reversal of (∂f/∂T)ₗ at high strain | 5 marks |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Hack:** Whenever a KTU question asks *"Show that rubber elasticity is entropic"*, you must always include Joule's experimental plot of f vs T, derive (∂U/∂L)ₜ = 0, and quote the Gough–Joule Effect as the qualitative evidence. Missing any one of these three earns only partial marks.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🖼️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Thermodynamic Block Architecture of the Gough–Joule Effect

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Rubber at rest: Random coil chains] --> B{Apply rapid stretch}
    B -->|Adiabatic dQ=0| C[Chains forced to align: Omega decreases]
    C --> D[Configurational entropy S decreases]
    D --> E[Energy conservation: dS_total = 0]
    E --> F[Vibrational phonon entropy rises]
    F --> G[Sample temperature increases: T+]
    G --> H[Heat dissipated to surroundings after process]

    A --> I{Release tension}
    I -->|Adiabatic dQ=0| J[Chains recoil to random coils]
    J --> K[Configurational entropy S increases]
    K --> L[Energy conservation: dS_total = 0]
    L --> M[Phonon entropy decreases]
    M --> N[Sample temperature decreases: T-]

    A --> O[Molecular Level: Entropy S = k ln Omega]
    C --> P[Gaussian to Aligned Conformation]
    O --> P
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read the diagram:** The left branch (apply stretch) shows the heating path; the right branch (release) shows the cooling path. The bottom row identifies the underlying statistical mechanics origin (entropy of microstates).

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture (Joule's Thermodynamic Identity)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Inputs
        A1[Sample: Crosslinked Elastomer]
        A2[Instrument: Constant-Length Rig]
        A3[Variables: T, L, f, U, S]
    end

    subgraph Measurement
        B1[Measure f at fixed L, varying T]
        B2[Plot f versus T curve]
        B3[Compute slope: dF/dT at constant L]
    end

    subgraph Computation
        C1[Apply First Law: dU = f dL + T dS]
        C2[Cross-differentiate Maxwell relation]
        C3[Derive identity: f = dU/dL + T times dF/dT]
    end

    subgraph Interpretation
        D1{Check value of dU/dL at constant T}
        D2[If dU/dL = 0: Ideal Entropic Rubber]
        D3[If dU/dL not 0: Energetic Contribution Exists]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    A3 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> C3
    C1 --> C3
    C2 --> C3
    C3 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
    D1 --> D3
```

# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
*Define the Gough–Joule Effect. Why is it considered experimental proof that rubber elasticity is entropic in origin?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** The Gough–Joule Effect is the phenomenon in which a stretched rubber band undergoes a **reversible temperature change** — heating up when suddenly stretched adiabatically and cooling down when the tension is suddenly released.

**Explanation (2 marks):** When a rubber band is stretched, its long-chain molecules are pulled from a **random-coil (disordered)** conformation into an **aligned (ordered)** conformation. The number of accessible microstates **Ω decreases**, hence the configurational entropy S = k ln Ω also **decreases**. Since the process is adiabatic (dQ = 0) and total entropy cannot decrease, the *configurational* entropy loss is compensated by a rise in *thermal* (vibrational) entropy of the lattice, raising the **sample temperature**. This is the **direct macroscopic proof** that rubber's retractive force arises from entropy changes, not from energetic bond distortion.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
*State Joule's experimental result on stretched rubber and write the thermodynamic identity he used.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Joule's experimental result (1 mark):** Joule measured the tension f of a rubber strip at constant length L as a function of temperature T. He found that **(∂U/∂L)ₜ ≈ 0** for natural rubber, meaning the change in internal energy with elongation is essentially zero.

**Thermodynamic identity (2 marks):** Starting from dU = T dS + f dL, Maxwell-type differentiation yields:

$$
f = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial L}\right)_{T} + T\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial T}\right)_{L}
$$

Since (∂U/∂L)ₜ = 0, this reduces to f = T(∂f/∂T)ₗ, confirming that the retractive force in rubber is purely entropic in origin.

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern Reminder:** Each Part-B question is for **14 marks** with **internal choice** (you attempt either Choice A or Choice B). Each question has **two sub-parts: (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks.**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – July 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 marks]** *Derive the thermodynamic expression for the tension f in a stretched rubber in terms of internal energy and entropy. Explain how Joule's experiment confirms that the retractive force is largely entropic.*

**(b) [7 marks]** *For a natural rubber sample at 300 K, the heat capacity at constant length is Cₗ = 1.9 kJ·kg⁻¹·K⁻¹. The force-temperature coefficient (∂f/∂T)ₗ is measured to be 1.4 × 10⁻³ N·K⁻¹·m⁻². Estimate the adiabatic temperature rise when the rubber is stretched by 100% (i.e., λ = 2.0).*

---

**Model Solution to A(a) — [7 marks]**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Apply the First Law to a rubber band undergoing a reversible change:

$$
dU = T\,dS + f\,dL
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Define the Helmholtz free energy A = U − TS. Its differential is:

$$
dA = -S\,dT + f\,dL
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Apply the cross-partial equality (Maxwell-type relation for A):

$$
\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial L}\right)_{T} = -\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial T}\right)_{L}
$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** From the First Law, isolate the partial derivative (∂U/∂L)ₜ:

$$
\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial L}\right)_{T} = f - T\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial T}\right)_{L}
$$

This is the **fundamental thermodynamic tension identity** for an elastomer.

**Step 5 (2 marks):** Joule's experiment: Plot f vs T at constant L. Measure slope (∂f/∂T)ₗ. Substitute into the identity. For natural rubber, Joule found (∂U/∂L)ₜ ≈ 0, meaning **f ≈ T(∂f/∂T)ₗ**. This is mathematically equivalent to **f = −T(∂S/∂L)ₜ**, i.e., the force is proportional to a *temperature-driven entropy gradient*. The retractive force is therefore **predominantly entropic (≈ 80–90%)** with a small energetic contribution (≈ 10–20%) from bond distortion.

**Conclusion:** Rubber elasticity is primarily an **entropy-driven** phenomenon — the Gough–Joule Effect is the qualitative confirmation, and Joule's measurement is the quantitative proof.

---

**Model Solution to A(b) — [7 marks]**

**Given:**
- T = 300 K
- Cₗ = 1.9 kJ·kg⁻¹·K⁻¹ = 1900 J·kg⁻¹·K⁻¹
- (∂f/∂T)ₗ = 1.4 × 10⁻³ N·K⁻¹·m⁻²
- λ = 2.0 (i.e., 100% elongation)

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Use the adiabatic temperature coefficient formula:

$$
\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial L}\right)_{S} = \frac{T}{C_{L}}\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial T}\right)_{L}
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Substitute the values:

$$
\frac{\partial T}{\partial L} = \frac{300 \times 1.4 \times 10^{-3}}{1900} = \frac{0.42}{1900} = 2.21 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{K·m}^{-1}
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** For λ = 2.0, the actual change in length per kg of rubber depends on initial length. For a reference sample of length L₀ = 1 m, ΔL = (λ − 1)L₀ = 1.0 m.

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Compute ΔT:

$$
\Delta T = \left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial L}\right)_{S} \times \Delta L = 2.21 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.0 = 2.21 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{K}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Realistic value correction:** In actual practice, for 100% elongation, observed ΔT is on the order of **+1 to +3 °C** for typical rubber samples (mass-dependent). The order of magnitude is consistent with the small calculated value scaled to actual sample mass. Per kg of rubber, the temperature rise of a few degrees is the expected experimental observation.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** A modest but measurable temperature rise is predicted, confirming the Gough–Joule Effect quantitatively.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 marks]** *Explain the molecular origin of the Gough–Joule Effect. Discuss with neat sketches (a) the random-coil configuration of polymer chains in relaxed rubber, and (b) the aligned configuration in stretched rubber. Relate this to the change in configurational entropy.*

**(b) [7 marks]** *A rubber band has 5.0 × 10²⁵ network chains per cubic meter. At 298 K, it is stretched uniaxially to twice its original length. Using the affine network model f = NkT(λ − λ⁻²), calculate: (i) the elastic retractive force per unit area, and (ii) the percentage contribution of entropic and energetic components assuming the energetic contribution is 15% of the total.*

---

**Model Solution to B(a) — [7 marks]**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Describe the **relaxed state.** In an undeformed rubber, polymer chains between crosslink junctions adopt **random-coil** (Gaussian) conformations. The number of accessible microstates Ω_relaxed is enormous → configurational entropy S_relaxed is **maximum**.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Describe the **stretched state.** Upon uniaxial stretching, the chains are forced into **elongated, partially aligned** conformations. The number of accessible microstates Ω_stretched is **much smaller** than Ω_relaxed. Hence S_stretched < S_relaxed → entropy **decreases**.

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Mathematical relation (Gaussian chain):

$$
S = -\frac{3Nk}{2}\left(\lambda^{2}+\frac{2}{\lambda}-3\right) + \text{const}
$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Adiabatic condition: dQ = 0, so total entropy of (chain + lattice) is conserved. The loss of *configurational* entropy is balanced by a gain in *vibrational (thermal)* entropy of the lattice. Since thermal entropy increases with temperature, the **sample temperature rises**. This is the Gough–Joule Effect.

**Sketch (qualitative, no marks deducted if textual):**
- Relaxed: tangled spaghetti-like coils, random directions.
- Stretched: parallel-oriented chains along the stretching axis, with reduced end-to-end distance freedom.

---

**Model Solution to B(b) — [7 marks]**

**Given:**
- N = 5.0 × 10²⁵ chains·m⁻³
- T = 298 K
- λ = 2.0
- k = 1.381 × 10⁻²³ J·K⁻¹

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Compute the affine network tension:

$$
f = NkT\left(\lambda - \lambda^{-2}\right)
$$

$$
f = (5.0 \times 10^{25})(1.381 \times 10^{-23})(298)\left(2.0 - \frac{1}{4}\right)
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Calculate step by step:

$$
(5.0 \times 10^{25})(1.381 \times 10^{-23}) = 6.905 \times 10^{2} = 690.5
$$

$$
690.5 \times 298 = 2.058 \times 10^{5}
$$

$$
\lambda - \lambda^{-2} = 2.0 - 0.25 = 1.75
$$

$$
f = 2.058 \times 10^{5} \times 1.75 = 3.60 \times 10^{5}\ \text{N·m}^{-2} = 0.360\ \text{MPa}
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** State the answer to part (i):

$$
\boxed{f \approx 0.36\ \text{MPa}}
$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Part (ii) — Energetic vs Entropic split:

- Energetic contribution: 15% of f = 0.15 × 0.360 = 0.054 MPa
- Entropic contribution: 85% of f = 0.85 × 0.360 = 0.306 MPa

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The entropic component dominates (~85%), consistent with the Gough–Joule interpretation of rubber elasticity.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Gough–Joule Effect Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing (∂U/∂L)ₜ = 0 with U = 0.** The partial derivative is zero, not the internal energy itself. (−2 marks if mistaken)
> 2. **Forgetting the Maxwell-relation derivation.** Most students write f = T(∂f/∂T)ₗ directly without showing the Maxwell cross-derivative steps. (−3 marks)
> 3. **Wrong sign convention for ΔT.** Stretching gives +ΔT (heating), releasing gives −ΔT (cooling). Confusing these loses 2 marks.
> 4. **Not specifying "adiabatic" condition.** The Gough–Joule Effect is fundamentally adiabatic; if the stretching is slow (isothermal), there is no temperature change. (−1 mark)
> 5. **Ignoring thermoelastic inversion at high strain.** At elongations >300%, the sign of (∂f/∂T)ₗ reverses, and the rubber *cools* on stretching. Missing this in long answers loses 1–2 marks for incomplete analysis.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ The **Gough–Joule Effect** = heating on rapid stretching, cooling on rapid release of an elastomer.
- ✅ It is the **macroscopic proof** of **entropic origin** of rubber elasticity.
- ✅ The **fundamental identity**: f = (∂U/∂L)ₜ + T(∂f/∂T)ₗ
- ✅ For an **ideal rubber**: (∂U/∂L)ₜ = 0, so f = T(∂f/∂T)ₗ
- ✅ The **Maxwell relation**: (∂S/∂L)ₜ = −(∂f/∂T)ₗ
- ✅ The **adiabatic temperature coefficient**: (∂T/∂L)ₛ = (T/Cₗ)(∂f/∂T)ₗ
- ✅ Molecular basis: stretching reduces **configurational entropy** S = k ln Ω of network chains.
- ✅ Adiabatic constraint: total entropy conserved → loss of configurational S = gain in thermal S → temperature rises.
- ✅ **Affine network model**: f = NkT(λ − λ⁻²); force is **linear in T**, confirming entropic origin.
- ✅ **Thermoelastic inversion** at high strain (>300%) reverses the sign of (∂f/∂T)ₗ due to strain-induced crystallization.
- ✅ For natural rubber: ~80–90% entropic, ~10–20% energetic contribution to retractive force.
- ✅ **Key experimenters:** John Gough (1802, qualitative), James Prescott Joule (1859, quantitative).
- ✅ **Engineering applications:** Soft robotics actuators, tire heat build-up analysis, biomedical devices.
- ✅ **Exam-favorite derivations to memorize:** (1) Entropic tension from Gaussian chain, (2) Adiabatic ΔT formula, (3) Joule's identity.

> [!TIP]
> **One-line summary for the night-before exam:** *"Stretch rubber fast, it heats (entropy drops, lattice vibrates more); release fast, it cools. Rubber's elasticity is entropy-driven — proven by Gough's touch and Joule's thermometer."*
