topics.title: Thermoelastic Experiment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT402 - POLYMER PHYSICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Thermoelastic Experiment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Thermoelastic Experiment** (also known as the **Force-Temperature Experiment** or **Thermokinetic Experiment**) is a classical experiment in polymer physics used to resolve the **tensile force** ($f$) in a stretched elastomer into its **internal energy** ($f_e$) and **entropy** ($f_s$) components. By measuring the variation of retractive tension with temperature at a *constant length* (and ideally at constant volume), one obtains the **Thermodynamic Equation of State for Rubber Elasticity**:

$$f = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V} + T \left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial T}\right)_{L,V}$$

where the first term represents the **energetic contribution** and the second term represents the **entropic contribution** to the total elastic force.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The thermoelastic experiment is the *only* direct experimental proof that the elasticity of rubber is **predominantly entropic** in origin, validating the **Gaussian chain statistics** of the statistical theory of rubber elasticity (Flory, 1953).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Spaghetti Bridge Analogy"

Imagine a bowl of cooked spaghetti (representing a polymer network) sitting on a table. If you pull the strands apart, the *heat* in the bowl is related to how disordered the strands are:
- When you **stretch** the network, you reduce the number of possible conformations each chain can adopt → the system becomes more **ordered** → **entropy decreases**.
- To restore the lost entropy, the chains pull back → generating a **retractive force**.
- The hotter the polymer is, the more vigorously the chains seek disorder, so the **retractive force increases with temperature** at fixed length. This is the hallmark of *entropic* elasticity — exactly opposite to a metal spring (where force *decreases* with temperature).

A real metal spring feels softer when hot. A stretched rubber band feels *tighter* when heated. This opposite behavior is what the thermoelastic experiment quantifies.

### 1.3 Key Constants and Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Value/Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Absolute Temperature | $T$ | **K** (Kelvin) |
| Retractive Force | $f$ | **N (Newton)** |
| Sample Length | $L$ | **m (metre)** |
| Volume | $V$ | **m³** |
| Universal Gas Constant | $R$ | **8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹** |
| Boltzmann Constant | $k_B$ | **1.381 × 10⁻²³ J·K⁻¹** |

> [!NOTE]
> The experiment is performed under **isometric (constant length)** conditions, NOT isobaric or isotensional conditions. This is critical because the **thermodynamic identity** used to separate energy and entropy components is valid only at constant $L$ and constant $V$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — First and Second Laws of Thermodynamics

The starting point is the **fundamental thermodynamic relation** for the Helmholtz free energy $A = U - TS$:

$$dA = -S\,dT - P\,dV + f\,dL$$

From this, the force at constant $T$ and $V$ is:

$$f = \left(\frac{\partial A}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V}$$

The **Maxwell relation** obtained by cross-differentiation gives:

$$\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V} = -\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial T}\right)_{L,V}$$

### 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Reasoning

- **Step 1:** Apply the definition of Helmholtz free energy to express the total force.
- **Step 2:** Use the Maxwell relation to convert the entropy derivative into an experimentally measurable quantity — the slope $(\partial f/\partial T)_{L,V}$.
- **Step 3:** Substitute back to obtain the **thermodynamic equation of state**:

$$f = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V} + T \left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial T}\right)_{L,V}$$

- **Step 4:** Identify the two terms:
  - **Energy term:** $f_e = (\partial U/\partial L)_{T,V}$ — change in internal energy per unit extension.
  - **Entropy term:** $f_s = T(\partial f/\partial T)_{L,V}$ — temperature-scaled force derivative.
- **Step 5:** Define the **Thermoelastic Inversion Temperature** $T_i$ as the temperature where $(\partial f/\partial T)_{L,V} = 0$. At this point, the entropic contribution vanishes and the force is purely energetic.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Physical Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f = f_e + f_s$ | Additivity of energetic and entropic components | Force decomposition |
| 2 | $f = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V} + T \left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial T}\right)_{L,V}$ | **Thermodynamic Equation of State** | Core formula of experiment |
| 3 | $f_s = T \left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial T}\right)_{L,V}$ | Entropic component of force | Calculated from slope of $f$ vs $T$ |
| 4 | $f_e = f - T \left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial T}\right)_{L,V}$ | Energetic component of force | Calculated by difference |
| 5 | $\frac{f_e}{f} = 1 - \frac{T}{f}\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial T}\right)_{L,V}$ | Fractional energy contribution | Quantifying ideality of rubber |
| 6 | $\frac{f_s}{f} = \frac{T}{f}\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial T}\right)_{L,V}$ | Fractional entropy contribution | Comparing with theory |
| 7 | $T_i: \left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial T}\right)_{L,V} = 0$ | Thermoelastic Inversion Temperature | Characteristic material property |
| 8 | $f = \nu k_B T \left(\frac{L}{N b^2} - \frac{N b^2}{L^2}\right)$ | **Gaussian Force Law** (for ideal network) | Statistical theory prediction |
| 9 | $\Delta S = -\frac{1}{2}\nu k_B \left[\left(\frac{L}{L_0}\right)^2 + 2\frac{L_0}{L} - 3\right]$ | Entropy change on deformation | Origin of entropic elasticity |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Notation:** In the table, the symbol $\nu$ represents the number of active network chains (crosslink density per unit volume), $N$ is the number of statistical segments per chain, $b$ is the segment length, and $L_0$ is the unstretched length.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Tyre Design:** Engineers use thermoelastic data to predict how tyre tension varies during operation (cold morning start vs. hot highway driving).
- **Elastomer Selection:** Materials with low $f_e/f$ (high entropic character, e.g., **natural rubber**) are preferred for dynamic applications because their mechanical response is predictable from temperature alone.
- **Smart Materials:** Thermoelastic behavior underlies **shape-memory polymers** and **dielectric elastomer actuators**.
- **Vibration Dampers:** The $T$-dependence of stiffness must be compensated in aerospace elastomeric bearings.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Thermodynamic Equation of State

We start from the **Helmholtz free energy** of an elastomer held at constant volume:

$$A = U - TS$$

The differential form is:

$$dA = dU - T\,dS - S\,dT$$

Substituting the first law at constant volume ($dU = \delta Q + \delta W = T\,dS + f\,dL$, with no $PdV$ work):

$$dA = -S\,dT + f\,dL$$

This gives the two fundamental partial derivatives:

$$\left(\frac{\partial A}{\partial T}\right)_{L,V} = -S \quad \text{(Equation 3.1)}$$

$$\left(\frac{\partial A}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V} = f \quad \text{(Equation 3.2)}$$

Now we use the **equality of mixed partial derivatives** (Schwarz theorem on the smoothness of $A(T,L,V)$):

$$\frac{\partial^2 A}{\partial L \, \partial T} = \frac{\partial^2 A}{\partial T \, \partial L}$$

Differentiating (3.1) with respect to $L$ and (3.2) with respect to $T$:

$$\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial T}\right)_{L,V} = \frac{\partial^2 A}{\partial T\, \partial L} = -\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V} \quad \text{(Maxwell Relation)}$$

Now express the total force using the definition $A = U - TS$ at constant $T$ and $V$:

$$f = \left(\frac{\partial A}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V} = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V} - T\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V}$$

Substituting the Maxwell relation $-(\partial S/\partial L)_{T,V} = (\partial f/\partial T)_{L,V}$:

$$f = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V} + T\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial T}\right)_{L,V}$$

This is the **Thermodynamic Equation of State for Rubber Elasticity**. $\blacksquare$

### 3.2 Derivation of the Force-Temperature Relation for an Ideal Gaussian Network

For an **ideal rubber** (purely entropic elasticity), the internal energy is independent of length:

$$\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V} = 0$$

The thermodynamic equation reduces to:

$$f = T\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial T}\right)_{L,V}$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{1}{f}\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial T}\right)_{L,V} = \frac{1}{T}$$

This is a separable differential equation in $f$ and $T$ (with $L$ held constant):

$$\frac{df}{f} = \frac{dT}{T}$$

Integrating at constant $L$:

$$\int_{f_0}^{f} \frac{df'}{f'} = \int_{T_0}^{T} \frac{dT'}{T'}$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{f}{f_0}\right) = \ln\left(\frac{T}{T_0}\right)$$

Exponentiating:

$$f = f_0 \cdot \frac{T}{T_0} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad f \propto T \quad \text{(at constant L)}$$

This is the **signature prediction** of purely entropic rubber elasticity: **at fixed extension, force is directly proportional to absolute temperature**. $\blacksquare$

### 3.3 Separation of Energy and Entropy Contributions

Define the entropy contribution explicitly:

$$f_s = T\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial T}\right)_{L,V}$$

By the additivity of force:

$$f_e = f - f_s = f - T\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial T}\right)_{L,V}$$

Define the **energy fraction** and **entropy fraction**:

$$\frac{f_e}{f} = 1 - \frac{T}{f}\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial T}\right)_{L,V}$$

$$\frac{f_s}{f} = \frac{T}{f}\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial T}\right)_{L,V}$$

For an ideal rubber: $f \propto T$, so $(\partial f/\partial T)_{L,V} = f/T$, and:

$$\frac{f_s}{f} = 1, \quad \frac{f_e}{f} = 0$$

For a typical **natural rubber** at room temperature:

$$\frac{f_e}{f} \approx 0.10 \text{ to } 0.20, \quad \frac{f_s}{f} \approx 0.80 \text{ to } 0.90$$

This experimentally confirms that rubber elasticity is ~80–90% entropic. $\blacksquare$

### 3.4 Derivation of the Thermoelastic Inversion Temperature

The inversion temperature $T_i$ is defined by the condition:

$$\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial T}\right)_{L,V} = 0$$

Under the thermodynamic equation, this means:

$$f = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V} = f_e \quad \text{at } T = T_i$$

**Physical interpretation:** At $T_i$, the entropic retractive force $f_s$ becomes exactly zero, and the total force equals the energetic contribution. For $T > T_i$, $f_s$ reappears with the *opposite* sign — this corresponds to the regime where intramolecular steric and van der Waals forces dominate and the rubber exhibits **anti-entropic** behavior (resembles a metal spring). $\blacksquare$

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A natural rubber sample is held at constant length. At $T_1 = 300$ K, the retractive force is $f_1 = 2.50$ N. At $T_2 = 320$ K, the force is $f_2 = 2.70$ N. Assuming the slope is linear in this range, calculate $f_e$ and $f_s$ at 310 K.

**Solution:**

Step 1: Calculate the slope $(\partial f/\partial T)_{L,V}$:

$$\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial T}\right)_{L,V} = \frac{f_2 - f_1}{T_2 - T_1} = \frac{2.70 - 2.50}{320 - 300} = \frac{0.20}{20} = 0.010 \text{ N/K}$$

Step 2: Calculate the force at 310 K by linear interpolation:

$$f(310) = 2.50 + 0.010 \times (310 - 300) = 2.50 + 0.10 = 2.60 \text{ N}$$

Step 3: Calculate the entropy contribution at 310 K:

$$f_s(310) = T \left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial T}\right)_{L,V} = 310 \times 0.010 = 3.10 \text{ N}$$

Step 4: Calculate the energy contribution at 310 K:

$$f_e(310) = f(310) - f_s(310) = 2.60 - 3.10 = -0.50 \text{ N}$$

Step 5: Calculate the energy and entropy fractions:

$$\frac{f_e}{f} = \frac{-0.50}{2.60} = -0.192$$

$$\frac{f_s}{f} = \frac{3.10}{2.60} = 1.192$$

> [!NOTE]
> The negative energy contribution indicates that the internal energy of this rubber *decreases* upon stretching — typical of natural rubber, where conformational changes reduce van der Waals interactions between chain segments. The sum of the fractions exceeds 1 because $f_s$ is computed from the *slope*, not from a direct difference.

### 3.6 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Parameter | Origin | Sign in Natural Rubber | Sign in Ideal Gaussian Network |
|---|---|---|---|
| $f$ (total force) | Sum of $f_e$ and $f_s$ | Positive | Positive |
| $f_e = (\partial U/\partial L)_T$ | Energetic (bond stretching, vdW) | **Negative** (typically) | **Zero** |
| $f_s = T(\partial f/\partial T)_L$ | Entropic (conformational) | **Positive** (dominant) | **Positive** (equals $f$) |
| $(\partial f/\partial T)_L$ | Slope of $f$ vs $T$ plot | Positive (large) | $f/T$ |
| $T_i$ | Where slope vanishes | Very high (often $>500$ K) | — |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Experimental Setup

The following Mermaid diagram maps the functional architecture of a thermoelastic experiment, including the instrument layout, the feedback loop for constant-length control, and the data processing pipeline.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["SAMPLE HOLDER CLAMP A - FIXED END"] --> nodeB["ELASTOMER STRIP - CONSTANT LENGTH L"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["SAMPLE HOLDER CLAMP B - MOVABLE END"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["FORCE TRANSDUCER - LOAD CELL"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["DATA ACQUISITION UNIT - DAQ"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["TEMPERATURE RECORDER - T LOGGER"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["THERMOSTATIC BATH - WATER OR AIR"]
    nodeG --> nodeB
    nodeE --> nodeH["COMPUTER INTERFACE - f vs T PLOT"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["LINEAR FIT - SLOPE df by dT"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["ENTROPY COMPONENT - f sub s"]
    nodeI --> nodeK["ENERGY COMPONENT - f sub e"]
    nodeJ --> nodeL["INVERSION TEMPERATURE - T sub i"]
    nodeK --> nodeL
    nodeL --> nodeM["REPORT - f sub e by f AND f sub s by f"]
```

### 4.2 Process Flow — Analysis Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeP1["RAW DATA - f T PAIRS"] --> nodeP2["PLOT f vs T AT CONSTANT L"]
    nodeP2 --> nodeP3["COMPUTE SLOPE - df by dT"]
    nodeP3 --> nodeP4["CALCULATE f sub s - T times slope"]
    nodeP4 --> nodeP5["CALCULATE f sub e - f minus f sub s"]
    nodeP5 --> nodeP6["COMPUTE RATIOS - f sub e by f AND f sub s by f"]
    nodeP6 --> nodeP7["EXTRAPOLATE TO T sub i - WHERE SLOPE ZERO"]
    nodeP7 --> nodeP8["VALIDATE - COMPARE WITH GAUSSIAN PREDICTION"]
```

### 4.3 Causal Flow — Origin of Retractive Force

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeQ1["NETWORK CHAINS - GAUSSIAN COILS"] --> nodeQ2["STRETCHING AT CONSTANT L"]
    nodeQ2 --> nodeQ3["REDUCE END TO END DISTANCE CONFORMATIONS"]
    nodeQ3 --> nodeQ4["ENTROPY DECREASE - DELTA S NEGATIVE"]
    nodeQ4 --> nodeQ5["ENTROPIC FORCE - f sub s POSITIVE"]
    nodeQ1 --> nodeQ6["CHANGE IN INTRA CHAIN INTERACTIONS"]
    nodeQ6 --> nodeQ7["VAN DER WAALS AND STERIC ENERGY SHIFT"]
    nodeQ7 --> nodeQ8["ENERGETIC FORCE - f sub e USUALLY SMALL"]
    nodeQ5 --> nodeQ9["TOTAL RETRACTIVE FORCE - f equals f sub e plus f sub s"]
    nodeQ8 --> nodeQ9
    nodeQ9 --> nodeQ10["TEMPERATURE INCREASE - AMPLIFIES f sub s"]
    nodeQ10 --> nodeQ11["SLOPE df by dT - POSITIVE FOR NATURAL RUBBER"]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** *Define the thermoelastic effect. Why is the experiment performed at constant length rather than at constant force?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **thermoelastic effect** is the dependence of the retractive tension in a stretched elastomer on temperature. The experiment is performed at **constant length** (isometrically) because the thermodynamic identity used to separate the energy and entropy contributions, namely the Maxwell relation $-(\partial S/\partial L)_{T,V} = (\partial f/\partial T)_{L,V}$, is valid only at constant $L$ (and constant $V$). At constant force, the analogous identity involves $(\partial L/\partial T)_f$, which gives the *thermal expansion* under tension — a related but distinct quantity. Holding $L$ fixed also eliminates the large thermal expansion contribution that would otherwise obscure the small energetic component.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** *What is the thermoelastic inversion temperature? What does its existence tell us about the nature of rubber elasticity?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **thermoelastic inversion temperature** $T_i$ is the temperature at which the slope $(\partial f/\partial T)_{L,V}$ vanishes, i.e., the retractive force becomes independent of temperature. Below $T_i$, the slope is positive (entropic behavior, as in natural rubber at room temperature); above $T_i$, the slope becomes negative (energetic behavior resembling a metal spring). The *existence* of an inversion temperature proves that the entropic contribution is **not the entire story** — there is a finite energetic component $f_e$ that competes with the entropic term. For a *purely* ideal Gaussian network, $f_e = 0$ and $T_i$ would be infinite. Thus, the finite $T_i$ observed in real rubbers confirms that rubber elasticity is *predominantly* (but not exclusively) entropic.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **ESE Module-Internal Choice Format:** Answer **ONE** of the following two full questions.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a)** Starting from the Helmholtz free energy, derive the **thermodynamic equation of state** for rubber elasticity. Clearly identify the energy and entropy terms. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **experimental procedure** for the thermoelastic experiment and show how the data is used to compute $f_e$ and $f_s$. State the typical result for natural rubber. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

Start with the Helmholtz free energy:
$$A = U - TS \implies dA = dU - T\,dS - S\,dT$$

At constant volume with tensile work only:
$$dU = T\,dS + f\,dL \implies dA = -S\,dT + f\,dL$$

Therefore:
$$f = \left(\frac{\partial A}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V} \quad \text{and} \quad S = -\left(\frac{\partial A}{\partial T}\right)_{L,V}$$

Apply the Schwarz theorem (equality of mixed partials):
$$\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial T}\right)_{L,V} = \frac{\partial^2 A}{\partial T\,\partial L} = -\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V}$$

Express the total force in terms of $U$ and $S$:
$$f = \left(\frac{\partial A}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V} = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V} - T\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V}$$

Substitute the Maxwell relation:
$$\boxed{f = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V} + T\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial T}\right)_{L,V}}$$

**Identification:** First term $f_e = (\partial U/\partial L)_{T,V}$ is the **energetic component**; second term $f_s = T(\partial f/\partial T)_{L,V}$ is the **entropic component**. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b) Experimental Procedure and Analysis (7 marks):**

1. A thin strip of vulcanized natural rubber is mounted between two clamps, one fixed and one attached to a **load cell** (force transducer). The sample is held at a fixed extension ratio $\alpha = L/L_0$. **(1 mark)**
2. The entire assembly is enclosed in a **thermostatic chamber** whose temperature is varied slowly (e.g., from 280 K to 360 K in 5 K steps). **(1 mark)**
3. At each temperature, after thermal equilibration (~10–15 minutes), the **retractive force** $f$ is recorded. The volume change of the rubber is monitored to allow correction to constant-volume conditions. **(1 mark)**
4. The data is plotted as **$f$ versus $T$** at constant $L$. For natural rubber, this is approximately a straight line with positive slope. **(1 mark)**
5. The slope $(\partial f/\partial T)_{L,V}$ is obtained by linear regression. **(1 mark)**
6. At a reference temperature (e.g., 300 K):
   - $f_s = T \cdot (\partial f/\partial T)_{L,V}$
   - $f_e = f - f_s$
   - $f_e/f$ and $f_s/f$ are computed. **(1 mark)**
7. **Typical result for natural rubber at 300 K:** $f_s/f \approx 0.80$ to $0.90$, $f_e/f \approx 0.10$ to $0.20$, with $f_e$ slightly negative. This confirms that rubber elasticity is **~80–90% entropic**. **(1 mark)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Alternative Choice]

**(a)** Explain the **statistical theory of rubber elasticity** for a Gaussian network. Derive the expression for the retractive force and show that it predicts $f \propto T$ at constant $L$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **concept of the thermoelastic inversion temperature** $T_i$. Sketch a typical $f$ vs $T$ curve at constant $L$ and indicate how $T_i$ is located experimentally. Why is the observation of $T_i$ important for validating the statistical theory? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Statistical Theory of Rubber Elasticity (7 marks):**

Consider an affine network of $\nu$ Gaussian chains, each with $N$ freely jointed segments of length $b$. The entropy of a single chain with end-to-end distance $r$ is:

$$S_{chain} = k_B \ln \Omega(r) = C - \frac{3k_B r^2}{2Nb^2}$$

where $\Omega(r)$ is the number of conformations. For the network with macroscopic dimension $L$ along the stretch axis, assuming affine deformation ($r_x = (L/L_0)\,r_{x,0}$, etc.):

$$\Delta S_{net} = -\frac{1}{2}\nu k_B \left[\alpha^2 + \frac{2}{\alpha} - 3\right]$$

where $\alpha = L/L_0$. Since elasticity is purely entropic for an ideal network:

$$f = -T\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V} = -T \cdot \frac{1}{L_0}\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial \alpha}\right)_T$$

Computing the derivative:
$$f = \frac{\nu k_B T}{L_0}\left(\alpha - \frac{1}{\alpha^2}\right)$$

Substituting $\alpha = L/L_0$:
$$f = \nu k_B T \left(\frac{L}{N b^2} - \frac{N b^2}{L^2}\right) \cdot (\text{const.}) \quad \Rightarrow \quad f \propto T \quad \text{at constant } L$$

This linear proportionality $f = cT$ is the hallmark of purely entropic Gaussian elasticity. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b) Thermoelastic Inversion Temperature (7 marks):**

The **inversion temperature** $T_i$ is defined as the temperature at which the entropic contribution $f_s$ is exactly cancelled by the energetic contribution $f_e$ (i.e., the slope of $f$ vs $T$ at constant $L$ becomes zero):

$$\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial T}\right)_{L,V}\bigg|_{T_i} = 0$$

**Sketch of the $f$ vs $T$ curve at constant $L$:** (described below as it is text-based)
- For $T < T_i$: the curve has a **positive slope** (entropic regime; behavior of natural rubber at room temperature).
- At $T = T_i$: the curve has a **horizontal tangent** (minimum or inflection point).
- For $T > T_i$: the curve has a **negative slope** (energetic regime; rubber behaves like a metal spring).

**Experimental location of $T_i$:** One performs the $f$ vs $T$ measurement over a wide temperature range (often above room temperature, sometimes up to the degradation limit). The temperature at which the slope changes sign — or more precisely, where the linear fit gives zero slope — is $T_i$. For natural rubber, $T_i$ is very high (extrapolated to ~500–600 K, well above the degradation temperature, so it is rarely observed directly; instead, $f_e$ is measured at room temperature and assumed temperature-independent).

**Importance for validating the statistical theory:** The statistical theory of an *ideal* Gaussian network predicts $f_e = 0$ (purely entropic), which would imply an *infinite* $T_i$. The fact that real rubbers show a *finite* $T_i$ (i.e., a non-zero $f_e$) demonstrates that:
- The real network deviates from ideality due to **interchain interactions**, **chain stiffness**, and **non-Gaussian effects** at high extensions.
- The energetic contribution, while small (~10–20% of total force), is finite and measurable.
- The theory must be corrected to account for the internal energy changes — this led to the development of the **non-Gaussian** and **constrained-junction** models. **(7 marks)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing isometric (constant L) with isotensional (constant f) conditions.** The thermodynamic equation $f = (\partial U/\partial L)_{T,V} + T(\partial f/\partial T)_{L,V}$ is **only valid at constant length**. Applying it at constant force is a 2-mark deduction.
> 2. **Forgetting the constant-volume condition.** The Maxwell relation $-(\partial S/\partial L)_{T,V} = (\partial f/\partial T)_{L,V}$ requires *both* constant $T$ and constant $V$. The thermal expansion of rubber is ~$6 \times 10^{-4}$ K⁻¹, which is non-negligible; failure to correct for it gives errors of 5–10% in $f_e$.
> 3. **Sign confusion in $f_e$.** For natural rubber, $f_e$ is typically **negative** (not zero!). This means internal energy *decreases* upon stretching — students often incorrectly report $f_e$ as positive because they equate stretching with bond energy storage.
> 4. **Mixing up the inversion temperature with the glass transition temperature $T_g$.** These are entirely different physical phenomena. $T_i$ is a thermodynamic consequence of the energy-entropy balance; $T_g$ is a kinetic freezing point of segmental motion.
> 5. **Units inconsistency.** Force must be in N, temperature in K (not °C!), and slopes in N/K. Using Celsius in the slope is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Core Equation (memorize verbatim):**
$$f = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V} + T \left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial T}\right)_{L,V}$$

- **Experimental Conditions:** Always **isometric** ($L$ = constant) and ideally **isochoric** ($V$ = constant).

- **Two Components of Force:**
  - **Entropic** $f_s = T(\partial f/\partial T)_{L,V}$ — dominant in rubber (~80–90%).
  - **Energetic** $f_e = f - f_s$ — small, often negative for natural rubber.

- **Key Prediction of Ideal Theory:** $f \propto T$ at constant $L$ (purely entropic).

- **Thermoelastic Inversion Temperature $T_i$:** Temperature where $(\partial f/\partial T)_{L,V} = 0$ and force is purely energetic.

- **Material Comparison:**
  - **Natural rubber:** $f_e/f \approx 0.10$–$0.20$ (mostly entropic).
  - **Ideal Gaussian network:** $f_e/f = 0$ (100% entropic).
  - **Polysiloxane (silicone rubber):** $f_e/f$ can be positive (energetic-dominated).

- **What the Experiment Proves:** Rubber elasticity is **predominantly entropic**, validating the statistical (Gaussian chain) theory of Flory, Treloar, and others.

- **Equation Chain to Remember:** Helmholtz $\to$ Maxwell $\to$ Equation of State $\to$ Force Decomposition $\to$ Inversion Condition.

- **SI Units Throughout:** Force in **N**, Length in **m**, Temperature in **K**, Slope in **N·K⁻¹**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Difference in the Elasticity of Metals and Rubbers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT402 - POLYMER PHYSICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Difference in the Elasticity of Metals and Rubbers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Elasticity** is the ability of a material to resist deformation under an applied stress and to recover its original shape upon removal of the stress. Quantitatively, it is expressed through the **elastic (Young's) modulus** $E$, defined as the ratio of tensile stress $\sigma$ to tensile strain $\varepsilon$ in the linear (Hookean) regime:

$$E = \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon} = \frac{F/A}{\Delta L / L_0}$$

In the context of KTU Module 4 (Polymer Physics), the comparison between **metals** and **rubbers** is fundamental because, although both classes of materials obey the general stress–strain response, the **microscopic origin**, **magnitude**, **temperature dependence**, and **recoverability** of the elastic deformation are profoundly different.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 4):**
> The difference in elasticity arises primarily from two distinct mechanisms:
> 1. **Energy Elasticity (Metals):** Elastic strain is dominated by changes in **interatomic potential energy** (bond stretching). The internal energy $U$ changes with deformation.
> 2. **Entropy Elasticity (Rubbers):** Elastic strain is dominated by changes in **conformational entropy** of long polymer chains. The internal energy remains nearly constant, and the restoring force arises from a decrease in entropy upon stretching.

### 1.2 Comparative Magnitude of Elastic Modulus

| Material Class | Typical Young's Modulus ($E$) | Characteristic Strain |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Metals** (steel, copper, aluminum) | $\mathbf{70 - 200\ \text{GPa}}$ | $\varepsilon \le 0.1 - 1\%$ |
| **Rubbers / Elastomers** (natural rubber, silicone) | $\mathbf{0.001 - 0.1\ \text{GPa}}$ | $\varepsilon \approx 100 - 1000\%$ |
| **Typical Polymer Glass** (PS, PMMA) | $\mathbf{1 - 5\ \text{GPa}}$ | $\varepsilon \le 5\%$ |

Thus, rubbers are **roughly $10^{3}$ to $10^{5}$ times more compliant** than metals, yet they can recover from enormous strains that would permanently deform any metal.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Spring vs. The Coiled Snake

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Picture:**
> - **A metal behaves like a stiff mechanical spring:** When you pull it, you are stretching the atomic bonds themselves (like stretching a tiny spring between atoms). The resistance comes from stored **potential energy** in the bonds. Pull too hard, the bonds yield plastically (permanent slip).
> - **A rubber behaves like a tangled bowl of cooked spaghetti in a closed box:** Each polymer chain is a long, wriggling, random-coil noodle. At rest, the chains are coiled in countless possible configurations (high entropy). When you stretch the rubber, you straighten the chains into fewer possible configurations (low entropy). The restoring force is the chains' statistical "desire" to return to a state of maximum disorder — a purely **entropy-driven** force.
> - **Temperature effect:** Heating a metal makes it *softer* (thermal expansion weakens bonds). Heating a rubber makes it *stiffer* (thermal agitation drives chains to coil more, increasing the entropic restoring force).

### 1.4 Thermodynamic Definition of Elastic Force

For any material under tensile load at temperature $T$, the Helmholtz free energy $A = U - TS$ gives the equation of state for the elastic force $f$:

$$f = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V} - T\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V}$$

The two terms represent the **energy contribution** and the **entropy contribution** to elasticity, respectively.

> [!NOTE]
> - **Metals:** $\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial L}\right)_{T} \gg T\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial L}\right)_{T}$ → **Energy-elastic**
> - **Rubbers:** $\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial L}\right)_{T} \approx 0$, and $f \approx -T\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial L}\right)_{T}$ → **Entropy-elastic**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Microscopic Origin of Elasticity

#### 2.1.1 Metals — Energy Elasticity

- **Atomic structure:** Crystalline lattice of metallic cations embedded in a "sea" of delocalized electrons.
- **Bonding type:** Non-directional **metallic bonding**. The equilibrium lattice spacing $r_0$ corresponds to the minimum of the interatomic potential $U(r)$.
- **Under tension:** Atomic separation increases from $r_0$ to $r_0 + \delta r$. The interatomic potential rises along its anharmonic branch. The restoring force per bond is $f = -dU/dr$.
- **Linear region:** For small $\delta r$, $U(r)$ can be Taylor-expanded to second order, giving Hooke's law. For larger strains, higher-order (anharmonic) terms dominate, leading to non-linearity and eventual plastic flow.
- **Yielding:** Once the stress exceeds the elastic limit, atomic planes slip (dislocation motion) — the deformation becomes **irreversible**.

#### 2.1.2 Rubbers — Entropy Elasticity

- **Molecular structure:** Long, flexible polymer chains (e.g., *cis*-1,4-polyisoprene in natural rubber) of molecular weight $10^{5} - 10^{6}$ g/mol, lightly crosslinked by sulfur vulcanization.
- **Chain conformation:** A single chain adopts a random-coil shape with end-to-end distance $R \sim \sqrt{N}\,b$, where $N$ is the number of backbone bonds and $b$ the bond length.
- **Number of conformations:** The chain can adopt $\Omega(R)$ configurations with end-to-end distance $R$. By Boltzmann's relation, $S = k_B \ln \Omega(R)$.
- **Gaussian chain statistics:** $\Omega(R) \propto \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{3R^{2}}{2Nb^{2}}\right)$, hence the entropy decreases parabolically with $R$.
- **Stretching the rubber** straightens the chains, reduces the number of accessible conformations, decreases entropy, and produces a restoring force proportional to temperature: $f \propto T$.

### 2.2 Theoretical Steps for Elastic Comparison

**Step 1 — Apply Thermodynamic Identity:**
The Helmholtz free energy is $A = U - TS$. At constant $T$ and $V$, the equilibrium condition gives the equation of state for force: $f = (\partial A / \partial L)_{T,V}$.

**Step 2 — Decompose into Energy and Entropy Terms:**
$$f = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V} + T\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V} \cdot (-1) = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V} - T\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V}$$

**Step 3 — Identify the Dominant Term:**
- Metals: $(\partial U/\partial L)$ dominates because bond stretching changes potential energy directly.
- Rubbers: $(\partial U/\partial L) \approx 0$ (bond angles barely change on stretching), so $f \approx -T(\partial S/\partial L)$.

**Step 4 — Compute Entropy Change from Chain Statistics:**
For a Gaussian network, $S = S_0 - \frac{1}{2} N k_B \lambda^{2}$ (per chain), where $\lambda = L/L_0$ is the extension ratio. Differentiating yields $f \propto T \cdot \lambda$.

**Step 5 — Temperature Coefficient of Modulus:**
Differentiating $f$ with respect to $T$ at constant $L$:
- Metals: $\partial f / \partial T < 0$ (thermal softening — bonds weaken with $T$).
- Rubbers: $\partial f / \partial T > 0$ (entropic stiffening — more thermal agitation drives chains toward higher-entropy coiled state).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations for KTU 2024 Examinations**

| # | Equation | Symbol Meaning | Applies To |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $f = (\partial U / \partial L)_T - T(\partial S / \partial L)_T$ | Thermodynamic force decomposition | All materials |
| 2 | $E = \sigma / \varepsilon = F L_0 / A\, \Delta L$ | Young's modulus definition | Linear elastic regime |
| 3 | $U(r) = U_0 + \tfrac{1}{2} k (r - r_0)^{2}$ | Harmonic interatomic potential | Metals (small strain) |
| 4 | $\Omega(R) \propto \exp(-3R^{2}/2Nb^{2})$ | Gaussian chain conformations | Polymer chains |
| 5 | $S = S_0 - \tfrac{1}{2}\nu k_B (\lambda^{2} + 2\lambda^{-1} - 3)$ | Network entropy change | Crosslinked rubber |
| 6 | $\sigma = \nu k_B T (\lambda - \lambda^{-2})$ | Neo-Hookean stress–strain | Ideal rubber |
| 7 | $E_{\text{rubber}} = 3 \nu k_B T$ | Small-strain modulus of rubber | Gaussian network |
| 8 | $(\partial E / \partial T)_P = -\beta E$ | Temperature coefficient (metals) | Metals |
| 9 | $(\partial E / \partial T)_P = +\beta' E$ | Temperature coefficient (rubber) | Rubbers |
| 10 | $\tau = \tau_0 \exp(U_0 / k_B T)$ | Eyring-like activated flow | Plastic flow (metals) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:**
> - The large, recoverable strain of rubber ($\lambda \approx 10$) is exploited in **tyres, vibration dampers, seals, gaskets, flexible couplings, and biomedical implants** (e.g., silicone heart valves).
> - The high stiffness and dimensional stability of metals are essential in **structural beams, aircraft skins, and load-bearing frameworks**.
> - The opposite temperature coefficients allow design of **temperature-compensated composite systems** (e.g., metal springs in series with rubber).

### 2.4 Comparison Summary Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Comparison Table (Frequently Tested in KTU ESE)**

| Property | Metals | Rubbers |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Bonding | Metallic (non-directional) | Covalent C–C backbone, weak van der Waals between chains |
| Microstructure | Crystalline grains, dislocations | Amorphous network of crosslinked chains |
| Elastic origin | **Energy** (bond stretching) | **Entropy** (conformational change) |
| Young's modulus | $10^{10} - 10^{11}$ Pa | $10^{6} - 10^{7}$ Pa |
| Max elastic strain | $\le 1\%$ | $\approx 100 - 1000\%$ |
| Hookean? | Yes (small strain) | No — J-shaped stress–strain |
| Effect of heating | Softer ($E$ decreases) | Stiffer ($E$ increases) |
| Volume change on stretching | Slight increase | Nearly constant (incompressible) |
| Plastic deformation | Yes (dislocation glide) | No (crosslinks prevent flow) |
| Failure mode | Yield → fracture | Chain scission → fracture |
| Typical stress–strain curve | Linear up to yield, then plateau | Concave-up, S-shaped to fracture |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Thermodynamic Equation of State for Elasticity

We begin with the Helmholtz free energy $A$ of a material as a function of its length $L$, temperature $T$, and volume $V$:

$$A = U - TS$$

The elastic force $f$ required to hold the material at extension $L$ is, by definition:

$$f = \left(\frac{\partial A}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V}$$

Substituting $A = U - TS$:

$$f = \left(\frac{\partial (U - TS)}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V}$$

Applying the product rule:

$$f = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V} - T\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V}$$

> This is the **master thermodynamic identity** separating energy-elastic and entropy-elastic contributions. It is the foundation for understanding why metals and rubbers behave so differently.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Hooke's Law from the Interatomic Potential (Metals)

Let the interatomic pair potential between two neighboring atoms be $U(r)$, with equilibrium separation $r_0$. We Taylor-expand $U(r)$ about $r_0$:

$$U(r) = U(r_0) + \left.\frac{dU}{dr}\right|_{r_0} (r - r_0) + \frac{1}{2}\left.\frac{d^{2}U}{dr^{2}}\right|_{r_0} (r - r_0)^{2} + \frac{1}{6}\left.\frac{d^{3}U}{dr^{3}}\right|_{r_0} (r - r_0)^{3} + \cdots$$

Since $r_0$ is the equilibrium, $dU/dr \vert_{r_0} = 0$. Define the **force constant** $k \equiv d^{2}U/dr^{2}\vert_{r_0}$. Then for small displacement $\delta r = r - r_0$:

$$U(r) \approx U(r_0) + \frac{1}{2} k (\delta r)^{2}$$

The interatomic restoring force is:

$$f_{\text{bond}} = -\frac{dU}{dr} = -k\,\delta r$$

For $N$ parallel bonds per unit cross-sectional area $A$, with bond length $a$, the macroscopic stress and strain are:

$$\sigma = \frac{N}{A} f_{\text{bond}} = \frac{N}{A} k\,\delta r, \qquad \varepsilon = \frac{\delta r}{a}$$

Dividing:

$$E = \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon} = \frac{N k a}{A} = \text{constant}$$

> This linear proportionality is **Hooke's law**, valid for metals only when $\delta r \ll r_0$ (i.e., strains below $\sim 0.1\%$). Beyond this, the cubic anharmonic term dominates and the stress–strain curve becomes non-linear, eventually yielding plastically.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Entropic Force in a Single Polymer Chain

Consider a single Gaussian chain of $N$ freely-jointed segments, each of length $b$. The probability density for the end-to-end vector $\vec{R}$ is:

$$P(\vec{R}) = \left(\frac{3}{2\pi N b^{2}}\right)^{3/2} \exp\!\left(-\frac{3R^{2}}{2Nb^{2}}\right)$$

By Boltzmann's relation, the conformational entropy is $S = k_B \ln P(\vec{R}) + \text{const}$:

$$S(\vec{R}) = S_0 - \frac{3 k_B R^{2}}{2 N b^{2}}$$

where $S_0$ is a constant. Now constrain the chain ends to a separation $L$ along the $x$-axis (one-dimensional analogue of stretching):

$$S(L) = S_0 - \frac{3 k_B L^{2}}{2 N b^{2}}$$

The entropic force is then:

$$f = -T\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial L}\right)_{T} = -T \cdot \left(-\frac{3 k_B L}{N b^{2}}\right) = \frac{3 k_B T}{N b^{2}} L$$

> This shows that the **single-chain force is proportional to absolute temperature $T$** — a hallmark of entropy elasticity. The proportionality constant $\tfrac{3k_B T}{Nb^{2}}$ plays the role of a "chain spring constant."

---

### 3.4 Derivation of the Small-Strain Modulus of a Crosslinked Rubber Network

A crosslinked rubber contains $\nu$ network chains per unit volume. In the affine network model, each chain deforms affinely with the macroscopic strain. For uniaxial extension by factor $\lambda = L/L_0$, the change in entropy per chain is:

$$\Delta S_{\text{chain}} = -\frac{1}{2} k_B \left(\lambda^{2} + \frac{2}{\lambda} - 3\right)$$

Total entropy change per unit volume:

$$\Delta S_{\text{tot}} = -\frac{1}{2} \nu k_B \left(\lambda^{2} + \frac{2}{\lambda} - 3\right)$$

The free energy change per unit volume (with $\Delta U \approx 0$):

$$\Delta A = -T \Delta S_{\text{tot}} = \frac{1}{2} \nu k_B T \left(\lambda^{2} + \frac{2}{\lambda} - 3\right)$$

The nominal (engineering) stress $\sigma = \partial \Delta A / \partial \lambda$:

$$\sigma = \nu k_B T \left(\lambda - \frac{1}{\lambda^{2}}\right)$$

This is the **Neo-Hookean** constitutive equation. For small strains, $\lambda = 1 + \varepsilon$ with $\varepsilon \ll 1$. Taylor-expand $\lambda - \lambda^{-2}$ about $\lambda = 1$:

$$\lambda - \lambda^{-2} = (1+\varepsilon) - (1+\varepsilon)^{-2} \approx 1 + \varepsilon - (1 - 2\varepsilon + 3\varepsilon^{2} - \cdots) = 3\varepsilon - 3\varepsilon^{2} + \cdots$$

Keeping only the linear term:

$$\sigma \approx 3 \nu k_B T \cdot \varepsilon$$

Therefore, the **small-strain Young's modulus** of an ideal rubber network is:

$$\boxed{E_{\text{rubber}} = 3 \nu k_B T}$$

> **Critical contrast:** $E_{\text{rubber}} \propto T$, whereas $E_{\text{metal}}$ is independent of $T$ in the harmonic approximation and decreases with $T$ when anharmonic effects are included. This opposite sign of $(\partial E/\partial T)$ is one of the most testable differences in KTU examinations.

---

### 3.5 Derivative of Modulus with Respect to Temperature (Sign Analysis)

From the Helmholtz identity, the isothermal force is:

$$f = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial L}\right)_T - T\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial L}\right)_T$$

Differentiating with respect to $T$ at constant $L$:

$$\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial T}\right)_L = \left(\frac{\partial^{2} U}{\partial T\, \partial L}\right) - \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial L}\right)_T - T\left(\frac{\partial^{2} S}{\partial T\, \partial L}\right)$$

By the Maxwell relation $\left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial L}\right)_T = -\left(\dfrac{\partial f}{\partial T}\right)_L$, one can show that:

$$\left(\frac{\partial E}{\partial T}\right)_P = \frac{E}{T}\left[1 - \frac{T (\partial S / \partial L)_T}{f}\right]$$

**Metals:** $({\partial S}/{\partial L})_T$ is small, so the bracket $\approx 1$ but the temperature enters via anharmonic softening of bonds. The net result is $dE/dT < 0$.

**Rubbers:** $f \approx -T(\partial S/\partial L)_T$, so the bracket $\approx 2$, giving $dE/dT = +2E/T > 0$. Heating stiffens rubber.

> This is the celebrated **Gough–Joule effect**: a stretched rubber band contracts when heated. It is the experimental signature of entropy elasticity.

### 3.6 Summary of Key Numerical Estimates

| Quantity | Typical Value (Metal: Steel) | Typical Value (Rubber: NR) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Young's modulus $E$ | $200\ \text{GPa}$ | $0.002 - 0.01\ \text{GPa}$ |
| Yield strain | $\approx 0.2\%$ | $\approx 500\%$ (fracture strain) |
| $dE/dT$ | $-0.4\ \text{GPa/K}$ | $+0.00004\ \text{GPa/K}$ |
| Poisson's ratio $\nu_p$ | $0.30$ | $\approx 0.49$ (nearly incompressible) |
| Density | $7850\ \text{kg/m}^{3}$ | $920\ \text{kg/m}^{3}$ |
| Specific heat at constant $L$ | dominated by phonons | dominated by chain conformations |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define entropic elasticity. Why is rubber called an entropy-elastic material while metals are called energy-elastic?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Entropic elasticity is the mechanism by which a material develops a restoring force **entirely (or predominantly) due to a decrease in conformational entropy upon deformation**, with negligible change in internal energy.

> **Rubber is entropy-elastic** because:
> 1. It consists of long, flexible polymer chains crosslinked into a network.
> 2. At rest, each chain adopts a random coil with a very large number of possible conformations (high entropy $S$).
> 3. Stretching straightens the chain, drastically reducing the number of accessible conformations ($\Delta S < 0$).
> 4. The restoring force is $f = -T(\partial S/\partial L)_T$, which is purely entropic.

> **Metals are energy-elastic** because stretching directly increases the interatomic potential energy $U(r)$ as atomic bonds deviate from their equilibrium length $r_0$. The restoring force comes mainly from $f = (\partial U/\partial L)_T$, with entropy playing a minor role.

> **[1 mark]** Definition of entropic elasticity. **[1 mark]** Justification for rubber. **[1 mark]** Justification for metals.

---

#### **Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Compare the elastic moduli of metals and rubbers. Explain why heating affects them oppositely.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Property | Metals | Rubbers |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Young's modulus $E$ | $70 - 200\ \text{GPa}$ | $0.001 - 0.1\ \text{GPa}$ |
| $dE/dT$ sign | Negative (softens on heating) | Positive (stiffens on heating) |

> **[1 mark]** Magnitudes. **[1 mark]** Why metals soften: bond anharmonicity increases atomic vibration amplitude, lowering effective bond stiffness. **[1 mark]** Why rubbers stiffen: heating increases thermal agitation of chains, driving them toward higher-entropy coiled configurations and hence increasing the entropic restoring force $\propto T$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

#### Part (a) — Discuss the thermodynamic basis of elasticity in metals and rubbers. Derive the relevant equation of state and explain the dominant term in each case. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — State the Helmholtz Free Energy and Force Identity (2 marks)**

The Helmholtz free energy of a material at length $L$, temperature $T$, volume $V$ is:

$$A = U - TS$$

The elastic force at constant $T$ and $V$ is:

$$f = \left(\frac{\partial A}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V} = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V} - T\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V}$$

**Step 2 — Identify Energy-Elastic Behaviour of Metals (2 marks)**

In metals, stretching increases interatomic separation $r$ above $r_0$. The interatomic potential $U(r)$ rises along its anharmonic branch (Lennard-Jones or similar), so:

$$\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V} \gg 0 \quad \text{and dominant}$$

The entropy change is small because lattice vibrations change only slightly. Hence:

$$f_{\text{metal}} \approx \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V}$$

The restoring force is **bond-energy-driven**. Young's modulus $E = (1/V)(\partial^{2}U/\partial\varepsilon^{2})$ evaluated at $\varepsilon = 0$.

**Step 3 — Identify Entropy-Elastic Behaviour of Rubbers (2 marks)**

In a rubber, the C–C covalent bonds barely stretch. The internal energy change is negligible: $(\partial U/\partial L)_T \approx 0$. However, the random-coil chains are pulled from many accessible microstates into fewer elongated ones, decreasing entropy:

$$\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V} < 0$$

The restoring force is:

$$f_{\text{rubber}} = -T\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V} > 0$$

This is **entropy-driven**, and its magnitude is proportional to $T$.

**Step 4 — Temperature Coefficient Conclusion (1 mark)**

Differentiating $f$ with respect to $T$ gives:

- Metals: $\partial f/\partial T < 0$ → softens on heating.
- Rubbers: $\partial f/\partial T > 0$ → stiffens on heating.

This opposite sign is the **Gough–Joule effect**, an experimentally verifiable signature of entropic elasticity.

---

#### Part (b) — Derive the small-strain Young's modulus of an ideal rubber network and show that it is proportional to absolute temperature. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Conformation Statistics of a Single Chain (2 marks)**

For a Gaussian chain of $N$ freely-jointed segments of length $b$, the probability of an end-to-end distance $R$ is:

$$P(R) \propto \exp\!\left(-\frac{3R^{2}}{2Nb^{2}}\right)$$

The conformational entropy is:

$$S(R) = S_0 - \frac{3k_B R^{2}}{2Nb^{2}}$$

**Step 2 — Affine Network Approximation (2 marks)**

In a crosslinked network with $\nu$ chains per unit volume, each chain deforms affinely with macroscopic extension ratio $\lambda = L/L_0$. The entropy change per chain for uniaxial extension is:

$$\Delta S_{\text{chain}} = -\frac{1}{2}k_B\!\left(\lambda^{2} + \frac{2}{\lambda} - 3\right)$$

Total entropy change per unit volume:

$$\Delta S_{\text{tot}} = -\frac{1}{2}\nu k_B\!\left(\lambda^{2} + \frac{2}{\lambda} - 3\right)$$

**Step 3 — Free Energy and Stress (2 marks)**

Since $\Delta U \approx 0$:

$$\Delta A = -T\Delta S_{\text{tot}} = \frac{1}{2}\nu k_B T\!\left(\lambda^{2} + \frac{2}{\lambda} - 3\right)$$

The engineering stress:

$$\sigma = \frac{\partial \Delta A}{\partial \lambda} = \nu k_B T\!\left(\lambda - \frac{1}{\lambda^{2}}\right)$$

This is the **Neo-Hookean** equation.

**Step 4 — Linearization and Modulus (1 mark)**

For small strain $\varepsilon = \lambda - 1 \ll 1$:

$$\sigma \approx 3\nu k_B T\,\varepsilon$$

$$\boxed{E_{\text{rubber}} = 3\nu k_B T \propto T}$$

> **Result:** The small-strain modulus of an ideal rubber network is **directly proportional to absolute temperature**, in stark contrast to metals whose modulus is roughly temperature-independent (or slightly decreasing with $T$).

---

### **Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

#### Part (a) — Compare and contrast the stress–strain behavior of metals and rubbers. Sketch the typical curves and explain the microscopic origin of the differences. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Qualitative Description (2 marks)**

| Feature | Metal (Steel) | Rubber (NR) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Initial region | Linear, slope $E \approx 200$ GPa | Shallow, slope $E \approx 0.005$ GPa |
| Yield point | Sharp upper/lower yield | No yield point |
| Strain hardening | Mild up-turn before fracture | Strong up-turn (J-curve) |
| Fracture strain | $\approx 0.2 - 0.5\%$ | $\approx 500 - 1000\%$ |

**Step 2 — Metal Microscopic Origin (2 marks)**

In metals, the initial linear slope corresponds to elastic stretching of metallic bonds within the crystalline lattice. At the yield stress, dislocations begin to glide on slip planes, producing the plastic plateau. Strain hardening arises from dislocation entanglement and multiplication. Fracture occurs when microvoids coalesce or when the cleavage stress is reached.

**Step 3 — Rubber Microscopic Origin (2 marks)**

In rubber, the shallow initial slope arises because uncoiling of random-coil chains requires little force. As chains approach full extension, the entropic restoring force grows rapidly (since $\partial S/\partial R \propto R$), producing the steep J-shape upturn. Network chains cannot flow plastically due to chemical crosslinks; failure occurs by chain scission.

**Step 4 — Energy Storage (1 mark)**

The area under the stress–strain curve represents stored elastic energy per unit volume:

$$u = \int_0^{\varepsilon_f} \sigma\,d\varepsilon$$

Rubbers can store $\sim 10 - 100\ \text{MJ/m}^{3}$, vastly exceeding metals ($\sim 1\ \text{MJ/m}^{3}$), which is why rubber is used in energy-absorbing applications (tyres, earthquake dampers).

---

#### Part (b) — Explain the Gough–Joule effect. How does it experimentally establish that rubber elasticity is entropic? (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Statement of the Effect (2 marks)**

The Gough–Joule effect refers to two coupled observations in stretched natural rubber:
1. **Mechanical:** A rubber band under constant tension **contracts** when heated.
2. **Calorimetric:** A stretched rubber band **heats up** when rapidly allowed to contract (adiabatic retraction).

**Step 2 — Thermodynamic Analysis (3 marks)**

At constant length $L$ and pressure $P$, the thermal coefficient of force is:

$$\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial T}\right)_{L,P} = -\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial L}\right)_{T,P}$$

For rubber, $(\partial S/\partial L)_T < 0$ (stretching reduces entropy), so $(\partial f/\partial T)_L > 0$. This means that at fixed length, the tension **increases** with temperature. Equivalently, at fixed tension, increasing $T$ requires the rubber to **shorten** to maintain equilibrium — hence contraction on heating.

The adiabatic heating on retraction follows from $dQ = T\,dS = 0$ (adiabatic), so $dU = -f\,dL$. For $dL < 0$ (contraction), $dU > 0$, raising the temperature.

**Step 3 — Experimental Establishment of Entropic Origin (2 marks)**

The contraction on heating is the **inverse** of what ordinary metals exhibit (metals expand on heating at constant stress). This counter-intuitive behaviour is a direct macroscopic fingerprint of entropy elasticity. By measuring $(\partial f/\partial T)_L$ and $(\partial L/\partial T)_f$ independently and comparing with:

$$\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial T}\right)_{L,P} = -\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial L}\right)_{T,P}$$

one can compute the entropy contribution. For rubber, this term accounts for nearly $100\%$ of the elastic force, confirming entropic origin.

> **Historical note (not for marks):** The effect was discovered by John Gough (1805) and quantified by James Joule (1859). It is one of the foundational pieces of evidence for the kinetic theory of rubber elasticity.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU ESE:**
> 1. **Mixing up the signs in the thermodynamic identity.** The correct force equation is $f = (\partial U/\partial L)_T - T(\partial S/\partial L)_T$. Students often miss the **minus** sign on the entropy term — this single error loses 2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing "modulus increases with $T$" with thermal expansion.** A rubber stiffening on heating does NOT mean it expands more — it means its force at fixed strain grows. Always specify the constraint (constant $L$ vs constant $f$).
> 3. **Forgetting units.** Always quote Young's modulus in Pa (or GPa). Specifying only "modulus" without numerical value loses at least 0.5 mark.
> 4. **Drawing stress–strain curves without axes labels.** Always label $x$-axis as "Strain $\varepsilon$" or "Extension ratio $\lambda$" and $y$-axis as "Stress $\sigma$". Missing labels = –1 mark.
> 5. **Stating "rubber is Hookean."** It is **not** — Hooke's law is only an approximation valid for very small strains ($\varepsilon < 1\%$). The full response is Neo-Hookean or even more complex (Mooney-Rivlin).
> 6. **Omitting the Maxwell relation** when deriving $(\partial f/\partial T)_L$. The KTU examiner expects explicit use of $(\partial S/\partial L)_T = (\partial f/\partial T)_L$.
> 7. **Forgetting to mention crosslinks** when discussing rubber elasticity. Without crosslinks, rubber chains would flow viscously. The network structure is what enables a solid-like elastic response.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Final-Day Reading)**
> - **Two mechanisms of elasticity:** Energy (metals) vs Entropy (rubbers).
> - **Master equation:** $f = (\partial U/\partial L)_T - T(\partial S/\partial L)_T$.
> - **Hooke's law** applies to metals (small strain) and only approximately to rubber at very low strain.
> - **Gaussian chain:** $\Omega(R) \propto \exp(-3R^{2}/2Nb^{2})$.
> - **Neo-Hookean stress:** $\sigma = \nu k_B T (\lambda - \lambda^{-2})$.
> - **Rubber modulus:** $E_{\text{rubber}} = 3\nu k_B T \propto T$.
> - **Temperature coefficient:** $dE/dT < 0$ for metals; $dE/dT > 0$ for rubbers.
> - **Gough–Joule effect:** Stretched rubber contracts on heating; evidence for entropic origin.
> - **Strain capacity:** Metals $\le 1\%$; Rubbers up to $1000\%$.
> - **Energy storage density:** Rubber stores $10 - 100 \times$ more elastic energy per unit volume than metal.
> - **Poisson's ratio:** Metals $\approx 0.3$; Rubbers $\approx 0.49$ (nearly incompressible).
> - **Failure mode:** Metals yield by dislocation glide; Rubbers fracture by chain scission.
> - **Practical examples to cite in answers:** Tyres, dampers, seals (rubber); beams, gears, cables (metals).
> - **Common pitfall:** Always write the full Helmholtz decomposition; never write only Hooke's law and claim it explains rubber.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy Driven Elasticity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT402 - POLYMER PHYSICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Energy Driven Elasticity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Energy-driven elasticity** (also called *energetic elasticity* or *internal-energy elasticity*) is the component of the elastic restoring force in a deformed polymer network that originates from changes in the **internal energy** ($U$) of the chains rather than from changes in conformational entropy ($S$). For a polymer network held at constant temperature $T$ and volume $V$, the total elastic force $f$ at a deformation (extension) $L$ is the derivative of the **Helmholtz free energy** $A = U - TS$:

$$f = \left(\frac{\partial A}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V} = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V} - T\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V}$$

The first term is the **energetic contribution** $f_E$ and the second is the **entropic contribution** $f_S$. Energy-driven elasticity becomes dominant when the conformation of the chains does not change appreciably during deformation, e.g., during bond-angle or bond-length stretching.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In the KTU Polymer Physics course, Module 4 focuses on the *thermodynamic and statistical mechanical treatment of rubber elasticity*. Students must clearly distinguish between **entropic elasticity** (the classical rubber elasticity governed by chain conformations) and **energy-driven elasticity** (the energetic strain contribution arising from distorting bond angles, bond lengths, and weak van der Waals interactions).

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The Two-Spring Polymer Network

Imagine a polymer network as a **trampoline made of rubber bands**:

- **Entropic spring (the rubber bands themselves):** When you stretch the trampoline, each rubber band *straightens out* and loses the disorder (entropy) of its coiled shape. The pull-back force comes from the chain's *desire to coil back into many random conformations* — this is entropy-driven.
- **Energy spring (the bonds inside each rubber band):** Inside every rubber band there are chemical bonds. If you pull *very* hard, you start stretching the chemical bonds themselves and bending bond angles. The pull-back now comes from *stored potential energy* in the bonds trying to return to their equilibrium length — this is energy-driven.

> A real polymer exhibits **both** effects. For small/moderate strains, the entropic contribution dominates. For large strains or in stiff polymers (e.g., glassy polymers near $T_g$), the energetic contribution dominates.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for Examiners:**
> - **Entropic elasticity** → Force from disorder restoration (dominant in ideal rubber above $T_g$).
> - **Energy elasticity** → Force from bond deformation (dominant in stiff, glassy, or highly stretched networks).
> - Most real networks show a *mixture* quantified by the **energy/entropy ratio** $f_E / f$.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Value / Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $k$ | Boltzmann constant | $1.381 \times 10^{-23}\ \text{J/K}$ |
| $R$ | Universal gas constant | $8.314\ \text{J/(mol·K)}$ |
| $N_A$ | Avogadro's number | $6.022 \times 10^{23}\ \text{mol}^{-1}$ |
| $\nu$ | Network chain density (chains per unit volume) | $\text{mol/m}^{3}$ or $\text{chains/m}^{3}$ |
| $T_g$ | Glass transition temperature | K or °C |
| $T$ | Absolute temperature | K |
| $G$ | Shear modulus | Pa (N/m²) |
| $E$ | Young's modulus | Pa (N/m²) |
| $\lambda$ | Extension ratio (relative length) | dimensionless |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Thermodynamic Foundation

The Helmholtz free energy of a deformed polymer network can be decomposed into two physically meaningful parts:

$$A(T, L) = A_0(T) + A_E(L) + A_S(L)$$

where:
- $A_E(L)$ → change in internal energy due to bond distortions (energy part)
- $A_S(L)$ → change in conformational entropy of chains (entropy part)

The total elastic force is therefore:

$$f = f_E + f_S = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V} + T\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V}$$

### 2.2 The Energy/Entropy Force Ratio

A useful diagnostic parameter (introduced by Flory and Gee) is:

$$\frac{f_E}{f} = 1 - \frac{T}{f}\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial T}\right)_{L,V} = 1 - \left(\frac{\partial \ln f}{\partial \ln T}\right)_{L,V}$$

**Interpretation:**
- If $f_E / f = 0$ → elasticity is *purely entropic* (ideal rubber behavior).
- If $f_E / f = 1$ → elasticity is *purely energetic* (glass-like or bond-stretching).
- Typical lightly crosslinked rubbers at small strain: $f_E / f \approx 0.1$ to $0.2$, meaning ~80–90% of the force is entropic.

### 2.3 Gaussian Network Theory (Affine Model)

For an *ideal* rubber network of $N$ chains per unit volume, each chain having $n$ freely-jointed segments of length $l$, the entropic part of the free energy per unit volume is:

$$\Delta A_S = \frac{1}{2} \nu k T \left(\lambda_x^{2} + \lambda_y^{2} + \lambda_z^{2} - 3\right)$$

For an *incompressible* sample under uniaxial extension $\lambda$ (with lateral contractions $\lambda^{-1/2}$), the nominal stress (engineering stress) is:

$$\sigma = \nu k T \left(\lambda - \lambda^{-2}\right)$$

and the true (Cauchy) stress:

$$\tau = \nu k T \left(\lambda^{2} - \lambda^{-1}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **neo-Hookean** form. It describes the *entropic* part of elasticity only. The energy part must be added separately.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — it covers 90% of Module 4 numerical/derivative questions.**

| # | Equation | Meaning / When to Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $A = U - TS$ | Helmholtz free energy definition |
| 2 | $f = (\partial A / \partial L)_{T,V}$ | Total elastic force (definition) |
| 3 | $f = f_E + f_S$ | Additive decomposition of force |
| 4 | $f_E = (\partial U / \partial L)_{T,V}$ | Energy contribution (positive for stiff bonds) |
| 5 | $f_S = -T (\partial S / \partial L)_{T,V}$ | Entropic contribution (positive in ideal rubber) |
| 6 | $f_E / f = 1 - T/f \cdot (\partial f / \partial T)_{L,V}$ | Flory–Gee diagnostic ratio |
| 7 | $\Delta S = -\tfrac{1}{2} \nu k \left(\lambda_x^{2}+\lambda_y^{2}+\lambda_z^{2} - 3\right)$ | Gaussian entropy change per unit volume |
| 8 | $\Delta U = \tfrac{1}{2} \nu k T \cdot \beta \left(\lambda_x^{2}+\lambda_y^{2}+\lambda_z^{2} - 3\right)$ | Energetic part (with $\beta = f_E/f$ for affine model) |
| 9 | $\sigma = \nu k T (\lambda - \lambda^{-2})$ | Nominal stress (Gaussian, uniaxial) |
| 10 | $\tau = \nu k T (\lambda^{2} - \lambda^{-1})$ | True stress (Cauchy, uniaxial) |
| 11 | $G = \nu k T$ | Shear modulus (affine network) |
| 12 | $E = 3 \nu k T$ | Young's modulus (incompressible affine) |
| 13 | $G_{\text{ph}} = \frac{\nu k T}{2} (2 - f_E/f)$ | Phantom network modulus (James–Guth) |
| 14 | $C_1 = \tfrac{1}{2} \nu k T$ | Mooney–Rivlin constant (entropic) |
| 15 | $C_2 = \tfrac{1}{2} \nu k T \cdot f_E / f$ | Mooney–Rivlin constant (energy) |
| 16 | $\tau = 2(\lambda - \lambda^{-1})(C_1 + C_2 \lambda^{-1})$ | Mooney–Rivlin equation |
| 17 | $\sigma = K \cdot T$ (linear) | Entropic stress is proportional to $T$ |
| 18 | $W = \tfrac{1}{2} G (\lambda_1^{2}+\lambda_2^{2}+\lambda_3^{2} - 3)$ | Neo-Hookean strain energy density |
| 19 | $W = C_1(I_1 - 3) + C_2(I_2 - 3)$ | Mooney–Rivlin strain energy |
| 20 | $f_E/f = d \ln \langle r^{2} \rangle_0 / d \ln T$ | Flory's thermoelastic inversion |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Tire Design:** Knowing the $f_E/f$ ratio allows engineers to predict how a rubber compound's stiffness changes with temperature — critical for tire performance in summer vs. winter.
- **Elastomer Selection:** Polymers with low $f_E/f$ (e.g., natural rubber, polyisoprene) are ideal for high-elasticity uses; those with high $f_E/f$ are used for damping and energy absorption.
- **Smart Materials:** Energy-driven elasticity dominates in shape-memory polymers and certain hydrogels used in soft robotics.
- **Predictive Modeling:** The $f_E/f$ ratio is an input to constitutive models (Mooney–Rivlin, Ogden, Yeoh) used in finite element simulations of rubber components (seals, gaskets, vibration dampers).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I: Helmholtz Force Decomposition

**Goal:** Show that the total force equals the sum of an energetic part and an entropic part.

**Starting point:** The Helmholtz free energy is defined as

$$A = U - TS$$

For a reversible, isothermal deformation at constant volume, the work done on the system is stored as a change in $A$:

$$dA = dU - T\,dS$$

By the **first law** (no heat flow at constant $T$ after subtracting $T\,dS$):

$$dU = T\,dS + f\,dL \quad \text{(mechanical work only)}$$

Substituting back into the expression for $dA$:

$$dA = (T\,dS + f\,dL) - T\,dS = f\,dL$$

Therefore:

$$f = \left(\frac{\partial A}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V}$$

Now substitute $A = U - TS$ and differentiate with respect to $L$ at constant $T$ and $V$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f &= \left[\frac{\partial (U - TS)}{\partial L}\right]_{T,V} \\
&= \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V} - T\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V} \\
&= f_E + f_S
\end{aligned}
$$

with the identifications

$$f_E \equiv \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V}, \qquad f_S \equiv -T\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V}$$

**Conclusion:** The total restoring force decomposes additively into an energy term and an entropy term — this is the foundation of all thermoelastic analysis of polymers.

---

### 3.2 Derivation II: The Flory–Gee Energy/Entropy Ratio

**Goal:** Derive the experimental expression for $f_E / f$.

**Starting point:** We have the total force

$$f = f_E - T\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V}$$

Differentiate both sides with respect to $T$ at constant $L$ and $V$:

$$
\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial T}\right)_{L,V} = \left(\frac{\partial f_E}{\partial T}\right)_{L,V} - \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V} - T\left(\frac{\partial^{2} S}{\partial L \, \partial T}\right)_{V}
$$

**Key assumption (valid for many rubbers):** The energy contribution $f_E = (\partial U / \partial L)_{T,V}$ is *approximately independent of temperature*. That is, $(\partial f_E / \partial T)_{L,V} = 0$. Physically, this means the bond-stretching potential energy landscape does not shift much with $T$.

Under this assumption:

$$
\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial T}\right)_{L,V} = - \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V} - T\left(\frac{\partial^{2} S}{\partial L \, \partial T}\right)_{V}
$$

But note that $(\partial S / \partial L)_{T,V} = (f_S / T) = (f - f_E)/T$. Substituting and rearranging gives:

$$
f - f_E = T \left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial T}\right)_{L,V} - T \left(\frac{\partial^{2} S}{\partial L \, \partial T}\right) \cdot \text{... (continues after rearrangement)}
$$

A cleaner route: start from $f = f_E - T (\partial S / \partial L)_{T,V}$ and use the Maxwell relation that follows from the thermodynamic identity $dA = f\,dL - S\,dT - p\,dV$:

$$\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V} = -\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial T}\right)_{L,V}$$

This is the **crucial Maxwell relation** obtained by equating mixed second derivatives of $A$:

$$
\frac{\partial^{2} A}{\partial L \, \partial T} = \frac{\partial^{2} A}{\partial T \, \partial L}
\quad \Rightarrow \quad
-\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V} = \left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial T}\right)_{L,V}
$$

Substituting back into the force equation:

$$f = f_E + T\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial T}\right)_{L,V}$$

Solving for the energy ratio:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f - T\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial T}\right)_{L,V} &= f_E \\
\therefore \quad \frac{f_E}{f} &= 1 - \frac{T}{f}\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial T}\right)_{L,V}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **Flory–Gee equation**. It is a *measurable* quantity: by plotting force vs. temperature at fixed length, the slope $(\partial f / \partial T)_{L,V}$ gives the energetic contribution.

---

### 3.3 Derivation III: Energetic Part of the Gaussian Free Energy

**Goal:** Show how an energy term can be added to the Gaussian entropic free energy.

The classical **Gaussian (affine) free energy** (entropic only) for an incompressible network is:

$$W_S = \tfrac{1}{2} \nu k T \left(\lambda_1^{2} + \lambda_2^{2} + \lambda_3^{2} - 3\right)$$

Flory proposed a phenomenological extension that includes an energy contribution with the same functional form but a different prefactor:

$$W_E = \tfrac{1}{2} \nu k T \cdot \beta \left(\lambda_1^{2} + \lambda_2^{2} + \lambda_3^{2} - 3\right)$$

where $\beta = f_E / f$ is the Flory–Gee ratio. The total strain energy is then:

$$W = W_S + W_E = \tfrac{1}{2} \nu k T (1 + \beta)\left(\lambda_1^{2} + \lambda_2^{2} + \lambda_3^{2} - 3\right)$$

For uniaxial extension with $\lambda_1 = \lambda$, $\lambda_2 = \lambda_3 = \lambda^{-1/2}$ (incompressibility):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda_1^{2} + \lambda_2^{2} + \lambda_3^{2} - 3 &= \lambda^{2} + 2\lambda^{-1} - 3
\end{aligned}
$$

Differentiating $W$ with respect to $\lambda$ (engineering stress $\sigma = \partial W / \partial \lambda$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma &= \frac{\partial W}{\partial \lambda} = \tfrac{1}{2} \nu k T (1 + \beta) \cdot \frac{\partial}{\partial \lambda}\left(\lambda^{2} + 2\lambda^{-1} - 3\right) \\
&= \tfrac{1}{2} \nu k T (1 + \beta) \left(2\lambda - 2\lambda^{-2}\right) \\
&= \nu k T (1 + \beta)\left(\lambda - \lambda^{-2}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comparison with purely entropic neo-Hookean:**

$$\sigma_{\text{neo-Hookean}} = \nu k T (\lambda - \lambda^{-2})$$

So the energy contribution simply **renormalizes the front factor** by $(1 + \beta)$.

---

### 3.4 Derivation IV: Mooney–Rivlin Form

**Goal:** Show how the Mooney–Rivlin constants $C_1$ and $C_2$ connect to $f_E / f$.

A more general strain energy, valid for a wider range of strains, is:

$$W = C_1 (I_1 - 3) + C_2 (I_2 - 3)$$

where $I_1 = \lambda_1^{2} + \lambda_2^{2} + \lambda_3^{2}$ and $I_2 = \lambda_1^{2}\lambda_2^{2} + \lambda_2^{2}\lambda_3^{2} + \lambda_3^{2}\lambda_1^{2}$.

For uniaxial extension, with $I_1 = \lambda^{2} + 2\lambda^{-1}$ and $I_2 = 2\lambda + \lambda^{-2}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma &= \frac{\partial W}{\partial \lambda} = 2 C_1 \left(\lambda - \lambda^{-2}\right) + 2 C_2 \left(1 - \lambda^{-3}\right) \cdot \text{(after algebra)} \\
&= 2\left(\lambda - \lambda^{-1}\right)\left(C_1 + C_2 \lambda^{-1}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Comparing with the Flory form (which has $I_2$ dependence absent in the Gaussian model), one identifies:

$$C_1 = \tfrac{1}{2}\nu k T \quad \text{(entropic)}$$

$$C_2 = \tfrac{1}{2}\nu k T \cdot \beta \quad \text{(energetic)}$$

**Key insight for the examiner:** The $C_2$ constant in Mooney–Rivlin is essentially a measure of the **energy-driven** elasticity. If $C_2 = 0$, the material is purely entropic (neo-Hookean, Gaussian). The presence of $C_2$ reflects the energetic corrections due to non-Gaussian chain statistics, junction fluctuations, and entanglement constraints.

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A rubber sample at $L_0 = 0.10\ \text{m}$ is stretched to $L = 0.15\ \text{m}$ at $T = 300\ \text{K}$. The force is measured as $f = 12\ \text{N}$. When the temperature is raised to $T' = 320\ \text{K}$ (at the same $L$), the force becomes $f' = 13.0\ \text{N}$. Calculate $f_E / f$.

**Solution using Flory–Gee:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial T}\right)_{L,V} &\approx \frac{\Delta f}{\Delta T} = \frac{13.0 - 12.0}{320 - 300} = \frac{1.0}{20} = 0.05\ \text{N/K} \\
\frac{f_E}{f} &= 1 - \frac{T}{f}\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial T}\right)_{L,V} \\
&= 1 - \frac{300 \times 0.05}{12} \\
&= 1 - \frac{15}{12} = 1 - 1.25 = -0.25
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The negative value indicates that the energetic component is *negative* — i.e., the internal energy *decreases* upon stretching. This is sometimes observed in real rubbers where the unperturbed chain dimensions $\langle r^2 \rangle_0$ change with temperature. A small negative $f_E/f$ (~ −0.1 to −0.3) is typical for natural rubber.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Decomposition of Polymer Elasticity

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Total Elastic Force f]
    A --> B[Energy Contribution fE]
    A --> C[Entropy Contribution fS]
    B --> B1[Bond length distortion]
    B --> B2[Bond angle bending]
    B --> B3[Van der Waals interactions]
    C --> C1[Conformational entropy change]
    C --> C2[Chain straightening on stretching]
    B1 --> D[High strain regime]
    B2 --> D
    B3 --> D
    C1 --> E[Low to moderate strain regime]
    C2 --> E
    D --> F[Combined response of real polymer]
    E --> F
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Thermoelastic Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Input1[Deformation: extension L] --> Helmholtz[Helmholtz Free Energy A = U - TS]
    Input2[Temperature T] --> Helmholtz
    Helmholtz --> Diff[Differentiation: dA/dL at constant T,V]
    Diff --> Force[Total Force f]
    Force --> Split[Additive Split]
    Split --> FeBlock[Energy term: dU/dL]
    Split --> FsBlock[Entropy term: -T dS/dL]
    FeBlock --> Meas1[Measured by T-slope of force-strain curve]
    FsBlock --> Meas2[Measured by T-proportionality of force]
    Meas1 --> Ratio[Flory-Gee ratio fE / f]
    Meas2 --> Ratio
    Ratio --> Output[Output: Characterize energy vs entropy contribution]
```

### 4.3 Decision Tree: Choosing the Right Constitutive Model

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Polymer Network] --> Q1{Know strain range?}
    Q1 -- Small strain, ideal rubber --> NH[Neo-Hookean: sigma = nu kT lambda - lambda^-2]
    Q1 -- Moderate to large strain --> MR[Mooney-Rivlin: sigma = 2 lambda - lambda^-1 C1 + C2 lambda^-1]
    MR --> C1a[C1 = 0.5 nu kT: Entropic]
    MR --> C2a[C2 = 0.5 nu kT times beta: Energy]
    Q1 -- Very large strain --> OG[Ogden Model: series of power-law terms]
    Q1 -- Need to separate energy and entropy --> FG[Flory-Gee thermoelastic measurement]
    FG --> Beta[beta = fE / f]
    Beta --> MatSelection[Material Selection for Engineering Application]
    C2a --> MatSelection
    NH --> MatSelection
    OG --> MatSelection
```

### 4.4 Force–Temperature Diagnostic Plot

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    XAxis[X-axis: Temperature T / K]
    YAxis[Y-axis: Force f / N]
    XAxis --> Plot[Force vs Temperature at fixed L]
    Plot --> PosSlope{Positive slope?}
    PosSlope -- Yes, slope greater than f/T --> EntDom[Entropic dominant: fE/f is negative]
    PosSlope -- Yes, slope less than f/T --> MixDom[Mixed: small positive fE/f]
    PosSlope -- No, slope negative or zero --> EnergyDom[Energy dominant: fE/f is positive and large]
    EntDom --> Result1[Ideal rubber behavior]
    MixDom --> Result2[Real elastomer with constraints]
    EnergyDom --> Result3[Glassy polymer or stiff chain network]
```
