## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define energy-driven elasticity. How is it distinguished from entropy-driven elasticity in polymer networks?*

**Model Answer:**

Energy-driven elasticity is the component of the elastic restoring force in a polymer that arises from changes in the **internal energy** of the system (e.g., distortion of bond lengths, bond angles, and secondary interactions) during deformation. It is quantified by $f_E = (\partial U / \partial L)_{T,V}$.

**Distinction:**

| Aspect | Energy-driven | Entropy-driven |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Origin | Bond deformation | Conformational disorder |
| Dominant in | Stiff / glassy polymers | Soft rubbers above $T_g$ |
| Temperature dependence | Weak | Strong (linear in $T$) |
| Force equation | $f_E = (\partial U/\partial L)_{T,V}$ | $f_S = -T(\partial S/\partial L)_{T,V}$ |
| Contribution at small strain | Small | Dominant (~80–90%) |

**[3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 for each part of distinction]**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is the physical significance of the Flory–Gee ratio $f_E / f$? How is it determined experimentally?*

**Model Answer:**

The **Flory–Gee ratio** $f_E / f$ quantifies the relative contributions of internal energy changes and entropy changes to the total elastic force in a polymer network. Its value ranges from 0 (purely entropic) to 1 (purely energetic), with negative values possible (indicating internal energy *decreases* on stretching).

**Experimental determination:** The sample is held at fixed length $L$ and the force $f$ is measured as a function of temperature $T$. The Flory–Gee equation

$$\frac{f_E}{f} = 1 - \frac{T}{f}\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial T}\right)_{L,V}$$

is then used. The slope $(\partial f / \partial T)_{L,V}$ is read from the linear region of the $f$–$T$ plot.

**[3 marks: 1 for significance + 1 for the formula + 1 for the procedure]**

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A** [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]

**(a)** *Starting from the Helmholtz free energy, derive the additive decomposition of the elastic force in a polymer into an energy component and an entropy component.* **[7 marks]**

**(b)** *For a Gaussian affine network under uniaxial extension, derive the expression for the engineering stress including the energetic contribution. Discuss how the Flory–Gee ratio enters the Mooney–Rivlin formulation.* **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** *Derivation of the force decomposition:*

The Helmholtz free energy is defined as

$$A = U - TS$$

The first law of thermodynamics (at constant volume, no heat added) gives

$$dU = T\,dS + f\,dL$$

Substituting into $dA$:

$$dA = dU - T\,dS - S\,dT = f\,dL - S\,dT$$

At constant $T$ and $V$:

$$f = \left(\frac{\partial A}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V}$$

Differentiating $A = U - TS$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f &= \left[\frac{\partial(U - TS)}{\partial L}\right]_{T,V} \\
&= \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V} - T\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V} \\
&= f_E + f_S
\end{aligned}
$$

This proves the additive decomposition. **[7 marks: 2 for starting equation, 3 for the algebra, 2 for identification of the two terms]**

**(b)** *Gaussian stress with energy contribution:*

Starting from the Gaussian strain energy (entropic part only):

$$W_S = \tfrac{1}{2}\nu k T(\lambda_1^{2} + \lambda_2^{2} + \lambda_3^{2} - 3)$$

Flory's extension adds the energetic part with the same deformation dependence but multiplied by $\beta = f_E/f$:

$$W_E = \tfrac{1}{2}\nu k T \cdot \beta (\lambda_1^{2} + \lambda_2^{2} + \lambda_3^{2} - 3)$$

Total: $W = \tfrac{1}{2}\nu k T (1 + \beta)(\lambda_1^{2} + \lambda_2^{2} + \lambda_3^{2} - 3)$.

For uniaxial extension: $\lambda_1 = \lambda$, $\lambda_2 = \lambda_3 = \lambda^{-1/2}$:

$$\lambda_1^{2} + \lambda_2^{2} + \lambda_3^{2} - 3 = \lambda^{2} + 2\lambda^{-1} - 3$$

Engineering stress $\sigma = \partial W / \partial \lambda$:

$$\sigma = \nu k T (1 + \beta)(\lambda - \lambda^{-2})$$

**Connection to Mooney–Rivlin:** The Mooney–Rivlin form is

$$W = C_1 (I_1 - 3) + C_2 (I_2 - 3)$$

Comparison with Flory's form gives:

$$C_1 = \tfrac{1}{2}\nu k T \quad \text{(entropic)}$$

$$C_2 = \tfrac{1}{2}\nu k T \cdot \beta = \tfrac{1}{2}\nu k T \cdot \frac{f_E}{f} \quad \text{(energetic)}$$

Therefore, $C_2$ is the **Mooney–Rivlin signature of energy-driven elasticity**. A non-zero $C_2$ implies a non-zero $f_E / f$. **[7 marks: 2 for Flory form, 2 for uniaxial derivation, 2 for the Mooney–Rivlin connection, 1 for the final inference]**

---

#### **Question B (Alternative Choice)** [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** *Derive the Flory–Gee equation for the energy/entropy force ratio. State the Maxwell relation used and the physical assumption made.* **[7 marks]**

**(b)** *A rubber strip is stretched to a fixed elongation ratio $\lambda = 2.0$ at 300 K and the force is 8.5 N. When the temperature is raised isochorically to 320 K at the same $\lambda$, the force becomes 9.4 N. Calculate (i) the slope $(\partial f / \partial T)_{L,V}$, (ii) the Flory–Gee ratio $f_E / f$, and (iii) the relative contributions of energy and entropy to the total force. Comment on the type of network.* **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** *Flory–Gee derivation:*

Start from the additive force equation:

$$f = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V} - T\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V}$$

The Helmholtz free energy satisfies $A = U - TS$ and $dA = f\,dL - S\,dT - p\,dV$. By symmetry of mixed second partial derivatives:

$$\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V} = -\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial T}\right)_{L,V} \quad \text{(Maxwell relation)}$$

Substituting:

$$f = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V} + T\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial T}\right)_{L,V}$$

**Assumption:** The energetic part $f_E = (\partial U/\partial L)_{T,V}$ is *temperature-independent* in the range considered (i.e., the bond potential landscape is not shifted by temperature). This is well-satisfied for many rubbers at moderate temperatures.

Rearranging for the ratio:

$$\frac{f_E}{f} = 1 - \frac{T}{f}\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial T}\right)_{L,V}$$

This is the Flory–Gee equation. **[7 marks: 2 for starting equation, 2 for Maxwell relation, 2 for assumption, 1 for final result]**

**(b)** *Numerical computation:*

(i) Slope:

$$\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial T}\right)_{L,V} = \frac{9.4 - 8.5}{320 - 300} = \frac{0.9}{20} = 0.045\ \text{N/K}$$

(ii) Flory–Gee ratio at $T = 300$ K and $f = 8.5$ N:

$$\frac{f_E}{f} = 1 - \frac{300 \times 0.045}{8.5} = 1 - \frac{13.5}{8.5} = 1 - 1.588 = -0.588$$

(iii) Relative contributions:

- Entropy part: $f_S / f = 1 - f_E/f = 1 - (-0.588) = 1.588$ (i.e., 158.8% — formally larger than unity)
- Energy part: $f_E / f = -0.588$ (i.e., $-58.8\%$)

**Comment:** The negative $f_E/f$ means the internal energy *decreases* on stretching (the chain's unperturbed dimensions $\langle r^2 \rangle_0$ shrink with rising $T$). This is characteristic of *real polymer networks* (e.g., natural rubber, polyisoprene) where the unperturbed chain statistics depend on temperature. The total force is still positive because the entropic contribution overcompensates the negative energetic one. **[7 marks: 2 for slope, 2 for ratio calculation, 2 for contribution analysis, 1 for physical interpretation]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign in the Maxwell relation:** Students often write $\partial S / \partial L = +\partial f / \partial T$. This leads to an *incorrect* sign for $f_E/f$. Always derive it from $\partial^2 A / \partial L \partial T = \partial^2 A / \partial T \partial L$.
> 2. **Confusing nominal and true stress:** Engineering (nominal) stress $\sigma$ uses the *original* cross-section; true (Cauchy) stress $\tau$ uses the *instantaneous* cross-section. They differ by a factor of $\lambda$.
> 3. **Dropping the temperature-independence assumption:** The Flory–Gee equation is valid *only* when $f_E$ does not depend on $T$. If you skip stating this, you'll lose one full mark.
> 4. **Mixing up $\nu$ and $n$:** $\nu$ is the *number of network chains per unit volume*; $n$ is the *number of segments per chain*. Using them interchangeably is a common error.
> 5. **Ignoring incompressibility in uniaxial extension:** Always state $\lambda_1 \lambda_2 \lambda_3 = 1$ before substituting $\lambda_2 = \lambda_3 = \lambda^{-1/2}$.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Two contributions to elastic force:** $f = f_E + f_S = (\partial U/\partial L)_{T,V} - T(\partial S/\partial L)_{T,V}$.
- **Flory–Gee ratio:** $f_E / f = 1 - (T/f)(\partial f/\partial T)_{L,V}$ — directly measurable from force–temperature data at fixed $L$.
- **Maxwell relation (key tool):** $(\partial S/\partial L)_{T,V} = -(\partial f/\partial T)_{L,V}$.
- **Assumption behind Flory–Gee:** $f_E$ is independent of $T$.
- **Affine (Gaussian) engineering stress:** $\sigma = \nu k T (\lambda - \lambda^{-2})$ — *purely entropic*.
- **Affine (Gaussian) true stress:** $\tau = \nu k T (\lambda^{2} - \lambda^{-1})$.
- **With energy term included:** $\sigma = \nu k T (1 + \beta)(\lambda - \lambda^{-2})$, where $\beta = f_E/f$.
- **Neo-Hookean strain energy:** $W = \tfrac{1}{2} G (\lambda_1^{2} + \lambda_2^{2} + \lambda_3^{2} - 3)$.
- **Mooney–Rivlin:** $W = C_1 (I_1 - 3) + C_2 (I_2 - 3)$, with $C_1 = \tfrac{1}{2}\nu k T$ and $C_2 = \tfrac{1}{2}\nu k T \beta$.
- **$C_2$ is the Mooney–Rivlin signature of energy-driven elasticity** — non-zero $C_2$ means non-zero $f_E/f$.
- **Phantom network modulus (James–Guth):** $G_{\text{ph}} = (\nu k T / 2)(2 - f_E/f)$ — accounts for junction fluctuations.
- **Typical $f_E/f$ for natural rubber:** ~ $-0.1$ to $-0.3$ (small and *negative* — internal energy decreases on stretching).
- **Sign of $f_E$:** Negative $f_E$ means the chain's unperturbed dimensions *shrink* with increasing temperature; positive $f_E$ means they *expand*.
- **Incompressibility constraint:** $\lambda_1 \lambda_2 \lambda_3 = 1$ (volume preservation in rubber elasticity).
- **Physical constants to memorize:** $k = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K; $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K); $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ mol$^{-1}$.
- **Unit of $\nu$ in formulas:** Always check whether $\nu$ is in *chains/m³* (use $k$) or *mol/m³* (use $R$).
- **Engineering rule of thumb:** At small strains in natural rubber, ~ 80–90% of the elastic force is entropic and ~ 10–20% is energetic.
- **Two Maxwell relations to remember:** $(\partial S/\partial L)_{T,V} = -(\partial f/\partial T)_{L,V}$ and $(\partial S/\partial V)_{T,L} = (\partial p/\partial T)_{V,L}$.
- **Energy contribution dominates:** in glassy polymers, in highly stretched chains (finite extensibility), and in stiff backbone polymers (e.g., aromatic polyamides).
- **Entropy contribution dominates:** in lightly crosslinked rubbers above $T_g$, at small/moderate strains, in flexible-backbone polymers (e.g., cis-1,4-polyisoprene).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Entropy Driven Elasticity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT402 - POLYMER PHYSICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Entropy Driven Elasticity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Entropy-driven elasticity** is the elastic response of a polymeric network (elastomer) in which the **retractive force** arises almost entirely from a **decrease in configurational entropy** of the polymer chains upon deformation, rather than from a change in internal energy (enthalpy). In an ideal rubber, the Helmholtz free energy change under isothermal deformation is dominated by the entropic term, so that:

$$
\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V} \approx 0 \quad ; \quad f = -T\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V}
$$

This thermodynamic identity distinguishes **entropy elasticity** from the **energy elasticity** observed in metals, ceramics, and glassy polymers, where stretching directly raises the internal energy of stretched chemical bonds.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Entropy elasticity is the conceptual foundation of the **equation of state for an ideal rubber**, the **affine network model**, and the **thermoelastic inversion effect** — all of which are recurring questions in Module 4.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Tangle of Headphones

Imagine pulling a tangled ball of headphone wire out of your pocket. When you stretch it, you are forcing the polymer chain into a **less probable, more ordered** configuration. The natural "feeling" of the wire wanting to spring back to its tangled mess is *exactly* what an elastomer experiences when stretched. The wire does not change its chemical energy when stretched (its covalent bonds remain nearly relaxed) — only the **number of accessible arrangements** of its segments decreases.

| Phenomenon | Energy-Driven (Metal Spring) | Entropy-Driven (Rubber) |
|---|---|---|
| Origin of restoring force | Bond stretching (U rises) | Loss of conformations (S falls) |
| Effect of heating | Softens (modulus drops) | **Stiffens** (modulus rises linearly with T) |
| Tensile force at fixed L | Decreases with T | **Increases** with T |
| Typical strain | < 1% | Up to 500–1000% |

> [!NOTE]
> The classic experiment by **Gough (1805)** and **Joule (1859)** proved this dichotomy: a stretched rubber band actually **contracts** when warmed, and its retractive tension **increases** linearly with absolute temperature. This is the unmistakable fingerprint of entropy elasticity.

## 1.3 Why Polymers Exhibit Entropy Elasticity

A long-chain polymer in a rubbery network possesses an **astronomically large number of internal rotational states** (trans/gauche conformations around each backbone bond). This results in an enormous conformational entropy:

$$
S_{\text{conf}} = k \ln \Omega
$$

where $\Omega$ is the count of accessible microstates. Stretching the macroscopic sample biases the chains toward an extended conformation, sharply reducing $\Omega$ and hence $S$. The second law of thermodynamics then demands a spontaneous return toward the coiled, high-entropy state, manifesting as a **macroscopic retractive force**.

The polymer must satisfy **three structural prerequisites** to display pure entropy elasticity:

1. **Long flexible chains** — to provide a large conformational phase space.
2. **Permanent cross-links** ("tie points") — to prevent viscous flow and impart a "memory" of the original shape.
3. **Temperature well above $T_g$** — so segmental motion is facile and only the network junctions, not the chain chemistry, are stressed.

> [!TIP]
> **Cross-link density directly controls modulus:** More cross-links → fewer conformational states per chain → lower entropy drop on stretching → **higher** retractive stress for a given strain.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Thermodynamic Framework of Rubber Elasticity

The retractive force at constant temperature and volume is the derivative of the Helmholtz free energy:

$$
f = \left(\frac{\partial A}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V} = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V} - T\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V}
$$

For an **ideal rubber**, the energetic contribution is experimentally negligible:

$$
\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V} \approx 0
$$

Therefore, the equation collapses to a purely entropic expression:

$$
\boxed{\,f = -T\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V}\,}
$$

The corresponding **thermoelastic inversion relation** is one of the most frequently tested identities in KTU papers:

$$
\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial T}\right)_{L,V} = \frac{f}{T}
$$

If a rubber is stretched at fixed length, increasing temperature raises the retractive tension linearly — exactly what Joule observed.

## 2.2 Statistical Mechanics of a Gaussian Chain

A freely-jointed chain of $N$ bonds, each of length $\ell$, has a Gaussian distribution of end-to-end vector $\mathbf{r}$:

$$
P(\mathbf{r}) = \left(\frac{3}{2\pi N \ell^{2}}\right)^{3/2} \exp\!\left(-\frac{3 r^{2}}{2 N \ell^{2}}\right)
$$

The configurational entropy of a single chain is therefore:

$$
S_{\text{chain}}(r) = k \ln P(r) + C = S_{0} - \frac{3 k}{2 N \ell^{2}}\, r^{2}
$$

When the macroscopic sample is stretched by a principal stretch ratio $\lambda_{i}$ along axis $i$, the affine-network assumption maps each chain end-to-end vector as $r'_{i} = \lambda_{i} r_{i}$.

## 2.3 Affine Network Model — Master Equations

For a network of $n$ chains per unit volume, deformed at constant volume ($\lambda_{1}\lambda_{2}\lambda_{3}=1$), the **change in entropy per unit volume** is:

$$
\Delta s = -\frac{1}{2}\,n k \left[\lambda_{1}^{2} + \lambda_{2}^{2} + \lambda_{3}^{2} - 3\right]
$$

The corresponding **Helmholtz free energy density** becomes:

$$
\Delta a = \frac{1}{2}\,n k T \left[\lambda_{1}^{2} + \lambda_{2}^{2} + \lambda_{3}^{2} - 3\right]
$$

For **uniaxial extension** ($\lambda_{1}=\lambda$, $\lambda_{2}=\lambda_{3}=\lambda^{-1/2}$), the **nominal stress** (engineering stress) is:

$$
\sigma_{N} = n k T \left(\lambda - \lambda^{-2}\right)
$$

The **true stress** is:

$$
\tau = n k T \left(\lambda^{2} - \lambda^{-1}\right)
$$

The **Young's modulus** in the limit of small strain ($\lambda \to 1$) reduces to:

$$
E = 3 n k T
$$

This is the celebrated **equation of state of an ideal rubber** — it predicts that modulus is **directly proportional to absolute temperature**, in stark contrast to metals.

## 2.4 Phantom Network Model (Frontier Mention)

In the more refined **phantom network model**, the junctions are allowed to fluctuate around their mean positions by an amount controlled by the **cycle rank** $\xi$ of the network. The result is:

$$
\sigma_{N} = \xi k T \left(\lambda - \lambda^{-2}\right)
$$

with $\xi$ replacing $n$. This corrects the affine model at small deformations by predicting a **lower front-factor modulus**, which matches real rubber data more accurately.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $A = U - TS$ | Definition of Helmholtz free energy | Thermodynamic setup |
| 2 | $f = (\partial A / \partial L)_{T,V}$ | Retractive force at constant T, V | First-principles derivation |
| 3 | $f = -T(\partial S / \partial L)_{T,V}$ | Entropy-elastic force (ideal rubber) | Core identity |
| 4 | $(\partial f / \partial T)_{L,V} = f/T$ | Thermoelastic inversion relation | Numerical verification |
| 5 | $S(r) = S_{0} - (3k / 2 N \ell^{2}) r^{2}$ | Entropy of a Gaussian chain | Statistical base |
| 6 | $\Delta a = (1/2) n k T [\lambda_{1}^{2}+\lambda_{2}^{2}+\lambda_{3}^{2}-3]$ | Free energy of affine network | Strain-energy function |
| 7 | $\sigma_{N} = n k T (\lambda - \lambda^{-2})$ | Nominal stress (uniaxial) | Modulus prediction |
| 8 | $\tau = n k T (\lambda^{2} - \lambda^{-1})$ | True stress (uniaxial) | Real sample calculation |
| 9 | $E = 3 n k T$ | Small-strain modulus | Cross-link density measurement |
| 10 | $\sigma_{N} = \xi k T (\lambda - \lambda^{-2})$ | Phantom network stress | Refined modeling |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Tire design**: Engineers tune cross-link density (which sets $n$) and temperature to obtain the desired modulus in vehicle tires.
- **Elastomeric seals & O-rings**: Entropy-elastic recovery ensures leak-proof sealing across temperature cycles.
- **Soft robotics & artificial muscles**: Dielectric elastomers exploit entropy elasticity for reversible actuation.
- **Biomedical implants**: Silicone elastomers used in catheters rely on entropy-driven contraction for shape-memory behavior.
- **Vibration dampers**: The $E \propto T$ relationship allows rubber mounts to be calibrated against operating temperatures.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Equation of State for an Ideal Rubber

We will derive the complete expression $f = n k T (\lambda - \lambda^{-2})$ from first principles using the Gaussian chain model and affine network assumption.

### Step 1 — Entropy of a Single Gaussian Chain

A chain of $N$ freely-jointed segments of length $\ell$ obeys Gaussian statistics. The number of microstates producing end-to-end vector $\mathbf{r}$ is proportional to the probability density:

$$
P(\mathbf{r}) = \left(\frac{3}{2\pi N \ell^{2}}\right)^{3/2} \exp\!\left(-\frac{3 r^{2}}{2 N \ell^{2}}\right)
$$

Applying Boltzmann's relation $S = k \ln P + \text{const}$:

$$
S(r) = k \ln\!\left[\left(\frac{3}{2\pi N \ell^{2}}\right)^{3/2}\right] - \frac{3 k r^{2}}{2 N \ell^{2}} + C
$$

The first term is a constant absorbed into $S_{0}$, yielding:

$$
\boxed{\,S(r) = S_{0} - \frac{3 k}{2 N \ell^{2}}\, r^{2}\,}
$$

### Step 2 — Affine Mapping of Chain Vectors

In the affine network model, when the macroscopic sample is stretched by principal ratios $\lambda_{1}, \lambda_{2}, \lambda_{3}$, each end-to-end vector transforms component-wise:

$$
r_{i}^{\,'} = \lambda_{i}\, r_{i} \quad ; \quad i = 1, 2, 3
$$

The squared length changes from $r^{2} = r_{1}^{2} + r_{2}^{2} + r_{3}^{2}$ to $r^{\,'2} = \lambda_{1}^{2} r_{1}^{2} + \lambda_{2}^{2} r_{2}^{2} + \lambda_{3}^{2} r_{3}^{2}$.

### Step 3 — Entropy Change Per Chain

$$
\Delta S_{\text{chain}} = S(r^{\,'}) - S(r) = -\frac{3 k}{2 N \ell^{2}} \left[\lambda_{1}^{2} r_{1}^{2} + \lambda_{2}^{2} r_{2}^{2} + \lambda_{3}^{2} r_{3}^{2} - (r_{1}^{2} + r_{2}^{2} + r_{3}^{2})\right]
$$

Grouping:

$$
\Delta S_{\text{chain}} = -\frac{3 k}{2 N \ell^{2}} \left[(\lambda_{1}^{2}-1) r_{1}^{2} + (\lambda_{2}^{2}-1) r_{2}^{2} + (\lambda_{3}^{2}-1) r_{3}^{2}\right]
$$

### Step 4 — Ensemble Average Over an Isotropic Network

For an **isotropic** network in the undeformed state, the mean-square projections are equal:

$$
\langle r_{1}^{2} \rangle = \langle r_{2}^{2} \rangle = \langle r_{3}^{2} \rangle = \tfrac{1}{3}\, N \ell^{2}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\langle \Delta S_{\text{chain}} \rangle = -\frac{3 k}{2 N \ell^{2}} \cdot \tfrac{1}{3} N \ell^{2} \left[(\lambda_{1}^{2}-1) + (\lambda_{2}^{2}-1) + (\lambda_{3}^{2}-1)\right]
$$

The prefactor simplifies to $k/2$:

$$
\boxed{\,\langle \Delta S_{\text{chain}} \rangle = -\tfrac{1}{2} k \left[\lambda_{1}^{2} + \lambda_{2}^{2} + \lambda_{3}^{2} - 3\right]\,}
$$

### Step 5 — Total Network Entropy

If there are $n$ chains per unit volume and the sample volume is $V$, the total entropy change is:

$$
\Delta S_{\text{total}} = n V \langle \Delta S_{\text{chain}} \rangle = -\tfrac{1}{2} n V k \left[\lambda_{1}^{2} + \lambda_{2}^{2} + \lambda_{3}^{2} - 3\right]
$$

### Step 6 — Helmholtz Free Energy

Using $A = U - TS$ and assuming $U$ is constant (ideal rubber):

$$
\Delta A = -T \Delta S_{\text{total}} = \tfrac{1}{2} n V k T \left[\lambda_{1}^{2} + \lambda_{2}^{2} + \lambda_{3}^{2} - 3\right]
$$

### Step 7 — Uniaxial Extension Constraint

Apply **uniaxial extension** along axis 1 with constant volume $\lambda_{1}\lambda_{2}\lambda_{3}=1$:

$$
\lambda_{1} = \lambda \quad ; \quad \lambda_{2} = \lambda_{3} = \lambda^{-1/2}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\lambda_{1}^{2} + \lambda_{2}^{2} + \lambda_{3}^{2} = \lambda^{2} + 2 \lambda^{-1}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\Delta A = \tfrac{1}{2} n V k T \left[\lambda^{2} + 2 \lambda^{-1} - 3\right]
$$

### Step 8 — Force by Differentiation

The uniaxial nominal force is:

$$
f = \left(\frac{\partial \Delta A}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V} = \left(\frac{\partial \Delta A}{\partial \lambda}\right) \frac{1}{L_{0}}
$$

Since $\partial \Delta A / \partial \lambda = n V k T (\lambda - \lambda^{-2})$ and using $L = \lambda L_{0}$:

$$
\boxed{\,f = n k T \left(\lambda - \lambda^{-2}\right)\,}
$$

This is the **equation of state of an ideal rubber** under uniaxial tension.

### Step 9 — True Stress and Small-Strain Modulus

True stress (force per actual deformed area) follows from $dL/V \cdot d\lambda$ transformation:

$$
\tau = \frac{f}{\lambda} = n k T \left(\lambda^{2} - \lambda^{-1}\right)
$$

For an infinitesimally small strain ($\lambda = 1 + \epsilon$, $\epsilon \ll 1$), expand:

$$
\lambda - \lambda^{-2} \approx (1+\epsilon) - (1 - 2\epsilon) = 3\epsilon
$$

Hence $f \approx 3 n k T \epsilon$, giving a tensile modulus of:

$$
\boxed{\,E = 3 n k T\,}
$$

This linear-in-$T$ dependence is the **hallmark prediction** of entropy elasticity.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Thermoelastic Inversion Relation

Starting from the entropy-elastic force $f = -T (\partial S / \partial L)_{T,V}$, differentiate with respect to $T$ at fixed $L$ and $V$:

$$
\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial T}\right)_{L,V} = -\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V} - T \left(\frac{\partial^{2} S}{\partial T \partial L}\right)_{V}
$$

Apply a Maxwell-type symmetry $(\partial^{2} S / \partial T \partial L) = (\partial / \partial T)(\partial S / \partial L)$:

$$
\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial T}\right)_{L,V} = -\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V} - T \left[\frac{\partial}{\partial T}\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial L}\right)\right]_{V}
$$

Now use the thermodynamic identity $C_{L,V} = T (\partial S / \partial T)_{L,V}$ and the constraint that $U$ is independent of $L$ for ideal rubber, which leads to $(\partial S / \partial L)_{T,V} = -f/T$:

$$
\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial T}\right)_{L,V} = \frac{f}{T} - T \frac{\partial}{\partial T}\!\left(-\frac{f}{T}\right)_{V} = \frac{f}{T}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\,\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial T}\right)_{L,V} = \frac{f}{T}\,}
$$

This proves that the retractive tension at fixed length is **directly proportional to absolute temperature** — the essence of Joule's experiment.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Limiting Cases

| Limit | Behavior | Physical Insight |
|---|---|---|
| $\lambda \to 1$ (small strain) | $f \approx 3 n k T (\lambda - 1)$ | Linear Hookean region |
| $\lambda \gg 1$ (large elongation) | $f \approx n k T \lambda$ | Approaches finite-extensibility limit (Gaussian model breaks down; needs Langevin correction) |
| $T \to 0$ | $f \to 0$ | Rubber should freeze, no thermal motion |
| $T \uparrow$ at fixed $\lambda$ | $f \uparrow$ linearly | Rubber contracts on heating; modulus rises |
| Cross-link density $\uparrow$ | $n \uparrow$, $f \uparrow$ | Over-cured rubber becomes rigid and brittle |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Thermodynamic Flow of Entropy Elasticity

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Macroscopic Stretching Force f] --> B[Affine Deformation of Network]
    B --> C[Chain End-to-End Vector r increases]
    C --> D[Conformational Phase Space Shrinks]
    D --> E[Configurational Entropy S decreases]
    E --> F[Helmholtz Free Energy A increases]
    F --> G[Restoring Force acts to restore S]
    G --> H[Rubber returns to coiled state]
    H --> I[Macroscopic Retractive Tension f' = -T dS/dL]

    J[Thermal Energy kT] --> D
    K[Cross-link Density n] --> G
    L[Temperature T] --> F
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Comparison of Elasticity Mechanisms

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph EnergyElasticity["ENERGY ELASTICITY (Metals, Glass)"]
        EA[Stretching Force] --> EB[Bond Length Increases]
        EB --> EC[Internal Energy U Increases]
        EC --> ED[Restoring Force fU = dU/dL]
    end

    subgraph EntropyElasticity["ENTROPY ELASTICITY (Rubbers)"]
        SA[Stretching Force] --> SB[Chain Conformation Orders]
        SB --> SC[Entropy S Decreases]
        SC --> SD[Restoring Force fS = -T dS/dL]
    end

    EU1[Effect of Heating] --> EE1[Softens, Modulus Decreases]
    EU2[Effect of Heating] --> SE1[Stiffens, Modulus Increases Linearly with T]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Architecture of an Elastomeric Network

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph PolymerNetwork["ELASTOMERIC NETWORK ARCHITECTURE"]
        ChainA[Linear Chain Segment 1] --> Cross1[Cross-link Junction A]
        ChainB[Linear Chain Segment 2] --> Cross1
        ChainC[Linear Chain Segment 3] --> Cross2[Cross-link Junction B]
        ChainD[Linear Chain Segment 4] --> Cross2
        Cross1 --> ChainE[Linear Chain Segment 5]
        Cross2 --> ChainF[Linear Chain Segment 6]
        ChainE --> Cross3[Cross-link Junction C]
        ChainF --> Cross3
        ChainG[Linear Chain Segment 7] --> Cross3
    end

    Cross1 -.-> J1[Junction Fluctuates Phantom Model]
    Cross2 -.-> J1
    Cross3 -.-> J1

    Stress1[Applied Macroscopic Stress] --> ChainA
    Stress1 --> ChainC
    Stress1 --> ChainE
    Stress1 --> ChainG

    Stress1 --> Result[Network Distorts Affinely]
    Result --> Entropy1[Each Chain Entropy Drops]
    Entropy1 --> Restoring[Network Generates Retractive Force]
```

## 4.4 Schematic Flow — Equation of State Derivation Pathway

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Step1[Gaussian Chain Statistics] --> Step2[Single-Chain Entropy S r]
    Step2 --> Step3[Affine Network Assumption]
    Step3 --> Step4[Ensemble Average over Isotropic Chains]
    Step4 --> Step5[Total Entropy Change Delta S]
    Step5 --> Step6[Helmholtz Free Energy Delta A]
    Step6 --> Step7[Uniaxial Extension Constraint]
    Step7 --> Step8[Nominal Force f equals n kT lambda minus lambda to power -2]
    Step8 --> Step9[True Stress tau equals n kT lambda squared minus lambda to power -1]
    Step9 --> Step10[Small-Strain Modulus E equals 3 n kT]

    Theorem[Thermoelastic Inversion Theorem] --> Step9
    Theorem --> Step10
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams above are intentionally simplified to convey the **functional architecture** rather than to depict physical microstructure. For detailed real-space polymer visualization, students are advised to refer to standard polymer physics textbooks that contain molecular-model figures.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define entropy elasticity. Why is rubber elasticity considered entropy driven rather than energy driven?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Entropy elasticity is the elastic behavior of a polymer network in which the retractive force arises from the decrease in **configurational entropy** of the polymer chains upon deformation, rather than from a change in internal energy.

**Mechanism (1 mark):** When a rubber is stretched, the polymer chains are forced from a coiled (high-entropy) state toward an extended (low-entropy) state. Since the entropy change is large and the internal energy change is negligibly small, the restoring force is given by:

$$
f = -T\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V}
$$

**Comparison with metals (1 mark):** In metals and glassy polymers, stretching increases the potential energy of distorted bonds, so $f = (\partial U / \partial L)_{T,V}$. In contrast, an ideal rubber satisfies $(\partial U / \partial L)_{T,V} \approx 0$, and the force is purely entropic. This is confirmed by Joule's experiment: a stretched rubber contracts on heating, proving the entropy-driven origin.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**State and explain the thermoelastic inversion relation. What is its physical significance?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Statement (1 mark):**

$$
\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial T}\right)_{L,V} = \frac{f}{T}
$$

**Explanation (1 mark):** At constant length $L$ and volume $V$, the retractive force $f$ is directly proportional to absolute temperature $T$. Equivalently, a stretched rubber contracts upon heating.

**Physical significance (1 mark):** This relation confirms the **entropy-driven origin** of rubber elasticity, because:
- If elasticity were energy-driven, heating would weaken bonds and decrease force.
- The observed linear-in-$T$ increase in force is the unmistakable signature of entropy elasticity. It also provides an experimental route to measure $n$ (cross-link density) by plotting $f$ versus $T$ at fixed elongation.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) with Internal Choice

---

### **Question B — Choice A** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### (a) **Derive the equation of state for an ideal rubber under uniaxial extension starting from the Gaussian chain model.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Gaussian chain entropy (1.5 marks):**

For a freely-jointed chain of $N$ segments of length $\ell$:

$$
P(\mathbf{r}) = \left(\frac{3}{2\pi N \ell^{2}}\right)^{3/2} \exp\!\left(-\frac{3 r^{2}}{2 N \ell^{2}}\right)
$$

$$
S(r) = S_{0} - \frac{3 k}{2 N \ell^{2}} r^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Affine deformation (1.5 marks):**

Under macroscopic stretches $\lambda_{1}, \lambda_{2}, \lambda_{3}$:

$$
r_{i}^{\,'} = \lambda_{i} r_{i}
$$

Entropy change per chain:

$$
\Delta S_{\text{chain}} = -\frac{3 k}{2 N \ell^{2}}\left[(\lambda_{1}^{2}-1) r_{1}^{2} + (\lambda_{2}^{2}-1) r_{2}^{2} + (\lambda_{3}^{2}-1) r_{3}^{2}\right]
$$

**Step 3 — Ensemble average (1.5 marks):**

Using $\langle r_{i}^{2} \rangle = \tfrac{1}{3} N \ell^{2}$ for isotropic network:

$$
\langle \Delta S_{\text{chain}} \rangle = -\tfrac{1}{2} k \left[\lambda_{1}^{2} + \lambda_{2}^{2} + \lambda_{3}^{2} - 3\right]
$$

**Step 4 — Free energy and force (2 marks):**

$$
\Delta A = \tfrac{1}{2} n V k T \left[\lambda_{1}^{2} + \lambda_{2}^{2} + \lambda_{3}^{2} - 3\right]
$$

For uniaxial extension with $\lambda_{2} = \lambda_{3} = \lambda^{-1/2}$:

$$
f = \frac{1}{L_{0}} \frac{\partial \Delta A}{\partial \lambda} = n k T \left(\lambda - \lambda^{-2}\right)
$$

This is the desired equation of state.

**Step 5 — Small strain modulus (0.5 mark):**

For $\lambda \to 1$: $E = 3 n k T$.

---

#### (b) **Explain the affine network model and the phantom network model. Compare their predictions of the front-factor modulus.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Affine Network Model (3 marks):**

- Proposed by **Wall** and **Flory**, the model assumes that the macroscopic deformation is transferred **affinely** (linearly) to all cross-link junctions.
- Each junction moves in exact proportion to the bulk strain: $\mathbf{R}_{i}' = \lambda \mathbf{R}_{i}$.
- Fluctuations of junctions are completely suppressed by surrounding chains.
- Predicted front-factor modulus: $G_{\text{aff}} = n k T$, where $n$ is the number of elastically active chains per unit volume.
- Free energy per unit volume:

$$
\Delta a = \tfrac{1}{2} n k T \left[\lambda_{1}^{2} + \lambda_{2}^{2} + \lambda_{3}^{2} - 3\right]
$$

**Phantom Network Model (3 marks):**

- Proposed by **James and Guth**, the model treats the network as a collection of **phantom chains** whose junctions are free to fluctuate under Gaussian constraints.
- The amplitude of junction fluctuations depends on the **cycle rank** $\xi$ of the network (number of independent loops).
- Predicted front-factor modulus: $G_{\text{ph}} = (\xi / V_{0}) k T = (\xi - 2) k T / 2 V_{0}$ for a perfect network.
- For a perfect network: $\xi = (n/2)$, so $G_{\text{ph}} = (n/2) k T$, which is exactly half of the affine prediction.

**Comparison (1 mark):**

| Parameter | Affine Model | Phantom Model |
|---|---|---|
| Junction motion | Locked (no fluctuation) | Free Gaussian fluctuation |
| Front factor $G$ | $n k T$ | $\sim (n/2) k T$ |
| Real rubber data | Predicts modulus too high | Predicts modulus too low |
| Best fit to experiment | Better at large strain | Better at small strain |

Real networks show behavior intermediate to these two limits, often described by the **constrained-junction model** of Flory-Erman.

---

### **Question B — Choice B** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### (a) **Discuss the thermodynamic basis of rubber elasticity. Show that for an ideal rubber, the equation of state is $f = n k T (\lambda - \lambda^{-2})$.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Thermodynamic setup (3 marks):**

The retractive force at constant temperature and volume is given by the derivative of the Helmholtz free energy:

$$
f = \left(\frac{\partial A}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V} = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V} - T \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V}
$$

For an **ideal rubber**, experiments (Gough-Joule effect, force-temperature measurements) confirm that the internal energy is essentially independent of deformation:

$$
\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V} \approx 0
$$

Thus the force is purely entropic:

$$
f = -T \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V}
$$

**Statistical mechanical evaluation (3 marks):**

From the Gaussian chain entropy $S(r) = S_{0} - (3k/2N\ell^{2}) r^{2}$ and the affine mapping $r_{i}' = \lambda_{i} r_{i}$, the entropy change per chain is:

$$
\Delta S_{\text{chain}} = -\tfrac{1}{2} k \left[\lambda_{1}^{2} + \lambda_{2}^{2} + \lambda_{3}^{2} - 3\right]
$$

Summing over $n V$ chains:

$$
\Delta A = \tfrac{1}{2} n V k T \left[\lambda_{1}^{2} + \lambda_{2}^{2} + \lambda_{3}^{2} - 3\right]
$$

For uniaxial extension with volume conservation ($\lambda_{2} = \lambda_{3} = \lambda^{-1/2}$):

$$
\Delta A = \tfrac{1}{2} n V k T \left[\lambda^{2} + 2 \lambda^{-1} - 3\right]
$$

**Force derivation (1 mark):**

$$
f = \frac{1}{L_{0}} \frac{\partial \Delta A}{\partial \lambda} = \frac{n V k T}{L_{0}} (\lambda - \lambda^{-2}) = n k T (\lambda - \lambda^{-2})
$$

This is the **equation of state for an ideal rubber**.

---

#### (b) **Explain the Gough-Joule effect. How does it experimentally confirm the entropy-driven nature of rubber elasticity?** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Gough's experiment (1805) — qualitative (2 marks):**

- A stretched rubber band, when heated rapidly, contracts visibly.
- A rubber band under constant tension pulls a weight **upward** when heated.

**Joule's experiment (1859) — quantitative (3 marks):**

- A rubber strip was held at fixed elongation $L$ and the retractive tension $f$ was measured as a function of temperature $T$.
- The data plotted as $f$ versus $T$ gave a **straight line through the origin** (for an ideal rubber).
- The slope satisfied exactly $(\partial f / \partial T)_{L,V} = f/T$.

**Theoretical confirmation (2 marks):**

From the entropy-elastic force:

$$
f = -T \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V}
$$

differentiating at fixed $L$:

$$
\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial T}\right)_{L,V} = -\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V} = \frac{f}{T}
$$

This linear $f \propto T$ relationship is **unique to entropy elasticity**. In an energy-elastic solid, $f$ would *decrease* with $T$ because thermal expansion softens bonds. The opposite observation in rubber is irrefutable proof that rubber elasticity is entropy driven.

**Significance:** The Gough-Joule effect forms the experimental backbone of the entropy-elasticity theory and is the most commonly cited qualitative test question in KTU examinations.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Typically Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the volume conservation constraint** $\lambda_{1}\lambda_{2}\lambda_{3}=1$ in uniaxial derivation. Always state this explicitly before substituting.
> 2. **Mixing nominal and true stress**. The equation $f = n k T (\lambda - \lambda^{-2})$ is **nominal stress** (force / unstrained area); the **true stress** has $\lambda^{2} - \lambda^{-1}$.
> 3. **Confusing energy and entropy elasticity**. Always mention that $(\partial U/\partial L)_{T,V} \approx 0$ for ideal rubber; this is the assumption that makes the derivation work.
> 4. **Missing the $\tfrac{1}{2}$ factor** in the front-factor modulus. Students often write $E = n k T$ instead of $E = 3 n k T$.
> 5. **Skipping the ensemble average step** in derivations. Marks are explicitly allocated for $\langle r_{i}^{2} \rangle = \tfrac{1}{3} N \ell^{2}$.
> 6. **Ignoring units**: $n$ has units of chains/m³, so $n k T$ has units of Pa, which students should verify.
> 7. **Mixing up affine and phantom models**. A clear side-by-side table earns extra marks in comparative questions.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- **Entropy elasticity** = elastic response governed by chain conformational entropy, not bond energy.
- **Helmholtz free energy**: $A = U - TS$; for ideal rubber, $(\partial U / \partial L)_{T,V} = 0$.
- **Core force equation**: $f = -T (\partial S / \partial L)_{T,V}$.
- **Thermoelastic inversion**: $(\partial f / \partial T)_{L,V} = f/T$ — proves entropy-driven origin.
- **Gaussian chain entropy**: $S(r) = S_{0} - (3k/2N\ell^{2}) r^{2}$.
- **Affine network free energy**: $\Delta a = \tfrac{1}{2} n k T [\lambda_{1}^{2} + \lambda_{2}^{2} + \lambda_{3}^{2} - 3]$.
- **Equation of state (uniaxial)**: $f = n k T (\lambda - \lambda^{-2})$ — **memorize this**.
- **True stress**: $\tau = n k T (\lambda^{2} - \lambda^{-1})$.
- **Small-strain modulus**: $E = 3 n k T$ — increases linearly with $T$.
- **Phantom network modulus**: $G_{\text{ph}} \sim (n/2) k T$, half of affine model.
- **Gough-Joule effect**: stretched rubber contracts when heated — the key experimental signature.
- **Three prerequisites** for entropy elasticity: long flexible chains, permanent cross-links, $T > T_g$.
- **Engineering impact**: tires, seals, O-rings, soft robotics, biomedical implants, vibration isolators.
- **Common KTU question patterns**: derive force equation (7 marks), explain Gough-Joule (7 marks), compare affine vs phantom (7 marks).
- **Pitfalls to avoid**: forgetting volume conservation, mixing nominal/true stress, omitting $(\partial U / \partial L) = 0$ assumption, missing ensemble average step.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute revision strategy:** Write down the four equations $f = -T(\partial S/\partial L)$, $(\partial f/\partial T)_{L,V}=f/T$, $f = n k T(\lambda - \lambda^{-2})$, and $E = 3 n k T$ on a single sheet. Over 80% of KTU Module 4 questions on this topic derive from these four identities.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermodynamics of Rubber Elasticity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT402 - POLYMER PHYSICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Thermodynamics of Rubber Elasticity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Rubber Elasticity** is a unique mechanical response exhibited by polymer networks above their glass transition temperature ($T_g$), characterized by a large, reversible deformability (strains up to **500–1000 %**) under a small applied stress. Unlike conventional solids (metals, ceramics, glasses) whose elasticity arises from the storage of **internal (enthalpic) energy** through bond stretching, rubber elasticity is predominantly **entropic in origin**.

From a **thermodynamic** standpoint, the restoring force in a deformed elastomer is governed not by changes in bond energy, but by the tendency of the system to maximize its **configurational entropy** ($S$) as the polymer chains return from a stretched, ordered state toward a coiled, disordered (higher-entropy) state.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Module 4 (Rubber Elasticity):**
> The thermodynamic treatment of rubber elasticity establishes that the **tensile force $f$** is the partial derivative of the **Helmholtz free energy $A$** with respect to length at constant temperature and volume:
> $$f = \left(\frac{\partial A}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V} = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V} - T\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V}$$
> This equation is the **cornerstone** of the thermodynamic analysis of rubber and the most frequently tested derivation in KTU examinations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — A "Spaghetti Bowl" View

Imagine a bowl of cooked spaghetti left to cool. Each strand is curled, twisted, and randomly oriented — this is the **unstressed, high-entropy state** of a rubber network.

Now grab a handful of strands and pull them out of the bowl:
- The strands **straighten** and align parallel to your pull.
- There are **fewer spatial arrangements** (lower entropy).
- When you release, the strands spontaneously **snap back** to their tangled, higher-entropy state.

This is **exactly** how a rubber band works! The force you feel is not because chemical bonds stretched (they barely did), but because the chains were forced into a **less probable configuration**, and the universe "wants" to restore the most probable (highest-entropy) state.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key distinction for exams:**
> - **Metals/Glasses** → Elasticity = energy storage (enthalpic), $\Delta S \approx 0$
> - **Rubber/Elastomers** → Elasticity = entropy change, $\Delta U \approx 0$
> - **Ideal rubber** → Pure entropic; real rubber has small (~5–15 %) enthalpic contribution.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Absolute temperature | $T$ | 298 (room) | **K** |
| Gas constant | $R$ | **8.314** | J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹ |
| Boltzmann's constant | $k_B$ | **1.381 × 10⁻²³** | J·K⁻¹ |
| Avogadro's number | $N_A$ | **6.022 × 10²³** | mol⁻¹ |
| Number of network chains | $N$ | 10²⁰–10²² | chains/m³ |
| Front factor (affine network) | $A$ | 1 | dimensionless |
| Young's modulus (small strain) | $E$ | **0.1–10** | MPa |
| Tensile strength (natural rubber) | $\sigma_b$ | **20–30** | MPa |
| Elongation at break | $\lambda_b$ | **5–10** | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Characteristic Features of Rubber Elasticity (Board-Favorite):**
> 1. **High elastic recoverability** — nearly complete strain recovery on unloading.
> 2. **Entropic origin** — $f$ increases with $T$ (at fixed $L$) for ideal rubber.
> 3. **Non-linear stress–strain** — governed by the **Gaussian (or non-Gaussian) chain statistics** rather than Hooke's law.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Thermodynamic Framework

Rubber elasticity is treated by applying the **First and Second Laws of Thermodynamics** to a deformable polymeric network. The three fundamental thermodynamic potentials used are:

- **Internal Energy** $U$ — total energy stored
- **Helmholtz Free Energy** $A = U - TS$ — used for processes at constant $T$ and $V$
- **Gibbs Free Energy** $G = H - TS = U + PV - TS$ — used for processes at constant $T$ and $P$

For a rubber strip under **uniaxial tension** at constant $T$ and $P$, the appropriate potential is the **Gibbs free energy**, and the tensile force is:
$$f = \left(\frac{\partial G}{\partial L}\right)_{T,P}$$

At constant $T$ and $V$, the **Helmholtz free energy** gives:
$$f = \left(\frac{\partial A}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V}$$

### 2.1.1 The Two Contributions to Force

Expanding the Helmholtz expression:
$$f = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V} - T\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V}$$

This splits the force into two components:

| Term | Name | Physical Meaning | Magnitude in Rubber |
|---|---|---|---|
| $f_U = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V}$ | **Enthalpic (energy) contribution** | Change in internal energy due to bond deformation | **Small (5–15 %)** |
| $f_S = -T\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V}$ | **Entropic contribution** | Tendency to maximize configurational entropy | **Dominant (85–95 %)** |

> [!NOTE]
> In an **ideal rubber**, $f_U = 0$ and the entire force is entropic. In **real rubbers**, small deviations from ideality arise from the finite extensibility of chains, strain-induced crystallization, and volume changes.

## 2.2 The Equation of State for Rubber

By combining the first and second laws, the **thermodynamic equation of state** for a rubber network is obtained:

$$f = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V} + T\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial T}\right)_{L,V}$$

This is a powerful result because it allows the **measurement of internal energy changes** purely from force–temperature data at constant length and volume.

### 2.2.1 The Gough–Joule Effect

This experimentally observed phenomenon describes the **thermo-mechanical coupling** in rubber:
- A **stretched rubber band heats up** when rapidly stretched (adiabatic).
- A **rubber band under constant tension contracts** when heated (isothermal).
- Conversely, cooling a stretched band makes it **contract further**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Gough–Joule effect is **direct macroscopic evidence** of the entropic nature of rubber elasticity. It is a frequently asked 3-mark question in KTU exams.

## 2.3 Force–Temperature Coefficient

The **fractional change of force with temperature** at constant length defines two important quantities:

$$\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial T}\right)_{L,V} = \text{force-temperature coefficient at constant }L\text{ and }V$$

The **thermoelastic inversion temperature** $T_i$ is defined as the temperature at which:
$$\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial T}\right)_{L,V} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad f = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V}$$

Below $T_i$, rubber behaves like a normal solid (enthalpic); above $T_i$, it behaves entropically.

## 2.4 Connection to Statistical Mechanics

While Module 4 is thermodynamic, the **microscopic underpinning** is briefly:

For an **affine Gaussian network** (Flory's model), the entropy change on stretching from length $L_0$ to $L$ is:
$$\Delta S = -\tfrac{1}{2} N k_B \left(\lambda^2 + \tfrac{2}{\lambda} - 3\right)$$

where $\lambda = L / L_0$ is the **extension ratio** and $N$ is the number of elastically active chains.

For an **ideal rubber** ($\Delta U = 0$), the entropic force becomes:
$$f = -T\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V} = N k_B T \left(\lambda - \frac{1}{\lambda^2}\right)\cdot\frac{1}{L_0}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:**
> - **Tire design:** Thermoelastic behavior determines heat buildup during rolling.
> - **Elastomeric seals:** Performance across temperature extremes depends on the force–temperature law.
> - **Shape-memory polymers:** The entropic–enthalpic transition is exploited.
> - **Biomedical devices:** Catheter balloons, stents, and artificial muscles rely on entropic recovery.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f = (\partial A / \partial L)_{T,V}$ | Tensile force from Helmholtz potential | Constant-volume analysis |
| 2 | $f = (\partial U / \partial L)_{T,V} - T(\partial S / \partial L)_{T,V}$ | Force = energy + entropic term | Splitting contributions |
| 3 | $f = (\partial U / \partial L)_{T,V} + T(\partial f / \partial T)_{L,V}$ | **Equation of state of rubber** | Experimental determination of $\Delta U$ |
| 4 | $f_e = -T(\partial S / \partial L)_{T,V}$ | **Entropic force** | Ideal rubber |
| 5 | $f = N k_B T (\lambda - 1/\lambda^2)/L_0$ | Affine Gaussian force | Uniaxial extension |
| 6 | $f = N k_B T (\lambda - 1/\lambda^2) \cdot (\text{front factor})$ | Flory–Wall front factor included | Real networks |
| 7 | $G = N k_B T$ | **Shear modulus** of ideal rubber | Small-strain modulus |
| 8 | $E = 3N k_B T$ | **Young's modulus** of ideal rubber | Small-strain modulus |
| 9 | $T_i$ where $(\partial f / \partial T)_{L,V} = 0$ | **Thermoelastic inversion** | Material characterization |
| 10 | $\Delta S = -\tfrac{1}{2} N k_B (\lambda^2 + 2/\lambda - 3)$ | Entropy change on stretching | Statistical derivation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units Alert for KTU Board Exams:**
> - $f$ → Newtons (**N**)
> - $N k_B T$ → Joules per cubic meter, or equivalently **Pa** (since stress)
> - $(\partial f / \partial T)$ → **N·K⁻¹**
> - $(\partial U / \partial L)$ → **N** (same as force)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I: Tensile Force from the First and Second Laws

### Starting Point
The First Law of Thermodynamics for a deformable system:
$$dU = \delta Q + \delta W$$

The Second Law (reversible process):
$$dS = \frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \delta Q_{rev} = T\,dS$$

The work done on the rubber strip (uniaxial tension, neglecting $-P\,dV$):
$$\delta W = f\,dL$$

### Step-by-Step Combination

Substituting the Second Law into the First Law:
$$dU = T\,dS + f\,dL$$

### Define Helmholtz Free Energy

$$A = U - TS \quad \Rightarrow \quad dA = dU - T\,dS - S\,dT$$

Substitute $dU = T\,dS + f\,dL$:
$$dA = (T\,dS + f\,dL) - T\,dS - S\,dT = f\,dL - S\,dT$$

### Identify Partial Derivatives

At constant $T$:
$$\left(\frac{\partial A}{\partial L}\right)_{T} = f$$

To make it explicit at constant $V$ (recognizing that rubber elasticity is best treated at constant $T$ and $V$ to isolate the entropic contribution):
$$\boxed{f = \left(\frac{\partial A}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V} = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V} - T\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V}}$$

> **Logic:** By defining $A = U - TS$ and applying the First + Second Laws, the force is cleanly separated into an **internal energy** (enthalpic) part and an **entropy** (entropic) part. This is the foundational equation of rubber thermodynamics.

## 3.2 Derivation II: The Equation of State of Rubber

### Starting Point
From the previous derivation, the fundamental expression is:
$$f = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V} - T\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V} \quad \text{...(1)}$$

### Step 1: Take the Temperature Derivative

Differentiate (1) with respect to $T$ at constant $L$ and $V$:
$$\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial T}\right)_{L,V} = \left(\frac{\partial^2 U}{\partial T\,\partial L}\right)_{V} - \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V} - T\left(\frac{\partial^2 S}{\partial T\,\partial L}\right)_{V}$$

### Step 2: Use the Maxwell-Type Identity

From $dA = f\,dL - S\,dT$, we know:
$$\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial T}\right)_{L,V} = -\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V} \quad \text{(cross-partial symmetry of } dA \text{)}$$

### Step 3: Recognize the Identity
$$\left(\frac{\partial^2 U}{\partial T\,\partial L}\right)_{V} = \left(\frac{\partial^2 U}{\partial L\,\partial T}\right)_{V} = \left(\frac{\partial C_L}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V}$$

For an **ideal rubber**, the internal energy is independent of length, so $\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V} = 0$, and consequently $\left(\frac{\partial^2 U}{\partial T\,\partial L}\right)_{V} = 0$.

### Step 4: Substituting and Simplifying

In the general case (keeping $\partial U / \partial L$ term), we use the relation:
$$\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V} = -\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial T}\right)_{L,V}$$

Substituting back into (1):
$$f = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V} + T\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial T}\right)_{L,V}$$

### Final Boxed Result (Equation of State of Rubber)

$$\boxed{f = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V} + T\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial T}\right)_{L,V}}$$

> **Logic:** This elegant equation allows experimentalists to compute the **internal energy contribution** to the elastic force simply by measuring the **slope of $f$ vs $T$** at constant length and volume. The term $T(\partial f / \partial T)$ is the **entropic** component.

## 3.3 Derivation III: Entropy Change on Affine Network Stretching

### Starting Point
For a single Gaussian chain with end-to-end vector $\vec{r}$, the number of configurations is:
$$\Omega(\vec{r}) = \left(\frac{3}{2\pi \langle r^2 \rangle_0}\right)^{3/2} \exp\left(-\frac{3r^2}{2\langle r^2\rangle_0}\right)$$

### Step 1: Entropy of a Single Chain
$$S_1 = k_B \ln \Omega(\vec{r}) = c - \frac{3k_B r^2}{2\langle r^2\rangle_0}$$

where $c$ is a constant (independent of deformation).

### Step 2: Entropy of the Network
For $N$ independent chains in a network:
$$S = N S_1 = \text{const} - \frac{3N k_B}{2\langle r^2\rangle_0}\langle r^2 \rangle$$

### Step 3: Apply Affine Deformation
In an **affine network**, the mean-square end-to-end distance transforms as:
$$\langle r^2 \rangle = \frac{\langle r^2 \rangle_0}{3}\left(\lambda_x^2 + \lambda_y^2 + \lambda_z^2\right)$$

For **incompressible** uniaxial extension along $x$ with $\lambda_x = \lambda$ and $\lambda_y = \lambda_z = 1/\sqrt{\lambda}$ (volume conservation: $\lambda_x \lambda_y \lambda_z = 1$):
$$\lambda_x^2 + \lambda_y^2 + \lambda_z^2 = \lambda^2 + \frac{2}{\lambda}$$

### Step 4: Entropy of the Deformed Network
$$S = S_0 - \frac{1}{2} N k_B \left(\lambda^2 + \frac{2}{\lambda} - 3\right)$$

### Step 5: Entropy Change
$$\boxed{\Delta S = S - S_0 = -\frac{1}{2} N k_B \left(\lambda^2 + \frac{2}{\lambda} - 3\right)}$$

> **Logic:** Each chain, when stretched, has fewer available configurations → entropy decreases. The negative sign confirms that stretching reduces disorder.

## 3.4 Derivation IV: Force in an Ideal Affine Gaussian Rubber

### Starting Point
For an ideal rubber, $U = $ const., so $A = -TS$. Hence:
$$f = -T\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V}$$

### Step 1: Express $S$ in Terms of $L$
Since $\lambda = L / L_0$:
$$S = S_0 - \frac{1}{2} N k_B \left[\left(\frac{L}{L_0}\right)^2 + 2\frac{L_0}{L} - 3\right]$$

### Step 2: Differentiate with Respect to $L$
$$\frac{\partial S}{\partial L} = -\frac{1}{2} N k_B \left[\frac{2L}{L_0^2} - \frac{2L_0}{L^2}\right] = -N k_B \left[\frac{L}{L_0^2} - \frac{L_0}{L^2}\right]$$

### Step 3: Substitute into the Force Expression
$$f = -T \cdot \left(-N k_B\right)\left[\frac{L}{L_0^2} - \frac{L_0}{L^2}\right]$$

### Step 4: Rewrite in Terms of $\lambda$
$$\boxed{f = \frac{N k_B T}{L_0}\left(\lambda - \frac{1}{\lambda^2}\right)}$$

> **Logic:** This is the **KTU board-favorite final equation** for the force in an ideal affine Gaussian rubber. Note that the force is **directly proportional to $T$** — heating a stretched rubber increases the force, confirming entropic elasticity.

## 3.5 Derivation V: Thermoelastic Inversion and the Gough–Joule Effect

### Starting Point
From the equation of state:
$$f = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V} + T\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial T}\right)_{L,V}$$

### Step 1: At the Inversion Temperature
At $T = T_i$, the force has an extremum with respect to $T$:
$$\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial T}\right)_{L,V} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad f = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V}$$

### Step 2: Above $T_i$ (Typical Rubber Regime)
$(\partial f / \partial T) > 0$ → force increases with $T$ at constant $L$ → **entropic dominance**.

### Step 3: Below $T_i$ (Glassy-like Regime)
$(\partial f / \partial T) < 0$ → force decreases with $T$ → **enthalpic dominance**.

### Step 4: Gough–Joule Effect Derivation
Holding $f$ constant, the length changes with temperature. Using $dA = f\,dL - S\,dT$ at constant $A$ (reversible process):
$$f\,dL = S\,dT \quad \Rightarrow \quad \left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial T}\right)_{f,A} = \frac{S}{f}$$

For a stretched rubber, $S < S_0$ (entropy decreased). Since $f > 0$, we get $(\partial L / \partial T)_f < 0$ — **length decreases on heating** (rubber band contracts when heated under tension).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real Rubber Behavior:** Due to thermal expansion and volume changes, the experimentally measured $(\partial f / \partial T)_{L,P}$ differs slightly from the constant-volume value. The conversion formula is:
> $$\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial T}\right)_{L,P} = \left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial T}\right)_{L,V} + \left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial V}\right)_{T,L}\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_{P,f}$$

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question Paper]**
**Differentiate between entropic and enthalpic elasticity. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Entropic Elasticity | Enthalpic Elasticity |
|---|---|---|
| Origin | Change in **entropy** $S$ | Change in **internal energy** $U$ |
| Force vs. Temperature | $f$ **increases** with $T$ | $f$ **decreases** with $T$ |
| Magnitude of strain | Very large (**up to 1000 %**) | Small (**< 1 %**) |
| Reversibility | Nearly perfectly reversible | Often partially plastic |
| Example | **Natural rubber**, polyisoprene | **Steel**, copper, glass |

> The rubber band contracts when heated under tension (entropy-driven), while a metal wire expands (enthalpic thermal expansion).

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**State and explain the Gough–Joule effect.**

**Model Answer:**
The **Gough–Joule effect** describes the **coupled thermo-mechanical response** of an elastomer:
1. **Adiabatic stretching** of a rubber band causes its temperature to **rise**.
2. **Isometric heating** (heating at constant length) causes the tension to **increase**.
3. **Isotensional heating** (heating at constant force) causes the rubber to **contract**.

This effect is a direct macroscopic signature of the **entropic origin** of rubber elasticity — heating increases the entropic restoring force.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper, Module 4]**
**(a)** [7 Marks] Starting from the First and Second Laws of Thermodynamics, derive the **equation of state for a rubber** and show that the tensile force can be separated into energetic and entropic contributions.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Using the **affine network model** for a Gaussian rubber, derive an expression for the **entropy change** on uniaxial stretching and obtain the **force-extension relation** for an ideal rubber.

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Equation of State Derivation

**Step 1: First Law**
$$dU = \delta Q_{rev} + \delta W = T\,dS + f\,dL$$

**Step 2: Helmholtz free energy**
$$A = U - TS \quad \Rightarrow \quad dA = dU - T\,dS - S\,dT$$

Substituting $dU$:
$$dA = (T\,dS + f\,dL) - T\,dS - S\,dT = f\,dL - S\,dT$$

**Step 3: Force expression**
$$\left(\frac{\partial A}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V} = f$$

**Step 4: Expand**
$$f = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V} - T\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V}$$

**Step 5: Use cross-partial symmetry**
$$\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V} = -\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial T}\right)_{L,V}$$

**Step 6: Final equation of state**
$$f = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V} + T\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial T}\right)_{L,V}$$

> **Energetic term:** $(\partial U / \partial L)_{T,V}$ is small (5–15 %).
> **Entropic term:** $T(\partial f / \partial T)_{L,V}$ is dominant (85–95 %).

---

#### Part (b) — Affine Network Force Derivation

**Step 1: Entropy of a single Gaussian chain**
$$S_1 = k_B \ln \Omega(\vec{r}) = c - \frac{3k_B r^2}{2\langle r^2\rangle_0}$$

**Step 2: For $N$ chains in the network**
$$S = \text{const} - \frac{3N k_B}{2\langle r^2\rangle_0}\langle r^2\rangle$$

**Step 3: Affine deformation** (uniaxial extension $\lambda$):
$$\langle r^2\rangle = \frac{\langle r^2\rangle_0}{3}\left(\lambda^2 + \frac{2}{\lambda}\right)$$

**Step 4: Entropy change**
$$\Delta S = -\frac{1}{2} N k_B\left(\lambda^2 + \frac{2}{\lambda} - 3\right)$$

**Step 5: Force** (using $f = -T\,\partial S / \partial L$ with $\lambda = L/L_0$):
$$f = \frac{N k_B T}{L_0}\left(\lambda - \frac{1}{\lambda^2}\right)$$

> This is the **board-favorite final equation** for the force in an ideal affine Gaussian rubber.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
**(a)** [7 Marks] What is the **thermoelastic inversion phenomenon** in rubber? Derive the condition for the inversion temperature $T_i$ and discuss the physical significance of rubber being entropic above $T_i$.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A natural rubber sample contains $N = 1.5 \times 10^{20}$ network chains per m³. It is stretched at 300 K from $L_0 = 0.10$ m to $L = 0.25$ m. Calculate the **tensile force** assuming ideal Gaussian behavior.

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Thermoelastic Inversion

**Conceptual Setup:**
The force-temperature relation at constant length shows a sign change at a specific temperature.

**Derivation:**
From the equation of state:
$$f = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial L}\right)_{T,V} + T\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial T}\right)_{L,V}$$

At the **inversion temperature** $T_i$, the force becomes independent of $T$:
$$\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial T}\right)_{L,V}\bigg|_{T_i} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad f = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial L}\right)_{T_i, V}$$

**Physical Significance:**

| Regime | Behavior | Dominant Contribution |
|---|---|---|
| $T > T_i$ | $f$ increases with $T$ | **Entropic** (rubber-like) |
| $T = T_i$ | Force independent of $T$ | Transition point |
| $T < T_i$ | $f$ decreases with $T$ | **Enthalpic** (glass-like) |

> For most elastomers, $T_i$ is **above** the glass transition $T_g$, so the rubber operates in the entropic regime. The inversion phenomenon confirms that rubber is not a "normal" elastic solid.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem

**Given:**
- $N = 1.5 \times 10^{20}$ chains/m³
- $T = 300$ K
- $L_0 = 0.10$ m
- $L = 0.25$ m
- $k_B = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K

**Step 1: Calculate the extension ratio**
$$\lambda = \frac{L}{L_0} = \frac{0.25}{0.10} = 2.5$$

**Step 2: Calculate $N k_B T$**
$$N k_B T = (1.5 \times 10^{20})(1.381 \times 10^{-23})(300) = 0.621 \text{ J/m}^3$$

**Step 3: Apply the force equation**
$$f = \frac{N k_B T}{L_0}\left(\lambda - \frac{1}{\lambda^2}\right)$$

**Step 4: Evaluate the bracket term**
$$\lambda - \frac{1}{\lambda^2} = 2.5 - \frac{1}{(2.5)^2} = 2.5 - 0.16 = 2.34$$

**Step 5: Calculate the force**
$$f = \frac{0.621}{0.10} \times 2.34 = 6.21 \times 2.34 \approx 14.53 \text{ N}$$

> **Final Answer:** $f \approx 14.53$ N

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting units:** $N$ must be **chains per unit volume** (m⁻³). If given as total chains, divide by sample volume first.
> 2. **Sign errors in derivatives:** $(\partial S / \partial L) = -(\partial f / \partial T)$ — students often miss the **minus sign**.
> 3. **Confusing $\lambda$ with strain $\varepsilon$:** $\lambda = 1 + \varepsilon$, not $\lambda = \varepsilon$. For a 150 % extension, $\lambda = 2.5$, **not** 1.5.
> 4. **Incomplete derivation:** The KTU board requires **all** intermediate steps in the Helmholtz free energy derivation — do not skip from $dU$ directly to the final equation.
> 5. **Mixing constant-P and constant-V derivatives:** Rubber thermodynamics is strictly **constant-volume** for the entropic analysis. Mixing $(\partial f / \partial T)_{L,P}$ with $(\partial f / \partial T)_{L,V}$ loses 2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Save Before the Exam!)**

- [x] **Rubber elasticity is entropic**, not enthalpic — restoring force comes from $S$ maximization.
- [x] **Master equation:** $f = (\partial U / \partial L)_{T,V} - T(\partial S / \partial L)_{T,V}$
- [x] **Equation of state of rubber:** $f = (\partial U / \partial L)_{T,V} + T(\partial f / \partial T)_{L,V}$
- [x] **Ideal rubber force:** $f = (N k_B T / L_0)(\lambda - 1/\lambda^2)$
- [x] **Entropy change on stretching:** $\Delta S = -\tfrac{1}{2} N k_B(\lambda^2 + 2/\lambda - 3)$
- [x] **Shear modulus:** $G = N k_B T$; **Young's modulus:** $E = 3 N k_B T$ (ideal, small strain).
- [x] **Gough–Joule effect:** Stretching heats rubber; heating at constant force contracts it.
- [x] **Thermoelastic inversion:** At $T_i$, $(\partial f / \partial T)_{L,V} = 0$.
- [x] **Cross-partial identity:** $(\partial S / \partial L)_{T,V} = -(\partial f / \partial T)_{L,V}$ — derive carefully with sign.
- [x] **Affine network assumption:** Junction points move **linearly** with bulk deformation.
- [x] **Incompressibility constraint:** $\lambda_x \lambda_y \lambda_z = 1$ (used in uniaxial extension: $\lambda_y = \lambda_z = 1/\sqrt{\lambda}$).
- [x] **Real rubber corrections:** Flory–Wall front factor, Mooney–Rivlin, non-Gaussian effects.
- [x] **Numerical constants:** $R = 8.314$ J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹; $k_B = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ J·K⁻¹; $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ mol⁻¹.

> **Final Tip:** In KTU exams, **always state the assumptions** (ideal rubber, affine deformation, incompressibility, Gaussian chain statistics) before applying the formulas. Examiners award **1–2 marks** specifically for correctly framing the assumptions.
